THE VIRGINIANS 





 CHAPTER I. in which one of the Virginians visit home 

 on the library wall of one of the most famous writer of America , there hang two crossed sword , which his relative wear in the great War of Independence . the one sword be gallantly draw in the service of the king , the other be the weapon of a brave and honoured republican soldier . the possessor of the harmless trophy have earn for himself a name alike honour in his ancestor ' country and his own , where genius such as his have always a peaceful welcome . 
 the ensue history remind I of yonder sword in the historian 's study at Boston . in the Revolutionary War , the subject of this story , native of America , and child of the Old Dominion , find themselves engage on different side in the quarrel , come together peaceably at its conclusion , as brother should , their love ever have materially diminish , however angrily the contest divide they . the colonel in scarlet , and the general in blue and buff , hang side by side in the wainscoted parlour of the Warringtons , in England , where a descendant of one of the brother have show their portrait to I , with many of the letter which they write , and the book and paper which belong to they . in the Warrington family , and to distinguish they from other personage of that respectable race , these effigy have always go by the name of " the Virginians " ; by which name their memoir be christen . 
 they both of they pass much time in Europe . they live just on the verge of that Old World from which we be drift away so swiftly . they be familiar with many variety of man and fortune . their lot bring they into contact with personage of whom we read only in book , who seem alive , as I read in the Virginians ' letter regard they , whose voice I almost fancy I hear , as I read the yellow page write score of year since , blot with the boyish tear of disappointed passion , dutifully despatch after famous ball and ceremony of the grand Old World , scribble by camp - fire , or out of prison ; nay , there be one that have a bullet through it , and of which a great portion of the text be blot out with the blood of the bearer . 
 these letter have probably never be preserve , but for the affectionate thrift of one person , to whom they never fail in their dutiful correspondence . their mother keep all her son ' letter , from the very first , in which Henry , the young of the twin , send his love to his brother , then ill of a sprain at his grandfather 's house of Castlewood , in Virginia , and thank his grandpapa for a horse which he rid with his tutor , down to the last , " from my beloved son , " which reach she but a few hour before her death . the venerable lady never visit Europe , save once with her parent in the reign of George the Second ; take refuge in Richmond when the house of Castlewood be burn down during the war ; and be call Madam Esmond ever after that event ; never care much for the name or family of Warrington , which she hold in very slight estimation as compare to her own . 
 the letter of the Virginians , as the reader will presently see , from specimen to be show to he , be by no means full . they be hint rather than description indication and outline chiefly : it may be , that the present writer have mistake the form , and fill in the colour wrongly : but , pore over the document , I have try to imagine the situation of the writer , where he be , and by what person surround . I have draw the figure as I fancy they be ; set down conversation as I think I might have hear they ; and so , to the good of my ability , endeavour to revivify the bygone time and people . with what success the task have be accomplish , with what profit or amusement to himself , the kind reader will please to determine . 
 one summer morning in the year 1756 , and in the reign of his Majesty King George the Second , the Young Rachel , virginian ship , Edward Franks master , come up the Avon river on her happy return from her annual voyage to the Potomac . she proceed to Bristol with the tide , and moor in the stream as near as possible to Trail 's wharf , to which she be consign . Mr. Trail , her part owner , who could survey his ship from his counting - house window , straightway take boat and come up her side . the owner of the Young Rachel , a large grave man in his own hair , and of a demure aspect , give the hand of welcome to Captain Franks , who stand on his deck , and congratulate the captain upon the speedy and fortunate voyage which he have make . and , remark that we ought to be thankful to Heaven for its mercy , he proceed presently to business by ask particular relative to cargo and passenger . 
 Franks be a pleasant man , who love a joke . " we have , " say he , " but yonder ugly negro boy , who be fetch the trunk , and a passenger who have the state cabin to himself . " 
 Mr. Trail look as if he would have prefer more mercy from Heaven . " confound you , Franks , and your luck ! the Duke William , which come in last week , bring fourteen , and she be not half of our tonnage . " 
 " and this passenger , who have the whole cabin , do not   pay nothing , " continue the Captain . " swear now , it will do you good , Mr. Trail , indeed it will . I have try the medicine . " 
 " a passenger take the whole cabin and not pay ? gracious mercy , be you a fool , Captain Franks ? " 
 " ask the passenger himself , for here he come . " and , as the master speak , a young man of some nineteen year of age come up the hatchway . he have a cloak and a sword under his arm , and be dress in deep mourning , and call out , " Gumbo , you idiot , why do not   you fetch the baggage out of the cabin ? well , shipmate , our journey be end . you will see all the little folk to - night whom you have be talk about . give my love to Polly , and Betty , and Little Tommy ; not forget my duty to Mrs. Franks . I think , yesterday , the voyage would never be do , and now I be almost sorry it be over . that little berth in my cabin look very comfortable now I be go to leave it . " 
 Mr. Trail scowl at the young passenger who have pay no money for his passage . he scarcely nod his head to the stranger , when Captain Franks say , " this here gentleman be Mr. Trail , sir , whose name you have a - heerd of . " 
 " it be pretty well know in Bristol , sir , " say Mr. Trail , majestically . 
 " and this be Mr. Warrington , Madam Esmond Warrington 's son , of Castlewood , " continue the Captain . 
 the british merchant 's hat be instantly off his head , and the owner of the beaver be make a prodigious number of bow as if a crown prince be before he . 
 " gracious power , Mr. Warrington ! this be a delight , indeed ! what a crown mercy that your voyage should have be so prosperous ! you must have my boat to go on shore . let I cordially and respectfully welcome you to England : let I shake your hand as the son of my benefactress and patroness , Mrs. Esmond Warrington , whose name be know and honour on Bristol ' Change , I warrant you . be not   it , Franks ? " 
 " there be no sweet tobacco come from Virginia , and no well brand than the Three Castles , " say Mr. Franks , draw a great brass tobacco - box from his pocket , and thrust a quid into his jolly mouth . " you do not   know what a comfort it be , sir ! you will take to it , bless you , as you grow old . will not   he , Mr. Trail ? I wish you have ten shipload of it instead of one . you might have ten shipload : I have   tell Madam Esmond so ; I have   ride over her plantation ; she treat I like a lord when I go to the house ; she do not   grudge I the good of wine , or keep I cool my heel in the counting - room as some folk do "   with a look at Mr. Trail . " she be a real bear lady , she be ; and might have a thousand hogshead as easy as her hundred , if there be but hand enough . " 
 " I have lately engage in the Guinea trade , and could supply her ladyship with any number of healthy young negro before next fall , " say Mr. Trail , obsequiously . 
 " we be averse to the purchase of negro from Africa , " say the young gentleman , coldly . " my grandfather and my mother have always object to it , and I do not like to think of sell or buy the poor wretch . " 
 " it be for their good , my dear young sir ! for their temporal and their spiritual good ! " cry Mr. Trail . " and we purchase the poor creature only for their benefit ; let I talk this matter over with you at my own house . I can introduce you to a happy home , a christian family , and a british merchant 's honest fare . can not   I , Captain Franks ? " 
 " can not   say , " growl the Captain . " never ask I to take bite or sup at your table . ask I to psalm - singing once , and to hear Mr. Ward preach : do not   care for they sort of entertainment . " 
 not choose to take any notice of this remark , Mr. Trail continue in his low tone : " business be business , my dear young sir , and I know , ' tis only my duty , the duty of all of we , to cultivate the fruit of the earth in their season . as the heir of Lady Esmond 's estate for I speak , I believe , to the heir of that great property ? " 
 the young gentleman make a bow . 
 " I would urge upon you , at the very early moment , the propriety , the duty of increase the ample mean with which Heaven have bless you . as an honest factor , I could not do otherwise ; as a prudent man , should I scruple to speak of what will tend to your profit and mine ? no , my dear Mr. George . " 
 " my name be not George ; my name be Henry , " say the young man as he turn his head away , and his eye fill with tear . 
 " gracious power ! what do you mean , sir ? do you not say you be my lady 's heir ? and be not George Esmond Warrington , Esq . " 
 " hold your tongue , you fool ! " cry Mr. Franks , strike the merchant a tough blow on his sleek side , as the young lad turn away . " do not   you see the young gentleman a - swab his eye , and note his black clothe ? " 
 " what do you mean , Captain Franks , by lay your hand on your owner ? Mr. George be the heir ; I know the Colonel 's will well enough . " 
 " Mr. George be there , " say the Captain , point with his thumb to the deck . 
 " where ? " cry the factor . 
 " Mr. George be there ! " reiterate the Captain , again lift up his finger towards the topmast , or the sky beyond . " he be dead a year , sir , come next 9th of July . he would go out with General Braddock on that dreadful business to the Belle Riviere . he and a thousand more never come back again . every man of they be murder as he fall . you know the indian way , Mr. Trail ? " and here the Captain pass his hand rapidly round his head . " horrible ! be not   it , sir ? horrible ! he be a fine young man , the very picture of this one ; only his hair be black , which be now hang in a bloody indian wigwam . he be often and often on board of the Young Rachel , and would have his chest of book break open on deck before they be land . he be a shy and silent young gent : not like this one , which be the merry , wild young fellow , full of his song and fun . he take on dreadful at the news ; go to his bed , have that fever which lay so many of them by the heel along that swampy Potomac , but he be get well on the voyage : the voyage make every one well ; and , in course , the young gentleman can not   be for ever a - crying after a brother who die and leave he a great fortune . ever since we sight Ireland he have be quite gay and happy , only he would go off at time , when he be most merry , say , ' I wish my dear georgy could enjoy this here sight along with I , and when you mention the t'other 's name , you see , he could not   stand it . ' " and the honest Captain 's own eye fill with tear , as he turn and look towards the object of his compassion . 
 Mr. Trail assume a lugubrious countenance befit the tragic compliment with which he prepare to greet the young Virginian ; but the latter answer he very curtly , decline his offer of hospitality , and only stay in Mr. Trail 's house long enough to drink a glass of wine and to take up a sum of money of which he stand in need . but he and Captain Franks part on the very warm term , and all the little crew of the Young Rachel cheer from the ship 's side as their passenger leave it . 
 again and again Harry Warrington and his brother have pore over the english map , and determine upon the course which they should take upon arrive at home . all Americans who love the old country and what gently - nurture man or woman of anglo - Saxon race do not ? have ere this rehearse their english travel , and visit in fancy the spot with which their hope , their parent ' fond story , their friend ' description , have render they familiar . there be few thing to I more affect in the history of the quarrel which divide the two great nation than the recurrence of that word Home , as use by the young towards the eld country . Harry Warrington have his chart lay out . before London , and its glorious temple of St. Paul 's and St. Peter 's ; its grim Tower , where the brave and loyal have shed their blood , from Wallace down to Balmerino and Kilmarnock , pity by gentle heart ; before the awful window of Whitehall , whence the martyr Charles have issue , to kneel once more , and then ascend to Heaven ; before Playhouses , Parks , and Palaces , wondrous resort of wit , pleasure , and splendour ; before Shakspeare 's resting - place under the tall spire which rise by Avon , amidst the sweet Warwickshire pasture ; before Derby , and Falkirk , and Culloden , where the cause of honour and loyalty have fall , it might be to rise no more : before all these point of their pilgrimage there be one which the young virginian brother hold even more sacred , and that be the home of their family , that old Castlewood in Hampshire , about which their parent have talk so fondly . from Bristol to Bath , from Bath to Salisbury , to winchester , to Hexton , to home ; they know the way , and have map the journey many and many a time . 
 we must fancy our american traveller to be a handsome young fellow , whose suit of sable only make he look the more interesting . the plump landlady from her bar , surround by her china and punch - bowl , and stout gild bottle of strong water , and glitter row of silver flagon , look kindly after the young gentleman as he pass through the inn - hall from his post - chaise , and the obsequious chamberlain bow he upstairs to the Rose or the dolphin . the trim chambermaid drop her good curtsey for his fee , and Gumbo , in the inn - kitchen , where the townsfolk drink their mug of ale by the great fire , brag of his young master 's splendid house in Virginia , and of the immense wealth to which he be heir . the postchaise whirl the traveller through the most delightful home - scenery his eye have ever light on . if english landscape be pleasant to the American of the present day , who must need contrast the rich wood and glow pasture , and picturesque ancient village of the old country with the rough aspect of his own , how much pleasanter must Harry Warrington 's course have be , whose journey have lie through swamp and forest solitude from one virginian ordinary to another log - house at the end of the day 's route , and who now light suddenly upon the busy , happy , splendid scene of english summer ? and the highroad , a hundred year ago , be not that grass - grow desert of the present time . it be alive with constant travel and traffic : the country town and inn swarm with life and gaiety . the ponderous waggon , with its bell and plod team ; the light post - coach that achieve the journey from the White Hart , Salisbury , to the Swan with two Necks , London , in two day ; the string of packhorse that have not yet leave the road ; my lord 's gilt postchaise - and - six , with the outrider gallop on ahead ; the country squire 's great coach and heavy Flanders mare ; the farmer trot to market , or the parson jolt to the cathedral town on Dumpling , his wife behind on the pillion all these crowd sight and brisk people greet the young traveller on his summer journey . Hodge , the farmer 's boy , take off his hat , and Polly , the milkmaid , bob a curtsey , as the chaise whirl over the pleasant village - green , and the white - head child lift their chubby face and cheer . the church - spire glisten with gold , the cottage - gable glare in sunshine , the great elm murmur in summer , or cast purple shadow over the grass . young Warrington never have such a glorious day , or witness a scene so delightful . to be nineteen year of age , with high health , high spirit , and a full purse , to be make your first journey , and roll through the country in a postchaise at nine mile an hour o happy youth ! almost it make one young to think of he ! but Harry be too eager to give more than a pass glance at the Abbey at Bath , or gaze with more than a moment 's wonder at the mighty Minster at Salisbury . until he behold Home it seem to he he have no eye for any other place . 
 at last the young gentleman 's postchaise draw up at the rustic inn on Castlewood Green , of which his grandsire have many a time talk to he , and which bear as its ensign , swing from an elm near the inn porch , the Three Castles of the Esmond family . they have a sign , too , over the gateway of Castlewood House , bear the same cognisance . this be the hatchment of Francis , Lord Castlewood , who now lie in the chapel hard by , his son reign in his stead . 
 Harry Warrington have often hear of Francis , Lord Castlewood . it be for Frank 's sake , and for his great love towards the boy , that Colonel Esmond determine to forgo his claim to the english estate and rank of his family , and retire to Virginia . the young man have lead a wild youth ; he have fight with distinction under Marlborough ; he have marry a foreign lady , and most lamentably adopt her religion . at one time he have be a Jacobite   for loyalty to the sovereign be ever hereditary in the Esmond family , but have receive some slight or injury from the Prince , which have cause he to rally to King George 's side . he have , on his second marriage , renounce the error of Popery which he have temporarily embrace , and return to the Established Church again . he have , from his constant support of the King and the Minister of the time being , be reward by his Majesty George II . , and die an english peer . an earl 's coronet now figure on the hatchment which hang over Castlewood gate and there be an end of the jolly gentleman . between Colonel Esmond , who have become his stepfather , and his lordship there have ever be a brief but affectionate correspondence on the Colonel 's part especially , who love his stepson , and have a hundred story to tell about he to his grandchild . Madam Esmond , however , say she could see nothing in her half - brother . he be dull , except when he drink too much wine , and that , to be sure , be every day at dinner . then he be boisterous , and his conversation not pleasant . he be good - look yes a fine tall stout animal ; she have rather her boy should follow a different model . in spite of the grandfather 's encomium of the late lord , the boy have no very great respect for their kinsman 's memory . the lad and their mother be staunch Jacobites , though have every respect for his present Majesty ; but right be right , and nothing could make their heart swerve from their allegiance to the descendant of the martyr Charles . 
 with a beat heart Harry Warrington walk from the inn towards the house where his grandsire 's youth have be pass . the little village - green of Castlewood slop down towards the river , which be span by an old bridge of a single broad arch , and from this the ground rise gradually towards the house , grey with many gable and buttress , and back by a darkle wood . an old man sate at the wicket on a stone bench in front of the great arched entrance to the house , over which the earl 's hatchment be hang . an old dog be crouch at the man 's foot . immediately above the ancient sentry at the gate be an open casement with some homely flower in the window , from behind which good - humour girl ' face be peep . they be watch the young traveller dress in black as he walk up gaze towards the castle , and the ebony attendant who follow the gentleman 's step also accoutre in mourning . so be he at the gate in mourning , and the girl when they come out have black ribbon . 
 to Harry 's surprise , the old man accost he by his name . " you have have a nice ride to Hexton , Master Harry , and the sorrel carry you well . " 
 " I think you must be Lockwood , " say Harry , with rather a tremulous voice , hold out his hand to the old man . his grandfather have often tell he of Lockwood , and how he have accompany the Colonel and the young Viscount in Marlborough 's war forty year ago . the veteran seem puzzle by the mark of affection which Harry extend to he . the old dog gaze at the new - comer , and then go and put his head between his knee . " I have hear of you often . how do you know my name ? " 
 " they say I forget most thing , " say the old man , with a smile ; " but I be not   so bad as that quite . only this mornin ' , when you go out , my darter say , ' Father , do you know why you have a black coat on ? ' ' in course I know why I have a black coat , ' say I. ' my lord be dead . they say ' twa a foul blow , and Master Frank be my lord now , and Master Harry ' why , what have you do since you have   go out this morning ? why , you have a - grow'd tall and change your hair though I know I know you . " 
 one of the young woman have trip out by this time from the porter 's lodge , and drop the strange a pretty curtsey . " Grandfather sometimes do not recollect very well , " she say , point to her head . " your honour seem to have hear of Lockwood ? " 
 " and you , have you never hear of Colonel Francis Esmond ? " 
 " he be Captain and Major in Webb 's foot , and I be with he in two campaign , sure enough , " cry Lockwood . " be not   I , Ponto ? " 
 " the Colonel as marry Viscountess Rachel , my late lord 's mother ? and go to live amongst the Indians ? we have hear of he . sure we have his picture in our gallery , and hisself paint it . " 
 " go to live in Virginia , and die there seven year ago , and I be his grandson . " 
 " Lord , your honour ! why , your honour 's skin 's as white as mine , " cry molly . " Grandfather , do you hear this ? his honour be Colonel Esmond 's grandson that use to send you tobacco , and his honour have come all the way from Virginia . " 
 " to see you , Lockwood , " say the young man , " and the family . I only set foot on english ground yesterday , and my first visit be for home . I may see the house , though the family be from home ? " molly dare to say Mrs. Barker would let his honour see the house , and Harry Warrington make his way across the court , seem to know the place as well as if he have be bear there , Miss Molly think , who follow , accompany by Mr. Gumbo make she a profusion of polite bow and speech . 




 CHAPTER II . in which Harry have to pay for his Supper 

 Colonel Esmond 's grandson ring for a while at his ancestor ' house of Castlewood , before any one within seem inclined to notice his summon . the servant , who at length issue from the door , seem to be very little affect by the announcement that the visitor be a relation of the family . the family be away , and in their absence John care very little for their relative , but be eager to get back to his game at card with Thomas in the window - seat . the housekeeper be busy get ready for my lord and my lady , who be expect that evening . only by strong entreaty could Harry gain leave to see my lady 's sitting - room and the picture - room , where , sure enough , be a portrait of his grandfather in periwig and breastplate , the counterpart of their picture in Virginia , and a likeness of his grandmother , as Lady Castlewood , in a yet early habit of Charles II . 's time ; her neck bare , her fair golden hair wave over her shoulder in ringlet which he remember to have see snowy white . from the contemplation of these sight the sulky housekeeper drive he . her family be about to arrive . there be my lady the Countess , and my lord and his brother , and the young lady , and the Baroness , who be to have the state bedroom . who be the Baroness ? the Baroness Bernstein , the young lady ' aunt . Harry write down his name on a paper from his own pocket - book , and lay it on a table in the hall . " Henry Esmond Warrington , of Castlewood , in Virginia , arrive in England yesterday stay at the Three Castles in the village . " the lackey rise up from their card to open the door to he , in order to get their " wail , " and Gumbo quit the bench at the gate , where he have be talk with old Lockwood , the porter , who take Harry 's guinea , hardly know the meaning of the gift . during the visit to the home of his father , Harry have only see little Polly 's countenance that be the least unselfish or kindly : he walk away , not care to own how disappointed he be , and what a damp have be strike upon he by the aspect of the place . they ought to have know he . have any of they ride up to his house in Virginia , whether the master be present or absent , the guest would have be make welcome , and , in sight of his ancestor ' hall , he have to go and ask for a dish of bacon and egg at a country alehouse ! 
 after his dinner , he go to the bridge and sate on it , look towards the old house , behind which the sun be descend as the rook come caw home to their nest in the elm . his young fancy picture to itself many of the ancestor of whom his mother and grandsire have tell he . he fancy knight and huntsman cross the ford ; cavalier of King Charles 's day ; my Lord Castlewood , his grandmother 's first husband , rid out with hawk and hound . the recollection of his dear lose brother come back to he as he indulge in these revery , and smite he with a pang of exceed tenderness and longing , insomuch that the young man hang his head and feel his sorrow renew for the dear friend and companion with whom , until of late , all his pleasure and grief have be share . as he sate plunge in his own thought , which be mingle up with the mechanical clinking of the blacksmith 's forge hard by , the noise of the evening , the talk of the rook , and the calling of the bird round about a couple of young man on horseback dash over the bridge . one of they , with an oath , call he a fool , and tell he to keep out of the way the other , who fancy he might have jostle the foot - passenger , and possibly might have send he over the parapet , push on more quickly when he reach the other side of the water , call likewise to Tom to come on ; and the pair of young gentleman be up the hill on their way to the house before Harry have recover himself from his surprise at their appearance , and wrath at their behaviour . in a minute or two , this advanced guard be follow by two livery servant on horseback , who scowl at the young traveller on the bridge a true british welcome of Curse you , who be you ? after these , in a minute or two , come a coach - and - six , a ponderous vehicle have need of the horse which draw it , and contain three lady , a couple of maid , and an armed man on a seat behind the carriage . three handsome pale face look out at Harry Warrington as the carriage pass over the bridge , and do not return the salute which , recognise the family arm , he give it . the gentleman behind the carriage glare at he haughtily . Harry feel terribly alone . he think he would go back to Captain Franks . the Rachel and her little tossing cabin seem a cheery spot in comparison to that on which he stand . the inn - folk do not know his name of Warrington . they tell he that be my lady in the coach , with her stepdaughter , my Lady Maria , and her daughter , my Lady Fanny ; and the young gentleman in the grey frock be Mr. William , and he with powder on the chestnut be my lord . it be the latter have swear the loud , and call he a fool ; and it be the grey frock which have nearly gallop Harry into the ditch . 
 the landlord of the Three Castles have show Harry a bedchamber , but he have refuse to have his portmanteaux unpacked , think that , for a certainty , the folk of the great house would invite he to theirs . one , two , three hour pass , and there come no invitation . Harry be fain to have his trunk open at last , and to call for his slipper and gown . just before dark , about two hour after the arrival of the first carriage , a second chariot with four horse have pass over the bridge , and a stout , high - coloured lady , with a very dark pair of eye , have look hard at Mr. Warrington . that be the Baroness Bernstein , the landlady say , my lord 's aunt , and Harry remember the first Lady Castlewood have come of a german family . Earl , and Countess , and Baroness , and postillion , and gentleman , and horse , have all disappear behind the castle gate , and Harry be fain to go to bed at last , in the most melancholy mood and with a cruel sense of neglect and loneliness in his young heart . he could not sleep , and , besides , ere long , hear a prodigious noise , and cursing , and giggling , and scream from my landlady 's bar , which would have serve to keep he awake . 
 then Gumbo 's voice be hear without , remonstrate , " you can not go in , sar my master asleep , sar ! " but a shrill voice , with many oath , which Harry Warrington recognise , curse Gumbo for a stupid , negro woolly - pate , and he be push aside , give entrance to a flood of oath into the room , and a young gentleman behind they . 
 " beg your pardon , Cousin Warrington , " cry the young blasphemer , " be you asleep ? beg your pardon for rid you over on the bridge . do not   know you course should not   have do it think it be a lawyer with a writ dress in black , you know . Gad ! think it be Nathan come to nab I . " and Mr. William laugh incoherently . it be evident that he be excited with liquor . 
 " you do I great honour to mistake I for a sheriff's - officer , cousin , " say Harry , with great gravity , sit up in his tall nightcap . 
 " Gad ! I think it be Nathan , and be go to send you souse into the river . but I ask your pardon . you see I have be drink at the Bell at Hexton , and the punch be good at the Bell at Hexton . Hullo ! you , Davis ! a bowl of punch ; d'you hear ? " 
 " I have have my share for to - night , cousin , and I should think you have , " Harry continue , always in the dignified style . 
 " you want I to go , Cousin What's - your - name , I see , " Mr. William say , with gravity . " you want I to go , and they want I to come , and I do not   want to come . I say , I would see he hang first , that be what I say . why should I trouble myself to come down all alone of an evening , and look after a fellow I do not   care a pin for ? zackly what I say . zackly what Castlewood say . why the devil should he go down ? Castlewood say , and so say my lady , but the Baroness would have you . it be all the Baroness 's doing , and if she say a thing , it must be do ; so you must just get up and come . " Mr. Esmond deliver these word with the most amiable rapidity and indistinctness , run they into one another , and tack about the room as he speak . but the young Virginian be in great wrath . " I tell you what , cousin , " he cry , " I will not   move for the Countess , or for the Baroness , or for all the cousin in Castlewood . " and when the landlord enter the chamber with the bowl of punch , which Mr. Esmond have order , the young gentleman in bed call out fiercely to the host , to turn that sot out of the room . 
 " Sot , you little tobacconist ! Sot , you Cherokee ! " scream out Mr. William . " jump out of bed , and I will drive my sword through your body . why do not   I do it to - day when I take you for a bailiff a confounded pettifog bum - bailiff ! " and he go on screech more oath and incoherency , until the landlord , the drawer , the hostler , and all the folk of the kitchen be bring to lead he away . after which Harry Warrington close his tent round he in sulky wrath , and , no doubt , finally go fast to sleep . 
 my landlord be very much more obsequious on the next morning when he meet his young guest , have now fully learn his name and quality . other messenger have come from the castle on the previous night to bring both the young gentleman home , and poor Mr. William , it appear , have return in a wheelbarrow , be not altogether unaccustomed to that mode of conveyance . " he never remember nothing about it the next day . he be of a real kind nature , Mr. William , " the landlord vow , " and the man get crown and half - crown from he by say that he beat they overnight when he be in liquor . he be the devil when he be tipsy , Mr. William , but when he be sober he be the very kind of young gentleman . " 
 as nothing be unknown to writer of biography of the present kind , it may be as well to state what have occur within the wall of Castlewood House , whilst Harry Warrington be without , await some token of recognition from his kinsman . on their arrival at home the family have find the paper on which the lad 's name be inscribe , and his appearance occasion a little domestic council . my Lord Castlewood suppose that must have be the young gentleman whom they have see on the bridge , and as they have not drown he they must invite he . let a man go down with the proper message , let a servant carry a note . Lady Fanny think it would be more civil if one of the brother would go to their kinsman , especially consider the original greeting which they have give . Lord Castlewood have not the slight objection to his brother William go yes , William should go . upon this Mr. William say   with a yet strong expression   that he would be hang if he would go . Lady Maria think the young gentleman whom they have remark at the bridge be a pretty fellow enough . Castlewood be dreadfully dull , I be sure neither of my brother do anything to make it amusing . he may be vulgar no doubt , he be vulgar but let we see the American . such be Lady Maria 's opinion . Lady Castlewood be neither for invite nor for refuse he , but for delaying . " wait till your aunt come , child ; perhaps the Baroness will not   like to see the young man ; at least , let we consult she before we ask he . " and so the hospitality to be offer by his near kinsfolk to poor Harry Warrington remain yet in abeyance . 
 at length the equipage of the Baroness Bernstein make its appearance , and whatever doubt there might be as to the reception of the virginian stranger , there be no lack of enthusiasm in this generous family regard their wealthy and powerful kinswoman . the state - chamber have already be prepare for she . the cook have arrive the previous day with instruction to get ready a supper for her such as her ladyship like . the table sparkle with old plate , and be set in the oak dining - room with the picture of the family round the wall . there be the late Viscount , his father , his mother , his sister these two lovely picture . there be his predecessor by Vandyck , and his Viscountess . there be Colonel Esmond , their relative in Virginia , about whose grandson the lady and gentleman of the Esmond family show such a very moderate degree of sympathy . 
 the feast set before their aunt , the Baroness , be a very good one , and her ladyship enjoy it . the supper occupy an hour or two , during which the whole Castlewood family be most attentive to their guest . the Countess press all the good dish upon she , of which she freely partook : the butler no soon see her glass empty than he fill it with champagne : the young folk and their mother keep up the conversation , not so much by talk , as by listen appropriately to their friend . she be full of spirit and humour . she seem to know everybody in Europe , and about those everybodie the wicked story . the Countess of Castlewood , ordinarily a very demure , severe woman , and a stickler for the propriety , smile at the very bad of these anecdote ; the girl look at one another and laugh at the maternal signal ; the boy giggle and roar with especial delight at their sister ' confusion . they also partook freely of the wine which the butler hand round , nor do they , or their guest , disdain the bowl of smoking punch , which be lay on the table after the supper . many and many a night , the Baroness say , she have drink at that table by her father 's side . " that be his place , " she point to the place where the Countess now sit . she see none of the old plate . that be all melt to pay his gambling debt . she hop , " young gentleman , that you do not   play . " 
 " never , on my word , " say Castlewood . 
 " never , ' pon honour , " say will wink at his brother . 
 the Baroness be very glad to hear they be such good boy . her face grow red with the punch ; and she become voluble , might have be think coarse , but that time be different , and those critic be inclined to be especially favourable . 
 she talk to the boy about their father , their grandfather other man and woman of the house . " the only man of the family be that , " she say , point   with an arm that be yet beautifully round and white   towards the picture of the military gentleman in the red coat and cuirass , and great black periwig . 
 " the Virginian ? what be he good for ? I always think he be good for nothing but to cultivate tobacco and my grandmother , " say my lord , laugh . 
 she strike her hand upon the table with an energy that make the glass dance . " I say he be the good of you all . there never be one of the male Esmonds that have more brain than a goose , except he . he be not fit for this wicked , selfish old world of ours , and he be right to go and live out of it . where would your father have be , young people , but for he ? " 
 " be he particularly kind to our papa ? " say Lady Maria . 
 " old story , my dear Maria ! " cry the Countess . " I be sure my dear Earl be very kind to he in give he that great estate in Virginia . " 
 " since his brother 's death , the lad who have be here to - day be heir to that . Mr. Draper tell I so ! peste ! I do not   know why my father give up such a property . " 
 " who have be here to - day ? " ask the Baroness , highly excited . 
 " Harry Esmond Warrington , of Virginia , " my lord answer : " a lad whom will nearly pitch into the river , and whom I press my lady the Countess to invite to stay here . " 
 " you mean that one of the virginian boy have be to Castlewood , and have not be ask to stay here ? " 
 " there be but one of they , my dear creature , " interpose the Earl . " the other , you know , have just be " 
 " for shame , for shame ! " 
 " oh ! it be not   pleasant , I confess , to be se " 
 " do you mean that a grandson of Henry Esmond , the master of this house , have be here , and none of you have offer he hospitality ? " 
 " since we do not   know it , and he be stay at the Castles ? " interpose Will . 
 " that he be stay at the Inn , and you be sit there ! " cry the old lady . " this be too bad call somebody to I . get I my hood I will go to the boy myself . come with I this instant , my Lord Castlewood . " 
 the young man rise up , evidently in wrath . " madame the Baroness of Bernstein , " he say , " your ladyship be welcome to go ; but as for I , I do not   choose to have such word as ' shameful ' apply to my conduct . I will not   go and fetch the young gentleman from Virginia , and I propose to sit here and finish this bowl of punch . eugene ! do not   eugene I , madam . I know her ladyship have a great deal of money , which you be desirous should remain in our amiable family . you want it more than I do . cringe for it I will not . " and he sink back in his chair . 
 the Baroness look at the family , who hold their head down , and then at my lord , but this time without any dislike . she lean over to he and say rapidly in German , " I have unright when I say the Colonel be the only man of the family . thou canst , if thou willest , Eugene . " to which remark my lord only bow . 
 " if you do not wish an old woman to go out at this hour of the night , let William , at least , go and fetch his cousin , " say the Baroness . 
 " the very thing I propose to he . " 
 " and so do we and so do we ! " cry the daughter in a breath . 
 " I be sure , I only want the dear Baroness 's consent ! " say their mother , " and shall be charm for my part to welcome our young relative . " 
 " will ! put on thy patten and get a lantern , and go fetch the Virginian , " say my lord . 
 " and we will have another bowl of punch when he come , " say William , who by this time have already have too much . and he go forth how we have see ; and how he have more punch ; and how ill he succeed in his embassy . 
 the worthy lady of Castlewood , as she catch sight of young Harry Warrington by the river - side , must have see a very handsome and interesting youth , and very likely have reason of her own for not desire his presence in her family . all mother be not eager to encourage the visit of interesting youth of nineteen in family where there be virgin of twenty . if Harry 's acre have be in Norfolk or Devon , in place of Virginia , no doubt the good Countess would have be rather more eager in her welcome . have she want he she would have give he her hand readily enough . if our people of ton be selfish , at any rate they show they be selfish ; and , be cold - hearted , at least have no hypocrisy of affection . 
 why should Lady Castlewood put herself out of the way to welcome the young stranger ? because he be friendless ? only a simpleton could ever imagine such a reason as that . People of fashion , like her ladyship , be friendly to those who have plenty of friend . a poor lad , alone , from a distant country , with only very moderate mean , and those not as yet in his own power , with uncouth manner very likely , and coarse provincial habit ; be a great lady call upon to put herself out of the way for such a youth ? allon donc ! he be quite as well at the alehouse as at the castle . 
 this , no doubt , be her ladyship 's opinion , which her kinswoman , the Baroness Bernstein , who know she perfectly well , entirely understand . the Baroness , too , be a woman of the world , and , possibly , on occasion , could be as selfish as any other person of fashion . she fully understand the cause of the deference which all the Castlewood family show to her mother , and daughter , and son , and be a woman of great humour , play upon the disposition of the various member of this family , amuse herself with their greediness , their humiliation , their artless respect for her money - box , and cling attachment to her purse . they be not very rich ; Lady Castlewood 's own money be settle on her child . the two elder have inherit nothing but flaxen head from their german mother , and a pedigree of prodigious distinction . but those who have money , and those who have none , be alike eager for the Baroness 's ; in this matter the rich be surely quite as greedy as the poor . 
 so if Madam Bernstein strike her hand on the table , and cause the glass and the person round it to tremble at her wrath , it be because she be excited with plenty of punch and champagne , which her ladyship be in the habit of take freely , and because she may have have a generous impulse when generous wine warm her blood , and feel indignant as she think of the poor lad yonder , sit friendless and lonely on the outside of his ancestor ' door ; not because she be specially angry with her relative , who she know would act precisely as they have do . 
 the exhibition of their selfishness and humiliation alike amuse she , as do Castlewood 's act of revolt . he be as selfish as the rest of the family , but not so mean ; and , as he candidly state , he could afford the luxury of a little independence , have tolerable estate to fall back upon . 
 Madam Bernstein be an early woman , restless , resolute , extraordinarily active for her age . she be up long before the languid Castlewood lady   just home from their London rout and ball   have quit their feather - bed , or jolly will have sleep off his various potation of punch . she be up , and pace the green terrace that sparkle with the sweet morning dew , which lie twinkling , also , on a flowery wilderness of trim parterre , and on the crisp wall of the dark box hedge , under which marble faun and dryad be cool themselves , whilst a thousand bird sing , the fountain plash and glitter in the rosy morning sunshine , and the rook caw from the great wood . 
 have the well - remember scene   for she have visit it often in childhood   a freshness and charm for she ? do it recall day of innocence and happiness , and do its calm beauty soothe or please , or awaken remorse in her heart ? her manner be more than ordinarily affectionate and gentle , when , presently , after pace the walk for a half - hour , the person for whom she be wait come to she . this be our young Virginian , to whom she have despatch an early billet by one of the Lockwoods . the note be sign B. Bernstein , and inform Mr. Esmond Warrington that his relative at Castlewood , and among they a dear friend of his grandfather , be most anxious that he should come to " Colonel Esmond 's house in England . " and now , accordingly , the lad make his appearance , pass under the old Gothic doorway , trip down the step from one garden terrace to another , hat in hand , his fair hair blow from his flushed cheek , his slim figure clothe in mourning . the handsome and modest look , the comely face and person , of the young lad please the lady . he make she a low bow which would have do credit to Versailles . she hold out a little hand to he , and , as his own palm close over it , she lay the other hand softly on his ruffle . she look very kindly and affectionately in the honest blushing face . 
 " I know your grandfather very well , Harry , " she say . " so you come yesterday to see his picture , and they turn you away , though you know the house be his of right ? " 
 Harry blush very red . " the servant do not know I . a young gentleman come to I last night , " he say , " when I be peevish , and he , I fear , be tipsy . I speak rudely to my cousin , and would ask his pardon . your ladyship know that in Virginia our manner towards stranger be different . I own I have expect another kind of welcome . be it you , madam , who send my cousin to I last night ? " 
 " I send he ; but you will find your cousin most friendly to you to - day . you must stay here . Lord Castlewood would have be with you this morning , only I be so eager to see you . there will be breakfast in an hour ; and meantime you must talk to I . we will send to the Three Castles for your servant and your baggage . give I your arm . stop , I drop my cane when you come . you shall be my cane . " 
 " my grandfather use to call we his crutch , " say Harry . 
 " you be like he , though you be fair . " 
 " you should have see you should have see George , " say the boy , and his honest eye well with tear . the recollection of his brother , the bitter pain of yesterday 's humiliation , the affectionateness of the present greeting all , perhaps , contribute to soften the lad 's heart . he feel very tenderly and gratefully towards the lady who have receive he so warmly . he be utterly alone and miserable a minute since , and here be a home and a kind hand hold out to he . no wonder he cling to it . in the hour during which they talk together , the young fellow have pour out a great deal of his honest heart to the kind new - find friend ; when the dial tell breakfast - time , he wonder to think how much he have tell she . she take he to the breakfast - room ; she present he to his aunt , the Countess , and bid he embrace his cousin . Lord Castlewood be frank and gracious enough . Honest will have a headache , but be utterly unconscious of the proceeding of the past night . the lady be very pleasant and polite , as lady of their fashion know how to be . how should Harry Warrington , a simple truth - tell lad from a distant colony , who have only yesterday put his foot upon English shore , know that my lady , so smile and easy in demeanour , be furious against he , and aghast at the favour with which Madam Bernstein seem to regard he ? 
 she be folle of he , talk of no one else , scarce notice the Castlewood young people , trot with he over the house , and tell he all its story , show he the little room in the courtyard where his grandfather use to sleep , and a cunning cupboard over the fireplace which have be make in the time of the catholic persecution ; drive out with he in the neighbouring country , and point out to he the most remarkable site and house , and have in return the whole of the young man 's story . 
 this brief biography the kind reader will please to accept , not in the precise word in which Mr. Harry Warrington deliver it to Madam Bernstein , but in the form in which it have be cast in the Chapters next ensue . 




 CHAPTER III . the Esmonds in Virginia 

 Henry Esmond , Esq . , an office who have serve with the rank of Colonel during the war of Queen Anne 's reign , find himself , at its close , compromise in certain attempt for the restoration of the Queen 's family to the throne of these realm . happily for itself , the nation prefer another dynasty ; but some of the few opponent of the house of Hanover take refuge out of the three kingdom , and amongst other , Colonel Esmond be counsel by his friend to go abroad . as Mr. Esmond sincerely regret the part which he have take , and as the august Prince who come to rule over England be the most pacable of sovereign , in a very little time the Colonel 's friend find mean to make his peace . 
 Mr. Esmond , it have be say , belong to the noble English family which take its title from Castlewood , in the county of hant ; and it be pretty generally know that King James II . and his son have offer the title of Marquis to Colonel Esmond and his father , and that the former might have assume the   irish   peerage hereditary in his family , but for an informality which he do not choose to set right . tired of the political struggle in which he have be engage , and annoy by family circumstance in Europe , he prefer to establish himself in Virginia , where he take possession of a large estate confer by King Charles I. upon his ancestor . here Mr. Esmond 's daughter and grandson be bear , and his wife die . this lady , when she marry he , be the widow of the Colonel 's kinsman , the unlucky Viscount Castlewood , kill in a duel by Lord Mohun , at the close of King William 's reign . 
 Mr. Esmond call his american house Castlewood , from the patrimonial home in the old country . the whole usage of Virginia , indeed , be fondly model after the english custom . it be a loyal colony . the Virginians boast that King Charles II . have be king in Virginia before he have be king in England . english king and english church be alike faithfully honour there . the resident gentry be ally to good english family . they hold their head above the dutch trader of New York , and the money - get roundhead of Pennsylvania and New England . never be people less republican than those of the great province which be soon to be foremost in the memorable revolt against the British Crown . 
 the gentry of Virginia dwelt on their great land after a fashion almost patriarchal . for its rough cultivation , each estate have a multitude of hand of purchase and assign servant who be subject to the command of the master . the land yield their food , live stock , and game . the great river swarm with fish for the taking . from their bank the passage home be clear . their ship take the tobacco off their private wharf on the bank of the Potomac or the James river , and carry it to London or Bristol , bring back english good and article of home manufacture in return for the only produce which the virginian gentry choose to cultivate . their hospitality be boundless . no stranger be ever send away from their gate . the gentry receive one another , and travel to each other 's house , in a state almost feudal . the question of Slavery be not bear at the time of which we write . to be the proprietor of black servant shock the feeling of no virginian gentleman ; nor , in truth , be the despotism exercise over the negro race generally a savage one . the food be plenty ; the poor black people lazy and not unhappy . you might have preach negro emancipation to Madam Esmond of Castlewood as you might have tell she to let the horse run loose out of her stable ; she have no doubt but that the whip and the corn - bag be good for both . 
 her father may have think otherwise , be of a sceptical turn on very many point , but his doubt do not break forth in active denial , and he be rather disaffect than rebellious . at one period , this gentleman have take a part in active life at home , and possibly might have be eager to share its reward ; but in latter day he do not seem to care for they . a something have occur in his life , which have cast a tinge of melancholy over all his existence . he be not unhappy to those about he most kind most affectionate , obsequious even to the woman of his family , whom be scarce ever contradict ; but there have be some bankruptcy of his heart , which his spirit never recover . he submit to life , rather than enjoy it , and never be in well spirit than in his last hour when he be go to lay it down . 
 have lose his wife , his daughter take the management of the Colonel and his affair ; and he give they up to her charge with an entire acquiescence . so that he have his book and his quiet , he care for no more . when company come to Castlewood , he entertain they handsomely , and be of a very pleasant , sarcastical turn . he be not in the least sorry when they go away . 
 " my love , I shall not be sorry to go myself , " he say to his daughter , " and you , though the most affectionate of daughter , will console yourself after a while . why should I , who be so old , be romantic ? you may , who be still a young creature . " this he say , not mean all he say , for the lady whom he address be a matter - of - fact little person , with very little romance in her nature . 
 after fifteen year ' residence upon his great virginian estate , affair prosper so well with the worthy proprietor , that he acquiesce in his daughter 's plan for the building of a mansion much grander and more durable than the plain wooden edifice in which he have be content to live , so that his heir might have a habitation worthy of their noble name . several of Madam Warrington 's neighbour have build handsome house for themselves ; perhaps it be her ambition to take rank in the country , which inspire this desire for improved quarter . Colonel Esmond , of Castlewood , neither care for quarter nor for quartering . but his daughter have a very high opinion of the merit and antiquity of her lineage ; and her sire , grow exquisitely calm and good - natured in his serene , decline year , humour his child 's peculiarity in an easy , banter way , nay , help she with his antiquarian learning , which be not inconsiderable , and with his skill in the art of painting , of which he be a proficient . a knowledge of heraldry , a hundred year ago , form part of the education of most noble lady and gentleman : during her visit to Europe , Miss Esmond have eagerly study the family history and pedigree , and return thence to Virginia with a store of document relative to her family on which she rely with implicit gravity and credence , and with the most edify volume then publish in France and England , respect the noble science . these work prove , to her perfect satisfaction , not only that the Esmonds be descend from noble Norman warrior , who come into England along with their victorious chief , but from native English of royal dignity : and two magnificent heraldic tree , cunningly paint by the hand of the Colonel , represent the family spring from the Emperor Charlemagne on the one hand , who be draw in plate - armour , with his imperial mantle and diadem , and on the other from Queen Boadicea , whom the Colonel insist upon paint in the light costume of an ancient british queen , with a prodigious gild crown , a trifling mantle of fur , and a lovely symmetrical person , tastefully tattooed with figure of a brilliant blue tint . from these two illustrious stock the family - tree rise until it unite in the thirteenth century somewhere in the person of the fortunate Esmond who claim to spring from both . 
 of the Warrington family , into which she marry , good Madam Rachel think but little . she write herself Esmond Warrington , but be universally call Madam Esmond of Castlewood , when after her father 's decease she come to rule over that domain . it be even to be fear that quarrel for precedence in the colonial society occasionally disturb her temper ; for though her father have have a marquis 's patent from King James , which he have burn and disown , she would frequently act as if that document exist and be in full force . she consider the English Esmonds of an inferior dignity to her own branch ; and as for the colonial aristocracy , she make no scruple of assert her superiority over the whole body of they . hence quarrel and angry word , and even a scuffle or two , as we gather from her note , at the Governor 's assembly at Jamestown . wherefore recall the memory of these squabble ? be not the person who engage in they beyond the reach of quarrel now , and have not the republic put an end to these social inequality ? Ere the establishment of Independence , there be no more aristocratic country in the world than Virginia ; so the Virginians , whose history we have to narrate , be breed to have the full respect for the institution of home , and the rightful king have not two more faithful little subject than the young twin of Castlewood . 
 when the boy ' grandfather die , their mother , in great state , proclaim her eld son George her successor and heir of the estate ; and Harry , George 's young brother by half an hour , be always enjoin to respect his senior . all the household be equally instruct to pay he honour ; the negro , of whom there be a large and happy family , and the assign servant from Europe , whose lot be make as bearable as it might be under the government of the lady of Castlewood . in the whole family there scarcely be a rebel save Mrs. Esmond 's faithful friend and companion , Madam Mountain , and Harry 's foster - mother , a faithful negro woman , who never could be make to understand why her child should not be first , who be handsomer , and strong , and cleverer than his brother , as she vow ; though , in truth , there be scarcely any difference in the beauty , strength , or stature of the twin . in disposition , they be in many point exceedingly unlike ; but in feature they resemble each other so closely , that but for the colour of their hair it have be difficult to distinguish they . in their bed , and when their head be cover with those vast ribboned nightcap which our great and little ancestor wear , it be scarcely possible for any but a nurse or mother to tell the one from the other child . 
 Howbeit alike in form , we have say that they differ in temper . the elder be peaceful , studious , and silent ; the young be warlike and noisy . he be quick at learn when he begin , but very slow at begin . no threat of the ferule would provoke Harry to learn in an idle fit , or would prevent George from help his brother in his lesson . Harry be of a strong military turn , drill the little negro on the estate and can they like a corporal , have many good boxing - match with they , and never bear malice if he be worst ; whereas George be spar of blow and gentle with all about he . as the custom in all family be , each of the boy have a special little servant assign he ; and it be a known fact that George , find his little wretch of a blackamoor asleep on his master 's bed , sit down beside it and brush the fly off the child with a feather fan , to the horror of old Gumbo , the child 's father , who find his young master so engage , and to the indignation of Madam Esmond , who order the young negro off to the proper officer for a whipping . in vain George implored and entreat burst into passionate tear , and besought a remission of the sentence . his mother be inflexible regard the young rebel 's punishment , and the little negro go off beseech his young master not to cry . 
 a fierce quarrel between mother and son ensue out of this event . her son would not be pacify . he say the punishment be a shame a shame ; that he be the master of the boy , and no one no , not his mother , have a right to touch he ; that she might order he to be correct , and that he would suffer the punishment , as he and Harry often have , but no one should lay a hand on his boy . tremble with passionate rebellion against what he conceive the injustice of procedure , he vow actually shriek out an oath , which shock his fond mother and governor , who never before hear such language from the usually gentle child that on the day he come of age he would set young Gumbo free go to visit the child in the slave ' quarter , and give he one of his own toy . 
 the young black martyr be an impudent , lazy , saucy little personage , who would be none the bad for a whipping , as the Colonel no doubt think ; for he acquiesce in the child 's punishment when Madam Esmond insist upon it , and only laugh in his good - natured way when his indignant grandson call out , 
 " you let mamma rule you in everything , grandpapa . " 
 " why , so I do , " say grandpapa . " Rachel , my love , the way in which I be petticoat - ride be so evident that even this baby have find it out . " 
 " then why do not   you stand up like a man ? " say little Harry ' , who always be ready to abet his brother . 
 Grandpapa look queerly . 
 " because I like sit down well , my dear , " he say . " I be an old gentleman , and stand fatigue I . " 
 on account of a certain apish drollery and humour which exhibit itself in the lad , and a liking for some of the old man 's pursuit , the first of the twin be the grandfather 's favourite and companion , and would laugh and talk out all his infantine heart to the old gentleman , to whom the young have seldom a word to say . George be a demure studious boy , and his sens seem to brighten up in the library , where his brother be so gloomy . he know the book before he could well - nigh carry they , and read in they long before he could understand they . Harry , on the other hand , be all alive in the stable or in the wood , eager for all party of hunting and fishing , and promise to be a good sportsman from a very early age . their grandfather 's ship be sail for Europe once when the boy be child , and they be ask , what present Captain Franks should bring they back ? George be divide between book and a fiddle ; Harry instantly declare for a little gun : and Madam Warrington   as she then be call   be hurt that her eld boy should have low taste , and applaud the young 's choice as more worthy of his name and lineage . " book , papa , I can fancy to be a good choice , " she reply to her father , who try to convince she that George have a right to his opinion , " though I be sure you must have pretty nigh all the book in the world already . but I never can desire I may be wrong , but I never can desire that my son , and the grandson of the Marquis of Esmond , should be a fiddler . " 
 " should be a fiddlestick , my dear , " the old Colonel answer . 
 " remember that Heaven 's way be not ours , and that each creature bear have a little kingdom of thought of his own , which it be a sin in we to invade . suppose George love music ? you can no more stop he than you can order a rise not to smell sweet , or a bird not to sing . " 
 " a bird ! a bird sing from nature ; George do not come into the world with a fiddle in his hand , " say Mrs. Warrington , with a toss of her head . " I be sure I hat the harpsichord when a chit at Kensington School , and only learn it to please my mamma . say what you will , dear sir , I can not believe that this fiddling be work for person of fashion . " 
 " and King David who play the harp , my dear ? " 
 " I wish my papa would read he more , and not speak about he in that way , " say Mrs. Warrington . 
 " Nay , my dear , it be but by way of illustration , " the father reply gently . it be Colonel Esmond 's nature , as he have own in his own biography , always to be lead by a woman ; and , his wife dead , he coax and dandle and spoil his daughter ; laugh at her caprice , but humour they ; make a joke of her prejudice , but let they have their way ; indulge , and perhaps increase , her natural imperiousness of character , though it be his maxim that we can not   change disposition by meddling , and only make hypocrite of our child by command they over - much . 
 at length the time come when Mr. Esmond be to have do with the affair of this life , and he lay they down as if glad to be rid of their burthen . we must not ring in an opening history with tolling bell , or preface it with a funeral sermon . all who read and hear that discourse , wonder where Parson Broadbent of Jamestown find the eloquence and the Latin which adorn it . perhaps Mr. Dempster know , the boy ' Scotch tutor , who correct the proof of the oration , which be print , by desire of his Excellency and many person of honour , at Mr. Franklin 's press in Philadelphia . no such sumptuous funeral have ever be see in the country as that which Madam Esmond Warrington ordain for her father , who would have be the first to smile at that pompous grief . the little lad of Castlewood , almost smother in black train and hatband , head the procession , and be follow by my Lord Fairfax from Greenway Court , by his Excellency the Governor of Virginia   with his coach , by the Randolphs , the Careys , the Harrisons , the Washingtons , and many other , for the whole county esteem the depart gentleman , whose goodness , whose high talent , whose benevolence and unobtrusive urbanity have earn for he the just respect of his neighbour . when inform of the event , the family of Colonel Esmond 's stepson , the Lord Castlewood of Hampshire in England , ask to be at the charge of the marble slab which record the name and virtue of his lordship 's mother and her husband ; and after due time of preparation , the monument be set up , exhibit the arm and coronet of the Esmonds , support by a little chubby group of weeping cherub , and recite an epitaph which for once do not tell any falsehood . 




 CHAPTER IV . in which Harry find a New Relative 

 kind friend , neighbour hospitable , cordial , even respectful , an ancient name , a large estate and a sufficient fortune , a comfortable home , supply with all the necessary and many of the luxury of life , and a troop of servant , black and white , eager to do your bidding ; good health , affectionate child , and , let we humbly add , a good cook , cellar , and library ought not a person in the possession of all these benefit to be consider very decently happy ? Madam Esmond Warrington possess all these cause for happiness ; she remind herself of they daily in her morning and evening prayer . she be scrupulous in her devotion , good to the poor , never knowingly do anybody a wrong . yonder I fancy she enthrone in her principality of Castlewood , the country gentlefolk pay her court , the son dutiful to she , the domestic tumble over each other 's black heel to do her bidding , the poor white grateful for her bounty and implicitly take her dos when they be ill , the small gentry always acquiesce in her remark , and for ever let her win at backgammon well , with all these benefit , which be more sure than fate allot to most mortal , I do not   think the little Princess Pocahontas , as she be call , be to be envy in the midst of her dominion . the Princess 's husband , who be cut off in early life , be as well perhaps out of the way . have he survive his marriage by many year , they would have quarrel fiercely , or , he would infallibly have be a henpecke husband , of which sort there be a few specimen still extant a hundred year ago . the truth be , little Madam Esmond never come near man or woman , but she try to domineer over they . if people obey , she be their very good friend ; if they resist , she fight and fight until she or they give in . we be all miserable sinner that be a fact we acknowledge in public every Sunday no one announce it in a more clear resolute voice than the little lady . as a mortal , she may have be in the wrong , of course ; only she very seldom acknowledge the circumstance to herself , and to other never . her father , in his old age , use to watch her freak of despotism , haughtiness , and stubbornness , and amuse himself with they . she feel that his eye be upon she ; his humour , of which quality she possess little herself , subdue and bewilder she . but , the Colonel go , there be nobody else whom she be dispose to obey , and so I be rather glad for my part that I do not live a hundred year ago at Castlewood in Westmorland County in Virginia . I fancy , one would not have be too happy there . happy , who be happy ? be not there a serpent in Paradise itself ? and if Eve have be perfectly happy beforehand , would she have listen to he ? 
 the management of the house of Castlewood have be in the hand of the active little lady long before the Colonel sleep the sleep of the just . she now exercise a rigid supervision over the estate ; dismiss Colonel Esmond 's english factor and employ a new one ; build , improve , plant , grow tobacco , appoint a new overseer , and import a new tutor . much as she love her father , there be some of his maxim by which she be not inclined to abide . have she not obey her papa and mamma during all their life , as a dutiful daughter should ? so ought all child to obey their parent , that their day might be long in the land . the little queen domineer over her little dominion , and the Princes her son be only her first subject . Ere long she discontinue her husband 's name of Warrington and go by the name of Madam Esmond in the country . her family pretension be know there . she have no objection to talk of the Marquis 's title which King James have give to her father and grandfather . her papa 's enormous magnanimity might induce he to give up his title and rank to the young branch of the family , and to her half - brother , my Lord Castlewood and his child ; but she and her son be of the eld branch of the Esmonds , and she expect that they should be treat accordingly . Lord Fairfax be the only gentleman in the colony of Virginia to whom she would allow precedence over she . she insist on the pas before all Lieutenant - Governors ' and Judges ' lady ; before the wife of the Governor of a colony she would , of course , yield as to the representative of the Sovereign . account be extant , in the family paper and letter , of one or two tremendous battle which Madam fight with the wife of colonial dignitary upon these question of etiquette . as for her husband 's family of Warrington , they be as naught in her eye . she marry an English baronet 's young son out of Norfolk to please her parent , whom she be always bind to obey . at the early age at which she marry a chit out of a boarding - school she would have jump overboard if her papa have order . " and that be always the way with the Esmonds , " she say . 
 the English Warringtons be not over - much flatter by the little American Princess 's behaviour to they , and her manner of speak about they . once a year a solemn letter use to be address to the Warrington family , and to her noble kinsman the Hampshire Esmonds ; but a Judge 's lady with whom Madam Esmond have quarrel return to England out of Virginia chance to meet Lady Warrington , who be in London with Sir Miles attend Parliament , and this person repeat some of the speech which the Princess Pocahontas be in the habit of make regard her own and her husband 's english relative , and my Lady Warrington , I suppose , carry the story to my Lady Castlewood ; after which the letter from Virginia be not answer , to the surprise and wrath of Madam Esmond , who speedily leave off write also . 
 so this good woman fall out with her neighbour , with her relative , and , as it must be own , with her son also . 
 a very early difference which occur between the Queen and Crown Prince arise out of the dismissal of Mr. Dempster , the lad 's tutor and the late Colonel 's secretary . in her father 's life Madam Esmond bear he with difficulty , or it should be rather say Mr. Dempster could scarce put up with she . she be jealous of book somehow , and think your bookworm dangerous folk , insinuate bad principle . she have hear that Dempster be a Jesuit in disguise , and the poor fellow be oblige to go build himself a cabin in a clearing , and teach school and practise medicine where he could find customer among the sparse inhabitant of the province . Master George vow he never would forsake his old tutor , and keep his promise . Harry have always love fishing and sporting well than book , and he and the poor Dominie have never be on term of close intimacy . another cause of dispute presently ensue . 
 by the death of an aunt , and at his father 's demise , the heir of Mr. George Warrington become entitled to a sum of six thousand pound , of which their mother be one of the trustee . she never could be make to understand that she be not the proprietor , and not merely the trustee of this money ; and be furious with the London lawyer , the other trustee , who refuse to send it over at her order . " be not all I have my son ' ? " she cry , " and would I not cut myself into little piece to serve they ? with the six thousand pound I would have buy Mr. Boulter 's estate and negro , which would have give we a good thousand pound a year , and make a handsome provision for my Harry . " her young friend and neighbour , Mr. Washington of Mount Vernon , could not convince she that the London agent be right , and must not give up his trust except to those for whom he hold it . Madam Esmond give the London lawyer a piece of her mind , and , I be sorry to say , inform Mr. Draper that he be an insolent pettifogger , and deserve to be punish for doubt the honour of a mother and an Esmond . it must be own that the Virginian Princess have a temper of her own . 
 George Esmond , her firstborn , when this little matter be refer to he , and his mother vehemently insist that he should declare himself , be of the opinion of Mr. Washington , and Mr. Draper , the London lawyer . the boy say he could not help himself . he do not want the money : he would be very glad to think otherwise , and to give the money to his mother , if he have the power . but Madam Esmond would not hear any of these reason . feeling be her reason . here be a chance of make Harry 's fortune dear Harry , who be leave with such a slender young brother 's ; pittance and the wretch in London would not help he ; his own brother , who inherit all her papa 's estate , would not help he . to think of a child of hers be so mean at fourteen year of age ! etc . etc . add tear , scorn , frequent innuendo , long estrangement , bitter outbreak , passionate appeal to Heaven , and the like , and we may fancy the widow 's state of mind . be there not beloved being of the gentler sex who argue in the same way nowadays ? the book of female logic be blot all over with tear , and Justice in their court be for ever in a passion . 
 this occurrence set the widow resolutely save for her young son , for whom , as in duty bind , she be eager to make a portion . the fine building be stop which the Colonel have commence at Castlewood , who have freight ship from New York with dutch brick , and import , at great charge , mantelpiece , carve cornice - work , sash and glass , carpet and costly upholstery from home . no more book be buy . the agent have order to discontinue send wine . Madam Esmond deeply regret the expense of a fine carriage which she have have from England , and only ride in it to church groaning in spirit , and cry to the son opposite she , " Harry , Harry ! I wish I have put by the money for thee , my poor portionless child three hundred and eighty guineas of ready money to Messieurs Hatchett ! " 
 " you will give I plenty while you live , and George will give I plenty when you die , " say Harry , gaily . 
 " not unless he change in spirit , my dear , " say the lady , with a grim glance at her eld boy . " not unless Heaven soften his heart and teach he charity , for which I pray day and night ; as Mountain know ; do you not , Mountain ? " 
 Mrs. Mountain , Ensign Mountain 's widow , Madam Esmond 's companion and manager , who take the fourth seat in the family coach on these Sundays , say , " Humph ! I know you be always disturb yourself and cry out about this legacy , and I do not   see that there be any need . " 
 " oh no ! no need ! " cry the widow , rustle in her silk ; " of course I have no need to be disturb , because my eld bear be a disobedient son and an unkind brother because he have an estate , and my poor Harry , bless he , but a mess of pottage . " 
 George look despairingly at his mother until he could see she no more for eye well up with tear . " I wish you would bless I , too , o my mother ! " he say , and burst into a passionate fit of weeping . Harry 's arm be in a moment round his brother 's neck , and he kiss George a score of time . 
 " never mind , George . I know whether you be a good brother or not . do not   mind what she say . she do not   mean it . " 
 " I do mean it , child , " cry the mother . " would to Heaven " 
 " hold your TONGUE , I SAY " roar out Harry . " it be a shame to speak so to he , ma'am . " 
 " and so it be , Harry , " say Mrs. Mountain , shake his hand . " you never say a truer word in your life . " 
 " Mrs. Mountain , do you dare to set my child against I ? " cry the widow . " from this very day , madam " 
 " turn I and my child into the street ? do , " say Mrs. Mountain . " that will be a fine revenge because the english lawyer will not   give you the boy 's money . find another companion who will tell you black be white , and flatter you : it be not my way , madam . when shall I go ? I shall not   be long a - packing . I do not bring much into Castlewood House , and I shall not take much out . " 
 " Hush ! the bell be ring for church , Mountain . let we try , if you please , and compose ourselves , " say the widow , and she look with eye of extreme affection , certainly at one perhaps at both of her child . George keep his head down , and Harry , who be near , get quite close to he during the sermon , and sit with his arm round his brother 's neck . 
 Harry have proceed in his narrative after his own fashion , intersperse it with many youthful ejaculation , and answer a number of incidental question ask by his listener . the old lady seem never tired of hear he . her amiable hostess and her daughter come more than once , to ask if she would ride , or walk , or take a dish of tea , or play a game at card ; but all these amusement Madam Bernstein decline , say that she find infinite amusement in Harry 's conversation . especially when any of the Castlewood family be present , she redouble her caress , insist upon the lad speak close to her ear , and would call out to the other , " Hush , my dear !
