I can not   hear our cousin speak . " and they would quit the room , strive still to look pleased . 
 " be you my cousin , too ? " ask the honest boy . " you see kind than my other cousin . " 
 their talk take place in the wainscote parlour , where the family have take their meal in ordinary for at least two century past , and which , as we have say , be hang with portrait of the race . over Madam Bernstein 's great chair be a Kneller , one of the most brilliant picture of the gallery , represent a young lady of three or four and twenty , in the easy flow dress and loose robe of Queen Anne 's time a hand on a cushion near she , a quantity of auburn hair part off a fair forehead , and flow over pearly shoulder and a lovely neck . under this sprightly picture the lady sate with her knitting - needles . 
 when Harry ask , " be you my cousin , too ? " she say , " that picture be by Sir Godfrey , who think himself the great painter in the world . but he be not so good as Lely , who paint your grandmother my my Lady Castlewood , Colonel Esmond 's wife ; nor he so good as Sir Anthony Van Dyck , who paint your great - grandfather , yonder and who look , Harry , a much fine gentleman than he be . some of we be paint blacker than we be . do you recognise your grandmother in that picture ? she have the lovely fair hair and shape of any woman of her time . " 
 " I fancy I know the portrait from instinct , perhaps , and a certain likeness to my mother . " 
 " do Mrs. Warrington I beg her pardon , I think she call herself Madam or my Lady Esmond now ? " 
 " they call my mother so in our province , " say the boy . 
 " do she never tell you of another daughter her mother have in England , before she marry your grandfather ? " 
 " she never speak of one . " 
 " nor your grandfather ? " 
 " never . but in his picture - book , which he constantly make for us child , he use to draw a head very like that above your ladyship . that , and Viscount Francis , and King James III . , he draw a score of time , I be sure . " 
 " and the picture over I remind you of no one , Harry ? " 
 " no , indeed . " 
 " ah ! here be a sermon ! " say the lady , with a sigh . " Harry , that be my face once yes , it be and then I be call Beatrix Esmond . and your mother be my half - sister , child , and she have never even mention my name ! " 




 CHAPTER V. Family Jars 

 as Harry Warrington relate to his new - find relative the simple story of his adventure at home , no doubt Madam Bernstein , who possess a great sense of humour and a remarkable knowledge of the world , form her judgment respect the person and event describe ; and if her opinion be not in all respect favourable , what can be say but that man and woman be imperfect , and human life not entirely pleasant or profitable ? the court and city - breed lady recoil at the mere thought of her american sister 's countrified existence . such a life would be rather wearisome to most city - breed lady . but little Madam Warrington know no well , and be satisfied with her life , as indeed she be with herself in general . because you and I be epicure or dainty feeder , it do not follow that Hodge be miserable with his homely meal of bread and bacon . Madam Warrington have a life of duty and employment which might be humdrum , but at any rate be pleasant to she . she be a brisk little woman of business , and all the affair of her large estate come under her cognisance . no pie be bake at Castlewood but her little finger be in it . she set the maid to their spinning , she see the kitchen wench at their work , she trot afield on her pony , and oversee the overseer and the negro hand as they work in the tobacco - and corn - field . if a slave be ill , she would go to his quarter in any weather , and doctor he with great resolution . she have a book full of receipt after the old fashion , and a closet where she distil water and compound elixir , and a medicine - chest which be the terror of her neighbour . they tremble to be ill , lest the little lady should be upon they with her decoction and her pill . 
 a hundred year back there be scarce any town in Virginia ; the establishment of the gentry be little village in which they and their vassal dwelt . Rachel Esmond rule like a little queen in Castlewood ; the prince , her neighbour , govern their estate round about . many of these be rather needy potentate , live plentifully but in the roughest fashion , have numerous domestic whose livery be often rag ; keep open house , and turn away no stranger from their gate ; proud , idle , fond of all sort of field sport as become gentleman of good lineage . the widow of Castlewood be as hospitable as her neighbour , and a well economist than most of they . More than one , no doubt , would have have no objection to share her life - interest in the estate , and supply the place of papa to her boy . but where be the man good enough for a person of her ladyship 's exalted birth ? there be a talk of make the Duke of Cumberland viceroy , or even king , over America . Madam Warrington 's gossip laugh , and say she be wait for he . she remark , with much gravity and dignity , that person of as high birth as his Royal Highness have make offer of alliance to the Esmond family . 
 she have , as lieutenant under she , an officer 's widow who have be before name , and who have be Madam Esmond 's companion at school , as her late husband have be the regimental friend of the late Mr. Warrington . when the english girl at the Kensington Academy , where Rachel Esmond have her education , tease and torture the little american stranger , and laugh at the princified air which she give herself from a very early age , Fanny Parker defend and befriend she . they both marry ensign in Kingsley 's . they become tenderly attach to each other . it be " my fanny " and " my Rachel " in the letter of the young lady . then , my Fanny 's husband die in sad out - at - elbow circumstance , leave no provision for his widow and her infant ; and , in one of his annual voyage , Captain Franks bring over Mrs. Mountain , in the Young Rachel , to Virginia . 
 there be plenty of room in Castlewood House , and Mrs. Mountain serve to enliven the place . she play card with the mistress : she have some knowledge of music , and could help the eld boy in that way : she laugh and be pleased with the guest : she see to the stranger ' chamber , and preside over the press and the linen . she be a kind , brisk , jolly - look widow , and more than one unmarried gentleman of the colony ask she to change her name for his own . but she choose to keep that of Mountain , though , and perhaps because , it have bring she no good fortune . one marriage be enough for she , she say . Mr. Mountain have amiably spend her little fortune and his own . her last trinket go to pay his funeral ; and , as long as Madam Warrington would keep she at Castlewood , she prefer a home without a husband to any which as yet have be offer to she in Virginia . the two lady quarrel plentifully ; but they love each other : they make up their difference : they fall out again , to be reconcile presently . when either of the boy be ill , each lady vie with the other in maternal tenderness and care . in his last day and illness , Mrs. Mountain 's cheerfulness and kindness have be greatly appreciate by the Colonel , whose memory Madam Warrington regard more than that of any live person . so that , year after year , when Captain Franks would ask Mrs. Mountain , in his pleasant way , whether she be go back with he that voyage ? she would decline , and say that she propose to stay a year more . 
 and when suitor come to Madam Warrington , as come they would , she would receive their compliment and attention kindly enough , and ask more than one of these lover whether it be Mrs. Mountain he come after ? she would use her good office with Mountain . Fanny be the good creature , be of a good english family , and would make any gentleman happy . do the Squire declare it be to she and not she dependant that he pay his address ; she would make he her grave curtsey , say that she really have be utterly mistake as to his view , and let he know that the daughter of the Marquis of Esmond live for her people and her son , and do not propose to change her condition . have we not read how Queen Elizabeth be a perfectly sensible woman of business , and be pleased to inspire not only terror and awe , but love in the bosom of her subject ? so the little virginian princess have her favourite , and accept their flattery , and grow tired of they , and be cruel or kind to they as suit her wayward imperial humour . there be no amount of compliment which she would not graciously receive and take as her due . her little foible be so well know that the wag use to practise upon it . rattle Jack Firebrace of Henrico county have free quarter for month at Castlewood , and be a prime favourite with the lady there , because he address verse to she which he steal out of the pocket - book . Tom Humbold of Spotsylvania wager fifty hogshead against five that he would make her institute an order of knighthood , and win his wager . 
 the eld boy see these freak and oddity of his good mother 's disposition , and chafe and rag at they privately . from very early day he revolt when flattery and compliment be pay to the little lady , and strove to expose they with his juvenile satire ; so that his mother would say gravely , " the Esmonds be always of a jealous disposition , and my poor boy take after my father and mother in this . " George hat Jack Firebrace and Tom Humbold , and all their like ; whereas Harry go out sport with they , and fowling , and fishing , and cock - fighting , and enjoy all the fun of the country . 
 one winter , after their first tutor have be dismiss , Madam Esmond take they to Williamsburg , for such education as the school and college there afford , and there it be the fortune of the family to listen to the preaching of the famous Mr. Whitfield , who have come into Virginia , where the habit and preaching of the establish clergy be not very edifying . unlike many of the neighbouring province , Virginia be a Church of England colony : the clergyman be pay by the State and have glebe allot to they ; and , there be no Church of England bishop as yet in America , the colonist be oblige to import their divine from the mother - country . such as come be not , naturally , of the very good or most eloquent kind of pastor . nobleman 's hanger - on , insolvent parson who have quarrel with justice or the bailiff , bring their stain cassock into the colony in the hope of find a living there . no wonder that Whitfield 's great voice stir those whom harmless Mr. Broadbent , the Williamsburg chaplain , never could awaken . at first the boy be as much excited as their mother by Mr. Whitfield : they sing hymn , and listen to he with fervour , and , could he have remain long enough among they , Harry and George have both wear black coat probably instead of epaulette . the simple boy communicate their experience to one another , and be on the daily and nightly look - out for the sacred " call , " in the hope or the possession of which such a vast multitude of Protestant England be thrill at the time . 
 but Mr. Whitfield could not stay always with the little congregation of Williamsburg . his mission be to enlighten the whole benighted people of the Church , and from the East to the West to trumpet the truth and bid slumber sinner awaken . however , he comfort the widow with precious letter , and promise to send she a tutor for her son who should be capable of teach they not only profane learning , but of strengthen and confirm they in science much more precious . 
 in due course , a choose vessel arrive from England . young Mr. Ward have a voice as loud as Mr. Whitfield 's , and could talk almost as readily and for as long a time . night and evening the hall sound with his exhortation . the domestic negro creep to the door to listen to he . other servant darken the porch window with their crisp head to hear he discourse . it be over the black sheep of the Castlewood flock that Mr. Ward somehow have the most influence . these woolly lambling be immensely affect by his exhortation , and , when he give out the hymn , there be such a negro chorus about the house as might be hear across the Potomac such a chorus as would never have be hear in the Colonel 's time for that worthy gentleman have a suspicion of all cassock , and say he would never have any controversy with a clergyman but upon backgammon . where money be want for charitable purpose no man be more ready , and the good , easy virginian clergyman , who love backgammon heartily , too , say that the worthy Colonel 's charity must cover his other shortcoming . 
 Ward be a handsome young man . his preaching please Madam Esmond from the first , and , I daresay , satisfy she as much as Mr. Whitfield 's . of course it can not be the case at the present day when they be so finely educated , but woman , a hundred year ago , be credulous , eager to admire and believe , and apt to imagine all sort of excellence in the object of their admiration . for week , nay , month , Madam Esmond be never tired of hear Mr. Ward 's great glib voice and voluble commonplace : and , accord to she will not , she insist that her neighbour should come and listen to he , and order they to be convert . her young favourite , Mr. Washington , she be especially anxious to influence ; and again and again press he to come and stay at Castlewood and benefit by the spiritual advantage there to be obtain . but that young gentleman find he have particular business which call he home or away from home , and always order his horse of evening when the time be come for Mr. Ward 's exercise . and what boy be just towards their pedagogue ? the twin grow speedily tired and even rebellious under their new teacher . 
 they find he a bad scholar , a dull fellow , and ill - breed to boot . George know much more latin and Greek than his master , and catch he in perpetual blunder and false quantity . Harry , who could take much great liberty than be allow to his eld brother , mimicked Ward 's manner of eat and talk , so that Mrs. Mountain and even Madam Esmond be force to laugh , and little Fanny Mountain would crow with delight . Madam Esmond would have find the fellow out for a vulgar quack but for her son ' opposition , which she , on her part , oppose with her own indomitable will . " what matter whether he have more or less of profane learning ? " she ask ; " in that which be most precious , Mr. W. be able to be a teacher to all of we . what if his manner be a little rough ? Heaven do not choose its elect from among the great and wealthy . I wish you know one book , child , as well as Mr. Ward do . it be your wicked pride the pride of all the Esmonds which prevent you from listen to he . go down on your knee in your chamber and pray to be correct of that dreadful fault . " Ward 's discourse that evening be about Naaman the Syrian , and the pride he have in his native river of Abana and Pharpar , which he vainly imagine to be superior to the healing water of Jordan the moral being , that he , Ward , be the keeper and guardian of the undoubted water of Jordan , and that the unhappy , conceited boy must go to perdition unless they come to he . 
 George now begin to give way to a wicked sarcastic method , which , perhaps , he have inherit from his grandfather , and with which , when a quiet , skilful young person choose to employ it , he can make a whole family uncomfortable . he take up Ward 's pompous remark and make joke of they , so that that young divine chafe and almost choke over his great meal . he make Madam Esmond angry , and doubly so when he send off Harry into fit of laughter . her authority be defy , her officer scorn and insult , her young child pervert , by the obstinate eld brother . she make a desperate and unhappy attempt to maintain her power . 
 the boy be fourteen year of age , Harry be tall and much more advanced than his brother , who be delicate , and as yet almost childlike in stature and appearance . the baculine method be a quite common mode of argument in those day . sergeant , schoolmaster , slave - overseer , use the cane freely . our little boy have be horse many a day by Mr. Dempster , their Scotch tutor , in their grandfather 's time ; and Harry , especially , have get to be quite accustomed to the practice , and make very light of it . but , in the interregnum after Colonel Esmond 's death , the cane have be lay aside , and the young gentleman of Castlewood have be allow to have their own way . her own and her lieutenant 's authority be now spurn by the youthful rebel , the unfortunate mother think of restore it by mean of coercion . she take counsel of Mr. Ward . that athletic young pedagogue could easily find chapter and verse to warrant the course which he wish to pursue in fact , there be no doubt about the wholesomeness of the practice in those clay . he have begin by flatter the boy , find a good berth and snug quarter at Castlewood , and hop to remain there . 
 but they laugh at his flattery , they scorn his bad manner , they yawn soon at his sermon ; the more their mother favour he , the more they dislike he ; and so the tutor and the pupil cordially hat each other . Mrs. Mountain , who be the boy ' friend , especially George 's friend , whom she think unjustly treat by his mother , warn the lad to be prudent , and that some conspiracy be hatch against they . " Ward be more obsequious than ever to your mamma . it turn my stomach , it do , to hear he flat , and to see he gobble the odious wretch ! you must be on your guard , my poor boy you must learn your lesson , and not anger your tutor . a mischief will come , I know it will . your mamma be talk about you to Mr. Washington the other day , when I come into the room . I do not   like that Major Washington , you know I do not . do not   say o Mounty ! Master Harry . you always stand up for your friend , you do . the Major be very handsome and tall , and he may be very good , but he be much too old a young man for I . bless you , my dear , the quantity of wild oats your father sow and my own poor Mountain when they be ensign in Kingsley 's , would fill sack full ! show I Mr. Washington 's wild oat , I say not a grain ! well , I happen to step in last Tuesday , when he be here with your mamma ; and I be sure they be talk about you , for he say , ' Discipline be discipline , and must be preserve . there can be but one command in a house , ma'am , and you must be the mistress of your . ' " 
 " the very word he use to I , " cry Harry . " he tell I that he do not like to meddle with other folk ' affair , but that our mother be very angry , dangerously angry , he say , and he beg I to obey Mr. Ward , and specially to press George to do so . " 
 " let he manage his own house , not mine , " say George , very haughtily . and the caution , far from benefit he , only render the lad more supercilious and refractory . 
 on the next day the storm break , and vengeance fall on the little rebel 's head . word pass between George and Mr. Ward during the morning study . the boy be quite insubordinate and unjust : even his faithful brother cry out , and own that he be in the wrong . Mr. Ward keep his temper to compress , bottle up , cork down , and prevent your anger from present furious explosion , be call keep your temper and say he should speak upon this business to Madam Esmond . when the family meet at dinner , Mr. Ward request her ladyship to stay , and , temperately enough , lay the subject of dispute before she . 
 he ask Master Harry to confirm what he have say : and poor Harry be oblige to admit all the dominie 's statement . 
 George , stand under his grandfather 's portrait by the chimney , say haughtily that what Mr. Ward have say be perfectly correct . 
 " to be a tutor to such a pupil be absurd , " say Mr. Ward , make a long speech , intersperse with many of his usual scripture phrase , at each of which , as they occur , that wicked young George smile , and pishe scornfully , and at length Ward end by ask her honour 's leave to retire . 
 " not before you have punish this wicked and disobedient child , " say Madam Esmond , who have be gather anger during Ward 's harangue , and especially at her son 's behaviour . 
 " punish ! " say George . 
 " yes , sir , punish ! if mean of love and entreaty fail , as they have with your proud heart , other mean must be find to bring you to obedience . I punish you now , rebellious boy , to guard you from great punishment hereafter . the discipline of this family must be maintain . there can be but one command in a house , and I must be the mistress of mine . you will punish this refractory boy , Mr. Ward , as we have agree that you should do , and if there be the least resistance on his part , my overseer and servant will lend you aid . " 
 in some such word the widow no doubt must have speak , but with many vehement Scriptural allusion , which it do not become this chronicler to copy . to be for ever apply to the Sacred Oracles , and accommodate their sentence to your purpose to be for ever take Heaven into your confidence about your private affair , and passionately call for its interference in your family quarrel and difficulty to be so familiar with its design and scheme as to be able to threaten your neighbour with its thunder , and to know precisely its intention regard he and other who differ from your infallible opinion this be the schooling which our simple widow have receive from her impetuous young spiritual guide , and I doubt whether it bring she much comfort . 
 in the midst of his mother 's harangue , in spite of it , perhaps , George Esmond feel he have be wrong . " there can be but one command in the house , and you must be mistress I know who say those word before you , " George say , slowly , and look very white " and and I know , mother , that I have act wrongly to Mr. Ward . " 
 " he own it ! he ask pardon ! " cry Harry . " that be right , George ! that be enough : be not   it ? " 
 " no , it be not enough ! " cry the little woman . " the disobedient boy must pay the penalty of his disobedience . when I be headstrong , as I sometimes be as a child before my spirit be change and humble , my mamma punish I , and I submit . so must George . I desire you will do your duty , Mr. Ward . " 
 " stop , mother ! you do not   quite know what you be do , " George say , exceedingly agitated . 
 " I know that he who spar the rod spoil the child , ungrateful boy ! " say Madam Esmond , with more reference of the same nature , which George hear , look very pale and desperate . 
 upon the mantelpiece , under the Colonel 's portrait , stand a china cup , by which the widow set great store , as her father have always be accustom to drink from it . George suddenly take it , and a strange smile pass over his pale face . 
 " stay one minute . do not   go away yet , " he cry to his mother , who be leave the room . " you you be very fond of this cup , mother ? " and Harry look at he , wonder . " if I break it , it could never be mend , could it ? all the tinker ' rivet would not make it a whole cup again . my dear old grandpapa 's cup ! I have be wrong . Mr. Ward , I ask pardon . I will try and amend . " 
 the widow look at her son indignantly , almost scornfully . " I think , " she say , " I think an Esmond have be more of a man than to be afraid , and " here she give a little scream as Harry utter an exclamation , and dash forward with his hand stretch out towards his brother . 
 George , after look at the cup , raise it , open his hand , and let it fall on the marble slab below he . Harry have try in vain to catch it . 
 " it be too late , Hal , " George say . " you will never mend that again never . now , mother , I be ready , as it be your wish . will you come and see whether I be afraid ? Mr. Ward , I be your servant . your servant ? your slave ! and the next time I meet Mr. Washington , madam , I will thank he for the advice which he give you . " 
 " I say , do your duty , sir ! " cry Mrs. Esmond , stamp her little foot . and George , make a low bow to Mr. Ward , beg he to go first out of the room to the study . 
 " stop ! for God 's sake , mother , stop ! " cry poor Hal . but passion be boil in the little woman 's heart , and she would not hear the boy 's petition . " you only abet he , sir ! " she cry . " if I have to do it myself , it should be do ! " and Harry , with sadness and wrath in his countenance , leave the room by the door through which Mr. Ward and his brother have just issue . 
 the widow sink down on a great chair near it , and sit a while vacantly look at the fragment of the broken cup . then she incline her head towards the door one of half a dozen of carve mahogany which the Colonel have bring from Europe . for a while there be silence : then a loud outcry , which make the poor mother start . 
 in another minute Mr. Ward come out bleed , from a great wound on his head , and behind he Harry , with flare eye , and brandish a little couteau - de - chasse of his grandfather , which hung , with other of the Colonel 's weapon , on the library wall . 
 " I do not   care . I do it , " say Harry . " I could not   see this fellow strike my brother ; and , as he lift his hand , I fling the great ruler at he . I could not   help it . I will not   bear it ; and , if one lift a hand to I or my brother , I will have his life , " shout Harry , brandish the hanger . 
 the widow give a great gasp and a sigh as she look at the young champion and his victim . she must have suffer terribly during the few minute of the boy ' absence ; and the stripe which she imagine have be inflict on the elder have smite her own heart . she long to take both boy to it . she be not angry now . very likely she be delighted with the thought of the young 's prowess and generosity . " you be a very naughty disobedient child , " she say , in an exceedingly peaceable voice . " my poor Mr. Ward ! what a rebel , to strike you ! Papa 's great ebony ruler , be it ? lay down that hanger , child . ' Twas General Webb give it to my papa after the siege of Lille . let I bathe your wound , my good Mr. Ward , and thank Heaven it be no bad . mountain ! go fetch I some court - plaster out of the middle drawer in the japan cabinet . here come George . put on your coat and waistcoat , child ! you be go to take your punishment , sir , and that be sufficient . ask pardon , Harry , of good Mr. Ward , for your wicked rebellious spirit , I do , with all my heart , I be sure . and guard against your passionate nature , child and pray to be forgive . my son , o my son ! " here , with a burst of tear which she could no long control , the little woman throw herself on the neck of her eldest - bear ; whilst Harry , lay the hanger down , go up very feebly to Mr. Ward , and say , " indeed , I ask your pardon , sir . I could not   help it ; on my honour I could not ; nor bear to see my brother strike . " 
 the widow be scared , as after her embrace she look up at George 's pale face . in reply to her eager caress , he coldly kiss she on the forehead , and separate from she . " you mean for the good , mother , " he say , " and I be in the wrong . but the cup be break ; and all the king 's horse and all the king 's man can not mend it . there put the fair side outward on the mantelpiece , and the wound will not show . " 
 again Madam Esmond look at the lad , as he place the fragment of the poor cup on the ledge where it have always be use to stand . her power over he be go . he have dominate she . she be not sorry for the defeat ; for woman like not only to conquer , but to be conquer ; and from that day the young gentleman be master at Castlewood . his mother admire he as he go up to Harry , graciously and condescendingly give Hal his hand , and say , " thank you , brother ! " as if he be a prince , and Harry a general who have help he in a great battle . 
 then George go up to Mr. Ward , who be still piteously bath his eye and forehead in the water . " I ask pardon for Hal 's violence , sir , " George say , in great state . " you see , though we be very young , we be gentleman , and can not brook an insult from stranger . I should have submit , as it be mamma 's desire ; but I be glad she no long entertain it . " 
 " and pray , sir , who be to compensate I ? " say Mr. Ward ; " who be to repair the insult do to I ? " 
 " we be very young , " say George , with another of his old - fashioned bow . " we shall be fifteen soon . any compensation that be usual amongst gentleman " 
 " this , sir , to a minister of the Word ! " bawl out Ward , start up , and who know perfectly well the lad ' skill in fence , have a score of time be foil by the pair of they . 
 " you be not a clergyman yet . we think you might like to be consider as a gentleman . we do not know . " 
 " a gentleman ! I be a Christian , sir ! " say Ward , glare furiously , and clench his great fist . 
 " well , well , if you will not   fight , why do not   you forgive ? " say Harry . " if you do not   forgive , why do not   you fight ? that be what I call the horn of a dilemma ; " and he laugh his frank , jolly laugh . 
 but this be nothing to the laugh a few day afterwards , when , the quarrel have be patch up , along with poor Mr. Ward 's eye , the unlucky tutor be hold forth accord to his custom . he try to preach the boy into respect for he , to reawaken the enthusiasm which the congregation have feel for he ; he wrestle with their manifest indifference , he implore Heaven to warm their cold heart again , and to lift up those who be fall back . all be in vain . the widow weep no more at his harangue , be no long excite by his loud trope and simile , nor appear to be much frighten by the very hot menace with which he pepper his discourse . Nay , she plead headache , and would absent herself of an evening , on which occasion the remainder of the little congregation be very cold indeed . one day , then , Ward , still make desperate effort to get back his despise authority , be preach on the beauty of subordination , the present lax spirit of the age , and the necessity of obey our spiritual and temporal ruler . " for why , my dear friend , " he nobly ask   he be in the habit of ask immensely dull question , and straightway answer they with corresponding platitude , " why be governor appoint , but that we should be govern ? why be tutor engage , but that child should be teach ? "   here a look at the boy . " why be ruler " here he pause , look with a sad , puzzled face at the young gentleman . he see in their countenance the double meaning of the unlucky word he have utter , and stammer , and thump the table with his fist . " why , I say , be ruler " 
 " ruler , " say George , look at Harry . 
 " ruler ! " say Hal , put his hand to his eye , where the poor tutor still bear mark of the late scuffle . ruler , o - ho ! it be too much . the boy burst out in an explosion of laughter . Mrs. Mountain , who be full of fun , could not help join in the chorus ; and little Fanny , who have always behave very demurely and silently at these ceremony , crow again , and clap she little hand at the other laugh , not in the least know the reason why . 
 this could not be bear . Ward shut down the book before he ; in a few angry , but eloquent and manly word , say he would speak no more in that place ; and leave Castlewood not in the least regret by Madam Esmond , who have dot on he three month before . 




 CHAPTER VI . the Virginians begin to see the world 

 after the departure of her unfortunate spiritual adviser and chaplain , Madam Esmond and her son seem to be quite reconcile : but although George never speak of the quarrel with his mother , it must have weigh upon the boy 's mind very painfully , for he have a fever soon after the last recount domestic occurrence , during which illness his brain once or twice wander , when he shriek out , " Broken ! break ! it never , never can be mend ! " to the silent terror of his mother , who sate watch the poor child as he toss wakeful upon his midnight bed . his malady defy her skill , and increase in spite of all the nostrum which the good widow keep in her closet and administer so freely to her people . she have to undergo another humiliation , and one day little Mr. Dempster behold she at his door on horseback . she have ride through the snow on her pony , to implore he to give his aid to her poor boy . " I shall bury my resentment , madam , " say he , " as your ladyship bury your pride . please God , I maybe time enough to help my dear young pupil ! " so he put up his lancet , and his little provision of medicament ; call his only negro - boy after he , shut up his lonely hut , and once more return to Castlewood . that night and for some day afterwards it seem very likely that poor Harry would become heir of Castlewood ; but by Mr. Dempster 's skill the fever be get over , the intermittent attack diminish in intensity , and George be restore almost to health again . a change of air , a voyage even to England , be recommend , but the widow have quarrel with her child 's relative there , and own with contrition that she have be too hasty . a journey to the north and east be determine on , and the two young gentleman , with Mr. Dempster as their tutor , and a couple of servant to attend they , take a voyage to New York , and thence up the beautiful Hudson river to Albany , where they be receive by the first gentry of the province , and thence into the french province , where they have the good recommendation , and be hospitably entertain by the french gentry . Harry camp with the Indians , and take fur and shot bear . George , who never care for field - sport , and whose health be still delicate , be a special favourite with the french lady , who be accustomed to see very few young english gentleman speak the french language so readily as our young gentleman . George especially perfect his accent so as to be able to pass for a Frenchman . he have the bel air completely , every person allow . he dance the minuet elegantly . he learn the late import french catch and song , and play they beautifully on his violin , and would have sing they too but that his voice break at this time , and change from treble to bass ; and , to the envy of poor Harry , who be absent on a bear - hunt , he even have an affair of honour with a young ensign of the regiment of Auvergne , the Chevalier de la Jabotiere , whom he pink in the shoulder , and with whom he afterwards swear an eternal friendship . Madame de Mouchy , the superintendent 's lady , say the mother be blest who have such a son , and write a complimentary letter to Madam Esmond upon Mr. George 's behaviour . I fear , Mr. Whitfield would not have be over - pleased with the widow 's elation on hearing of her son 's prowess . 
 when the lad return home at the end of ten delightful month , their mother be surprised at their growth and improvement . George especially be so grow as to come up to his younger - bear brother . the boy could hardly be distinguish one from another , especially when their hair be powder ; but that ceremony be too cumbrous for country life , each of the gentleman commonly wear his own hair , George his raven black , and Harry his light lock tie with a ribbon . 
 the reader who have be so kind as to look over the first page of the lad 's simple biography , must have observe that Mr. George Esmond be of a jealous and suspicious disposition , most generous and gentle and incapable of an untruth , and though too magnanimous to revenge , almost incapable of forgive any injury . George leave home with no goodwill towards an honourable gentleman , whose name afterwards become one of the most famous in the world ; and he return from his journey not in the least altered in his opinion of his mother 's and grandfather 's friend . Mr. Washington , though then but just of age , look and feel much old . he always exhibit an extraordinary simplicity and gravity ; he have manage his mother 's and his family 's affair from a very early age , and be trust by all his friend and the gentry of his county more than person twice his senior . 
 Mrs. Mountain , Madam Esmond 's friend and companion , who dearly love the two boy and her patroness , in spite of many quarrel with the latter , and daily threat of parting , be a most amusing , droll letter - writer , and use to write to the two boy on their travel . now , Mrs. Mountain be of a jealous turn likewise ; especially she have a great turn for match - making , and fancy that everybody have a design to marry everybody else . there scarce come an unmarried man to Castlewood but Mountain imagine the gentleman have an eye towards the mistress of the mansion . she be positive that odious Mr. Ward intend to make love to the widow , and pretty sure the latter like he . she know that Mr. Washington want to be marry , be certain that such a shrewd young gentleman would look out for a rich wife , and , as for the difference of age , what matter that the Major   major be his rank in the militia   be fifteen year young than Madam Esmond ? they be use to such marriage in the family ; my lady her mother be how many year old than the Colonel when she marry he ? when she marry he and be so jealous that she never would let the poor Colonel out of her sight . the poor Colonel ! after his wife , he have be henpecke by his little daughter . and she would take after her mother , and marry again , be sure of that . Madam be a little chit of a woman , not five foot in her high headdress and shoe , and Mr. Washington a great tall man of six foot two . great tall man always marry little chit of woman : therefore , Mr. W. must be look after the widow . what could be more clear than the deduction ? 
 she communicate these sage opinion to her boy , as she call George , who beg she , for Heaven 's sake , to hold her tongue . this she say she could do , but she could not keep her eye always shut ; and she narrate a hundred circumstance which have occur in the young gentleman 's absence , and which tend , as she think , to confirm her notion . have Mountain impart these pretty suspicion to his brother ? George ask sternly . no . George be her boy ; Harry be his mother 's boy . " she like he good , and I like you well , George , " cry Mountain . " besides , if I be to speak to he , he would tell your mother in a minute . Poor Harry can keep nothing quiet , and then there would be a pretty quarrel between Madam and I ! " 
 " I beg you to keep this quiet , Mountain , " say Mr. George , with great dignity , " or you and I shall quarrel too . neither to I nor to any one else in the world must you mention such an absurd suspicion . " 
 Absurd ! why absurd ? Mr. Washington be constantly with the widow . his name be forever in her mouth . she be never tired of point out his virtue and example to her son . she consult he on every question respect her estate and its management . she never buy a horse or sell a barrel of tobacco without his opinion . there be a room at Castlewood regularly call Mr. Washington 's room . " he actually leave his clothe here and his portmanteau when he go away . ah ! George , George ! one day will come when he will not   go away , " groan Mountain , who , of course , always return to the subject of which she be forbid to speak . meanwhile Mr. George adopt towards his mother 's favourite a frigid courtesy , at which the honest gentleman chafe but do not care to remonstrate , or a stinging sarcasm , which he would break through as he would burst through so many bramble on those hunting excursion in which he and Harry Warrington ride so constantly together ; whilst George , retreat to his tent , read mathematic , and French , and Latin , and sulk in his book - room more and more lonely . 
 Harry be away from home with some other sporting friend   it be to be fear the young gentleman 's acquaintance be not all as eligible as Mr. Washington , when the latter come to pay a visit at Castlewood . he be so peculiarly tender and kind to the mistress there , and receive by she with such special cordiality , that George Warrington 's jealousy have well - nigh break out in open rupture . but the visit be one of adieu , as it appear . 
 Major Washington be go on a long and dangerous journey , quite to the western Virginia frontier and beyond it . the French have be for some time past make inroad into our territory . the government at home , as well as those of Virginia and Pennsylvania , be alarmed at this aggressive spirit of the Lords of Canada and Louisiana . some of our settler have already be drive from their holding by frenchman in arm , and the governor of the british province be desirous to stop their incursion , or at any rate to protest against their invasion . 
 we choose to hold our american colony by a law that be at least convenient for its framer . the maxim be , that whoever possess the coast have a right to all the territory inland as far as the Pacific ; so that the british charter only lay down the limit of the colony from north to south , leave they quite free from east to west . the French , meanwhile , have their colony to the north and south , and aim at connect they by the Mississippi and the St. Lawrence and the great intermediate lake and water lie to the westward of the british possession . in the year 1748 , though peace be sign between the two european kingdom , the colonial question remain unsettled , to be open again when either party should be strong enough to urge it . in the year 1753 , it come to an issue , on the Ohio river , where the british and french settler meet . to be sure , there exist other people besides french and British , who think they have a title to the territory about which the child of their White Fathers be battle , namely , the native Indians and proprietor of the soil . but the logician of St. James 's and Versailles wisely choose to consider the matter in dispute as a european and not a Red - man 's question , eliminate he from the argument , but employ his tomahawk as it might serve the turn of either litigant . 
 a company , call the Ohio Company , have grant from the Virginia government of land along that river , find themselves invade in their settlement by french military detachment , who roughly eject the Britons from their holding . these latter apply for protection to Mr. Dinwiddie , Lieutenant - Governor of Virginia , who determine upon send an ambassador to the french command officer on the Ohio , demand that the French should desist from their inroad upon the territory of his Majesty King George . 
 Young Mr. Washington jump eagerly at the chance of distinction which this service afford he , and volunteer to leave his home and his rural and professional pursuit in Virginia , to carry the governor 's message to the french officer . take a guide , an interpreter , and a few attendant , and follow the indian track , in the fall of the year 1753 , the intrepid young envoy make his way from Williamsburg almost to the shore of Lake Erie , and find the french commander at Fort le Boeuf . that officer 's reply be brief : his order be to hold the place and drive all the English from it . the French avow their intention of take possession of the Ohio . and with this rough answer the messenger from Virginia have to return through danger and difficulty , across lonely forest and frozen river , shape his course by the compass , and camping at night in the snow by the forest fire . 
 Harry Warrington curse his ill - fortune that he have be absent from home on a cock - fight , when he might have have chance of sport so much nobler ; and on his return from his expedition , which he have conduct with an heroic energy and simplicity , Major Washington be a great favourite than ever with the lady of Castlewood . she point he out as a model to both her son . " ah , Harry ! " she would say , " think of you , with your cock - fighting and your racing - match , and the Major away there in the wilderness , watch the French , and battle with the frozen river ! ah , George ! learning may be a very good thing , but I wish my eld son be do something in the service of his country ! " 
 " I desire no well than to go home and seek for employment , ma'am , " say George . " you surely will not have I serve under Mr. Washington , in his new regiment , or ask a commission from Mr. Dinwiddie ? " 
 " an Esmond can only serve with the king 's commission , " say Madam , " and as for ask a favour from Mr. Lieutenant - Governor Dinwiddie , I would rather beg my bread . " 
 Mr. Washington be at this time raise such a regiment as , with the scanty pay and patronage of the virginian government , he could get together , and propose , with the help of these man - of - war , to put a more peremptory veto upon the french invader than the solitary ambassador have be enable to lay . a small force under another officer , Colonel Trent , have be already despatch to the west , with order to fortify themselves so as to be able to resist any attack of the enemy . the french troop , greatly outnumber our , come up with the english outpost , who be fortify themselves at a place on the confine of Pennsylvania where the great city of Pittsburg now stand . a virginian officer with but forty man be in no condition to resist twenty time that number of Canadians , who appear before his incomplete work . he be suffer to draw back without molestation ; and the French , take possession of his fort , strengthen it , and christen it by the name of the canadian governor , Du Quesne . up to this time no actual blow of war have be strike . the troop represent the hostile nation be in presence the gun be load , but no one as yet have cry " Fire . " it be strange , that in a savage forest of Pennsylvania , a young virginian officer should fire a shot , and waken up a war which be to last for sixty year , which be to cover his own country and pass into Europe , to cost France her american colony , to sever our from we , and create the great western republic ; to rage over the Old World when extinguish in the New ; and , of all the myriad engage in the vast contest , to leave the prize of the great fame with he who strike the first blow ! 
 he little know of the fate in store for he . a simple gentleman , anxious to serve his king and do his duty , he volunteer for the first service , and execute it with admirable fidelity . in the ensue year he take the command of the small body of provincial troop with which he march to repel the Frenchmen . he come up with their advanced guard and fire upon they , kill their leader . after this he have himself to fall back with his troop , and be compel to capitulate to the superior french force . on the 4th of July , 1754 , the Colonel march out with his troop from the little fort where he have hastily entrench himself   and which they call Fort Necessity , give up the place to the conqueror , and take his way home . 
 his command be over : his regiment disband after the fruitless , inglorious march and defeat . Saddened and humble in spirit , the young officer present himself after a while to his old friend at Castlewood . he be very young : before he set forth on his first campaign he may have indulge in exaggerated hope of success , and utter they . " I be angry when I part from you , " he say to George Warrington , hold out his hand , which the other eagerly take . " you seem to scorn I and my regiment , George . I think you laugh at we , and your ridicule make I angry . I boast too much of what we would do . " 
 " Nay , you have do your good , George , " say the other , who quite forget his previous jealousy in his old comrade 's misfortune . " everybody know that a hundred and fifty starve man , with scarce a round of ammunition leave , could not face five time their number perfectly armed , and everybody who know Mr. Washington know that he would do his duty . Harry and I see the French in Canada last year . they obey but one will : in our province each governor have his own . they be royal troop the French send against you ... " 
 " oh , but that some of our be here ! " cry Madam Esmond , toss her head up . " I promise you a few good english regiment would make the white - coat run . " 
 " you think nothing of the provincial : and I must say nothing now we have be so unlucky , " say the Colonel , gloomily . " you make much of I when I be here before . do not   you remember what victory you prophesy for I how much I boast myself very likely over your good wine ? all those fine dream be over now . ' tis kind of your ladyship to receive a poor beat fellow as you do : " and the young soldier hang down his head . 
 George Warrington , with his extreme acute sensibility , be touch at the other 's emotion and simple testimony of sorrow under defeat . he be about to say something friendly to Mr. Washington , have not his mother , to whom the Colonel have be speak , reply herself : " kind of we to receive you , Colonel Washington ! " say the widow . " I never hear that when man be unhappy , our sex be less their friend . " 
 and she make the Colonel a very fine curtsey , which straightway cause her son to be more jealous of he than ever . 




 CHAPTER VII . preparation for War 

 Surely no man can have well claim to sympathy than bravery , youth , good look , and misfortune . Madam Esmond might have have twenty son , and yet have a right to admire her young soldier . Mr. Washington 's room be more than ever Mr. Washington 's room now . she rave about he and praise he in all company . she more than ever point out his excellence to her son , contrast his sterling quality with Harry 's love of pleasure   the wild boy !   and George 's listless musing over his book . George be not disposed to like Mr. Washington any well for his mother 's extravagant praise . he coax the jealous demon within he until he must have become a perfect pest to himself and all the friend round about he . he utter joke so deep that his simple mother do not know their meaning , but sate bewilder at his sarcasm , and powerless what to think of his moody , saturnine humour . 
 meanwhile , public event be occur which be to influence the fortune of all our homely family . the quarrel between the French and English North Americans , from be a provincial , have grow to be a national , quarrel . reinforcement from France have already arrive in Canada ; and english troop be expect in Virginia . " alas ! my dear friend ! " write Madame la Presidente de Mouchy , from Quebec , to her young friend George Warrington . " how contrary be the destiny to we ! I see you quit the embrace of an adore mother to precipitate yourself in the arm of Bellona . I see you pass wound after combat . I hesitate almost to wish victory to our lily when I behold you range under the banner of the Leopard . there be enmity which the heart do not recognise our assuredly be at peace among the tumult . all here love and salute you , as well as Monsieur the Bear - hunter , your brother   that cold Hippolyte who prefer the chase to the soft conversation of our lady !   your friend , your enemy , the Chevalier de la Jabotiere , burn to meet on the field of Mars his generous rival . M. Du Quesne speak of you last night at supper . M. Du Quesne , my husband , send affectuous remembrance to their young friend , with which be ever join those of your sincere Presidente de Mouchy . " 
 " the banner of the Leopard , " of which George 's fair correspondent write , be , indeed , fling out to the wind , and a number of the king 's soldier be rally round it . it be resolve to wrest from the French all the conquest they have make upon british dominion . a couple of regiment be raise and pay by the king in America , and a fleet with a couple more be despatch from home under an experienced commander . in February , 1755 , Commodore Keppel , in the famous ship centurion , in which Anson have make his voyage round the world , anchor in Hampton Roads with two ship of war under his command , and have on board General Braddock , his staff , and a part of his troop . Mr. Braddock be appoint by the Duke . a hundred year ago the Duke of Cumberland be call the Duke par excellence in England as another famous warrior have since be call . not so great a Duke certainly be that first - name Prince as his party esteem he , and surely not so bad a one as his enemy have paint he . a fleet of transport speedily follow Prince William 's general , bring store , and man , and money in plenty . 
 the great man land his troop at Alexandria on the Potomac river , and repair to Annapolis in Maryland , where he order the governor of the different colony to meet he in council , urge they each to call upon their respective province to help the common cause in this strait . 
 the arrival of the General and his little army cause a mighty excitement all through the province , and nowhere great than at Castlewood . Harry be off forthwith to see the troop under canvas at Alexandria . the sight of their line delight he , and the inspire music of their fife and drum . he speedily make acquaintance with the officer of both regiment ; he long to join in the expedition upon which they be bind , and be a welcome guest at their mess . 
 Madam Esmond be pleased that her son should have an opportunity of enjoy the society of gentleman of good fashion from England . she have no doubt their company be improve , that the english gentleman be very different from the horse - racing , cock - fight virginian squire , with whom Master Harry would associate , and the lawyer , and pettifogger , and toad - eater at the lieutenant - governor 's table . Madam Esmond have a very keen eye for detect flatterer in other folk ' house . against the little knot of official people at Williamsburg she be especially satirical , and have no patience with their etiquette and squabble for precedence . 
 as for the company of the king 's officer , Mr. Harry and his eld brother both smile at their mamma 's compliment to the elegance and propriety of the gentleman of the camp . if the good lady have but know all , if she could but have hear their joke and the song which they sing over their wine and punch , if she could have see the condition of many of they as they be carry away to their lodging , she would scarce have be so ready to recommend their company to her son . man and officer swagger the country round , and frighten the peaceful farm and village folk with their riot : the General rave and storm against his troop for their disorder ; against the provincial for their traitorous niggardliness ; the soldier take possession almost as of a conquered country , they scorn the provincial , they insult the wife even of their indian ally , who have come to join the english warrior , upon their arrival in America , and to march with they against the French . the General be compel to forbid the indian woman his camp . Amazed and outrage their husband retire , and but a few month afterwards their service be lose to he , when their aid would have be most precious . 
 some story against the gentleman of the camp , Madam Esmond might have hear , but she would have none of they . soldier would be soldier , that everybody know ; those officer who come over to Castlewood on her son 's invitation be most polite gentleman , and such indeed be the case . the widow receive they most graciously , and give they the good sport the country afford . presently , the General himself send polite message to the mistress of Castlewood . his father have serve with hers under the glorious Marlborough , and Colonel Esmond 's name be still know and respect in England . with her ladyship 's permission , General Braddock would have the honour of wait upon she at Castlewood , and pay his respect to the daughter of so meritorious an officer . 
 if she have know the cause of Mr. Braddock 's politeness , perhaps his compliment would not have charm Madam Esmond so much . the Commander - in - chief hold levee at Alexandria , and among the gentry of the country , who pay he their respect , be our twin of Castlewood , who mount their good nag , take with they their last London suit , and , with their two negro - boy , in smart livery behind they , ride in state to wait upon the great man . he be sulky and angry with the provincial gentry , and scarce take any notice of the young gentleman , only ask , casually , of his aide - de - camp at dinner , who the young squire Gawkeys be in blue and gold and red waistcoat ? 
 Mr. Dinwiddie , the Lieutenant - Governor of Virginia , the Agent from Pennsylvania , and a few more gentleman , happen to be din with his Excellency . " oh ! " say Mr. Dinwiddie , " those be the son of the Princess Pocahontas ; " on which , with a tremendous oath , the General ask , " who the deuce be she ? " 
 Dinwiddie , who do not love she , have indeed undergo a hundred pertness from the imperious little lady , now give a disrespectful and ridiculous account of Madam Esmond , make merry with her pomposity and immense pretension , and entertain General Braddock with anecdote regard she , until his Excellency fall asleep . 
 when he awake , Dinwiddie be go , but the Philadelphia gentleman be still at table , deep in conversation with the officer there present . the General take up the talk where it have be leave when he fall asleep , and speak of Madam Esmond in curt , disrespectful term , such as soldier be in the habit of use in those day , and ask , again , what be the name of the old fool about whom Dinwiddie have be talk ? he then break into expression of contempt and wrath against the gentry , and the country in general . 
 Mr. Franklin of Philadelphia repeat the widow 's name , take quite a different view of her character from that Mr. Dinwiddie have give , seem to know a good deal about she , her father , and her estate ; as , indeed , he do about every man or subject which come under discussion ; explain to the General that Madam Esmond have beef , and horse , and store in plenty , which might be very useful at the present juncture , and recommend he to conciliate she by all mean . the General have already make up his mind that Mr. Franklin be a very shrewd , intelligent person , and graciously order an aide - de - camp to invite the two young man to the next day 's dinner . when they appear he be very pleasant and good - natured ; the gentleman of the General 's family make much of they . they behave , as become person of their name , with modesty and good - breeding ; they return home delighted with their entertainment , nor be their mother less pleased at the civility which his Excellency have show to her boy . in reply to Braddock 's message , Madam Esmond pen a billet in her good style , acknowledge his politeness , and beg his Excellency to fix the time when she might have the honour to receive he at Castlewood . 
 we may be sure that the arrival of the army and the approach campaign form the subject of continue conversation in the Castlewood family . to make the campaign be the dear wish of Harry 's life . he dream only of war and battle ; he be for ever with the officer at Williamsburg ; he scour and clean and polish all the gun and sword in the house ; he renew the amusement of his childhood , and have the negro under arm . his mother , who have a gallant spirit , know that the time be come when one of her boy must leave she and serve the king . she scarce dare to think on whom the lot should fall . she admire and respect the elder , but she feel that she love the young boy with all the passion of her heart . 
 Eager as Harry be to be a soldier , and with all his thought bent on that glorious scheme , he too scarcely dare to touch on the subject near his heart . once or twice when he venture on it with George , the latter 's countenance wear an ominous look . Harry have a feudal attachment for his eld brother , worship he with an extravagant regard , and in all thing give way to he as the chief . so Harry see , to his infinite terror , how George , too , in his grave way , be occupy with military matter . George have the war of Eugene and Marlborough down from his bookshelf , all the military book of his grandfather , and the most warlike of Plutarch 's life . he and Dempster be practise with the foil again . the old Scotchman be an adept in the military art , though somewhat shy of say where he learn it . 
 Madam Esmond make she two boy the bearer of the letter in reply to his Excellency 's message , accompany her note with such large and handsome present for the General 's staff and the officer of the two Royal Regiments , as cause the General more than once to thank Mr. Franklin for have be the mean of bring this welcome ally into the camp . " would not one of the young gentleman like to see the campaign ? " the General ask . " a friend of theirs , who often speak of they Mr. Washington , who have be unlucky in the affair of last year have already promise to join he as aide - de - camp , and his Excellency would gladly take another young virginian gentleman into his family . " Harry 's eye brighten and his face flush at this offer . " he would like with all his heart to go ! " he cry out . George say , look hard at his young brother , that one of they would be proud to attend his Excellency , whilst it would be the other 's duty to take care of their mother at home . Harry allow his senior to speak . his will be even still obedient to George 's . however much he desire to go , he would not pronounce until George have declare himself . he long so for the campaign , that the actual wish make he timid . he dare not speak on the matter as he go home with George . they ride for mile in silence , or strove to talk upon indifferent subject ; each know what be pass in the other 's mind , and afraid to bring the awful question to an issue . 
 on their arrival at home the boy tell their mother of General Braddock 's offer . " I know it must happen , " she say ; " at such a crisis in the country our family must come forward . have you have you settle yet which of you be to leave I ? " and she look anxiously from one to another , dread to hear either name . 
 " the young ought to go , mother ; of course I ought to go ! " cry Harry , turn very red . 
 " of course he ought , " say Mrs. Mountain , who be present at their talk . 
 " there ! mountain say so ! I tell you so ! " again cry Harry , with a sidelong look at George . 
 " the head of the family ought to go , mother , " say George , sadly . 
 " no ! no ! you be ill , and have never recover your fever . ought he to go , mountain ? " 
 " you would make the good soldier , I know that , dear Hal . you and George Washington be great friend , and could travel well together , and he do not care for I , nor I for he , however much he be admire in the family . but , you see , ' tis the law of Honour , my Harry . "   he here speak to his brother with a voice of extraordinary kindness and tenderness .   " the grief I have have in this matter have be that I must refuse thee . I must go . have Fate give you the benefit of that extra half - hour of life which I have have before you , it would have be your lot , and you would have claim your right to go first , you know you would . " 
 " yes , George , " say poor Harry , " I own I should . " 
 " you will stay at home , and take care of Castlewood and our mother . if anything happen to I , you be here to fill my place . I would like to give way , my dear , as you , I know , would lay down your life to serve I . but each of we must do his duty . what would our grandfather say if he be here ? " 
 the mother look proudly at her two son . " my papa would say that his boy be gentleman , " falter Madam Esmond , and leave the young man , not choose , perhaps , to show the emotion which be fill her heart . it be speedily know amongst the servant that Mr. George be go on the campaign . Dinah , George 's foster - mother , be loud in her lamentation at lose he ; Phillis , Harry 's old nurse , be as noisy because Master George , as usual , be prefer over Master Harry . Sady , George 's servant , make preparation to follow his master , brag incessantly of the deed which he would do , while Gumbo , Harry 's boy , pretend to whimper at be leave behind , though , at home , Gumbo be anything but a fire - eater . 
 but , of all in the house , Mrs. Mountain be the most angry at George 's determination to go on the campaign . she have no patience with he . he do not know what he be do by leave home . she beg , implored , insist that he should alter his determination ; and vow that nothing but mischief would come from his departure . 
 George be surprised at the pertinacity of the good lady 's opposition . " I know , Mountain , " say he , " that Harry would be the well soldier ; but , after all , to go be my duty . " 
 " to stay be your duty ! " say Mountain , with a stamp of her foot . 
 " why do not my mother own it when we talk of the matter just now ? " 
 " your mother ! " say Mrs. Mountain , with a most gloomy , sardonic laugh ; " your mother , my poor child ! " 
 " what be the meaning of that mournful countenance , mountain ? " 
 " it may be that your mother wish you away , George ! " Mrs. Mountain continue , wag her head . " it may be , my poor delude boy , that you will find a father - in - law when you come back . " 
 " what in heaven do you mean ? " cry George , the blood rush into his face . 
 " do you suppose I have no eye , and can not see what be go on ? I tell you , child , that Colonel Washington want a rich wife . when you be go , he will ask your mother to marry he , and you will find he master here when you come back . that be why you ought not to go away , you poor , unhappy , simple boy ! do not   you see how fond she be of he ? how much she make of he ? how she be always hold he up to you , to Harry , to everybody who come here ? " 
 " but he be go on the campaign , too , " cry George . 
 " he be go on the marrying campaign , child ! " insist the widow . 
 " Nay ; General Braddock himself tell I that Mr. Washington have accept the appointment of aide - de - camp . " 
 " an artifice ! an artifice to blind you , my poor child ! " cry Mountain . " he will be wound and come back you will see if he do not . I have proof of what I say to you proof under his own hand look here ! " and she take from her pocket a piece of paper in Mr. Washington 's well - know handwriting . 
 " how come you by this paper ? " ask George , turn ghastly pale . 
 " I I find it in the Major 's chamber ! " say Mrs. Mountain , with a shamefaced look . 
 " you read the private letter of a guest stay in our house ? " cry George . " for shame ! I will not look at the paper ! " and he fling it from he on to the fire before he . 
 " I could not help it , George ; ' twa by chance , I give you my word , by the mere chance . you know Governor Dinwiddie be to have the Major 's room , and the state - room be get ready for Mr. Braddock , and we be expect ever so much company , and I have to take the thing which the major leaf here he treat the house just as if it be his own already into his new room , and this half - sheet of paper fall out of his writing - book , and I just give one look at it by the mere chance , and when I see what it be it be my duty to read it . " 
 " oh , you be a martyr to duty , Mountain ! " George say grimly . " I dare say Mrs. Bluebeard think it be her duty to look through the keyhole . " 
 " I never do look through the keyhole , George . it be a shame you should say so ! I , who have watch , and tend , and nurse you , like a mother ; who have sate up whole week with you in fever , and carry you from your bed to the sofa in these arm . there , sir , I do not   want you there now . my dear Mountain , indeed ! do not   tell I ! you fly into a passion , and , call name , and wound my feeling , who have love you like your mother like your mother ? I only hope she may love you half as well . I say you be all ungrateful . my Mr. Mountain be a wretch , and every one of you be as bad . " 
 there be but a smoulder log or two in the fireplace , and no doubt Mountain see that the paper be in no danger as it lie amongst the ash , or she would have seize it at the risk of burn her own finger , and ere she utter the above passionate defence of her conduct . perhaps George be absorb in his dismal thought ; perhaps his jealousy overpower he , for he do not resist any far when she stoop down and pick up the paper . 
 " you should thank your star , child , that I save the letter , " cry she . " see ! here be his own word , in his great big handwriting like a clerk . it be not my fault that he write they , or that I find they . read for yourself , I say , George Warrington , and be thankful that your poor dear old Mounty be watch over you ! " 
 every word and letter upon the unlucky paper be perfectly clear . George 's eye could not help take in the content of the document before he . " not a word of this , Mountain , " he say , give she a frightful look . " I I will return this paper to Mr. Washington . " 
 Mountain be scared at his face , at the idea of what she have do , and what might ensue . when his mother , with alarm in her countenance , ask he at dinner what ail he that he look so pale ? " do you suppose , madam , " say he , fill himself a great bumper of wine , " that to leave such a tender mother as you do not cause I cruel grief ? " 
 the good lady could not understand his word , his strange , fierce look , and stranger laughter . he banter all at the table ; call to the servant and laugh at they , and drink more and more . each time the door be open , he turn towards it ; and so do Mountain , with a guilty notion that Mr. Washington would step in . 




 CHAPTER VIII . in which George suffer from a Common Disease 

 on the day appoint for Madam Esmond 's entertainment to the General , the house of Castlewood be set out with the great splendour ; and Madam Esmond array herself in a much more magnificent dress than she be accustom to wear . indeed , she wish to do every honour to her guest , and to make the entertainment which , in reality , be a sad one to she as pleasant as might be for her company . the General 's new aide - de - camp be the first to arrive . the widow receive he in the cover gallery before the house . he dismount at the step , and his servant lead away his horse to the well - know quarter . no young gentleman in the colony be well mount or a well horseman than Mr. Washington . 
 for a while ere the Major retire to divest himself of his riding - boot , he and his hostess pace the gallery in talk . she have much to say to he ; she have to hear from he a confirmation of his own appointment as aide - de - camp to General Braddock , and to speak of her son 's approach departure . the negro servant bear the dish for the approach feast be pass perpetually as they talk . they descend the step down to the rough lawn in front of the house , and pace a while in the shade . Mr. Washington announce his Excellency 's speedy approach , with Mr. Franklin of Pennsylvania in his coach . 
 this Mr. Franklin have be a common printer 's boy , Mrs. Esmond have hear ; a pretty pass thing be come to when such person ride in the coach of the Commander - in - Chief ! Mr. Washington say , a more shrewd and sensible gentleman never ride in coach or walk on foot . Mrs. Esmond think the Major be too liberally disposed towards this gentleman ; but Mr. Washington stoutly maintain against the widow that the printer be a most ingenious , useful , and meritorious man . 
 " I be glad , at least , that , as my boy be go to make the campaign , he will not be with tradesman , but with gentleman , with gentleman of honour and fashion , " say Madam Esmond , in her most stately manner . 
 Mr. Washington have see the gentleman of honour and fashion over their cup , and perhaps think that all their saying and doing be not precisely such as would tend to instruct or edify a young man on his entrance into life ; but he wisely choose to tell no tale out of school , and say that Harry and George , now they be come into the world , must take their share of good and bad , and hear what both sort have to say . 
 " to be with a veteran officer of the fine army in the world , " falter the widow ; " with gentleman who have be breed in the midst of the Court ; with friend of his Royal Highness , the Duke " 
 the widow 's friend only incline his head . he do not choose to allow his countenance to depart from its usual handsome gravity . 
 " and with you , dear Colonel Washington , by whom my father always set such store . you do not   know how much he trust in you . you will take care of my boy , sir , will not you ? you be but five year old , yet I trust to you more than to his senior ; my father always tell the child , I alway bid they , to look up to Mr. Washington . " 
 " you know I would have do anything to win Colonel Esmond 's favour . Madam , how much would I not venture to merit his daughter 's ? " 
 the gentleman bow with not too ill a grace . the lady blush , and drop one of the low curtsy .   Madam Esmond 's curtsey be consider unrivalled over the whole province .   " Mr. Washington , " she say , " will be always sure of a mother 's affection , whilst he give so much of his to her child . " and so say she give he her hand , which he kiss with profound politeness . the little lady presently re - enter her mansion , lean upon the tall young officer 's arm . here they be join by George , who come to they , accurately powder and richly attire , salute his parent and his friend alike with low and respectful bow . nowadays , a young man walk into his mother 's room with hobnail high - low , and a wideawake on his head ; and instead of make she a bow , puff a cigar into her face . 
 but George , though he make the low possible bow to Mr. Washington and his mother , be by no mean in good - humour with either of they . a polite smile play round the low part of his countenance , whilst watchfulness and wrath glare out from the two upper window . what have be say or do ? nothing that might not have be perform or utter before the most decent , polite , or pious company . why then should Madam Esmond continue to blush , and the brave Colonel to look somewhat red , as he shake his young friend 's hand ? 
 the Colonel ask Mr. George if he have have good sport ? " no , " say George , curtly . " have you ? " and then he look at the picture of his father , which hang in the parlour . 
 the Colonel , not a talkative man ordinarily , straightway enter into a long description of his sport , and describe where he have be in the morning , and what wood he have hunt with the king 's officer ; how many bird they have shoot , and what game they have bring down . though not a jocular man ordinarily , the Colonel make a long description of Mr. Braddock 's heavy person and great boot , as he flounder through the virginian wood , hunting , as they call it , with a pack of dog gather from various house , with a pack of negro bark as loud as the dog , and actually shoot the deer when they come in sight of he . " Great God , sir ! " say Mr. Braddock , puff and blow , " what would Sir Robert have say in Norfolk , to see a man hunt with a fowling - piece in his hand , and a pack of dog actually lay on to a turkey ! " 
 " indeed , Colonel , you be vastly comical this afternoon ! " cry Madam Esmond , with a neat little laugh , whilst her son listen to the story , look more glum than ever . " what Sir Robert be there at Norfolk ? be he one of the newly arrive army - gentleman ? " 
 " the General mean Norfolk at home , madam , not Norfolk in Virginia , " say Colonel Washington . " Mr. Braddock have be talk of a visit to Sir Robert Walpole , who live in that county , and of the great hunt the old Minister keep there , and of his grand palace , and his picture at Houghton . I should like to see a good field and a good fox - chase at home well than any sight in the world , " the honest sportsman add with a sigh . 
 " nevertheless , there be good sport here , as I be say , " say young Esmond , with a sneer . 
 " what sport ? " cry the other , look at he . 
 " why , sure you know , without look at I so fiercely , and stamp your foot , as if you be go to charge I with the foil . be you not the good sportsman of the country - side ? be there not all the fish of the field , and the beast of the tree , and the fowl of the sea no the fish of the tree , and the beast of the sea and the bah ! you know what I mean . I mean shad , and salmon , and rock - fish , and roe - deer , and hog , and buffalo , and bison , and elephant , for what I know . I be no sportsman . " 
 " no , indeed , " say Mr. Washington , with a look of scarcely repress scorn . 
 " yes , I understand you . I be a milksop . I have be breed at my mamma 's knee . look at these pretty apron - string , Colonel ! who would not like to be tie to they ? see of what a charming colour they be ! I remember when they be black that be for my grandfather . " 
 " and who would not mourn for such a gentleman ? " say the Colonel , as the widow , surprised , look at her son . 
 " and , indeed , I wish my grandfather be here , and would resurge , as he promise to do on his tombstone ; and would bring my father , the Ensign , with he . " 
 " ah , Harry ! " cry Mrs. Esmond , burst into tear , as at this juncture her second son enter the room in just such another suit , gold - cord frock , braid waistcoat , silver - hilte sword , and solitaire , as that which his eld brother wear . " oh , Harry , Harry ! " cry Madam Esmond , and fly to her young son . 
 " what be it , mother ? " ask Harry , take she in his arm . " what be the matter , Colonel ? " 
 " upon my life , it would puzzle I to say , " answer the Colonel , bite his lip . 
 " a mere question , Hal , about pink ribbon , which I think vastly become to our mother ; as , no doubt , the Colonel do . " 
 " Sir , will you please to speak for yourself ? " cry the Colonel , bustle up , and then sink his voice again . 
 " he speak too much for himself , " weep the widow . 
 " I protest I do not   any more know the source of these tear , than the source of the Nile , " say George , " and if the picture of my father be to begin to cry , I should almost as much wonder at the paternal tear . what have I utter ? an allusion to ribbon ! be there some poison pin in they , which have be strike into my mother 's heart by a guilty fiend of a London mantua - maker ? I profess to wish to be lead in these lovely rein all my life long , " and he turn a pirouette on his scarlet heel . 
 " George Warrington ! what devil 's dance be you dance now ? " ask Harry , who love his mother , who love Mr. Washington , but who , of all creature , love and admire his brother George . 
 " my dear child , you do not understand dance you care not for the politer art you can get no more music out of a spinet than by pull a dead hog by the ear . by nature you be make for a man a man of war I do not mean a seventy - four , Colonel George , like that hulk which bring the hulking Mr. Braddock into our river . his Excellency , too , be a man of warlike turn , a follower of the sport of the field . I be a milksop , as I have have the honour to say . " 
 " you never show it yet . you beat that great Maryland man be twice your size , " break out Harry . 
 " under compulsion , Harry .
