' tis tuptu , my lad , or else ' tis tuptomai , as thy breech well know when we follow school . but I be of a quiet turn , and would never lift my hand to pull a trigger , no , nor a nose , nor anything but a rose , " and here he take and handle one of Madam Esmond 's bright pink apron ribbon . " I hate sporting , which you and the Colonel love , and I want to shoot nothing alive , not a turkey , nor a titmouse , nor an ox , nor an ass , nor anything that have ear . those curl of Mr. Washington 's be prettily powdered . " 
 the militia colonel , who have be offend by the first part of the talk , and very much puzzle by the last , have take a modest draught from the great china bowl of apple - toddy which stand to welcome the guest in this as in all virginian house , and be far cool himself by pace the balcony in a very stately manner . 
 again almost reconcile with the elder , the appeased mother stand give a hand to each of her son . George put his disengaged hand on Harry 's shoulder . " I say one thing , George , " say he with a flush face . 
 " say twenty thing , Don Enrico , " cry the other . 
 " if you be not fond of sporting and that , and do not   care for kill game and hunting , be clever than I , why shouldst thou not stop at home and be quiet , and let I go out with Colonel George and Mr. Braddock ? that be what I say , " say Harry , deliver himself of his speech . 
 the widow look eagerly from the dark - haired to the fair - haired boy . she know not from which she would like to part . 
 " one of our family must go because honneur oblige , and my name be number one , number one must go first , " say George . 
 " tell you so , " say poor Harry . 
 " one must stay , or who be to look after mother at home ? we can not afford to be both scalp by Indians or fricasseed by French . " 
 " fricasseed by French ! " cry Harry ; " the good troop of the world ! englishman ! I should like to see they fricasseed by the French ! what a mortal thrashing you will give they ! " and the brave lad sigh to think he should not be present at the battue . 
 George sate down to the harpsichord and play and sing " malbrouk s'en va - t - en guerre , Mironton , mironton , mirontaine , " at the sound of which music the gentleman from the balcony enter . " I be play ' God save the king , ' Colonel , in compliment to the new expedition . " 
 " I never know whether thou art laugh or in earnest , " say the simple gentleman , " but surely methink that be not the air . " 
 George perform ever so many trill and quaver upon his harpsichord , and their guest watch he , wonder , perhaps , that a gentleman of George 's condition could set himself to such an effeminate business . then the Colonel take out his watch , say that his Excellency 's coach would be here almost immediately , and ask leave to retire to his apartment , and put himself in a fit condition to appear before her ladyship 's company . 
 " Colonel Washington know the way to his room pretty well , " say George , from the harpsichord , look over his shoulder , but never offer to stir . 
 " let I show the Colonel to his chamber , " cry the widow , in great wrath , and sail out of the apartment , follow by the enraged and bewildered Colonel , as George continue crash among the key . her high - spirited guest feel himself insult , he could hardly say how ; he be outraged and he could not speak ; he be almost stifle with anger . 
 Harry Warrington remark their friend 's condition . " for heaven 's sake , George , what do this all mean ? " he ask his brother . " why should not   he kiss her hand ? "   George have just before fetch out his brother from their library , to watch this harmless salute .   " I tell you it be nothing but common kindness . " 
 " nothing but common kindness ! " shriek out George . " look at that , Hal ! be that common kindness ? " and he show his junior the unlucky paper over which he have be brood for some time . it be but a fragment , though the meaning be indeed clear without the precede text . 
 the paper commence : " ... be old than myself , but I , again , be old than my year ; and you know , dear brother , have ever be consider a sober person . all child be well for a father 's superintendence , and her two , I trust , will find in I a tender friend and guardian . " 
 " friend and guardian ! curse he ! " shriek out George , clench his fist and his brother read on : 
 " ... the flattering offer which General Braddock hath make I , will , of course , oblige I to postpone this matter until after the campaign . when we have give the French a sufficient drubbing , I shall return to repose under my own vine and fig - tree . " 
 " he mean Castlewood . these be his vine , " George cry again , shake his fist at the creeper sun themselves on the wall . 
 " ... under my own vine and fig - tree ; where I hope soon to present my dear brother to his new sister - in - law . she have a pretty scripture name , which be ... " and here the document end . 
 " which be Rachel , " George go on bitterly . " Rachel be by no mean weep for her child , and have every desire to be comfort . now , Harry ! let we upstairs at once , kneel down as become we , and say , ' dear papa , welcome to your house of Castlewood . ' " 




 CHAPTER IX . hospitality 

 his Excellency the Commander - in - chief set forth to pay his visit to Madam Esmond in such a state and splendour as become the first personage in all his Majesty 's colony , plantation , and possession of North America . his guard of dragoon precede he out of Williamsburg in the midst of an immense shouting and yelling of a loyal , and principally negro , population . the General ride in his own coach . Captain Talmadge , his Excellency 's Master of the Horse , attend he at the door of the ponderous emblazon vehicle , and rid by the side of the carriage during the journey from Williamsburg to Madam Esmond 's house . Major Danvers , aide - de - camp , sate in the front of the carriage with the little postmaster from Philadelphia , Mr. Franklin , who , printer 's boy as he have be , be a wonderful shrewd person , as his Excellency and the gentleman of his family be fain to acknowledge , have a quantity of the most curious information respect the colony , and regard England too , where Mr. Franklin have be more than once . " ' twas extraordinary how a person of such humble origin should have acquire such a variety of learning and such a politeness of breed too , Mr. Franklin ! " his Excellency be pleased to observe , touch his hat graciously to the postmaster . 
 the postmaster bow , say it have be his occasional good fortune to fall into the company of gentleman like his Excellency , and that he have take advantage of his opportunity to study their honours ' manner , and adapt himself to they as far as he might . as for education , he could not boast much of that his father be but in straiten circumstance , and the advantage small in his native country of New England : but he have do to the utmost of his power , and gather what he could he know nothing like what they have in England . 
 Mr. Braddock burst out laugh , and say , " as for education , there be gentleman of the army , by George , who do not   know whether they should spell bull with two b 's or one . he have hear the Duke of Marlborough be no special good penman . he have not the honour of serve under that noble commander his grace be before his time but he thrash the French soundly , although he be no scholar . " 
 Mr. Franklin say he be aware of both those fact . 
 " nor be my Duke a scholar , " go on Mr. Braddock " aha , Mr. Postmaster , you have hear that , too I see by the wink in your eye . " 
 Mr. Franklin instantly withdraw the obnoxious or satirical wink in his eye , and look in the General 's jolly round face with a pair of orb as innocent as a baby 's . " he be no scholar , but he be a match for any french general that ever swallow the English for fricassee de crapaud . he save the crown for the good of king , his royal father , his Most gracious Majesty King George . " 
 Off go Mr. Franklin 's hat , and from his large buckle wig escape a great halo of powder . 
 " he be the soldier 's good friend , and have be the uncompromise enemy of all beggarly red - shank Scotch rebel and intrigue Romish Jesuits who would take our liberty from we , and our religion , by George . his Royal Highness , my gracious master , be not a scholar neither , but he be one of the fine gentleman in the world . " 
 " I have see his Royal Highness on horseback , at a review of the Guards , in Hyde Park , " say Mr. Franklin . " the Duke be indeed a very fine gentleman on horseback . " 
 " you shall drink his health to - day , Postmaster . he be the good of master , the good of friend , the good of son to his royal old father ; the good of gentleman that ever wear an epaulet . " 
 " epaulet be quite out of my way , sir , " say Mr. Franklin , laugh . " you know I live in a Quaker City . " 
 " of course they be out of your way , my good friend . every man to his business . you , and gentleman of your class , to your book , and welcome . we do not   forbid you ; we encourage you . we , to fight the enemy and govern the country . hey , gentleman ? Lord ! what road you have in this colony , and how this confound coach plunge ! who have we here , with the two negro boy in livery ? he rid a good gelding . " 
 " it be Mr. Washington , " say the aide - de - camp . 
 " I would like he for a corporal of the Horse Grenadiers , " say the General . " he have a good figure on a horse . he know the country too , Mr. Franklin . " 
 " yes , indeed . " 
 " and be a monstrous genteel young man , consider the opportunity he have have . I should have think he have the polish of Europe , by George I should . " 
 " he do his good , " say Mr. Franklin , look innocently at the stout chief , the exemplar of english elegance , who sit swag from one side to the other of the carriage , his face as scarlet as his coat swear at every other word ; ignorant on every point off parade , except the merit of a bottle and the look of a woman ; not of high birth , yet absurdly proud of his no - ancestry ; brave as a bulldog ; savage , lustful , prodigal , generous ; gentle in soft mood ; easy of love and laughter ; dull of wit ; utterly unread ; believe his country the first in the world , and he as good a gentleman as any in it . " yes , he be mighty well for a provincial , upon my word . he be beat at Fort What - d'ye - call - um last year , down by the Thingamy river . what be the name on't , Talmadge ? " 
 " the Lord know , sir , " say Talmadge ; " and I dare say the Postmaster , too , who be laugh at we both . " 
 " oh , Captain ! " 
 " be catch in a regular trap . he have only militia and Indians with he . good day , Mr. Washington . a pretty nag , sir . that be your first affair , last year ? " 
 " that at Fort Necessity ? yes , sir , " say the gentleman , gravely salute , as he ride up , follow by a couple of natty negro groom , in smart livery - coat and velvet hunting - cap . " I begin ill , sir , never have be in action until that unlucky day . " 
 " you be all raw levy , my good fellow . you should have see our militia run from the Scotch , and be curse to they . you should have have some troop with you . " 
 " your Excellency know ' tis my passionate desire to see and serve with they , " say Mr. Washington . 
 " by George , we shall try and gratify you , sir , " say the General , with one of his usual huge oath ; and on the heavy carriage roll towards Castlewood ; Mr. Washington ask leave to gallop on ahead , in order to announce his Excellency 's speedy arrival to the lady there . 
 the progress of the Commander - in - Chief be so slow , that several humbler person who be invite to meet his Excellency come up with his carriage , and , not like to pass the great man on the road , form quite a procession in the dusty wake of his chariot - wheel . first come Mr. Dinwiddie , the Lieutenant - Governor of his Majesty 's province , attend by his negro servant , and in company of Parson Broadbent , the jolly Williamsburg chaplain . these be presently join by little Mr. Dempster , the young gentleman 's schoolmaster , in his great Ramillies wig , which he keep for occasion of state . Anon appear Mr. Laws , the judge of the court , with Madam Laws on a pillion behind he , and their negro man carry a box contain her ladyship 's cap , and bestride a mule . the procession look so ludicrous , that Major Danvers and Mr. Franklin espy it , laugh outright , though not so loud as to disturb his Excellency , who be asleep by this time , bid the whole of this queer rearguard move on , and leave the Commander - in - Chief and his escort of dragoon to follow at their leisure . there be room for all at Castlewood when they come . there be meat , drink , and the good tobacco for his Majesty 's soldier ; and laughing and jollity for the negro ; and a plenteous welcome for their master . 
 the honest General require to be help to most dish at the table , and more than once , and be for ever hold out his glass for drink ; Nathan 's sangaree he pronounce to be excellent , and have drink largely of it on arrive before dinner . there be cider , ale , brandy , and plenty of good Bordeaux wine , some which Colonel Esmond himself have bring home with he to the colony , and which be fit for ponteeficis coeni , say little Mr. Dempster , with a wink to Mr. Broadbent , the clergyman of the adjoining parish . Mr. Broadbent return the wink and nod , and drink the wine without care about the Latin , as why should he , never have hitherto trouble himself about the language ? Mr. Broadbent be a gambling , guzzling , cock - fight divine , who have pass much time in the Fleet Prison , at Newmarket , at Hockley - in - the - Hole ; and have go of all sort of errand for his friend , Lord Cingbars , Lord Ringwood 's son   my Lady Cingbars 's waiting - woman be Mr. B. 's mother I dare say the modern reader have good not be too particular regard Mr. Broadbent 's father 's pedigree , have be of late send out to a church - living in Virginia . he and young George have fight many a match of cock together , take many a roe in company , haul in countless quantity of shad and salmon , slain wild geese and wild swan , pigeon and plover , and destroy myriad of canvas - back duck . it be say by the envious that Broadbent be the midnight poacher on whom Mr. Washington set his dog , and whom he can by the river - side at Mount Vernon . the fellow get away from his captor 's grip , and scramble to his boat in the dark ; but Broadbent be lay up for two sunday afterwards , and when he come abroad again have the evident remain of a black eye and a new collar to his coat . all the game at the card have George Esmond and Parson Broadbent play together , besides hunt all the bird in the air , the beast in the forest , and the fish of the sea . indeed , when the boy ride together to get their reading with Mr. Dempster , I suspect that Harry stay behind and take lesson from the other professor of european learning and accomplishment , George go his own way , read his own book , and , of course , tell no tale of his young brother . 
 all the bird of the Virginia air , and all the fish of the sea in season be here lay on Madam Esmond 's board to feed his Excellency and the rest of the english and american gentleman . the gumbo be declare to be perfection   young Mr. George 's black servant be name after this dish , be discover behind the door with his head in a bowl of this delicious hotch - potch , by the late Colonel , and grimly christen on the spot , the shad be rich and fresh , the stew terrapin be worthy of London aldermen   before George , he would like the Duke himself to taste they , his Excellency deign to say , and indeed , stew terrapin be worthy of any duke or even emperor . the negro - woman have a genius for cookery , and in Castlewood kitchen there be adept in the art bring up under the keen eye of the late and the present Madam Esmond . certain of the dish , especially the sweet and flan , Madam Esmond prepare herself with great neatness and dexterity ; carve several of the principal piece , as the kindly cumbrous fashion of the day be , put up the laced lappet of her sleeve , and show the prettiest round arm and small hand and wrist as she perform this ancient rite of a hospitality not so languid as ours . the old law of the table be that the mistress be to press her guest with a decent eagerness , to watch and see whom she could encourage to far enjoyment , to know culinary anatomic secret , and execute carving operation upon fowl , fish , game , joint of meat , and so forth ; to cheer her guest to fresh effort , to whisper her neighbour , Mr. Braddock " I have keep for your Excellency the jowl of this salmon . I will take no denial ! Mr. Franklin , you drink only water , sir , though our cellar have wholesome wine which give no headache . Mr. Justice , you love woodcock pie ? " 
 " because I know who make the pastry , " say Mr. Laws , the judge , with a profound bow . " I wish , madam , we have such a happy knack of pastry at home as you have at Castlewood . I often say to my wife , ' my dear , I wish you have Madam Esmond 's hand . ' " 
 " it be a very pretty hand ; I be sure other would like it too , " say Mr. Postmaster of Boston , at which remark Mr. Esmond look but half - pleased at the little gentleman . 
 " such a hand for a light pie - crust , " continue the Judge , " and my service to you , madam . " and he think the widow can not but be propitiate by this compliment . she say simply that she have lesson when she be at home in England for her education , and that there be certain dish which her mother teach she to make , and which her father and son both like . she be very glad if they please her company . More such remark follow : more dish ; ten time as much meat as be needful for the company . Mr. Washington do not embark in the general conversation much , but he and Mr. Talmadge , and Major Danvers , and the Postmaster , be deep in talk about road , river , conveyance , sumpter - horse and artillery train ; and the provincial militia Colonel have bit of bread lay at interval on the table before he , and station mark out , on which he have his finger , and regard which he be talk to his brother aide - de - camp , till a negro servant , change the course , brush off the Potomac with a napkin , and sweep up the Ohio in a spoon . 
 at the end of dinner , Mr. Broadbent leave his place and walk up behind the Lieutenant - Governor 's chair , where he say grace , return to his seat and resume his knife and fork when this work of devotion be over . and now the sweet and pudding be come , of which I can give you a list , if you like ; but what young lady care for the pudding of to - day , much more for those which be eat a hundred year ago , and which Madam Esmond have prepare for her guest with so much neatness and skill ? then , the table be clear , Nathan , her chief manager , lay a glass to every person , and fill his mistress 's . bow to the company , she say she drink but one toast , but know how heartily all the gentleman present will join she . then she call , " his Majesty , " bow to Mr. Braddock , who with his aide - de - camp and the colonial gentleman all loyally repeat the name of their beloved and gracious Sovereign . and hereupon , have drink her glass of wine and salute all the company , the widow retire between a row of negro servant , perform one of her very handsome curtsy at the door . 
 the kind mistress of Castlewood bear her part in the entertainment with admirable spirit , and look so gay and handsome , and speak with such cheerfulness and courage to all her company , that the few lady who be present at the dinner could not but congratulate Madam Esmond upon the elegance of the feast , and especially upon her manner of preside at it . but they be scarcely get to her drawing - room when her artificial courage fail she , and she burst into tear on the sofa by Mrs. Laws ' side , just in the midst of a compliment from that lady . " ah , madam ! " she say , " it may be an honour , as you say , to have the King 's representative in my house , and our family have receive great personage than Mr. Braddock . but he come to take one of my son away from I . who know whether my boy will return , or how ? I dream of he last night as wound , and quite white , with blood stream from his side . I would not be so ill - mannered as to let my grief be visible before the gentleman ; but , my good Mrs. Justice , who have part with child , and who have a mother 's heart of her own , would like I none the well , if mine be very easy this evening . " 
 the lady administer such consolation as seem proper or palatable to their hostess , who try not to give way far to her melancholy , and remember that she have other duty to perform , before yield to her own sad mood . " it will be time enough , madam , to be sorry when they be go , " she say to the Justice 's wife , her good neighbour . " my boy must not see I follow he with a wistful face , and have our parting make more dismal by my weakness . it be good that gentleman of his rank and station should show themselves where their country call they . that have always be the way of the Esmonds , and the same power which graciously preserve my dear father through twenty great battle in the Queen 's time , I trust and pray , will watch over my son now his turn be come to do his duty . " and , now , instead of lament her fate , or far allude to it , I dare say the resolute lady sate down with her female friend to a pool of card and a dish of coffee , whilst the gentleman remain in the neighbouring parlour , still call their toast and drink their wine . when one lady object that these latter be sit rather long , Madam Esmond say : " it would improve and amuse the boy to be with the english gentleman . such society be very rarely to be have in their distant province , and though their conversation sometimes be free , she be sure that gentleman and man of fashion would have regard to the youth of her son , and say nothing before they which young people should not hear . " 
 it be evident that the english gentleman relish the good cheer provide for they . whilst the lady be yet at their card , Nathan come in and whisper Mrs. Mountain , who at first cry out " no ! she would give no more the common Bordeaux they might have , and welcome , if they still want more but she would not give any more of the Colonel 's . " it appear that the dozen bottle of particular claret have be already drunk up by the gentleman , " besides ale , cider , Burgundy , Lisbon , and Madeira , " say Mrs. Mountain , enumerate the supply . 
 but Madam Esmond be for have no stint in the hospitality of the night . Mrs. Mountain be fain to bustle away with her key to the sacred vault where the Colonel 's particular Bordeaux lay , survive its master , who , too , have long pass underground . as they go on their journey , Mrs. Mountain ask whether any of the gentleman have have too much ? Nathan think Mister Broadbent be tipsy he always tipsy ; be then think the General gentleman be tipsy ; and he think Master George be a lilly drunk . 
 " Master George ! " cry Mrs. Mountain : " why , he will sit for day without touch a drop . " 
 nevertheless , Nathan persist in his notion that Master George be a lilly drunk . he be always fill his glass , he have talk , he have sing , he have cut joke , especially against Mr. Washington , which make Mr. Washington quite red and angry , Nathan say . " well , well ! " Mrs. Mountain cry eagerly ; " it be right a gentleman should make himself merry in good company , and pass the bottle along with his friend . " and she trot to the particular Bordeaux cellar with only the more alacrity . 
 the tone of freedom and almost impertinence which young George Esmond have adopt of late day towards Mr. Washington have very deeply vex and annoy that gentleman . there be scarce half a dozen year ' difference of age between he and the Castlewood twin ; but Mr. Washington have always be remark for a discretion and sobriety much beyond his time of life , whilst the boy of Castlewood seem young than theirs . they have always be till now under their mother 's anxious tutelage , and have look up to their neighbour of Mount Vernon as their guide , director , friend as , indeed , almost everybody seem to do who come in contact with the simple and upright young man . himself of the most scrupulous gravity and good breeding , in his communication with other folk he appear to exact , or , at any rate , to occasion , the same behaviour . his nature be above levity and joke : they seem out of place when address to he . he be slow of comprehend they : and they slunk as it be abash out of his society . " he always seem great to I , " say Harry Warrington , in one of his letter many year after the date of which we be write ; " and I never think of he otherwise than of a hero . when he come over to Castlewood and teach we boy survey , to see he rid to hound be as if he be charge an army . if he fire a shot , I think the bird must come down , and if be fling a net , the large fish in the river be sure to be in it . his word be always few , but they be always wise ; they be not idle , as our word be , they be grave , sober , and strong , and ready on occasion to do their duty . in spite of his antipathy to he , my brother respect and admire the General as much as I do that be to say , more than any mortal man . " 
 Mr. Washington be the first to leave the jovial party which be do so much honour to Madam Esmond 's hospitality . Young George Esmond , who have take his mother 's place when she leave it , have be free with the glass and with the tongue . he have say a score of thing to his guest which wound and chafe the latter , and to which Mr. Washington could give no reply . angry beyond all endurance , he leave the table at length , and walk away through the open window into the broad verandah or porch which belong to Castlewood as to all virginian house . 
 here Madam Esmond catch sight of her friend 's tall frame as it stride up and down before the window ; and , the evening be warm , or her game over , she give up her card to one of the other lady , and join her good neighbour out of door . he try to compose his countenance as well as he could : it be impossible that he should explain to his hostess why and with whom he be angry . 
 " the gentleman be long over their wine , " she say ; " gentleman of the army be always fond of it . " 
 " if drinking make good soldier , some yonder be distinguish themselves greatly , madam , " say Mr. Washington . 
 " and I dare say the General be at the head of his troop ? " 
 " no doubt , no doubt , " answer the Colonel , who always receive this lady 's remark , playful or serious , with a peculiar softness and kindness . " but the General be the General , and it be not for I to make remark on his Excellency 's doing at table or elsewhere . I think very likely that military gentleman bear and breed at home be different from we of the colony . we have such a hot sun , that we need not wine to fire our blood as they do . and drinking toast seem a point of honour with they . talmadge hiccup to I I should say , whisper to I just now , that an officer could no more refuse a toast than a challenge , and he say that it be after the great difficulty and dislike at first that he learn to drink . he have certainly overcome his difficulty with uncommon resolution . " 
 " what , I wonder , can you talk of for so many hour ? " ask the lady . 
 " I do not   think I can tell you all we talk of , madam , and I must not tell tale out of school . we talk about the war , and of the force Mr. Contrecoeur have , and how we be to get at he . the General be for make the campaign in his coach , and make light of it and the enemy . that we shall beat they , if we meet they , I trust there be no doubt . " 
 " how can there be ? " say the lady , whose father have serve under Marlborough . 
 " Mr. Franklin , though he be only from New England , " continue the gentleman , ' speak great good sense , and would have speak more if the english gentleman would let he ; but they reply invariably that we be only raw provincial , and do not   know what discipline british troop can do . have they not well hasten forwards and make turnpike road and have comfortable inn ready for his Excellency at the end of the day 's march ? ' there be some sort of inn , I suppose , ' say Mr. Danvers , ' not so comfortable as we have in England : we can not   expect that . ' ' no , you can not   expect that , ' say Mr. Franklin , who seem a very shrewd and facetious person . he drink his water , and seem to laugh at the Englishmen , though I doubt whether it be fair for a water - drinker to sit by and spy out the weakness of gentleman over their wine . " 
 " and my boy ? I hope they be prudent ? " say the widow , lay her hand on her guest 's arm . " Harry promise I , and when he give his word , I can trust he for anything . George be always moderate . why do you look so grave ? " 
 " indeed , to be frank with you , I do not know what have come over George in these last day , " say Mr. Washington . " he have some grievance against I which I do not understand , and of which I do not   care to ask the reason . he speak to I before the gentleman in a way which scarcely become he . we be go the campaign together , and ' tis a pity we begin such ill friend . " 
 " he have be ill . he be always wild and wayward , and hard to understand . but he have the most affectionate heart in the world . you will bear with he , you will protect he promise I you will . " 
 " dear lady , I will do so with my life , " Mr. Washington say with great fervour . " you know I would lay it down cheerfully for you or any you love . " 
 " and my father 's blessing and mine go with you , dear friend ! " cry the widow , full of thank and affection . 
 as they pursue their conversation , they have quit the porch under which they have first begin to talk , and where they could hear the laughter and toast of the gentleman over their wine , and be pace a walk on the rough lawn before the house . young George Warrington , from his place at the head of the table in the dining - room , could see the pair as they pass to and fro , and have listen for some time past , and reply in a very distracted manner to the remark of the gentleman round about he , who be too much engage with their own talk and joke , and drinking , to pay much attention to their young host 's behaviour . Mr. Braddock love a song after dinner , and Mr. Danvers , his aide - de - camp , who have a fine tenor voice , be delight his General with the late ditty from Marybone Gardens , when George Warrington , jump up , run towards the window , and then return and pull his brother Harry by the sleeve , who sate with his back towards the window . 
 " what be it ? " say Harry , who , for his part , be charm , too , with the song and chorus . 
 " come , " cry George , with a stamp of his foot , and the young follow obediently . 
 " what be it ? " continue George , with a bitter oath . " do not   you see what it be ? they be bill and coo this morning ; they be bill and coo now before go to roost . have we not well both go into the garden , and pay our duty to our mamma and papa ? " and he point to Mr. Washington , who be take the widow 's hand very tenderly in his . 




 CHAPTER X. a Hot Afternoon 

 General Braddock and the other guest of Castlewood be duly consign to their respective quarter , the boy retire to their own room , and there pour out to one another their opinion respect the great event of the day . they would not bear such a marriage no . be the representative of the Marquises of Esmond to marry the young son of a colonial family , who have be breed up as a land - surveyor ? Castlewood , and the boy at nineteen year of age , hand over to the tender mercy of a stepfather of three - and - twenty ! oh , it be monstrous ! Harry be for go straightway to his mother in her bedroom where her black maiden be divest her ladyship of the simple jewel and finery which she have assume in compliment to the feast protest against the odious match , and announce that they would go home , live upon their little property there , and leave she for ever , if the unnatural union take place . 
 George advocate another way of stop it , and explain his plan to his admire brother . " our mother , " he say , " can not   marry a man with whom one or both of we have be out on the field , and who have wound we or kill we , or whom we have wound or kill . we must have he out , Harry . " 
 Harry see the profound truth convey in George 's statement , and admire his brother 's immense sagacity . " no , George , " say he , " you be right . mother can not   marry our murderer ; she will not   be as bad as that . and if we pink he he be do for . ' cadit quaestio , ' as Mr. Dempster use to say . shall I send my boy with a challenge to Colonel George now ? " 
 " my dear Harry , " the elder reply , think with some complacency of his affair of honour at Quebec , " you be not accustomed to affair of this sort . " 
 " no , " own Harry , with a sigh , look with envy and admiration on his senior . 
 " we can not   insult a gentleman in our own house , " continue George , with great majesty ; " the law of honour forbid such inhospitable treatment . but , sir , we can ride out with he , and , as soon as the park gate be closed , we can tell he our mind . " 
 " that we can , by George ! " cry Harry , grasp his brother 's hand , " and that we will , too . I say , Georgy ... " here the lad 's face become very red , and his brother ask he what he would say ? 
 " this be my turn , brother , " Harry plead . " if you go the campaign , I ought to have the other affair . indeed , indeed , I ought . " and he pray for this bit of promotion . 
 " again the head of the house must take the lead , my dear , " George say , with a superb air . " if I fall , my Harry will avenge I . but I must fight George Washington , Hal : and ' tis good I should ; for , indeed , I hate he the bad . be it not he who counsel my mother to order that wretch , Ward , to lay hand on I ? " 
 " ah , George , " interpose the more pacable young brother , " you ought to forget and forgive . " 
 " Forgive ? never , sir , as long as I remember . you can not   order remembrance out of a man 's mind ; and a wrong that be a wrong yesterday must be a wrong to - morrow . I never , of my knowledge , do one to any man , and I never will suffer one , if I can help it . I think very ill of Mr. Ward , but I do not   think so badly of he as to suppose he will ever forgive thee that blow with the ruler . Colonel Washington be our enemy , mine especially . he have advise one wrong against I , and he meditate a great . I tell you , brother , we must punish he . " 
 the grandsire 's old Bordeaux have set George 's ordinarily pale countenance into a flame . Harry , his brother 's fond worshipper , could not but admire George 's haughty bearing and rapid declamation , and prepare himself , with his usual docility , to follow his chief . so the boy go to their bed , the eld convey special injunction to his junior to be civil to all the guest so long as they remain under the maternal roof on the morrow . 
 good manner and a repugnance to tell tale out of school , forbid we from say which of Madam Esmond 's guest be the first to fall under the weight of her hospitality . the respectable descendant of Messrs. Talmadge and Danvers , aide - de - camp to his Excellency , might not care to hear how their ancestor be intoxicate a hundred year ago ; and yet the gentleman themselves take no shame in the fact , and there be little doubt they or their comrade be tipsy twice or thrice in the week . let we fancy they reel to bed , support by sympathise negro ; and their vinous General , too stout a toper to have surrender himself to a half - dozen bottle of Bordeaux , conduct to his chamber by the young gentleman of the house , and speedily sleep the sleep which friendly Bacchus give . the good lady of Castlewood see the condition of her guest without the least surprise or horror ; and be up early in the morning , provide cool drink for their hot palate , which the servant carry to their respective chamber . at breakfast , one of the english officer rally Mr. Franklin , who take no wine at all , and therefore refuse the morning cool draught of toddy , by show how the Philadelphia gentleman lose two pleasure , the drink and the toddy . the young fellow say the disease be pleasant and the remedy delicious , and laughingly propose to continue repeat they both . the General 's new american aide - de - camp , Colonel Washington , be quite sober and serene . the british officer vow they must take he in hand , and teach he what the way of the english army be ; but the virginian gentleman gravely say he do not care to learn that part of the english military education . 
 the widow , occupy as she have be with the care of a great dinner , follow by a great breakfast on the morning ensuing , have scarce leisure to remark the behaviour of her son very closely , but at least see that George be scrupulously polite to her favourite , Colonel Washington , as to all the other guest of the house . 
 before Mr. Braddock take his leave , he have a private audience of Madam Esmond , in which his Excellency formally offer to take her son into his family ; and when the arrangement for George 's departure be settle between his mother and future chief , Madam Esmond , though she might feel they , do not show any squeamish terror about the danger of the bottle , which she see be amongst the severest and most certain which her son would have to face . she know her boy must take his part in the world , and encounter his portion of evil and good . " Mr. Braddock be a perfect fine gentleman in the morning , " she say stoutly to her aide - de - camp , Mrs. Mountain ; " and though my papa do not drink , ' tis certain that many of the good company in England do . " the jolly General good - naturedly shake hand with George , who present himself to his Excellency after the maternal interview be over , and bade George welcome , and to be in attendance at Frederick three day hence ; shortly after which time the expedition would set forth . 
 and now the great coach be again call into requisition , the General 's escort prance round it , the other guest and their servant go to horse . the lady of Castlewood attend his Excellency to the step of the verandah in front of her house , the young gentleman follow , and stand on each side of his coach - door . the guard trumpeter blow a shrill blast , the negro shout " Huzzay , and God sabe de King , " as Mr. Braddock most graciously take leave of his hospitable entertainer , and roll away on his road to headquarters . 
 as the boy go up the step , there be the Colonel once more take leave of their mother . no doubt she have be once more recommend George to his namesake 's care ; for Colonel Washington say : " with my life . you may depend on I , " as the lad return to their mother and the few guest still remain in the porch . the Colonel be boot and ready to depart . " farewell , my dear Harry , " he say . " with you , George , ' tis no adieu . we shall meet in three day at the camp . " 
 both the young man be go to danger , perhaps to death . Colonel Washington be take leave of she , and she be to see he no more before the campaign . no wonder the widow be very much move . 
 George Warrington watch his mother 's emotion , and interpret it with a pang of malignant scorn . " stay yet a moment , and console our mamma , " he say with a steady countenance , " only the time to get ourselves boot , and my brother and I will ride with you a little way , George . " George Warrington have already order his horse . the three young man be speedily under way , their negro groom behind they , and Mrs. Mountain , who know she have make mischief between they and tremble for the result , feel a vast relief that Mr. Washington be go without a quarrel with the brother , without , at any rate , an open declaration of love to their mother . 
 no man could be more courteous in demeanour than George Warrington to his neighbour and namesake , the Colonel . the latter be pleased and surprised at his young friend 's altered behaviour . the community of danger , the necessity of future fellowship , the soften influence of the long friendship which bind he to the Esmond family , the tender adieux which have just pass between he and the mistress of Castlewood , incline the Colonel to forget the unpleasantness of the past day , and make he more than usually friendly with his young companion . George be quite gay and easy : it be Harry who be melancholy now : he ride silently and wistfully by his brother , keep away from Colonel Washington , to whose side he use always to press eagerly before . if the honest Colonel remark his young friend 's conduct , no doubt he attribute it to Harry 's know affection for his brother , and his natural anxiety to be with George now the day of their parting be so near . 
 they talk far about the war , and the probable end of the campaign : none of the three doubt its successful termination . two thousand veteran british troop with their commander must get the well of any force the French could bring against they , if only they move in decent time . the ardent young virginian soldier have an immense respect for the experienced valour and tactic of the regular troop . King George II . have no more loyal subject than Mr. Braddock 's new aide - de - camp . 
 so the party ride amicably together , until they reach a certain rude log - house , call Benson 's , of which the proprietor , accord to the custom of the day and country , do not disdain to accept money from his guest in return for hospitality provide . there be a recruiting station here , and some officer and man of Halkett 's regiment assemble , and here Colonel Washington suppose that his young friend would take leave of he . 
 whilst their horse be bait , they enter the public room , and find a rough meal prepare for such as be disposed to partake . George Warrington enter the place with a particularly gay and lively air , whereas poor Harry 's face be quite white and woebegone . 
 " one would think , Squire Harry , ' twa you who be go to leave home and fight the French and Indians , and not Mr. George , " say Benson . 
 " I may be alarmed about danger to my brother , " say Harry , " though I might bear my own share pretty well . ' tis not my fault that I stay at home . " 
 " no , indeed , brother , " cry George . 
 " Harry Warrington 's courage do not need any proof ! " cry Mr. Washington . 
 " you do the family honour by speak so well of we , Colonel , " say Mr. George , with a low bow . " I dare say we can hold our own , if need be . " 
 whilst his friend be vaunt his courage , Harry look , to say the truth , by no mean courageous . as his eye meet his brother 's , he read in George 's look an announcement which alarm the fond faithful lad . " you be not go to do it now ? " he whisper his brother . 
 " yes , now , " say Mr. George , very steadily . 
 " for God 's sake , let I have the turn . you be go on the campaign , you ought not to have everything and there may be an explanation , George . we may be all wrong . " 
 " Psha , how can we ? it must be do now do not   be alarm . no name shall be mention I shall easily find a subject . " 
 a couple of Halkett 's officer , whom our young gentleman know , be sit under the porch , with the Virginian toddy - bowl before they . 
 " what be you conspire , gentleman ? " cry one of they . " be it a drink ? " 
 by the tone of their voice and their flushed cheek , it be clear the gentleman have already be engage in drink that morning . 
 " the very thing , sir , " George say gaily . " fresh glass , Mr. Benson ! what , no glass ? then we must have at the bowl . " 
 " many a good man have drunk from it , " say Mr. Benson ; and the lad one after another , and bow first to their military acquaintance , touch the bowl with their lip . the liquor do not seem to be much diminish for the boy ' drinking , though George especially give himself a toper 's air , and protest it be delicious after their ride . he call out to Colonel Washington , who be at the porch , to join his friend , and drink . 
 the lad 's tone be offensive , and resemble the manner lately adopt by he , and which have so much chafe Mr. Washington . he bow , and say he be not thirsty . 
 " Nay , the liquor be pay for , " say George ; " never fear , Colonel . " 
 " I say I be not thirsty . I do not say the liquor be not pay for , " say the young Colonel , drum with his foot . 
 " when the King 's health be propose , an officer can hardly say no . I drink the health of his Majesty , gentleman , " cry George . " Colonel Washington can drink it or leave it . the king ! " 
 this be a point of military honour . the two british officer of Halkett 's , Captain Grace and Mr. Waring , both drink " the king . " Harry Warrington drink " the king . " Colonel Washington , with glare eye , gulp , too , a slight draught from the bowl . 
 then Captain Grace propose " the Duke and the Army , " which toast there be likewise no gainsaying . Colonel Washington have to swallow " the Duke and the Army . " 
 " you do not   seem to stomach the toast , Colonel , " say George . 
 " I tell you again , I do not   want to drink , " reply the Colonel . " it seem to I the Duke and the Army would be serve all the well if their health be not drunk so often . " 
 " you be not up to the way of regular troop as yet , " say Captain Grace , with rather a thick voice . 
 " may be not , sir . " 
 " a british officer , " continue Captain Grace , with great energy but doubtful articulation , " never neglect a toast of that sort , nor any other duty . a man who refuse to drink the health of the Duke hang I , such a man should be try by a court - martial ! " 
 " what mean this language to I ? you be drunk , sir ! " roar Colonel Washington , jump up , and strike the table with his fist . 
 " a cursed provincial officer say I be drunk ! " shriek out Captain Grace . " Waring , do you hear that ? " 
 " I hear it , sir ! " cry George Warrington . " we all hear it . he enter at my invitation the liquor call for be mine : the table be mine and I be shocked to hear such monstrous language use at it as Colonel Washington have just employ towards my esteemed guest , Captain Waring . " 
 " confound your impudence , you infernal young jackanape ! " bellow out Colonel Washington . " you dare to insult I before british officer , and find fault with my language ? for month past , I have bear with such impudence from you , that if I have not love your mother yes , sir , and your good grandfather and your brother I would I would " here his word fail he , and the irate Colonel , with glare eye and purple face , and every limb quiver with wrath , stand for a moment speechless before his young enemy . 
 " you would what , sir ? " say George , very quietly , " if you do not love my grandfather , and my brother , and my mother . you be make her petticoat a plea for some conduct of yours you would do what , sir , may I ask again ? " 
 " I would put you across my knee and whip you , you snarl little puppy , that be what I would do ! " cry the Colonel , who have find breath by this time , and vent another explosion of fury . 
 " because you have know we all our life , and make our house your own , that be no reason you should insult either of we ! " here cry Harry , start up . " what you have say , George Washington , be an insult to I and my brother alike . you will ask pardon , sir ! " 
 " Pardon ? " 
 " or give we the reparation that be due to gentleman , " continue Harry . 
 the stout Colonel 's heart smite he to think that he should be at mortal quarrel or call upon to shed the blood of one of the lad he love . as Harry stand face he , with his fair hair , flush cheek , and quiver voice , an immense tenderness and kindness fill the bosom of the eld man . " I I be bewilder , " he say . " my word , perhaps , be very hasty . what have be the meaning of George 's behaviour to I for month back ? only tell I , and , perhaps " 
 the evil spirit be awake and victorious in young George Warrington : his black eye shoot out scorn and hatred at the simple and guileless gentleman before he . " you be shirk from the question , sir , as you do from the toast just now , " he say . " I be not a boy to suffer under your arrogance . you have publicly insult I in a public place , and I demand a reparation . " 
 " in Heaven 's name , be it ! " say Mr. Washington , with the deep grief in his face . 
 " and you have insult I , " continue Captain Grace , reel towards he . " what be it he say ? confound the militia captain colonel , what be he ? you have   insult I ! oh , Waring ! to think I should be insult by a captain of militia ! " and tear bedew the noble Captain 's cheek as this harrowing thought cross his mind . 
 " I insult you , you hog ! " the Colonel again yell out , for he be little affect by humour , and have no disposition to laugh as the other have at the scene . and , behold , at this minute a fourth adversary be upon he . 
 " Great Powers , sir ! " say Captain Waring , " be three affair not enough for you , and must I come into the quarrel , too ? you have a quarrel with these two young gentleman . " 
 " Hasty word , sir ! " cry poor Harry once more . 
 " Hasty word , sir ! " cry Captain Waring . " a gentleman tell another gentleman that he will put he across his knee and whip he , and you call those hasty word ? let I tell you if any man be to say to I , ' Charles Waring , ' or ' Captain Waring , I will put you across my knee and whip you , ' I would say , ' I will drive my cheese - toaster through his body , ' if he be as big as Goliath , I would . that be one affair with young Mr. George Warrington . Mr. Harry , of course , as a young man of spirit , will stand by his brother . that be two . between Grace and the Colonel apology be impossible . and , now run I through the body ! you call an officer of my regiment of Halkett 's , sir ! a hog before my face ! great heaven , sir ! Mr. Washington , be you all like this in Virginia ? excuse I , I would use no offensive personality , as , by George ! I will suffer none from any man ! but , by Gad , Colonel ! give I leave to tell you that you be the most quarrelsome man I ever see in my life . call a disabled officer of my regiment for he be disabled , be not   you , Grace ? call he a hog before I ! you withdraw it , sir you withdraw it ? " 
 " be this some infernal conspiracy in which you be all league against I ? " shout the Colonel . " it would seem as if I be drunk , and not you , as you all be . I withdraw nothing . I apologise for nothing . by heaven ! I will meet one or half a dozen of you in your turn , young or old , drunk or sober . " 
 " I do not wish to hear myself call more name , " cry Mr. George Warrington . " this affair can proceed , sir , without any further insult on your part . when will it please you to give I the meeting ? " 
 " the soon the well , sir ! " say the Colonel , fume with rage . 
 " the soon the well , " hiccupped Captain Grace , with many oath needless to print   in those day , oath be the customary garnish of all gentleman 's conversation   and he rise staggering from his seat , and reel towards his sword , which he have lay by the door , and fall as he reach the weapon . " the soon the well ! " the poor tipsy wretch again cry out from the ground , wave his weapon and knock his own hat over his eye . 
 " at any rate , this gentleman 's business will keep cool till to - morrow , " the militia Colonel say , turn to the other king 's officer . " you will hardly bring your man out to - day , Captain Waring ? " 
 " I confess that neither his hand nor mine be particularly steady , " say Waring . 
 " mine be ! " cry Mr. Warrington , glare at his enemy . 
 his comrade of former day be as hot and as savage . " be it so with what weapon , sir ? " Washington say sternly . 
 " not with small - sword , Colonel . we can beat you with they . you know that from our old bout . pistol have well be the word . " 
 " as you please , George Warrington and God forgive you , George ! God pardon you , Harry ! for bring I into this quarrel , " say the Colonel , with a face full of sadness and gloom . 
 Harry hang his head , but George continue with perfect calmness : " I , sir ? it be not I who call name , who talk of a cane , who insult a gentleman in a public place before gentleman of the army . it be not the first time you have choose to take I for a negro , and talk of the whip for I . " 
 the Colonel start back , turn very red , and as if strike by a sudden remembrance . 
 " great heaven , George ! be it that boyish quarrel you be still recall ? " 
 " who make you the overseer of Castlewood ? " say the boy , grind his tooth . " I be not your slave , George Washington , and I never will be . I hat you then , and I hate you now . and you have insult I , and I be a gentleman , and so be you . be that not enough ? " 
 " too much , only too much , " say the Colonel , with a genuine grief on his face , and at his heart . " do you bear malice too , Harry ? I have not think this of thee ! " 
 " I stand by my brother , " say Harry , turn away from the Colonel 's look , and grasp George 's hand . the sadness on their adversary 's face do not depart . " Heaven be good to we ! ' tis all clear now , " he mutter to himself . " the time to write a few letter , and I be at your service , Mr. Warrington , " he say . 
 " you have your own pistol at your saddle . I do not ride out with any ; but will send Sady back for mine . that will give you time enough , Colonel Washington ? " 
 " plenty of time , sir . " and each gentleman make the other a low bow , and , put his arm in his brother 's , George walk away . the virginian officer look towards the two unlucky captain , who be by this time helpless with liquor . Captain Benson , the master of the tavern , be prop the hat of one of they over his head . 
 " it be not altogether their fault , Colonel , " say my landlord , with a grim look of humour . " Jack Firebrace and Tom Humbold of Spotsylvania be here this morning , chant horse with them . and Jack and Tom get them to play card ; and they do not   win the british Captains do not . and Jack and Tom challenge they to drink for the honour of Old England , and they do not   win at that game , neither , much . they be kind , free - handed fellow when they be sober , but they be a pretty pair of fool they be . " 
 " Captain Benson , you be an old frontier man , and an officer of ours , before you turn farmer and taverner . you will help I in this matter with yonder young gentleman ? " say the Colonel . 
 " I will stand by and see fair play , Colonel . I will not   have no hand in it , beyond see fair play . Madam Esmond have help I many a time , tend my poor wife in her lying - in , and doctor our Betty in the fever . you be not   a - go to be very hard with they poor boy ? though I see them both shoot : the fair one hunt well , as you know , but the old one be a wonder at an ace of spade . " 
 " will you be pleased to send my man with my valise , Captain , into any private room which you can spare I ? I must write a few letter before this business come on . God grant it be well over ! " and the Captain lead the Colonel into almost the only other room of his house , call , with many oath , to a pack of negro servant , to disperse thence , who be chatter loudly among one another , and no doubt discuss the quarrel which have just take place . Edwin , the Colonel 's man , return with his master 's portmanteau , and as he look from the window , he see Sady , George Warrington 's negro , gallop away upon his errand , doubtless , and in the direction of Castlewood . the Colonel , young and naturally hot - headed , but the most courteous and scrupulous of man , and ever keep his strong passion under guard , could not but think with amazement of the position in which he find , himself , and of the three , perhaps four enemy , who appear suddenly before he , menace his life . how have this strange series of quarrel be bring about ? he have ride away a few hour since from Castlewood , with his young companion , and , to all seem , they be perfect friend . a shower of rain send they into a tavern , where there be a couple of recruiting officer , and they be not seat for half an hour at a social table , but he have quarrel with the whole company , call this one name , agree to meet another in combat , and threaten chastisement to a third , the son of his most intimate friend ! 




 CHAPTER XI . wherein the two Georges prepare for blood 

 the Virginian Colonel remain in one chamber of the tavern , occupy with gloomy preparation for the ensue meeting ; his adversary in the other room think fit to make his testamentary disposition , too , and dictate , by his obedient brother and secretary , a grandiloquent letter to his mother , of whom , and by that writing , he take a solemn farewell . she would hardly , he suppose , pursue the scheme which she have in view   a peculiar satirical emphasis be lay upon the scheme which she have in view , after the event of that morning , should he fall , as , probably , would be the case . 
 " my dear , dear George , do not   say that ! " cry the affrighted secretary . 
 " ' as probably will be the case , ' " George persist with great majesty . " you know what a good shot Colonel George be , Harry . I , myself , be pretty fair at a mark , and ' tis probable that one or both of we will drop . ' I scarcely suppose you will carry out the intention you have at present in view . ' " this be utter in a tone of still great bitterness than George have use even in the previous phrase . Harry weep as he take it down . 
 " you see I say nothing ; Madame Esmond 's name do not even appear in the quarrel . do you not remember in our grandfather 's life of himself , how he say that Lord Castlewood fight Lord Mohun on a pretext of a quarrel at card ? and never so much as hint at the lady 's name , who be the real cause of the duel ? I take my hint , I confess , from that , Harry . our mother be not compromise in the why , child , what have you be write , and who teach thee to spell ? " Harry have write the last word " in view , " in vew , and a great blot of salt water from his honest , boyish eye may have obliterate some other bad spelling . 
 " I can not   think about the spelling now , Georgy , " whimper George 's clerk . " I be too miserable for that . I begin to think , perhaps it be all nonsense , perhaps Colonel George never " 
 " never mean to take possession of Castlewood ; never give himself air , and patronise we there ; never advise my mother to have I flog , never intend to marry she ; never insult I , and be insult before the king 's officer ; never write to his brother to say we should be the well for his parental authority ? the paper be there , " cry the young man , slap his breast - pocket , " and if anything happen to I , Harry Warrington , you will find it on my corse ! " 
 " write yourself , Georgy , I can not   write , " say Harry , dig his fist into his eye , and smear over the whole composition , bad spelling and all , with his elbow . 
 on this , George , take another sheet of paper , sate down at his brother 's place , and produce a composition in which he introduce the long word , the grand Latin quotation , and the most profound satire of which the youthful scribe be master . he desire that his negro boy , Sady , should be set free ; that his horace , a choice of his book , and , if possible , a suitable provision should be make for his affectionate tutor , Mr. Dempster ; that his silver fruit - knife , his music - book , and harpsichord , should be give to little Fanny Mountain ; and that his brother should take a lock of his hair , and wear it in memory of his ever fond and faithfully attach George . and he seal the document with the seal of arm that his grandfather have wear . 
 " the watch , of course , will be your , " say George , take out his grandfather 's gold watch , and look at it . " why , two hour and a - half be go ! ' tis time that Sady should be back with the pistol . take the watch , Harry dear . " 
 " it be no good ! " cry out Harry , fling his arm round his brother . " if he fight you , I will fight he , too . if he kill my Georgy , he , he shall have a shot at I ! " and the poor lad utter more than one of those expression , which be say peculiarly to affect record angel , who have to take they down at celestial chancery . 
 meanwhile , General Braddock 's new aide - de - camp have write five letter in his large resolute hand , and seal they with his seal . one be to his mother , at Mount Vernon ; one to his brother ; one be address M. C. only ; and one to his Excellency , Major - General Braddock . " and one , young gentleman , be for your mother , Madam Esmond , " say the boy ' informant . 
 again the record angel have to fly off with a violent expression , which part from the lip of George Warrington . the chancery previously mention be crowd with such case , and the messenger must have be for ever on the wing . but I fear for young George and his oath there be no excuse ; for it be an execration utter from a heart full of hatred , and rage , and jealousy . 
 it be the landlord of the tavern who communicate these fact to the young man . the Captain have put on his old militia uniform to do honour to the occasion , and inform the boy that the Colonel be walk up and down the garden a - wait for them , and that the Reg'lars be a'most sober , too , by this time . 
 a plot of ground near the Captain 's log - house have be enclose with shingle , and clear for a kitchen - garden ; there indeed pace Colonel Washington , his hand behind his back , his head bow down , a grave sorrow on his handsome face . the negro servant be crowd at the paling , and look over . the officer under the porch have waken up also , as their host remark . Captain Waring be walk , almost steadily , under the balcony form by the slop porch and roof of the wooden house ; and Captain Grace be loll over the railing , with eye which star very much , though perhaps they do not see very clearly . Benson 's be a famous rendezvous for cock - fight , horse - match , boxing , and wrestling - match , such as bring the virginian country - folk together . there have be many brawl at Benson 's , and man who come thither sound and sober , have go thence with rib break and eye gouge out . and squire , and farmer , and negro , all participate in the sport . 
 there , then , stalk the tall young Colonel , plunge in dismal meditation . there be no way out of his scrape , but the usual cruel one , which the law of honour and the practice of the country order . goad into fury by the impertinence of a boy , he have use insult word . the young man have ask for reparation . he be shocked to think that George Warrington 's jealousy and revenge should have rankle in the young fellow so long but the wrong have be the Colonel 's , and he be bind to pay the forfeit . 
 a great hallooing and shouting , such as negro use , who love noise at all time , and especially delight to yell and scream when gallop on horseback , be now hear at a distance , and all the head , woolly and powdered , be turn in the direction of this outcry . it come from the road over which our traveller have themselves pass three hour before , and presently the clattering of a horse 's hoof be hear , and now Mr. Sady make his appearance on his foaming horse , and actually fire a pistol off in the midst of a prodigious uproar from his woolly brother . then he fire another pistol off , to which noise Sady 's horse , which have carry Harry Warrington on many a hunt , be perfectly accustom ; and now he be in the courtyard , surround by a score of his bawl comrade , and be descend amidst flutter fowl and turkey , kick horse and shriek frantic pig ; and brother - negroes crowd round he , to whom he instantly begin to talk and chatter . 
 " Sady , sir , come here ! " roar out Master Harry . 
 " Sady , come here ! confound you ! " shout Master George .   again the record angel be in requisition , and have to be off on one of his endless errand to the register office .   " come directly , mas'r , " say Sady , and resume his conversation with his woolly brother . he grin . he take the pistol out of the holster . he snap the lock . he point they at a grunter , which plunge through the farmyard . he point down the road , over which he have just gallop , and towards which the woolly head again turn . he say again , " Comin ' , mas'r . everybody a - comin ' . " and now , the gallop of other horse be hear . and who be yonder ? little Mr. Dempster , spur and dig into his pony ; and that lady in a riding - habit on Madam Esmond 's little horse , can it be Madam Esmond ? no . it be too stout . as I live it be Mrs. Mountain on Madam 's grey ! 
 " o Lor ! o Golly ! hoop ! here dey come ! hurray ! " a chorus of negro rise up . " here dey be ! " Dr. Dempster and Mrs. Mountain have clatter into the yard , have jump from their horse , have elbow through the negro , have rush into the house , have run through it and across the porch , where the british officer be sit in muzzy astonishment ; have run down the stair to the garden where George and Harry be walk , their tall enemy stalk opposite to they ; and almost ere George Warrington have have time sternly to say , " what do you do here , madam ? " Mrs. Mountain have fling her arm round his neck and cry : " oh , George , my darling ! it be a mistake ! it be a mistake , and be all my fault ! " 
 " what be a mistake ? " ask George , majestically separate himself from the embrace . 
 " what be it , Mounty ? " cry Harry , all of a tremble . 
 " that paper I take out of his portfolio , that paper I pick up , child ; where the Colonel say he be go to marry a widow with two child . who should it be but you , child , and who should it be but your mother ? " 
 " well ? " 
 " well , it be it be not your mother . it be that little widow Custis whom the Colonel be go to marry . he would always take a rich one ; I know he would . it be not Mrs. Rachel Warrington . he tell Madam so to - day , just before he be go away , and that the marriage be to come off after the campaign . and and your mother be furious , boy . and when Sady come for the pistol , and tell the whole house how you be go to fight , I tell he to fire the pistol off ; and I gallop after he , and I have   nearly break my poor old bone in come to you . " 
 " I have a mind to break Mr. Sady 's , " growl George . " I specially enjoin the villain not to say a word . " 
 " thank God he do , brother ! " say poor Harry . " thank God he do ! " 
 " what will Mr. Washington and those gentleman think of my servant tell my mother at home that I be go to fight a duel ? " ask Mr. George , still in wrath . 
 " you have show your proof before , George , " say Harry , respectfully . " and , thank Heaven , you be not go to fight our old friend , our grandfather 's old friend . for it be a mistake and there be no quarrel now , dear , be there ? you be unkind to he under a wrong impression . " 
 " I certainly act under a wrong impression , " own George , " but " 
 " George ! George Washington ! " Harry here cry out , spring over the cabbage - garden towards the bowling - green , where the Colonel be stalk , and though we can not hear he , we see he , with both his hand out , and with the eagerness of youth , and with a hundred blunder , and with love and affection thrilling in his honest voice we imagine the lad tell his tale to his friend . 
 there be a custom in those day which have disappear from our manner now , but which then linger . when Harry have finish his artless story , his friend the Colonel take he fairly to his arm , and hold he to his heart : and his voice falter as he say , " thank God , thank God for this ! " 
 " oh , George , " say Harry , who feel now how he love his friend with all his heart , " how I wish I be go with you on the campaign ! " the other press both the boy 's hand , in a grasp of friendship , which each know never would slacken . 
 then the Colonel advance , gravely hold out his hand to Harry 's eld brother . perhaps Harry wonder that the two do not embrace as he and the Colonel have just do . but , though hand be join , the salutation be only formal and stern on both side . 
 " I find I have do you a wrong , Colonel Washington , " George say , " and must apologise , not for the error , but for much of my late behaviour which have result from it . " 
 " the error be mine ! it be I who find that paper in your room , and show it to George , and be jealous of you , Colonel . all woman be jealous , " cry Mrs. Mountain . 
 " ' tis a pity you could not have keep your eye off my paper , madam , " say Mr. Washington . " you will permit I to say so . a great deal of mischief have come because I choose to keep a secret which concerned only myself and another person . for a long time George Warrington 's heart have be black with anger against I , and my feeling towards he have , I own , scarce be more friendly . all this pain might have be spar to both of we , have my private paper only be read by those for whom they be write . I shall say no more now , lest my feeling again should betray I into hasty word . Heaven bless thee , Harry ! Farewell , George ! and take a true friend 's advice , and try and be less ready to think evil of your friend . we shall meet again at the camp , and will keep our weapon for the enemy . gentleman ! if you remember this scene to - morrow , you will know where to find I . " and with a very stately bow to the english officer , the Colonel leave the abash company , and speedily ride away . 




 CHAPTER XII . news from the Camp 

 we must fancy that the parting between the brother be over , that George have take his place in Mr. Braddock 's family , and Harry have return home to Castlewood and his duty . his heart be with the army , and his pursuit at home offer the boy no pleasure . he do not care to own how deep his disappointment be , at be oblige to stay under the homely , quiet roof , now more melancholy than ever since George be away . Harry pass his brother 's empty chamber with an avert face ; take George 's place at the head of the table , and sigh as he drink from his silver tankard . Madam Warrington call the toast of " the king " stoutly every day ; and , on Sundays , when Harry read the service , and pray for all traveller by land and by water , she say , " we beseech Thee to hear we , " with a peculiar solemnity . she insist on talk about George constantly , but quite cheerfully , and as if his return be certain . she walk into his vacant room , with head upright , and no outward sign of emotion . she see that his book , linen , paper , etc . , be arrange with care ; talk of he with a very special respect , and specially appealing to the old servant at meal , and so forth , regard thing which be to be do " when Mr. George come home . " Mrs. Mountain be constantly on the whimper when George 's name be mention , and Harry 's face wear a look of the most ghastly alarm ; but his mother 's be invariably grave and sedate . she make more blunder at piquet and backgammon than you would expect from she ; and the servant find she awake and dressed , however early they may rise . she have pray Mr. Dempster to come back into residence at Castlewood . she be not severe or haughty   as she will not certainly be   with any of the party , but quiet in her talk with they , and gentle in assertion and reply . she be for ever talk of her father and his campaign , who come out of they all with no very severe wound to hurt he ; and so she hop and trust will her eld son . 
 George write frequent letter home to his brother , and , now the army be on its march , compile a rough journal , which he forward as occasion serve . this document be peruse with great delight and eagerness by the youth to whom it be address , and more than once read out in family council , on the long summer night , as Madam Esmond sit upright at her tea - table   she never condescend to use the back of a chair   as little Fanny Mountain be busy with her sewing , as Mr. Dempster and Mrs. Mountain sit over their card , as the hush old servant of the house move about silently in the gloaming , and listen to the word of the young master . Hearken to Harry Warrington read out his brother 's letter ! as we look at the slim character on the yellow page , fondly keep and put aside , we can almost fancy he alive who write and who read it and yet , lo ! they be as if they never have be ; their portrait faint image in frame of tarnished gold . be they real once , or be they mere phantasm ? do they live and die once ? do they love each other as true brother , and loyal gentleman ? can we hear their voice in the past ? sure I know Harry 's , and yonder he sit in the warm summer evening , and read his young brother 's simple story : 
 " it must be own that the province be act scurvily by his Majesty King George II . , and his representative here be in a flame of fury . Virginia be bad enough , and poor Maryland not much well , but Pennsylvania be bad of all . we pray they to send we troop from home to fight the French ; and we promise to maintain the troop when they come . we not only do not   keep our promise , and make scarce any provision for our defender , but our people insist upon the most exorbitant price for their cattle and store , and actually cheat the soldier who be come to fight their battle . no wonder the General swear , and the troop be sulky . the delay have be endless . owe to the failure of the several province to provide their promise store and mean of locomotion , week and month have elapse , during which time , no doubt , the French have be strengthen themselves on our frontier and in the fort they have turn we out of . though there never will be any love lose between I and Colonel Washington , it must be own that your favourite   I be not jealous , Hal   be a brave man and a good officer . the family respect he very much , and the General be always ask his opinion . indeed , he be almost the only man who have see the Indians in their war - paint , and I own I think he be right in fire upon mon . Jumonville last year . 
 " there be to be no more suite to that other quarrel at Benson 's Tavern than there be to the propose battle between Colonel W. and a certain young gentleman who shall be nameless . Captain Waring wish to pursue it on come into camp , and bring the message from Captain Grace , which your friend , who be as bold as Hector , be for take up , and employ a brother aide - de - camp , Colonel Wingfield , on his side . but when Wingfield hear the circumstance of the quarrel , how it have arise from Grace be drunk , and be foment by war be tipsy , and how the two 44th gentleman have choose to insult a militia officer , he swear that Colonel Washington should not meet the 44th man ; that he would carry the matter straightway to his Excellency , who would bring the two captain to a court - martial for brawl with the militia , and drunkenness , and indecent behaviour , and the captain be fain to put up their toasting - iron , and swallow their wrath . they be good - natured enough out of their cup , and eat their humble - pie with very good appetite at a reconciliation dinner which Colonel W. have with the 44th , and where he be as perfectly stupid and correct as Prince Prettyman need be . hang he ! he have no fault , and that be why I dislike he . when he marry that widow ah I ! what a dreary life she will have of it . " 
 " I wonder at the taste of some man , and the effrontery of some woman , " say Madam Esmond , lay her teacup down . " I wonder at any woman who have be marry once , so forget herself as to marry again ! do not   you , Mountain ? " 
 " monstrous ! " say Mountain , with a queer look . 
 Dempster keep his eye steadily fix on his glass of punch . Harry look as if he be choke with laughter , or with some other conceal emotion , but his mother say , " go on , Harry ! continue with your brother 's journal . he write well : but , ah , will he ever be able to write like my papa ? " 
 Harry resume : " we keep the strict order here in camp , and the order against drunkenness and ill - behaviour on the part of the man be very severe . the roll of each company be call at morning , noon , and night , and a return of the absent and disorderly be give in by the officer to the command officer of the regiment , who have to see that they be properly punish . the man be punish , and the drummer be always at work . oh , Harry , but it make one sick to see the first blood draw from a great strong white back , and to hear the piteous yell of the poor fellow . " 
 " oh , horrid ! " say Madam Esmond . 
 " I think I should have murder Ward if he have flog I . thank Heaven he get off with only a crack of the ruler ! the man , I say , be look after carefully enough . I wish the officer be . the Indians have just break up their camp , and retire in dudgeon , because the young officer be for ever drink with the squaw and and hum ha . " here Mr. Harry pause , as not care to proceed with the narrative , in the presence of little Fanny , very likely , who sit primly in her chair by her mother 's side , work her little sampler . 
 " pass over that about the odious tipsy creature , " say Madam . and Harry commence , in a loud tone , a much more satisfactory statement : " each regiment have Divine Service perform at the head of its colour every Sunday . the General do everything in the power of mortal man to prevent plundering , and to encourage the people round about to bring in provision . he have declare soldier shall be shoot who dare to interrupt or molest the market - people . he have order the price of provision to be raise a penny a pound , and have lend money out of his own pocket to provide the camp . altogether , he be a strange compound , this General . he flog his man without mercy , but he give without stint . he swear most tremendous oath in conversation , and tell story which Mountain would be shock to hear " 
 " why I ? " ask Mountain ; " and what have I to do with the General 's silly story ? " 
 " never mind the story ; and go on , Harry , " cry the mistress of the house . 
 " would be shock to hear after dinner ; but he never miss service . he adore his Great Duke , and have his name constantly on his lip . our two regiment both serve in Scotland , where I dare say Mr. Dempster know the colour of their facing . " 
 " we see the tail of their coat , as well as their facing , " growl the little Jacobite tutor . 
 " Colonel Washington have have the fever very smartly , and have hardly be well enough to keep up with the march . have he not well go home and be nurse by his widow ? when either of we be ill , we be almost as good friend again as ever . but I feel somehow as if I can not   forgive he for have wrong he . good Powers ! how I have be hat he for these month past ! oh , Harry !
