I be in a fury at the tavern the other day , because Mountain come up so soon , and put an end to our difference . we ought to have burn a little gunpowder between we , and clear the air . but though I do not   love he , as you do , I know he be a good soldier , a good officer , and a brave , honest man ; and , at any rate , shall love he none the bad for not want to be our stepfather . " 
 " a stepfather , indeed ! " cry Harry 's mother . " why , jealousy and prejudice have perfectly madden the poor child ! do you suppose the Marquis of Esmond 's daughter and heiress could not have find other stepfather for her son than a mere provincial surveyor ? if there be any more such allusion in George 's journal , I beg you skip them , Harry , my dear . about this piece of folly and blundering , there hath be quite talk enough already . " 
 " ' tis a pretty sight , " Harry continue , read from his brother 's journal , " to see a long line of redcoat , thread through the wood or take their ground after the march . the care against surprise be so great and constant , that we defy prowl Indians to come unawares upon we , and our advanced sentry and savage have on the contrary fall in with the enemy and take a scalp or two from they . they be such cruel villain , these French and their paint ally , that we do not think of show they mercy . only think , we find but yesterday a little boy scalp but yet alive in a lone house , where his parent have be attack and murder by the savage enemy , of whom so great be his indignation at their cruelty our General have offer a reward of five pound for all the indian scalp bring in . 
 " when our march be over , you should see our camp , and all the care bestow on it . our baggage and our General 's tent and guard be place quite in the centre of the camp . we have outlying sentry by two , by three , by ten , by whole company . at the least surprise , they be instruct to run in on the main body and rally round the tent and baggage , which be so arrange themselves as to be a strong fortification . Sady and I , you must know , be march on foot now , and my horse be carry baggage . the Pennsylvanians send such rascally animal into camp that they speedily give in . what good horse be leave , ' twa our duty to give up : and Roxana have a couple of pack upon she back instead of her young master . she know I right well , and whinny when she see I , and I walk by her side , and we have many a talk together on the march . 
 " July 4 . to guard against surprise , we be all warn to pay especial attention to the beat of the drum ; always halt when they hear the long roll beat , and march at the beat of the long march . we be more on the alert regard the enemy now . we have our advanced picket double , and two sentry at every post . the man on the advanced picket be constantly under arm , with fix bayonet , all through the night , and relieve every two hour . the half that be relieve lie down by their arm , but be not suffer to leave their picket . ' tis evident that we be draw very near to the enemy now . this packet go out with the General 's to Colonel Dunbar 's camp , who be thirty mile behind we ; and will be carry thence to Frederick , and thence to my honoured mother 's house at Castlewood , to whom I send my duty , with kind remembrance , as to all friend there , and bow much love I need not say to my dear brother from his affectionate GEORGE E. WARRINGTON . " 
 the whole land be now lie parch and scorch in the July heat . for ten day no news have come from the column advance on the Ohio . their march , though it toil but slowly through the painful forest , must bring they ere long up with the enemy ; the troop , lead by consummate captain , be accustom now to the wilderness , and not afraid of surprise . every precaution have be take against ambush . it be the outlying enemy who be discover , pursue , destroy , by the vigilant scout and skirmisher of the british force . the last news hear be that the army have advance considerably beyond the ground of Mr. Washington 's discomfiture on the previous year , and two day after must be within a day 's march of the french fort . about take it no fear be entertain ; the amount of the french reinforcement from Montreal be know . Mr. Braddock , with his two veteran regiment from Britain , and their ally of Virginia and Pennsylvania , be more than a match for any troop that could be collect under the white flag . 
 such continue to be the talk , in the sparse town of our virginian province , at the gentry 's house , and the rough roadside tavern , where people meet and canvass the war . the few messenger who be send back by the General report well of the main force . ' twa think the enemy would not stand or defend himself at all . have he intend to attack , he might have seize a dozen occasion for assault our troop at pass through which they have be allow to go entirely free . so George have give up his favourite mare , like a hero as he be , and be march afoot with the line ? Madam Esmond vow that he should have the good horse in Virginia or Carolina in place of Roxana . there be horse enough to be have in the province , and for money . it be only for the King 's service that they be not forthcoming . 
 although at their family meeting and repast the inmate of Castlewood always talk cheerfully , never anticipate any but a triumphant issue to the campaign , or acknowledge any feeling of disquiet , yet , it must be own they be mighty uneasy when at home , quit it ceaselessly , and for ever on the trot from one neighbour 's house to another in quest of news . it be prodigious how quickly report run and spread . when , for instance , a certain note border warrior , call Colonel Jack , have offer himself and his huntsman to the General , who have decline the ruffian 's term or his proffered service , the defection of Jack and his man be the talk of thousand of tongue immediately . the house negro , in their midnight gallop about the country , in search of junketing or sweetheart , bring and spread news over amazingly wide district . they have a curious knowledge of the incident of the march for a fortnight at least after its commencement . they know and laugh at the cheat practise on the army , for horse , provision , and the like ; for a good bargain over the foreigner be not an unfrequent or unpleasant practice among New Yorkers , Pennsylvanians , or marylander ; though ' tis know that american folk have become perfectly artless and simple in later time , and never grasp , and never overreach , and be never selfish now . for three week after the army 's departure , the thousand report regard it be cheerful ; and when our Castlewood friend meet at their supper , their tone be confident and their news pleasant . 
 but on the 10th of July a vast and sudden gloom spread over the province . a look of terror and doubt seem to fall upon every face . affrighted negro wistfully eye their master and retire , and hum and whisper with one another . the fiddle cease in the quarter : the song and laugh of those cheery black folk be hush . right and left , everybody 's servant be on the gallop for news . the country tavern be throng with horseman , who drink and curse and brawl at the bar , each bring his gloomy story . the army have be surprised . the troop have fall into an ambuscade , and have be cut up almost to a man . all the officer be take down by the french marksman and the savage . the General have be wound , and carry off the field in his sash . four day afterwards the report be that the General be dead , and scalp by a French Indian . 
 ah , what a scream poor Mrs. Mountain give , when Gumbo bring this news from across the James River , and little Fanny spring cry to her mother 's arm ! " Lord God Almighty , watch over we , and defend my boy ! " say Mrs. Esmond , sink down on her knee , and lift her rigid hand to Heaven . the gentleman be not at home when this rumour arrive , but they come in an hour or two afterwards , each from his hunt for news . the scot tutor do not dare to look up and meet the widow 's agonising look . Harry Warrington be as pale as his mother . it might not be true about the manner of the General 's death but he be dead . the army have be surprise by Indians , and have flee , and be kill without see the enemy . an express have arrive from Dunbar 's camp . fugitive be pour in there . should he go and see ? he must go and see . he and stout little Dempster arm themselves and mount , take a couple of mount servant with they . 
 they follow the northward track which the expeditionary army have hew out for itself , and at every step which bring they near to the scene of action , the disaster of the fearful day seem to magnify . the day after the defeat a number of the miserable fugitive from the fatal battle of the 9th July have reach Dunbar 's camp , fifty mile from the field . Thither poor Harry and his companion ride , stop straggler , ask news , give money , get from one and all the same gloomy tale a thousand man be slay two - third of the officer be down all the General 's aide - de - camp be hit . be hit ? but be they kill ? those who fall never rise again . the tomahawk do its work upon they . o brother , brother ! all the fond memory of their youth , all the dear remembrance of their childhood , the love and the laughter , the tender romantic vow which they have pledge to each other as lad , be recall by Harry with pang inexpressibly keen . wounded man look up and be soften by his grief : rough woman melt as they see the woe write on the handsome young face : the hardy old tutor could scarcely look at he for tear , and grieve for he even more than for his dear pupil who lie dead under the savage indian knife . 




 CHAPTER XIII . Profitless Quest 

 at every step which Harry Warrington take towards Pennsylvania , the report of the british disaster be magnify and confirm . those two famous regiment which have fight in the scottish and continental war , have flee from an enemy almost unseen , and their boasted discipline and valour have not enable they to face a band of savage and a few french infantry . the unfortunate commander of the expedition have show the utmost bravery and resolution . four time his horse have be shoot under he . twice he have be wound , and the last time of the mortal hurt which end his life three day after the battle . More than one of Harry 's informant describe the action to the poor lad , the passage of the river , the long line of advance through the wilderness , the firing in front , the vain struggle of the man to advance , and the artillery to clear the way of the enemy ; then the ambush fire from behind every bush and tree , and the murderous fusillade , by which at least half of the expeditionary force have be shoot down . but not all the General 's suite be kill , Harry hear . one of his aide - de - camp , a virginian gentleman , be ill of fever and exhaustion at Dunbar 's camp . 
 one of they but which ? to the camp Harry hurry , and reach it at length . it be George Washington Harry find stretch in a tent there , and not his brother . a sharp pain than that of the fever Mr. Washington declare he feel , when he see Harry Warrington , and could give he no news of George . 
 Mr. Washington do not dare to tell Harry all . for three day after the fight his duty have be to be near the General . on the fatal 9th of July , he have see George go to the front with order from the chief , to whose side he never return . after Braddock himself die , the aide - de - camp have find mean to retrace his course to the field . the corps which remain there be strip and horribly mutilate . one body he bury which he think to be George Warrington 's . his own illness be increase , perhaps occasion , by the anguish which he undergo in his search for the unhappy young volunteer . 
 " ah , George ! if you have love he you would have find he dead or alive , " Harry cry out . nothing would satisfy he but that he , too , should go to the ground and examine it . with money he procure a guide or two . he ford the river at the place where the army have pass over : he go from one end to the other of the dreadful field . it be no long haunt by Indians now . the bird of prey be feed on the mangle fester carcase . save in his own grandfather , lie very calm , with a sweet smile on his lip , Harry have never yet see the face of death . the horrible spectacle of mutilation cause he to turn away with shudder and loathing . what news could the vacant wood , or those fester corps lie under the tree , give the lad of his lost brother ? he be for go , unarmed and with a white flag , to the french fort , whither , after their victory , the enemy have return ; but his guide refuse to advance with he . the French might possibly respect they , but the Indians would not . " keep your hair for your lady mother , my young gentleman , " say the guide . " ' tis enough that she lose one son in this campaign . " 
 when Harry return to the english encampment at Dunbar 's , it be his turn to be down with the fever . delirium set in upon he , and he lie some time in the tent and on the bed from which his friend have just rise convalescent . for some day he do not know who watch he ; and poor Dempster , who have tend he in more than one of these malady , think the widow must lose both her child ; but the fever be so far subdue that the boy be enable to rally somewhat , and get to horseback . Mr. Washington and Dempster both escort he home . it be with a heavy heart , no doubt , that all three beheld once more the gate of Castlewood . 
 a servant in advance have be send to announce their coming . first come Mrs. Mountain and her little daughter , welcome Harry with many tear and embrace , but she scarce give a nod of recognition to Mr. Washington ; and the little girl cause the young officer to start , and turn deadly pale , by come up to he with her hand behind she , and ask , " why have you not bring George back too ? " Harry do not hear . the sob and caress of his good friend and nurse luckily keep he from listen to little Fanny . 
 Dempster be graciously receive by the two lady . " whatever could be do , we know you would do , Mr. Dempster , " say Mrs. Mountain , give he her hand . " make a curtsey to Mr. Dempster , Fanny , and remember , child , to be grateful to all who have be friendly to our benefactor . will it please you to take any refreshment before you ride , Colonel Washington ? " 
 Mr. Washington have have a sufficient ride already , and count as certainly upon the hospitality of Castlewood , as he would upon the shelter of his own house . 
 " the time to feed my horse , and a glass of water for myself , and I will trouble Castlewood hospitality no far , " Mr. Washington say . 
 " sure , George , you have your room here , and my mother be above - stair get it ready ! " cry Harry . " that poor horse of yours stumble with you , and can not   go far this evening . " 
 " hush ! your mother will not   see he , child , " whisper Mrs. Mountain . 
 " not see George ? why , he be like a son of the house , " cry Harry . 
 " she have well not see he . I do not   meddle any more in family matter , child : but when the Colonel 's servant ride in , and say you be come , Madam Esmond leave this room , my dear , where she be sit read Drelincourt , and say she feel she could not see Mr. Washington . will you go to she ? " Harry take his friend 's arm , and excuse himself to the Colonel , to whom he say he would return in a few minute , he leave the parlour in which they have assemble , and go to the upper room , where Madam Esmond be . 
 he be hasten across the corridor , and , with an avert head , pass by one especial door , which he do not like to look at , for it be that of his brother 's room ; but as he come to it , Madam Esmond issue from it , and fold he to her heart , and lead he in . a settee be by the bed , and a book of psalm lay on the coverlet . all the rest of the room be exactly as George have leave it . 
 " my poor child ! how thin thou art grow how haggard you look ! never mind . a mother 's care will make thee well again . ' twa nobly do to go and brave sickness and danger in search of your brother . have other be as faithful , he might be here now . never mind , my Harry ; our hero will come back to we , I know he be not dead . one so good , and so brave , and so gentle , and so clever as he be , I know be not lose to we altogether . "   perhaps Harry think within himself that his mother have not always be accustom so to speak of her eld son .   " dry up thy tear , my dear ! he will come back to we , I know he will come . " and when Harry press she to give a reason for her belief , she say she have see her father two night run in a dream , and he have tell she that her boy be a prisoner among the Indians . 
 Madam Esmond 's grief have not prostrate she as Harry 's have when first it fall upon he ; it have rather stir and animate she : her eye be eager , her countenance angry and revengeful . the lad wonder almost at the condition in which he find his mother . 
 but when he beseech she to go downstairs , and give a hand of welcome to George Washington , who have accompany he , the lady 's excitement painfully increase . she say she should shudder at touch his hand . she declare Mr. Washington have take her son from she , she could not sleep under the same roof with he . 
 " he give I his bed when I be ill , mother ; and if our George be alive , how have George Washington a hand in his death ? ah ! please God it be only as you say , " cry Harry , in bewilderment . 
 " if your brother return , as return he will , it will not be through Mr. Washington 's help , " say Madam Esmond . " he neither defend George on the field , nor would he bring he out of it . " 
 " but he tend I most kindly in my fever , " interpose Harry . " he be yet ill when he give up his bed to I , and be think only of his friend , when any other man would have think only of himself . " 
 " a friend ! a pretty friend ! " sneer the lady . " of all his Excellency 's aide - de - camp , my gentleman be the only one who come back unwounded . the brave and noble fall , but he , to be sure , be unhurt . I confide my boy to he , the pride of my life , whom he will defend with his , forsooth ! and he leave my George in the forest , and bring I back himself ! oh , a pretty welcome I must give he ! " 
 " no gentleman , " cry Harry , warmly , " be ever refuse shelter under my grandfather 's roof . " 
 " oh no no gentleman ! " exclaim the little widow ; " let we go down , if you like , son , and pay our respect to this one . will you please to give I your arm ? " and take an arm which be very little able to give her support , she walk down the broad stair , and into the apartment where the Colonel sate . 
 she make he a ceremonious curtsey , and extend one of the little hand , which she allow for a moment to rest in his . " I wish that our meeting have be happy , Colonel Washington , " she say . 
 " you do not grieve more than I do that it be otherwise , madam , " say the Colonel . 
 " I might have wish that the meeting have be spar , that I might not have keep you from friend whom you be naturally anxious to see , that my boy 's indisposition have not detain you . home and his good nurse Mountain , and his mother and our good Doctor Dempster , will soon restore he . ' twa scarce necessary , Colonel , that you , who have so many affair on your hand , military and domestic , should turn doctor too . " 
 " Harry be ill and weak , and I think it be my duty to ride by he , " falter the Colonel . 
 " you yourself , sir , have go through the fatigue and danger of the campaign in the most wonderful manner , " say the widow , curtsey again , and look at he with her impenetrable black eye . 
 " I wish to Heaven , madam , some one else have come back in my place ! " 
 " Nay , sir , you have tie which must render your life more than ever valuable and dear to you , and duty to which , I know , you must be anxious to betake yourself . in our present deplorable state of doubt and distress , Castlewood can be a welcome place to no stranger , much less to you , and so I know , sir , you will be for leave we ere long . and you will pardon I if the state of my own spirit oblige I for the most part to keep my chamber . but my friend here will bear you company as long as you favour we , whilst I nurse my poor Harry upstairs . mountain , you will have the cedar - room on the ground - floor ready for Mr. Washington , and anything in the house be at his command . Farewell , sir . will you be pleased to present my compliment to your mother , who will be thankful to have her son safe and sound out of the war , as also to my young friend Martha Custis , to whom and to whose child I wish every happiness . come , my son ! " and with these word , and another freeze curtsey , the pale little woman retreat , look steadily at the Colonel , who stand dumb on the floor . 
 Strong as Madam Esmond 's belief appear to be respect her son 's safety , the house of Castlewood naturally remain sad and gloomy . she might forbid mourning for herself and family ; but her heart be in black , whatever face the resolute little lady persist in wear before the world . to look for her son , be hop against hope . no authentic account of his death have indeed arrive , and no one appear who have see he fall ; but hundred more have be so stricken on that fatal day , with no eye to behold their last pang , save those of the lurk enemy and the comrade die by their side . a fortnight after the defeat , when Harry be absent on his quest , George 's servant , Sady , reappear wounded and maim at Castlewood . but he could give no coherent account of the battle , only of his flight from the centre , where he be with the baggage . he have no news of his master since the morning of the action . for many day Sady lurk in the negro quarter away from the sight of Madam Esmond , whose anger he do not dare to face . that lady 's few neighbour speak of she as labour under a delusion . so strong be it , that there be time when Harry and the other member of the little Castlewood family be almost bring to share in it . it seem nothing strange to she , that her father out of another world should promise she her son 's life . in this world or the next , that family sure must be of consequence , she think . nothing have ever yet happen to her son , no accident , no fever , no important illness , but she have a prevision of it . she could enumerate half a dozen instance , which , indeed , her household be oblige more or less to confirm , how , when anything have happen to the boy at ever so great a distance , she have know of their mishap and its consequence . no , George be not dead ; George be a prisoner among the Indians ; George would come back and rule over Castlewood ; as sure , as sure as his Majesty would send a great force from home to recover the tarnish glory of the british arm , and to drive the French out of the Americas . 
 as for Mr. Washington , she would never with her own goodwill behold he again . he have promise to protect George with his life . why be her son go and the Colonel alive ? how dare he to face she after that promise , and appear before a mother without her son ? she trust she know her duty . she bear illwill to no one : but as an Esmond , she have a sense of honour , and Mr. Washington have forfeit her in let her son out of his sight . he have to obey superior order   some one perhaps object ? Psha ! a promise be a promise . he have promise to guard George 's life with his own , and where be her boy ? and be not the Colonel   a pretty Colonel , indeed !   sound and safe ? do not tell I that his coat and hat have shot through they !   this be her answer to another humble plea in Mr. Washington 's behalf .   can not   I go into the study this instant and fire two shot with my papa 's pistol through this paduasoy skirt , and should I be kill ? she laugh at the notion of death result from any such operation ; nor be her laugh very pleasant to hear . the satire of people who have little natural humour be seldom good sport for bystander . I think dull man 's faceticae be mostly cruel . 
 so , if Harry want to meet his friend , he have to do so in secret , at court - house , tavern , or various place of resort ; or in their little town , where the provincial gentry assemble . no man of spirit , she vow , could meet Mr. Washington after his base desertion of her family . she be exceedingly excited when she hear that the Colonel and her son absolutely have meet . what a heart must Harry have to give his hand to one whom she consider as little well than George 's murderer ! " for shame to say so ! for shame upon you , ungrateful boy , forget the dear , noblest , most perfect of brother , for that tall , gawky , fox - hunting Colonel , with his horrid oath ! how can he be George 's murderer , when I say my boy be not dead ? he be not dead , because my instinct never deceive I : because , as sure as I see his picture now before I , only ' tis not near so noble or so good as he use to look , so surely two night run do my papa appear to I in my dream . you doubt about that , very likely ? ' tis because you never love anybody sufficiently , my poor Harry ; else you might have leave to see they in dream , as have be vouchsafe to some . " 
 " I think I love George , mother , " cry Harry . " I have often pray that I might dream about he , and I do not . " 
 " how you can talk , sir , of love George , and then go and meet your Mr. Washington at horse - race , I can not   understand ! can you , Mountain ? " 
 " we can not   understand many thing in our neighbour ' character . I can understand that our boy be unhappy , and that he do not get strength , and that he be do no good here , in Castlewood , or mop at the tavern and court - house with horse - couper and idle company , " grumble Mountain in reply to her patroness ; and , in truth , the dependant be right . 
 there be not only grief in the Castlewood House , but there be disunion . " I can not tell how it come , " say Harry , as he bring the story to an end , which we have narrate in the last two number , and which he confide to his new - find english relative , Madame de Bernstein ; " but since that fatal day of July , last year , and my return home , my mother never have be the same woman . she seem to love none of we as she use . she be for ever praise George , and yet she do not seem as if she like he much when he be with we . she hath plunge , more deeply than ever , into her book of devotion , out of which she only manage to extract grief and sadness , as I think . such a gloom have fall over our wretched virginian house of Castlewood , that we all grow ill , and pale as ghost , who inhabit it . Mountain tell I , madam , that , for night , my mother would not close her eye . I have have she at my bedside , look so ghastly , that I have start from my own sleep , fancy a ghost before I . by one mean or other she have work herself into a state of excitement which if not delirium , be akin to it . I be again and again strike down by the fever , and all the Jesuits ' bark in America could not cure I . we have a tobacco - house and some land about the new town of Richmond , in our province , and go thither , as Williamsburg be no wholesomer than our own place ; and there I mend a little , but still do not get quite well , and the physician strongly counsel a sea - voyage . my mother , at one time , have thought of come with I , but "   and here the lad blush and hang his head down   " we do not agree very well , though I know we love each other very heartily , and ' twa determined that I should see the world for myself . so I take passage in our ship from the James River , and be land at Bristol . and ' twa only on the 9th of July , this year , at sea , as have be agree between I and Madam Esmond , that I put mourn on for my dear brother . " 
 so that little mistress of the Virginian Castlewood , for whom , I be sure , we have all the great respect , have the knack of render the people round about her uncomfortable ; quarrel with those she love well , and exercise over they her wayward jealousy and imperious humour , until they be not sorry to leave she . here be money enough , friend enough , a good position , and the respect of the world ; a house store with all manner of plenty , and good thing , and poor Harry Warrington be glad to leave they all behind he . happy ! who be happy ? what good in a stall ox for dinner every day , and no content therewith ? be it good to be love and plague by those you love , or to have an easy , comfortable indifference at home ; to follow your fancy , live there unmoleste , and die without cause any painful regret or tear ? 
 to be sure , when her boy be go , Madam Esmond forget all these little tiff and difference . to hear her speak of both her child , you would fancy they be perfect character , and have never cause she a moment 's worry or annoyance . these go , Madam fall naturally upon Mrs. Mountain and her little daughter , and worry and annoy they . but woman bear with hard word more easily than man , be more ready to forgive injury , or , perhaps , to dissemble anger . let we trust that Madam Esmond 's dependant find their life tolerable , that they give her ladyship sometimes as good as they get , that if they quarrel in the morning they be reconcile at night , and sate down to a tolerably friendly game at card and an amicable dish of tea . 
 but , without the boy , the great house of Castlewood be dreary to the widow . she leave an overseer there to manage her estate , and only pay the place an occasional visit . she enlarge and beautify her house in the pretty little city of Richmond , which begin to grow daily in importance . she have company there , and card - assembly , and preacher in plenty ; and set up her little throne there , to which the gentlefolk of the province be welcome to come and bow . all her domestic negro , who love society as negro will do , be delighted to exchange the solitude of Castlewood for the gay and merry little town ; where , for a time , and while we pursue Harry Warrington 's progress in Europe , we leave the good lady . 




 CHAPTER XIV . Harry in England 

 when the famous Trojan wanderer narrate his escape and adventure to Queen Dido , her Majesty , as we read , take the very great interest in the fascinating story - teller who tell his peril so eloquently . a history ensue , more pathetic than any of the previous occurrence in the life of Pius Aeneas , and the poor princess have reason to rue the day when she listen to that glib and dangerous orator . Harry Warrington have not pious Aeneas 's power of speech , and his elderly aunt , we may presume , be by no mean so soft - hearted as the sentimental Dido ; but yet the lad 's narrative be touching , as he deliver it with his artless eloquence and cordial voice ; and more than once , in the course of his story , Madam Bernstein find herself move to a softness to which she have very seldom before allow herself to give way . there be not many fountain in that desert of a life not many sweet , refresh resting - place . it have be a long loneliness , for the most part , until this friendly voice come and sound in her ear and cause her heart to beat with strange pang of love and sympathy . she dot on this lad , and on this sense of compassion and regard so new to she . save once , faintly , in very very early youth , she have feel no tender sentiment for any human being . such a woman would , no doubt , watch her own sensation very keenly , and must have smile after the appearance of this boy , to mark how her pulse rise above their ordinary beat . she long after he . she feel her cheek flush with happiness when he come near . her eye greet he with welcome , and follow he with fond pleasure . " ah , if she could have have a son like that , how she would have love he ! " " wait , " say Conscience , the dark scoffer mock within she , " wait , Beatrix Esmond ! you know you will weary of this inclination , as you have of all . you know , when the pass fancy have subside , that the boy may perish , and you will not   have a tear for he ; or talk , and you weary of his story ; and that your lot in life be to be lonely lonely . " well ? suppose life be a desert ? there be halting - place and shade , and refreshing water ; let we profit by they for to - day . we know that we must march when to - morrow come , and tramp on our destiny onward . 
 she smile inwardly , whilst follow the lad 's narrative , to recognise in his simple tale about his mother , trait of family resemblance . Madam Esmond be very jealous ? yes , that Harry own . she be fond of Colonel Washington ? she like he , but only as a friend , Harry declare . a hundred time he have hear his mother vow that she have no other feeling towards he . he be ashamed to have to own that he himself have be once absurdly jealous of the Colonel . " well , you will see that my half - sister will never forgive he , " say Madam Beatrix . " and you need not be surprised , sir , at woman take a fancy to man young than themselves ; for do not   I dote upon you ; and do not   all these Castlewood people crevent with jealousy ? " 
 however great might be their jealousy of Madame de Bernstein 's new favourite , the family of Castlewood allow no feeling of illwill to appear in their language or behaviour to their young guest and kinsman . after a couple of day ' stay in the ancestral house , Mr. Harry Warrington have become Cousin Harry with young and middle - aged . especially in Madame Bernstein 's presence , the Countess of Castlewood be most gracious to her kinsman , and she take many amiable private opportunity of inform the Baroness how charming the young Huron be , of vaunt the elegance of his manner and appearance , and wonder how , in his distant province , the child should ever have learn to be so polite ? 
 these note of admiration or interrogation , the Baroness take with equal complacency   speak parenthetically , and , for his own part , the present chronicler can not help put in a little respectful remark here , and signify his admiration of the conduct of lady towards one another , and of the thing which they say , which they forbear to say , and which they say behind each other 's back . with what smile and curtsey they stab each other ! with what compliment they hate each other ! with what determination of long - suffering they will not   be offend ! with what innocent dexterity they can drop the drop of poison into the cup of conversation , hand round the goblet , smile , to the whole family to drink , and make the dear , domestic circle miserable !   I burst out of my parenthesis . I fancy my Baroness and Countess smile at each other a hundred year ago , and give each other the hand or the cheek , and call each other , my dear , my dear creature , my dear Countess , my dear Baroness , my dear sister even , when they be most ready to fight . 
 " you wonder , my dear Maria , that the boy should be so polite ? " cry Madame de Bernstein . " his mother be breed up by two very perfect gentlefolk . Colonel Esmond have a certain grave courteousness , and a grand manner , which I do not see among the gentleman nowadays . " 
 " eh , my dear , we all of we praise our own time ! my grandmamma use to declare there be nothing like Whitehall and Charles the Second . " 
 " my mother see King James the Second 's court for a short while , and though not a court - educate person , as you know , her father be a country clergyman yet be exquisitely well - breed . the Colonel , her second husband , be a person of great travel and experience , as well as of learn , and have frequent the fine company of Europe . they could not go into their retreat and leave their good manner behind they , and our boy have have they as his natural inheritance . " 
 " Nay , excuse I , my dear , for think you too partial about your mother . she could not have be that perfection which your filial fondness imago . she leave off like her daughter my dear creature , you have own that she do and I can not fancy a complete woman who have a cold heart . no , no , my dear sister - in - law ! manner be very requisite , no doubt , and , for a country parson 's daughter , your mamma be very well I have see many of the cloth who be very well . Mr. Sampson , our chaplain , be very well . Dr. Young be very well . Mr. Dodd be very well ; but they have not the true air as how should they ? I protest , I beg pardon ! I forget my lord bishop , your ladyship 's first choice . but , as I say before , to be a complete woman , one must have , what you have , what I may say and bless Heaven for , I think I have a good heart . without the affection , all the world be vanity , my love ! I protest I only live , exist , eat , drink , rest , for my sweet , sweet child ! for my wicked Willy , for my self - willed Fanny , dear naughty love ! "   she rapturously kiss a bracelet on each arm which contain the miniature representation of those two young person .   " yes , Mimi ! yes , Fanchon ! you know I do , you dear , dear little thing ! and if they be to die , or you be to die , your poor mistress would die too ! " Mimi and Fanchon , two quiver italian greyhound , jump into their lady 's arm , and kiss her hand , but respect her cheek , which be cover with rouge . " no , my dear ! for nothing do I bless Heaven so much   though it put I to excruciate torture very often   as for have endow I with sensibility and a feeling heart ! " 
 " you be full of feeling , dear Anna , " say the Baroness . " you be celebrate for your sensibility . you must give a little of it to our american nephew cousin I scarce know his relationship . " 
 " Nay , I be here but as a guest in Castlewood now . the house be my Lord Castlewood 's , not mine , or his lordship be whenever he shall choose to claim it . what can I do for the young Virginian that have not be do ? he be charming . be we even jealous of he for be so , my dear ? and though we see what a fancy the Baroness de Bernstein have take for he , do your ladyship 's nephew and niece your real nephew and niece cry out ? my poor child might be mortify , for indeed , in a few hour , the charming young man have make as much way as my poor thing have be able to do in all their life : but be they angry ? willy hath take he out to ride . this morning , be not Maria play the harpsichord whilst my Fanny teach he the minuet ? ' twas a charming young group , I assure you , and it bring tear into my eye to look at the young creature . poor lad ! we be as fond of he as you be , dear Baroness ! " 
 now , Madame de Bernstein have happen , through her own ear or her maid 's , to overhear what really take place in consequence of this harmless little scene . Lady Castlewood have come into the room where the young people be thus engage in amusing and instruct themselves , accompany by her son William , who arrive in his boot from the kennel . 
 " Bravi , bravi ! oh , charming ! " say the Countess , clap her hand , nod with one of her good smile to Harry Warrington , and dart a look at his partner , which my Lady Fanny perfectly understand ; and so , perhaps , do my Lady Maria at her harpsichord , for she play with redoubled energy , and nod her wave curl , over the chord . 
 " infernal young Choctaw ! be he teach Fanny the war - dance ? and be Fan go to try her trick upon he now ? " ask Mr. William , whose temper be not of the good . 
 and that be what Lady Castlewood 's look say to Fanny . " be you go to try your trick upon he now ? " 
 she make Harry a very low curtsey , and he blush , and they both stop dancing , somewhat disconcert . Lady Maria rise from the harpsichord and walk away . 
 " Nay , go on dancing , young people ! do not   let I spoil sport , and let I play for you , " say the Countess ; and she sate down to the instrument and play . 
 " I do not   know how to dance , " say Harry , hang his head down , with a blush that the Countess 's fine carmine could not equal . 
 " and Fanny be teach you ? go on teach he , dearest Fanny ! " 
 " go on , do ! " say William , with a sidelong growl . 
 " I I have rather not show off my awkwardness in company , " add Harry , recover himself . " when I know how to dance a minuet , be sure I will ask my cousin to walk one with I . " 
 " that will be very soon , dear Cousin Warrington , I be certain , " remark the Countess , with her most gracious air . 
 " what game be she hunt now ? " think Mr. William to himself , who can not penetrate his mother 's way ; and that lady , fondly call her daughter to her elbow , leave the room . 
 they be no soon in the tapestried passage lead away to their own apartment , but Lady Castlewood 's bland tone entirely change . " you booby ! " she begin to her adore Fanny . " you double idiot ! what be you go to do with the Huron ? you do not   want to marry a creature like that , and be a squaw in a wigwam ? " 
 " do not , mamma ! " gasp Lady Fanny . Mamma be pinch her ladyship 's arm black - and - blue . " I be sure our cousin be very well , " Fanny whimper , " and you say so yourself . " 
 " very well ! yes ; and heir to a swamp , a negro , a log - cabin and a barrel of tobacco ! my Lady Frances Esmond , do you remember what your ladyship 's rank be , and what your name be , and who be your ladyship 's mother , when , at three day ' acquaintance , you commence dance a pretty dance , indeed with this brat out of Virginia ? " 
 " Mr. Warrington be our cousin , " plead Lady Fanny . 
 " a creature come from nobody know where be not your cousin ! how do we know he be your cousin ? he may be a valet who have take his master 's portmanteau , and run away in his postchaise . " 
 " but Madame de Bernstein say he be our cousin , " interpose Fanny ; " and he be the image of the Esmonds . " 
 " Madame de Bernstein have her like and dislike , take up people and forget people ; and she choose to profess a mighty fancy for this young man . because she like he to - day , be that any reason why she should like he to - morrow ? before company , and in your aunt 's presence , your ladyship will please to be as civil to he as necessary ; but , in private , I forbid you to see he or encourage he . " 
 " I do not   care , madam , whether your ladyship forbid I or not ! " cry out Lady Fanny , work up to a pitch of revolt . 
 " very good , Fanny ! then I speak to my lord , and we return to Kensington . if I can not   bring you to reason , your brother will . " 
 at this juncture the conversation between mother and daughter stop , or Madame de Bernstein 's informer have no further mean of hear or report it . 
 it be only in after day that she tell Harry Warrington a part of what she know . at present he but see that his kinsfolk receive he not unkindly . Lady Castlewood be perfectly civil to he ; the young lady pleasant and please ; my Lord Castlewood , a man of cold and haughty demeanour , be not more reserved towards Harry than to any of the rest of the family ; Mr. William be ready to drink with he , to ride with he , to go to race with he , and to play card with he . when he propose to go away , they one and all press he to stay . Madame de Bernstein do not tell he how it arise that he be the object of such eager hospitality . he do not know what scheme he be serve or disarrange , whose or what anger he be create . he fancy he be welcome because those around he be his kinsman , and never think that those could be his enemy out of whose cup he be drink , and whose hand he be press every night and morning . 




 CHAPTER XV . a Sunday at Castlewood 

 the second day after Harry 's arrival at Castlewood be a Sunday . the chapel appertain to the castle be the village church . a door from the house communicate with a great state pew which the family occupy , and here after due time they all take their place in order , whilst a rather numerous congregation from the village fill the seat below . a few ancient dusty banner hang from the church roof ; and Harry please himself in imagine that they have be bear by retainer of his family in the Commonwealth war , in which , as he know well , his ancestor have take a loyal and distinguished part . within the altar - rail be the effigy of the Esmond of the time of King James the First , the common forefather of all the group assemble in the family pew . Madame de Bernstein , in her quality of Bishop 's widow , never fail in attendance , and conduct her devotion with a gravity almost as exemplary as that of the ancestor yonder , in his square beard and red gown , for ever kneel on his stone hassock before his great marble desk and book , under his emblazon shield of arm . the clergyman , a tall , high - colour , handsome young man , read the service in a lively , agreeable voice , give almost a dramatic point to the chapter of scripture which he read . the music be good one of the young lady of the family touch the organ and would have be well but for an interruption and something like a burst of laughter from the servant ' pew , which be occasion by Mr. Warrington 's lacquey Gumbo , who , know the air give out for the psalm , begin to sing it in a voice so exceedingly loud and sweet , that the whole congregation turn towards the african warbler ; the parson himself put his handkerchief to his mouth , and the liveried gentleman from London be astonish out of all propriety . please , perhaps , with the sensation which he have create , Mr. Gumbo continue his performance until it become almost a solo , and the voice of the clerk himself be silence . for the truth be , that though Gumbo hold on to the book , along with pretty Molly , the porter 's daughter , who have be the first to welcome the stranger to Castlewood , he sing and recite by ear and not by note , and could not read a syllable of the verse in the book before he . 
 this choral performance over , a brief sermon in due course follow , which , indeed , Harry think a deal too short . in a lively , familiar , strike discourse the clergyman describe a scene of which he have be witness the previous week the execution of a horse - stealer after assize . he describe the man and his previous good character , his family , the love they bear one another , and his agony at part from they . he depict the execution in a manner startle , terrible , and picturesque . he do not introduce into his sermon the scripture phraseology , such as Harry have be accustom to hear it from those somewhat calvinistic preacher whom his mother love to frequent , but rather speak as one man of the world to other sinful people , who might be likely to profit by good advice . the unhappy man just go , have begin as a farmer of good prospect ; he have take to drinking , card - playing , horse - racing , cock - fighting , the vice of the age ; against which the young clergyman be generously indignant . then he have get to poach and to horse - stealing , for which he suffer . the divine rapidly draw striking and fearful picture of these rustic crime . he startle his hearer by show that the Eye of the Law be watch the poacher at midnight , and set trap to catch the criminal . he gallop the steal horse over highway and common , and from one county into another , but show Retribution ever gallop after , seize the malefactor in the country fair , carry he before the justice , and never unlock his manacle till he drop they at the gallow - foot . Heaven be pitiful to the sinner ! the clergyman act the scene . he whisper in the criminal 's ear at the cart . he drop his handkerchief on the clerk 's head . Harry start back as that handkerchief drop . the clergyman have be talk for more than twenty minute . Harry could have hear he for an hour more , and think he have not be five minute in the pulpit . the gentlefolk in the great pew be very much enliven by the discourse . once or twice , Harry , who could see the pew where the house servant sate , remark these very attentive ; and especially Gumbo , his own man , in an attitude of intense consternation . but the smockfrock do not seem to heed , and clamp out of church quite unconcerned . Gaffer Brown and Gammer Jones take the matter as it come , and the rosy - cheeked , red - cloak village lass sate under their broad hat entirely unmoved . my lord , from his pew , nod slightly to the clergyman in the pulpit , when that divine 's head and wig surge up from the cushion . 
 " Sampson have be strong to - day , " say his lordship . " he have assault the Philistines in great force . " 
 " beautiful , beautiful ! " say Harry . 
 " bet five to four it be his Assize sermon . he have be over to Winton to preach , and to see those dog , " cry William . 
 the organist have play the little congregation out into the sunshine . only Sir Francis Esmond , temp . Jac . I. , still kneel on his marble hassock , before his prayer - book of stone . Mr. Sampson come out of his vestry in his cassock , and nod to the gentleman still linger in the great pew . 
 " come up , and tell we about those dog , " say Mr. William , and the divine nod a laugh assent . 
 the gentleman pass out of the church into the gallery of their house , which connect they with that sacred building . Mr. Sampson make his way through the court , and presently join they . he be present by my lord to the virginian cousin of the family , Mr. Warrington : the chaplain bow very profoundly , and hop Mr. Warrington would benefit by the virtuous example of his european kinsman . be he relate to Sir Miles Warrington of Norfolk ? Sir Miles be Mr. Warrington 's father 's eld brother . what a pity he have a son ! ' twas a pretty estate , and Mr. Warrington look as if he would become a baronetcy , and a fine estate in Norfolk . 
 " tell I about my uncle , " cry Virginian Harry . 
 " tell we about those dog ! " say English Will , in a breath . 
 " two more jolly dog , two more drunken dog , save your presence , Mr. Warrington , than Sir Miles and his son , I never see . Sir Miles be a staunch friend and neighbour of Sir Robert 's . he can drink down any man in the county , except his son and a few more . the other dog about which Mr. William be anxious , for Heaven hath make he a prey to dog and all kind of bird , like the Greeks in the Iliad " 
 " I know that line in the Iliad , " say Harry , blushing . " I only know five more , but I know that one . " and his head fall . he be think , " ah , my dear brother George know all the Iliad and all the odyssey , and almost every book that be ever write besides ! " 
 " what on earth "   only he mention a place under the earth   " be you talk about now ? " ask Will of his reverence . 
 the chaplain revert to the dog and their performance . he think Mr. William 's dog be more than a match for they . from dog they go off to horse . Mr. William be very eager about the Six Year Old Plate at Huntingdon . " have you bring any news of it , Parson ? " 
 " the odd be five to four on Brilliant against the field , " say the parson , gravely , " but , mind you , Jason be a good horse . " 
 " whose horse ? " ask my lord . 
 " Duke of Ancaster 's . by Cartouche out of Miss Langley , " say the divine . " have you horse - race in Virginia , Mr. Warrington ? " 
 " have not   we ! " cry Harry ; " but oh ! I long to see a good english race ! " 
 " do you do you bet a little ? " continue his reverence . 
 " I have do such a thing , " reply Harry with a smile . 
 " I will take Brilliant even against the field , for pony with you , cousin ! " shout out Mr. William . 
 " I will give or take three to one against Jason ! " say the clergyman . 
 " I do not   bet on horse I do not   know , " say Harry , wonder to hear the chaplain now , and remember his sermon half an hour before . 
 " had not   you well write home , and ask your mother ? " say Mr. William , with a sneer . 
 " will , Will ! " call out my lord , " our cousin Warrington be free to bet , or not , as he like . have a care how you venture on either of they , Harry Warrington . Will be an old file , in spite of his smooth face , and as for Parson Sampson , I defy our ghostly enemy to get the well of he . " 
 " he and all his work , my lord ! " say Mr. Sampson , with a bow . 
 Harry be highly indignant at this allusion to his mother . " I will tell you what , cousin will , " he say , " I be in the habit of manage my own affair in my own way , without ask any lady to arrange they for I . and I be use to make my own bet upon my own judgment , and do not   need any relation to select they for I , thank you . but as I be your guest , and , no doubt , you want to show I hospitality , I will take your bet there . and so Done and do . " 
 " do , " say will , look askance . 
 " of course it be the regular odd that be in the paper which you give I , cousin ? " 
 " well , no , it be not , " growl Will . " the odd be five to four , that be the fact , and you may have them , if you like . " 
 " Nay , cousin , a bet be a bet ; and I take you , too , Mr. Sampson . " 
 " three to one against Jason . I lie it . very good , " say Mr. Sampson . 
 " be it to be pony too , Mr. Chaplain ? " ask Harry with a superb air , as if he have Lombard Street in his pocket . 
 " no , no . thirty to ten . it be enough for a poor priest to win . " 
 " here go a great slice out of my quarter 's hundred , " think Harry . " well , I shall not   let these englishman fancy that I be afraid of they . I do not   begin , but for the honour of Old Virginia I will not   go back . " 
 these pecuniary transaction arrange , William Esmond go away scowl towards the stable , where he love to take his pipe with the groom ; the brisk parson go off to pay his court to the lady , and partake of the Sunday dinner which would presently be serve . Lord Castlewood and Harry remain for a while together . since the Virginian 's arrival my lord have scarcely speak with he . in his manner he be perfectly friendly , but so silent that he would often sit at the head of his table , and leave it without utter a word . 
 " I suppose yonder property of yours be a fine one by this time ? " say my lord to Harry . 
 " I reckon it be almost as big as an English county , " answer Harry , " and the land 's as good , too , for many thing . " Harry would not have the Old Dominion , nor his share in it , underrated . 
 " indeed ! " say my lord , with a look of surprise . " when it belong to my father it do not yield much . " 
 " pardon I , my lord . you know how it belong to your father , " cry the youth , with some spirit . " it be because my grandfather do not choose to claim his right . "   this matter be discuss in the Author 's previous work , the Memoirs of Colonel Esmond . 
 " of course , of course , " say my lord , hastily . 
 " I mean , cousin , that we of the virginian house owe you nothing but our own , " continue Harry Warrington ; " but our own , and the hospitality which you be now show I . " 
 " you be heartily welcome to both . you be hurt by the betting just now ? " 
 " well , " reply the lad , " I be sort o ' hurt . your welcome , you see , be different to our welcome , and that be the fact . at home we be glad to see a man , hold out a hand to he , and give he of our good . here you take we in , give we beef and claret enough , to be sure , and do not   seem to care when we come , or when we go . that be the remark which I have be make since I have be in your lordship 's house ; I can not   help tell it out , you see , now ' tis on my mind ; and I think I be a little easy now I have say it . " and with this , the excited young fellow knock a billiard - ball across the table , and then laugh , and look at his eld kinsman . 
 " A la bonne heure ! we be cold to the stranger within and without our gate . we do not   take Mr. Harry Warrington into our arm , and cry when we see our cousin . we do not   cry when he go away but do we pretend ? " 
 " no , you do not . but you try to get the well of he in a bet , " say Harry , indignantly . 
 " be there no such practice in Virginia , and do not   sport man there try to overreach one another ? what be that story I hear you tell our aunt , of the british officer and Tom somebody of Spotsylvania ! " 
 " that be fair ! " cry Harry . " that is , it be usual practice , and a stranger must look out . I do not   mind the parson ; if he win , he may have , and welcome . but a relation ! to think that my own blood cousin want money out of I ! " 
 " a Newmarket man would get the well of his father . my brother have be on the turf since he ride over to it from Cambridge . if you play at card with he and he will if you will let he he will beat you if he can . " 
 " well , I be ready ! " cry Harry . " I will play any game with he that I know , or I will jump with he , or I will ride with he , or I will row with he , or I will wrestle with he , or I will shoot with he there now . " 
 the senior be greatly entertain , and hold out his hand to the boy . " anything , but do not   fight with he , " say my lord . 
 " if I do , I will whip he ! hang if I do not ! " cry the lad . but a look of surprise and displeasure on the nobleman 's part recall he to well sentiment . " a hundred pardon , my lord ! " he say , blush very red , and seize his cousin 's hand . " I talk of ill manner , be angry and hurt just now ; but ' tis doubly ill - mannered of I to show my anger , and boast about my prowess to my own host and kinsman . it be not the practice with we Americans to boast , believe I , it be not . " 
 " you be the first I ever meet , " say my lord , with a smile , " and I take you at your word . and I give you fair warning about the card , and the betting , that be all , my boy . " 
 " leave a Virginian alone ! we be a match for most man , we be , " resume the boy . 
 Lord Castlewood do not laugh . his eyebrow only arch for a moment , and his grey eye turn towards the ground . " so you can bet fifty guineas , and afford to lose they ? so much the well for you , cousin . those great virginian estate yield a great revenue , do they ? " 
 " More than sufficient for all of we for ten time as many as we be now , " reply Harry .   " what , he be pump I , " think the lad . 
 " and your mother make her son and heir a handsome allowance ? " 
 " as much as ever I choose to draw , my lord ! " cry Harry . 
 " Peste ! I wish I have such a mother ! " cry my lord . " but I have only the advantage of a stepmother , and she draw I . there be the dinner - bell . shall we go into the eating - room ? " and take his young friend 's arm , my lord lead he to the apartment where that meal be wait . 
 Parson Sampson form the delight of the entertainment , and amuse the lady with a hundred agreeable story . besides be chaplain to his lordship , he be a preacher in London , at the new chapel in Mayfair , for which my Lady Whittlesea   so well know in the reign of George I.   have leave an endowment . he have the choice story of all the club and coterie the very late news of who have run away with whom the last bon - mot of Mr. Selwyn the last wild bet of March and Rockingham . he know how the old king have quarrel with Madame Walmoden , and the Duke be suspect of have a new love ; who be in favour at Carlton House with the Princess of Wales , and who be hang last Monday , and how well he behave in the cart . my lord 's chaplain pour out all this intelligence to the amuse lady and the delighted young provincial , season his conversation with such plain term and lively joke as make Harry stare , who be newly arrive from the colony , and unused to the elegance of London life . the lady , old and young , laugh quite cheerfully at the lively joke . do not be frightened , ye fair reader of the present day ! we be not go to outrage your sweet modesty , or call blush on your maiden cheek . but ' tis certain that their ladyship at Castlewood never once think of be shock , but sate listen to the parson 's funny tale , until the chapel bell , clink for afternoon service , summon his reverence away for half an hour . there be no sermon . he would be back in the drinking of a bottle of Burgundy . Mr. Will call a fresh one , and the chaplain toss off a glass ere he run out . 
 Ere the half - hour be over , Mr. Chaplain be back again bawl for another bottle . this discuss , they join the lady , and a couple of card - table be set out , as , indeed , they be for many hour every day , at which the whole of the family party engage . Madame de Bernstein could beat any one of her kinsfolk at piquet , and there be only Mr. Chaplain in the whole circle who be at all a match for her ladyship . 
 in this easy manner the Sabbath - day pass . the evening be beautiful , and there be talk of adjourn to a cool tankard and a game of whist in a summer - house ; but the company vote to sit indoor , the lady declare they think the aspect of three honour in their hand , and some good court - card , more beautiful than the lovely scene of nature ; and so the sun go behind the elm , and still they be at their card ; and the rook come home caw their evensong , and they never stir except to change partner ; and the chapel clock toll hour after hour unheeded , so delightfully be they spend over the pasteboard ; and the moon and star come out ; and it be nine o'clock , and the groom of the chamber announce that supper be ready . 
 whilst they sate at that meal , the postboy 's twang horn be hear , as he trot into the village with his letter - bag . my lord 's bag be bring in presently from the village , and his letter , which he put aside , and his newspaper which he read . he smile as he come to a paragraph , look at his virginian cousin , and hand the paper over to his brother Will , who by this time be very comfortable , have have pretty good luck all the evening , and a great deal of liquor . 
 " read that , will , " say my lord . 
 Mr. William take the paper , and , read the sentence point out by his brother , utter an exclamation which cause all the lady to cry out . 
 " gracious heaven , William ! what have happen ? " cry one or the other fond sister . 
 " mercy , child , why do you swear so dreadfully ? " ask the young man 's fond mamma . 
 " what be the matter ? " inquire Madame de Bernstein , who have fall into a doze after her usual modicum of punch and beer . 
 " read it , Parson ! " say Mr. William , thrust the paper over to the chaplain , and look as fierce as a Turk . 
 " Bit , by the Lord ! " roar the chaplain , dash down the paper . 
 " Cousin Harry , you be in luck , " say my lord , take up the sheet , and read from it . " the Six Year Old Plate at Huntingdon be win by Jason , beat Brilliant , Pytho , and ginger . the odd be five to four on Brilliant against the field , three to one against Jason , seven to two against Pytho , and twenty to one against Ginger . " 
 " I owe you a half - year 's income of my poor living , Mr. Warrington , " groan the parson . " I will pay when my noble patron settle with I . " 
 " a curse upon the luck ! " growl Mr. William ; " that come of bet on a Sunday , " and he seek consolation in another great bumper . 
 " Nay , cousin Will . it be but in jest , " cry Harry . " I can not   think of take my cousin 's money . " 
 " curse I , sir , do you suppose , if I lose , I can not   pay ? " ask Mr. William ; " and that I want to be beholden to any man alive ? that be a good joke . be not   it , Parson ? " 
 " I think I have hear well , " say the clergyman ; to which William reply , " hang it , let we have another bowl . " 
 let we hope the lady do not wait for this last replenishment of liquor , for it be certain they have have plenty already during the evening . 




 CHAPTER XVI . in which Gumbo show skill with the Old English Weapon 

 our young Virginian have win these sum of money from his cousin and the chaplain , be in duty bind to give they a chance of recover their money , and I be afraid his mamma and other sound moralist would scarcely approve of his way of life . he play at card a great deal too much . besides the daily whist or quadrille with the lady , which set in soon after dinner at three o'clock , and last until supper - time , there occur game involve the gain or loss of very considerable sum of money , in which all the gentleman , my lord include , take part . since their Sunday 's conversation , his lordship be more free and confidential with his kinsman than he have previously be , bet with he quite affably , and engage he at backgammon and piquet . Mr. William and the pious chaplain like a little hazard ; though this diversion be enjoy on the sly , and unknown to the lady of the house , who have exact repeat promise from cousin will that he would not lead the Virginian into mischief , and that he would himself keep out of it . so will promise as much as his aunt or his mother choose to demand from he , give they his word that he would never play no , never ; and when the family retire to rest , Mr. Will would walk over with a dice - box and a rum - bottle to cousin Harry 's quarter , where he , and Hal , and his reverence would sit and play until daylight . 
 when Harry give to Lord Castlewood those flourish description of the maternal estate in America , he have not wish to mislead his kinsman , or to boast , or to tell falsehood , for the lad be of a very honest and truth - tell nature ; but , in his life at home , it must be own that the young fellow have have acquaintance with all sort of queer company , horse - jockey , tavern lounger , gambling and sporting man , of whom a great number be find in his native colony . a land aristocracy , with a population of negro to work their field , and cultivate their tobacco and corn , have little other way of amusement than in the hunting - field , or over the card and the punch - bowl . the hospitality of the province be unbounded : every man 's house be his neighbour 's ; and the idle gentlefolk ride from one mansion to another , find in each pretty much the same sport , welcome , and rough plenty . the virginian squire have often a barefooted valet , and a cobbled saddle ; but there be plenty of corn for the horse , and abundance of drink and venison for the master within the tumble - down fence , and behind the crack window of the hall . Harry have sleep on many a straw mattress , and engage in endless jolly night - bout over claret and punch in crack bowl till morning come , and it be time to follow the hound . his poor brother be of a much more sober sort , as the lad own with contrition . so it be that nature make folk ; and some love book and tea , and some like Burgundy and a gallop across country . our young fellow 's taste be speedily make visible to his friend in England . none of they be partial to the Puritan discipline ; nor do they like Harry the bad for not be the least of a milksop . manner , you see , be loose a hundred year ago ; tongue be vastly more free - and - easy ; name be name , and thing be do , which we should screech now to hear mention . yes , madam , we be not as our ancestor be . ought we not to thank the Fates that have improve our moral so prodigiously , and make we so eminently virtuous ? 
 so , keep a shrewd keen eye upon people round about he , and fancy , not incorrectly , that his cousin be disposed to pump he , Harry Warrington have think fit to keep his own counsel regard his own affair , and in all game of chance or matter of sport be quite a match for the three gentleman into whose company he have fall . even in the noble game of billiard he could hold his own after a few day ' play with his cousin and their revered pastor . his grandfather love the game , and have over from Europe one of the very few table which exist in his Majesty 's province of Virginia . nor , though Mr. Will could beat he at the commencement , could he get undue odd out of the young gamester . after their first bet , Harry be on his guard with Mr. Will , and cousin William own , not without respect , that the American be his match in most thing , and his well in many . but though Harry play so well that he could beat the parson , and soon be the equal of Will , who of course could beat both the girl , how come it , that in the contest with these , especially with one of they , Mr. Warrington frequently come off second ? he be profoundly courteous to every being who wear a petticoat ; nor have that traditional politeness yet leave his country . all the woman of the Castlewood establishment love the young gentleman . the grim housekeeper be mollify by he : the fat cook greet he with blowsy smile ; the ladies'-maid , whether of the French or the english nation , smirk and giggle in his behalf ; the pretty porter 's daughter at the lodge have always a kind word in reply to his . Madame de Bernstein take note of all these thing , and , though she say nothing , watch carefully the boy 's disposition and behaviour . 
 who can say how old Lady Maria Esmond be ? book of the peerage be not so many in those day as they be in our blessed time , and I can not tell to a few year , or even a lustre or two . when will use to say she be five - and - thirty , he be abusive , and , besides , be always give to exaggeration . Maria be Will 's half - sister . she and my lord be child of the late Lord Castlewood 's first wife , a german lady , whom , ' tis know , my lord marry in the time of Queen Anne 's war . Baron Bernstein , who marry Maria 's Aunt Beatrix , Bishop Tusher 's widow , be also a German , a hanoverian nobleman , and relative of the first Lady Castlewood . if my Lady Maria be bear under George I. , and his Majesty George II . have be thirty year on the throne , how could she be seven - and - twenty , as she tell Harry Warrington she be ? " I be old , child , " she use to say . she use to call Harry " child " when they be alone . " I be a hundred year old . I be seven - and - twenty . I might be your mother almost . " to which Harry would reply , " your ladyship might be the mother of all the cupid , I be sure . you do not   look twenty , on my word you do Dot ! " 
 Lady Maria look any age you like . she be a fair beauty with a dazzle white and red complexion , an abundance of fair hair which flow over her shoulder , and beautiful round arm which show to uncommon advantage when she play at billiard with cousin Harry . when she have to stretch across the table to make a stroke , that youth catch glimpse of a little ankle , a little clocked stocking , and a little black satin slipper with a little red heel , which fill he with unutterable rapture , and make he swear that there never be such a foot , ankle , clocked stocking , satin slipper in the world . and yet , oh , you foolish Harry ! your mother 's foot be ever so much more slender , and half an inch short , than Lady Maria 's . but , somehow , boy do not look at their mamma ' slipper and ankle with rapture . 
 no doubt Lady Maria be very kind to Harry when they be alone . before her sister , aunt , stepmother , she make light of he , call he a simpleton , a chit , and who know what trivial name ? behind his back , and even before his face , she mimic his accent , which smack somewhat of his province . Harry blush and correct the faulty intonation , under his english monitresse . his aunt pronounce that they would soon make he a pretty fellow . 
 Lord Castlewood , we have say , become daily more familiar and friendly with his guest and relative . till the crop be off the ground there be no sporting , except an occasional cock - match at Winchester , and a bull - baiting at Hexton Fair . Harry and will ride off to many jolly fair and race round about the young Virginian be present to some of the county family the henley of the Grange , the Crawleys of Queen 's Crawley , the Redmaynes of Lionsden , and so forth . the neighbour come in their great heavy coach , and pass two or three day in country fashion . More of they would have come , but for the fear all the Castlewood family have of offend Madame de Bernstein . she do not like country company ; the rustical society and conversation annoy she . " we shall be merry when my aunt leave we , " the young folk own . " we have cause , as you may imagine , for be very civil to she . you know what a favourite she be with our papa ? and with reason . she get he his earldom , be very well indeed at Court at that time with the King and Queen . she command here naturally , perhaps a little too much . we be all afraid of she : even my eld brother stand in awe of she , and my stepmother be much more obedient to she than she ever be to my papa , whom she rule with a rod of iron . but Castlewood be merry when our aunt be not here . at least we have much more company . you will come to we in our gay day , Harry , will not   you ? of course you will : this be your home , sir . I be so pleased oh , so pleased when my brother say he consider it be your home ! " 
 a soft hand be hold out after this pretty speech , a pair of very well preserve blue eye look exceedingly friendly . Harry grasp his cousin 's hand with ardour . I do not know what privilege of cousinship he would not like to claim , only he be so timid . they call the english selfish and cold . he at first think his relative be so : but how mistaken he be ! how kind and affectionate they be , especially the Earl , and dear , dear Maria ! how he wish he could recall that letter which he have write to Mrs. Mountain and his mother , in which he hint that his welcome have be a cold one ! the Earl his cousin be everything that be kind , have promise to introduce he to London society , and present he at Court , and at White 's . he be to consider Castlewood as his english home . he have be most hasty in his judgment regard his relative in Hampshire . all this , with many contrite expression , he write in his second despatch to Virginia . and he add , for it hath be hint that the young gentleman do not spell at this early time with especial accuracy , " my cousin , the Lady Maria , be a perfect Angle . " 
 " Ille praeter omne angulus ridet , " mutter little Mr. Dempster , at home in Virginia . 
 " the child can not   be fall in love with his angle , as he call she ! " cry out Mountain . 
 " Pooh , pooh ! my niece Maria be forty ! " say Madam Esmond . " I perfectly well recollect she when I be at home a great , gawky , carroty creature , with a foot like a pair of bellow . " where be truth , forsooth , and who knoweth it ? be Beauty beautiful , or be it only our eye that make it so ? do Venus squint ? have she get a splay - foot , red hair , and a crooked back ? anoint my eye , good Fairy Puck , so that I may ever consider the Beloved Object a paragon ! above all , keep on anoint my mistress 's dainty peeper with the very strong ointment , so that my noddle may ever appear lovely to she , and that she may continue to crown my honest ear with fresh rose ! 
 now , not only be Harry Warrington a favourite with some in the drawing - room , and all the lady of the servants'-hall , but , like master like man , his valet Gumbo be very much admire and respect by very many of the domestic circle . Gumbo have a hundred accomplishment . he be famous as a fisherman , huntsman , blacksmith . he could dress hair beautifully , and improve himself in the art under my lord 's own swiss gentleman . he be great at cook many of his virginian dish , and learn many new culinary secret from my lord 's french man . we have hear how exquisitely and melodiously he sing at church ; and he sing not only sacred but secular music , often invent air and compose rude word after the habit of his people . he play the fiddle so charmingly , that he set all the girl dance in Castlewood Hall , and be ever welcome to a gratis mug of ale at the Three Castles in the village , if he would but bring his fiddle with he . he be good - natured and love to play for the village child : so that Mr. Warrington 's negro be a universal favourite in all the Castlewood domain . 
 now it be not difficult for the servants'-hall folk to perceive that Mr. Gumbo be a liar , which fact be undoubted in spite of all his good quality . for instance , that day at church , when he pretend to read out of Molly 's psalm - book , he sing quite other word than those which be down in the book , of which he could not decipher a syllable . and he pretend to understand music , whereupon the swiss valet bring he some , and Master Gumbo turn the page upside down . these instance of long - bow practice daily occur , and be patent to all the Castlewood household . they know Gumbo be a liar , perhaps not think the bad of he for this weakness ; but they do not know how great a liar he be , and believe he much more than they have any reason for do , and because , I suppose , they like to believe he . 
 whatever might be his feeling of wonder and envy on first view the splendour and comfort of Castlewood , Mr. Gumbo keep his sentiment to himself , and examine the place , park , appointment , stable , very coolly . the horse , he say , be very well , what there be of they ; but at Castlewood in Virginia they have six time as many , and let I see , fourteen eighteen groom to look after they . Madam Esmond 's carriage be much fine than my lord 's , great deal more gold on the panel . as for her garden , they cover acre , and they grow every kind of flower and fruit under the sun . pineapple and peach ? pineapple and peach be so common , they be give to pig in his country . they have twenty forty gardener , not white gardener , all black gentleman , like hisself . in the house be twenty forty gentleman in livery , besides woman - servant never could remember how many woman - servant , dere be so many : tink dere be fifty woman - servant all Madam Esmond 's property , and worth ever so many hundred piece of eight apiece . how much be a piece of eight ? big than a guinea , a piece of eight be . Tink , Madam Esmond have twenty thirty thousand guineas a year , have whole room full of gold and plate . come to England in one of her ship ; have ever so many ship , Gumbo can not   count how many ship ; and estate , cover all over with tobacco and negro , and reach out for a week 's journey . be Master Harry heir to all this property ? of course , now Master George be kill and scalp by the Indians . Gumbo have kill ever so many Indians , and try to save Master George , but he be Master Harry 's boy , and Master Harry be as rich , oh , as rich as ever he like . he wear black now , because Master George be dead ; but you should see his chest full of gold clothe , and lace , and jewel at Bristol . of course , Master Harry be the rich man in all Virginia , and might have twenty sixty servant ; only he like travel with one good , and that one , it need scarcely be say , be Gumbo . 
 this story be not invent at once , but gradually elicit from Mr. Gumbo , who might have utter some trifling contradiction during the progress of the narrative , but by the time he have tell his tale twice or thrice in the servants'-hall or the butler 's private apartment , he be pretty perfect and consistent in his part , and know accurately the number of slave Madam Esmond keep , and the amount of income which she enjoy . the truth be , that as four or five black be require to do the work of one white man , the domestic in american establishment be much more numerous than in our ; and , like the house of most other virginian landed proprietor , Madam Esmond 's mansion and stable swarm with negro .
