 Mr. Gumbo 's account of his mistress 's wealth and splendour be carry to my lord by his lordship 's man , and to Madame de Bernstein and my lady by their respective waiting - woman , and , we may be sure , lose nothing in the telling . a young gentleman in England be not the less liked because he be repute to be the heir to vast wealth and possession ; when Lady Castlewood come to hear of Harry 's prodigious expectation , she repent of her first cool reception of he , and of have pinch her daughter 's arm till it be black - and - blue for have be extend towards the youth in too friendly a manner . be it too late to have he back into those fair arm ? Lady Fanny be welcome to try , and resume the dancing - lesson . the Countess would play the music with all her heart . but , how provoking ! that odious , sentimental Maria would always insist upon be in the room ; and , as sure as Fanny walk in the garden or the park , so sure would her sister come trail after she . as for Madame de Bernstein , she laugh , and be amuse at the story of the prodigious fortune of her virginian relative . she know her half - sister 's man of business in London , and very likely be aware of the real state of Madame Esmond 's money matter ; but she do not contradict the rumour which Gumbo and his fellow - servant have set afloat ; and be not a little divert by the effect which these report have upon the behaviour of the Castlewood family towards their young kinsman . 
 " hang he ! be he so rich , Molly ? " say my lord to his eld sister . " then good - bye to our chance with your aunt . the Baroness will be sure to leave he all her money to spite we , and because he do not   want it . nevertheless , the lad be a good lad enough , and it be not his fault be rich , you know . " 
 " he be very simple and modest in his habit for one so wealthy , " remark Maria . 
 " rich people often be so , " say my lord . " if I be rich , I often think I would be the great miser , and live in rag and on a crust . depend on it there be no pleasure so enduring as money - getting . it grow on you , and increase with old age . but because I be as poor as Lazarus , I dress in purple and fine linen , and fare sumptuously every day . " 
 Maria go to the book - room and get the history of Virginia , by R. B. Gent and read therein what an admirable climate it be , and how all kind of fruit and corn grow in that province , and what noble river be those of Potomac and Rappahannoc , abound in all sort of fish . and she wonder whether the climate would agree with she , and whether her aunt would like she ? and Harry be sure his mother would adore she , so would mountain . and when he be ask about the number of his mother 's servant , he say , they certainly have more servant than be see in England he do not know how many . but the negro do not do near as much work as english servant do hence the necessity of keep so great a number . as for some other of Gumbo 's detail which be bring to he , he laugh and say the boy be wonderful as a romancer , and in tell such story he suppose be try to speak out for the honour of the family . 
 so Harry be modest as well as rich ! his denial only serve to confirm his relative ' opinion regard his splendid expectation . More and more the Countess and the lady be friendly and affectionate with he . More and more Mr. will bet with he , and want to sell he bargain . Harry 's simple dress and equipage only serve to confirm his friend ' idea of his wealth . to see a young man of his rank and mean with but one servant , and without horse or a carriage of his own what modesty ! when he go to London he would cut a well figure ? of course he would . Castlewood would introduce he to the good society in the capital , and he would appear as he ought to appear at St. James 's . no man could be more pleasant , wicked , lively , obsequious than the worthy chaplain , Mr. Sampson . how proud he would be if he could show his young friend a little of London life ! if he could warn rogue off he , and keep he out of the way of harm ! Mr. Sampson be very kind : everybody be very kind . Harry like quite well the respect that be pay to he . as Madam Esmond 's son he think perhaps it be his due : and take for grant that he be the personage which his family imagine he to be . how should he know well , who have never as yet see any place but his own province , and why should he not respect his own condition when other people respect it so ? so all the little knot of people at Castlewood House , and from these the people in Castlewood village , and from thence the people in the whole county , choose to imagine that Mr. Harry Esmond Warrington be the heir of immense wealth , and a gentleman of very great importance , because his negro valet tell lie about he in the servants'-hall . 
 Harry 's aunt , Madame de Bernstein , after a week or two , begin to tire of Castlewood and the inhabitant of that mansion , and the neighbour who come to visit they . this clever woman tired of most thing and people soon or later . so she take to nodding and sleep over the chaplain 's story , and to doze at her whist and over her dinner , and to be very snappish and sarcastic in her conversation with her Esmond nephew and niece , hit out blow at my lord and his brother the jockey , and my lady , widow and unmarried , who wince under her scornful remark , and bear they as they good might . the cook , whom she have so praise on first come , now give she no satisfaction ; the wine be cork ; the house be damp , dreary , and full of draught ; the door would not shut , and the chimney be smoky . she begin to think the Tunbridge water be very necessary for she , and order the doctor , who come to she from the neighbouring town of Hexton , to order those water for her benefit . 
 " I wish to heaven she would go ! " growl my lord , who be the most independent member of his family . " she may go to Tunbridge , or she may go to Bath , or she may go to Jericho , for I . " 
 " Shall Fanny and I come with you to Tunbridge , dear baroness ? " ask Lady Castlewood of her sister - in - law . 
 " not for world , my dear ! the doctor order I absolute quiet , and if you come I should have the knocker go all day , and Fanny 's lover would never be out of the house , " answer the Baroness , who be quite weary of Lady Castlewood 's company . 
 " I wish I could be of any service to my aunt ! " say the sentimental Lady Maria , demurely . 
 " my good child , what can you do for I ? you can not play piquet so well as my maid , and I have hear all your song till I be perfectly tired of they ! one of the gentleman might go with I : at least make the journey , and see I safe from highwayman . " 
 " I be sure , ma'am , I shall be glad to ride with you , " say Mr. Will . 
 " oh , not you ! I do not   want you , William , " cry the young man 's aunt . " why do not you offer , and where be your american manner , you ungracious Harry Warrington ? do not   swear , Will , Harry be much well company than you be , and much well ton too , sir . " 
 " Tong , indeed ! confound his tong , " growl envious Will to himself . 
 " I dare say I shall be tired of he , as I be of other folk , " continue the Baroness . " I have scarcely see Harry at all in these last day . you shall ride with I to Tunbridge , Harry ! " 
 at this direct appeal , and to no one 's wonder more than that of his aunt , Mr. Harry Warrington blush , and hem and ha'd and at length say , " I have promise my cousin Castlewood to go over to Hexton Petty Sessions with he to - morrow . he think I should see how the Courts here be conduct and and the partridge - shooting will soon begin , and I have promise to be here for that , ma'am . " say which word , Harry Warrington look as red as a poppy , whilst Lady Maria hold her meek face downward , and nimbly ply her needle . 
 " you actually refuse to go with I to Tunbridge Wells ? " call out Madame Bernstein , her eye lighten , and her face flush up with anger , too . 
 " not to ride with you , ma'am ; that I will do with all my heart ; but to stay there I have promise ... " 
 " enough , enough , sir ! I can go alone , and do not   want your escort , " cry the irate old lady , and rustle out of the room . 
 the Castlewood family look at each other with wonder . will whistle . Lady Castlewood glance at Fanny , as much as to say , his chance be over . Lady Maria never lift up her eye from her tambour - frame . 




 CHAPTER XVII . on the Scent 

 Young Harry Warrington 's act of revolt come so suddenly upon Madame de Bernstein , that she have no other way of reply to it , than by the prompt outbreak of anger with which we leave she in the last chapter . she dart two fierce glance at Lady Fanny and her mother as she quit the room . Lady Maria over her tambour - frame escape without the least notice , and scarcely lift up her head from her embroidery , to watch the aunt retreating , or the look which mamma - in - law and sister throw at one another . 
 " so , in spite of all , you have , madam ? " the maternal look seem to say . 
 " have what ? " ask Lady Fanny 's eye . but what good in look innocent ? she look puzzle . she do not look one - tenth part as innocent as Maria . have she be guilty , she would have look not guilty much more cleverly ; and would have take care to study and compose a face so as to be ready to suit the plea . whatever be the expression of Fanny 's eye , mamma glare on she as if she would have like to tear they out . 
 but Lady Castlewood could not operate upon the said eye then and there , like the barbarous monster in the stage - direction in King Lear . when her ladyship be go to tear out her daughter 's eye , she would retire smile , with an arm round her dear child 's waist , and then gouge she in private . 
 " so you do not   fancy go with the old lady to Tunbridge Wells ? " be all she say to Cousin Warrington , wear at the same time a perfectly well - breed simper on her face . 
 " and small blame to our cousin ! " interpose my lord .   the face over the tambour - frame look up for one instant .   " a young fellow must not have it all idle and holiday . let he mix up something useful with his pleasure , and go to the fiddle and pump - room at Tunbridge or the bath later . Mr. Warrington have to conduct a great estate in America : let he see how our in England be carry on . will hath show he the kennel and the stable ; and the game in vogue , which I think , cousin , you seem to play as well as your teacher . after harvest we will show he a little English fowling and shooting : in winter we will take he out a - hunting . though there have be a coolness between we and our aunt - kinswoman in Virginia , yet we be of the same blood . Ere we send our cousin back to his mother , let we show he what an english gentleman 's life at home be . I should like to read with he as well as sport with he , and that be why I have be press he of late to stay and bear I company . " 
 my lord speak with such perfect frankness that his mother - in - law and half - brother and sister could not help wonder what his meaning could be . the three last - name person often hold little conspiracy together , and cabal or grumble against the head of the house . when he adopt that frank tone , there be no fathom his meaning : often it would not be discover until month have pass . he do not say , " this be true , " but , " I mean that this statement should be accept and believe in my family . " it be then a thing convenue , that my Lord Castlewood have a laudable desire to cultivate the domestic affection , and to educate , amuse , and improve his young relative ; and that he have take a great fancy to the lad , and wish that Harry should stay for some time near his lordship . 
 " what be Castlewood 's game now ? " ask William of his mother and sister as they disappear into the corridor . " stop ! by George , I have it ! " 
 " what , William ? " 
 " he intend to get he to play , and to win the Virginia estate back from he . that be what it be ! " 
 " but the lad have not get the Virginia estate to pay , if he lose , " remark mamma . 
 " if my brother have not some scheme in view , may I be . " 
 " Hush ! of course he have a scheme in view . but what be it ? " 
 " he can not   mean Maria Maria be as old as Harry 's mother , " muse Mr. William . 
 " Pooh ! with her old face and sandy hair and freckle skin ! impossible ! " cry Lady Fanny , with somewhat of a sigh . 
 " of course , your ladyship have a fancy for the Iroquois , too ! " cry mamma . 
 " I trust I know my station and duty well , madam ! if I have like he , that be no reason why I should marry he . your ladyship hath teach I as much as that . " 
 " my Lady Fanny ! " 
 " I be sure you marry our papa without like he . you have tell I so a thousand time ! " 
 " and if you do not love our father before marriage , you certainly do not fall in love with he afterwards , " break in Mr. William , with a laugh . " Fan and I remember how our honour parent use to fight . do not   we , Fan ? and our brother Esmond keep the peace . " 
 " do not   recall those dreadful low scene , William ! " cry mamma . " when your father take too much drink , he be like a madman ; and his conduct should be a warning to you , sir , who be fond of the same horrid practice . " 
 " I be sure , madam , you be not much the happy for marry the man you do not like , and your ladyship 's title hath bring very little along with it , " whimper out Lady Fanny . " what be the use of a coronet with the jointure of a tradesman 's wife ? how many of they be rich than we be ? there be come lately to live in our Square , at Kensington , a grocer 's widow from London Bridge , whose daughter have three gown where I have one ; and who , though they be wait on but by a man and a couple of maid , I know eat and drink a thousand time well than we do with our scrap of cold meat on our plate , and our great flaunting , trapesing , impudent , lazy lacquey ! " 
 " he ! he ! glad I dine at the palace , and not at home ! " say Mr. Will .   Mr. Will , through his aunt 's interest with Count Puffendorff , Groom of the Royal   and Serene Electoral   Powder - Closet , have one of the many small place at Court , that of Deputy Powder . 
 " why should I not be happy without any title except my own ? " continue Lady Frances . " many people be . I dare say they be even happy in America . " 
 " yes ! with a mother - in - law who be a perfect Turk and Tartar , for all I hear with indian war - whoop howl all around you and with a danger of lose your scalp , or of be eat up by a wild beast every time you go to church . " 
 " I would not   go to church , " say Lady Fanny . 
 " you would go with anybody who ask you , Fan ! " roar out Mr. will : " and so would old Maria , and so would any woman , that be the fact . " and will laugh at his own wit . 
 " pray , good folk , what be all your merriment about ? " here ask Madame Bernstein , peep in on her relative from the tapestried door which lead into the gallery where their conversation be hold . 
 will tell she that his mother and sister have be have a fight   which be not a novelty , as Madame Bernstein know , because Fanny want to marry their cousin , the wild Indian , and my lady Countess would not let she . Fanny protest against this statement . since the very first day when her mother have tell she not to speak to the young gentleman , she have scarcely exchange two word with he . she know her station well . she do not want to be scalp by wild Indians , or eat up by bear . 
 Madame de Bernstein look puzzle . " if he be not stay for you , for whom be he stay ? " she ask . " at the house to which he have be carry , you have take care not to show he a woman that be not a fright or in the nursery ; and I think the boy be too proud to fall in love with a dairymaid , Will . " 
 " Humph ! that be a matter of taste , ma'am , " say Mr. William , with a shrug of his shoulder . 
 " of Mr. William Esmond 's taste , as you say ; but not of yonder boy 's . the esmond of his grandfather 's nurture , sir , would not go a - courting in the kitchen . " 
 " well , ma'am , every man to his taste , I say again . a fellow might go far and fare bad than my brother 's servants'-hall , and besides Fan , there be only the maid or old Maria to choose from . " 
 " Maria ! impossible ! " and yet , as she speak the very word , a sudden thought cross Madame Bernstein 's mind , that this elderly Calypso might have captivate her young Telemachus . she call to mind half a dozen instance in her own experience of young man who have be infatuate by old woman . she remember how frequent Harry Warrington 's absence have be of late absence which she attribute to his love for field sport . she remember how often , when he be absent , Maria Esmond be away too . walk in cool avenue , whispering in garden temple , or behind clipt hedge , casual squeeze of the hand in twilight corridor , or sweet glance and ogle in meeting on the stair , a lively fancy , an intimate knowledge of the world , very likely a considerable personal experience in early day , suggest all these possibility and chance to Madame de Bernstein , just as she be say that they be impossible . 
 " Impossible , ma'am ! I do not   know , " will continue . " my mother warn Fan off he . " 
 " oh , your mother do warn Fanny off ? " 
 " certainly , my dear Baroness ! " 
 " do not   she ? do not   she pinch Fanny 's arm black - and - blue ? do not   they fight about it ? " 
 " Nonsense , William ! for shame , William ! " cry both the implicate lady in a breath . 
 " and now , since we have hear how rich he be , perhaps it be sour grape , that be all . and now , since he be warn off the young bird , perhaps he be hunt the old one , that be all . impossible why impossible ? you know old Lady Suffolk , ma'am ? " 
 " William , how can you speak about Lady Suffolk to your aunt ? " 
 a grin pass over the countenance of the young gentleman . " because Lady Suffolk be a special favourite at Court ? well , other folk have succeed she . " 
 " Sir ! " cry Madame de Bernstein , who may have have her reason to take offence . 
 " so they have , I say ; or who , pray , be my Lady Yarmouth now ? and do not   old Lady Suffolk go and fall in love with George Berkeley , and marry he when she be ever so old ? Nay , ma'am , if I remember right and we hear a deal of town - talk at our table Harry Estridge go mad about your ladyship when you be somewhat rise twenty ; and would have change your name a third time if you would but have let he . " 
 this allusion to an adventure of her own later day , which be , indeed , pretty notorious to all the world , do not anger Madame de Bernstein , like Will be former hint about his aunt have be a favourite at George the Second 's Court ; but , on the contrary , set she in good - humour . 
 " Au fait , " she say , musing , as she play a pretty little hand on the table , and no doubt think about mad young Harry Estridge ; " ' tis not impossible , William , that old folk , and young folk , too , should play the fool . " 
 " but I can not   understand a young fellow be in love with Maria , " continue Mr. William , " however he might be with you , ma'am . that be oter shose , as our french tutor use to say . you remember the Count , ma'am ; he ! he ! and so do Maria ! " 
 " William ! " 
 " and I dare say the Count remember the bastinado Castlewood have give to he . a confound french dancing - master call himself a count , and dare to fall in love in our family ! whenever I want to make myself uncommonly agreeable to old Maria , I just say a few word of parly voo to she . she know what I mean . " 
 " have you abuse she to your cousin , Harry Warrington ? " ask Madame de Bernstein . 
 " well I know she be always abuse I and I have say my mind about she , " say will . 
 " oh , you idiot ! " cry the old lady . " who but a gaby ever speak ill of a woman to her sweetheart ? he will tell she everything , and they both will hate you . " 
 " the very thing , ma'am ! " cry Will , burst into a great laugh . " I have a sort of a suspicion , you see , and two day ago , as we be rid together , I tell Harry Warrington a bit of my mind about Maria ; why should not   I , I say ? she be always abuse I , be not   she , Fan ? and your favourite turn as red as my plush waistcoat wonder how a gentleman could malign his own flesh and blood , and , tremble all over with rage , say I be no true Esmond . " 
 " why do not   you chastise he , sir , as my lord do the dancing - master ? " cry Lady Castlewood . 
 " well , mother , you see that at quarter - staff there be two stick use , " reply Mr. William ; " and my opinion be , that Harry Warrington can guard his own head uncommonly well . perhaps that be one of the reason why I do not offer to treat my cousin to a caning . and now you say so , ma'am , I know he have tell Maria . she have be look battle , murder , and sudden death at I ever since . all which show " and here he turn to his aunt . 
 " all which show what ? " 
 " that I think we be on the right scent ; and that we have   find Maria the old fox ! " and the ingenuous youth here clap his hand to his mouth , and give a loud halloo . 
 how far have this pretty intrigue go ? now be the question . Mr. Will say , that at her age , Maria would be for conduct matter as rapidly as possible , not have much time to lose . there be not a great deal of love lose between Will and his half - sister . 
 " who would sift the matter to the bottom ? scold one party or the other be of no avail . threat only serve to aggravate people in such case . I never be in danger but once , young people , " say Madame de Bernstein , " and I think that be because my poor mother contradict I . if this boy be like other of his family , the more we oppose he , the more entete he will be ; and we shall never get he out of his scrape . " 
 " Faith , ma'am , suppose we leave he in it ? " grumble Will . " Old Maria and I do not   love each other too much , I grant you ; but an English earl 's daughter be good enough for an american tobacco - planter , when all be say and do . " 
 here his mother and sister break out . they would not hear of such a union . to which will answer , " you be like the dog in the manger . you do not   want the man yourself , Fanny " 
 " I want he , indeed ! " cry Lady Fanny , with a toss of her head . 
 " then why grudge he to Maria ? I think Castlewood want she to have he . " 
 " why grudge he to Maria , sir ? " cry Madame de Bernstein , with great energy . " do you remember who the poor boy be , and what your house owe to his family ? his grandfather be the good friend your father ever have , and give up this estate , this title , this very castle , in which you be conspire against the friendless virginian lad , that you and yours might profit by it . and the reward for all this kindness be , that you all but shut the door on the child when he knock at it , and talk of marry he to a silly elderly creature who might be his mother ! he shall not   marry she . " 
 " the very thing we be say and think , my dear baroness ! " interpose Lady Castlewood . " our part of the family be not eager about the match , though my lord and Maria may be . " 
 " you would like he for yourself , now that you hear he be rich and may be rich , young people , mind you that , " cry Madam Beatrix , turn upon the other woman . 
 " Mr. Warrington may be ever so rich , madam , but there be no need why your ladyship should perpetually remind we that we be poor , " break in Lady Castlewood , with some spirit . " at least there be very little disparity in Fanny 's age and Mr. Harry 's ; and you surely will be the last to say that a lady of our name and family be not good enough for any gentleman bear in Virginia or elsewhere . " 
 " let fanny take an english gentleman , Countess , not an American . with such a name and such a mother to help she , and with all her good look and accomplishment , sure , she can not   fail of find a man worthy of she . but from what I know about the daughter of this house , and what I imagine about our young cousin , I be certain that no happy match could be make between they . " 
 " what do my aunt know about I ? " ask Lady Fanny , turn very red . 
 " only your temper , my dear . you do not   suppose that I believe all the tittle - tattle and scandal which one can not help hear in town ? but the temper and early education be sufficient . only fancy one of you condemn to leave St. James 's and the Mall , and live in a plantation surround by savage ! you would die of ennui , or worry your husband 's life out with your ill - humour . you be bear , lady , to ornament court not wigwam . let this lad go back to his wilderness with a wife who be suit to he . " 
 the other two lady declare in a breath that , for their part , they desire no well , and , after a few more word , go on their way , while Madame de Bernstein , lift up her tapestried door , retire into her own chamber . she see all the scheme now ; she admire the way of woman , call a score of little circumstance back to mind . she wonder at her own blindness during the last few day , and that she should not have perceive the rise and progress of this queer little intrigue . how far have it go ? be now the question . be Harry 's passion of the serious and tragical sort , or a mere fire of straw which a day or two would burn out ? how deeply be he commit ? she dread the strength of Harry 's passion , and the weakness of Maria 's . a woman of her age be so desperate , Madame Bernstein may have think , that she will make any effort to secure a lover . scandal , bah ! she will retire and be a princess in Virginia , and leave the folk in England to talk as much scandal as they choose . 
 be there always , then , one thing which woman do not tell to one another , and about which they agree to deceive each other ? do the concealment arise from deceit or modesty ? a man , as soon as he feel an inclination for one of the other sex , seek for a friend of his own to whom he may impart the delightful intelligence . a woman   with more or less skill   burie her secret away from her kind . for day and week past , have not this old Maria make fool of the whole house , Maria , the butt of the family ? 
 I forbear to go into too curious inquiry regard the Lady Maria 's antecedent . I have my own opinion about Madame Bernstein 's . a hundred year ago people of the great world be not so straitlaced as they be now , when everybody be good , pure , moral , modest ; when there be no skeleton in anybody 's closet ; when there be no scheming ; no slurring over old story ; when no girl try to sell herself for wealth , and no mother abet she . suppose my Lady Maria try to make her little game , wherein be her ladyship 's great eccentricity ? 
 on these point no doubt the Baroness de Bernstein thought , as she commune with herself in her private apartment . 




 CHAPTER XVIII . an Old Story 

 as my Lady Castlewood and her son and daughter pass through one door of the saloon where they have all be seat , my Lord Castlewood depart by another issue ; and then the demure eye look up from the tambour - frame on which they have persist hitherto in examine the innocent violet and jonquil . the eye look up at Harry Warrington , who stand at an ancestral portrait under the great fireplace . he have gather a great heap of blush   those flower which bloom so rarely after gentlefolk ' springtime , and with they ornament his honest countenance , his cheek , his forehead , nay , his youthful ear . 
 " why do you refuse to go with our aunt , cousin ? " ask the lady of the tambour frame . 
 " because your ladyship bid I stay , " answer the lad . 
 " I bid you stay ! La ! child ! what one say in fun , you take in earnest ! be all you virginian gentleman so obsequious as to fancy every idle word a lady say be a command ? Virginia must be a pleasant country for our sex if it be so ! " 
 " you say when when we walk in the terrace two night since , o heaven ! " cry Harry , with a voice tremble with emotion . 
 " ah , that sweet night , cousin ! " cry the Tambour - frame . 
 " Whe whe when you give I this rise from your own neck , " roar out Harry , pull suddenly a crumpled and decay vegetable from his waistcoat " which I will never part with with , no , by heaven , whilst this heart continue to beat ! you say , ' Harry , if your aunt ask you to go away , you will go , and if you go , you will forget I . ' do not   you say so ? " 
 " all man forget ! " say the Virgin , with a sigh . 
 " in this cold selfish country they may , cousin , not in ours , " continue Harry , yet in the same state of exaltation " I have rather have lose an arm almost than refuse the old lady . I tell you it go to my heart to say no to she , and she so kind to I , and who have be the mean of introduce I to to o heaven ! " 
  here a kick to an intervene spaniel , which fly yelp from before the fire , and a rapid advance on the tambour - frame .   " look here , cousin ! if you be to bid I jump out of yonder window , I should do it ; or murder , I should do it . " 
 " La ! but you need not squeeze one 's hand so , you silly child ! " remark Maria . 
 " I can not   help it we be so in the south . where my heart be , I can not   help speak my mind out , cousin and you know where that heart be ! ever since that evening that o heaven ! I tell you I have hardly sleep since I want to do something to distinguish myself to be ever so great . I wish there be giant , Maria , as I have read of in in book , that I could go and fight them . I wish you be in distress , that I might help you , somehow . I wish you want my blood , that I might spend every drop of it for you . and when you tell I not to go with Madame Bernstein ... " 
 " I tell thee , child ? never . " 
 " I think you tell I . you say you know I prefer my aunt to my cousin , and I say then what I say now , ' Incomparable Maria ! I prefer thee to all the woman in the world and all the angel in Paradise and I would go anywhere , be it to dungeon , if you order I ! ' and do you think I would not stay anywhere , when you only desire that I should be near you ? " he add , after a moment 's pause . 
 " man always talk in that way that be , that is , I have hear so , " say the spinster , correct herself ; " for what should a country - breed woman know about you creature ? when you be near we , they say you be all rapture and flame and promise and I do not   know what ; when you be away , you forget all about we . " 
 " but I think I never want to go away as long as I live , " groan out the young man . " I have tired of many thing ; not book and that , I never care for study much , but game and sport which I use to be fond of when I be a boy . before I see you , it be to be a soldier I most desire ; I tear my hair with rage when my poor dear brother go away instead of I on that expedition in which we lose he . but now , I only care for one thing in the world , and you know what that be . " 
 " you silly child ! do not   you know I be almost old enough to be ... ? " 
 " I know I know ! but what be that to I ? have not   your br ... well , never mind who , some of them - tell I story against you , and do not   they show I the Family Bible , where all your name be down , and the date of your birth ? " 
 " the coward ! who do that ? " cry out Lady Maria . " Dear Harry , tell I who do that ? be it my mother - in - law , the grasping , odious , abandon , brazen harpy ? do you know all about she ? how she marry my father in his cup the horrid hussey ! and ... " 
 " indeed it be not   Lady Castlewood , " interpose the wonder Harry . 
 " then it be my aunt , " continue the infuriate lady . " a pretty moralist , indeed ! a bishop 's widow , forsooth , and I should like to know whose widow before and afterwards . why , Harry , she intrigue : with the Pretender , and with the Court of Hanover , and , I dare say , would with the Court of Rome and the Sultan of Turkey if she have have the mean . do you know who her second husband be ? a creature who ... " 
 " but our aunt never speak a word against you , " break in Harry , more and more amazed at the nymph 's vehemence . 
 she check her anger . in the inquisitive countenance opposite to she she think she read some alarm as to the temper which she be exhibit . 
 " well , well ! I be a fool , " she say . " I want thee to think well of I , Harry ! " 
 a hand be somehow put out and seize and , no doubt , kiss by the rapturous youth . " Angel ! " he cry , look into her face with his eager , honest eye . 
 two fish - pool irradiate by a pair of star would not kindle to great warmth than do those elderly orb into which Harry pour his gaze . nevertheless , he plunge into their blue depth , and fancy he see heaven in their calm brightness . so that silly dog   of whom Aesop or the Spelling - book use to tell we in youth   behold a beef - bone in the pond , and snap at it , and lose the beef - bone he be carry . o absurd cur ! he see the beefbone in his own mouth reflect in the treacherous pool , which dimple , I dare say , with ever so many smile , coolly suck up the meat , and return to its usual placidity . ah ! what a heap of wreck lie beneath some of those quiet surface ! what treasure we have drop into they ! what chase golden dish , what precious jewel of love , what bone after bone , and sweetest heart 's flesh ! do not some very faithful and unlucky dog jump in bodily , when they be swallow up head and tail entirely ? when some woman come to be drag , it be a marvel what will be find in the depth of they . Cavete , cane ! have a care how ye lap that water . what do they want with we , the mischievous siren slut ? a green - eyed Naiad never rest until she have inveigle a fellow under the water ; she sing after he , she dance after he ; she wind round he , glitter tortuously ; she warble and whisper dainty secret at his cheek , she kiss his foot , she leer at he from out of her rush : all her bed sigh out , " come , sweet youth ! Hither , hither , rosy Hylas ! " pop go Hylas .   surely the fable be renew for ever and ever ?   have his captivator any pleasure ? Doth she take any account of he ? no more than a fisherman landing at Brighton do of one out of a hundred thousand herring .... the last time . Ulysses row by the Sirens ' bank , he and his man do not care though a whole shoal of they be sing and comb their long lock . Young Telemachus be for jump overboard : but the tough old crew hold the silly , bawl lad . they be deaf , and could not hear his bawling nor the sea - nymph ' singing . they be dim of sight , and do not see how lovely the witch be . the stale , old , leer witch ! away with ye ! I dare say you have paint your cheek by this time ; your wretched old song be as out of fashion as Mozart , and it be all false hair you be comb ! 
 in the last sentence you see Lector Benevolus and Scriptor Doctissimus figure as tough old Ulysses and his tough old Boatswain , who do not care a quid of tobacco for any Siren at Sirens ' point ; but Harry Warrington be green Telemachus , who , be sure , be very unlike the soft youth in the good Bishop of Cambray 's twaddling story . he do not see that the siren paint the lash from under which she ogle he ; will put by into a box when she have do the ringlet into which she would inveigle he ; and if she eat he , as she propose to do , will crunch his bone with a new set of grinder just from the dentist 's , and warrant for mastication . the song be not stale to Harry Warrington , nor the voice crack or out of tune that sing it . but but oh , dear I , Brother Boatswain ! do not   you remember how pleasant the opera be when we first hear it ? Cosi fan tutti be its name Mozart 's music . now , I dare say , they have other word , and other music , and other singer and fiddler , and another great crowd in the pit . well , well , Cosi fan tutti be still upon the bill , and they be go on sing it over and over and over . 
 any man or woman with a pennyworth of brain , or the like precious amount of personal experience , or who have read a novel before , must , when Harry pull out those fade vegetable just now , have go off into a digression of his own , as the writer confess for himself he be diverge whilst he have be write the last brace of paragraph . if he see a pair of lover whisper in a garden alley or the embrasure of a window , or a pair of glance shoot across the room from Jenny to the artless Jessamy , he fall to musing on former day when , etc . etc . these thing follow each other by a general law , which be not as old as the hill , to be sure , but as old as the people who walk up and down they . when , I say , a lad pull a bunch of amputated and now decompose green from his breast and fall to kiss it , what be the use of say much more ? as well tell the market - gardener 's name from whom the slip - rose be buy the watering , clipping , trimming , manuring , the plant have undergo as tell how Harry Warrington come by it . Rose , elle a vecu la vie des rose , have be trim , have be water , have be pot , have be stick , have be cut , wear , give away , transfer to yonder boy 's pocket - book and bosom , accord to the law and fate appertain to rose . 
 and how come Maria to give it to Harry ? and how do he come to want it and to prize it so passionately when he get the bit of rubbish ? be not one story as stale as the other ? be not they all alike ? what be the use , I say , of tell they over and over ? Harry value that rise because Maria have ogle he in the old way ; because she have happen to meet he in the garden in the old way ; because he have take her hand in the old way ; because they have whisper to one another behind the old curtain   the gap old rag , as if everybody could not peep through it ! ; because , in this delicious weather , they have happen to be early riser and go into the park ; because dear Goody Jenkins in the village happen to have a bad knee , and my lady Maria go to read to she , and give her calves'-foot jelly , and because somebody , of course , must carry the basket . whole chapter might have be write to chronicle all these circumstance , but a quoi bon ? the incident of life , and love - making especially , I believe to resemble each other so much , that I be surprised , gentleman and lady , you read novel any more . Psha ! of course that rise in young Harry 's pocket - book have grow , and have bud , and have bloom , and be now rot , like other rose . I suppose you will want I to say that the young fool kiss it next ? of course he kiss it . what be lip make for , pray , but for smile and simpering , and   possibly   humbug , and kissing , and opening to receive mutton - chop , cigar , and so forth ? I can not write this part of the story of our Virginians , because Harry do not dare to write it himself to anybody at home , because , if he write any letter to Maria   which , of course , he do , as they be in the same house , and might meet each other as much as they like , they be destroy ; because he afterwards choose to be very silent about the story , and we can not   have it from her ladyship , who never tell the truth about anything . but cui bono ? I say again . what be the good of tell the story ? my gentle reader , take your story : take mine . to - morrow it shall be Miss Fanny 's , who be just walk away with her doll to the schoolroom and the governess   poor victim ! she have a version of it in her desk : and next day it shall be Baby 's , who be bawl out on the stair for his bottle . 
 Maria might like to have and exercise power over the young Virginian ; but she do not want that Harry should quarrel with his aunt for her sake , or that Madame de Bernstein should be angry with she . Harry be not the Lord of Virginia yet : he be only the Prince , and the Queen might marry and have other Princes , and the law of primogeniture might not be establish in Virginia , qu'en savait elle ? my lord her brother and she have exchange no word at all about the delicate business . but they understand each other , and the Earl have a way of understand thing without speak . he know his Maria perfectly well : in the course of a life of which not a little have be spend in her brother 's company and under his roof , Maria 's disposition , way , trick , fault , have come to be perfectly understand by the head of the family ; and she would find her little scheme check or aid by he , as to his lordship seem good , and without need of any word between they . thus three day before , when she happen to be go to see that poor dear old Goody , who be ill with the sore knee in the village   and when Harry Warrington happen to be walk behind the elm on the green too , my lord with his dog about he , and his gardener walk after he , cross the court , just as Lady Maria be trip to the gate - house and his lordship call his sister , and say : " molly , you be go to see Goody Jenkins . you be a charitable soul , my dear . give Gammer Jenkins this half - crown for I unless our cousin , Warrington , have already give her money . a pleasant walk to you . let she want for nothing . " and at supper , my lord ask Mr. Warrington many question about the poor in Virginia , and the mean of maintain they , to which the young gentleman give the good answer he might . his lordship wish that in the old country there be no more poor people than in the new : and recommend Harry to visit the poor and people of every degree , indeed , high and low in the country to look at the agriculture , in the city at the manufacture and municipal institution to which edify advice Harry accede with become modesty and few word , and Madame Bernstein nod approval over her piquet with the chaplain . next day , Harry be in my lord 's justice - room : the next day he be out ever so long with my lord on the farm and come home , what do my lord do , but look in on a sick tenant ? I think Lady Maria be out on that day , too ; she have be read good book to that poor dear Goody Jenkins , though I do not   suppose Madame Bernstein ever think of ask about her niece . 
 " CASTLEWOOD , HAMPSHIRE , ENGLAND , August 5 , 1757 . 
 " MY dear mountain at first , as I write , I do not like Castlewood , nor my cousin there , very much . now , I be use to their way , and we begin to understand each other much well . with my duty to my mother , tell she , I hope , that consider her ladyship 's great kindness to I , Madam Esmond will be reconcile to her half - sister , the Baroness de Bernstein . the Baroness , you know , be my Grandmamma 's daughter by her first husband , Lord Castlewood   only Grandpapa really be the real lord ; however , that be not his , that is , the other Lord Castlewood 's fault , you know , and he be very kind to Grandpapa , who always speak most kindly of he to we as you know . 
 " madame the Baroness Bernstein first marry a clergyman , Reverend Mr. Tusher , who be so learned and good , and such a favourite of his Majesty , as be my aunt too , that he be make a Bishop . when he die , our gracious King continue his friendship to my aunt ; who marry a hanoverian nobleman , who occupy a post at the Court and , I believe , leave the Baroness very rich . my cousin , my Lord Castlewood , tell I so much about she , and I be sure I have find from she the great kindness and affection . 
 " the   Dowiger   Countess Castlewood and my cousin Will and Lady Fanny have be describe per last , that go by the Falmouth packet on the 20th ult . the lady be not change since then . I and Cousin will be very good friend . we have ride out a good deal . we have have some famous cocking match at Hampton and Winton . my cousin be a sharp blade , but I think I have show he that we in Virginia know a thing or two . reverend Mr. Sampson , chaplain of the famaly , most excellent preacher , without any biggatry . 
 " the kindness of my cousin the Earl improve every day , and by next year 's ship I hope my mother will send his lordship some of our good roll tobacco   for tennant   and hamms . he be most charatable to the poor . his sister , Lady Maria , equally so . she sit for hour read good book to the sick : she be most beloved in the village . " 
 " nonsense ! " say a lady to whom Harry submit his precious manuscript . " why do you flatter I , cousin ? " 
 " you be belove in the village and out of it , " say Harry , with a knowing emphasis , " and I have flatter you , as you call it , a little more still , far on . " 
 " there be a sick old woman there , whom Madam Esmond would like , a most raligious , good , old lady . 
 " Lady Maria go very often to read to she ; which , she say , give her comfort . but though her ladyship hath the sweetest voice , both in speak and singeing   she play the church organ , and singe there most beautifully , I can not think Gammer Jenkins can have any comfort from it , be very deaf , by reason of her great age . she have her memory perfectly , however , and remember when my honour Grandmother Rachel Lady Castlewood live here . she say , my Grandmother be the good woman in the whole world , give she a cow when she be marry , and cure her husband , Gaffer Jenkins , of the collect , which he use to have very bad . I suppose it be with the Pills and Drops which my honour Mother put up in my box , when I leave dear Virginia . have never be ill since , have have no use for the pill . Gumbo hath , eat and drink a great deal too much in the Servants ' Hall . the next angel to my Grandmother   N.B. I think I spelt angel wrong per last , Gammer Jenkins say , be Lady Maria , who send her duty to her Aunt in Virginia , and remember she , and my Grandpapa and Grandmamma when they be in Europe , and she be a little girl . you know they have Grandpapa 's picture here , and I live in the very room which he have , and which be to be call mine , my Lord Castlewood say . 
 " have no more to say , at present , I close with good love and duty to 
 my honoured Mother , and with respect to Mr. Dempster , and a kiss for 
 Fanny , and kind remembrance to Old Gumbo , Nathan , Old and Young Dinah , 
 and the pointer dog and slut , and all friend , from their well - wisher 

 " HENRY ESMOND WARRINGTON . " 
 " have write and send my duty to my Uncle Warrington in Norfolk . no anser as yet . " 
 " I hope the spelling be right , cousin ? " ask the author of the letter , from the critic to whom he show it . 
 " ' tis quite well enough spell for any person of fashion , " answer Lady Maria , who do not choose to be examine too closely regard the orthography . 
 " one word ' Angel , ' I know , I spelt wrong in write to my mamma , but I have learn a way of spell it right , now . " 
 " and how be that , sir ? " 
 " I think ' tis by look at you , cousin ; " say which word , Mr. Harry make her ladyship a low bow , and accompany the bow by one of his good blush , as if he be offer she a bow and a bouquet . 




 CHAPTER XIX . contain both Love and luck 

 at the next meal , when the family party assemble , there be not a trace of displeasure in Madame de Bernstein 's countenance , and her behaviour to all the company , Harry include , be perfectly kind and cordial . she praise the cook this time , declare the fricassee be excellent , and that there be no eel anywhere like those in the Castlewood moat ; would not allow that the wine be cork , or hear of such extravagance as open a fresh bottle for a useless old woman like she ; give Madam Esmond Warrington , of Virginia , as her toast , when the new wine be bring , and hop Harry have bring away his mamma 's permission to take back an english wife with he . he do not remember his grandmother ; she , Madame de Bernstein 's , dear mother ? the Baroness amuse the company with numerous story of her mother , of her beauty and goodness , of her happiness with her second husband , though the wife be so much old than Colonel Esmond . to see they together be delightful , she have hear . their attachment be celebrate all through the country . to talk of disparity in marriage be vain after that . my Lady Castlewood and her two child hold their peace whilst Madame Bernstein prattle . Harry be enrapture , and Maria surprise . Lord Castlewood be puzzle to know what sudden freak or scheme have occasion this prodigious amiability on the part of his aunt ; but do not allow the slight expression of solicitude or doubt to appear on his countenance , which wear every mark of the most perfect satisfaction . 
 the Baroness 's good - humour infect the whole family ; not one person at table escape a gracious word from she . in reply to some compliment to Mr. Will , when that artless youth utter an expression of satisfaction and surprise at his aunt 's behaviour , she frankly say : " Complimentary , my dear ! of course I be . I want to make up with you for have be exceedingly rude to everybody this morning . when I be a child , and my father and mother be alive , and live here , I remember I use to adopt exactly the same behaviour . if I have be naughty in the morning , I use to try and coax my parent at night . I remember in this very room , at this very table oh , ever so many hundred year ago ! so coax my father , and mother , and your grandfather , Harry Warrington ; and there be eel for supper , as we have have they to - night , and it be that dish of collared eel which bring the circumstance back to my mind . I have be just as wayward that day , when I be seven year old , as I be to - day , when I be seventy , and so I confess my sin , and ask to be forgive , like a good girl . " 
 " I absolve your ladyship ! " cry the chaplain , who make one of the party . 
 " but your reverence do not know how cross and ill - temper I be . I scold my sister , Castlewood : I scold her child , I box Harry Warrington 's ear : and all because he would not go with I to Tunbridge Wells . " 
 " but I will go , madam ; I will ride with you with all the pleasure in life , " say Mr. Warrington . 
 " you see , Mr. Chaplain , what good , dutiful child they all be . ' twa I alone who be cross and peevish . oh , it be cruel of I to treat they so ! Maria , I ask your pardon , my dear . " 
 " sure , madam , you have do I no wrong , " say Maria to this humble suppliant . 
 " indeed , I have , a very great wrong , child ! because I be weary of myself , I tell you that your company would be wearisome to I . you offer to come with I to Tunbridge , and I rudely refuse you . " 
 " Nay , ma'am , if you be sick , and my presence annoy you ... 
 " but it will not annoy I ! you be most kind to say that you would come . I do , of all thing , beg , pray , entreat , implore , command that you will come . " 
 my lord fill himself a glass , and sip it . most utterly unconscious do his lordship look . this , then , be the meaning of the previous comedy . 
 " anything which can give my aunt pleasure , I be sure , will delight I , " say Maria , try to look as happy as possible . 
 " you must come and stay with I , my dear , and I promise to be good and good - humour . my dear lord , you will spare your sister to I ? " 
 " Lady Maria Esmond be quite of age to judge for herself about such a matter , " say his lordship , with a bow . " if any of we can be of use to you , madam , you sure ought to command we . " which sentence , be interpret , no doubt mean , " plague take the old woman ! she be take Maria away in order to separate she from this young Virginian . " 
 " oh , Tunbridge will be delightful ! " sigh Lady Maria . 
 " Mr. Sampson will go and see Goody Jones for you , " my lord continue . 
 Harry draw picture with his finger on the table . what delight have he not be speculate on ? what walk , what rid , what interminable conversation , what delicious shrubbery and sweet sequester summer - house , what pore over music - book , what moonlight , what billing and cooing , have he not imagine ! yes , the day be come . they be all depart my Lady Castlewood to her friend , Madame Bernstein to her water and he be to be leave alone with his divine charmer alone with her and unutterable rapture ! the thought of the pleasure be madden . that these people be all go away . that he be to be leave to enjoy that heaven to sit at the foot of that angel and kiss the hem of that white robe . o Gods ! ' twa too great bliss to be real ! " I know it could not   be , " think poor Harry . " I know something would happen to take she from I . " 
 " but you will ride with we to Tunbridge , nephew Warrington , and keep we from the highwayman ? " say Madame de Bernstein . 
 Harry Warrington hop the company do not see how red he grow . he try to keep his voice calm and without tremor . yes , he would ride with their ladyship , and he be sure they need fear no danger . danger ! Harry feel he would rather like danger than not . he would slay ten thousand highwayman if they approach his mistress 's coach . at least , he would ride by that coach , and now and again see her eye at the window . he might not speak to she , but he should be near she . he should press the blessed hand at the inn at night , and feel it repose on his as he lead she to the carriage at morning . they would be two whole day go to Tunbridge , and one day or two he might stay there . be not the poor wretch who be leave for execution at newgate thankful for even two or three day of respite ? 
 you see , we have only indicate , we have not choose to describe , at length , Mr. Harry Warrington 's condition , or that utter depth of imbecility into which the poor young wretch be now plunge . some boy have the complaint of love favourably and gently . other , when they get the fever , be sick unto death with it ; or , recover , carry the mark of the malady down with they to the grave , or to remotest old age . I say , it be not fair to take down a young fellow 's word when he be rag in that delirium . suppose he be in love with a woman twice as old as himself ; have we not all read of the young gentleman who commit suicide in consequence of his fatal passion for Mademoiselle Ninon de l'Enclos who turn out to be his grandmother ? suppose thou art make an ass of thyself , young Harry Warrington , of Virginia ! be there not people in England who heehaw too ? kick and abuse he , you who have never bray ; but bear with he , all honest fellow - cardophagi : long - eared messmate , recognise a brother - donkey ! 
 " you will stay with we for a day or two at the Wells , " Madame Bernstein continue . " you will see we put into our lodging . then you can return to Castlewood and the partridge - shooting , and all the fine thing which you and my lord be to study together . " 
 Harry bow an acquiescence . a whole week of heaven ! life be not altogether a blank , then . 
 " and as there be sure to be plenty of company at the Wells , I shall be able to present you , " the lady graciously add . 
 " Company ! ah ! I shall not   need company , " sigh out Harry . " I mean that I shall be quite content in the company of you two lady , " he add , eagerly ; and no doubt Mr. Will wonder at his cousin 's taste . 
 as this be to be the last night of cousin Harry 's present visit to Castlewood , cousin will suggest that he , and his reverence , and Warrington should meet at the quarter of the latter and make up account , to which process , Harry , be a considerable winner in his play transaction with the two gentleman , have no objection . accordingly , when the lady retire for the night , and my lord withdraw as his custom be to his own apartment , the three gentleman all find themselves assemble in Mr. Harry 's little room before the punch - bowl , which be Will 's usual midnight companion . 
 but Will 's method of settling account be by produce a couple of fresh pack of card , and offer to submit Harry 's debt to the process of be double or acquit . the poor chaplain have no more ready cash than Lord Castlewood 's young brother . Harry Warrington want to win the money of neither . would he give pain to the brother of his adore Maria , or allow any one of her near kinsfolk to tax he with any want of generosity or forbearance ? he be ready to give they their revenge , as the gentleman propose . up to midnight he would play with they for what stake they choose to name . and so they set to work , and the dice - box be rattle and the card shuffle and deal . 
 very likely he do not think about the card at all . very likely he be think ; " at this moment , my beloved one be sit with her beauteous golden lock outspread under the finger of her maid . happy maid ! now she be on her knee , the sainted creature , address prayer to that Heaven which be the abode of angel like she . now she have sink to rest behind her damask curtain . oh , bless , bless she ! " " you double we all round ? I will take a card upon each of my two . thank you , that will do a ten now , upon the other , a queen , two natural vingt - et - uns , and as you double we you owe I so - and - so . " 
 I imagine volley of oath from Mr. William , and brisk pattering of imprecation from his reverence , at the young Virginian 's luck . he win because he do not want to win . Fortune , that notoriously coquettish jade , come to he , because he be think of another nymph , who possibly be as fickle . Will and the chaplain may have play against he , solicitous constantly to increase their stake , and suppose that the wealthy Virginian wish to let they recover all their losing . but this be by no means Harry Warrington 's notion . when he be at home he have take a part in score of such game as these   whereby we may be lead to suppose that he keep many little circumstance of his life mum from his lady mother , and have learn to play and pay . and as he practise fair play towards his friend he expect it from they in return . 
 " the luck do seem to be with I , cousin , " he say , in reply to some more oath and growl of Will , " and I be sure I do not want to press it ; but you do not   suppose I be go to be such a fool as to fle it away altogether ? I have quite a heap of your promise on paper by this time . if we be to go on playing , let we have the dollar on the table , if you please ; or , if not the money , the worth of it . " 
 " always the way with you rich man , " grumble Will . " never lend except on security always win because you be rich . " 
 " Faith , cousin , you have be of late for ever fling my rich into my face . I have enough for my want and for my creditor . " 
 " oh , that we could all say as much ! " groan the chaplain . " how happy we , and how happy the dun would be ! what have we get to play against our conqueror ? there be my new gown , Mr. Warrington . will you set I five piece against it ? I have but to preach in stuff if I lose . stop ! I have a Chrysostom , a Foxe 's Martyrs , a Baker 's Chronicle , and a cow and her calf . what shall we set against these ? " 
 " I will bet one of cousin Will 's note for twenty pound , " cry Mr. Warrington , produce one of those document . 
 " or I have my brown mare , and will back her red against your honour 's note of hand , but against ready money . " 
 " I have my horse . I will back my horse against you for fifty , " bawl out Will . 
 Harry take the offer of both gentleman . in the course of ten minute the horse and the bay mare have both change owner . Cousin William swear more fiercely than ever . the parson dash his wig to the ground , and emulate his pupil in the loudness of his objurgation . Mr. Harry Warrington be quite calm , and not the least elated by his triumph . they have ask he to play , and he have play . he know he should win . o beloved slumber angel ! he think , be I not sure of victory when you be kind to I ? he be look out from his window towards the casement on the opposite side of the court , which he know to be her . he have forget about his victim and their groan , and ill - luck , ere they cross the court . under yonder brilliant flickering star , behind yonder casement where the lamp be burn faintly , be his joy , and heart , and treasure . 




 CHAPTER XX . Facilis Descensus 

 Whilst the good old Bishop of Cambray , in his romance lately mention , describe the disconsolate condition of Calypso at the departure of Ulysses , I forget whether he mention the grief of Calypso 's lady 's maid on take leave of Odysseus 's own gentleman . the menial must have weep together in the kitchen precinct whilst the master and mistress take a last wild embrace in the drawing - room ; they must have hang round each other in the fore - cabin , whilst their principal break their heart in the grand saloon . when the bell ring for the last time , and Ulysses 's mate bawl , " now ! any one for shore ! " Calypso and her female attendant must have both walk over the same plank , with beat heart and stream eye ; both must have wave pocket - handkerchief   of far different value and texture , as they stand on the quay , to their friend on the depart vessel , whilst the people on the land , and the crew crowd in the ship 's bow , shout hip , hip , huzzay   or whatever may be the equivalent Greek for the salutation   to all engage on that voyage . but the point to be remember be , that if Calypso ne pouvait se consoler , Calypso 's maid ne pouvait se consoler non plus . they have to walk the same plank of grief , and feel the same pang of separation ; on their return home , they might not use pocket - handkerchief of the same texture and value , but the tear , no doubt , be as salt and plentiful which one shed in her marble hall , and the other pour forth in the servant ' ditto . 
 not only do Harry Warrington leave Castlewood a victim to love , but Gumbo quit the same premise a prey to the same delightful passion . his wit , accomplishment , good - humour , his skill in dancing , cookery , and music , have endear he to the whole female domestic circle . More than one of the man might be jealous of he , but the lady all be with he . there be no such objection to the poor black man then in England as have obtain since among white - skin people . Theirs be a condition not perhaps of equality , but they have a sufferance and a certain grotesque sympathy from all ; and from woman , no doubt , a kindness much more generous . when Ledyard and Parke , in Blackmansland , be persecute by the man , do they not find the black woman pitiful and kind to they ? woman be always kind towards our sex . what   mental   negro do they not cherish ? what   moral   hunchback do they not adore ? what leper , what idiot , what dull driveller , what misshapen monster   I speak figuratively   do they not fondle and cuddle ? Gumbo be treat by the woman as kindly as many people no well than himself : it be only the man in the servants'-hall who rejoice at the virginian lad 's departure . I should like to see he take leave . I should like to see molly housemaid steal to the terrace - garden in the grey dawn to cull a wistful posy . I should like to see Betty kitchenmaid cut off a thick lock of her chestnut ringlet which she propose to exchange for a woolly token from young Gumbo 's pate . of course he say he be regum progeny , a descendant of ashantee king . in Caffraria , Connaught and other place now inhabit by hereditary bondsman , there must have be vast number of these potent sovereign in former time , to judge from their descendant now extant . 
 at the morning announce for Madame de Bernstein 's departure , all the numerous domestic of Castlewood crowd about the door and passage , some to have a last glimpse of her ladyship 's man and the fascinating Gumbo , some to take leave of her ladyship 's maid , all to waylay the Baroness and her nephew for parting fee , which it be the custom of that day largely to distribute among household servant . one and the other give liberal gratuity to the liveried society , to the gentleman in black and ruffle , and to the swarm of female attendant . Castlewood be the home of the Baroness 's youth ; and as for her honest Harry , who have not only live at free charge in the house , but have win horse and money or promise of money from his cousin and the unlucky chaplain , he be naturally of a generous turn , and feel that at this moment he ought not to stint his benevolent disposition . " my mother , I know , " he think , " will wish I to be liberal to all the retainer of the Esmond family . " so he scatter about his gold piece to right and leave , and as if he have be as rich as Gumbo announce he to be . there be no one who come near he but have a share in his bounty . from the major - domo to the shoeblack , Mr. Harry have a peace - offering for they all . to the grim housekeeper in her still - room , to the feeble old porter in his lodge , he distribute some token of his remembrance . when a man be in love with one woman in a family , it be astonish how fond he become of every person connect with it . he ingratiate himself with the maid ; he be bland with the butler ; he interest himself about the footman ; he run on errand for the daughter ; he give advice and lend money to the young son at college ; he pat little dog which he would kick otherwise ; he smile at old story which would make he break out in yawn , be they utter by any one but papa ; he drink sweet port wine for which he would curse the steward and the whole committee of a club ; he bear even with the cantankerous old maiden aunt ; he beat time when darle little Fanny perform her piece on the piano ; and smile when wicked , lively little Bobby upset the coffee over his shirt . 
 Harry Warrington , in his way , and accord to the custom of that age , have for a brief time past   by which I conclude that only for a brief time have his love be declare and accept   give to the Castlewood family all these artless testimony of his affection for one of they . Cousin will should have win back his money and welcome , or have win as much of Harry 's own as the lad could spare . nevertheless , the lad , though a lover , be shrewd , keen , and fond of sport and fair play , and a judge of a good horse when he see one . have play for and win all the money which will have , besides a great number of Mr. Esmond 's valuable autograph , Harry be very well pleased to win Will 's brown horse that very quadrupe which have nearly push he into the water on the first evening of his arrival at Castlewood . he have see the horse 's performance often , and in the midst of all his passion and romance , be not sorry to be possess of such a sound , swift , well - breed hunter and roadster . when he have gaze at the star sufficiently as they shine over his mistress 's window , and put her candle to bed , he repair to his own dormitory , and there , no doubt , think of his Maria and his horse with youthful satisfaction , and how sweet it would be to have one pillione on the other , and to make the tour of all the island on such an animal with such a pair of white arm round his waist . he fall asleep ruminating on these thing , and meditate a million of blessing on his Maria , in whose company he be to luxuriate at least for a week more . 
 in the early morning poor Chaplain Sampson send over his little black mare by the hand of his groom , footman , and gardener , who weep and bestow a great number of kiss on the beast 's white nose as he hand he over to Gumbo . Gumbo and his master be both affect by the fellow 's sensibility ; the negro servant show his sympathy by weeping , and Harry by produce a couple of guineas , with which he astonish and speedily comfort the chaplain 's boy . then Gumbo and the late groom lead the beast away to the stable , have command to bring he round with Mr. William 's horse after breakfast , at the hour when Madam Bernstein 's carriage be order . 
 so courteous be he to his aunt , or so grateful for her departure , that the master of the house even make his appearance at the morning meal , in order to take leave of his guest . the lady and the chaplain be present the only member of the family absent be will : who , however , leave a note for his cousin , in which will state , in exceedingly bad spelling , that he be oblige to go away to Salisbury Races that morning , but that he have leave the horse which his cousin win last night , and which Tom , Mr. Will 's groom , would hand over to Mr. Warrington 's servant . will 's absence do not prevent the rest of the party from drink a dish of tea amicably , and in due time the carriage roll into the courtyard , the servant pack they with the Baroness 's multiply luggage , and the moment of departure arrive . 
 a large open landau contain the stout Baroness and her niece ; a couple of man - servant mount on the box before they with pistol and blunderbuss ready in event of a meeting with highwayman . in another carriage be their ladyship ' maid , and another servant in guard of the trunk , which , vast and numerous as they be , be as nothing compare to the enormous baggage - train accompany a lady of the present time . Mr. Warrington 's modest valise be place in this second carriage under the maid 's guardianship , and Mr. Gumbo propose to ride by the window for the chief part of the journey . 
 my lord , with his stepmother and Lady Fanny , accompany their kinswoman to the carriage step , and bade her farewell with many dutiful embrace . her Lady Maria follow in a riding - dress , which Harry Warrington think the most become costume in the world . a host of servant stand around , and beg Heaven bless her ladyship . the Baroness 's departure be know in the village , and score of the folk there stand wait under the tree outside the gate , and huzzaye and wave their hat as the ponderous vehicle roll away . 
 Gumbo be go for Mr. Warrington 's horse , as my lord , with his arm under his young guest 's , pace up and down the court . " I hear you carry away some of our horse out of Castlewood ? " my lord say . 
 Harry blush . " a gentleman can not refuse a fair game at the card , " he say . " I never want to play , nor would have play for money have not my cousin William force I . as for the chaplain , it go to my heart to win from he , but he be as eager as my cousin . " 
 " I know I know ! there be no blame to you , my boy . at Rome you can not   help do as Rome do ; and I be very glad that you have be able to give Will a lesson . he be mad about play would gamble his coat off his back and I and the family have have to pay his debt ever so many time . may I ask how much you have win of he ? " 
 " well , some eighteen piece the first day or two , and his note for a hundred and twenty more , and the brown horse , sixty that make nigh upon two hundred . but , you know , cousin , all be fair , and it be even against my will that we play at all . will ai not   a match for I , my lord that be the fact . indeed he be not . " 
 " he be a match for most people , though , " say my lord . " his brown horse , I think you say ? " 
 " yes . his brown horse Prince William , out of Constitution . you do not   suppose I would set he sixty against his bay , my lord ? " 
 " oh , I do not   know . I see will rid out this morning ; most likely I do not remark what horse he be on . and you win the black mare from the parson ? " 
 " for fourteen . he will mount Gumbo very well . why do not the rascal come round with the horse ? " Harry 's mind be away to lovely Maria . he long to be trot by her side . 
 " when you get to Tunbridge , cousin Harry , you must be on the look - out against sharp player than the chaplain and will . there be all sort of queer company at the Wells . " 
 " a Virginian learn pretty well to take care of himself , my lord , say Harry , with a know nod . 
 " so it seem ! I recommend my sister to thee , Harry . although she be not a baby in year , she be as innocent as one . thou wilt see that she come to no mischief ? " 
 " I will guard she with my life , my lord ! " cry Harry . 
 " thou art a brave fellow . by the way , cousin , unless you be very fond of Castlewood , I would in your case not be in a great hurry to return to this lonely , tumble - down old house . I want myself to go to another place I have , and shall scarce be back here till the partridge - shooting . go you and take charge of the woman , of my sister and the Baroness , will you ? " 
 " indeed I will , " say Harry , his heart beat with happiness at the thought . 
 " and I will write thee word when you shall bring my sister back to I . here come the horse . have you bid adieu to the Countess and Lady Fanny ? they be kiss their hand to you from the music - room balcony . " 
 Harry run up to bid these lady a farewell . he make that ceremony very brief , for he be anxious to be off to the charmer of his heart ; and come downstairs to mount his newly - get steed , which Gumbo , himself astride on the parson 's black mare , hold by the rein . 
 there be Gumbo on the black mare , indeed , and hold another horse . but it be a bay horse , not a brown a bay horse with broken knee an aged , wear - out quadrupe . 
 " what be this ? " cry Harry . 
 " your honour 's new horse , " say the groom , touch his cap . 
 " this brute ? " exclaim the young gentleman , with one or more of those expression then in use in England and Virginia . " go and bring I round Prince William , Mr. William 's horse , the brown horse . " 
 " Mr. William have ride Prince William this morning away to Salisbury Races . his last word be , ' Sam , saddle my bay horse , Cato , for Mr. Warrington this morning . he be Mr. Warrington 's horse now . I sell he to he last night . ' and I know your honour be bountiful : you will consider the groom . " 
 my lord could not help break into a laugh at these word of Sam the groom , whilst Harry , for his part , indulge in a number more of those remark which politeness do not admit of our inserting here . 
 " Mr. William say he never could think of part with the Prince under a hundred and twenty , " say the groom , look at the young man . 
 Lord Castlewood only laugh the more . " will have be too much for thee , Harry Warrington . " 
 " too much for I , my lord ! so may a fellow with loaded dice throw six , and be too much for I . I do not call this betting , I call it ch " 
 " Mr. Warrington ! spare I bad word about my brother , if you please . depend on it , I will take care that you be right . farewell . ride quickly , or your coach will be at Farnham before you ; " and wave he an adieu , my lord enter into the house , whilst Harry and his companion ride out of the courtyard . the young Virginian be much too eager to rejoin the carriage and his charmer , to remark the unutterable love and affection which Gumbo shoot from his fine eye towards a young creature in the porter 's lodge . 
 when the youth be go , the chaplain and my lord sate down to finish their breakfast in peace and comfort . the two lady do not return to this meal . 
 " that be one of Will 's confound rascally trick , " say my lord . " if our cousin break Will 's head I should not wonder . " 
 " he be use to the operation , my lord , and yet , " add the chaplain , with a grin , " when we be play last night , the colour of the horse be not mention . I could not escape , have but one : and the black boy have ride off on he . the young virginian play like a man , to do he justice . " 
 " he win because he do not care about lose . I think there can be little doubt but that he be very well to do . his mother 's law - agent be my lawyer , and they write that the property be quite a principality , and grow rich every year . " 
 " if it be a kingdom I know whom Mr. Warrington would make queen of it , " say the obsequious chaplain . 
 " who can account for taste , parson ? " ask his lordship , with a sneer . " all man be so . the first woman I be in love with myself be forty ; and as jealous as if she have be fifteen . it run in the family . Colonel Esmond   he in scarlet and the breastplate yonder   marry my grandmother , who be almost old enough to be his . if this lad choose to take out an elderly princess to Virginia , we must not balk he . " 
 " ' twere a consummation devoutly to be wish ! " cry the chaplain . " have I not well go to Tunbridge Wells myself , my lord , and be on the spot , and ready to exercise my sacred function in behalf of the young couple ? " 
 " you shall have a pair of new nag , parson , if you do , " say my lord . and with this we leave they peaceable over a pipe of tobacco after breakfast . 
 Harry be in such a haste to join the carriage that he almost forget to take off his hat , and acknowledge the cheer of the Castlewood villager : they all like the lad , whose frank cordial way and honest face get he a welcome in most place . legend be still extant in Castlewood , of his grandparent , and how his grandfather , Colonel Esmond , might have be Lord Castlewood , but would not . Old Lockwood at the gate often tell of the Colonel 's gallantry in Queen Anne 's war . his feat be exaggerated , the behaviour of the present family be contrast with that of the old lord and lady : who might not have be very popular in their time , but be well folk than those now in possession . Lord Castlewood be a hard landlord : perhaps more disliked because he be know to be poor and embarrassed than because he be severe . as for Mr. Will , nobody be fond of he . the young gentleman have have many brawl and quarrel about the village , have receive and give broken head , have bill in the neighbouring town which he could not or would not pay ; have be arraign before the magistrate for tamper with village girl , and waylay and cudgel by injured husband , father , sweetheart . a hundred year ago his character and action might have be describe at length by the painter of manner ; but the Comic Muse , nowadays , do not lift up Molly Seagrim 's curtain ; she only indicate the presence of some one behind it , and pass on primly , with expression of horror , and a fan before her eye . the village have hear how the young virginian squire have beat Mr. Will at riding , at jump , at shooting , and finally at card - playing , for everything be know ; and they respect Harry all the more for this superiority . above all , they admire he on account of the reputation of enormous wealth which Gumbo have make for his master . this fame have travel over the whole county , and be precede he at this moment on the box of Madame Bernstein 's carriage , from which the valet , as they descend at the inn to bait , spread astounding report of the young Virginian 's rank and splendour . he be a prince in his own country . he have gold mine , diamond mine , fur , tobacco , who know what , or how much ? no wonder the honest Britons cheer he and respect he for his prosperity , as the noble - hearted fellow always do . I be surprised city corporation do not address he , and offer gold box with the freedom of the city he be so rich . ah , a proud thing it be to be a Briton , and think that there be no country where prosperity be so much respect as in our ; and where success receive such constant affecting testimonial of loyalty ! 
 so , leave the villager bawl , and their hat toss in the air , Harry spur his sorry beast , and gallop , with Gumbo behind he , until he come up with the cloud of dust in the midst of which his charmer 's chariot be envelop . penetrate into this cloud , he find himself at the window of the carriage . the Lady Maria have the back seat to herself ; by keep a little behind the wheel , he could have the delight of see her divine eye and smile . she hold a finger to her lip . Madame Bernstein be already doze on her cushion .
