Harry do not care to disturb the old lady . to look at his cousin be bliss enough for he . the landscape around he might be beautiful , but what do he heed it ? all the sky and tree of summer be as nothing compare to yonder face ; the hedgerow bird sing no such sweet music as her sweet monosyllable . 
 the Baroness 's fat horse be accustom to short journey , easy pace , and plenty of feeding ; so that , ill as Harry Warrington be mount , he could , without much difficulty , keep pace with his elderly kinswoman . at two o'clock they bait for a couple of hour for dinner . Mr. Warrington pay the landlord generously . what price could be too great for the pleasure which he enjoy in be near his adore Maria , and have the blissful chance of a conversation with she , scarce interrupt by the soft breathing of Madame de Bernstein , who , after a comfortable meal , indulge in an agreeable half - hour 's slumber ? in voice soft and low , Maria and her young gentleman talk over and over again those delicious nonsense which people in Harry 's condition never tire of hearing and uttering . 
 they be go to a crowded watering - place , where all sort of beauty and fashion would be assemble ; timid Maria be certain that amongst the young beauty , Harry would discover some , whose charm be far more worthy to occupy his attention , than any her homely face and figure could boast of . by all the god , Harry vow that Venus herself could not tempt he from her side . it be he who for his part have occasion to fear . when the young man of fashion beheld his peerless Maria they would crowd round her car ; they would cause she to forget the rough and humble american lad who know nothing of fashion or wit , who have only a faithful heart at her service . 
 Maria smile , she cast her eye to heaven , she vow that Harry know nothing of the truth and fidelity of woman ; it be his sex , on the contrary , which proverbially be faithless , and which delight to play with poor female heart . a scuffle ensue ; a clatter be hear among the knife and fork of the dessert ; a glass tumble over and break . an " oh ! " escape from the innocent lip of Maria , the disturbance have be cause by the broad cuff of Mr. Warrington 's coat , which have be stretch across the table to seize Lady Maria 's hand , and have upset the wine - glass in so do . surely nothing could be more natural , or indeed necessary , than that Harry , upon hear his sex 's honour impeach , should seize upon his fair accuser 's hand , and vow eternal fidelity upon those charming finger ? 
 what a part they play , or use to play , in love - making , those hand ! how quaintly they be squeeze at that period of life ! how they be push into conversation ! what absurd vow and protest be palm off by their aid ! what good can there be in pull and press a thumb and four finger ? I fancy I see Alexis laugh , who be haply read this page by the side of Araminta . to talk about thumb indeed ! ... Maria look round , for her part , to see if Madame Bernstein have be awaken by the crash of glass ; but the old lady slumber quite calmly in her arm - chair , so her niece think there can be no harm in yield to Harry 's gentle pressure . 
 the horse be put to : Paradise be over at least until the next occasion . when my landlord enter with the bill , Harry be stand quite at a distance from his cousin , look from the window at the cavalcade gather below . Madame Bernstein wake up from her slumber , smile and quite unconscious . with what profound care and reverential politeness Mr. Warrington hand his aunt to her carriage ! how demure and simple look Lady Maria as she follow ! away go the carriage , in the midst of a profoundly bow landlord and waiter ; of country - folk gather round the blaze inn - sign ; of shopman gaze from their homely little door ; of boy and market - folk under the colonnade of the old town - hall ; of lounger along the gable street . " it be the famous Baroness Bernstein . that be she , the old lady in the capuchin . it be the rich young American who be just come from Virginia , and be worth million and million . well , sure , he might have a well horse . " the cavalcade disappear , and the little town lapse into its usual quiet . the landlord go back to his friend at the club , to tell how the great folk be go to sleep at the Bush , at Farnham , to - night . 
 the inn dinner have be plentiful , and all the three guest of the inn have do justice to the good cheer . Harry have the appetite natural to his period of life . Maria and her aunt be also not indifferent to a good dinner : Madame Bernstein have have a comfortable nap after her , which have no doubt help she to bear all the good thing of the meal the meat pie , and the fruit pie , and the strong ale , and the heady port wine . she recline at ease on her seat of the landau , and look back affably , and smile at Harry and exchange a little talk with he as he ride by the carriage side . but what ail the beloved being who sate with she back to the horse ? her complexion , which be exceedingly fair , be far ornament with a pair of red cheek , which Harry take to be natural rose .   you see , madam , that your surmise regard the Lady Maria 's conduct with her cousin be quite wrong and uncharitable , and that the timid lad have make no such experiment as you suppose , in order to ascertain whether the rose be real or artificial . a kiss , indeed ! I blush to think you should imagine that the present writer could indicate anything so shocking !   Maria 's bright red cheek , I say still , continue to blush as it seem with a strange metallic bloom : but the rest of her face , which have use to rival the lily in whiteness , become of a jonquil colour . her eye star round with a ghastly expression . Harry be alarm at the agony depict in the charmer 's countenance ; which not only exhibit pain , but be exceedingly unbecoming . Madame Bernstein also at length remark her niece 's indisposition , and ask she if sit backwards in the carriage make her ill , which poor Maria confess to be the fact . on this , the eld lady be force to make room for her niece on her own side , and , in the course of the drive to Farnham , utter many gruff , disagreeable , sarcastic remark to her fellow - traveller , indicate her great displeasure that Maria should be so impertinent as to be ill on the first day of a journey . 
 when they reach the Bush Inn at Farnham , under which name a famous inn have stand in Farnham town for these three hundred year the dear invalid retire with she maid to her bedroom : scarcely glance a piteous look at Harry as she retreat , and leave the lad 's mind in a strange confusion of dismay and sympathy . those yellow , yellow cheek , those livid wrinkle eyelid , that ghastly red how ill his bless Maria look ! and not only how ill , but how away , horrible thought , unmanly suspicion ! he try to shut the idea out from his mind . he have little appetite for supper , though the jolly baroness partook of that repast as if she have have no dinner ; and certainly as if she have no sympathy with her invalid niece . 
 she send her major - domo to see if Lady Maria would have anything from the table . the servant bring back word that her ladyship be still very unwell , and decline any refreshment . 
 " I hope she intend to be well to - morrow morning , " cry Madame Bernstein , rap her little hand on the table . " I hate people to be ill in an inn , or on a journey . will you play piquet with I , Harry ? " 
 Harry be happy to be able to play piquet with his aunt . " that absurd Maria ! " say Madame Bernstein , drink from a great glass of negus , ' she take liberty with herself . she never have a good constitution . she be forty - one year old . all her upper tooth be false , and she can not   eat with they . thank Heaven , I have still get every tooth in my head . how clumsily you deal , child ! " 
 deal clumsily indeed ! have a dentist be extract Harry 's own grinder at that moment , would he have be expect to mind his card and deal they neatly ? when a man be lay on the rack at the inquisition , be it natural that he should smile and speak politely and coherently to the grave , quiet inquisitor ? beyond that little question regard the card , Harry 's Inquisitor do not show the small disturbance . her face indicate neither surprise , nor triumph , nor cruelty . Madame Bernstein do not give one more stab to her niece that night : but she play at card , and prattle with Harry , indulge in her favourite talk about old time , and parting from he with great cordiality and good - humour . very likely he do not heed her story . very likely other thought occupy his mind . Maria be forty - one year old , Maria have false . oh , horrible , horrible ! have she a false eye ? have she false hair ? have she a wooden leg ? I envy not that boy 's dream that night . 
 Madame Bernstein , in the morning , say she have sleep as sound as a top . she have no remorse , that be clear .   some folk be happy and easy in mind when their victim be stab and do for .   Lady Maria make her appearance at the breakfast - table , too . her ladyship 's indisposition be fortunately over : her aunt congratulate she affectionately on her good look . she sate down to her breakfast . she look appealingly in Harry 's face . he remark , with his usual brilliancy and originality , that he be very glad her ladyship be well . why , at the tone of his voice , do she start , and again gaze at he with frightened eye ? there sate the Chief Inquisitor , smile , perfectly calm , eat ham and muffin . o poor writhing , rack - rent victim ! o stony inquisitor ! o Baroness Bernstein ! it be cruel ! cruel ! 
 Round about Farnham the hop be gloriously green in the sunshine , and the carriage drive through the rich , most beautiful country . Maria insist upon take her old seat . she thank her dear aunt . it would not in the least incommode she now . she gaze , as she have do yesterday , in the face of the young knight rid by the carriage side . she look for those answer signal which use to be light up in yonder two window , and tell that love be burn within . she smile gently at he , to which token of regard he try to answer with a sickly grin of recognition . miserable youth ! those be not false tooth he see when she smile . he think they be , and they tear and lacerate he . 
 and so the day speed on sunshiny and brilliant overhead , but all over cloud for Harry and Maria . he see nothing : he think of Virginia : he remember how he have be in love with Parson Broadbent 's daughter at Jamestown , and how quickly that business have end . he long vaguely to be at home again . a plague on all these cold - hearted english relation ! do they not all mean to trick he ? be they not all scheme against he ? have not that confound will cheat he about the horse ? 
 at this very juncture , Maria give a scream so loud and shrill that Madame Bernstein wake , that the coachman pull his horse up , and the footman beside he spring down from his box in a panic . 
 " let I out ! let I out ! " scream Maria . " let I go to he ! let I go to he ! " 
 " what be it ? " ask the Baroness . 
 it be that Will 's horse have come down on his knee and nose , have send his rider over his head , and Mr. Harry , who ought to have know well , be lie on his own face quite motionless . 
 Gumbo , who have be dally with the maid of the second carriage , clatter up , and mingle his howl with Lady Maria 's lamentation . Madame Bernstein descend from her landau , and come slowly up , tremble a good deal . 
 " he be dead he be dead ! " sob Maria . 
 " do not   be a goose , Maria ! " her aunt say . " ring at that gate , some one ! " 
 will 's horse have gather himself up and stand perfectly quiet after his feat : but his late rider give not the slight sign of life . 




 CHAPTER XXI . samaritan 

 lest any tender - hearted reader should be in alarm for Mr. Harry Warrington 's safety , and fancy that his break - kneed horse have carry he altogether out of this life and history , let we set her mind easy at the beginning of this chapter by assure she that nothing very serious have happen . how can we afford to kill off our hero , when they be scarcely out of their teen , and we have not reach the age of manhood of the story ? we be in mourn already for one of our Virginians , who have come to grief in America ; surely we can not kill off the other in England ? no , no . Heroes be not despatch with such hurry and violence unless there be a cogent reason for make away with they . be a gentleman to perish every time a horse come down with he , not only the hero , but the author of this chronicle would have go under ground , whereas the former be but sprawl outside it , and will be bring to life again as soon as he have be carry into the house where Madame de Bernstein 's servant have ring the bell . 
 and to convince you that at least this young of the Virginians be still alive , here be an authentic copy of a letter from the lady into whose house he be take after his fall from Mr. Will be brute of a break - kneed horse , and in whom he appear to have find a kind friend : 
  " to MRS . ESMOND WARRINGTON , of castlewood 

  " at her House at Richmond , in Virginia 
 " if Mrs. Esmond Warrington of Virginia can call to mind twenty - three year ago , when Miss Rachel Esmond be at Kensington Boarding School , she may perhaps remember Miss Molly Benson , her class - mate , who have forget all the little quarrel which they use to have together   in which Miss Molly be very often in the wrong , and only remember the generous , high - spirit , sprightly , Miss Esmond , the Princess Pocahontas , to whom so many of our school - fellow pay court . 
 " dear madam ! I can not forget that you be dear Rachel once upon a time , as I be your dearest molly . though we part not very good friend when you go home to Virginia , yet you know how fond we once be . I still , Rachel , have the gold etui your papa give I when he come to our speech - day at Kensington , and we two perform the quarrel of Brutus and Cassius out of Shakspeare ; and ' twa only yesterday morning I be dream that we be both call up to say our lesson before the awful Miss Hardwood , and that I do not know it , and that as usual Miss Rachel Esmond go above I . how well remember those old day be ! how young we grow as we think of they ! I remember our walk and our exercise , our good king and Queen as they walk in Kensington Gardens , and their court follow they , whilst we of Miss Hardwood 's school curtsey in a row . I can tell still what we have for dinner on each day of the week , and point to the place where your garden be , which be always so much well keep than mine . so be Miss Esmond 's chest of drawer a model of neatness , whilst mine be in a sad condition . do you remember how we use to tell story in the dormitory , and Madame Hibou , the french governess , would come out of bed and interrupt we with her hoot ? have you forget the poor dancing - master , who tell we he have be waylay by assassin , but who be beat , it appear , by my lord your brother 's footman ? my dear , your cousin , the Lady Maria Esmond   her papa be , I think , but Viscount Castlewood in those time , have just be on a visit to this house , where you may be sure I do not recall those sad time to her remembrance , about which I be now chatter to Mrs. Esmond . 
 " her ladyship have be stay here , and another relative of your , the Baroness of Bernstein , and the two lady be both go on to Tunbridge Wells ; but another and dearer relative still remain in my house , and be sound asleep , I trust , in the very next room , and the name of this gentleman be Mr. Henry Esmond Warrington . now , do you understand how you come to hear from an old friend ? do not be alarm , dear madam ! I know you be think at this moment , ' my boy be ill . that be why Miss Molly Benson write to I . ' no , my dear ; Mr. Warrington be ill yesterday , but to - day he be very comfortable ; and our doctor , who be no less a person than my dear husband , Colonel Lambert , have blood he , have set his shoulder , which be dislocate , and pronounce that in two day more Mr. Warrington will be quite ready to take the road . 
 " I fear I and my girl be sorry that he be so soon to be well . yesterday evening , as we be at tea , there come a great ringing at our gate , which disturb we all , as the bell very seldom sound in this quiet place , unless a pass beggar pull it for charity ; and the servant , run out , return with the news , that a young gentleman , who have a fall from his horse , be lie lifeless on the road , surround by the friend in whose company he be travel . at this , my Colonel   who be sure the most Samaritan of man !   hasten away , to see how he can serve the fallen traveller , and presently , with the aid of the servant , and follow by two lady , bring into the house such a pale , lifeless , beautiful , young man ! ah , my dear , how I rejoice to think that your child have find shelter and succour under my roof ! that my husband have save he from pain and fever , and have be the mean of restore he to you and health ! we shall be friend again now , shall we not ? I be very ill last year , and ' twa even think I should die . do you know , that I often think of you then , and how you have part from I in anger so many year ago ? I begin then a foolish note to you , which I be too sick to finish , to tell you that if I go the way appoint for we all , I should wish to leave the world in charity with every single be I have know in it . 
 " your cousin , the Right Honourable Lady Maria Esmond , show a great deal of maternal tenderness and concern for her young kinsman after his accident . I be sure she hath a kind heart . the Baroness de Bernstein , who be of an advanced age , could not be expect to feel so keenly as we young people ; but be , nevertheless , very much move and interested until Mr. Warrington be restore to consciousness , when she say she be anxious to get on towards Tunbridge , whither she be bind , and be afraid of all thing to lie in a place where there be no doctor at hand . my Aesculapius laughingly say , he would not offer to attend upon a lady of quality , though he would answer for his young patient . indeed , the Colonel , during his campaign , have have plenty of practice in accident of this nature , and I be certain , be we to call in all the faculty for twenty mile round , Mr. Warrington could get no well treatment . so , leave the young gentleman to the care of I and my daughter , the Baroness and her ladyship take their leave of we , the latter very loth to go . when he be well enough , my Colonel will ride with he as far as Westerham , but on his own horse , where an old army - comrade of Mr. Lambert 's reside . and , as this letter will not take the post for Falmouth until , by God 's blessing , your son be well and perfectly restore , you need be under no sort of alarm for he whilst under the roof of , madam , your affectionate , humble servant , MARY LAMBERT . 
 " P.S. Thursday . 
 " I be glad to hear   Mr. Warrington 's coloured gentleman hath inform our people of the gratify circumstance   that Providence hath bless Mrs. Esmond with such vast wealth , and with an heir so likely to do credit to it . our present mean be amply sufficient , but will be small when divide amongst our survivor . ah , dear madam ! I have hear of your calamity of last year . though the Colonel and I have rear many child   five , we have lose two , and a mother 's heart can feel for your ! I own to you , mine yearn to your boy to - day , when   in a manner inexpressibly affect to I and Mr. Lambert   he mention his dear brother . ' tis impossible to see your son , and not to love and regard he . I be thankful that it have be our lot to succour he in his trouble , and that in receive the stranger within our gate we should be give hospitality to the son of an old friend . " 
 Nature have write a letter of credit upon some man 's face , which be honour almost wherever present . Harry Warrington 's countenance be so stamp in his youth . his eye be so bright , his cheek so red and healthy , his look so frank and open , that almost all who behold he , nay , even those who cheat he , trust he . nevertheless , as we have hint , the lad be by no mean the artless stripling he seem to be . he be know enough with all his blushing cheek ; perhaps more wily and wary than he grow to be in after - age . sure , a shrewd and generous man   who have lead an honest life and have no secret blush for his conscience   grow simple as he grow old ; arrive at his sum of right by more rapid process of calculation ; learn to eliminate false argument more readily , and hit the mark of truth with less previous trouble of aim , and disturbance of mind . or be it only a senile delusion , that some of our vanity be cure with our grow year , and that we become more just in our perception of our own and our neighbour 's shortcoming ? ... I would humbly suggest that young people , though they look pretty , have large eye , and not near so many wrinkle about their eyelid , be often as artful as some of their elder . what little monster of cunne your frank schoolboy be ! how they cheat mamma ! how they hoodwink papa ! how they humbug the housekeeper ! how they cringe to the big boy for whom they fag at school ! what a long lie and five year ' hypocrisy and flattery be their conduct towards Dr. Birch ! and the little boy ' sister ? be they any well , and be it only after they come out in the world that the little darling learn a trick or two ? 
 you may see , by the above letter of Mrs. Lambert , that she , like all good woman   and , indeed , almost all bad woman , be a sentimental person ; and , as she look at Harry Warrington lay in her good bed , after the Colonel have bleed he and clap in his shoulder , as hold by her husband 's hand she behold the lad in a sweet slumber , murmur a faint inarticulate word or two in his sleep , a faint blush quiver on his cheek , she own he be a pretty lad indeed , and confess with a sort of compunction that neither of her two boy Jack who be at Oxford , and Charles who be just go back to school after the bartlemytide holiday be half so handsome as the Virginian . what a good figure the boy have ! and when papa bleed he , his arm be as white as any lady 's ! 
 " yes , as you say , Jack might have be as handsome but for the small - pox : and as for Charley " " always take after his papa , my dear Molly , " say the Colonel , look at his own honest face in a little look - glass with a cut border and a japanned frame , by which the chief guest of the worthy gentleman and lady have survey their patch and powder , or shave their hospitable beard . 
 " do I say so , my love ? " whisper Mrs. Lambert , look rather scared . 
 " no ; but you think so , Mrs. Lambert . " 
 " how can you tell one 's thought so , Martin ? " ask the lady . 
 " because I be a conjurer , and because you tell they yourself , my dear , " answer her husband . " do not   be frighten : he will not   wake after that draught I give he . because you never see a young fellow but you be compare he with your own . because you never hear of one but you be think which of our girl he shall fall in love with and marry . " 
 " do not   be foolish , sir , " say the lady , put a hand up to the Colonel 's lip . they have softly trodden out of their guest 's bedchamber by this time , and be in the adjoining dressing - closet , a snug little wainscote room look over garden , with India curtain , more Japan chest and cabinet , a treasure of china , and a most refreshing odour of fresh lavender . 
 " you can not   deny it , Mrs. Lambert , " the Colonel resume ; " as you be look at the young gentleman just now , you be think to yourself which of my girl will he marry ? shall it be Theo , or shall it be hester ? and then you think of Lucy who be at boarding - school . " 
 " there be no keep anything from you , Martin Lambert , " sigh the wife . 
 " there be no keep it out of your eye , my dear . what be this burn desire all you woman have for sell and marry your daughter ? we man do not   wish to part with them . I be sure , for my part , I should not like yonder young fellow half as well if I think he intend to carry one of my darling away with he . " 
 " sure , Martin , I have be so happy myself , " say the fond wife and mother , look at her husband with her very good eye , " that I must wish my girl to do as I have do , and be happy , too ! " 
 " then you think good husband be common , Mrs. Lambert , and that you may walk any day into the road before the house and find one shoot out at the gate like a sack of coal ? " 
 " be not   it providential , sir , that this young gentleman should be throw over his horse 's head at our very gate , and that he should turn out to be the son of my old schoolfellow and friend ? " ask the wife . " there be something more than accident in such case , depend upon that , Mr. Lambert ! " 
 " and this be the stranger you see in the candle three night run , I suppose ? " 
 " and in the fire , too , sir ; twice a coal jump out close by Theo . you may sneer , sir , but these thing be not to be despise . do I not see you distinctly come back from Minorca , and dream of you at the very day and hour when you be wound in Scotland ? " 
 " how many time have you see I wound , when I have not a scratch , my dear ? how many time have you see I ill when I have no sort of hurt ? you be always prophesy , and ' twere very hard on you if you be not sometimes right . come ! let we leave our guest asleep comfortably , and go down and give the girl their french lesson . " 
 so say , the honest gentleman put his wife 's arm under his , and they descend together the broad oak staircase of the comfortable old hall , round which hang the effigy of many foregone lambert , worthy magistrate , soldier , country gentleman , as be the Colonel whose acquaintance we have just make . the Colonel be a gentleman of pleasant , waggish humour . the french lesson which he and his daughter con together be a scene out of Monsieur Moliere 's comedy of " Tartuffe , " and papa be pleased to be very facetious with Miss Theo , by call her Madam , and by treat she with a great deal of mock respect and ceremony . the girl read together with their father a scene or two of his favourite author   nor be they less modest in those day , though their tongue be a little more free , and papa be particularly arch and funny as he read from Orgon 's part in that celebrate play : 
  " ORGON . 
  or sus , nous voila bien . J'ai , Mariane , en vous 
  Reconnu de tout temp un esprit assez doux , 
  Et de tout temp aussi vous m'avez ete chere . 

  MARIANE . 
  Je suis fort redevable a cet amour de pere . 

  ORGON . 
  Fort bien . Que dite - vous de Tartuffe notre hote ? 

  MARIANE . 
  Qui ? Moi ? 

  ORGON . 
  Vous . Voyez bien comme vous repondrez . 

  MARIANE . 
  Helas ! J'en dirai , moi , tout ce que vous voudrez ! 
  Mademoiselle Mariane laugh and blush in spite of herself , whilst read this line . 
  ORGON . 
  C'est parler sagement . dite - moi donc , ma fille , 
  Qu'en toute sa personne un haut merite brille , 
  Qu'il touche votre coeur , et qu'il vous seroit doux 
  De le voir par man choix devenir votre epoux ! " 
 " have we not read the scene prettily , Elmire ? " say the Colonel , laughing , and turn round to his wife . 
 Elmira prodigiously admire Orgon 's reading , and so do his daughter , and almost everything besides which Mr. Lambert say or do . Canst thou , o friendly reader , count upon the fidelity of an artless and tender heart or two , and reckon among the blessing which Heaven hath bestow on thee the love of faithful woman ! purify thine own heart , and try to make it worthy theirs . on thy knee , on thy knee , give thank for the blessing award thee ! all the prize of life be nothing compare to that one . all the reward of ambition , wealth , pleasure , only vanity and disappointment grasp at greedily and fight for fiercely , and , over and over again , find worthless by the weary winner . but love seem to survive life , and to reach beyond it . I think we take it with we past the grave . do we not still give it to those who have leave we ? may we not hope that they feel it for we , and that we shall leave it here in one or two fond bosom , when we also be go ? 
 and whence , or how , or why , pray , this sermon ? you see I know more about this Lambert family than you do to whom I be just present they : as how should you who never hear of they before ! you may not like my friend ; very few people do like stranger to whom they be present with an outrageous flourish of praise on the part of the introducer . you say   quite naturally , what ? be this all ? be these the people he be so fond of ? why , the girl be not a beauty the mother be good - natured , and may have be good - look once , but she have no trace of it now and , as for the father , he be quite an ordinary man . grant but do not   you acknowledge that the sight of an honest man , with an honest , love wife by his side , and surround by love and obedient child , present something very sweet and affect to you ? if you be make acquainted with such a person , and see the eager kindness of the fond face round about he , and that pleasant confidence and affection which beam from his own , do you mean to say you be not touch and gratify ? if you happen to stay in such a man 's house , and at morning or evening see he and his child and domestic gather together in a certain name , do you not join humbly in the petition of those servant , and close they with a reverent amen ? that first night of his stay at Oakhurst , Harry Warrington , who have have a sleep potion , and be awake sometimes rather feverish , think he hear the Evening Hymn , and that his dear brother George be sing it at home , in which delusion the patient go off again to sleep . 




 CHAPTER XXII . in hospital 

 sinking into a sweet slumber , and lull by those harmonious sound , our young patient pass a night of pleasant unconsciousness , and awake in the morning to find a summer sun stream in at the window , and his kind host and hostess smile at his bed - curtain . he be ravenously hungry , and his doctor permit he straightway to partake of a mess of chicken , which the doctor 's wife tell he have be prepare by the hand of one of her daughter . 
 one of her daughter ? a faint image of a young person of two young person with red cheek and black wave lock , smile round his couch , and suddenly depart thence , soon after he have come to himself , arise in the young man 's mind . then , then , there return the remembrance of a female lovely , it be true , but more elderly certainly considerably old and with f . oh , horror and remorse ! he writhe with anguish , as a certain recollection cross he . an immense gulf of time gap between he and the past . how long be it since he have hear that those pearl be artificial , that those golden lock be only pinchbeck ? a long , long time ago , when he be a boy , an innocent boy . now he be a man , quite an old man . he have be bleed copiously ; he have a little fever ; he have have nothing to eat for very many hour ; he have a sleeping - draught , and a long , deep slumber after . 
 " what be it , my dear child ? " cry kind Mrs. Lambert , as he start . 
 " nothing , madam ; a twinge in my shoulder , " say the lad . " I speak to my host and hostess ? sure you have be very kind to I . " 
 " we be old friend , Mr. Warrington . my husband , Colonel Lambert , know your father , and I and your mamma be schoolgirl together at Kensington . you be no strange to we when your aunt and cousin tell we who you be . " 
 " be they here ? " ask Harry , look a little blank . 
 " they must have lain at Tunbridge Wells last night . they send a horseman from Reigate yesterday for news of you . " 
 " ah ! I remember , " say Harry , look at his bandaged arm . 
 " I have make a good cure of you , Mr. Warrington . and now Mrs. Lambert and the cook must take charge of you . " 
 " Nay ; Theo prepare the chicken and rice , Mr. Lambert , " say the lady . " will Mr. Warrington get up after he have have his breakfast ? we will send your valet to you . " 
 " if howling prove fidelity , your man must be a most fond , attach creature , " say Mr. Lambert . 
 " he let your baggage travel off after all in your aunt 's carriage , " say Mrs. Lambert . " you must wear my husband 's linen , which , I dare say , be not so fine as your . " 
 " Pish , my dear ! my shirt be good shirt enough for any Christian , " cry the Colonel . 
 " they be Theo 's and hester 's work , " say mamma . at which her husband arch his eyebrow and look at she . " and Theo hath rip and sew your sleeve to make it quite comfortable for your shoulder , " the lady add . 
 " what beautiful rose ! " cry Harry , look at a fine China vase full of they that stand on the toilet - table , under the japan - frame glass . 
 " my daughter Theo cut they this morning . well , Mr. Lambert ? she do cut they ! " 
 I suppose the Colonel be think that his wife introduce Theo too much into the conversation , and trod on Mrs. Lambert 's slipper , or pull her robe , or otherwise nudge she into a sense of propriety . 
 " and I fancy I hear some one sing the Evening Hymn very sweetly last night or be it only a dream ? " ask the young patient . 
 " Theo again , Mr. Warrington ! " say the Colonel , laugh . " my servant say your negro man begin to sing it in the kitchen as if he be a church organ . " 
 " our people sing it at home , sir . my grandpapa use to love it very much . his wife 's father be a great friend of good Bishop Ken , who write it ; and and my dear brother use to love it too ; " say the boy , his voice drop . 
 it be then , I suppose , that Mrs. Lambert feel inclined to give the boy a kiss . his little accident , illness and recovery , the kindness of the people round about he , have soften Harry Warrington 's heart , and open it to well influence than those which have be bring to bear on it for some six week past . he be breathe a purer air than that tainted atmosphere of selfishness , and worldliness , and corruption , into which he have be plunge since his arrival in England . sometimes the young man 's fate , or choice , or weakness , lead he into the fellowship of the giddy and vain ; happy he , whose lot make he acquaint with the wise company , whose lamp be trim , and whose pure heart keep modest watch . 
 the pleased matron leave her young patient devour Miss Theo 's mess of rice and chicken , and the Colonel seat by the lad 's bedside . gratitude to his hospitable entertainer , and contentment after a comfortable meal , cause in Mr. Warrington a very pleasant condition of mind and body . he be ready to talk now more freely than usually be his custom ; for , unless excite by a strong interest or emotion , the young man be commonly taciturn and cautious in his converse with his fellow , and be by no mean of an imaginative turn . of book our youth have be but a very remiss student , nor be his remark on such simple work as he have read , very profound or valuable ; but regard dog , horse , and the ordinary business of life , he be a far well critic ; and , with any person interested in such subject , converse on they freely enough . 
 Harry 's host , who have considerable shrewdness , and experience of book , and cattle , and man , be pretty soon able to take the measure of his young guest in the talk which they now have together . it be now , for the first time , the Virginian learn that Mrs. Lambert have be an early friend of his mother 's , and that the Colonel 's own father have serve with Harry 's grandfather , Colonel Esmond , in the famous war of Queen Anne . he find himself in a friend 's country . he be soon at ease with his honest host , whose manner be quite simple and cordial , and who look and seem perfectly a gentleman , though he wear a plain fustian coat , and a waistcoat without a particle of lace . 
 " my boy be both away , " say Harry 's host , " or they would have show you the country when you get up , Mr. Warrington . now you can only have the company of my wife and her daughter . Mrs. Lambert hath tell you already about one of they , Theo , our eld , who make your broth , who cut your rose , and who mend your coat . she be not such a wonder as her mother imagine she to be : but little Theo be a smart little housekeeper , and a very good and cheerful lass , though her father say it . " 
 " it be very kind of Miss Lambert to take so much care for I , " say the young patient . 
 " she be no kinder to you than to any other mortal , and doth but her duty . " here the Colonel smile . " I laugh at their mother for praise our child , " he say , " and I think I be as foolish about they myself . the truth be , God hath give we very good and dutiful child , and I see no reason why I should disguise my thankfulness for such a blessing . you have never a sister , I think ? " 
 " no , sir , I be alone now , " Mr. Warrington say . 
 " ay , truly , I ask your pardon for my thoughtlessness . your man hath tell our people what befell last year . I serve with Braddock in Scotland ; and hope he mend before he die . a wild fellow , sir , but there be a fund of truth about the man , and no little kindness under his rough swaggering manner . your black fellow talk very freely about his master and his affair . I suppose you permit he these freedom as he rescue you " 
 " rescue I ? " cry Mr. Warrington . 
 " from ever so many Indians on that very expedition . my Molly and I do not know we be go to entertain so prodigiously wealthy a gentleman . he saith that half Virginia belong to you ; but if the whole of North America be your , we could but give you our good . " 
 " those negro boy , sir , lie like the father of all lie . they think it be for our honour to represent we as ten time as rich as we be . my mother have what would be a vast estate in England , and be a very good one at home . we be as well off as most of our neighbour , sir , but no well ; and all our splendour be in Mr. Gumbo 's foolish imagination . he never rescue I from an Indian in his life , and would run away at the sight of one , as my poor brother 's boy do on that fatal day when he fall . " 
 " the brave man will do so at unlucky time , " say the Colonel . " I myself see the good troop in the world run at Preston , before a ragged mob of Highland savage . " 
 " that be because the Highlanders fight for a good cause , sir . " 
 " do you think , " ask Harry 's host , " that the French Indians have the good cause in the fight of last year ? " 
 " the scoundrel ! I would have the scalp of every murderous redskin among them ! " cry Harry , clench his fist . " they be rob and invade the british territory , too . but the Highlanders be fight for their king . " 
 " we , on our side , be fight for our king ; and we end by win the battle , " say the Colonel , laugh . 
 " ah ! " cry Harry ; " if his Royal Highness the Prince have not turn back at Derby , your king and mine , now , would be his Majesty King James the Third ! " 
 " who make such a Tory of you , Mr. Warrington ? " ask Lambert . 
 " Nay , sir , the Esmonds be always loyal ! " answer the youth . " have we live at home , and twenty year soon , brother and I often and often agree that our head would have be in danger . we certainly would have stake they for the king 's cause . " 
 " yours be well on your shoulder than on a pole at Temple Bar . I have see they there , and they do not   look very pleasant , Mr. Warrington . " 
 " I shall take off my hat , and salute they , whenever I pass the gate , " cry the young man , " if the king and the whole court be stand by ! " 
 " I doubt whether your relative , my Lord Castlewood , be as staunch a supporter of the king over the water , " say Colonel Lambert , smile : " or your aunt , the Baroness of Bernstein , who leave you in our charge . whatever her old partiality may have be , she have repent of they ; she have rally to our side , land her nephew in the Household , and look to find a suitable match for her niece . if you have Tory opinion , Mr. Warrington , take an old soldier 's advice , and keep they to yourself . " 
 " why , sir , I do not think that you will betray I ! " say the boy . 
 " not i , but other might . you do not talk in this way at Castlewood ? I mean the old Castlewood which you have just come from . " 
 " I might be safe amongst my own kinsman , surely , sir ! " cry Harry . 
 " Doubtless . I would not say no . but a man 's own kinsman can play he slippery trick at time , and he find himself none the well for trust they . I mean no offence to you or any of your family ; but lacquey have ear as well as their master , and they carry about all sort of story . for instance , your black fellow be ready to tell all he know about you , and a great deal more besides , as it would appear . " 
 " hath he tell about the break - kneed horse ? " cry out Harry , turn very red . 
 " to say truth , my groom seem to know something of the story , and say it be a shame a gentleman should sell another such a brute ; let alone a cousin . I be not here to play the Mentor to you , or to carry about servant ' tittle - tattle . when you have see more of your cousin , you will form your own opinion of they ; meanwhile , take an old soldier 's advice , I say again , and be cautious with whom you deal , and what you say . " 
 very soon after this little colloquy , Mr. Lambert 's guest rise , with the assistance of Gumbo , his valet , to whom he , for the hundredth time at least , promise a sound can if ever he should hear that Gumbo have venture to talk about his affair again in the servants'-hall , which prohibition Gumbo solemnly vow and declare he would for ever obey ; but I dare say he be chatter the whole of the Castlewood secret to his new friend of Colonel Lambert 's kitchen ; for Harry 's hostess certainly hear a number of story concern he which she could not prevent her housekeeper from tell ; though of course I would not accuse that worthy lady , or any of her sex or our , of undue curiosity regard their neighbour ' affair . but how can you prevent servant talk , or listen when the faithful attach creature talk to you ? 
 Mr. Lambert 's house stand on the outskirt of the little town of Oakhurst , which , if he but travel in the right direction , the patient reader will find on the road between Farnham and Reigate , and Madame Bernstein 's servant naturally pull at the first bell at hand , when the young Virginian meet with his mishap . a few hundred yard far , be the long street of the little old town , where hospitality might have be find under the great swing ensign of a couple of tun , and medical relief be to be have , as a blaze gilt pestle and mortar indicate . but what surgeon could have minister more cleverly to a patient than Harry 's host , who tend he without a fee , or what Boniface could make he more comfortably welcome ? 
 two tall gate , each surmount by a couple of heraldic monster , lead from the highroad up to a neat , broad stone terrace , whereon stand Oakhurst House ; a square brick building , with window face with stone , and many high chimney , and a tall roof surmount by a fair balustrade . behind the house stretch a large garden , where there be plenty of room for cabbage as well as rose to grow ; and before the mansion , separate from it by the highroad , be a field of many acre , where the Colonel 's cow and horse be at grass . over the centre window be a carve shield support by the same monster who prance or ramp upon the entrance - gate ; and a coronet over the shield . the fact be , that the house have be originally the jointure - house of Oakhurst Castle , which stand hard by , its chimney and turret appear over the surround wood , now bronze with the dark foliage of summer . Mr. Lambert 's be the great house in Oakhurst town ; but the Castle be of more importance than all the town put together . the Castle and the jointure - house have be friend of many year ' date . their father have fight side by side in Queen Anne 's war . there be two small piece of ordnance on the terrace of the jointure - house , and six before the Castle , which have be take out of the same privateer , which Mr. Lambert and his kinsman and commander , Lord Wrotham , have bring into harwich in one of their voyage home from Flanders with despatch from the great Duke . 
 his toilet complete with Mr. Gumbo 's aid , his fair hair neatly dress by that artist , and his open ribbone sleeve and wound shoulder support by a handkerchief which hang from his neck , Harry Warrington make his way out of the sick - chamber , precede by his kind host , who lead he first down a broad oak stair , round which hang many pike and musket of ancient shape , and so into a square marble - paved room , from which the living - room of the house branch off . there be more arm in this hall - pike and halbert of ancient date , pistol and jack - boot of more than a century old , that have do service in Cromwell 's war , a tattered french guidon which have be bear by a french gendarme at Malplaquet , and a pair of cumbrous Highland broadsword , which , have be carry as far as Derby , have be fling away on the fatal field of Culloden . here be breastplate and black morion of Oliver 's trooper , and portrait of stern warrior in buff jerkin and plain band and short hair . " they fight against your grandfather and King Charles , Mr. Warrington , " say Harry 's host . " I do not   hide that . they ride to join the Prince of Orange at Exeter . we be Whigs , young gentleman , and something more . John Lambert , the Major - General , be a kinsman of our house , and we be all more or less partial to short hair and long sermon . you do not seem to like either ? " indeed , Harry 's face manifest sign of anything but pleasure whilst he examine the portrait of the parliamentary hero . " be not alarmed , we be very good Churchmen now . my eld son will be in order ere long . he be now travel as governor to my Lord Wrotham 's son in Italy , and as for our woman , they be all for the Church , and carry I with them . every woman be a Tory at heart . Mr. Pope say a rake , but I think t'other be the more charitable word . come , let we go see they , " and , fling open the dark oak door , Colonel Lambert lead his young guest into the parlour where the lady be assemble . 
 " here be Miss Hester , " say the Colonel , " and this be Miss Theo , the soup - maker , the tailoress , the harpsichord - player , and the songstress , who set you to sleep last night . make a curtsey to the gentleman , young lady ! oh , I forget , and Theo be the mistress of the rose which you admire a short while since in your bedroom . I think she have keep some of they in her cheek . " 
 in fact , Miss Theo be make a profound curtsey and blush most modestly as her papa speak . I be not go to describe her person , though we shall see a great deal of she in the course of this history . she be not a particular beauty . Harry Warrington be not over head and ear in love with she at an instant 's warning , and faithless to to that other individual with whom , as we have see , the youth have lately be smite . Miss Theo have kind eye and a sweet voice ; a ruddy freckle cheek and a round white neck , on which , out of a little cap such as miss wear in those time , fall rich curl cluster of dark brown hair . she be not a delicate or sentimental - look person . her arm , which be wear bare from the elbow like other lady ' arm in those day , be very jolly and red . her foot be not so miraculously small but that you could see they without a telescope . there be nothing waspish about her waist . this young person be sixteen year of age , and look old . I do not   know what call she have to blush so when she make her curtsey to the stranger . it be such a deep ceremonial curtsey as you never see at present . she and her sister both make these " cheese " in compliment to the new comer , and with much stately agility . 
 as Miss Theo rise up out of this salute , her papa tap she under the chin   which be of the double sort of chin , and laughingly hum out the line which he have read the day . " eh bien ! que dite - vous , ma fille , de notre hote ? " 
 " Nonsense , Mr. Lambert ! " cry mamma . 
 " Nonsense be sometimes the good kind of sense in the world , " say Colonel Lambert . his guest look puzzle . 
 " be you fond of nonsense ? " the Colonel continue to Harry , see by the boy 's face that the latter have no great love or comprehension of his favourite humour . " we consume a vast deal of it in this house . Rabelais be my favourite reading . my wife be all for Mr. Fielding and Theophrastus . I think theo prefer Tom Brown , and Mrs. Hetty here love Dean Swift . " 
 " our papa be talk what he love , " say Miss Hetty . 
 " and what be that , miss ? " ask the father of his second daughter . 
 " sure , sir , you say yourself it be nonsense , " answer the young lady , with a saucy toss of her head . 
 " which of they do you like well , Mr. Warrington ? " ask the honest Colonel . 
 " which of whom , sir ? " 
 " the Curate of Meudon , or the Dean of St. Patrick 's , or honest Tom , or Mr. Fielding ? " 
 " and what be they , sir ? " 
 " they ! why , they write book . " 
 " indeed , sir . I never hear of either one of them , " say Harry , hang down his head . " I fear my book - learning be neglect at home , sir . my brother have read every book that ever be write , I think . he could have talk to you about them for hour together . " 
 with this little speech Mrs. Lambert 's eye turn to her daughter , and Miss Theo cast hers down and blush . 
 " never mind , honesty be well than book any day , Mr. Warrington ! " cry the jolly Colonel . " you may go through the world very honourably without read any of the book I have be talk of , and some of they might give you more pleasure than profit . " 
 " I know more about horse and dog than Greek and Latin , sir . we most of we do in Virginia , " say Mr. Warrington . 
 " you be like the Persians ; you can ride and speak the truth . " 
 " be the Prussians very good on horseback , sir ? I hope I shall see their king and a campaign or two , either with them or against them , " remark Colonel Lambert 's guest . why do Miss Theo look at her mother , and why do that good woman 's face assume a sad expression ? 
 why ? because young lass be breed in humdrum country town , do you suppose they never indulge in romance ? because they be modest and have never quit mother 's apron , do you suppose they have no thought of their own ? what happen in spite of all those precaution which the King and Queen take for their darling princess , those dragon , and that impenetrable forest , and that castle of steel ? the fairy prince penetrate the impenetrable forest , find the weak point in the dragon 's scale armour , and get the well of all the ogre who guard the castle of steel . away go the princess to he . she know he at once . her bandbox and portmanteaux be fill with her good clothe and all her jewel . she have be ready ever so long . 
 that be in fairy tale , you understand where the blessed hour and youth always arrive , the ivory horn be blow at the castle gate ; and far off in her beauteous bower the princess hear it , and start up , and know that there be the right champion . he be always ready . look ! how the giant ' head tumble off as , falchion in hand , he gallop over the bridge on his white charger ! how should that virgin , lock up in that inaccessible fortress , where she have never see any man that be not eighty , or humpbacke , or her father , know that there be such being in the world as young man ? I suppose there be an instinct . I suppose there be a season . I never speak for my part to a fairy princess , or hear as much from any unenchanted or enchanting maiden . Ne'er a one of they have ever whisper she pretty little secret to I , or perhaps confess they to herself , her mamma , or her near and dearest confidante . but they will fall in love . their little heart be constantly throb at the window of expectancy on the lookout for the champion . they be always hear his horn . they be for ever on the tower look out for the hero . Sister Ann , Sister Ann , do you see he ? surely ' tis a knight with curl mustachio , a flash scimitar , and a suit of silver armour . oh no ! it be only a costermonger with his donkey and a pannier of cabbage ! Sister Ann , Sister Ann , what be that cloud of dust ? oh , it be only a farmer 's man drive a flock of pig from market . Sister Ann , Sister Ann , who be that splendid warrior advance in scarlet and gold ? he near the castle , he clear the drawbridge , he lift the ponderous hammer at the gate . ah I , he knock twice ! ' tis only the postman with a double letter from Northamptonshire ! so it be we make false start in life . I do not   believe there be any such thing know as first love not within man 's or woman 's memory . no male or female remember his or her first inclination any more than his or her own christening . what ? you fancy that your sweet mistress , your spotless spinster , your blank maiden just out of the schoolroom , never care for any but you ? and she tell you so ? oh , you idiot ! when she be four year old she have a tender feeling towards the Buttons who bring the coal up to the nursery , or the little sweep at the crossing , or the music - master , or never mind whom . she have a secret longing towards her brother 's schoolfellow , or the third charity boy at church , and if occasion have serve , the comedy enact with you have be perform along with another . I do not mean to say that she confess this amatory sentiment , but that she have it . lay down this page , and think how many and many and many a time you be in love before you select the present Mrs. Jones as the partner of your name and affection ! 
 so , from the way in which Theo hold her head down , and exchange look with her mother , when poor unconscious Harry call the Persians the Prussians , and talk of serve a campaign with they , I make no doubt she be feel ashamed , and think within herself , " be this the hero with whom my mamma and I have be in love for these twenty - four hour , and whom we have endow with every perfection ? how beautiful , pale , and graceful he look yesterday as he lie on the ground ! how his curl fall over his face ! how sad it be to see his poor white arm , and the blood trickle from it when papa bleed he ! and now he be well and amongst we , he be handsome certainly , but oh , be it possible he be he be stupid ? " when she light the lamp and look at he , do Psyche find Cupid out ; and be that the meaning of the old allegory ? the wing of love drop off at this discovery . the fancy can no more soar and disport in skyey region , the beloved object cease at once to be celestial , and remain plod on earth , entirely unromantic and substantial . 




 CHAPTER XXIII . holiday 

 Mrs. Lambert 's little day - dream be over . Miss Theo and her mother be oblige to confess in their heart that their hero be but an ordinary mortal . they utter few word on the subject , but each know the other 's thought as people who love each other do ; and mamma , by an extra tenderness and special caressing manner towards her daughter , seek to console she for her disappointment . " never mind , my dear " the maternal kiss whisper on the filial cheek " our hero have turn out to be but an ordinary mortal , and none such be good enough for my Theo . Thou shalt have a real husband ere long , if there be one in England . why , I be scarce fifteen when your father see I at the Bury Assembly , and while I be yet at school , I use to vow that I never would have any other man . if Heaven give I such a husband the good man in the whole kingdom sure it will bless my child equally , who deserve a king if she fancy he ! " indeed , I be not sure that Mrs. Lambert who , of course , know the age of the Prince of Wales , and be aware how handsome and good a young prince he be do not expect that he too would come rid by her gate , and perhaps tumble down from his horse there , and be take into the house , and be cure , and cause his royal grandpapa to give Martin Lambert a regiment , and fall in love with Theo . 
 the Colonel for his part , and his second daughter , Miss Hetty , be on the laugh , scornful , unbelieve side . Mamma be always match - making . indeed , Mrs. Lambert be much addicted to novel , and cry her eye out over they with great assiduity . no coach ever pass the gate , but she expect a husband for her girl would alight from it and ring the bell . as for Miss Hetty , she allow her tongue to wag in a more than usually saucy way : she make a hundred sly allusion to their guest . she introduce Prussia and Persia into their conversation with abominable pertness and frequency . she ask whether the present King of Prussia be call the Shaw or the Sophy , and how far it be from Ispahan to Saxony , which his Majesty be at present invading , and about which war papa be so busy with his map and his newspaper ? she bring down the Persian Tales from her mamma 's closet , and lay they slily on the table in the parlour where the family sate . she would not marry a persian prince for her part ; she would prefer a gentleman who might not have more than one wife at a time . she call our young Virginian Theo 's gentleman , Theo 's prince . she ask her mamma if she wish she , Hetty , to take the other visitor , the black prince , for herself ? indeed , she rally her sister and her mother unceasingly on their sentimentality , and would never stop until she have make they angry , when she would begin to cry herself , and kiss they violently one after the other , and coax they back into good - humour . Simple Harry Warrington , meanwhile , know nothing of all the joke , the tear , quarrel , reconciliation , hymeneal plan , and so forth , of which he be the innocent occasion . a hundred allusion to the Prussians and Persians be shoot at he , and those parthian arrow do not penetrate his hide at all . a Shaw ? a Sophy ? very likely he think a Sophy be a lady , and would have deem it the height of absurdity that a man with a great black beard should have any such name . we fall into the midst of a quiet family : we drop like a stone , say , into a pool , we be perfectly compact and cool , and little know the flutter and excitement we make there , disturb the fish , frighten the duck , and agitate the whole surface of the water . how should Harry know the effect which his sudden appearance produce in this little , quiet , sentimental family ? he think quite well enough of himself on many point , but be diffident as yet regard woman , be of that age when young gentleman require encouragement and to be bring forward , and have be bring up at home in very modest and primitive relation towards the other sex . so Miss Hetty 's joke play round the lad , and he mind they no more than so many summer gnat . it be not that he be stupid , as she certainly think he : he be simple , too much occupy with himself and his own honest affair to think of other . why , what tragedy , comedy , interlude , intrigue , farce , be go on under our nose in friend ' drawing - room where we visit every day , and we remain utterly ignorant , self - satisfy , and blind ! as these sister sate and comb their flow ringlet of night , or talk with each other in the great bed where , accord to the fashion of the day , they lay together , how should Harry know that he have so great a share in their thought , joke , conversation ? three day after his arrival , his new and hospitable friend be walk with he in my Lord Wrotham 's fine park , where they be free to wander ; and here , on a piece of water , they come to some swan , which the young lady be in the habit of feed with bread . as the bird approach the young woman , Hetty say , with a queer look at her mother and sister , and then a glance at her father , who stand by , honest , happy , in a red waistcoat , Hetty say : " Mamma 's swan be something like these , papa . " 
 " what swan , my dear ? " say mamma . 
 " something like , but not quite . they have short neck than these , and be , score of they , on our common , " continue Miss Hetty . " I see Betty pluck one in the kitchen this morning . we shall have it for dinner , with apple - sauce and " 
 " do not   be a little goose ! " say Miss Theo . 
 " and sage and onion . do you love swan , Mr. Warrington ? " 
 " I shoot three last winter on our river , " say the virginian gentleman . " our be not such white bird as these they eat very well , though . " the simple youth have not the slight idea that he himself be an allegory at that very time , and that Miss Hetty be narrate a fable regard he . in some exceedingly recondite latin work I have read that , long before Virginia be discover , other folk be equally dull of comprehension . 
 so it be a premature sentiment on the part of Miss Theo that little tender flutter of the bosom which we have acknowledge she feel on first behold the Virginian , so handsome , pale , and bleed . this be not the great passion which she know her heart could feel . like the bird , it have waken and begin to sing at a false dawn . hop back to thy perch , and cover thy head with thy wing , thou tremulous little fluttering creature ! it be not yet light , and roosting be as yet well than sing . Anon will come morning , and the whole sky will redden , and you shall soar up into it and salute the sun with your music . 
 one little phrase , some three - and - thirty line back , perhaps the fair and suspicious reader have remark : " three day after his arrival , Harry be walk with , " etc . etc . if he could walk which it appear he could do perfectly well what business have he to be walk with anybody but Lady Maria Esmond on the Pantiles , Tunbridge Wells ? his shoulder be set : his health be entirely restore : he have not even a change of coat , as we have see , and be oblige to the Colonel for his raiment . surely a young man in such a condition have no right to be linger on at Oakhurst , and be bind by every tie of duty and convenience , by love , by relationship , by a gentle heart wait for he , by the washerwoman finally , to go to Tunbridge . why do he stay behind , unless he be in love with either of the young lady   and we say he be not   ? could it be that he do not want to go ? hath the gracious reader understand the meaning of the mystic S with which the last chapter commence , and in which the designer have feebly endeavour to depict the notorious Sinbad the Sailor , surmount by that odious old man of the sea ? what if Harry Warrington should be that sailor , and his fate that choke , deadening , inevitable old man ? what if for two day past he have feel those knee throttle he round the neck ? if his fell aunt 's purpose be answer , and if his late love be kill as dead by her poisonous communication as fair Rosamond be by her royal and legitimate rival ? be Hero then light the lamp up , and get ready the supper , whilst Leander be sit comfortably with some other party , and never in the least thinking of take to the water ? ever since that coward 's blow be strike in Lady Maria 's back by her own relative , surely kind heart must pity her ladyship . I know she have fault ay , and wear false hair and false never mind what . but a woman in distress , shall we not pity she a lady of a certain age , be we go to laugh at she because of her year ? between her old aunt and her unhappy delusion , be sure my Lady Maria Esmond be have no very pleasant time of it at Tunbridge Wells . there be no one to protect she . Madam Beatrix have she all to herself . Lady Maria be poor , and hope for money from her aunt . Lady Maria have a secret or two which the old woman know , and brandish over she . I for one be quite melt and grow soft - hearted as I think of she . imagine she alone , and a victim to that old woman ! paint to yourself that antique Andromeda   if you please we will allow that rich flow head of hair to fall over her shoulder   chain to a rock on Mount Ephraim , and give up to that dragon of a Baroness ! Succour , Perseus ! come quickly with thy wing foot and flash falchion ! Perseus be not in the least hurry . the dragon have her will of Andromeda for day after day . 
 Harry Warrington , who would not have allow his dislocated and mend shoulder to keep he from go out hunting , remain day after day contentedly at Oakhurst , with each day find the kindly folk who welcome he more to his liking . perhaps he have never , since his grandfather 's death , be in such good company . his lot have lain amongst fox - hunt virginian squire , with whose society he have put up very contentedly , rid their horse , live their life , and share their punch - bowl . the lady of his own and mother 's acquaintance be very well breed , and decorous , and pious , no doubt , but somewhat narrow - minded . it be but a little place , his home , with its pompous way , small etiquette and punctilio , small flattery , small conversation and scandal . until he have leave the place , some time after , he do not know how narrow and confine his life have be there . he be free enough personally . he have dog and horse , and might shoot and hunt for score of mile round about : but the little lady - mother domineer at home , and when there he have to submit to her influence and breathe her air . 
 here the lad find himself in the midst of a circle where everything about he be incomparably gayer , bright , and more free . he be live with a man and woman who have see the world , though they live retire from it , who have both of they happen to enjoy from their early time the use not only of good book , but of good company those live book , which be such pleasant and sometimes such profitable reading . society have this good at least : that it lessen our conceit , by teach we our insignificance , and make we acquaint with our better . if you be a young person who read this , depend upon it , sir or madam , there be nothing more wholesome for you than to acknowledge and to associate with your superior . if I could , I would not have my son Thomas first Greek and latin prize boy , first oar , and cock of the school . well for his soul 's and body 's welfare that he should have a good place , not the first a fair set of competitor round about he , and a good thrashing now and then , with a hearty shake afterwards of the hand which administer the beating . what honest man that can choose his lot would be a prince , let we say , and have all society walk backwards before he , only obsequious household - gentleman to talk to , and all mankind mum except when your High Mightiness ask a question and give permission to speak ? one of the great benefit which Harry Warrington receive from this family , before whose gate Fate have shoot he , be to begin to learn that he be a profoundly ignorant young fellow , and that there be many people in the world far well than he know himself to be . Arrogant a little with some folk , in the company of his superior he be magnanimously docile . we have see how faithfully he admire his brother at home , and his friend , the gallant young Colonel of Mount Vernon : of the gentleman , his kinsman at Castlewood , he have feel himself at least the equal . in his new acquaintance at Oakhurst he find a man who have read far more book than Harry could pretend to judge of , who have see the world and come unwounde out of it , as he have out of the danger and battle which he have confront , and who have goodness and honesty write on his face and breathe from his lip , for which quality our brave lad have always an instinctive sympathy and predilection . 
 as for the woman , they be the kind , merriest , most agreeable he have as yet know . they be pleasanter than Parson Broadbent 's black - eyed daughter at home , whose laugh carry as far as a gun . they be quite as well - breed as the Castlewood lady , with the exception of Madam Beatrix   who , indeed , be as grand as an empress on some occasion . but somehow , after a talk with Madam Beatrix , and vast amusement and interest in her story , the lad would come away as with a bitter taste in his mouth , and fancy all the world wicke round about he . they be not in the least squeamish ; and laugh over page of Mr. Fielding , and cry over volume of Mr. Richardson , contain joke and incident which would make Mrs. Grundy 's hair stand on end , yet their merry prattle leave no bitterness behind it : their tale about this neighbour and that be droll , not malicious ; the curtsey and salutation with which the folk of the little neighbouring town receive they , how kindly and cheerful ! their bounty how cordial ! of a truth it be good to be with good people . how good Harry Warrington do not know at the time , perhaps , or until subsequent experience show he contrast , or cause he to feel remorse . here be a tranquil , sunshiny day of a life that be to be agitate and stormy a happy hour or two to remember . not much happen during the happy hour or two . it be only sweet sleep , pleasant waking , friendly welcome , serene pastime . the gate of the old house seem to shut the wicked world out somehow , and the inhabitant within to be well , and purer , and kinder than other people . he be not in love ; oh no ! not the least , either with saucy Hetty or generous Theodosia but when the time come for go away , he fasten on both their hand , and feel an immense regard for they . he think he should like to know their brother , and that they must be fine fellow ; and as for Mrs. Lambert , I believe she be as sentimental at his departure as if he have be the last volume of Clarissa Harlowe . 
 " he be very kind and honest , " say Theo , gravely , as , look from the terrace , they see he and their father and servant rid away on the road to Westerham . 
 " I do not   think he stupid at all now , " say little Hetty ; " and , mamma , I think , he be very like a swan indeed . " 
 " it feel just like one of the boy go to school , " say mamma . 
 " just like it , " say Theo , sadly . 
 " I be glad he have get papa to ride with he to Westerham , " resume Miss Hetty , " and that he buy Farmer Briggs 's horse . I do not   like his go to those Castlewood people . I be sure that Madame Bernstein be a wicked old woman . I expect to see she ride away on her crooked stick . " 
 " Hush , Hetty ! " 
 " do you think she would float if they try she in the pond , as poor old mother hely do at Elmhurst ? the other old woman seem fond of he I mean the one with the fair tour . she look very melancholy when she go away ; but Madame Bernstein whisk she off with her crutch , and she be oblige to go . I do not   care , Theo . I know she be a wicked woman . you think everybody good , you do , because you never do anything wrong yourself . " 
 " my Theo be a good girl , " say the mother , look fondly at both her daughter . 
 " then why do we call she a miserable sinner ? " 
 " we be all so , my love , " say mamma . 
 " what , papa too ? you know you do not   think so , " cry Miss Hester . and to allow this be almost more than Mrs. Lambert could afford . 
 " what be that you tell John to give to Mr. Warrington 's black man ? " 
 Mamma own , with some shamefacedness , it be a bottle of her cordial water and a cake which she have bid Betty make . " I feel quite like a mother to he , my dear , I can not   help own it , and you know both our boy still like one of our cake to take to school or college with they . " 




 CHAPTER XXIV . from Oakhurst to tunbridge 

 have her lily handkerchief in token of adieu to the depart traveller , Mrs. Lambert and her girl watch they pace leisurely on the first few hundred yard of their journey , and until such time as a tree - clump corner of the road hide they from the lady ' view . behind that clump of lime the good matron have many a time watch those she love well disappear . husband depart to battle and danger , son to school , each after the other have go on his way behind yonder green tree , return as it please Heaven 's will at his good time , and bring pleasure and love back to the happy little family . besides their own instinctive nature   which to be sure aid wonderfully in the matter , the leisure and contemplation attendant upon their home life serve to foster the tenderness and fidelity of our woman . the man go , there be all day to think about they , and to - morrow and to - morrow when there certainly will be a letter and so on . there be the vacant room to go look at , where the boy sleep last night , and the impression of his carpet bag be still on the bed . there be his whip hang up in the hall , and his fishing - rod and basket mute memorial of the brief bygone pleasure . at dinner there come up that cherry - tart , half of which our darling eat at two o'clock in spite of his melancholy , and with a choking little sister on each side of he . the evening prayer be say without that young scholar 's voice to utter the due response . midnight and silence come , and the good mother lie wakeful , think how one of the dear accustomed brood be away from the nest . morn break , home and holiday have pass away , and toil and labour have begin for he . so those rustle lime form , as it be , a screen between the world and our lady of the house at Oakhurst . kind - hearted Mrs. Lambert always become silent and thoughtful , if by chance she and her girl walk up to the tree in the absence of the man of the family . she say she would like to carve their name up on the grey silver trunk , in the midst of true - lover ' knot , as be then the kindly fashion ; and Miss Theo , who have an exceeding elegant turn that way , make some verse regard the tree , which she delighted parent transmit to a periodical of those day . 
 " now we be out of sight of the lady , " say Colonel Lambert , give a parting salute with his hat , as the pair of gentleman trot past the lime in question . " I know my wife always watch at her window until we be round this corner . I hope we shall have you see the tree and the house again , Mr. Warrington ; and the boy be at home , mayhap there will be well sport for you . " 
 " I never want to be happy , sir , than I have be , " reply Mr. Warrington ; " and I hope you will let I say , that I feel as if I be leave quite old friend behind I . " 
 " the friend at whose house we shall sup to - night hath a son , who be an old friend of our family , too ; and my wife , who be an inveterate marriage - monger , would have make a match between he and one of my girl , but that the Colonel hath choose to fall in love with somebody else . " 
 " ah ! " sigh Mr. Warrington . 
 " other folk have do the same thing . there be brave fellow before Agamemnon . " 
 " I beg your pardon , sir . be the gentleman 's name Aga ? I do not quite gather it , " meekly inquire the young traveller . 
 " no , his name be James Wolfe , " cry the Colonel , smile . " he be a young fellow still , or what we call so , be scarce thirty year old . he be the young lieutenant - colonel in the army , unless , to be sure , we except a few score of our nobility , who take rank before we common folk . " 
 " of course of course ! " say the Colonel 's young companion with true colonial notion of aristocratic precedence . 
 " and I have see he command captain , and very brave captain , who be thirty year his senior , and who have neither his merit nor his good fortune . but , lucky as he hath be , no one envy his superiority , for , indeed , most of we acknowledge that he be our superior . he be belove by every man of our old regiment and know every one of they . he be a good scholar as well as a consummate soldier , and a master of many language . " 
 " ah , sir ! " say Harry Warrington , with a sigh of great humility ; " I feel that I have neglect my own youth sadly ; and be come to England but an ignoramus . have my dear brother be alive , he would have represent our name and our colony , too , well than I can do . George be a scholar ; George be a musician ; George could talk with the most learn people in our country , and I make no doubt would have hold his own here . do you know , sir , I be glad to have come home , and to you especially , if but to learn how ignorant I be . " 
 " if you know that well , ' tis a great gain already , " say the Colonel , with a smile . 
 " at home , especially of late , and since we lose my brother , I use to think myself a mighty fine fellow , and have no doubt that the folk round about flatter I . I be wise now , that is , I hope I be , though perhaps I be wrong , and only brag again . but you see , sir , the gentry in our colony do not   know very much , except about dog and horse , and bet and game . I wish I know more about book , and less about they . " 
 " Nay . dog and horse be very good book , too , in their way , and we may read a deal of truth out of them . some man be not make to be scholar , and may be very worthy citizen and gentleman in spite of their ignorance . what call have all of we to be especially learn or wise , or to take a first place in the world ? his Royal Highness be commander , and Martin Lambert be colonel , and Jack Hunt , who rid behind yonder , be a private soldier , and be now a very honest , worthy groom . so as we all do our good in our station , it matter not much whether that be high or low . Nay , how do we know what be high and what be low ? and whether Jack 's currycomb , or my epaulet , or his Royal Highness 's baton , may not turn out to be pretty equal ? when I begin life , et militavi non sine never mind what I dream of success and honour ; now I think of duty , and yonder folk , from whom we part a few hour ago . let we trot on , else we shall not reach Westerham before nightfall . " 
 at Westerham the two friend be welcome by their host , a stately matron , an old soldier , whose recollection and service be of five - and - forty year back , and the son of this gentleman and lady , the Lieutenant - Colonel of Kingsley 's regiment , that be then station at Maidstone , whence the Colonel have come over on a brief visit to his parent . Harry look with some curiosity at this officer , who , young as he be , have see so much service , and obtain a character so high . there be little of the beautiful in his face . he be very lean and very pale ; his hair be red , his nose and cheek - bone be high ; but he have a fine courtesy towards his elder , a cordial greeting towards his friend , and an animation in conversation which cause those who hear he to forget , even to admire , his homely look . 
 Mr. Warrington be go to Tunbridge ? their James would bear he company , the lady of the house say , and whisper something to Colonel Lambert at supper , which occasion smile and a knowing wink or two from that officer . he call for wine , and toast " Miss Lowther . " " with all my heart , " cry the enthusiastic Colonel James , and drain his glass to the very last drop . Mamma whisper her friend how James and the lady be go to make a match , and how she come of the famous Lowther family of the North . 
 " if she be the daughter of King Charlemagne , " cry Lambert , ' she be not too good for James Wolfe , or for his mother 's son . " 
 " Mr. Lambert would not say so if he know she , " the young Colonel declare . 
 " oh , of course , she be the priceless pearl , and you be nothing , " cry mamma . " no .
