I be of Colonel Lambert 's opinion ; and , if she bring all Cumberland to you for a jointure , I should say it be my James 's due . that be the way with them , Mr. Warrington . we tend our child through fever , and measle , and whooping - cough , and small - pox ; we send they to the army and can not   sleep at night for thinking ; we break our heart at part with them , and have they at home only for a week or two in the year , or maybe ten year , and , after all our care , there come a lass with a pair of bright eye , and away go our boy , and never care a fig for we afterwards . " 
 " and pray , my dear , how do you come to marry James 's papa ? " say the eld Colonel Wolfe . " and why do not   you stay at home with your parent ? " 
 " because James 's papa be gouty , and want somebody to take care of he , I suppose ; not because I like he a bit , " answer the lady : and so with much easy talk and kindness the evening pass away . 
 on the morrow , and with many expression of kindness and friendship for his late guest , Colonel Lambert give over the young Virginian to Mr. Wolfe 's charge , and turn his horse 's head homeward , while the two gentleman speed towards Tunbridge Wells . Wolfe be in a hurry to reach the place , Harry Warrington be , perhaps , not quite so eager : nay , when Lambert ride towards his own home , Harry 's thought follow he with a great deal of longing desire to the parlour at Oakhurst , where he have spend three day in happy calm . Mr. Wolfe agree in all Harry 's enthusiastic praise of Mr. Lambert , and of his wife , and of his daughter , and of all that excellent family . " to have such a good name , and to live such a life as Colonel Lambert 's , " say Wolfe , ' seem to I now the height of human ambition . " 
 " and glory and honour ? " ask Warrington , " be those nothing ? and would you give up the winning of they ? " 
 " they be my dream once , " answer the Colonel , who have now different idea of happiness , " and now my desire be much more tranquil . I have follow arm ever since I be fourteen year of age . I have see almost every kind of duty connect with my calling . I know all the garrison town in this country , and have have the honour to serve wherever there have be work to be do during the last ten year . I have do pretty near the whole of a soldier 's duty , except , indeed , the command of an army , which can hardly be hop for by one of my year ; and now , methink , I would like quiet , book to read , a wife to love I , and some child to dandle on my knee . I have imagine some such Elysium for myself , Mr. Warrington . true love be well than glory ; and a tranquil fireside , with the woman of your heart seat by it , the great good the god can send to we . " 
 Harry imagine to himself the picture which his comrade call up . he say " yes , " in answer to the other 's remark ; but , no doubt , do not give a very cheerful assent , for his companion observe upon the expression of his face . 
 " you say ' yes ' as if a fireside and a sweetheart be not particularly to your taste . " 
 " why , look you , Colonel , there be other thing which a young fellow might like to enjoy . you have have sixteen year of the world : and I be but a few month away from my mother 's apron - string . when I have see a campaign or two , or six , as you have : when I have distinguish myself like Mr. Wolfe , and make the world talk of I , I then may think of retire from it . " 
 to these remark , Mr. Wolfe , whose heart be full of a very different matter , reply by break out in a further encomium of the joy of marriage ; and a special rhapsody upon the beauty and merit of his mistress a theme intensely interesting to himself , though not so , possibly , to his hearer , whose view regard a married life , if he permit himself to entertain any , be somewhat melancholy and despondent . a pleasant afternoon bring they to the end of their ride ; nor do any accident or incident accompany it , save , perhaps , a mistake which Harry Warrington make at some few mile ' distance from Tunbridge Wells , where two horseman stop they , whom Harry be for charge , pistol in hand , suppose they to be highwayman . Colonel Wolfe , laughing , bade Mr. Warrington reserve his fire , for these folk be only innkeeper ' agent , and not robber   except in their calling . Gumbo , whose horse run away with he at this particular juncture , be bring back after a great deal of bawl on his master 's part , and the two gentleman ride into the little town , alight at their inn , and then separate , each in quest of the lady whom he have come to visit . 
 Mr. Warrington find his aunt instal in handsome lodging , with a guard of London lacquey in her anteroom , and to follow her chair when she go abroad . she receive he with the utmost kindness . his cousin , my Lady Maria , be absent when he arrive : I do not   know whether the young gentleman be unhappy at not see she : or whether he disguise his feeling , or whether Madame de Bernstein take any note regard they . 
 a beau in a rich figured suit , the first speciman of the kind Harry have see , and two dowager with voluminous hoop and plenty of rouge , be on a visit to the Baroness when her nephew make his bow to she . she introduce the young man to these personage as her nephew , the young Croesus out of Virginia , of whom they have hear . she talk about the immensity of his estate , which be as large as Kent ; and , as she have read , infinitely more fruitful . she mention how her half - sister , Madam Esmond , be call Princess Pocahontas in her own country . she never tired in her praise of mother and son , of their rich and their good quality . the beau shake the young man by the hand , and be delighted to have the honour to make his acquaintance . the lady praise he to his aunt so loudly that the modest youth be fain to blush at their compliment . they go away to inform the Tunbridge society of the news of his arrival . the little place be soon buzz with account of the wealth , the good breeding , and the good look of the Virginian . 
 " you could not have come at a well moment , my dear , " the Baroness say to her nephew , as her visitor depart with many curtsey and congee . " those three individual have the most active tongue in the Wells . they will trumpet your good quality in every company where they go . I have introduce you to a hundred people already , and , Heaven help I ! have tell all sort of fib about the geography of Virginia in order to describe your estate . it be a prodigious large one , but I be afraid I have magnify it . I have fill it with all sort of wonderful animal , gold mine , spice ; I be not sure I have not say diamond . as for your negro , I have give your mother army of they , and , in fact , represent she as a sovereign princess reign over a magnificent dominion . so she have a magnificent dominion : I can not tell to a few hundred thousand pound how much her yearly income be , but I have no doubt it be a very great one . and you must prepare , sir , to be treat here as the heir - apparent of this royal lady . do not let your head be turn . from this day forth you be go to be flatter as you have never be flatter in your life . " 
 " and to what end , ma'am ? " ask the young gentleman . " I see no reason why I should be repute so rich , or get so much flattery . " 
 " in the first place , sir , you must not contradict your old aunt , who have no desire to be make a fool of before her company . and as for your reputation , you must know we find it here almost ready - make on our arrival . a London newspaper have somehow hear of you , and come out with a story of the immense wealth of a young gentleman from Virginia lately land , and a nephew of my Lord Castlewood . immensely wealthy you be , and can not   help yourself . all the world be eager to see you . you shall go to church to - morrow morning , and see how the whole congregation will turn away from its book and prayer , to worship the golden calf in your person . you would not have have I undeceive they , would you , and speak ill of my own flesh and blood ? " 
 " but how be I better by this reputation for money ? " ask Harry . 
 " you be make your entry into the world , and the gold key will open most of its door to you . to be think rich be as good as to be rich . you need not spend much money . People will say that you hoard it , and your reputation for avarice will do you good rather than harm . you will see how the mother will smile upon you , and the daughter will curtsey ! do not   look surprised ! when I be a young woman myself I do as all the rest of the world do , and try to better myself by more than one desperate attempt at a good marriage . your poor grandmother , who be a saint upon earth to be sure , bat a little jealousy , use to scold I , and call I worldly . worldly , my dear ! so be the world worldly ; and we must serve it as it serve we ; and give it nothing for nothing . Mr. Henry Esmond Warrington I can not   help love the two first name , sir , old woman as I be , and that I tell you on come here or to London , would have be nobody . our protection would have help he but little . our family have little credit , and , entre nous , not much reputation . I suppose you know that Castlewood be more than suspect in ' 45 , and hath since ruin himself by play ? " 
 Harry have never hear about Lord Castlewood or his reputation . 
 " he never have much to lose , but he have lose that and more : his wretched estate be eat up with mortgage . he have be at all sort of scheme to raise money : my dear , he have be so desperate at time , that I do not think my diamond be safe with he ; and have travel to and from Castlewood without they . terrible , be not   it , to speak so of one 's own nephew ? but you be my nephew , too , and not spoil by the world yet , and I wish to warn you of its wickedness . I hear of your play - doing with Will and the chaplain , but they could do you no harm , nay , I be tell you have the well of they . have you play with Castlewood , you would have have no such luck : and you would have play , have not an old aunt of yours warn my Lord Castlewood to keep his hand off you . " 
 " what , ma'am , do you interfere to preserve I ? " 
 " I keep his clutch off from you : be thankful that you be come out of that ogre 's den with any flesh on your bone ! my dear , it have be the rage and passion of all our family . my poor silly brother play ; both his wife play , especially the last one , who have little else to live upon now but her nightly assembly in London , and the money for the card . I would not trust she at Castlewood alone with you : the passion be too strong for they , and they would fall upon you , and fleece you ; and then fall upon each other , and fight for the plunder . but for his place about the Court my poor nephew hath nothing , and that be Will 's fortune , too , sir , and Maria 's and her sister 's . " 
 " and be they , too , fond of the card ? " 
 " no ; to do poor Molly justice , gaming be not her passion : but when she be amongst they in London , little Fanny will bet her eye out of her head . I know what the passion be , sir : do not look so astonished ; I have have it , as I have the measle when I be a child . I be not cure quite . for a poor old woman there be nothing leave but that . you will see some high play at my card - table to - night . Hush ! my dear . it be that I want , and without which I mop so at Castlewood ! I could not win of my niece or their mother . they would not pay if they lose . ' tis good to warn you , my dear , in time , lest you should be shock by the discovery . I can not   live without the card , there be the truth ! " 
 a few day before , and while stay with his Castlewood relative , Harry , who love card , and cock - fighting , and bet , and every conceivable sport himself , would have laugh very likely at this confession . amongst that family into whose society he have fall , many thing be laugh at , over which some folk look grave . faith and honour be laugh at ; pure life be disbelieve ; selfishness be proclaim as common practice ; sacred duty be sneeringly speak of , and vice flippantly condone . these be no Pharisees : they profess no hypocrisy of virtue , they fling no stone at discover sinner : they smile , shrug their shoulder , and pass on . the member of this family do not pretend to be a whit well than their neighbour , whom they despise heartily ; they live quite familiarly with the folk about whom and whose wife they tell such wicked , funny story ; they take their share of what pleasure or plunder come to hand , and live from day to day till their last day come for they . of course there be no such people now ; and human nature be very much change in the last hundred year . at any rate , card - playing be greatly out of mode : about that there can be no doubt : and very likely there be not six lady of fashion in London who know the difference between Spadille and Manille . 
 " how dreadfully dull you must have find those humdrum people at that village where we leave you but the savage be very kind to you , child ! " say Madame de Bernstein , pat the young man 's cheek with her pretty old hand . 
 " they be very kind ; and it be not at all dull , ma'am , and I think they be some of the good people in the world , " say Harry , with his face flush up . his aunt 's tone jar upon he . he could not bear that any one should speak or think lightly of the new friend whom he have find . he do not want they in such company . 
 the old lady , imperious and prompt to anger , be about to resent the check she have receive , but a second thought make her pause . " those two girl , " she think , " a sick - bed an interesting stranger of course he have be fall in love with one of they . " Madame Bernstein look round with a mischievous glance at Lady Maria , who enter the room at this juncture . 




 CHAPTER XXV . New Acquaintances 

 Cousin Maria make her appearance , attend by a couple of gardener 's boy bear basket of flower , with which it be propose to decorate Madame de Bernstein 's drawing - room against the arrival of her ladyship 's company . three footman in livery , gorgeously laced with worst , set out twice as many card - table . a major - domo in black and a bag , with fine laced ruffle ; and look as if he ought to have a sword by his side , follow the lacquey bear fasce of wax candle , which he place a pair on each card - table , and in the silver sconce on the wainscote wall that be now gild with the slanting ray of the sun , as be the prospect of the green common beyond , with its rock and clump of tree and house twinkle in the sunshine . group of many - colour figure in hoop and powder and brocade saunter over the green , and dapple the plain with their shadow . on the other side from the Baroness 's window you see the Pantiles , where a perpetual fair be hold , and hear the clatter and buzzing of the company . a band of music be here perform for the benefit of the visitor to the Wells . Madame Bernstein 's chief sitting - room might not suit a recluse or a student , but for those who like bustle , gaiety , a bright cross light , and a view of all that be go on in the cheery busy place , no lodging could be pleasanter . and when the window be light up , the passenger walk below be aware that her ladyship be at home and hold a card - assembly , to which an introduction be easy enough . by the way , in speak of the past , I think the night - life of society a hundred year since be rather a dark life . there be not one wax - candle for ten which we now see in a lady 's drawing - room : let alone gas and the wondrous new illumination of club . horrible gutter tallow smoke and stunk in passage . the candle - snuffer be a notorious officer in the theatre . see Hogarth 's picture : how dark they be , and how his feast be , as it be , begrime with tallow ! in " marriage a la Mode , " in Lord Viscount Squanderfield 's grand saloon , where he and his wife be sit yawn before the horror - stricken steward when their party be over there be but eight candle one on each card - table , and half a dozen in a brass chandelier . if Jack Briefless convoke his friend to oyster and beer in his chamber , Pump Court , he would have twice as many . let we comfort ourselves by think that Louis Quatorze in all his glory hold his revel in the dark , and bless Mr. Price and other luciferous benefactor of mankind , for banish the abominable mutton of our youth . 
 so Maria with her flower   herself the fairest flower , pop her rose , sweet - williams , and so forth , in vas here and there , and adorn the apartment to the good of her art . she linger fondly over this bowl and that dragon jar , cast but sly timid glance the while at young cousin Harry , whose own blush would have become any young woman , and you might have think that she possibly intend to outstay her aunt ; but that Baroness , seat in her arm - chair , her crooked tortoiseshell stick in her hand , point the servant imperiously to their duty ; rat one and the other soundly : Tom for have a darn in his stocking ; John for have grease his lock too profusely out of the candle - box ; and so forth keep a stern domination over they . another remark concern poor jeame of a hundred year ago : Jeames sleep two in a bed , four in a room , and that room a cellar very likely , and he wash in a trough such as you would hardly see anywhere in London now out of the barrack of her Majesty 's Foot Guards . 
 if Maria hop a present interview , her fond heart be disappoint . " where be you go to dine , Harry ? " ask Madame de Bernstein . " my niece Maria and I shall have a chicken in the little parlour I think you should go to the good ordinary . there be one at the White Horse at three , we shall hear his bell in a minute or two . and you will understand , sir , that you ought not to spare expense , but behave like Princess Pocahontas 's son . your trunk have be take over to the lodging I have engage for you . it be not good for a lad to be always hang about the apron of two old woman . be it , Maria ? " 
 " no , " say her ladyship , drop her meek eye ; whilst the other lady 's glare in triumph . I think Andromeda have be a good deal expose to the Dragon in the course of the last five or six day : and if Perseus have cut the latter 's cruel head off he would have commit not unjustifiable monstricide . but he do not bare sword or shield ; he only look mechanically at the lacquey in tawny and blue as they creak about the room . 
 " and there be good mercer and tailor from London always here to wait on the company at the Wells . you have well see they , my dear , for your suit be not of the very last fashion a little lace " 
 " I can not   go out of mourning , ma'am , " say the young man , look down at his sable . 
 " Ho , sir , " cry the lady , rustle up from her chair and rise on her cane , " wear black for your brother till you be as old as Methuselah , if you like . I be sure I do not   want to prevent you . I only want you to dress , and to do like other people , and make a figure worthy of your name . " 
 " Madam , " say Mr. Warrington with great state , " I have not do anything to disgrace it that I know . " 
 why do the old woman stop and give a little start as if she have be strike ? let bygone be bygone . she and the boy have a score of little passage of this kind in which sword be cross and thrust rapidly deal or parry . she like Harry none the bad for his courage in face she . " sure a little fine linen than that shirt you wear will not be a disgrace to you , sir , " she say , with rather a forced laugh . 
 Harry bow and blush . it be one of the homely gift of his Oakhurst friend . he feel pleased somehow to think he wear it ; think of the new friend , so good , so pure , so simple , so kindly , with immense tenderness , and feel , while invest in this garment , as if evil could not touch he . he say he would go to his lodging , and make a point of return array in the good linen he have . 
 " come back here , sir , " say Madame Bernstein , " and if our company have not arrive , Maria and I will find some ruffle for you ! " and herewith , under a footman 's guidance , the young fellow walk off to his new lodging . 
 Harry find not only handsome and spacious apartment provide for he , but a groom in attendance wait to be engage by his honour , and a second valet , if he be inclined to hire one to wait upon Mr. Gumbo . Ere he have be many minute in his room , emissarie from a London tailor and bootmaker wait he with the card and compliment of their employer , Messrs. Regnier and Tull ; the good article in his modest wardrobe be lay out by Gumbo , and the fine linen with which his thrifty virginian mother have provide he . vision of the snow - surround home in his own country , of the crackling log and the trim quiet lady work by the fire , rise up before he . for the first time a little thought that the homely clothe be not quite smart enough , the home - work linen not so fine as it might be , cross the young man 's mind . that he should be ashamed of anything belong to he or to Castlewood ! that be strange . the simple folk there be only too well satisfied with all thing that be do , or say , or produce at Castlewood ; and Madam Esmond , when she send her son forth on his travel , think no young nobleman need be well provide . the clothe might have fit well and be of a later fashion , to be sure but still the young fellow present a comely figure enough when he issue from his apartment , his toilet over ; and Gumbo call a chair , march beside it , until they reach the ordinary where the young gentleman be to dine . 
 here he expect to find the beau whose acquaintance he have make a few hour before at his aunt 's lodging , and who have indicate to Harry that the White Horse be the most modish place for dining at the Wells , and he mention his friend 's name to the host : but the landlord and waiter lead he into the room with many smile and bow assure his honour that his honour do not need any other introduction than his own , help he to hang up his coat and sword on a peg , ask he whether he would drink Burgundy , Pontac , or champagne to his dinner , and lead he to a table . 
 though the most fashionable ordinary in the village , the White Horse do not happen to be crowd on this day . Monsieur Barbeau , the landlord , inform Harry that there be a great entertainment at Summer Hill , which have take away most of the company ; indeed , when Harry enter the room , there be but four other gentleman in it . two of these guest be drink wine , and have finish their dinner : the other two be young man in the midst of their meal , to whom the landlord , as he pass , must have whisper the name of the new - comer , for they look at he with some appearance of interest , and make he a slight bow across the table as the smile host bustle away for Harry 's dinner . 
 Mr. Warrington return the salute of the two gentleman , who bid he welcome to Tunbridge , and hop he would like the place upon well acquaintance . then they smile and exchange waggish look with each other , of which Harry do not understand the meaning , nor why they cast knowing glance at the two other guest over their wine . 
 one of these person be in a somewhat tarnish velvet coat with a huge queue and bag , and voluminous ruffle and embroidery . the other be a little beetle - browe , hook - nose , high - shoulder gentleman , whom his opposite companion address as milor , or my lord , in a very high voice . my lord , who be sip the wine before he , barely glanced at the new - comer , and then address himself to his own companion . 
 " and so you know the nephew of the old woman the Croesus who come to arrive ? " 
 " you be throw out there , Jack ! " say one young gentleman to the other . 
 " never could manage the lingo , " say Jack . the two elder have begin to speak in the french language . 
 " but assuredly , my dear lord ! " say the gentleman with the long queue . 
 " you have show energy , my dear Baron ! he have be here but two hour . my people tell I of he only as I come to dinner . " 
 " I know he before ! I have meet he often in London with the Baroness and my lord , his cousin , " say the Baron . 
 a smoking soup for Harry here come in , bear by the smile host . " Behold , sir ! behold a potage of my fashion ! " say my landlord , lay down the dish and whispering to Harry the celebrated name of the nobleman opposite . Harry thank Monsieur Barbeau in his own language , upon which the foreign gentleman , turn round , grin most graciously at Harry , and say , " fous bossedez notre langue barfaidement , monsieur . " Mr. Warrington have never hear the french language pronounce in that manner in Canada . he bow in return to the foreign gentleman . 
 " tell I more about the Croesus , my good Baron , " continue his lordship , speak rather superciliously to his companion , and take no notice of Harry , which perhaps somewhat nettle the young man . 
 " what will you , that I tell you , my dear lord ? Croesus be a youth like other youth ; he be tall , like other youth ; he be awkward , like other youth ; he have black hair , as they all have who come from the Indies . lodging have be take for he at Mrs. Rose 's toy - shop . " 
 " I have lodging there too , " think Mr. Warrington . " who be Croesus they be talk of ? how good the soup be ! " 
 " he travel with a large retinue , " the Baron continue , " four servant , two postchaise , and a pair of outrider . his chief attendant be a black man who save his life from the savage in America , and who will not hear , on any account , of be make free . he persist in wear mourning for his eld brother from whom he inherit his principality . " 
 " could anything console you for the death of your , Chevalier ? " cry out the eld gentleman . 
 " Milor ! his property might , " say the Chevalier , " which you know be not small . " 
 " your brother live on his patrimony which you have tell I be immense you by your industry , my dear Chevalier . " 
 " Milor ! " cry the individual address as Chevalier . 
 " by your industry or your esprit , how much more noble ! shall you be at the Baroness 's to - night ? she ought to be a little of your parent , Chevalier ? " 
 " again I fail to comprehend your lordship , " say the other gentleman , rather sulkily . 
 " why , she be a woman of great wit she be of noble birth she have undergo strange adventure she have but little principle   there you happily have the advantage of she . but what care we man of the world ? you intend to go and play with the young Creole , no doubt , and get as much money from he as you can . by the way , Baron , suppose he should be a guet - apen , that young Creole ? suppose our excellent friend have invent he up in London , and bring he down with his character for wealth to prey upon the innocent folk here ? " 
 " J'y ai souvent pense , milor , " say the little Baron , place his finger to his nose very knowingly , " that Baroness be capable of anything . " 
 " a Baron a Baroness , que voulez - vous , my friend ? I mean the late lamented husband . do you know who he be ? " 
 " intimately . a more notorious villain never deal a card . at Venice , at Brussels , at Spa , at Vienna the gaol of every one of which place he know . I know the man , my lord . " 
 " I think you would . I see he at the Hague , where I first have the honour of meet you , and a more disreputable rogue never enter my door . a minister must open they to all sort of people , Baron , spy , sharper , ruffian of every sort . " 
 " Parbleu , milor , how you treat they ! " say my lord 's companion . 
 " a man of my rank , my friend of the rank I hold then of course , must see all sort of people entre autre your acquaintance . what his wife could want with such a name as his I can not   conceive . " 
 " apparently , it be well than the lady 's own . " 
 " effectively ! so I have hear of my friend Paddy change clothe with the scarecrow . I do not   know which name be the most distinguish , that of the English bishop or the german baron . " 
 " my lord , " cry the other gentleman , rise and lay his hand on a large star on his coat , " you forget that I , too , be a Baron and a Chevalier of the Holy Roman " 
 " order of the Spur ! not in the least , my dear knight and baron ! you will have no more wine ? we shall meet at Madame de Bernstein 's to - night . " the knight and baron quit the table , feel in his embroider pocket , as if for money to give the waiter , who bring he his great laced hat , and wave that menial off with a hand surround by large ruffle and blazing ring , he stalk away from the room . 
 it be only when the person address as my lord have begin to speak of the bishop 's widow and the german baron 's wife that Harry Warrington be aware how his aunt and himself have be the subject of the two gentleman 's conversation . Ere the conviction have settle itself on his mind , one of the speaker have quit the room , and the other , turn to a table at which two gentleman sate , say , " what a little sharp it be ! everything I say about Bernstein relate mutato nomine to he . I know the fellow to be a spy and a rogue . he have change his religion I do not   know how many time . I have he turn out of the Hague myself when I be ambassador , and I know he be can in Vienna . " 
 " I wonder my Lord Chesterfield associate with such a villain ! " call out Harry from his table . the other couple of diner look at he . to his surprise the nobleman so address go on talk . 
 " there can not be a more fieffe coquin than this Poellnitz . why , Heaven be thank , he have actually leave I my snuff - box ! you laugh ? the fellow be capable of take it . " and my lord think it be his own satire at which the young man be laugh . 
 " you be quite right , sir , " say one of the two diner , turn to Mr. Warrington , " though , save your presence , I do not   know what business it be of your . my lord will play with anybody who will set he . do not   be alarm , he be as deaf as a post , and do not hear a word that you say ; and that be why my lord will play with anybody who will put a pack of card before he , and that be the reason why he consort with this rogue . " 
 " Faith , I know other nobleman who be not particular as to their company , " say Mr. Jack . 
 " do you mean because I associate with you ? I know my company , my good friend , and I defy most man to have the well of I . " 
 not have pay the least attention to Mr. Warrington 's angry interruption , my lord opposite be talk in his favourite French with Monsieur Barbeau , the landlord , and graciously compliment he on his dinner . the host bow again and again ; be enchant that his Excellency be satisfy : have not forget the art which he have learn when he be a young man in his Excellency 's kingdom of Ireland . the salmi be to my lord 's liking ? he have just serve a dish to the young american seigneur who sate opposite , the gentleman from Virginia . 
 " to whom ? " my lord 's pale face become red for a moment , as he ask this question , and look towards Harry Warrington , opposite to he . 
 " to the young gentleman from Virginia who have just arrive , and who perfectly possess our beautiful language ! " say Mr. Barbeau , think to kill two bird , as it be , with this one stone of a compliment . 
 " and to whom your lordship will be answerable for language reflect upon my family , and utter in the presence of these gentleman , " cry out Mr. Warrington , at the top of his voice , determine that his opponent should hear . 
 " you must go and call into his ear , and then he may perchance hear you , " say one of the young guest . 
 " I will take care that his lordship shall understand my meaning , one way or other , " Mr. Warrington say , with much dignity ; " and will not suffer calumny regard my relative to be utter by he or any other man ! " 
 Whilst Harry be speak , the little nobleman opposite to he do not hear he , but have time sufficient to arrange his own reply . he have rise , pass his handkerchief once or twice across his mouth , and lay his slim finger on the table . " Sir , " say he , " you will believe , on the word of a gentleman , that I have no idea before whom I be speak , and it seem that my acquaintance , Monsieur de Poellnitz , know you no well than myself . have I know you , believe I that I should have be the last man in the world to utter a syllable that should give you annoyance ; and I tender you my regret and apology , before my Lord March and Mr. Morris here present . " 
 to these word , Mr. Warrington could only make a bow , and mumble out a few word of acknowledgment : which speech have make believe to hear , my lord make Harry another very profound bow , and say he should have the honour of wait upon Mr. Warrington at his lodging , salute the company , and go away . 




 CHAPTER XXVI . in which we be at a very Great Distance from Oakhurst 

 within the precinct of the White Horse Tavern , and come up to the window of the eating - room , be a bowling - green , with a table or two , where guest might sit and partake of punch or tea . the three gentleman have come to an end of their dinner about the same time , Mr. Morris propose that they should adjourn to the Green , and there drink a cool bottle . " Jack Morris would adjourn to the Dust Hole , as a pretext for a fresh drink , " say my lord . on which Jack say he suppose each gentleman have his own favourite way of go to the deuce . his weakness , he own , be a bottle . 
 " my Lord Chesterfield 's deuce be deuce - ace , " say my Lord March . " his lordship can not   keep away from the card or dice . " 
 " my Lord March have not one devil , but several devil . he love gamble , he love horse - racing , he love bet , he love drink , he love eat , he love money , he love woman ; and you have fall into bad company , Mr. Warrington , when you light upon his lordship . he will play you for every acre you have in Virginia . " 
 " with the great pleasure in life , Mr. Warrington ! " interpose my lord . 
 " and for all your tobacco , and for all your spice , and for all your slave , and for all your oxen and ass , and for everything that be your . " 
 " shall we begin now ? Jack , you be never without a dice - box or a bottle - screw . I will set Mr. Warrington for what he like . " 
 " unfortunately , my lord , the tobacco , and the slave , and the ass , and the oxen , be not mine , as yet . I be just of age , and my mother , scarce twenty year old , have quite as good chance of long life as I have . " 
 " I will bet you that you survive she . I will pay you a sum now against four time the sum to be pay at her death . I will set you a fair sum over this table against the reversion of your estate in Virginia at the old lady 's departure . what do you call your place ? " 
 " Castlewood . " 
 " a principality , I hear it be . I will bet that its value have be exaggerate ten time at least amongst the quidnunc here . how come you by the name of Castlewood ? you be relate to my lord ? oh , stay : I know , my lady , your mother , descend from the real head of the house . he take the lose side in ' 15 . I have have the story a dozen time from my old duchess . she know your grandfather . he be friend of Addison and Steele , and Pope and Milton , I dare say , and the bigwig . it be a pity he do not stay at home , and transport the other branch of the family to the plantation . " 
 " I have just be stay at Castlewood with my cousin there , " remark Mr. Warrington . 
 " Hm ! do you play with he ? he be fond of pasteboard and bone . " 
 " never , but for sixpence and a pool of commerce with the lady . " 
 " so much the well for both of you . but you play with Will Esmond if he be at home ? I will lay ten to one you play with Will Esmond . " 
 Harry blush , and own that of an evening his cousin and he have have a few game at card . 
 " and Tom Sampson , the chaplain , " cry Jack Morris , " be he of the party ? I wager that Tom make a third , and the Lord deliver you from Tom and Will Esmond together ! " 
 " Nay ; the truth be , I win of both of they , " say Mr. Warrington . 
 " and they pay you ? well , miracle will never cease ! " 
 " I do not say anything about miracle , " remark Mr. Harry , smile over his wine . 
 " and you do not   tell tale out of school the volto sciolto hey , Mr. Warrington ? " say my lord . 
 " I beg your pardon , " say downright Harry , " French be the only language besides my own of which I know a little . " 
 " my Lord March have learn italian at the Opera , and a pretty penny his lesson have cost he , " remark Jack Morris . " we must show he the Opera must not   we , March ? " 
 " must we , Morris ? " say my lord , as if he only half like the other 's familiarity . 
 both of the two gentleman be dress alike , in small scratch - wig without powder , in blue frock with plate button , in buckskin and riding - boot , in little hat with a narrow cord of lace , and no outward mark of fashion . 
 " I do not   care about the Opera much , my lord , " say Harry , warm with his wine ; " but I should like to go to Newmarket , and long to see a good english hunting - field . " 
 " we will show you Newmarket and the hunting - field , sir . can you ride pretty well ? " 
 " I think I can , " Harry say ; " and I can shoot pretty well , and jump some . " 
 " what be your weight ? I bet you we weigh even , or I weigh most . I bet you Jack Morris beat you at bird or a mark , at five - and - twenty pace . I bet you I jump far than you on flat ground , here on this green . " 
 " I do not   know Mr. Morris 's shooting I never see either gentleman before but I take your bet , my lord , at what you please , " cry Harry , who by this time be more than warm with Burgundy . 
 " pony on each ! " cry my lord . 
 " done and do ! " cry my lord and Harry together . the young man think it be for the honour of his country not to be ashamed of any bet make to he . 
 " we can try the last bet now , if your foot be pretty steady , " say my lord , spring up , stretch his arm and limb , and look at the crisp , dry grass . he draw his boot off , then his coat and waistcoat , buckle his belt round his waist , and fling his clothe down to the ground . 
 Harry have more respect for his garment . it be his good suit . he take off the velvet coat and waistcoat , fold they up daintily , and , as the two or three table round be slop with drink , go to place the clothe on a table in the eating - room , of which the window be open . 
 here a new guest have enter ; and this be no other than Mr. Wolfe , who be soberly eat a chicken and salad , with a modest pint of wine . Harry be in high spirit . he tell the Colonel he have a bet with my Lord March would Colonel Wolfe stand he half ? the Colonel say he be too poor to bet . would he come out and see fair play ? that he would with all his heart . Colonel Wolfe set down his glass , and stalk through the open window after his young friend . 
 " who be that tallow - face put with the carroty hair ? " say Jack Morris , on whom the Burgundy have have its due effect . 
 Mr. Warrington explain that this be Lieutenant - Colonel Wolfe , of the 20th regiment . 
 " your humble servant , gentleman ! " say the Colonel , make the company a rigid military bow . 
 " never see such a figure in my life ! " cry Jack Morris . " do you March ? " 
 " I beg your pardon , I think you say March ? " say the Colonel , look very much surprised . 
 " I be the Earl of March , sir , at Colonel Wolfe 's service , " say the nobleman , bow . " my friend , Mr. Morris , be so intimate with I , that , after dinner , we be quite like brother . " 
 why be not all Tunbridge Wells by to hear this ? think Morris . and he be so delighted that he shout out , " two to one on my lord ! " 
 " do ! " call out Mr. Warrington ; and the enthusiastic Jack be oblige to cry " do ! " too . 
 " take he , Colonel , " Harry whisper to his friend . 
 but the Colonel say he could not afford to lose , and therefore could not hope to win . 
 " I see you have win one of our bet already , Mr. Warrington , " my Lord March remark . " I be tall than you by an inch or two , but you be broad round the shoulder . " 
 " Pooh , my dear will ! I bet you you weigh twice as much as he do ! " cry Jack Morris . 
 " Done , Jack ! " say my lord , laugh . " the bet be all pony . will you take he , Mr. Warrington ? " 
 " no , my dear fellow one be enough , " say Jack . 
 " very good , my dear fellow , " say my lord ; " and now we will settle the other wager . " 
 have already array himself in his good silk stocking , black satin - net breech , and neat pump , Harry do not care to take off his shoe as his antagonist have do , whose heavy riding - boot and spur be , to be sure , little calculate for leap . they have before they a fine even green turf of some thirty yard in length , enough for a run and enough for a jump . a gravel walk run around this green , beyond which be a wall and gate - sign a field azure , bear the Hanoverian White Horse rampant between two skittle proper , and for motto the name of the landlord and of the animal depict . 
 my lord 's friend lay a handkerchief on the ground as the mark whence the leaper be to take their jump , and Mr. Wolfe stand at the other end of the grass - plat to note the spot where each come down . " my lord go first , " write Mr. Warrington , in a letter to Mrs. Mountain , at Castlewood , Virginia , still extant . " he be for have I take the lead ; but , remember the story about the Battel of Fontanoy which my dearest George use to tell , I say , ' Monseigneur le Comte , tirez le premier , s'il vous play . ' so he take his run in his stocken foot , and for the honour of Old Virginia , I have the gratafacation of beat his lordship by more than two foot viz . , two foot nine inch I jump twenty - one foot three inch , by the drawer 's measured tape , and his lordship only eighteen six . I have win from he about my weight before   which I know the moment I set my eye upon he . so he and Mr. Jack pay I these two bett . and with my good duty to my mother she will not be displeased with I , for I bett for the honor of the Old Dominion , and my opponent be a nobleman of the first quality , himself hold two erldome , and heir to a Duke . Betting be all the rage here , and the blood and young fellow of fashion be bet away from morning till night . 
 " I tell they and that be my mischief perhaps that there be a gentleman at home who could beat I by a good foot ; and when they ask who it be , and I say Col . G. Washington , of Mount Vernon as you know he can , and he be the only man in his county or mine that can do it Mr. Wolfe ask I ever so many question about Col . G. W. , and show that he have hear of he , and talk over last year 's unhappy campane as if he know every inch of the ground , and he know the name of all our river , only he call the Potowmac Pottamac , at which we have a good laugh at he . my Lord of March and Ruglen be not in the least ill - humour about lose , and he and his friend hand I note out of their pocket - book , which fill mine that be get very empty , for the vale to the servant at my cousin Castlewood 's house and buy a horse at Oakhurst have very nearly put I on the necessity of make another draft upon my honoured mother or her London or Bristol agent . " 
 these feat of activity over , the four gentleman now stroll out of the tavern garden into the public walk , where , by this time , a great deal of company be assemble : upon whom Mr. Jack , who be of a frank and free nature , with a loud voice , choose to make remark that be not always agreeable . and here , if my Lord March make a joke , of which his lordship be not spar , Jack roar , " oh , ho , ho ! oh , good Gad ! oh , my dear earl ! oh , my dear lord , you will be the death of I ! " " it seem as if he wish everybody to know , " write Harry sagaciously to Mrs. Mountain , " that his friend and companion be an Erl ! " 
 there be , indeed , a great variety of character who pass . M. Poellnitz , no finer dress than he have be at dinner , grin , and salute with his great laced hat and tarnished feather . then come by my Lord Chesterfield , in a pearl - colour suit , with his blue ribbon and star , and salute the young man in his turn . 
 " I will back the old boy for take his hat off against the whole kingdom , and France either , " say my Lord March . " he have never change the shape of that hat of his for twenty year . look at it . there it go again ! do you see that great , big , awkward , pock - mark , snuff - colour man , who hardly touch his clumsy beaver in reply . d his confound impudence do you know who that be ? " 
 " no , curse he ! who be it , March ? " ask Jack , with an oath . 
 " it be one Johnson , a Dictionary - maker , about whom my Lord Chesterfield write some most capital paper , when his dixonary be come out , to patronise the fellow . I know they be capital . I have   hear Horry Walpole say so , and he know all about that kind of thing . confound the impudent schoolmaster ! " 
 " hang he , he ought to stand in the pillory ! " roar Jack . 
 " that fat man he be walk with be another of your writing fellow , a printer , his name be Richardson ; he write Clarissa , you know . " 
 " great heaven ! my lord , be that the great Richardson ? be that the man who write Clarissa ? " call out Colonel Wolfe and Mr. Warrington , in a breath . 
 Harry run forward to look at the old gentleman toddle along the walk with a train of admire lady surround he . 
 " indeed , my very dear sir , " one be say , " you be too great and good to live in such a world ; but sure you be send to teach it virtue ! " 
 " ah , my Miss Mulso ! who shall teach the teacher ? " say the good , fat old man , raise a kind , round face skyward . " even he have his fault and error ! even his age and experience do not prevent he from stumbl - . Heaven bless my soul , Mr. Johnson ! I ask your pardon if I have tread on your corn . " 
 " you have do both , sir . you have tread on the corn , and receive the pardon , " say Mr. Johnson , and go on mumble some verse , sway to and fro , his eye turn towards the ground , his hand behind he , and occasionally endanger with his great stick the honest , meek eye of his companion - author . 
 " they do not see very well , my dear mulso , " he say to the young lady , " but such as they be , I would keep my lash from Mr. Johnson 's cudgel . your servant , sir . " here he make a low bow , and take off his hat to Mr. Warrington , who shrink back with many blush , after salute the great author . the great author be accustom to be adore . a gentler wind never puff mortal vanity . enraptured spinster fling tea - leaf round he , and incense he with the coffee - pot . matron kiss the slipper they have work for he . there be a halo of virtue round his nightcap . all Europe have thrill , pant , admire , tremble , wept , over the page of the immortal little , kind , honest man with the round paunch . Harry come back quite glowing and proud at have a bow from he . " ah ! " say he , " my lord , I be glad to have see he ! " 
 " seen he ! why , dammy , you may see he any day in his shop , I suppose ? " say Jack , with a laugh . 
 " my brother declare that he , and Mr. Fielding , I think , be the name , be the great genius in England ; and often use to say , that when we come to Europe , his first pilgrimage would be to Mr. Richardson , " cry Harry , always impetuous , honest , and tender , when he speak of the dear friend . 
 " your brother speak like a man , " cry Mr. Wolfe , too , his pale face likewise flush up . " I would rather be a man of genius , than a peer of the realm . " 
 " every man to his taste , Colonel , " say my lord , much amuse . " your enthusiasm I do not   mean anything personal refresh I , on my honour it do . " 
 " so it do I by gad perfectly refresh I , " cry Jack 
 " so it do Jack you see it actually refresh Jack ! I say , Jack , which would you rather be ? a fat old printer , who have write a story about a confounded girl and a fellow that ruin she , or a peer of Parliament with ten thousand a year ? " 
 " March my Lord March , do you take I for a fool ? " say Jack , with a tearful voice . " have I do anything to deserve this language from you ? " 
 " I would rather win honour than honours : I would rather have genius than wealth . I would rather make my name than inherit it , though my father 's , thank God , be an honest one , " say the young Colonel . " but pardon I , gentleman , " and here make , they a hasty salutation , he run across the parade towards a young and elderly lady and a gentleman , who be now advance . 
 " it be the beautiful Miss Lowther . I remember now , " say my lord . " see ! he take her arm ! the report be , he be engage to she . " 
 " you do not   mean to say such a fellow be engage to any of the Lowthers of the North ? " cry out Jack . " curse I , what be the world come to , with your printer , and your half - pay ensign , and your schoolmaster , and your infernal nonsense ? " 
 the Dictionary - maker , who have show so little desire to bow to my Lord Chesterfield , when that famous nobleman courteously salute he , be here see to take off his beaver , and bow almost to the ground , before a florid personage in a large round hat , with band and a gown , who make his appearance in the Walk . this be my Lord Bishop of Salisbury , wear complacently the blue riband and badge of the Garter , of which Noble Order his lordship be prelate . 
 Mr. Johnson stand , hat in hand , during the whole time of his conversation with Dr. Gilbert ; who make many flattering and benedictory remark to Mr. Richardson , declare that he be the supporter of virtue , the preacher of sound moral , the mainstay of religion , of all which point the honest printer himself be perfectly convinced . 
 do not let any young lady trip to her grandpapa 's bookcase in consequence of this eulogium , and rashly take down Clarissa from the shelf . she would not care to read the volume , over which her pretty ancestress weep and thrill a hundred year ago ; which be commend by divine from pulpit and belaude all Europe over . I wonder , be our woman more virtuous than their grandmother , or only more squeamish ? if the former , then Miss Smith of New York be certainly more modest than Miss Smith of London , who still do not scruple to say that table , piano , and animal have leg . oh , my faithful , good old Samuel Richardson ! hath the news yet reach thee in Hades that thy sublime novel be huddle away in corner , and that our daughter may no more read Clarissa than Tom Jones ? go up , Samuel , and be reconcile with thy brother - scribe , whom in life thou didst hate so . I wonder whether a century hence the novel of to - day will be hide behind lock and wire , and make pretty little maiden blush ? 
 " who be yonder queer person in the high headdress of my grandmother 's time , who stop and speak to Mr. Richardson ? " ask Harry , as a fantastically dress lady come up , and perform a curtsey and a compliment to the bow printer . 
 Jack Morris nervously strike Harry a blow in the side with the butt end of his whip . Lord March laugh . 
 " yonder queer person be my gracious kinswoman , Katharine , Duchess of Dover and Queensberry , at your service , Mr. Warrington . she be a beauty price ! she be change now , be not   she ? what an old Gorgon it be ! she be a great patroness of your book - man and when that old frump be young , they actually make verse about she . " 
 the Earl quit his friend for a moment to make his bow to the old Duchess , Jack Morris explain to Mr. Warrington how , at the Duke 's death , my Lord of March and Ruglen would succeed to his cousin 's dukedom . 
 " I suppose , " say Harry , simply , " his lordship be here in attendance upon the old lady ? " 
 Jack burst into a loud laugh . 
 " oh yes ! very much ! exactly ! " say he . " why , my dear fellow , you do not   mean to say you have not   hear about the little opera - dancer ? " 
 " I be but lately arrive in England , Mr. Morris , " say Harry , with a smile , " and in Virginia , I own , we have not hear much about the little opera - dancer . " 
 luckily for we , the secret about the little opera - dancer never be reveal , for the young man 's conversation be interrupt by a lady in a cardinal cape , and a hat by no mean unlike those lovely headpiece which have return into vogue a hundred year after the date of our present history , who make a profound curtsey to the two gentleman and receive their salutation in return . she stop opposite to Harry ; she hold out her hand , rather to his wonderment : 
 " have you so soon forget I , Mr. Warrington ? " she say . 
 Off go Harry 's hat in an instant . he start , blush , stammer , and call out Good Heavens ! as if there have be any celestial wonder in the circumstance ! it be Lady Maria come out for a walk . he have not be think about she . she be , to say truth , for the moment so utterly out of the young gentleman 's mind , that she sudden re - entry there and appearance in the body startle Mr. Warrington 's faculty , and cause those guilty blush to crowd into his cheek . 
 no . he be not even think of she ! a week ago a year , a hundred year ago it seem he would not have be surprised to meet she anywhere . appear from amidst darkle shrubbery , glide over green garden terrace , loiter on stair or corridor , hover even in his dream , all day or all night , bodily or spiritually , he have be accustom to meet she . a week ago his heart use to beat . a week ago , and at the very instant when he jump out of his sleep , there be her idea smile on he . and it be only last Tuesday that his love be stab and slay , and he not only have leave off mourn for she , but have forget she ! 
 " you will come and walk with I a little ? " she say . " or would you like the music well ? I dare say you will like the music well . " 
 " you know , " say Harry , " I do not   care about any music much , except " he be think of the evening hymn " except of your playing . " he turn very red again as he speak , he feel he be perjure himself horribly . 
 the poor lady be agitate herself by the flutter and agitation which she see in her young companion . gracious Heaven ! could that tremor and excitement mean that she be mistaken , and that the lad be still faithful ? " give I your arm , and let we take a little walk , " she say , wave round a curtsey to the other two gentleman : " my aunt be asleep after her dinner . " Harry could not but offer the arm , and press the hand that lie against his heart . Maria make another fine curtsey to Harry 's bow companion , and walk off with her prize . in her grief , in her rage , in the pain and anguish of wrong and desertion , how a woman remember to smile , curtsey , caress , dissemble ! how resolutely they discharge the social propriety ; how they have a word , or a hand , or a kind little speech or reply for the pass acquaintance who cross unknowe the path of the tragedy , drop a light airy remark or two   happy self - satisfy rogue !   and pass on . he pass on , and think that woman be rather pleased with what I say . " that joke I make be rather neat . I do really think Lady Maria look rather favourably at I , and she be a dev'lish fine woman , begad she be ! " o you wiseacre ! such be Jack Morris 's observation and case as he walk away lean on the arm of his noble friend , and think the whole Society of the Wells be look at he . he have make some exquisite remark about a particular run of card at Lady Flushington 's the night before , and Lady Maria have reply graciously and neatly , and so away go Jack perfectly happy . 
 the absurd creature ! I declare we know nothing of anybody   but that for my part I know well and well every day . you enter smile to see your new acquaintance , Mrs. A. and her charming family . you make your bow in the elegant drawing - room of Mr. and Mrs. B. ? I tell you that in your course through life you be for ever put your great clumsy foot upon the mute invisible wound of bleed tragedy . Mrs. B. 's closet for what you know be stuff with skeleton . look there under the sofa - cushion . be that merely Missy 's doll , or be it the limb of a stifle Cupid peep out ? what do you suppose be those ash smoulder in the grate ? very likely a suttee have be offer up there just before you come in : a faithful heart have be burn out upon a callous corpse , and you be look on the cineri doloso . you see B. and his wife receive their company before dinner . gracious power ! do you know that that bouquet which she wear be a signal to Captain C. , and that he will find a note under the little bronze shakespeare on the mantelpiece in the study ? and with all this you go up and say some uncommonly neat thing   as you fancy   to Mrs. B. about the weather   clever dog ! , or about Lady E. 's last party   fashionable buck ! , or about the dear child in the nursery   insinuate rogue ! . Heaven and earth , my good sir , how can you tell that B. be not go to pitch all the child out of the nursery window this very night , or that his lady have not make an arrangement for leave they , and run off with the Captain ? how do you know that those footman be not disguised bailiff ? that yonder large - look butler   really a skeleton   be not the pawnbroker 's man ? and that there be not skeleton rotis and entree under every one of the cover ? look at their foot peep from under the tablecloth . mind how you stretch out your own lovely little slipper , madam , lest you knock over a rib or two . remark the death's - head moth flutter among the flower . see , the pale winding - sheet gleam in the wax - candle ! I know it be an old story , and especially that this preacher have yell vanita vanitatum five hundred time before . I can not   help always fall upon it , and cry out with particular loudness and wailing , and become especially melancholy , when I see a dead love tie to a live love . ha ! I look up from my desk , across the street : and there come in Mr. and Mrs. D. from their walk in Kensington Gardens . how she hang on he ! how jolly and happy he look , as the child frisk round ! my poor dear benighted Mrs. D. , there be a Regent 's Park as well as a Kensington Gardens in the world . go in , fond wretch ! smilingly lie before he what you know he like for dinner . show he the child 's copy and the report of their master . go with Missy to the piano , and play your artless duet together ; and fancy you be happy ! 
 there go Harry and Maria take their evening walk on the common , away from the village which be wake up from its after - dinner siesta , and where the people be begin to stir and the music to play . with the music Maria know Madame de Bernstein will waken : with the candle she must be back to the tea - table and the card . never mind . here be a minute . it may be my love be dead , but here be a minute to kneel over the grave and pray by it . he certainly be not think about she : he be startle and do not even know she . he be laugh and talk with Jack Morris and my Lord March . he be twenty year young than she . never mind . to - day be to - day in which we be all equal . this moment be ours . come , let we walk a little way over the heath , Harry . she will go , though she feel a deadly assurance that he will tell she all be over between they , and that he love the dark - haired girl at Oakhurst . 




 CHAPTER XXVII . Plenus Opus Aleae 

 " let I hear about those child , child , whom I see run about at the house where they take you in , poor dear boy , after your dreadful fall ? " say Maria , as they pace the common . " oh , that fall , Harry ! I think I should have die when I see it ! you need not   squeeze one 's arm so . you know you do not   care for I ? " 
 " the people be the very good , kind , dearest people I have ever meet in the world , " cry Mr. Warrington . " Mrs. Lambert be a friend of my mother when she be in Europe for her education . Colonel Lambert be a most accomplished gentleman , and have see service everywhere . he be in Scotland with his Royal Highness , in Flanders , at Minorca . no natural parent could be kind than they be to I . how can I show my gratitude to they ? I want to make they a present : I must make they a present , " say Harry , clap his hand into his pocket , which be fill with the crisp spoil of Morris and March . 
 " we can go to the toy - shop , my dear , and buy a couple of doll for the child , " say Lady Maria . " you would offend the parent by offer anything like payment for their kindness . " 
 " Dolls for Hester and Theo ! why , do you think a woman be not woman till she be forty , Maria ? "   the arm under Harry 's here give a wince perhaps , ever so slight a wince .   " I can tell you Miss Hester by no means consider herself a child , and Miss Theo be old than her sister . they know ever so many language . they have read book oh ! pile and pile of book ! they play on the harpsichord and sing together admirable ; and theo compose , and sing song of her own . " 
 " indeed ! I scarcely see they . I think they be child . they look quite childish . I have no idea they have all these perfection , and be such wonder of the world . " 
 " that be just the way with you woman ! at home , if I or George praise a woman , Mrs. Esmond , and Mountain , too , would be sure to find fault with she ! " cry Harry . 
 " I be sure I would find fault with no one who be kind to you , Mr. Warrington , " sigh Maria , " though you be not angry with I for envy they because they have to take care of you when you be wound and ill whilst I I have to leave you ? " 
 " you dear good Maria ! " 
 " no , Harry ! I be not dear and good . there , sir , you need not   be so press in your attention . look ! there be your black man walk with a score of other wretch in livery . the horrid creature be go to fuddle at the tea - garden , and get tipsy like their master . that dreadful Mr. Morris be perfectly tipsy when I come to you , and frighten you so . " 
 " I have just win great bet from both of they . what shall I buy for you , my dear cousin ? " and Harry narrate the triumph which he have just achieve . he be in high spirit : he laugh , he brag a little . " for the honour of Virginia I be determined to show they what jumping be , " he say . " with a little practice I think I could leap two foot far . " 
 Maria be pleased with the victory of her young champion . " but you must beware about play , child , " she say . " you know it hath be the ruin of our family . my brother Castlewood , will , our poor father , our aunt , Lady Castlewood herself , they have all be victim to it : as for my Lord March , he be the most dreadful gambler and the most successful of all the nobility . " 
 " I do not   intend to be afraid of he , nor of his friend Mr. Jack Morris neither , " say Harry , again finger the delightful note . " what do you play at Aunt Bernstein 's ? Cribbage , all - four , brag , whist , commerce , piquet , quadrille ? I be ready at any of them . what o'clock be that strike sure ' tis seven ! " 
 " and you want to begin now , " say the plaintive Maria . " you do not   care about walk with your poor cousin . not long ago you do . " 
 " hey ! youth be youth , cousin ! " cry Mr. Harry , toss up his head , " and a young fellow must have his fling ! " and he strut by his partner 's side , confident , happy , and eager for pleasure . not long ago he do like to walk with she . only yesterday , he like to be with Theo and hester , and good Mrs. Lambert ; but pleasure , life , gaiety , the desire to shine and to conquer , have also their temptation for the lad , who seize the cup like other lad , and do not care to calculate on the headache in store for the morning . whilst he and his cousin be talk , the fiddle from the open orchestra on the Parade make a great tuning and squeaking , preparatory to their usual evening concert . Maria know her aunt be awake again , and that she must go back to her slavery . Harry never ask about that slavery , though he must have know it , have he take the trouble to think . he never pity his cousin . he be not think about she at all . yet when his mishap befall he , she have be wound far more cruelly than he be . he have scarce ever be out of her thought , which of course she have have to bury under smile hypocrisy , as be the way with her sex . I know , my dear Mrs. Grundy , you think she be an old fool ? ah ! do you suppose fool ' cap do not cover grey hair , as well as jet or auburn ? bear gently with our elderly fredaine , o you Minerva of a woman ! or perhaps you be so good and wise that you do not   read novel at all . this I know , that there be late crop of wild oat , as well as early harvest of they ; and   from observation of self and neighbour   I have an idea that the avena fatua grow up to the very last day of the year . 
 like worldly parent anxious to get rid of a troublesome child , and go out to their evening party , Madame Bernstein and her attendant have put the sun to bed , whilst it be as yet light , and have draw the curtain over it , and be busy about their card and their candle , and their tea and negus , and other refreshment . one chair after another land lady at the Baroness 's door , more or less paint , patch , brocade . to these come gentleman in gala raiment . Mr. Poellnitz 's star be the large , and his coat the most embroider of all present . my Lord of March and Ruglen , when he make his appearance , be quite change from the individual with whom Harry have make acquaintance at the White Horse . his tight brown scratch be exchange for a neatly curl feather top , with a bag and grey powder , his jockey - dress and leather breech replace by a rich and elegant french suit . Mr. Jack Morris have just such another wig and a suit of stuff as closely as possible resemble his lordship 's . Mr. Wolfe come in attendance upon his beautiful mistress , Miss Lowther , and her aunt who love card , as all the world do . when my Lady Maria Esmond make her appearance , ' tis certain that her look belie Madame Bernstein 's account of she . her shape be very fine , and her dress show a great deal of it . her complexion be by nature exceed fair , and a dark frille ribbon , clasp by a jewel , round her neck , enhance its snowy whiteness . her cheek be not red than those of other lady present , and the rose be pretty openly purchase by everybody at the perfumery - shop . an artful patch or two , it be suppose , add to the lustre of her charm . her hoop be not large than the iron contrivance which lady of the present day hang round their person ; and we may pronounce that the costume , if absurd in some point , be pleasing altogether . suppose our lady take to wear of bangle and nose - ring ? I dare say we should laugh at the ornament , and not dislike they , and lover would make no difficulty about lift up the ring to be able to approach the rosy lip underneath . 
 as for the Baroness de Bernstein , when that lady take the pain of make a grand toilette , she appear as an object , handsome still , and magnificent , but melancholy , and even somewhat terrifying to behold . you read the past in some old face , while some other lapse into mere meekness and content . the fire go quite out of some eye , as the crow' - foot pucker round they ; they flash no long with scorn , or with anger , or love ; they gaze , and no one be melt by their sapphire glance ; they look , and no one be dazzle . my fair young reader , if you be not so perfect a beauty as the peerless Lindamira , queen of the ball ; if , at the end of it , as you retire to bed , you meekly own that you have have but two or three partner , whilst Lindamira have have a crowd round she all night console yourself with think that , at fifty , you will look as kind and pleasant as you appear now at eighteen . you will not have to lay down your coach - and - six of beauty and see another step into it , and walk yourself through the rest of life . you will have to forgo no long - accustomed homage ; you will not witness and own the depreciation of your smile . you will not see fashion forsake your quarter ; and remain all dust , gloom , cobwebs within your once splendid saloon , and placard in your sad window , gaunt , lonely , and to let ! you may not have know any grandeur , but you will not   feel any desertion . you will not have enjoy million , but you will have escape bankruptcy . " our hostess , " say my Lord Chesterfield to his friend in a confidential whisper , of which the utterer do not in the least know the loudness , " put I in mind of Covent Garden in my youth . then it be the court end of the town , and inhabit by the high fashion . now , a nobleman 's house be a gaming - house , or you may go in with a friend and call for a bottle . " 
 " hey ! a bottle and a tavern be good thing in their way , " say my Lord March , with a shrug of his shoulder . " I be not bear before the Georges come in , though I intend to live to a hundred . I never know the Bernstein but as an old woman ; and if she ever have beauty , hang I if I know how she spend it . " 
 " no , hang I , how do she spend it ? " laugh out Jack Morris . 
 " here be a table ! shall we sit down and have a game ? do not   let the Frenchman come in . he will not   pay . Mr. Warrington , will you take a card ? " Mr. Warrington and my Lord Chesterfield find themselves partner against Mr. Morris and the Earl of March . " you have come too late , Baron , " say the eld nobleman to the other nobleman who be advance . " we have make our game . what , have you forget Mr. Warrington of Virginia the young gentleman whom you meet in London ? " 
 " the young gentleman whom I meet at Arthur 's Chocolate House have black hair , a little cocked nose , and be by no mean so fortunate in his personal appearance as Mr. Warrington , " say the Baron , with much presence of mind . " Warrington , Dorrington , Harrington ? we of the continent can not retain your insular name . I certify that this gentleman be not the individual of whom I speak at dinner . " and , glance kindly upon he , the old beau sidle away to a farth end of the room , where Mr. Wolfe and Miss Lowther be engage in deep conversation in the embrasure of a window . here the Baron think fit to engage the Lieutenant - Colonel upon the prussian manual exercise , which have lately be introduce into King George II . 's army a subject with which Mr. Wolfe be thoroughly familiar , and which no doubt would have interest he at any other moment but that . nevertheless the old gentleman utter his criticism and opinion , and think he perfectly charm the two person to whom he communicate they . 
 at the commencement of the evening the Baroness receive her guest personally , and as they arrive engage they in talk and introductory courtesy . but as the room and table fill , and the party be make up , Madame de Bernstein become more and more restless , and finally retreat with three friend to her own corner , where a table specially reserve for she be occupy by her major - domo . and here the old lady sate down resolutely , never change her place or quit her game till cock - crow . the charge of receive the company devolve now upon my Lady Maria , who do not care for card , but dutifully do the honour of the house to her aunt 's guest , and often rustle by the table where her young cousin be engage with his three friend . 
 " come and cut the card for we , " say my Lord March to her ladyship as she pass on one of her wistful visit . " cut the card and bring we luck , Lady Maria ! we have have none to - night , and Mr. Warrington be win everything . " 
 " I hope you be not play high , Harry ? " say the lady , timidly . 
 " oh no , only sixpence , " cry my lord , deal . 
 " only sixpence , " echo Mr. Morris , who be Lord March 's partner . but Mr. Morris must have be very keenly alive to the value of sixpence , if the loss of a few such coin could make his round face look so dismal . my Lord Chesterfield sate opposite Mr. Warrington , sort his card . no one could say , by inspect that calm physiognomy , whether good or ill fortune be attend his lordship . 
 some word , not altogether indicative of delight , slip out of Mr. Morris 's lip , on which his partner cry out , " hang it , Morris , play your card , and hold your tongue ! " consider they be only play for sixpence , his lordship , too , be strangely affect . 
 Maria , still fondly linger by Harry 's chair , with her hand at the back of it , could see his card , and that a whole covey of trump be range in one corner . she have not take away his luck . she be pleased to think she have cut that pack which have deal he all those pretty trump . as Lord March be deal , he have say in a quiet voice to Mr. Warrington , " the bet as before , Mr. Warrington , or shall we double it ? " 
 " anything you like , my lord , " say Mr. Warrington , very quietly . 
 " we will say , then , shilling . " 
 " yes , shilling , " say Mr. Warrington , and the game proceed . 
 the end of the day 's , and some succeed day ' sport may be gather from the follow letter , which be never deliver to the person to whom it be address , but find its way to America in the paper of Mr. Henry Warrington : 
 " TUNBRIDGE WELLS , August 10 , 1756 . 
 " DEAR GEORGE as White 's two bottle of Burgundy and a pack of card constitute all the joy of your life , I take for grant that you be in London at this moment , prefer smoke and faro to fresh air and fresh haystack . this will be deliver to you by a young gentleman with whom I have lately make acquaintance , and whom you will be charm to know . he will play with you at any game for any stake , up to any hour of the night , and drink any reasonable number of bottle during the play . Mr. Warrington be no other than the Fortunate Youth about whom so many story have be tell in the Public Advertiser and other print . he have an estate in Virginia as big as Yorkshire , with the incumbrance of a mother , the reign sovereign ; but , as the country be unwholesome , and fever plentiful , let we hope that Mrs. Esmond will die soon , and leave this virtuous lad in undisturbed possession . she be aunt of that polisson of a Castlewood , who never pay his play - debt , unless he be more honourable in his dealing with you than he have be with I . Mr. W. be de bonne race . we must have he of our society , if it be only that I may win my money back from he . 
 " he have have the devil 's luck here , and have be win everything , whilst his old card - play beldam of an aunt have be lose . a few night ago , when I first have the ill - luck to make his acquaintance , he beat I in jump   have practise the art amongst the savage , and run away from bear in his native wood ; he win bet off I and Jack Morris about my weight ; and at night , when we sit down to play , at old Bernstein 's , he win from we all round . if you can settle our last Epsom account please hand over to Mr. Warrington 350 pound , which I still owe he , after pretty well empty my pocket - book . Chesterfield have drop six hundred to he , too ; but his lordship do not wish to have it know , have swear to give up play and live cleanly . Jack Morris , who have not be hit as hard as either of we , and can afford it quite as well , for the fat chuff have no house nor train to keep up , and all his misbegotten father 's money in hand , roar like a bull of Bashan about his loss . we have a second night 's play , en petit comite , and Barbeau serve we a fair dinner in a private room . Mr. Warrington hold his tongue like a gentleman , and none of we have talk about our loss ; but the whole place do , for we . yesterday the Cattarina look as sulky as thunder , because I would not give she a diamond necklace , and say I refuse she because I have lose five thousand to the Virginian . my old Duchess of Q. have the very same story , besides know to a fraction what Chesterfield and Jack have lose . 
 " Warrington treat the company to breakfast and music at the room ; and you should have see how the woman tear he to piece . that fiend of a Cattarina ogle he out of my vis - a - vis , and under my very nose , yesterday , as we be drive to Penshurst , and I have no doubt have send he a billet - doux ere this . he shoot Jack Morris all to piece at a mark : we shall try he with partridge when the season come . 
 " he be a fortunate fellow , certainly . he have youth   which be not deboshe by evil course in Virginia , as ours be in England ; he have good health , good look , and good luck . 
 " in a word , Mr. Warrington have win our money in a very gentlemanlike manner ; and , as I like he , and wish to win some of it back again , I put he under your worship 's saintly guardianship . adieu ! I be go to the North , and shall be back for Doncaster . 
 " your ever , dear George , 
 " M. et R. " 
 " to George Augustus Selwyn , Esq . , at White 's Chocolate House , St. James 's Street . " 




 CHAPTER XXVIII . the way of the World 

 our young Virginian find himself , after two or three day at Tunbridge Wells , by far the most important personage in that merry little watering - place . no nobleman in the place inspire so much curiosity . my Lord Bishop of Salisbury himself be scarce treat with more respect . People turn round to look after Harry as he pass , and country - folk star at he as they come into market . at the room , matron encourage he to come round to they , and find mean to leave he alone with their daughter , most of whom smile upon he . everybody know , to an acre and a shilling , the extent of his virginian property , and the amount of his income . at every tea - table in the Wells , his winning at play be tell and calculate . wonderful be the knowledge which our neighbour have of our affair ! so great be the interest and curiosity which Harry inspire , that people even smile upon his servant , and take Gumbo aside and treat he with ale and cold meat , in order to get news of the young Virginian . Mr. Gumbo fatten under the diet , become a lead member of the Society of Valets in the place , and lie more enormously than ever . no party be complete unless Mr. Warrington attend it . the lad be not a little amused and astonish by this prosperity , and bear his new honour pretty well . he have be breed at home to think too well of himself , and his present good fortune no doubt tend to confirm his self - satisfaction . but he be not too much elated . he do not brag about his victory or give himself any particular air . in engage in play with the gentleman who challenge he , he have act up to his queer code of honour . he feel as if he be bind to meet they when they summon he , and that if they invite he to a horse - race , or a drinking - bout , or a match at card , for the sake of Old Virginia he must not draw back . Mr. Harry find his new acquaintance ready to try he at all these sport and contest . he have a strong head , a skilful hand , a firm seat , an unflinche nerve . the representative of Old Virginia come off very well in his friendly rivalry with the mother - country .
