 Madame de Bernstein , who get her fill of card every night , and , no doubt , repair the ill - fortune of which we hear in the last chapter , be delighted with her nephew 's victory and reputation . he have shoot with Jack Morris and beat he ; he have ride a match with Mr. Scamper and win it . he play tennis with Captain Batts , and , though the boy have never try the game before , in a few day he hold his own uncommonly well . he have engage in play with those celebrate gamester , my Lords of Chesterfield and March ; and they both bear testimony to his coolness , gallantry , and good breeding . at his book Harry be not brilliant certainly ; but he could write as well as a great number of man of fashion ; and the naivete of his ignorance amuse the old lady . she have read book in her time , and could talk very well about they with bookish people : she have a relish for humour and delight in Moliere and Mr. Fielding , but she love the world far well than the library , and be never so interested in any novel but that she would leave it for a game of card . she superintend with fond pleasure the improvement of Harry 's toilette : rummage out fine lace for his ruffle and shirt , and find a pretty diamond - brooch for his frill . he attain the post of prime favourite of all her nephew and kinsfolk . I fear Lady Maria be only too well pleased at the lad 's success , and do not grudge he his superiority over her brother ; but those gentleman must have quake with fear and envy when they hear of Mr. Warrington 's prodigious success , and the advance which he have make in their wealthy aunt 's favour . 
 after a fortnight of Tunbridge , Mr. Harry have become quite a personage . he know all the good company in the place . be it his fault if he become acquainted with the bad likewise ? be he very wrong in take the world as he find it , and drink from that sweet sparkle pleasure - cup , which be fill for he to the brim ? the old aunt enjoy his triumph , and for her part only bid he pursue his enjoyment . she be not a rigorous old moralist , nor , perhaps , a very wholesome preceptress for youth . if the Cattarina write he billet - doux , I fear Aunt Bernstein would have bid he accept the invitation : but the lad have bring with he from his colonial home a stock of modesty which he still wear along with the honest homespun linen . Libertinism be rare in those thinly - people region from which he come . the vice of great city be scarce know or practise in the rough town of the american continent . Harry Warrington blush like a girl at the daring talk of his new european associate : even Aunt Bernstein 's conversation and joke astound the young Virginian , so that the worldly old woman would call he Joseph , or simpleton . 
 but , however innocent he be , the world give he credit for be as bad as other folk . how be he to know that he be not to associate with that saucy Cattarina ? he have see my Lord March drive she about in his lordship 's phaeton . Harry think there be no harm in give she his arm , and parade openly with she in the public walk . she take a fancy to a trinket at the toy - shop ; and , as his pocket be full of money , he be delighted to make she a present of the locket , which she covet . the next day it be a piece of lace : again Harry gratify she . the next day it be something else : there be no end to Madame Cattarina 's fancy : but here the young gentleman stop , turn off her request with a joke and a laugh . he be shrewd enough , and not reckless or prodigal , though generous . he have no idea of purchase diamond drop for the petulant little lady 's pretty ear . 
 but who be to give he credit for his Modesty ? Old Bernstein insist upon believe that her nephew be play Don Juan 's part , and supplant my Lord March . she insist the more when poor Maria be by ; love to stab the tender heart of that spinster , and enjoy her niece 's piteous silence and discomfiture . 
 " why , my dear , " say the Baroness , " boy will be boy , and I do not   want Harry to be the first milksop in his family ! " the bread which Maria eat at her aunt 's expense choke she sometimes . o I , how hard and indigestible some woman know how to make it ! 
 Mr. Wolfe be for ever come over from Westerham to pay court to the lady of his love ; and , know that the Colonel be entirely engage in that pursuit , Mr. Warrington scarcely expect to see much of he , however much he like that officer 's conversation and society . it be different from the talk of the ribald people round about Harry . Mr. Wolfe never speak of card , or horse ' pedigree ; or brag of his performance in the hunting - field ; or boast of the favour of woman ; or retail any of the innumerable scandal of the time . it be not a good time . that old world be more dissolute than ours . there be an old king with mistress openly in his train , to whom the great folk of the land do honour . there be a nobility , many of whom be mad and reckless in the pursuit of pleasure ; there be a looseness of word and act which we must note , as faithful historian , without go into particular , and needlessly shocking honest reader . our young gentleman have light upon some of the wild of these wild people , and have find an old relative who live in the very midst of the rout . 
 Harry then do not remark how Colonel Wolfe avoid he , or when they casually meet , at first , notice the Colonel 's cold and alter demeanour . he do not know the story that be tell of he . who do know the story that be tell of he ? who make they ? who be the father of those wondrous lie ? Poor Harry do not know the reputation he be get ; and that , whilst he be rid his horse and play his game and take his frolic , he be pass amongst many respectable person for be the most abandon and profligate and godless of young man . 
 Alas , and alas ! to think that the lad whom we like so , and who be so gentle and quiet when with we , so simple and so easily pleased , should be a harden profligate , a spendthrift , a confirm gamester , a frequenter of abandon woman ! these story come to honest Colonel Lambert at Oakhurst : first one bad story , then another , then crowd of they , till the good man 's kind heart be quite fill with grief and care , so that his family see that something annoy he . at first he would not speak on the matter at all , and put aside the wife 's fond query . Mrs. Lambert think a great misfortune have happen ; that her husband have be ruin ; that he have be order on a dangerous service ; that one of the boy be ill , disgrace , dead ; who can resist an anxious woman , or escape the cross - examination of the conjugal pillow ? Lambert be oblige to tell a part of what he know about Harry Warrington . the wife be as much grieve and amazed as her husband have be . from papa 's and mamma 's bedroom the grief , after be stifle for a while under the bed - pillow there , come downstairs . theo and Hester take the complaint after their parent , and have it very bad . o kind , little , wound heart ! at first Hester turn red , fly into a great passion , clench her little fist , and vow she would not believe a word of the wicked story ; but she end by believe they . scandal almost always do master people ; especially good and innocent people . oh , the serpent they have nurse by their fire ! oh , the wretched , wretched boy ! to think of his walk about with that horrible paint Frenchwoman , and give her diamond necklace , and parade his shame before all the society at the Wells ! the three lady have cry over the story , and the father be deeply move by it , take the parson into their confidence . in vain he preach at church next Sunday his favourite sermon about scandal , and inveigh against our propensity to think evil . we repent we promise to do so no more ; but when the next bad story come about our neighbour we believe it . so do those kind , wretche Oakhurst folk believe what they hear about poor Harry Warrington . 
 Harry Warrington meanwhile be a great deal too well pleased with himself to know how ill his friend be think of he , and be pursue a very idle and pleasant , if unprofitable , life , without have the least notion of the hubbub he be create , and the dreadful repute in which he be hold by many good man . come out from a match at tennis with Mr. Batts , and please with his play and all the world , Harry overtake Colonel Wolfe , who have be on one of his visit to the lady of his heart . Harry hold out his hand , which the Colonel take , but the latter 's salutation be so cold , that the young man could not help remark it , and especially note how Mr. Wolfe , in return for a fine bow from Mr. Batts 's hat , scarcely touch his own with his forefinger . the tennis Captain walk away look somewhat disconcert , Harry remain behind to talk with his friend of Westerham . Mr. Wolfe walk by he for a while , very erect , silent , and cold . 
 " I have not see you these many day , " say Harry . 
 " you have have other companion , " remark Mr. Wolfe , curtly . 
 " but I have rather be with you than any of they , " cry the young man . 
 " indeed I might be well company for you than some of they , " say the other . 
 " be it Captain Batts you mean ? " ask Harry . 
 " he be no favourite of mine , I own ; he bear a rascally reputation when he be in the army , and I doubt have not mend it since he be turn out . you certainly might find a well friend than Captain Batts . pardon the freedom which I take in say so , " say Mr. Wolfe , grimly . 
 " Friend ! he be no friend : he only teach I to play tennis : he be hand - in - glove with my lord , and all the people of fashion here who play . " 
 " I be not a man of fashion , " say Mr. Wolfe . 
 " my dear Colonel , what be the matter ? have I anger you in any way ? you speak almost as if I have , and I be not conscious of have do anything to forfeit your regard , " say Mr. Warrington . 
 " I will be free with you , Mr. Warrington , " say the Colonel , gravely , " and tell you with frankness that I do not   like some of your friend ! " 
 " why , sure , they be man of the first rank and fashion in England , " cry Harry , not choose to be offend with his companion 's bluntness . 
 " exactly , they be man of too high rank and too great fashion for a hard - work poor soldier like I ; and if you continue to live with such , believe I , you will find number of we humdrum people can not   afford to keep such company . I be here , Mr. Warrington , pay my address to an honourable lady . I meet you yesterday openly walk with a french ballet - dancer , and you take off your hat . I must frankly tell you , that I have rather you would not take off your hat when you go out in such company . " 
 " Sir , " say Mr. Warrington , grow very red , " do you mean that I be to forgo the honour of Colonel Wolfe 's acquaintance altogether ? " 
 " I certainly shall request you to do so when you be in company with that person , " say Colonel Wolfe , angrily ; but he use a word not to be write at present , though Shakespeare put it in the mouth of Othello . 
 " great heaven ! what a shame it be to speak so of any woman ! " cry Mr. Warrington . " how dare any man say that that poor creature be not honest ? " 
 " you ought to know good , sir , " say the other , look at Harry with some surprise , " or the world belie you very much . " 
 " what ought I to know well ? I see a poor little french dancer who be come hither with her mother , and be order by the doctor to drink the water . I know that a person of my rank in life do not ordinarily keep company with people of her ; but really , Colonel Wolfe , be you so squeamish ? have I not hear you say that you do not value birth , and that all honest people ought to be equal ? why should I not give this little unprotected woman my arm ? there be scarce half a dozen people here who can speak a word of her language . I can talk a little French , and she be welcome to it ; and if Colonel Wolfe do not choose to touch his hat to I , when I be walk with she , by George he may leave it alone , " cry Harry , flush up . 
 " you do not   mean to say , " say Mr. Wolfe , eye he , " that you do not   know the woman 's character ? " 
 " of course , sir , she be a dancer , and , I suppose , no well or bad than her neighbour . but I mean to say that , have she be a duchess , or your grandmother , I could not   have respect she more . " 
 " you do not   mean to say that you do not win she at dice , from Lord March ? " 
 " at what ? " 
 " at dice , from Lord March . everybody know the story . not a person at the Wells be ignorant of it . I hear it but now , in the company of that good old Mr. Richardson , and the lady be say that you would be a character for a colonial Lovelace . " 
 " what on earth else have they say about I ? " ask Harry Warrington ; and such story as he know the Colonel tell . the most alarming account of his own wickedness and profligacy be lay before he . he be a corrupter of virtue , an habitual drunkard and gamester , a notorious blasphemer and freethinker , a fitting companion for my Lord March , finally , and the company into whose society he have fall . " I tell you these thing , " say Mr. Wolfe , " because it be fair that you should know what be say of you , and because I do heartily believe , from your manner of meet the last charge bring against you , that you be innocent of most of the other count . I feel , Mr. Warrington , that I , for one , have be do you a wrong ; and sincerely ask you to pardon I . " 
 of course , Harry be eager to accept his friend 's apology , and they shake hand with sincere cordiality this time . in respect of most of the charge bring against he , Harry rebut they easily enough : as for the play , he own to it . he think that a gentleman should not refuse a fair challenge from other gentleman , if his mean allow he : and he never would play beyond his mean . after win considerably at first , he could afford to play large stake , for he be play with other people 's money . play , he think , be fair , it certainly be pleasant . why , do not all England , except the Methodists , play ? have he not see the good company at the Wells over the card his aunt amongst they ? 
 Mr. Wolfe make no immediate comment upon Harry 's opinion as to the person who form the good company at the Wells , but he frankly talk with the young man , whose own frankness have win he , and warn he that the life he be lead might be the pleasant , but surely be not the most profitable of life . " it can not   be , sir , " say the Colonel , " that a man be to pass his day at horse - racing and tennis , and his night carouse or at card . sure , every man be make to do some work : and a gentleman , if he have none , must make some . do you know the law of your country , Mr. Warrington ? be a great proprietor , you will doubtless one day be a magistrate at home . have you travel over the country , and make yourself acquainted with its trade and manufacture ? these be fit thing for a gentleman to study , and may occupy he as well as a cock - fight or a cricket - match . do you know anything of our profession ? that , at least , you will allow , be a noble one ; and , believe I , there be plenty in it to learn , and suit , I should think , to you . I speak of it rather than of book and the learn profession , because , as far as I can judge , your genius do not lie that way . but honour be the aim of life , " cry Mr. Wolfe , " and every man can serve his country one way or the other . be sure , sir , that idle bread be the most dangerous of all that be eat ; that card and pleasure may be take by way of pastime after work , but not instead of work , and all day . and do you know , Mr. Warrington , instead of be the Fortunate Youth , as all the world call you , I think you be rather Warrington the Unlucky , for you be follow by daily idleness , daily flattery , daily temptation , and the Lord , I say , send you a good , deliverance out of your good fortune . " 
 but Harry do not like to tell his aunt that afternoon why it be he look so grave . he think he would not drink , but there be some jolly fellow at the ordinary who pass the bottle round ; and he mean not to play in the evening , but a fourth be want at his aunt 's table , and how could he resist ? he be the old lady 's partner several time during the night , and he have Somebody 's own luck to be sure ; and once more he see the dawn , and feast on chicken and champagne at sunrise . 




 CHAPTER XXIX . in which Harry continue to enjoy Otium sine Dignitate 

 Whilst there be card - player enough to meet she at her lodging and the assembly - room , Madame de Bernstein remain pretty contentedly at the Wells , scold her niece , and play her rubber . at Harry 's age almost all place be pleasant , where you can have lively company , fresh air , and your share of sport and diversion . even all pleasure be pleasant at twenty . we go out to meet it with alacrity , speculate upon its coming , and when its visit be announce , count the day until it and we shall come together . how very gently and coolly we regard it towards the close of Life 's long season ! Madam , do not   you recollect your first ball ; and do not your memory stray towards that happy past , sometimes , as you sit ornament the wall whilst your daughter be dance ? I , for my part , can remember when I think it be delightful to walk three mile and back in the country to dine with old Captain Jones . Fancy like to walk three mile , now , to dine with Jones and drink his half - pay port ! no doubt it be buy from the little country - town wine - merchant , and cost but a small sum ; but ' twa offer with a kindly welcome , and youth give it a flavour which no age of wine or man can impart to it nowadays . Viximus nuper . I be not disposed to look so severely upon young Harry 's conduct and idleness , as his friend the stern Colonel of the Twentieth Regiment . o bless idleness ! divine lazy nymph ! reach I a novel as I lie in my dressing - gown at three o'clock in the afternoon ; compound a sherry - cobbler for I , and bring I a cigar ! dear slatternly , smile enchantress ! they may assail thee with bad name swear thy character away , and call thee the Mother of Evil ; but , for all that , thou art the good company in the world ! 
 my Lord of March go away to the North ; and my Lord Chesterfield , find the Tunbridge water do no good to his deafness , return to his solitude at Blackheath ; but other gentleman remain to sport and take their pleasure , and Mr. Warrington have quite enough of companion at his ordinary at the White Horse . he soon learn to order a french dinner as well as the good man of fashion out of St. James 's ; could talk to Monsieur Barbeau , in Monsieur B. 's native language , much more fluently than most other folk , discover a very elegant and decide taste in wine , and could distinguish between Clos Vougeot and Romande with remarkable skill . he be the young king of the Wells , of which the general frequenter be easygoing man of the world , who be by no mean shock at that reputation for gallantry and extravagance which Harry have get , and which have so frighten Mr. Wolfe . 
 though our Virginian live amongst the reveller , and swam and sport in the same water with the loose fish , the boy have a natural shrewdness and honesty which keep he clear of the snare and bait which be commonly set for the unwary . he make very few foolish bet with the jolly idle fellow round about he , and the old hand find it difficult to take he in . he engage in game outdoors and in , because he have a natural skill and aptitude for they , and be good to hold almost any match with any fair competitor . he be scrupulous to play only with those gentleman whom he know , and always to settle his own debt on the spot . he would have make but a very poor figure at a college examination ; though he possess prudence and fidelity , keen , shrewd perception , great generosity , and dauntless personal courage . 
 and he be not without occasion for showing of what stuff he be make . for instance , when that unhappy little Cattarina , who have bring he into so much trouble , carry her importunity beyond the mark at which Harry think his generosity should stop , he withdraw from the advance of the Opera - House Siren with perfect coolness and skill , leave she to exercise her blandishment upon some more easy victim . in vain the mermaid 's hysterical mother wait upon Harry , and vow that a cruel bailiff have seize all her daughter 's good for debt , and that her venerable father be at present languishing in a London gaol . Harry declare that between himself and the bailiff there could be no dealing , and that because he have have the good fortune to become known to Mademoiselle Cattarina , and to gratify her caprice by present she with various trinket and knick - knack for which she have a fancy , he be not bind to pay the past debt of her family , and must decline be bail for her papa in London , or settle her outstanding account at Tunbridge . the Cattarina 's mother first call he a monster and an ingrate , and then ask he , with a veteran smirk , why he do not take pay for the service he have render to the young person ? at first , Mr. Warrington could not understand what the nature of the payment might be : but when that matter be explain by the old woman , the honest lad rise up in horror , to think that a woman should traffic in her child 's dishonour , tell she that he come from a country where the very savage would recoil from such a bargain ; and , have bow the old lady ceremoniously to the door , order Gumbo to mark she well , and never admit she to his lodging again . no doubt she retire breathing vengeance against the Iroquois : no Turk or Persian , she declare , would treat a lady so : and she and her daughter retreat to London as soon as their anxious landlord would let they . then Harry have his peril of gaming , as well as his peril of gallantry . a man who play at bowl , as the phrase be , must expect to meet with rubber . after dinner at the ordinary , have decline to play piquet any far with Captain Batts , and be roughly ask his reason for refuse , Harry fairly tell the Captain that he only play with gentleman who pay , like himself : but express himself so ready to satisfy Mr. Batts , as soon as their outstanding little account be settle , that the Captain declare himself satisfied d'avance , and straightway leave the Wells without pay Harry or any other creditor . also he have an occasion to show his spirit by beat a chairman who be rude to old Miss Whiffler one evening as she be go to the assembly : and find that the calumny regard himself and that unlucky opera - dancer be repeat by Mr. Hector Buckler , one of the fierce frequenter of the Wells , Mr. Warrington step up to Mr. Buckler in the pump - room , where the latter be regale a number of water - drinker with the very calumny , and publicly inform Mr. Buckler that the story be a falsehood , and that he should hold any person accountable to himself who henceforth utter it . so that though our friend , be at Rome , certainly do as Rome do , yet he show himself to be a valorous and worthy Roman ; and , hurlant avec les loup , be acknowledge by Mr. Wolfe himself to be as brave as the good of the wolf . 
 if that officer have tell Colonel Lambert the story which have give the latter so much pain , we may be sure that when Mr. Wolfe find his young friend be innocent , he take the first opportunity to withdraw the odious charge against he . and there be joy among the lambert , in consequence of the lad 's acquittal something , doubtless , of that pleasure , which be feel by high nature than our , at the recovery of sinner . never have the little family be so happy no , not even when they get the news of Brother Tom win his scholarship as when Colonel Wolfe ride over with the account of the conversation which he have with Harry Warrington . " hadst thou bring I a regiment , James , I think I should not have be well pleased , " say Mr. Lambert . Mrs. Lambert call to her daughter who be in the garden , and kiss they both when they come in , and cry out the good news to they . Hetty jump for joy , and Theo perform some uncommonly brilliant operation upon the harpsichord that night ; and when Dr. Boyle come in for his backgammon , he could not , at first , account for the illumination in all their face , until the three lady , in a happy chorus , tell he how right he have be in his sermon , and how dreadfully they have wrong that poor dear , good young Mr. Warrington . 
 " what shall we do , my dear ? " say the Colonel to his wife . " the hay be in , the corn will not   be cut for a fortnight , the horse have nothing to do . suppose we ... " and here he lean over the table and whisper in her ear . 
 " my dear Martin ! the very thing ! " cry Mrs. Lambert , take her husband 's hand and press it . 
 " what be the very thing , mother ? " cry young Charley , who be home for his Bartlemytide holiday . 
 " the very thing be to go to supper . Come , Doctor ! we will have a bottle of wine to - night , and drink repentance to all who think evil . " 
 " amen , " say the Doctor ; " with all my heart ! " and with this the worthy family go to their supper . 




 CHAPTER XXX . contain a Letter to Virginia 

 have repair one day to his accustomed dinner at the White Horse ordinary , Mr. Warrington be pleased to see amongst the face round the table the jolly , good - look countenance of Parson Sampson , who be regale the company when Harry enter , with story and bon - mot , which keep they in roar of laughter . though he have not be in London for some month , the parson have the late London news , or what pass for such with the folk at the ordinary : what be do in the King 's house at Kensington ; and what in the Duke 's in Pall Mall : how Mr. Byng be behave in prison , and who come to he : what be the odd at Newmarket , and who be the last reign toast in Covent Garden ; the jolly chaplain could give the company news upon all these point , news that might not be very accurate indeed , but be as good as if it be for the country gentleman who hear it . for suppose that my Lord Viscount Squanderfield be ruin himself for Mrs. Polly , and Sampson call she Mrs. Lucy ? that it be Lady Jane who be in love with the actor , and not Lady Mary ? that it be Harry Hilton , of the Horse Grenadiers , who have the quarrel with Chevalier Solingen , at Marybone Garden , and not Tommy Ruffler , of the Foot Guards ? the name and date do not matter much . provide the story be lively and wicked , their correctness be of no great importance ; and Mr. Sampson laugh and chatter away amongst his country gentleman , charm they with his spirit and talk , and drink his share of one bottle after another , for which his delighted auditory persist in call . a hundred year ago , the Abbe Parson , the clergyman who frequent the theatre , the tavern , the racecourse , the world of fashion , be no uncommon character in english society : his voice might be hear the loud in the hunting - field ; he could sing the jolly song at the Rose or the Bedford Head , after the play be over at Covent Garden , and could call a main as well as any at the gaming - table . 
 it may have be modesty , or it may have be claret , which cause his reverence 's rosy face to redden deeply , but when he see Mr. Warrington enter , he whisper " Maxima debetur " to the laugh country squire who sit next he in his drab coat and gold - lace red waistcoat , and rise up from his chair and run , nay , stumble forward , in his haste to greet the Virginian : " my dear sir , my very dear sir , my conqueror of spade , and club , and heart , too , I be delighted to see your honour look so fresh and well , " cry the chaplain . 
 Harry return the clergyman 's greeting with great pleasure : he be glad to see Mr. Sampson ; he could also justly compliment his reverence upon his cheerful look and rosy gill . 
 the squire in the drab coat know Mr. Warrington ; he make a place beside himself ; he call out to the parson to return to his seat on the other side , and to continue his story about Lord Ogle and the grocer 's wife in . where he do not say , for his sentence be interrupt by a shout and an oath address to the parson for treading on his gouty toe . 
 the chaplain ask pardon , hurriedly turn round to Mr. Warrington , and inform he , and the rest of the company indeed , that my Lord Castlewood send his affectionate remembrance to his cousin , and have give special order to he   Mr. Sampson   to come to Tunbridge Wells and look after the young gentleman 's moral ; that my Lady Viscountess and my Lady Fanny be go to Harrogate for the water ; that Mr. Will have win his money at Newmarket , and be go on a visit to my Lord Duke ; that molly the housemaid be cry her eye out about Gumbo , Mr. Warrington 's valet ; in fine , all the news of Castlewood and its neighbourhood . Mr. Warrington be belove by all the country round , Mr. Sampson tell the company , manage to introduce the name of some person of the very high rank into his discourse . " all Hampshire have hear of his success at Tunbridge , success of every kind , " say Mr. Sampson , look particularly arch ; my lord hop , their ladyship hop , Harry would not be spoil for his quiet Hampshire home . 
 the guest drop off one by one , leave the young Virginian to his bottle of wine and the chaplain . 
 " though I have have plenty , " say the jolly chaplain , " that be no reason why I should not have plenty more , " and he drink toast after toast , and bumper after bumper , to the amusement of Harry , who always enjoy his society . 
 by the time when Sampson have have his " plenty more , " Harry , too , be become specially generous , warm - hearted , and friendly . a lodge why should Mr. Sampson go to the expense of an inn , when there be a room at Harry 's quarter ? the chaplain 's trunk be order thither , Gumbo be bid to make Mr. Sampson comfortable most comfortable ; nothing would satisfy Mr. Warrington but that Sampson should go down to his stable and see his horse ; he have several horse now ; and when at the stable Sampson recognise his own horse which Harry have win from he ; and the fond beast whinny with pleasure , and rub his nose against his old master 's coat ; Harry rap out a brisk energetic expression or two , and vow by Jupiter that Sampson should have his old horse back again : he would give he to Sampson , that he would ; a gift which the chaplain accept by seize Harry 's hand , and bless he , by fling his arm round the horse 's neck , and weep for joy there , weep tear of bordeaux and gratitude . arm - in - arm the friend walk to Madame Bernstein 's from the stable , of which they bring the odour into her ladyship 's apartment . their flushed cheek and brighten eye show what their amusement have be . many gentleman 's cheek be in the habit of flush in those day , and from the same cause . 
 Madame Bernstein receive her nephew 's chaplain kindly enough . the old lady relish Sampson 's broad joke and rattle talk from time to time , as she like a highly - spice dish or a new entree compose by her cook , upon its two or three first appearance . the only amusement of which she do not grow tired , she own , be card . " the card do not   cheat , " she use to say . " a bad hand tell you the truth to your face : and there be nothing so flattering in the world as a good suite of trump . " and when she be in a good humour , and sit down to her favourite pastime , she would laughingly bid her nephew 's chaplain say grace before the meal . Honest Sampson do not at first care to take a hand at Tunbridge Wells . her ladyship 's play be too high for he , he would own , slap his pocket with a comical piteous look , and its content have already be hand over to the fortunate youth at Castlewood . like most person of her age , and indeed her sex , Madame Bernstein be not prodigal of money . I suppose it must have be from Harry Warrington , whose heart be overflow with generosity as his purse with guineas , that the chaplain procure a small stock of ready coin , with which he be presently enable to appear at the card - table . 
 our young gentleman welcome Mr. Sampson to his coin , as to all the rest of the good thing which he have gather about he . ' twa surprising how quickly the young Virginian adapt himself to the habit of life of the folk amongst whom he live . his suit be still black , but of the fine cut and quality . " with a star and ribbon , and his stocking down , and his hair over his shoulder , he would make a pretty Hamlet , " say the gay old Duchess Queensberry . " and I make no doubt he have be the death of a dozen Ophelias already , here and amongst the Indians , " she add , think not at all the bad of Harry for his suppose success among the fair . Harry 's lace and linen be as fine as his aunt could desire . he purchase fine shaving - plate of the toy - shop woman , and a couple of magnificent brocade bedgown , in which his worship loll at ease , and sip his chocolate of a morning . he have sword and walking - cane , and french watch with paint back and diamond setting , and snuff box enamel by artist of the same cunning nation . he have a levee of groom , jockey , tradesman , daily wait in his anteroom , and admit one by one to he and Parson Sampson , over his chocolate , by Gumbo , the groom of the chamber . we have no account of the number of man whom Mr. Gumbo now have under he . certain it be that no single negro could have take care of all the fine thing which Mr. Warrington now possess , let alone the horse and the postchaise which his honour have buy . also Harry instruct himself in the art which become a gentleman in those day . a french fencing - master , and a dancing - master of the same nation , reside at Tunbridge during that season when Harry make his appearance : these man of science the young Virginian sedulously frequent , and acquire considerable skill and grace in the peaceful and warlike accomplishment which they teach . Ere many week be over he could handle the foil against his master or any frequenter of the fencing - school , and , with a sigh , Lady Maria   who dance very elegantly herself   own that there be no gentleman at court who could walk a minuet more gracefully than Mr. Warrington . as for riding , though Mr. Warrington take a few lesson on the great horse from a riding - master who come to Tunbridge , he declare that their own virginian manner be well enough for he , and that he see no one amongst the fine folk and the jockey who could ride well than his friend Colonel George Washington of Mount Vernon . 
 the obsequious Sampson find himself in well quarter than he have enjoy for ever so long a time . he know a great deal of the world , and tell a great deal more , and Harry be delighted with his story , real or fancy . the man of twenty look up to the man of thirty , admire the latter 's old joke , stale pun , and tarnished anecdote , that be slop with the wine of a hundred dinner - table . Sampson 's town and college pleasantry be all new and charming to the young Virginian . a hundred year ago , no doubt there be no such people leave in the world now , there use to be grow man in London who love to consort with fashionable youth enter life ; to tickle their young fancy with merry story ; to act as Covent Garden Mentors and master of ceremony at the Round - house ; to accompany lad to the gaming - table , and perhaps have an understanding with the punter ; to drink lemonade to Master Hopeful 's Burgundy , and to stagger into the street with perfectly cool head when my young lord reel out to beat the watch . of this , no doubt , extinct race , Mr. Sampson be a speciman : and a great comfort it be to think   to those who choose to believe the statement   that in Queen Victoria 's reign there be no flatterer leave , such as exist in the reign of her royal great - grandfather , no parasite pander to the folly of young man ; in fact , that all the toad have be eat off the face of the island   except one or two that be find in stone , where they have lie perdus these hundred year , and the toad - eater have perish for lack of nourishment . 
 with some sauce , as I read , the above - mention animal be say to be exceedingly fragrant , wholesome , and savoury eating . indeed , no man could look more rosy and healthy , or flourish more cheerfully , than friend Sampson upon the diet . he become our young friend 's confidential leader , and , from the follow letter , which be preserve in the Warrington correspondence , it will be see that Mr. Harry not only have dance and fence master , but likewise a tutor , chaplain , and secretary : 
 to MRS . esmond WARRINGTON of castlewood at her HOUSE at RICHMOND , VIRGINIA 
 Mrs. Bligh 's Lodgings , Pantiles , Tunbridge Wells , 
 " August 25th , 1756 . 
 " HONOURED MADAM your honoured letter of 20 June , per Mr. Trail of Bristol , have be forward to I duly , and I have to thank your goodness and kindness for the good advice which you be pleased to give I , as also for the remembrance of dear home , which I shall love never the bad for have be to the home of our ancestor in England . 
 " I writ you a letter by the last monthly packet , inform my honoured mother of the little accident I have on the road hither , and of the kind friend who I find and whom take I in . since then I have be profiting of the fine weather and the good company here , and have make many friend among our nobility , whose acquaintance I be sure you will not be sorry that I should make . among their lordship I may mention the famous Earl of Chesterfield , late Ambassador to Holland , and Viceroy of the Kingdom of Ireland ; the Earl of March and Ruglen , who will be Duke of Queensberry at the death of his grace ; and her grace the Duchess , a celebrate beauty of the Queen 's time , when she remember my grandpapa at Court . these and many more person of the first fashion attend my aunt 's assembly , which be the most crowded at this crowded place . also on my way hither I stay at Westerham , at the house of an officer , Lieut.-Gen . Wolfe , who serve with my grandfather and General Webb in the famous war of the Duke of Marlborough . Mr. Wolfe have a son , Lieut.-Col . James Wolfe , engage to be married to a beautiful lady now in this place , Miss Lowther of the North and though but 30 year old he be look up to as much as any officer in the whole army , and have serve with honour under his Royal Highness the Duke wherever our arm have be employ . 
 " I thank my honoured mother for announce to I that a quarter 's allowance of 52l . 10 . will be pay I by Mr. Trail . I be in no present want of cash , and by practise a rigid economy , which will be necessary   as I do not disguise   for the maintenance of horse , Gumbo , and the equipage and apparel requisite for a young gentleman of good family , hope to be able to maintain my credit without unduly trespassing upon your . the linnen and clothe which I bring with I will with due care last for some year as you say . ' tis not quite so fine as wear here by person of fashion , and I may have to purchase a few very fine shirt for great day : but those I have be excellent for daily wear . 
 " I be thankful that I have be quite without occasion to use your excellent family pill . Gumbo hath take they with great benefit , who grow fat and saucy upon english beef , ale , and air . he send his humble duty to his mistress , and prays Mrs. Mountain to remember he to all his fellow - servant , especially Dinah and Lily , for whom he have buy posey - ring at Tunbridge Fair . 
 " besides partaking of all the pleasure of the place , I hope my honoured mother will believe that I have not be unmindful of my education . I have have master in fencing and dancing , and my Lord Castlewood 's chaplain , the Reverend Mr. Sampson , have come hither to drink the water , have be so good as to take a vacant room at my lodging . Mr. S. breakfast with I , and we read together of a morning he say that I be not quite such a dunce as I use to appear at home . we have read in Mr. Rapin 's history , Dr. Barrow 's sermon , and , for amusement , Shakspeare , Mr. Pope 's Homer , and   in French   the translation of an Arabian Work of Tales , very divert . several man of learn have be stay here besides the person of fashion ; and amongst the former be Mr. Richardson , the author of the famous book which you and Mountain and my dear brother use to love so . he be pleased when I tell he that his work be in your closet in Virginia , and beg I to convey his respectful compliment to my lady - mother . Mr. R. be a short fat man , with little of the fire of genius visible in his eye or person . 
 " my aunt and my cousin , the Lady Maria , desire their affectionate compliment to you , and with good regard for Mountain , to whom I enclose a note , I be , honour madam , your dutiful son , H. ESMOND WARRINGTON . " 
 note in Madam Esmond 's handwriting ,

Packet of Infallible Family Pills for Gumbo . my papa 's large silver - gilt shoe - buckle for H. , and red silver - lace saddle - cloth . "

 " for Mrs. Mountain . 
 " what do you mien , you silly old Mountain , by send an order for your poor old divadend dew at Xmas ? I would have you to know I do not   want your 7l . 10 , and have toar your order up into 1000 bitt . I have   plenty of money . but I be obleaged to you all same . a kiss to Fanny from your loving HARRY . " 
 note in Madam Esmond 's Handwriting 
 " this note , which I desire M. to show to I , prove that she hath a good heart , and that she wish to show her gratitude to the family , by give up her half - yearly divd .   on L500 3 per ct .   to my boy . hence I reprimand she very slightly for dare to send money to Mr. E. Warrington , unknown to his mother . note to Mountain not so well spelt as letter to I . 
 " Mem . to write to Revd . Mr. Sampson desire to know what theolog . book he read with H. Recommend Law , Baxter , Drelincourt . Request H. to say his catechism to Mr. S. , which he have never quite be able to master . by next ship peach   3 , tobacco 1 2 cwt . Hams for Mr. S. " 
 the mother of the Virginians and her son have long long since pass away . so how be we to account for the fact , that of a couple of letter send under one enclosure and by one packet , one should be well spell , and the other not entirely orthographical ? have Harry find some wonderful instructor , such as exist in the present lucky time , and who would improve his writing in six lesson ? my view of the case , after deliberately examine the two note , be this : no . 1 , in which there appear a trifling grammatical slip   " the kind , friend who I find and whom take I in " , must have be re - write from a rough copy which have probably undergo the supervision of a tutor or friend . the more artless composition , no . 2 , be not refer to the scholar who prepare no . 1 for the maternal eye , and to whose correction of " who " and " whom " Mr. Warrington do not pay very close attention . who know how he may have be disturb ? a pretty milliner may have attract Harry 's attention out of window a dancing bear with pipe and tabor may have pass along the common a jockey come under his window to show off a horse there ? there be some day when any of we may be ungrammatical and spell ill . finally , suppose Harry do not care to spell so elegantly for Mrs. Mountain as for his lady - mother , what affair be that of the present biographer , century , reader ? and as for your objection that Mr. Warrington , in the above communication to his mother , show some little hypocrisy and reticence in his dealing with that venerable person , I dare say , young folk , you in your time have write more than one prim letter to your papa and mamma in which not quite all the transaction of your life be narrate , or if narrate , be exhibit in the most favourable light for yourself I dare say , old folk ! you , in your time , be not altogether more candid . there must be a certain distance between I and my son Jacky . there must be a respectful , an amiable , a virtuous hypocrisy between we . I do not in the least wish that he should treat I as his equal , that he should contradict I , take my arm - chair , read the newspaper first at breakfast , ask unlimited friend to dine when I have a party of my own , and so forth . no ; where there be not equality there must be hypocrisy . continue to be blind to my fault ; to hush still as mice when I fall asleep after dinner ; to laugh at my old joke ; to admire my saying ; to be astonish at the impudence of those unbelieve reviewer ; to be dear filial humbug , o my child ! in my castle I be king . let all my royal household back before I . ' tis not their natural way of walk , I know : but a decorous , become , and modest behaviour highly agreeable to I . away from I they may do , nay , they do do , what they like . they may jump , skip , dance , trot , tumble over head and heel , and kick about freely , when they be out of the presence of my majesty . do not then , my dear young friend , be surprised at your mother and aunt when they cry out , " oh , it be highly immoral and improper of Mr. Warrington to be write home humdrum demure letter to his dear mamma , when he be play all sort of merry prank ! " but drop a curtsey , and say , " yes , dear grandmamma   or aunt , as may be , it be very wrong of he : and I suppose you never have your fun when you be young . " of course , she do not ! and the sun never shine , and the blossom never bud , and the blood never dance , and the fiddle never sing , in her spring - time . eh , Babet ! mon lait de poule et mon bonnet de nuit ! Ho , Betty ! my gruel and my slipper ! and go , ye frisky , merry little soul ! and dance , and have your merry little supper of cake and ale ! 




 CHAPTER XXXI . the Bear and the Leader 

 our candid reader know the real state of the case regard Harry Warrington and that luckless Cattarina ; but a number of the old lady at Tunbridge Wells suppose the Virginian to be as dissipate as any young english nobleman of the high quality , and Madame de Bernstein be especially incredulous about her nephew 's innocence . it be the old lady 's firm belief that Harry be lead not only a merry life , but a wicked one , and her wish be father to the thought that the lad might be no well than his neighbour . an old Roman herself , she like her nephew to do as Rome do . all the scandal regard Mr. Warrington 's Lovelace adventure she eagerly and complacently accept . we have see how , on one or two occasion , he give tea and music to the company at the Wells ; and he be so gallant and amiable to the lady   to lady of a much well figure and character than the unfortunate Cattarina , that Madame Bernstein cease to be disquiet regard the silly love affair which have have a commencement at Castlewood , and relax in her vigilance over Lady Maria . some folk many old folk be too selfish to interest themselves long about the affair of their neighbour . the Baroness have her trump to think of , her dinner , her twinge of rheumatism : and her suspicion regard Maria and Harry , lately so lively , now doze , and keep a careless , unobservant watch . she may have think that the danger be over , or she may have cease to care whether it exist or not , or that artful Maria , by her conduct , may have quite cajole , soothe , and misguide the old Dragon , to whose charge she be give over . at Maria 's age , nay , early indeed , maidens have learn to be very sly , and at Madame Bernstein 's time of life dragon be not so fierce and alert . they can not turn so readily , some of their old tooth have drop out , and their eye require more sleep than they need in day when they be more active , venomous , and dangerous . I , for my part , know a few female dragon , de par le monde , and , as I watch they and remember what they be , admire the soften influence of year upon these whilom destroyer of man- and woman - kind . their scale be so soft that any knight with a moderate power of thrust can strike they : their claw , once strong enough to tear out a thousand eye , only fall with a feeble pat that scarce raise the skin : their tongue , from their toothless old gum , dart a venom which be rather disagreeable than deadly . see they trail their languid tail , and crawl home to their cavern at roosting - time ! how weak be their power of do injury ! their maleficence how feeble ! how change be they since the brisk day when their eye shoot wicked fire ; their tongue spat poison ; their breath blasted reputation ; and they gobble up a daily victim at least ! 
 if the good folk at Oakhurst could not resist the testimony which be bring to they regard Harry 's ill - doing , why should Madame Bernstein , who in the course of her long day have have more experience of evil than all the Oakhurst family put together , be less credulous than they ? of course every single old woman of her ladyship 's society believe every story that be tell about Mr. Harry Warrington 's dissipated habit , and be ready to believe as much more ill of he as you please . when the little dancer go back to London , as she do , it be because that heartless Harry desert she . he desert she for somebody else , whose name be confidently give , whose name ? whose half - dozen name the society at Tunbridge Wells would whisper about ; where there congregate people of all rank and degree , woman of fashion , woman of reputation , of demi - reputation , of virtue , of no virtue , all mingle in the same room , dance to the same fiddle , drink out of the same glass at the Wells , and alike in search of health , or society , or pleasure . a century ago , and our ancestor , the most free or the most straitlace , meet together at a score of such merry place as that where our present scene lie , and dance , and frisk , and game , and drink at Epsom , Bath , Tunbridge , Harrogate , as they do at Homburg and Baden now . 
 Harry 's bad reputation , then , comfort his old aunt exceedingly , and ease her mind in respect to the boy 's passion for Lady Maria . so easy be she in her mind , that when the chaplain say he come to escort her ladyship home , Madame Bernstein do not even care to part from her niece . she prefer rather to keep she under her eye , to talk to she about her wicked young cousin 's wild extravagance , to whisper to she that boy would be boy , to confide to Maria her intention of get a proper wife for Harry , some one of a suitable age , some one with a suitable fortune , all which pleasantrie poor Maria have to bear with as much fortitude as she could muster . 
 there live , during the last century , a certain french duke and marquis , who distinguish himself in Europe , and America likewise , and have oblige posterity by leave behind he a choice volume of memoir , which the gentle reader be specially warn not to consult . have perform the part of Don Juan in his own country , in ours , and in other part of Europe , he have kindly note down the name of many court - beauty who fall victim to his power of fascination ; and very pleasant read no doubt it must be for the grandson and descendant of the fashionable person amongst whom our brilliant nobleman move , to find the name of their ancestress adorn M. le Duc 's sprightly page , and their frailty record by the candid writer who cause they . 
 in the course of the peregrination of this nobleman , he visit North America , and , as have be his custom in Europe , proceed straightway to fall in love . and curious it be to contrast the elegant refinement of european society , where , accord to monseigneur , he have but to lay siege to a woman in order to vanquish she , with the simple life and habit of the colonial folk , amongst whom this European enslaver of heart do not , it appear , make a single conquest . have he do so , he would as certainly have narrate his victory in Pennsylvania and New England , as he describe his success in this and his own country . traveller in America have cry out quite loudly enough against the rudeness and barbarism of transatlantic manner ; let the present writer give the humble testimony of his experience that the conversation of american gentleman be generally modest , and , to the good of his belief , the life of the woman pure . 
 we have say that Mr. Harry Warrington bring his colonial modesty along with he to the old country ; and though he could not help hear the free talk of the person amongst whom he live , and who be man of pleasure and the world , he sit pretty silent himself in the midst of their rattle ; never indulge in double entendre in his conversation with woman ; have no victory over the sex to boast of ; and be shy and awkward when he hear such narrated by other . 
 this youthful modesty Mr. Sampson have remark during his intercourse with the lad at Castlewood , where Mr. Warrington have more than once show himself quite uneasy whilst cousin will be tell some of his choice story ; and my lord have curtly rebuke his brother , bid he keep his joke for the usher 's table at Kensington , and not give needless offence to their kinsman . hence the exclamation of " Reverentia pueris , " which the chaplain have address to his neighbour at the ordinary on Harry 's first appearance there . Mr. Sampson , if he have not strength sufficient to do right himself , at least have grace enough not to offend innocent young gentleman by his cynicism . 
 the chaplain be touch by Harry 's gift of the horse ; and feel a genuine friendliness towards the lad . " you see , sir , " say he , " I be of the world , and must do as the rest of the world do . I have lead a rough life , Mr. Warrington , and can not   afford to be more particular than my neighbour . Video meliora , deteriora sequor , as we say at college . I have get a little sister , who be at boarding - school , not very far from here , and , as I keep a decent tongue in my head when I be talk with my little Patty , and expect other to do as much , sure I may try and do as much by you . " 
 the chaplain be loud in his praise of Harry to his aunt , the old baroness . she like to hear he praise . she be as fond of he as she could be of anything ; be pleased in his company , with his good look , his manly courageous bearing , his blush , which come so readily , his bright eye , his deep youthful voice . his shrewdness and simplicity constantly amuse she ; she would have weary of he long before , have he be clever , or learn , or witty , or other than he be . " we must find a good wife for he , Chaplain , " she say to Mr. Sampson . " I have one or two in my eye , who , I think , will suit he . we must set he up here ; he never will bear go back to his savage again , or to live with his little Methodist of a mother . " 
 now about this point Mr. Sampson , too , be personally anxious , and have also a wife in his eye for Harry . I suppose he must have have some conversation with his lord at Castlewood , whom we have hear express some intention of compliment his chaplain with a good living or other provision , in event of his be able to carry out his lordship 's wish regard a marriage for Lady Maria . if his good office could help that anxious lady to a husband , Sampson be ready to employ they : and he now wait to see in what most effectual manner he could bring his influence to bear . 
 Sampson 's society be most agreeable , and he and his young friend be intimate in the course of a few hour . the parson rejoice in high spirit , good appetite , good humour ; pretend to no sort of squeamishness , and indulge in no sanctify hypocritical conversation ; nevertheless , he take care not to shock his young friend by any needless outbreak of levity or immorality of talk , initiate his pupil , perhaps from policy , perhaps from compunction , only into the minor mystery , as it be ; and not tell he the secret with which the unlucky adept himself be only too familiar . with Harry , Sampson be only a brisk , lively , jolly companion , ready for any drinking bout , or any sport , a cock - fight , a shooting - match , a game at card , or a gallop across the common ; but his conversation be decent , and he try much more to amuse the young man , than to lead he astray . the chaplain be quite successful : he have immense animal spirit as well as natural wit , and aptitude as well as experience in that business of toad - eater which have be his calling and livelihood from his very early year , ever since he first enter college as a servitor , and cast about to see by whose mean he could make his fortune in life . that be but satire just now , when we say there be no toad - eater leave in the world . there be many man of Sampson 's profession now , doubtless ; nay , little boy at our public school be send thither at the early age , instruct by their parent , and put out apprentice to toad - eating . but the flattery be not so manifest as it use to be a hundred year since . young man and old have hanger - on , and lead captain , but they assume an appearance of equality , borrow money , or swallow their toad in private , and walk abroad arm - in - arm with the great man , and call he by his name without his title . in those good old time , when Harry Warrington first come to Europe , a gentleman 's toad - eater pretend to no air of equality at all ; openly pay court to his patron , call he by that name to other folk , go on his errand for he , any sort of errand which the patron might devise , call he sir in speak to he , stand up in his presence until bid to sit down , and flatter he ex officio . Mr. Sampson do not take the least shame in speak of Harry as his young patron , as a young virginian nobleman recommend to he by his other noble patron , the Earl of Castlewood . he be proud of appear at Harry 's side , and as his humble retainer , in public talk about he to the company , give order to Harry 's tradesman , from whom , let we hope , he receive a percentage in return for his recommendation , perform all the function of aide - de - camp other , if our young gentleman demand they from the obsequious divine , who have gaily discharge the duty of ami du prince to ever so many young man of fashion , since his own entrance into the world . it must be confess that , since his arrival in Europe , Mr. Warrington have not be uniformly lucky in the friendship which he have make . 
 " what a reputation , sir , they have make for you in this place ! " cry Mr. Sampson , come back from the coffee - house to his patron . " Monsieur de Richelieu be nothing to you ! " 
 " how do you mean , Monsieur de Richelieu ? never be at Minorca in my life , " say downright Harry , who have not hear of those victory at home , which make the french duke famous . 
 Mr. Sampson explain . the pretty widow Patcham who have just arrive be certainly desperate about Mr. Warrington : her way of go on at the room , the night before , prove that . as for Mrs. Hooper , that be a known case , and the Alderman have fetch his wife back to London for no other reason . it be the talk of the whole Wells . 
 " who say so ? " cry out Harry , indignantly . " I should like to meet the man who dare say so , and confound the villain ! " 
 " I should not like to show he to you , " say Mr. Sampson , laugh . " it might be the bad for he . " 
 " it be a shame to speak with such levity about the character of lady or of gentleman either , " continue Mr. Warrington , pace up and down the room in a fume . 
 " so I tell they , " say the chaplain , wag his head and look very much moved and very grave , though , if the truth be know , it have never come into his mind at all to be angry at hear charge of this nature against Harry . 
 " it be a shame , I say , to talk away the reputation of any man or woman as people do here . do you know , in our country , a fellow 's ear would not be safe ; and a little before I leave home , three brother shoot down a man , for have speak ill of their sister . " 
 " serve the villain right ! " cry Sampson . 
 " already they have have that calumny about I set a - go here , Sampson , about I and the poor little french dancing - girl . " 
 " I have hear , " say Mr. Sampson , shake powder out of his wig . 
 " wicked ; be not   it ? " 
 " abominable . " 
 " they say the very same thing about my Lord March . be not   it shameful ? " 
 " indeed it be , " say Mr. Sampson , preserve a face of wonderful gravity . 
 " I do not   know what I should do if these story be to come to my mother 's ear . it would break her heart , I do believe it would . why , only a few day before you come , a military friend of mine , Mr. Wolfe , tell I how the most horrible lie be circulate about I . good heaven ! what do they think a gentleman of my name and country can be capable of I a seducer of woman ? they might as well say I be a horse - stealer or a housebreaker . I vow if I hear any man say so , I will have his ear ! " 
 " I have read , sir , that the Grand Seignior of Turkey have bushel of ear sometimes send in to he , " say Mr. Sampson , laugh . " if you take all those that have hear scandal against you or other , what basketsful you would fill ! " 
 " and so I would , Sampson , as soon as look at them : any fellow 's who say a word against a lady or a gentleman of honour ! " cry the Virginian . 
 " if you will go down to the Well , you will find a harvest of them . I just come from there . it be the high tide of scandal . detraction be at its height . and you may see the nymphas discente and the aure satyrorum acutas , " cry the chaplain , with a shrug of his shoulder . 
 " that may be as you say , Sampson , " Mr. Warrington reply , " but if ever I hear any man speak against my character I will punish he . mark that . " 
 " I shall be very sorry for his sake , that I should ; for you will mark he in a way he will not   like , sir ; and I know you be a man of your word . " 
 " you may be sure of that , Sampson . and now shall we go to dinner , and afterwards to my Lady Trumpington 's tea ? " 
 " you know , sir , I can not   resist a card or a bottle , " say Mr. Sampson . " let we have the last first and then the first shall come last . " and with this the two gentleman go off to their accustomed place of refection . 
 that be an age in which wine - bibbing be more common than in our politer time ; and , especially since the arrival of General Braddock 's army in his native country , our young Virginian have acquire rather a liking for the filling of bumper and the calling of toast ; have hear that it be a point of honour among the officer never to decline a toast or a challenge . so Harry and his chaplain drink their claret in peace and plenty , naming , as the simple custom be , some favourite lady with each glass . 
 the chaplain have reason of his own for desire to know how far the affair between Harry and my Lady Maria have go ; whether it be advance , or whether it be end ; and he and his young friend be just warm enough with the claret to be able to talk with that great eloquence , that candour , that admirable friendliness , which good wine take in rather injudicious quantity inspire . o kindly harvest of the aquitanian grape ! o sunny bank of Garonne ! o friendly cave of Gledstane and Morol , where the dusky flask lie recondite ! may we not say a word of thank for all the pleasure we owe you ? be the Temperance man to be allow to shout in the public place ? be the Vegetarians to bellow " Cabbage for ever ? " and may we modest enophilist not sing the praise of our favourite plant ? after the drinking of good Bordeaux wine , there be a point   I do not say a pint   at which man arrive , when all the generous faculty of the soul be awaken and in full vigour ; when the wit brighten and break out in sudden flash ; when the intellect be keen ; when the pen - up word and confine thought get a night - rule , and rush abroad and disport themselves ; when the kindliest affection , come out and shake hand with mankind , and the timid Truth jump up naked out of his well and proclaim himself to all the world . how , by the kind influence of the wine - cup , we succour the poor and humble ! how bravely we rush to the rescue of the oppressed ! I say , in the face of all the pump which ever spout , that there be a moment in a bout of good wine at which , if a man could but remain , wit , wisdom , courage , generosity , eloquence , happiness be his ; but the moment pass , and that other glass somehow spoil the state of beatitude . there be a headache in the morning ; we be not go into Parliament for our native town ; we be not go to shoot those french officer who have be speak disrespectfully of our country ; and poor Jeremy Diddler call about eleven o'clock for another half - sovereign , and we be unwell in bed , and can not   see he , and send he empty away . 
 well , then , as they sate over their generous cup , the company have depart , and the bottle of claret be bring in by Monsieur Barbeau , the chaplain find himself in an eloquent state , with a strong desire for inculcate sublime moral precept whilst Harry be move by an extreme longing to explain his whole private history , and to impart all his present feeling to his new friend . mark that fact . why must a man say everything that come uppermost in his noble mind , because , forsooth , he have swallow a half - pint more wine than he ordinarily drink ? suppose I have commit a murder   of course I allow the sherry , and champagne at dinner , should I announce that homicide somewhere about the third bottle   in a small party of man   of claret at dessert ? of course : and hence the fidelity to water - gruel announce a few page back . 
 " I be glad to hear what your conduct have really be with regard to the Cattarina , Mr. Warrington ; I be glad from my soul , " say the impetuous chaplain . " the wine be with you . you have show that you can bear down calumny , and resist temptation . ah ! my dear sir , man be not all so fortunate . what famous good wine this be ! " and he suck up a glass with " a toast from you , my dear sir , if you please ? " 
 " I give you ' Miss Fanny Mountain , of Virginia , ' " say Mr. Warrington , fill a bumper as his thought fly straightway , ever so many thousand mile , to home . 
 " one of your american conquest , I suppose ? " say the chaplain . 
 " Nay , she be but ten year old , and I have never make any conquest at all in Virginia , Mr. Sampson , " say the young gentleman . 
 " you be like a true gentleman , and do not   kiss and tell , sir . " 
 " I neither kiss nor tell . it be not   the custom of our country , Sampson , to ruin girl , or frequent the society of low woman . we virginian gentleman honour woman : we do not   wish to bring they to shame , " cry the young toper , look very proud and handsome . " the young lady whose name I mention hath live in our family since her infancy , and I would shoot the man who do she a wrong ; by Heaven , I would ! " 
 " your sentiment do you honour ! let I shake hand with you ! I will shake hand with you , Mr. Warrington , " cry the enthusiastic Sampson . " and let I tell you ' tis the grasp of honest friendship offer you , and not merely the poor retainer pay court to the wealthy patron . no ! with such liquor as this , all man be equal ; faith , all man be rich , whilst it last ! and Tom Sampson be as wealthy with his bottle as your honour with all the acre of your principality ! " 
 " let we have another bottle of rich , " say Harry , with a laugh . " Encore du cachet jaune , mon bon Monsieur Barbeau ! " and exit Monsieur Barbeau to the cave below . 
 " another bottle of rich ! Capital , capital ! how beautifully you speak French , Mr. Harry ! " 
 " I do speak it well , " say Harry . " at least , when I speak , Monsieur Barbeau understand I well enough . " 
 " you do everything well , I think . you succeed in whatever you try . that be why they have fancy here you have win the heart of so many woman , sir . " 
 " there you go again about the woman ! I tell you I do not   like these story about woman . confound I , Sampson , why be a gentleman 's character to be blacken so ? " 
 " well , at any rate , there be one , unless my eye deceive I very much indeed , sir ! " cry the chaplain . 
 " whom do you mean ? " ask Harry , flush very red . 
 " Nay , I name no name . it be not   for a poor chaplain to meddle with his better ' doing , or to know their thought , " say Mr. Sampson . 
 " thought ! what thought , Sampson ? " 
 " I fancy I see , on the part of a certain lovely and respected lady at Castlewood , a preference exhibit . I fancy , on the side of a certain distinguished young gentleman , a strong liking manifest itself : but I may have be wrong , and ask pardon . " 
 " oh , Sampson , Sampson ! " break out the young man . " I tell you I be miserable . I tell you I have be long for some one to confide in , or ask advice of . you do know , then , that there have be something go on something between I and help Mr. Sampson , Monsieur Barbeau and and some one else ? " 
 " I have watch it this month past , " say the chaplain . 
 " confound I , sir , do you mean you have be a spy on I ? " say the other hotly . 
 " a spy ! you make little disguise of the matter , Mr. Warrington , and her ladyship be not   a much well hand at deceive . you be always together . in the shrubbery , in the walk , in the village , in the gallery of the house , you always find a pretext for be together , and plenty of eye besides mine watch you . " 
 " gracious power ! what do you see , Sampson ? " cry the lad . 
 " Nay , sir , ' tis forbid to kiss and tell . I say so again , " say the chaplain . 
 the young man turn very red . " oh , Sampson ! " he cry , " can I can I confide in you ? " 
 " Dearest sir dear generous youth you know I would shed my heart 's blood for you ! " exclaim the chaplain , squeeze his patron 's hand , and turn a brilliant pair of eye ceilingward . 
 " oh , Sampson ! I tell you I be miserable . with all this play and wine , whilst I have be here , I tell you I have be try to drive away care . I own to you that when we be at Castlewood there be thing pass between a certain lady and I . " 
 the parson give a slight whistle over his glass of Bordeaux . 
 " and they have   make I wretche , those thing have . I mean , you see , that if a gentleman have give his word , why , it be his word , and he must stand by it , you know . I mean that I think I love she , and so I do very much , and she be a most dear , kind , darling , affectionate creature , and very handsome , too , quite beautiful ; but then , you know , our age , Sampson ! think of our age , Sampson ! she be as old as my mother ! " 
 " who would never forgive you . " 
 " I do not   intend to let anybody meddle in my affair , not Madam Esmond nor anybody else , " cry Harry : " but you see , Sampson , she be old and , oh , hang it ! why do Aunt Bernstein tell I ? " 
 " tell you what ? " 
 " something I can not   divulge to anybody , something that torture I ! " 
 " not about the the " the chaplain pause : he be go to say about her ladyship 's little affair with the french dancing - master ; about other little anecdote affect her character . but he have not drunk wine enough to be quite candid , or too much , and be past the real moment of virtue . 
 " yes , yes , every one of them false every one of them ! " shriek out Harry . 
 " great power , what do you mean ? " ask his friend . 
 " these , sir , these ! " say Harry , beat a tattoo on his own white tooth . " I do not   know it when I ask she . I swear I do not   know it . oh , it be horrible it be horrible ! and it have cause I night of agony , Sampson . my dear old grandfather have a set a Frenchman at Charleston make they for he , and we use to look at them grin in a tumbler , and when they be out , his jaw use to fall in I never think she have them . "

 " confound it , sir , do not   you see I mean tooth ? " say Harry , rap the table . 
 " Nay , only two . " 
 " and how the devil do you know , sir ? " ask the young man , fiercely . 
 " I I have it from she maid . she have two tooth knock out by a stone which cut her lip a little , and they have be replace . "

 " ' tis lovely I have see that . I can take my oath to that . her ladyship can sit upon it ; and her figure be very fine ; and her skin be as white as snow ; and her heart be the kind that ever be ; and I know , that is I feel sure , it be very tender about you , Mr. Warrington . " 
 " oh , Sampson ! Heaven , Heaven bless you ! what a weight you have   take off my mind with those those never mind they ! oh , Sam ! how happy that be , no , no ob , how miserable I be ! she be as old as Madam Esmond by George she be she be as old as my mother . you would not   have a fellow marry a woman as old as his mother ? it be too bad : by George it be . it be too bad . " and then lunge at M. Barbeau , who skip away from the lunge behind the chaplain , who look rather alarmed . I know we could have have a much more exciting picture than either of those we present of Harry this month , and the lad , with his hair dishevel , rag about the room flamberge au vent , and pink the affright innkeeper and chaplain , would have afford a good subject for the pencil . but oh , to think of he stumble over a stool , and prostrate by an enemy who have steal away his brain ! come , Gumbo ! and help your master to bed ! 




 CHAPTER XXXII . in which a Family Coach be order 

 our pleasing duty now be to divulge the secret which Mr. Lambert whisper in his wife 's ear at the close of the antepenultimate chapter , and the publication of which cause such great pleasure to the whole of the Oakhurst family . as the hay be in , the corn not ready for cut , and by consequence the farm horse disengage , why , ask Colonel Lambert , should they not be put into the coach , and should we not all pay a visit to Tunbridge Wells , take friend Wolfe at Westerham on our way ? 
 Mamma embrace this proposal , and I dare say the honest gentleman who make it . all the child jump for joy . the girl go off straightway to get together their good calamancoe , paduasoy , falbala , furbelow , cape , cardinal , sack , negligee , solitaire , cap , ribbon , mantuas , clocked stocking , and high - heeled shoe , and I know not what article of toilet . Mamma 's good robe be take from the press , whence they only issue on rare , solemn occasion , retire immediately afterwards to lavender and seclusion ; the brave Colonel produce his laced hat and waistcoat and silver - hilte hanger ; Charley rejoice in a rasee holiday suit of his father 's , in which the Colonel have be marry , and which Mrs. Lambert cut up , not without a pang . ball and Dumpling have their tail and mane tie with ribbon , and Chump , the old white cart - horse , go as unicorn leader , to help the carriage - horse up the first hilly five mile of the road from Oakhurst to Westerham . the carriage be an ancient vehicle , and be believe to have serve in the procession which have bring George I. from Greenwich to London , on his first arrival to assume the sovereignty of these realm . it have belong to Mr. Lambert 's father , and the family have be in the habit of regard it , ever since they could remember anything , as one of the most splendid coach in the three kingdom . Brian , coachman , and must it also be own ? ploughman , of the Oakhurst family , have a place on the box , with Mr. Charley by his side . the precious clothe be pack in imperial on the roof . the Colonel 's pistol be put in the pocket of the carriage , and the blunderbuss hang behind the box , in reach of Brian , who be an old soldier . no highwayman , however , molest the convoy ; not even an innkeeper levy contribution on Colonel Lambert , who , with a slender purse and a large family , be not to be plunder by those or any other depredator on the king 's highway ; and a reasonable cheap modest lodging have be engage for they by young Colonel Wolfe , at the house where he be in the habit of put up , and whither he himself accompany they on horseback . 
 it happen that these lodging be opposite Madame Bernstein 's ; and as the Oakhurst family reach their quarter on a Saturday evening , they could see chair after chair discharge powdered beaux and patch and brocade beauty at the Baroness 's door , who be hold one of her many card - party . the sun be not yet down   for our ancestor begin their dissipation at early hour , and be at meat , drink , or card , any time after three o'clock in the afternoon until any time in the night or morning , and the young country lady and their mother from their window could see the various personage as they pass into the Bernstein rout . Colonel Wolfe tell the lady who most of the character be . ' twas almost as delightful as go to the party themselves , Hetty and Theo think , for they not only could see the guest arrive , but look into the Baroness 's open casement and watch many of they there . of a few of the personage we have before have a glimpse . when the Duchess of Queensberry pass , and Mr. Wolfe explain who she be , Martin Lambert be ready with a score of line about " Kitty , beautiful and young , " from his favourite Mat Prior . 
 " think that that old lady be once like you , girl ! " cry the Colonel . 
 " like we , papa ? well , certainly we never set up for be beauty ! " say Miss Hetty , toss up her little head . 
 " yes , like you , you little baggage ; like you at this moment , who want to go to that drum yonder : 
  ' inflame with rage at sad restraint 
  which wise mamma ordain , 
  and sorely vex to play the saint 
  Whilst wit and beauty reign . ' " 
 " we be never invite , papa ; and I be sure if there be no beauty more worth see than that , the wit can not   be much worth the hearing , " again say the satirist of the family . 
 " oh , but he be a rare poet , Mat Prior ! " continue the Colonel ; " though , mind you , girl , you will skip over all the poem I have mark with a cross . a rare poet ! and to think you should see one of his heroine ! ' fondness prevail , mamma give way '   she always will , Mrs. Lambert !  
  ' Fondness prevail , mamma give way , 
  Kitty at heart 's desire 
  obtain the chariot for a day , 
  and set the world on fire ! ' " 
 " I be sure it must have be very inflammable , " say mamma . 
 " so it be , my dear , twenty year ago , much more inflammable than it be now , " remark the Colonel . 
 " Nonsense , Mr. Lambert , " be mamma 's answer . 
 " look , look ! " cry Hetty , run forward and point to the little square , and the cover gallery , where be the door lead to Madame Bernstein 's apartment , and round which stand a crowd of street urchin , idler , and yokel , watch the company . 
 " it be Harry Warrington ! " exclaim Theo , wave a handkerchief to the young Virginian : but Warrington do not see Miss Lambert . the Virginian be walk arm - in - arm with a portly clergyman in a crisp rustle silk gown , and the two go into Madame de Bernstein 's door . 
 " I hear he preach a most admirable sermon here last Sunday , " say Mr. Wolfe ; " a little theatrical , but most striking and eloquent . " 
 " you seem to be here most Sundays , James , " say Mrs. Lambert . 
 " and Monday , and soon till Saturday , " add the Colonel . " see , Harry have beautify himself already , hath his hair in buckle , and I have no doubt be go to the drum too . " 
 " I have rather sit quiet generally of a Saturday evening , " say sober Mr. Wolfe ; " at any rate , away from card - playing and scandal ; but I own , dear Mrs. Lambert , I be under order . shall I go across the way and send Mr. Warrington to you ? "
