 " no , let he have his sport . we shall see he to - morrow . he will not   care to be disturb amidst his fine folk by we country - people , " say meek Mrs. Lambert . 
 " I be glad he be with a clergyman who preach so well , " say Theo , softly ; and her eye seem to say , you see , good people , he be not so bad as you think he , and as I , for my part , never believe he to be . " the clergyman have a very kind , handsome face . " 
 " here come a great clergyman , " cry Mr. Wolfe . " it be my Lord of Salisbury , with his blue ribbon , and a chaplain behind he . " 
 " and whom a mercy 's name have we here ? " break in Mrs. Lambert , as a sedan - chair , cover with gilding , top with no less than five earl 's coronet , carry by bearer in richly laced clothe , and precede by three footman in the same splendid livery , now come up to Madame de Bernstein 's door . the Bishop , who have be about to enter , stop , and run back with the most respectful bow and curtsey to the sedan - chair , give his hand to the lady who step thence . 
 " who on earth be this ? " ask Mrs. Lambert . 
 " Sprechen sie Deutsch ? Ja , meinherr . Nichts verstand , " say the waggish Colonel . 
 " Pooh , Martin . " 
 " well , if you can not   understand High Dutch , my love , how can I help it ? your education be neglect at school . can you understand heraldry ? I know you can . " 
 " I make . " cry Charley , recite the shield , " three merion on a field or , with an earl 's coronet . " 
 " a countess 's coronet , my son . the Countess of Yarmouth , my son . " 
 " and pray who be she ? " 
 " it hath ever be the custom of our sovereign to advance person of distinction to honour , " continue the Colonel , gravely , " and this eminent lady hath be so promote by our gracious monarch , to the rank of Countess of this kingdom . " 
 " but why , papa ? " ask the daughter together . 
 " never mind , girl ! " say mamma . 
 but that incorrigible Colonel would go on . 
 " Y , my child , be one of the last and the most awkward letter of the whole alphabet . when I tell you story , you be always say why . why should my Lord Bishop be cringe to that lady ? look at he rub his fat hand together , and smile into her face ! it be not a handsome face any long . it be all paint red and white like Scaramouch 's in the pantomime . see , there come another blue - riband , as I live . my Lord Bamborough . the descendant of the Hotspurs . the proud man in England . he stop , he bow , he smile ; he be hat in hand , too . see , she tap he with her fan . get away , you crowd of little blackguard boy , and do not   tread on the robe of the lady whom the King delight to honour . " 
 " but why do the King honour she ? " ask the girl once more . 
 " there go that odious last letter but one ! do you ever hear of her grace the Duchess of Kendal ? no . of the Duchess of Portsmouth ? Non plus . of the Duchess of La Valliore ? of Fair Rosamond , then ? " 
 " hush , papa ! there be no need to bring blush on the cheek of my dear one , Martin Lambert ! " say the mother , put her finger to her husband 's lip . 
 " ' tis not I ; it be their sacred Majesties who be the cause of the shame , " cry the son of the old republican . " think of the bishop of the Church and the proud nobility of the world cringe and bow before that paint high dutch Jezebel . oh , it be a shame ! a shame ! " 
 " confusion ! " here break out Colonel Wolfe , and make a dash at his hat , run from the room . he have see the young lady whom he admire and her guardian walk across the Pantiles on foot to the Baroness 's party , and they come up whilst the Countess of Yarmouth - Walmoden be engage in conversation with the two lord spiritual and temporal , and these two make the low reverence and bow to the Countess , and wait until she have pass in at the door on the Bishop 's arm . 
 Theo turn away from the window with a sad , almost awestricken face . Hetty still remain there , look from it with indignation in her eye , and a little red spot on each cheek . 
 " a penny for little Hetty 's thought , " say mamma , come to the window to lead the child away . 
 " I be think what I should do if I see papa bow to that woman , " say Hetty . 
 Tea and a hiss kettle here make their appearance , and the family sate down to partake of their evening meal , leave , however , Miss Hetty , from her place , command of the window , which she beg her brother not to close . that young gentleman have be down amongst the crowd to inspect the armorial bearing of the Countess 's and other sedan , no doubt , and also to invest sixpence in a cheese - cake , by mamma 's order and his own desire , and he return presently with this delicacy wrap up in a paper . 
 " look , mother , " he come back and say , " do you see that big man in brown beat all the pillar with a stick ? that be the learn Mr. Johnson . he come to the Friars sometimes to see our master . he be sit with some friend just now at the tea - table before Mrs. Brown 's tart - shop . they have tea there , twopence a cup ; I hear Mr. Johnson say he have have seventeen cup that make two - and - tenpence what a sight of money for tea ! " 
 " what would you have , Charley ? " ask Theo . 
 " I think I would have cheese - cake , " say Charley , sighing , as his tooth close on a large slice , " and the gentleman whom Mr. Johnson be with , " continue Charley , with his mouth quite full , " be Mr. Richardson who write " 
 " Clarissa ! " cry all the woman in a breath , and run to the window to see their favourite writer . by this time the sun be sink , the star be twinkle overhead , and the footman come and light the candle in the Baroness 's room opposite our spy . 
 Theo and her mother be stand together look from their place of observation . there be a small illumination at Mrs. Brown 's tart- and tea - shop , by which our friend could see one lady get Mr. Richardson 's hat and stick , and another tie a shawl round his neck , after which he walk home . 
 " oh dear I ! he do not look like Grandison ! " cry Theo . 
 " I rather think I wish we have not see he , my dear , " say mamma , who have be describe as a most sentimental woman and eager novel - reader ; and here again they be interrupt by Miss Hetty , who cry : 
 " never mind that little fat man , but look yonder , mamma . " 
 and they look yonder . and they see , in the first place , Mr. Warrington undergo the honour of a presentation to the Countess of Yarmouth , who be still follow by the obsequious peer and prelate with blue riband . and now the Countess graciously sate down to a card - table , the Bishop and the Earl and a fourth person be her partner . and now Mr. Warrington come into the embrasure of the window with a lady whom they recognise as the lady whom they have see for a few minute at Oakhurst . 
 " how much fine he be ! " remark mamma . 
 " how he be improve in his look ! what have he do to himself ? " ask Theo . 
 " look at his grand lace frill and rule ! my dear , he have not get on our shirt any more , " cry the matron . 
 " what be you talk about , girl ? " ask papa , recline on his sofa , where , perhaps , he be doze after the fashion of honest house - father . 
 the girl say how Harry Warrington be in the window , talk with his cousin Lady Maria Esmond . 
 " come away ! " cry papa . " you have no right to be spy the young fellow . down with the curtain , I say ! " 
 and down the curtain go , so that the girl see no more of Madame Bernstein 's guest or doing for that night . 
 I pray you be not angry at my remarking , if only by way of contrast between these two opposite house , that while Madame Bernstein and her guest bishop , dignitary , nobleman , and what not be gamble or talk scandal , or devour champagne and chicken   which I hold to be venial sin , or do honour to her ladyship the king 's favourite , the Countess of Yarmouth - Walmoden , our country friend in their lodging kneel round their table , whither Mr. Brian the coachman come as silently as his creak shoe would let he , whilst Mr. Lambert , stand up , read in a low voice , a prayer that Heaven would lighten their darkness and defend they from the peril of that night , and a supplication that it would grant the request of those two or three gather together . 
 our young folk be up betime on Sunday morning , and array themselves in those smart new dress which be to fascinate the Tunbridge folk , and , with the escort of brother Charley , pace the little town , and the quaint Pantiles , and the pretty common , long ere the company be at breakfast , or the bell have ring to church . it be hester who find out where Harry Warrington 's lodging must be , by remark Mr. Gumbo in an undress , with his lovely hair in curl - paper , draw a pair of red curtain aside , and open a window - sash , whence he thrust his head and inhale the sweet morning breeze . Mr. Gumbo do not happen to see the young people from Oakhurst , though they behold he clearly enough . he lean gracefully from the window ; he wave a large feather brush , with which he condescend to dust the furniture of the apartment within ; he affably engage in conversation with a cherry - cheek milkmaid , who be linger under the casement , and kiss his lily hand to she . Gumbo 's hand sparkle with ring , and his person be decorate with a profusion of jewellery gift , no doubt , of the fair who appreciate the young African . once or twice more before breakfast - time the girl pass near that window . it remain open , but the room behind it be blank . no face of Harry Warrington appear there . neither speak to the other of the subject on which both be brood . Hetty be a little provoke with Charley , who be clamorous about breakfast , and tell he he be always think of eat . in reply to her sarcastic inquiry , he artlessly own he should like another cheese - cake , and good - natured Theo , laughing , say she have a sixpence , and if the cake - shop be open of a Sunday morning Charley should have one . the cake - shop be open : and Theo take out her little purse , net by her dear friend at school , and contain her pocket - piece , her grandmother 's guinea , her slender little store of shilling nay , some copper money at one end ; and she treat Charley to the meal which he love . 
 a great deal of fine company be at church . there be that funny old duchess , and old Madame Bernstein , with Lady Maria at her side ; and Mr. Wolfe , of course , by the side of Miss Lowther , and sing with she out of the same psalm - book ; and Mr. Richardson with a bevy of lady . one of they be Miss Fielding , papa tell they after church , Harry Fielding 's sister . " oh , girl , what good company he be ! and his book be worth a dozen of your milksop Pamelas and Clarissas , Mrs. Lambert : but what woman ever love true humour ? and there be Mr. Johnson sit amongst the charity child . do you see how he turn round to the altar at the Belief , and upset two or three of the scared little urchin in leather breech ? and what a famous sermon Harry 's parson give , do not   he ? a sermon about scandal . how , he touch up some of the old harridan who be seat round ! why be not   Mr. Warrington at church ? it be a shame he be not   at church . " 
 " I really do not remark whether he be there or not , " say Miss Hetty , toss her head up . 
 but Theo , who be all truth , say , " yes , I think of he , and be sorry he be not there ; and so do you think of he , Hetty . " 
 " I do no such thing , miss , " persist Hetty . 
 " then why do you whisper to I it be Harry 's clergyman who preach ? " 
 " to think of Mr. Warrington 's clergyman be not to think of Mr. Warrington . it be a most excellent sermon , certainly , and the child sing most dreadfully out of tune . and there be Lady Maria at the window opposite , smell at the rose ; and that be Mr. Wolfe 's step , I know his great military tramp . right leave right left ! how do you do , Colonel Wolfe ? " 
 " why do you look so glum , James ? " ask Colonel Lambert , good - naturedly . " have the charmer be scold thee , or be thy conscience prick by the sermon . Mr. Sampson , be not   the parson 's name ? a famous preacher , on my word ! " 
 " a pretty preacher , and a pretty practitioner ! " say Mr. Wolfe , with a shrug of his shoulder . 
 " why , I think the discourse do not last ten minute , and madam do not sleep one single wink during the sermon , didst thou , Molly ? " 
 " do you see when the fellow come into church ? " ask the indignant Colonel Wolfe . " he come in at the open door of the common , just in time , and as the psalm be over . " 
 " well , he have be read the service probably to some sick person ; there be many here , " remark Mrs. Lambert . 
 " read the service ! oh , my good Mrs. Lambert ! do you know where I find he ? I go to look for your young scapegrace of a Virginian . " 
 " his own name be a very pretty name , I be sure , " cry out Hetty . " it be not   scapegrace ! it be Henry Esmond Warrington , Esquire . " 
 " Miss Hester , I find the parson in his cassock , and Henry Esmond Warrington , Esquire , in his bedgown , at a quarter before eleven o'clock in the morning , when all the Sunday bell be ring , and they be play over a game of piquet they have have the night before ! " 
 " well , number of good people play at card of a Sunday . the King play at card of a Sunday . " 
 " Hush , my dear ! " 
 " I know he do , " say Hetty , " with that paint person we see yesterday that Countess what - d'you - call - she ? " 
 " I think , my dear Miss Hester , a clergyman have good take to God 's book instead of the Devil 's book on that day and so I take the liberty of tell your parson . " Hetty look as if she think it be a liberty which Mr. Wolfe have take . " and I tell our young friend that I think he have well have be on his way to church than there in his bedgown . " 
 " you would not   have Harry go to church in a dressing - gown and nightcap , Colonel Wolfe ? that would be a pretty sight , indeed ! " again say Hetty , fiercely . 
 " I would have my little girl 's tongue not wag quite so fast , " remark papa , pat the girl 's flush little cheek . 
 " not speak when a friend be attack , and nobody say a word in his favour ? no ; nobody ! " 
 here the two lip of the little mouth close on each other : the whole little frame shake : the child fling a parting look of defiance at Mr. Wolfe , and go out of the room , just in time to close the door , and burst out cry on the stair . 
 Mr. Wolfe look very much discomfited . " I be sure , Aunt Lambert , I do not intend to hurt hester 's feeling . " 
 " no , James , " she say , very kindly the young officer use to call her Aunt Lambert in quite early day and she give he her hand . 
 Mr. Lambert whistle his favourite tune of " over the hill and far away , " with a drum accompaniment perform by his finger on the window . " I say , you must not   whistle on Sunday , papa ! " cry the artless young gown - boy from Grey Friars ; and then suggest that it be three hour from breakfast , and he should like to finish Theo 's cheese - cake . 
 " oh , you greedy child ! " cry Theo . but here , hear a little exclamatory noise outside , she run out of the room , close the door behind she . and we will not pursue she . the noise be that sob which break from Hester 's panting , overloaded heart ; and , though we can not see , I be sure the little maid fling herself on her sister 's neck , and weep upon Theo 's kind bosom . 
 Hetty do not walk out in the afternoon when the family take the air on the common , but have a headache and lie on her bed , where her mother watch she . Charley have discover a comrade from Grey Friars : Mr. Wolfe of course pair off with Miss Lowther : and Theo and her father , take their sober walk in the Sabbath sunshine , find Madame Bernstein bask on a bench under a tree , her niece and nephew in attendance . Harry run up to greet his dear friend : he be radiant with pleasure at behold they the eld lady be most gracious to the Colonel and his wife , who have so kindly welcome their Harry . 
 how noble and handsome he look ! Theo think : she call he by his christian name , as if he be really her brother . " why do we not see you soon to - day , Harry ? " she ask . 
 " I never think you be here , Theo . " 
 " but you might have see we if you wish . " 
 " where ? " ask Harry . 
 " there , sir , " she say , point to the church . and she hold her hand up as if in reproof ; but a sweet kindness beam in her honest face . ah , friendly young reader , wander on the world and struggle with temptation , may you also have one or two pure heart to love and pray for you ! 




 CHAPTER XXXIII . contain a Soliloquy by hester 

 Martin Lambert 's first feeling , upon learn the little secret which his young daughter 's emotion have reveal , be to be angry with the lad who have rob his child 's heart away from he and her family . " a plague upon all scapegrace , English or Indian ! " cry the Colonel to his wife . " I wish this one have break his nose against any doorpost but our . " 
 " perhaps we be to cure he of be a scapegrace , my dear , " say Mrs. Lambert , mildly interposing , " and the fall at our door hath something providential in it . you laugh at I , Mr. Lambert , when I say so before ; but if Heaven do not send the young gentleman to we , who do ? and it may be for the blessing and happiness of we all that he come , too . " 
 " it be hard , Molly ! " groan the Colonel . " we cherish and fondle and rear them : we tend they through sickness and health : we toil and we scheme : we hoard away money in the stocking , and patch our own old coat : if they have   a headache we can not   sleep for thinking of their ailment ; if they have a wish or fancy , we work day and night to compass it , and ' tis darling daddy and dear pappy , and whose father be like our ? and so forth . on Tuesday morning I be king of my house and family . on Tuesday evening Prince Whippersnapper make his appearance , and my reign be over . a whole life be forget and forswear for a pair of blue eye , a pair of lean shank , and a head of yellow hair . " 
 " ' tis write that we woman should leave all to follow our husband . I think our courtship be not very long , dear Martin ! " say the matron , lay her hand on her husband 's arm . 
 " ' tis human nature , and what can you expect of the jade ? " sigh the Colonel . 
 " and I think I do my duty to my husband , though I own I leave my papa for he , " add Mrs. Lambert , softly . 
 " excellent wench ! perdition catch my soul ! but I do love thee , Molly ! " say the good Colonel ; " but , then , mind you , your father never do I ; and if ever I be to have son - in - law " 
 " ever , indeed ! of course my girl be to have husband , Mr. Lambert ! " cry mamma . 
 " well , when they come , I will hate they , madam , as your father do I ; and quite right too , for take his treasure away from he . " 
 " do not   be irreligious and unnatural , Martin Lambert ! I say you be unnatural , sir ! " continue the matron . 
 " Nay , my dear , I have an old tooth in my left jaw , here ; and ' tis natural that the tooth should come out . but when the toothdrawer pull it , ' tis natural that I should feel pain . do you suppose , madam , that I do not   love Hetty well than any tooth in my head ? " ask Mr. Lambert . but no woman be ever averse to the idea of her daughter get a husband , however father revolt against the invasion of the son - in - law . as for mother and grandmother , those good folk be married over again in the marriage of their young one ; and their soul attire themselves in the lace and muslin of twenty - forty year ago ; the postillion 's white ribbon bloom again , and they flutter into the postchaise , and drive away . what woman , however old , have not the bridal favour and raiment stow away , and pack in lavender , in the inmost cupboard of her heart ? 
 " it will be a sad thing , part with she , " continue Mrs. Lambert , with a sigh . 
 " you have settle that point already , molly , " laugh the Colonel . " have I not well go out and order raisin and corinth for the wedding - cake ? " 
 " and then I shall have to leave the house in their charge when I go to she , you know , in Virginia . how many mile be it to Virginia , Martin ? I should think it must be thousand of mile . " 
 " a hundred and seventy - three thousand three hundred and ninety - one and three - quarter , my dear , by the near way , " answer Lambert , gravely ; " that through Prester John 's country . by the other route , through Persia " 
 " oh , give I the one where there be the least of the sea , and your horrid ship , which I can not   bear ! " cry the Colonel 's spouse . " I hope Rachel Esmond and I shall be well friend . she have a very high spirit when we be girl at school . " 
 " have we not well go about the baby - linen , Mrs. Martin Lambert ? " here interpose her wonder husband . now , Mrs. Lambert , I dare say , think there be no matter for wonderment at all , and have remark some very pretty lace cap and bib in Mrs. Bobbinit 's toy - shop . and on that Sunday afternoon , when the discovery be make , and while little Hetty be lie upon her pillow with feverish cheek , closed eye , and a piteous face , her mother look at the child with the most perfect ease of mind , and seem to be rather pleased than otherwise at Hetty 's woe . 
 the girl be not only unhappy , but enrage with herself for have publish her secret . perhaps she have not know it until the sudden emotion acquaint she with her own state of mind ; and now the little maid choose to be as much ashamed as if she have do a wrong , and be discover in it . she be indignant with her own weakness , and break into transport of wrath against herself . she vow she never would forgive herself for submit to such a humiliation . so the young pard , wound by the hunter 's dart , chafe with rage in the forest , be angry with the surprise of the rankle steel in her side , and snarl and bite at her sister - cub , and the leopardess , her spotted mother . 
 Little Hetty tear and gnaw , and growl , so that I should not like to have be her fraternal cub , or her spot dam or sire . " what business have any young woman , " she cry out , " to indulge in any such nonsense ? Mamma , I ought to be whip , and send to bed . I know perfectly well that Mr. Warrington do not care a fig about I . I dare say he like french actress and the common little milliner - girl in the toy - shop well than I . and so he ought , and so they be well than I . why , what a fool I be to burst out cry like a ninny about nothing , and because Mr. Wolfe say Harry play card of a Sunday ! I know he be not clever , like papa . I believe he be stupid I be certain he be stupid : but he be not so stupid as I be . why , of course , I can not   marry he . how be I to go to America , and leave you and Theo ? of course , he like somebody else , at America , or at Tunbridge , or at Jericho , or somewhere . he be a prince in his own country , and can not   think of marry a poor half - pay officer 's daughter , with twopence to her fortune . use not you to tell I how , when I be a baby , I cry and want the moon ? I be a baby now , a most absurd , silly , little baby do not   talk to I , Mrs. Lambert , I be . only there be this to be say , he do not   know anything about it , and I would rather cut my tongue out than tell he . " 
 Dire be the threat with which Hetty menace Theo , in case her sister should betray she . as for the infantile Charley , his mind be altogether set on cheese - cake , he have not remark or be move by Miss Hester 's emotion ; and the parent and the kind sister of course all promise not to reveal the little maid 's secret . 
 " I begin to think it have be good for we to stay at home , " sigh Mrs. Lambert to her husband . 
 " Nay , my dear , " reply the other . " human nature will be human nature ; surely Hetty 's mother tell I herself that she have the beginning of a liking for a certain young curate before she fall over head and ear in love with a certain young officer of Kingsley 's . and as for I , my heart be wound in a dozen place ere Miss Molly Benson take entire possession of it . our son and daughter must follow in the way of their parent before they , I suppose . why , but yesterday , you be scold I for grumble at Miss Het 's precocious fancy . to do the child justice , she disguise her feeling entirely , and I defy Mr. Warrington to know from her behaviour how she be disposed towards he . " 
 " a daughter of mine and your , Martin , " cry the mother , with great dignity , " be not go to fle herself at a gentleman 's head ! " 
 " neither herself nor the teacup , my dear , " answer the Colonel . " little Miss Het treat Mr. Warrington like a vixen . he never come to we , but she box his ear in one fashion or t'other . I protest she be barely civil to he ; but , know what be go on in the young hypocrite 's mind , I be not go to be angry at her rudeness . " 
 " she hath no need to be rude at all , Martin ; and our girl be good enough for any gentleman in England or America . why , if their age suit , should not   they marry after all , sir ? " 
 " why , if he want she , should not   he ask she , my dear ? I be sorry we come . I be for put the horse into the carriage , and turn their head towards home again . " 
 but mamma fondly say , " depend on it , my dear , that these matter be wisely ordain for we . depend upon it , Martin , it be not for nothing that Harry Warrington be bring to our gate in that way ; and that he and our child be thus bring together again . if that marriage have be decree in Heaven , a marriage it will be . " 
 " at what age , Molly , I wonder , do woman begin and leave off match - making ? if our little chit fall in love and fall out again , she will not be the first of her sex , Mrs. Lambert . I wish we be on our way home again , and , if I have my will , would trot off this very night . " 
 " he have promise to drink his tea here to - night . you would not take away our child 's pleasure , Martin ? " ask the mother , softly . 
 in his fashion , the father be not less good - natured . " you know , my dear , " say Lambert , " that if either of them have a fancy to our ear , we would cut they off and serve they in a fricassee . " 
 Mary Lambert laugh at the idea of her pretty little delicate ear be so serve . when her husband be most tender - hearted , his habit be to be most grotesque . when he pull the pretty little delicate ear , behind which the matron 's fine hair be comb back , wherein twinkle a shin line or two of silver , I dare say he do not hurt she much . I dare say she be think of the soft , well - remember time of her own modest youth and sweet courtship . hallowed remembrance of sacred time ! if the sight of youthful love be pleasant to behold , how much more charming the aspect of the affection that have survive year , sorrow , fade beauty perhaps , and life 's doubt , difference , trouble ! 
 in regard of her promise to disguise her feeling for Mr. Warrington in that gentleman 's presence , Miss Hester be well , or bad if you will , than her word . Harry not only come to take tea with his friend , but invite they for the next day to an entertainment at the room , to be give in their special honour . 
 " a dance , and give for we ! " cry Theo . " oh , Harry , how delightful ! I wish we could begin this very minute ! " 
 " why , for a savage Virginian , I declare , Harry Warrington , thou art the most civilised young man possible ! " say the Colonel . " my dear , shall we dance a minuet together ? " 
 " we have do such a thing before , Martin Lambert ! " say the soldier 's fond wife . her husband hum a minuet tune ; whip a plate from the tea - table , and make a preparatory bow and flourish with it as if it be a hat , whilst madam perform her good curtsey . 
 only Hetty , of the party , persist in look glum and displease . " why , child , have you not a word of thank to throw to Mr. Warrington ? " ask Theo of her sister . 
 " I never do care for dance much , " say Hetty . " what be the use of stand up opposite a stupid man , and dance down a room with he ? " 
 " merci du compliment ! " say Mr. Warrington . 
 " I do not   say that you be stupid that be that is , I I only mean country dance , " say Hetty , bite her lip , as she catch her sister 's eye . she remember she have say Harry be stupid , and Theo 's droll humorous glance be her only reminder . 
 but with this Miss Hetty choose to be as angry as if it have be quite a cruel rebuke . " I hate dance there I own it , " she say , with a toss of her head . 
 " Nay , you use to like it well enough , child ! ! " interpose her mother . 
 " that be when she be a child : do not   you see she be grow up to be an old woman ? " remark Hetty 's father . " or perhaps Miss Hester have get the gout ? " 
 " fiddle ! " say Hester , snappishly , drub with her little foot . 
 " what be a dance without a fiddle ? " say imperturbed papa . 
 darkness have come over Harry Warrington 's face . " I come to try my good , and give they pleasure and a dance , " he think , " and the little thing tell I she hat dancing . we do not   practise kindness , or acknowledge hospitality so in our country . no nor speak to our parent so , neither . " I be afraid , in this particular usage have change in the United States during the last hundred year , and that the young folk there be considerably hettified . 
 not content with this , Miss Hester must proceed to make such fun of all the company at the Wells , and especially of Harry 's own immediate pursuit and companion , that the honest lad be still far pain at her behaviour ; and , when he see Mrs. Lambert alone , ask how or in what he have again offend , that hester be so angry with he ? the kind matron feel more than ever well disposed towards the boy , after her daughter 's conduct to he . she would have like to tell the secret which hester hide so fiercely . Theo , too , remonstrate with her sister in private ; but hester would not listen to the subject , and be as angry in her bedroom , when the girl be alone , as she have be in the parlour before her mother 's company . " suppose he hat I ? " say she . " I expect he will . I hate myself , I do , and scorn myself for be such an idiot . how ought he to do otherwise than hate I ? do not   I abuse he , call he goose , all sort of name ? and know he be not clever all the time . I know I have well wit than he have . it be only because he be tall , and have blue eye , and a pretty nose that I like he . what an absurd fool a girl must be to like a man merely because he have a blue nose and hooked eye ! so I be a fool , and I will not   have you say a word to the contrary , Theo ! " 
 now Theo think that her little sister , far from be a fool , be a wonder of wonder , and that if any girl be worthy of any prince in Christendom , Hetty be that spinster . " you be silly sometimes , Hetty , " say Theo , " that be when you speak unkindly to people who mean you well , as you do to Mr. Warrington at tea to - night . when he propose to we his party at the Assembly Rooms , and nothing could be more gallant of he , why do you say you do not   care for music , or dancing , or tea ? you know you love they all ! " 
 " I say it merely to vex myself , Theo , and annoy myself , and whip myself , as I deserve , child . and , besides , how can you expect such an idiot as I be to say anything but idiotic thing ? do you know , it quite please I to see he angry . I think , ah ! now I have hurt his feeling ! now he will say , Hetty Lambert be an odious little set - up , sour - temper vixen . and that will teach he , and you , and mamma , and papa , at any rate , that I be not go to set my cap at Mr. Harry . no ; our papa be ten time as good as he be . I will stay by our papa , and if he ask I to go to Virginia with he to - morrow , I would not , Theo . my sister be worth all the Virginians that ever be make since the world begin . " 
 and here , I suppose , follow osculation between the sister , and mother 's knock come to the door , who have overhear their talk through the wainscot , and call out , " Children , ' tis time to go to sleep . " Theo 's eye close speedily , and she be at rest ; but ob , poor little Hetty ! think of the hour toll one after another , and the child 's eye wide open , as she lie toss and wakeful with the anguish of the new wound ! 
 " it be a judgment upon I , " she say , " for have think and speak scornfully of he . only , why should there be a judgment upon I ? I be only in fun . I know I like he very much all the time : but I think Theo like he too , and I would give up anything for my darling Theo . if she have , no torture should ever have draw a word from I I would have get a rope - ladder to help she to run away with Harry , that I would , or fetch the clergyman to marry they . and then I would have retire alone , and alone , and alone , and take care of papa and mamma , and of the poor in the village , and have read sermon , though I hate them , and would have die without tell a word not a word and I shall die soon , I know I shall . " but when the dawn rise , the little maid be asleep , nestle by her sister , the stain of a tear or two upon her flush downy cheek . 
 Most of we play with edge tool at some period of our life , and cut ourselves accordingly . at first the cut hurt and sting , and down drop the knife , and we cry out like wound little baby as we be . some very very few and unlucky folk at the game cut their head sheer off , or stab themselves mortally , and perish outright , and there be an end of they . but , heaven help we ! many people have finger those ardente sagitta which love sharpen on his whetstone , and be stab , scarred , prick , perforate , tattooed all over with the wound , who recover , and live to be quite lively . wir auch have taste das irdische Glueck ; we also have gelebt and und so weiter . warble your death - song , sweet Thekla ! perish off the face of the earth , poor pulmonary victim , if so minded ! have you survive to a later period of life , my dear , you would have think of a sentimental disappointment without any reference to the undertaker . let we trust there be no present need of a sexton for Miss Hetty . but meanwhile , the very instant she wake , there , tear at her little heart , will that Care be , which have give she a few hour ' respite , melt , no doubt , by her youth and her tear . 




 CHAPTER XXXIV . in which Mr. Warrington treat the Company with Tea and a Ball 

 Generous with his very easily get money , hospitable and cordial to all , our young Virginian , in his capacity of man of fashion , could not do less than treat his country friend to an entertainment at the Assembly Rooms , whither , accord to the custom of the day , he invite almost all the remain company at the Wells . card - table be set in one apartment , for all those who could not spend an evening without the pastime then common to all european society : a supper with champagne in some profusion and bowl of negus be prepare in another chamber : the large assembly - room be set apart for the dance , of which enjoyment Harry Warrington 's guest partook in our ancestor ' homely fashion . I can not fancy that the amusement be especially lively . first , minuet be call , two or three of which be perform by as many couple . the spinster of the high rank in the assembly go out for the minuet , and my Lady Maria Esmond , be an earl 's daughter , and the person of the high rank present   with the exception of Lady Augusta Crutchley , who be lame , Mr. Warrington dance the first minuet with his cousin , acquit himself to the satisfaction of the whole room , and perform much more elegantly than Mr. Wolfe , who stand up with Miss Lowther . have complete the dance with Lady Maria , Mr. Warrington beg Miss Theo to do he the honour of walk the next minuet , and accordingly Miss Theo , blush and look very happy , go through her exercise to the great delight of her parent and the rage of Miss Humpleby , Sir John Humpleby 's daughter , of Liphook , who expect , at least , to have stand up next after my Lady Maria . then , after the minuet , come country dance , the music be perform by a harp , fiddle , and flageolet , perch in a little balcony , and thrum through the evening rather feeble and melancholy tune . take up an old book of music , and play a few of those tune now , and one wonder how people at any time could have find the air otherwise than melancholy . and yet they love and frisk and laugh and court to that sad accompaniment . there be scarce one of the air that have not an amari aliquid , a tang of sadness . perhaps it be because they be old and defunct , and their plaintive echo call out to we from the limbo of the past , whither they have be consign for this century . perhaps they be gay when they be alive ; and our descendant when they hear well , never mind name when they hear the work of certain maestri now popular , will say : Bon Dieu , be this the music which amuse our forefather ? 
 Mr. Warrington have the honour of a duchess 's company at his tea - drink Colonel Lambert 's and Mr. Prior 's heroine , the Duchess of Queensberry . and though the duchess carefully turn she back upon a countess who be present , laugh loudly , glance at the latter over her shoulder , and point at she with her fan , yet almost all the company push , and bow , and cringe , and smile , and back before this countess , scarcely take any notice of her Grace of Queensberry and her joke , and her fan , and her air . now this countess be no other than the Countess of Yarmouth - Walmoden , the lady whom his Majesty George the Second , of Great Britain , France , and Ireland , King , Defender of the Faith , delight to honour . she have meet Harry Warrington in the walk that morning , and have be mighty gracious to the young Virginian . she have tell he they would have a game at card that night ; and purblind old Colonel Blinkinsop , who fancy the invitation have be address to he , have make the profoundest of bow . " Pooh ! pooh ! " say the Countess of England and Hanover , " I do not   mean you . I mean the young Firshinian ! " and everybody congratulate the youth on his good fortune . at night , all the world , in order to show their loyalty , doubtless , throng round my Lady Yarmouth ; my Lord Bamborough be eager to make her parti at quadrille . my Lady Blanche Pendragon , that model of virtue ; Sir Lancelot Quintain , that pattern of knighthood and valour ; Mr. Dean of Ealing , that exemplary divine and preacher ; numerous gentleman , nobleman , general , colonel , matron , and spinster of the high rank , be on the watch for a smile from she , or eager to jump up and join her card - table . Lady Maria wait upon she with meek respect , and Madame de Bernstein treat the hanoverian lady with profound gravity and courtesy . 
 Harry 's bow have be no low than hospitality require ; but , such as it be , Miss Hester choose to be indignant with it . she scarce speak a word to her partner during their dance together ; and when he take she to the supper - room for refreshment she be little more communicative . to enter that room they have to pass by Madame Walmoden 's card - table , who good - naturedly call out to her host as he be pass , and ask he if his " breddy liddle bardner like tanze ? " 
 " I thank your ladyship , I do not   like tanze , and I do not   like card , " say Miss Hester , toss up her head ; and , drop a curtsey like a " cheese , " she strut away from the Countess 's table . 
 Mr. Warrington be very much offended . sarcasm from the young to the old pain he : flippant behaviour towards himself hurt he . courteous in his simple way to all person whom he meet , he expect a like politeness from they . Hetty perfectly well know what offence she be give ; could mark the displeasure redden on her partner 's honest face , with a sidelong glance of her eye ; nevertheless she try to wear her most ingenuous smile ; and , as she come up to the sideboard where the refreshment be set , artlessly say : 
 " what a horrid , vulgar old woman that be ; do not   you think so ? " 
 " what woman ? " ask the young man . 
 " that german woman my Lady Yarmouth to whom all the man be bow and cringe . " 
 " her ladyship have be very kind to I , " say Harry , grimly . " will not   you have some of this custard ? " 
 " and you have be bow to she , too ! you look as if your negus be not nice , " harmlessly continue Miss Hetty . 
 " it be not very good negus , " say Harry , with a gulp . 
 " and the custard be bad too ! I declare ' tis make with bad egg ! " cry Miss Lambert . 
 " I wish , Hester , that the entertainment and the company have be well to your liking , " say poor Harry . 
 " ' tis very unfortunate ; but I dare say you could not help it , " cry the young woman , toss her little curly head . 
 Mr. Warrington groan in spirit , perhaps in body , and clench his fist and his tooth . the little torturer artlessly continue , " you seem disturbed : shall we go to my mamma ? " 
 " yes , let we go to your mamma , " cry Mr. Warrington , with glare eye and a " curse you , why be you always stand in the way ? " to an unlucky waiter . 
 " La ! be that the way you speak in Virginia ? " ask Miss Pertness . 
 " we be rough there sometimes , madam , and can not   help be disturb , " he say slowly , and with a quiver in his whole frame , look down upon she with fire flash out of his eye . Hetty see nothing distinctly afterwards , and until she come to her mother . never have she see Harry look so handsome or so noble . 
 " you look pale , child ! " cry mamma , anxious , like all pavidae matre . 
 " ' tis the cold no , I mean the heat . thank you , Mr. Warrington . " and she make he a faint curtsey , as Harry bow a tremendous bow , and walk elsewhere amongst his guest . he hardly know what be happen at first , so angry be he . 
 he be arouse by another altercation , between his aunt and the Duchess of Queensberry . when the royal favourite pass the Duchess , her grace give her ladyship an awful stare out of eye that be not so bright now as they have be in the young day when they ' set the world on fire ; " turn round with an affected laugh to her neighbour , and shoot at the jolly hanoverian lady a ceaseless fire of giggle and sneer . the Countess pursue her game at card , not know , or not choose , perhaps , to know how her enemy be gibe at she . there have be a feud of many year ' date between their Graces of Queensberry and the family on the throne . 
 " how you all bow down to the idol ! do not   tell I ! you be as bad as the rest , my good Madame Bernstein ! " the Duchess say . " ah , what a true christian country this be ! and how your dear first husband , the Bishop , would have like to see such a sight ! " 
 " forgive I , if I fail quite to understand your grace . " 
 " we be both of we grow old , my good Bernstein , or , perhaps , we will not   understand when we do not   choose to understand . that be the way with we woman , my good young Iroquois . " 
 " your grace remark , that it be a christian country , " say Madame de Bernstein , " and I fail to perceive the point of the remark . " 
 " indeed , my good creature , there be very little point in it ! I mean we be such good Christians , because we be so forgiving . do not   you remember reading , when you be young , or your husband the Bishop reading , when he be in the pulpit , how when a woman amongst the Jews be catch do wrong , the Pharisees be for stone she out of hand ? far from stone such a woman now , look , how fond we be of she ! any man in this room would go round it on his knee if yonder woman bid he . yes , Madame Walmoden , you may look up from your card with your great paint face , and frown with your great paint eyebrow at I . you know I be talk about you ; and intend to go on talk about you , too . I say any man here would go round the room on his knee , if you bade he ! " 
 " I think , madam , I know two or three who would not ! " say Mr. Warrington , with some spirit . 
 " Quick , let I hug they to my heart of heart ! " cry the old duchess . " which be they ? bring them to I , my dear Iroquois ! let we have a game of four of honest man and woman ; that be to say , if we can find a couple more partner , Mr. Warrington ! " 
 " here be we three , " say the Baroness Bernstein , with a forced laugh ; " let we play a dummy . " 
 " pray , madam , where be the third ? " ask the old Duchess , look round . 
 " Madam ! " cry out the other elderly lady , " I leave your grace to boast of your honesty , which I have no doubt be spotless : but I will thank you not to doubt mine before my own relative and child ! " 
 " see how she fire up at a word ! I be sure , my dear creature , you be quite as honest as most of the company , " say the Duchess . 
 " which may not be good enough for her grace the Duchess of Queensberry and Dover , who , to be sure , might have stay away in such a case , but it be the good my nephew could get , madam , and his good he have give you . you look astonish , Harry , my dear and well you may . he be not use to our way , madam . " 
 " Madam , he have find an aunt who can teach he our way , and a great deal more ! " cry the Duchess , rap her fan . 
 " she will teach he to try and make all his guest welcome , old or young , rich or poor . that be the virginian way , be not   it , Harry ? she will tell he , when Catherine Hyde be angry with his old aunt , that they be friend as girl , and ought not to quarrel now they be old woman . and she will not be wrong , will she , Duchess ? " and herewith the one dowager make a superb curtsey to the other , and the battle just impend between they pass away . 
 " egad , it be like Byng and Galissoniere ! " cry Chaplain Sampson , as Harry talk over the night 's transaction with his tutor next morning . " no power on earth , I think , could have prevent those two from go into action ! " 
 " seventy - four at least both of them ! " laugh Harry . 
 " but the Baroness decline the battle , and sail out of fire with inimitable skill . " 
 " why should she be afraid ? I have hear you say my aunt be as witty as any woman alive , and need fear the tongue of no dowager in England . " 
 " Hem ! perhaps she have good reason for be peaceable ! " Sampson know very well what they be , and that poor Bernstein 's reputation be so hopelessly flawed and crack , that any sarcasm level at Madame Walmoden be equally applicable to she . 
 " Sir , " cry Harry , in great amazement , " you do not   mean to say there be anything against the character of my aunt , the Baroness de Bernstein ! " 
 the chaplain look at the young Virginian with such an air of utter wonderment , that the latter see there must be some history against his aunt , and some charge which Sampson do not choose to reveal . " good heaven ! " Harry groan out , " be there two then in the family , who be ? " 
 " which two ? " ask the chaplain . 
 but here Harry stop , blush very red . he remember , and we shall presently have to state , whence he have get his information regard the other family culprit , and bite his lip , and be silent . 
 " Bygones be always unpleasant thing , Mr. Warrington , " say the chaplain ; " and we have well hold our peace regard they . no man or woman can live long in this wicked world of our without some scandal attach to they , and I fear our excellent Baroness have be no more fortunate than her neighbour . we can not escape calumny , my dear young friend ! you have have sad proof enough of that in your brief stay amongst we . but we can have clear conscience , and that be the main point ! " and herewith the chaplain throw his handsome eye upward , and try to look as if his conscience be as white as the ceiling . 
 " have there be anything very wrong , then , about my Aunt Bernstein ? " continue Harry , remember how at home his mother have never speak of the Baroness . 
 " o sancta simplicita ! " the chaplain mutter to himself . " story , my dear sir , much old than your time or mine . story such as be tell about everybody , de I , de te ; you know with what degree of truth in your own case . " 
 " confound the villain ! I should like to hear any scoundrel say a word against the dear old lady , " cry the young gentleman . " why , this world , parson , be full of lie and scandal ! " 
 " and you be just begin to find it out , my dear sir , " cry the clergyman , with his most beatified air . " whose character have not be attack ? my lord 's , your , mine , every one 's . we must bear as well as we can , and pardon to the utmost of our power . " 
 " you may . it be your cloth , you know ; but , by George , I will not ! " cry Mr. Warrington , and again go down the fist with a thump on the table . " let any fellow say a word in my hearing against that dear old creature , and I will pull his nose , as sure as my name be Harry Esmond . how do you do , Colonel Lambert ? you find we late again , sir . I and his reverence keep it up pretty late with some of the young fellow , after the lady go away . I hope the dear lady be well , sir ? " and here Harry rise , greet his friend the Colonel very kindly , who have come to pay he a morning visit , and have enter the room follow by Mr. Gumbo   the latter preferred walking very leisurely about all the affair of life , just as Harry suit the action to the word be tweak the nose of Calumny . 
 " the lady be purely . whose nose be you pull when I come in , Mr. Warrington ? " say the Colonel , laugh . 
 " be not   it a shame , sir ? the parson , here , be tell I that there be villain here who attack the character of my aunt , the Baroness of Bernstein ! " 
 " you do not   mean to say so ! " cry Mr. Lambert . 
 " I tell Mr. Harry that everybody be calumniate ! " say the chaplain , with a clerical intonation ; but , at the same time , he look at Colonel Lambert and wink , as much as to say , " he know nothing keep he in the dark . " 
 the Colonel take the hint . " yes , " say he , " the jaw of slander be for ever wag . witness that story about the dancing - girl , that we all believe against you , Harry Warrington . " 
 " what , all , sir ? " 
 " no , not all . one do not   Hetty do not . you should have hear she stand up for you , Harry , t'other day , when somebody a little bird bring we another story about you ; about a game at card on Sunday morning , when you and a friend of yours might have be well employ . " and here there be a look of mingle humour and reproof at the clergyman . 
 " Faith , I own it , sir ! " say the chaplain . " it be mea culpa , mea maxima no , mea minima culpa , only the rehearsal of an old game at piquet , which we have be talk over . " 
 " and do Miss Hester stand up for I ? " say Harry . 
 " Miss Hester do . but why that wonder look ? " ask the Colonel . 
 " she scold I last night like like anything , " say downright Harry . " I never hear a young girl go on so . she make fun of everybody hit about at young and old so that I could not   help tell she , sir , that in our country , leastway in Virginia   they say the Yankees be very pert , young people do not   speak of their elder so . and , do you know , sir , we have a sort of a quarrel , and I be very glad you have   tell I she speak kindly of I , " say Harry , shake his friend 's hand , a ready boyish emotion glow in his cheek and in his eye . 
 " you will not   come to much hurt if you find no bad enemy than Hester , Mr. Warrington , " say the girl 's father , gravely , look not without a deep thrill of interest at the flushed face and moist eye of his young friend . " be he fond of she ? " think the Colonel . " and how fond ? ' tis evident he know nothing , and Miss Het have be perform some of her trick . he be a fine , honest lad , and God bless he ! " and Colonel Lambert look towards Harry with that manly , friendly kindness which our lucky young Virginian be not unaccustomed to inspire , for he be comely to look at , prone to blush , to kindle , nay , to melt , at a kind story . his laughter be cheery to hear : his eye shine confidently : his voice speak truth . 
 " and the young lady of the minuet ? she distinguish herself to perfection : the whole room admire , " ask the courtly chaplain . " I trust Miss Miss " 
 " Miss Theodosia be perfectly well , and ready to dance at this minute with your reverence , " say her father . " or stay , Chaplain , perhaps you only dance on Sunday ? " the Colonel then turn to Harry again . " you pay your court very neatly to the great lady , Mr. Flatterer . my Lady Yarmouth have be trumpet your praise at the Pump Room . she say she have get a leedel boy in Hannover dat be wery like you , and you be a sharme young man . " 
 " if her ladyship be a queen , people could scarcely be more respectful to she , " say the chaplain . 
 " let we call she a vice - queen , parson , " say the Colonel , with a twinkle of his eye . 
 " her Majesty pocket forty of my guineas at quadrille , " cry Mr. Warrington , with a laugh . 
 " she will play you on the same term another day . the Countess be fond of play , and she win from most people , " say the Colonel , drily . " why do not   you bet her ladyship five thousand on a bishopric , parson ? I have hear of a clergyman who make such a bet , and who lose it , and who pay it , and who get the bishopric . 
 " ah ! who will lend I the five thousand ? will you , sir ? ask the chaplain . 
 " no , sir ! I will not   give she five thousand to be make Commander - in - Chief or Pope of Rome , " say the Colonel , stoutly . " I shall fle no stone at the woman ; but I shall bow no knee to she , as I see a pack of rascal do . no offence I do not   mean you . and I do not   mean Harry Warrington , who be quite right to be civil to she , and to lose his money with good - humour . Harry , I be come to bid thee farewell , my boy . we have have our pleasuring my money be run out , and we must jog back to Oakhurst . will you ever come and see the old place again ? " 
 " now , sir , now ! I will ride back with you ! " cry Harry , eagerly . 
 " why no not now , " say the Colonel , in a hurried manner . " we have not   get room that be , we be we be expect some friend . "   " the Lord forgive I for the lie ! " he mutter .   " but but you will come to we when when Tom be at home yes , when Tom be at home . that will be famous fun and I would have you to know , sir , that my wife and I love you sincerely , sir and so do the girl , however much they scold you . and if you ever be in a scrape and such thing have happen , Mr. Chaplain ! you will please to count upon I . mind that , sir ! " 
 and the Colonel be for take leave of Harry then and there , on the spot , but the young man follow he down the stair , and insist upon say good - bye to his dear lady . 
 instead , however , of proceed immediately to Mr. Lambert 's lodging , the two gentleman take the direction of the common , where , look from Harry 's window , Mr. Sampson see the pair in earnest conversation . first , Lambert smile and look roguish . then , presently , at a farth stage of the talk , he fling up both his hand and perform other gesture indicate surprise and agitation . 
 " the boy be tell he , " think the chaplain . when Mr. Warrington come back in an hour , he find his reverence deep in the composition of a sermon . Harry 's face be grave and melancholy ; he fling down his hat , bury himself in a great chair , and then come from his lip something like an execration . 
 " the young lady be go , and our heart be affect ? " say the chaplain , look up from his manuscript . 
 " heart ! " sneer Harry . 
 " which of the young lady be the conqueror , sir ? I think the young 's eye follow you about at your ball . " 
 " confound the little termagant ! " break out Harry . " what do she mean by be so pert to I ? she treat I as if I be a fool ! " 
 " and no man be , sir , with a woman ! " say the scribe of the sermon . 
 " be not   they , Chaplain ? " and Harry growl out more naughty word expressive of inward disquiet . 
 " by the way , have you hear anything of your lose property ? " ask the chaplain , presently look up from his page . 
 Harry say " no ! " with another word , which I would not print for the world . 
 " I begin to suspect , sir , that there be more money than you like to own in that book . I wish I could find some . " 
 " there be note in it , " say Harry , very gloomily , " and and paper that I be very sorry to lose . what the deuce have come of it ? I have it when we din together . " 
 " I see you put it in your pocket , " cry the chaplain . " I see you take it out and pay at the toy - shop a bill for a gold thimble and workbox for one of your young lady . of course you have ask there , sir ? " 
 " of course I have , " say Mr. Warrington , plunge in melancholy . 
 " Gumbo put you to bed at least , if I remember right . I be so cut myself that I scarce remember anything . can you trust those black fellow , sir ? " 
 " I can trust he with my head . with my head ? " groan out Mr. Warrington , bitterly . , " I can not   trust myself with it . " 
 " ' oh , that a man should put an enemy into his mouth to steal away his brain ! ' " 
 " you may well call it an enemy , Chaplain . hang it , I have a great mind to make a vow never to drink another drop ! a fellow say anything when he be in drink . " 
 the chaplain laugh . " you , sir , " he say , " be close enough ! " and the truth be , that , for the last few day , no amount of wine would unseal Mr. Warrington 's lip , when the artless Sampson by chance touch on the subject of his patron 's loss . 
 " and so the little country nymph be go , or go , sir ? " ask the chaplain . " they be nice , fresh little thing ; but I think the mother be the fine woman of the three . I declare , a woman at five - and - thirty or so be at her prime . what do you say , sir ? " 
 Mr. Warrington look , for a moment , askance at the clergyman . " confound all woman , I say ! " mutter the young misogynist . for which sentiment every well - condition person will surely rebuke he . 




 CHAPTER XXXV . entanglement 

 our good Colonel have , no doubt , take counsel with his good wife , and they have determine to remove their little Hetty as speedily as possible out of the reach of the charmer . in complaint such as that under which the poor little maiden be suppose to be suffer , the remedy of absence and distance often act effectually with man ; but I believe woman be not so easily cure by the alibi treatment . some of they will go away ever so far , and forever so long , and the obstinate disease hang by they , spite of distance or climate . you may whip , abuse , torture , insult they , and still the little delude creature will persist in their fidelity . Nay , if I may speak , after profound and extensive study and observation , there be few well way of secure the faithfulness and admiration of the beautiful partner of our existence than a little judicious ill - treatment , a brisk dose of occasional violence as an alterative , and , for general and wholesome diet , a cooling but pretty constant neglect . at spar interval administer small quantity of love and kindness ; but not every day , or too often , as this medicine , much take , lose its effect . those dear creature who be the most indifferent to their husband , be those who be cloy by too much surfeiting of the sugar - plum and lollipop of Love . I have know a young being , with every wish gratify , yawn in her adore husband 's face , and prefer the conversation and petit soin of the mere booby and idiot ; whilst , on the other hand , I have see Chloe , at whom Strephon have fling his bootjack in the morning , or whom he have curse before the servant at dinner , come creep and fondle to his knee at tea - time , when he be comfortable after his little nap and his good wine ; and pat his head and play he his favourite tune ; and , when old John , the butler , or old Mary , the maid , come in with the bed - candle , look round proudly , as much as to say , now , John , look how good my dear Henry be ! make your game , gentleman , then ! there be the coax , fondling , adore line , when you be henpecke , and Louisa be indifferent , and bore out of her existence . there be the manly , selfish , effectual system , where she answer to the whistle and come in at " Down charge ; " and know her master ; and frisk and fawn about he ; and nuzzle at his knee ; and " lick the hand that be raise " that be raise to do she good , as   I quote from memory   Mr. Pope finely observe . what use the late lament O'Connell to say , over whom a grateful country have raise such a magnificent testimonial ? " hereditary bondsman , " he use to remark , " know ye not , who would be free , themselves must strike the blow ? " of course you must , in political as in domestic circle . so up with your cudgel , my enslave , injured boy ! 
 woman will be pleased with these remark , because they have such a taste for humour and understand irony ; and I should not be surprised if young Grubstreet , who correspond with three penny paper and describe the person and conversation of gentleman whom he meet at his " club , " will say , " I tell you so ! he advocate the thrashing of woman ! he have no nobility of soul ! he have no heart ! " nor have I , my eminent young Grubstreet ! any more than you have ear . dear lady ! I assure you I be only joke in the above remark , I do not advocate the thrashing of your sex at all , and , as you can not   understand the commonest bit of fun , beg leave flatly to tell you , that I consider your sex a hundred time more loving and faithful than our . 
 so , what be the use of Hetty 's parent take her home , if the little maid intend to be just as fond of Harry absent as of Harry present ? why not let she see he before Ball and Dobbin be put to , and say , " good - bye , Harry ! I be very wilful and fractious last night , and you be very kind : but good - bye , Harry ! " she will show no special emotion : she be so ashamed of her secret , that she will not betray it . Harry be too much preoccupied to discover it for himself . he do not know what grief be lie behind Hetty 's glance , or hide under the artifice of her innocent young smile . he have , perhaps , a care of his own . he will part from she calmly , and fancy she be happy to get back to her music and her poultry and her flower - garden . 
 he do not even ride part of the way homeward by the side of his friend 's carriage . he have some other party arrange for , that afternoon , and when he return thence , the good Lamberts be go from Tunbridge Wells . there be their window open , and the card in one of they signify that the apartment be once more to let . a little pass sorrow at the blank aspect of the room lately enliven by countenance so frank and friendly , may have cross the young gentleman 's mind ; but he din at the White Horse at four o'clock , and eat his dinner and call fiercely for his bottle . poor little hester will choke over her tea about the same hour when the lambert arrive to sleep at the house of their friend at Westerham . the young rose will be wan in her cheek in the morning , and there will be black circle round her eye . it be the thunder : the night be hot : she could not sleep : she will be well when she get home again the next day . and home they come . there be the gate where he fall . there be the bed he lie in , the chair in which he use to sit what age seem to have pass ! what a gulf between to - day and yesterday ! who be that little child call her chicken , or water her rose yonder ? be she and that girl the same Hester Lambert ? why , she be ever so much old than Theo now Theo , who have always be so composed , and so clever , and so old for her age . but in a night or two hester have live oh , long , long year ! so have many beside : and poppy and mandragora will never medicine they to the sweet sleep they taste yesterday . 
 Maria Esmond see the lambert cavalcade drive away , and feel a grim relief . she look with hot eye at Harry when he come into his aunt 's card - table , flush with Barbeau 's good wine . he laugh , rattle in reply to his aunt , who ask he which of the girl be his sweetheart ? he gaily say he love they both like sister . he have never see a well gentleman , nor well people , than the lambert . why be Lambert not a general ? he have be a most distinguished officer : his Royal Highness the Duke be very fond of he . Madame Bernstein say that Harry must make interest with Lady Yarmouth for his protege . 
 " Elle ravvole de fous , cher bedid anche ! " say Madame Bernstein , mimic the Countess 's german accent . the Baroness be delighted with her boy 's success . " you carry off the heart of all the old woman , do not   he , Maria ? " she say , with a sneer at her niece , who quiver under the stab . 
 " you be quite right , my dear , not to perceive that she cheat at card , and you play like a grand seigneur , " continue Madame de Bernstein . 
 " do she cheat ? " cry Harry , astonish . " I be sure , ma'am , I see no unfair play . " 
 " no more do I , my dear , but I be sure she cheat . Bah ! every woman cheat , I and Maria include , when we can get a chance . but when you play with the Walmoden , you do not   do wrong to lose in moderation ; and many man cheat in that way . cultivate she . she have take a fancy to your beaux yeux . why should your Excellency not be Governor of Virginia , sir ? you must go and pay your respect to the Duke and his Majesty at Kensington . the Countess of Yarmouth will be your good friend at court . " 
 " why should you not introduce I , aunt ? " ask Harry . 
 the old lady 's rouge cheek grow a little redder . " I be not in favour at Kensington , " she say . " I may have be once ; and there be no face so unwelcome to king as those they wish to forget . all of we want to forget something or somebody . I dare say our ingenu here would like to wipe a sum or two off the slate . wouldst thou not , Harry ? " 
 Harry turn red , too , and so do Maria , and his aunt laugh one of those wicked laugh which be not altogether pleasant to hear . what mean those guilty signal on the cheek of her nephew and niece ? what account be score upon the memory of either , which they be desirous to efface ? I fear Madame Bernstein be right , and that most folk have some ugly reckoning write up on their conscience , which we be glad to be quit of . 
 have Maria know one of the cause of Harry 's disquiet , the middle - aged spinster would have be more unquiet still . for some day he have miss a pocket - book . he have remember it in his possession on that day when he drink so much claret at the White Horse , and Gumbo carry he to bed . he seek for it in the morning , but none of his servant have see it . he have inquire for it at the White Horse , but there be no trace of it . he could not cry the book , and could only make very cautious inquiry respect it . he must not have it know that the book be lose . a pretty condition of mind Lady Maria Esmond would be in , if she know that the outpouring of her heart be in the hand of the public ! the letter contain all sort of disclosure : a hundred family secret be narrate by the artless correspondent : there be ever so much satire and abuse of person with whom she and Mr. Warrington come in contact . there be expostulation about his attention to other lady . there be scorn , scandal , joke , appeal , protest of eternal fidelity ; the usual farrago , dear madam , which you may remember you write to your Edward , when you be engage to he , and before you become Mrs. Jones . would you like those letter to be read by any one else ? do you recollect what you say about the Miss Browns in two or three of those letter , and the unfavourable opinion you express of Mrs. Thompson 's character ? do you happen to recall the word which you use regard Jones himself , whom you subsequently marry   for in consequence of dispute about the settlement your engagement with Edward be break off ? and would you like Mr. J. to see those remark ? you know you would not . then be pleased to withdraw that imputation which you have already cast in your mind upon Lady Maria Esmond . no doubt her letter be very foolish , as most love - letter be , but it do not follow that there be anything wrong in they . they be foolish when write by young folk to one another , and how much more foolish when write by an old man to a young lass , or by an old lass to a young lad ! no wonder Lady Maria should not like her letter to be read . why , the very spelling but that do not   matter so much in her ladyship 's day , and people be just as foolish now , though they spell well . no , it be not the spelling which matter so much ; it be the writing at all . I for one , and for the future , be determined never to speak or write my mind out regard anything or anybody . I intend to say of every woman that she be chaste and handsome ; of every man that he be handsome , clever , and rich ; of every book that it be delightfully interesting ; of Snobmore 's manner that they be gentlemanlike ; of Screwby 's dinner that they be luxurious ; of Jawkins 's conversation that it be lively and amusing ; of Xantippe , that she have a sweet temper ; of Jezebel , that her colour be natural ; of Bluebeard , that he really be most indulgent to his wife , and that very likely they die of bronchitis . what ? a word against the spotless Messalina ? what an unfavourable view of human nature ! what ? King Cheops be not a perfect monarch ? oh , you railer at royalty and slanderer of all that be noble and good ! when this book be conclude , I shall change the jaundiced livery which my book have wear since I begin to lisp in number , have rise - colour coat for they with cherub on the cover , and all the character within shall be perfect angel . 
 meanwhile we be in a society of man and woman , from whose shoulder no sort of wing have sprout as yet , and who , without any manner of doubt , have their little failing . there be Madame Bernstein : she have fall asleep after dinner , and eat and drink too much , those be her ladyship 's little failing . Mr. Harry Warrington have go to play a match at billiard with Count Caramboli : I suspect idleness be his failing . that be what Mr. Chaplain Sampson remark to Lady Maria , as they be talk together in a low tone , so as not to interrupt Aunt Bernstein 's doze in the neighbouring room . 
 " a gentleman of Mr. Warrington 's mean can afford to be idle , " say Lady Maria . " why , sure you love card and billiard yourself , my good Mr. Sampson ? " 
 " I do not   say , madam , my practice be good , only my doctrine be sound , " say Mr. Chaplain with a sigh . " this young gentleman should have some employment . he should appear at court , and enter the service of his country , as befit a man of his station . he should settle down , and choose a woman of a suitable rank as his wife . " Sampson look in her ladyship 's face as he speak . 
 " indeed , my cousin be waste his time , " say Lady Maria , blush slightly . 
 " Mr. Warrington might see his relative of his father 's family , " suggest Mr. Chaplain . 
 " Suffolk country booby drink beer and halloo after fox ! I do not   see anything to be gain by his frequent they , Mr. Sampson ! " 
 " they be of an ancient family , of which the chief have be knight of the shire these hundred year , " say the chaplain . " I have hear Sir Miles hath a daughter of Mr. Harry 's age and beauty , too . " 
 " I know nothing , sir , about Sir Miles Warrington , and his daughter , and his beauty ! " cry Maria , in a fluster . 
 " the Baroness stir no her ladyship be in a sweet sleep , " say the chaplain , in a very soft voice . " I fear , madam , for your ladyship 's cousin , Mr. Warrington . I fear for his youth ; for design person who may get about he ; for extravagance , folly , intrigue even into which he will be lead , and into which everybody will try to tempt he . his lordship , my kind patron , bid I to come and watch over he , and I be here accordingly , as your ladyship knoweth . I know the folly of young man . perhaps I have practise they myself . I own it with a blush , " add Mr. Sampson with much unction not , however , bring the promise blush forward to corroborate the asserted repentance . 
 " between ourselves , I fear Mr. Warrington be in some trouble now , madam , " continue the chaplain , steadily look at Lady Maria . 
 " what , again ? " shriek the lady . 
 " Hush ! your ladyship 's dear invalid ! " whisper the chaplain again point towards Madame Bernstein . " do you think your cousin have any partiality for any any member of Mr. Lambert 's family ? for example , Miss Lambert ? " 
 " there be nothing between he and Miss Lambert , " say Lady Maria . 
 " your ladyship be certain ? " 
 " woman be say to have good eye in such matter , my good Sampson , " say my lady , with an easy air . " I think the little girl seem to be follow he . " 
 " then I be at fault once more , " the frank chaplain say . " Mr. Warrington say of the young lady , that she ought to go back to her doll , and call she a pert , stuck - up little hussy . " 
 " ah ! " sigh Lady Maria , as if relieve by the news . 
 " then , madam , there must be somebody else , " say the chaplain . " have he confide nothing to your ladyship ? " 
 " to I , Mr. Sampson ? what ? where ? how ? " exclaim Maria . 
 " some six day ago , after we have be din at the White Horse , and drink too freely , Mr. Warrington lose a pocket - book contain letter . " 
 " letter ? " gasp Lady Maria . 
 " and probably more money than he like to own , " continue Mr. Sampson , with a grave nod of the head . " he be very much disturb about the book . we have both make cautious inquiry about it . we have gracious power , be your ladyship ill ? " 
 here my Lady Maria give three remarkably shrill scream , and tumble off her chair . 
 " I will see the Prince . I have a right to see he . what be this ? where be I ? what be the matter ? " cry Madame Bernstein , wake up from her sleep . she have be dream of old day , no doubt . the old lady shake in all her limb her face be very much flushed . she star about wildly a moment , and then totter forward on her tortoiseshell cane . " what what be the matter ? " she ask again . " have you kill she , sir ? " 
 " some sudden qualm must have come over her ladyship . shall I cut her lace , madam ? or send for a doctor ? " cry the chaplain , with every look of innocence and alarm . 
 " what have pass between you , sir ? " ask the old lady , fiercely . 
 " I give you my honour , madam , I have do I do not   know what . I but mention that Mr. Warrington have lose a pocket - book contain letter , and my lady swoon , as you see . " 
 Madame Bernstein dash water on her niece 's face . a feeble moan tell presently that the lady be come to herself . 
 the Baroness look sternly after Mr. Sampson , as she send he away on his errand for the doctor . her aunt 's grim countenance be of little comfort to poor Maria when she see it on wake up from her swoon . 
 " what have happen ? " ask the young lady , bewildered and gasp . 
 " H'm ! you know well what have happen , madam , I suppose . what hath happen before in our family ? " cry the old Baroness , glare at her niece with savage eye . 
 " ah , yes ! the letter have be lose ach lieber Himmel ! " and Maria , as she would sometimes do , when much move , begin to speak in the language of her mother . 
 " yes ! the seal have be break , and the letter have be lose , ' tis the old story of the Esmonds , " cry the elder , bitterly . 
 " Seal break , letter lose ? what do you mean , aunt ? " ask Maria , faintly . 
 " I mean that my mother be the only honest woman that ever enter the family ! " cry the Baroness , stamp her foot . " and she be a parson 's daughter of no family in particular , or she would have go wrong , too . good heavens ! be it decreed that we be all to be ... ? " 
 " to be what , madam ? " cry Maria . 
 " to be what my Lady Queensberry say we be last night . to be what we be ! you know the word for it ! " cry the indignant old woman . " I say , what have come to the whole race ? your father 's mother be an honest woman , Maria . why do I leave she ? why could not   you remain so ? " 
 " Madam ! " exclaim Maria , " I declare , before Heaven , I be as " 
 " bah ! do not   madam I ! do not   call heaven to witness there be nobody by ! and if you swear to your innocence till the rest of your tooth drop out of your mouth , my Lady Maria Esmond , I would not believe you ! " 
 " ah ! it be you tell he ! " gasp Maria . she recognise an arrow out of her aunt 's quiver . 
 " I see some folly go on between you and the boy , and I tell he that you be as old as his mother . yes , I do ! do you suppose I be go to let Henry Esmond 's boy fle himself and his wealth away upon such a battered old rock as you ? the boy shall not   be rob and cheat in our family . not a shilling of mine shall any of you have if he come to any harm amongst you . 
 " ah ! you tell he ! " cry Maria , with a sudden burst of rebellion . " well , then ! I would have you to know that I do not   care a penny , madam , for your paltry money ! I have Mr. Harry Warrington 's word yes , and his letter and I know he will die rather than break it . " 
 " he will die if he keep it ! "   Maria shrug her shoulder . 
 " but you do not   care for that you have   no more heart " 
 " than my father 's sister , madam ! " cry Maria again . the young woman , ordinarily submissive , have turn upon here persecutor . 
 " ah ! why do not I marry an honest man ? " say the of lady , shake her head sadly . " Henry Esmond be noble and good , and perhaps might have make I so . but no , no we have all get the taint in we all ! you do not   mean to sacrifice this boy , Maria ? " 
 " Madame ma tante , do you take I for a fool at my age ? " ask Maria . 
 " set he free ! I will give you five thousand pound in my in my will , Maria . I will , on my honour ! " 
 " when you be young , and you like Colonel Esmond , you throw he aside for an earl , and the earl for a duke ? " 
 " yes . " 
 " eh ! Bon sing ne peut mentir ! I have no money , I have no friend . my father be a spendthrift , my brother be a beggar . I have Mr. Warrington 's word , and I know , madam , he will keep it . and that be what I tell your ladyship ! " cry Lady Maria with a wave of her hand . " suppose my letter be publish to all the world to - morrow ? apre ? I know they contain thing I would as lieve not tell . thing not about I alone . comment ! do you suppose there be no story but mine in the family ? it be not my letter that I be afraid of , so long as I have his , madam . yes , his and his word , and I trust they both . " 
 " I will send to my merchant , and give you the money now , Maria , " plead the old lady . 
 " no , I shall have my pretty Harry , and ten time five thousand pound ! " cry Maria . 
 " not till his mother 's death , madam , who be just your age ! " 
 " we can afford to wait , aunt . at my age , as you say , I be not so eager as young chit for a husband . " 
 " but to wait my sister 's death , at least , be a drawback ? " 
 " offer I ten thousand pound , Madam Tusher , and then we will see ! " cry Maria . 
 " I have not so much money in the world , Maria , " say the old lady . 
 " then , madam , let I make what I can for myself ! " say Maria . 
 " ah , if he hear you ? "
