 " Apres ? I have his word . I know he will keep it . I can afford to wait , madam , " and she fling out of the room , just as the chaplain return . it be Madame Bernstein who want cordial now . she be immensely move and shock by the news which have be thus suddenly bring to she . 




 CHAPTER XXXVI . which seem to mean Mischief 

 though she have clearly have the bad of the battle describe in the last chapter , the Baroness Bernstein , when she next meet her niece show no rancour or anger . Delilah ! well , they can be no good to I , and I trust you may get they . when will you go ? the soon the well , you say ? we be woman of the world , Maria . we only call name when we be in a passion . we do not   want each other 's company ; and we part on good term . shall we go to my Lady Yarmouth 's ?  that night , on their return from the Countess 's drum , Mrs. Brett , Madame Bernstein 's maid , present herself to my Lady Maria 's call , when that lady ring her hand - bell upon retire to her room .  but on the night of the rum - punch , though Mr. Case find Gumbo and Mrs. Betty whispering in the doorway , in the cool breeze , and Gumbo would have turn pale with fear have he be able so to do , no one could be more gracious than Mr. Case . it be he who propose the bowl of punch , which be brew and drunk in Mrs. Betty 's room , and which Gumbo concoct with exquisite skill . he compliment Gumbo on his music . though a sober man ordinarily , he insist upon more and more drinking , until poor Mrs. Betty be reduce to the state which occasion her ladyship 's just censure .

 Harry relish the notion of a rattle journey to London , and a day or two of sport there . he promise that his pistol be good , and that he would hand the diamond over in safety to the banker 's strong - room . would he occupy his aunt 's London house ? no , that would be a dreary lodge with only a housemaid and a groom in charge of it . he would go to the Star and Garter in Pall Mall , or to an inn in Covent Garden . " 
 but , of course , this sorrow be not to be perpetual ; and we can fancy Mr. Warrington set out on his London journey eagerly enough , and very gay and happy , if it must be own , to be rid of his elderly attachment . yes . there be no help for it . at Castlewood , on one unlucky evening , he have make an offer of his heart and himself to his mature cousin , and she have accept the foolish lad 's offer . but the marriage now be out of the question . he must consult his mother . she be the mistress for life of the virginian property . of course she would refuse her consent to such a union . the thought of it be defer to a late period . meanwhile , it hang like a weight round the young man 's neck , and cause he no small remorse and disquiet . 
 no wonder that his spirit rise more gaily as he come near London , and that he look with delight from his postchaise window upon the city as he advance towards it . no highwayman stop our traveller on Blackheath . yonder be the gleam dome of Greenwich , canopy with wood . there be the famous Thames , with its countless shipping ; there actually be the Tower of London . " look , Gumbo ! there be the Tower ! "

 Whither should they go first ? first , Harry be for go to see the place where his grandfather and Lord Castlewood have fight a duel fifty - six year ago , in Leicester Field .  the chamberlain at the Bedford show Mr. Warrington to his room , bow before he with delightful obsequiousness , for Gumbo have already trumpet his master 's greatness , and Mr. Draper 's clerk announce that the new - comer be a " high fellar .  who be that shabby - look big man Mr. Warrington bow to as they go out after dinner for a walk in the garden ? that be Mr. Johnson , an author , whom he have meet at Tunbridge Wells .  the next morning Mr. Warrington , array in his brocade bedgown , take his breakfast , read the newspaper , and enjoy his ease in his inn . he read in the paper news from his own country . and when he see the word , Williamsburg , Virginia , June 7th , his eye grow dim somehow . he have just have letter by that packet of June 7th , but his mother do not tell how " a great number of the principal gentry of the colony have associate themselves under the command of the Honourable Peyton Randolph , Esquire , to march to the relief of their distressed fellow - subject , and revenge the cruelty of the French and their barbarous ally . they be in a uniform : viz . , a plain blue frock , nanquin or brown waistcoat and breech , and plain hat . they be arm each with a light firelock , a brace of pistol , and a cut sword . " 
 " ah , why be not   we there , Gumbo ? " cry out Harry . 
 " why be not   we dar ? " shout Gumbo .

 and now , read that the fine mare in England , and a pair of very genteel bay gelding , be to be sell at the Bull Inn , the low end of Hatton Garden , Harry determine to go and look at the animal , and inquire his way to the place . he then and there buy the genteel bay gelding , and pay for they with easy generosity . he never say what he do on that day , be shy of appear like a stranger ; but it be believe that he take a coach and go to Westminster Abbey , from which he bid the coachman drive he to the Tower , then to Mrs. Salmon 's Waxwork , then to Hyde Park and Kensington Palace ; then he have give order to go to the Royal Exchange , but catch a glimpse of Covent Garden , on his way to the Exchange , he bid Jehu take he to his inn , and cut short his enumeration of place to which he have be , by fling the fellow a guinea .

 as he issue forth the next day , the bell of St. Paul 's , Covent Garden , be ring for morning prayer , and remind he that friend Sampson be go to preach his sermon . Harry smile . he have begin to have a shrewd and just opinion of the value of Mr. Sampson 's sermon . 




 CHAPTER XXXVII . in which various Matches be fight 

 read in the London Advertiser , which be serve to his worship with his breakfast , an invitation to all lover of manly british sport to come and witness a trial of skill between the great champion Sutton and Figg , Mr. Warrington determine upon attend these performance , and accordingly proceed to the Wooden House , in Marybone Fields , drive thither the pair of horse which he have purchase on the previous day . the young charioteer do not know the road very well , and veer and tack very much more than be needful upon his journey from Covent Garden , lose himself in the green lane behind Mr. Whitfield 's round Tabernacle of Tottenham Road , and the field in the midst of which Middlesex Hospital stand . he reach his destination at length , however , and find no small company assemble to witness the valorous achievement of the two champion . 
 a crowd of London blackguard be gather round the door of this temple of british valour ; together with the horse and equipage of a few person of fashion , who come , like Mr. Warrington , to patronise the sport . a variety of beggar and cripple hustle round the young gentleman , and whine to he for charity . shoeblack - boy tumble over each other for the privilege of black his honour 's boot ; nosegay - woman and fly fruiterer ply Mr. Gumbo with their ware ; pieman , pad , tramp , stroller of every variety , hang round the battle - ground . a flag be fly upon the building ; and , on to the stage in front , accompany by a drummer and a horn - blower , a manager repeatedly issue to announce to the crowd that the noble english sport be just about to begin . 
 Mr. Warrington pay his money , and be accommodate with a seat in a gallery command a perfect view of the platform whereon the sport be perform ; Mr. Gumbo take his seat in the amphitheatre below ; or , when tired , issue forth into the outer world to drink a pot of beer , or play a game at card with his brother - lacquey , and the gentleman 's coachman on the box of the carriage wait without . lacquey , livery , footmen the old society be encumber with a prodigious quantity of these . gentleman or woman could scarce move without one , sometimes two or three , vassal in attendance . every theatre have its footman 's gallery : an army of the liveried race hustle around every chapel - door : they swarm in anteroom : they sprawl in hall and on landing : they guzzle , devour , debauch , cheat , play card , bully visitor for vail : that noble old race of footman be well - nigh go . a few thousand of they may still be leave among we . Grand , tall , beautiful , melancholy , we still behold they on levee day , with their nosegay and their buckle , their plush and their powder . so have I see in America specimen , nay camp and village , of Red Indians . but the race be doom . the fatal decree have go forth , and Uncas with his tomahawk and eagle 's plume , and Jeames with his cocked hat and long cane , be pass out of the world where they once walk in glory . 
 before the principal combatant make their appearance , minor warrior and exercise be exhibit . a boxing - match come off , but neither of the man be very game or severely punish , so that Mr. Warrington and the rest of the spectator have but little pleasure out of that encounter . then ensue some cudgel - playing ; but the head break be of so little note , and the wound give so trifling and unsatisfactory , that no wonder the company begin to hiss , grumble , and show other sign of discontent . " the master , the master ! " shout the people , whereupon those famous champion at length thought fit to appear . 
 the first who walk up the step to the stage be the intrepid Sutton , sword in hand , who salute the company with his warlike weapon , make an especial bow and salute to a private box or gallery in which sate a stout gentleman , who be seemingly a person of importance . Sutton be speedily follow by the famous Figg , to whom the stout gentleman wave a hand of approbation . both man be in their shirt , their head be shaven clean , but bear the crack and scar of many former glorious battle . on his burly sword - arm , each intrepid champion wear an " armiger , " or ribbon of his colour . and now the gladiator shake hand , and , as a contemporary poet say : " the word it be bilboe . "   the antiquarian reader know the pleasant poem in the sixth volume of Dodsley 's Collection , in which the above combat be describe . 
 at the commencement of the combat the great Figg deal a blow so tremendous at his opponent , that have it encounter the other 's honest head , that comely noddle would have be shorn off as clean as the carving - knife chop the carrot . but Sutton receive his adversary 's blade on his own sword , whilst Figg 's blow be deliver so mightily that the weapon brake in his hand , less constant than the heart of he who wield it . other sword be now deliver to the warrior . the first blood draw spout from the pant side of Figg amidst a yell of delight from Sutton 's supporter ; but the veteran appeal to his audience , and especially , as it seem , to the stout individual in the private gallery , show that his sword break in the previous encounter have cause the wound . 
 Whilst the parley occasion by this incident be go on , Mr. Warrington see a gentleman in a riding - frock and plain scratch - wig enter the box devote to the stout personage , and recognise with pleasure his Tunbridge Wells friend , my Lord of March and Ruglen . Lord March , who be by no means prodigal of politeness seem to show singular deference to the stout gentleman , and Harry remark how his lordship receive , with a profound bow , some bank - bill which the other take out from a pocket - book and hand to he . Whilst thus engage , Lord March spy out our Virginian , and , his interview with the stout personage finish , my lord come over to Harry 's gallery and warmly greet his young friend . they sit and beheld the combat wag with various success , but with immense skill and valour on both side . after the warrior have sufficiently fight with sword , they fall to with the quarter - staff , and the result of this long and delightful battle be , that victory remain with her ancient champion Figg . 
 whilst the warrior be at battle , a thunderstorm have break over the building , and Mr. Warrington gladly enough accept a seat in my Lord March 's chariot , leave his own phaeton to be drive home by his groom . Harry be in great delectation with the noble sight he have witness : be pronounce this indeed to be something like sport , and of the good he have see since his arrival in England : and , as usual , associate any pleasure which he enjoy with the desire that the dear companion of his boyhood should share the amusement in common with he , he begin by sigh out , " I wish ... " then he stop . " no , I do not , " say he . 
 " what do you wish and what do not   you wish ? " ask Lord March . 
 " I be think , my lord , of my eld brother , and wish he have be with I . we have promise to have our sport together at home , you see ; and many be the time we talk of it . but he would not   have like this rough sort of sport , and do not   care for fighting , though he be the brave lad alive . " 
 " oh ! he be the brave lad alive , be he ? " ask my lord , loll on his cushion , and eye his virginian friend with some curiosity . 
 " you should have see he in a quarrel with a very gallant officer , our friend an absurd affair , but it be hard to keep George off he . I never see a fellow so cool , nor more savage and determine , God help I . ah ! I wish for the honour of the country , you know , that he could have come here instead of I , and show you a real virginian gentleman . " 
 " Nay , sir , you will do very well . what be this I hear of Lady Yarmouth take you into favour ? " say the amused nobleman . 
 " I will do as well as another . I can ride , and , I think , I can shoot well than George ; but then my brother have the head , sir , the head ! " say Harry , tap his own honest skull . " why , I give you my word , my lord , that he have read almost every book that be ever write ; could play both on the fiddle and harpsichord , could compose poetry and sermon most elegant . what can I do ? I be only good to ride and play at card , and drink Burgundy . " and the penitent hang down his head . " but they I can do as well as most fellow , you see . in fact , my lord , I will back myself , " he resume , to the other 's great amusement . 
 Lord March relish the young man 's naivete , as the jade voluptuary still to the end always can relish the juicy wholesome mutton - chop . " by Gad , Mr. Warrington , " say he , " you ought to be take to Exeter ' Change , and put in a show . " 
 " and for why ? " 
 " a gentleman from Virginia who have lose his eld brother and absolutely regret he . the breed be not   know in this country . upon my honour and conscience , I believe that you would like to have he back again . " 
 " believe ! " cry the Virginian , grow red in the face . 
 " that be , you believe you believe you would like he back again . but depend on it you would not . ' tis not in human nature , sir ; not as I read it , at least . here be some fine house we be come to . that at the corner be Sir Richard Littleton 's , that great one be my Lord Bingley 's . ' tis a pity they do nothing well with this great empty space of Cavendish Square than fence it with these unsightly board . by George ! I do not   know where the town 's running . there 's Montagu House make into a confound Don Saltero 's museum , with book and stuff bird and rhinoceros . they have actually run a cursed cut New Road they call it at the back of Bedford House Gardens , and spoil the Duke 's comfort , though , I guess , they will console he in the pocket . I do not   know where the town will stop . shall we go down Tyburn Road and the Park , or through Swallow Street , and into the habitable quarter of the town ? we can dine at Pall Mall , or , if you like , with you ; and we can spend the evening as you like with the Queen of Spades , or ... " 
 " with the Queen of Spades , if your lordship please , " say Mr. Warrington , blushing . so the equipage drive to his hotel in Covent Garden , where the landlord come forward with his usual obsequiousness , and recognise my Lord of March and Ruglen , bow his wig on to my lord 's shoe in his humble welcome to his lordship . a rich young english peer in the reign of George the Second ; a wealthy patrician in the reign of Augustus ; which would you rather have be ? there be a question for any young gentleman 's debating - club of the present day . 
 the good english dinner which could be produce , of course , be at the service of the young Virginian and his noble friend . after dinner come wine in plenty , and of quality good enough even for the epicurean earl . over the wine there be talk of go to see the firework at Vauxhall , or else of card . Harry , who have never see a firework beyond an exhibition of a dozen squibs at Williamsburg on the fifth of November   which he think a sublime display , would have like the Vauxhall , but yield to his guest 's preference for piquet ; and they be very soon absorb in that game . 
 Harry begin by win as usual ; but , in the course of a half - hour , the luck turn and favour my Lord March , who be at first very surly when Mr. Draper , Mr. Warrington 's man of business , come bow into the room , where he accept Harry 's invitation to sit and drink . Mr. Warrington always ask everybody to sit and drink , and partake of his good . have he a crust , he would divide it ; have he a haunch , he would share it ; have he a jug of water , he would drink about with a kindly spirit ; have he a bottle of Burgundy , it be gaily drunk with a thirsty friend . and do not   fancy the virtue be common . you read of it in book , my dear sir , and fancy that you have it yourself because you give six dinner of twenty people and pay your acquaintance all round ; but the welcome , the friendly spirit , the kindly heart ? believe I , these be rare quality in our selfish world . we may bring they with we from the country when we be young , but they mostly wither after transplantation , and droop and perish in the stifle London air . 
 Draper do not care for wine very much , but it delight the lawyer to be in the company of a great man . he protest that he like nothing well than to see piquet play by two consummate player and man of fashion ; and , take a seat , undismaye by the sidelong scowl of his lordship , survey the game between the gentleman . Harry be not near a match for the experienced player of the London club . to - night , too , Lord March hold well card to aid his skill . 
 what their stake be be no business of Mr. Draper 's . the gentleman say they would play for shilling , and afterwards count up their gain and loss , with scarce any talking , and that in an undertone . a bow on both side , a perfectly grave and polite manner on the part of each , and the game go on . 
 but it be destine to a second interruption , which bring an execration from Lord March 's lip . First be hear a scuffling without then a whispering then an outcry as of a woman in tear , and then , finally , a female rush into the room , and produce that explosion of naughty language from Lord March . 
 " I wish your woman would take some other time for come , confound them , " say my lord , lay his card down in a pet . 
 " what , Mrs. Betty ! " cry Harry . 
 indeed it be no other than Mrs. Betty , Lady Maria 's maid ; and Gumbo stand behind she , his fine countenance beslobbere with tear . 
 " what have happen ? " ask Mr. Warrington , in no little perturbation of spirit . " the Baroness be well ? " 
 " help ! help ! sir , your honour ! " ejaculate Mrs. Betty , and proceed to fall on her knee . 
 " help whom ? " 
 a howl ensue from Gumbo . 
 " Gumbo ! you scoundrel ! have anything happen between Mrs. Betty and you ? " ask the black 's master . 
 Mr. Gumbo step back with great dignity , lay his hand on his heart , and say , " no , sir ; nothing hab happen ' twix ' this lady and I . " 
 " it be my mistress , sir , " cry Betty . " help ! help ! here be the letter she have write , sir ! they have go and take she , sir ! " 
 " be it only that old Molly Esmond ? she be know to be over head and heel in debt ! dry your eye in the next room , Mrs. Betty , and let I and Mr. Warrington go on with our game , " say my lord , take up his card . 
 " help ! help she ! " cry Betty again . " oh , Mr. Harry ! you will not   be a - go on with your card , when my lady call out to you to come and help she ! your honour use to come quick enough when my lady use to send I to fetch you at Castlewood ! " 
 " confound you ! can not   you hold your tongue ? " say my lord , with more choice word and oath . 
 but Betty would not cease weeping , and it be decree that Lord March be to cease win for that night . Mr. Warrington rise from his seat , and make for the bell , say : 
 " my dear lord , the game must be over for to - night . my relative write to I in great distress , and I be bind to go to she . " 
 " curse she ! why could not   she wait till to - morrow ? " cry my lord , testily . 
 Mr. Warrington order a postchaise instantly . his own horse would take he to Bromley . 
 " bet you , you do not   do it within the hour ! bet you , you do not   do it within five quarter of an hour ! bet you four to one or I will take your bet , which you please that you be not rob on Blackheath ! bet you , you be not at Tunbridge Wells before midnight ! " cry Lord March . 
 " do ! " say Mr. Warrington . and my lord carefully note down the term of the four wager in his pocket - book . 
 Lady Maria 's letter run as follow : 
 " MY DEAR COUSIN I be fall into a trapp , which I perceive the machination of villian . I be a prisner . Betty will tell you all . ah , my Henrico ! come to the resque of your MOLLY . " 
 in half an hour after the receipt of this missive , Mr. Warrington be in his postchaise and gallop over Westminster Bridge on the road to succour his kinswoman . 




 CHAPTER XXXVIII . Sampson and the Philistines 

 my happy chance in early life lead I to become intimate with a respectable person who be bear in a certain island , which be pronounce to be the first gem of the ocean by , no doubt , impartial judge of maritime jewellery . the story which that person impart to I regard his relative who inhabit the gem above - mention , be such as use to make my young blood curdle with horror to think there should be so much wickedness in the world . every crime which you can think of ; the entire ten Commandments break in a general smash ; such roguery and knavery as no storyteller could invent ; such murder and robbery as Thurtell or turpin scarce ever perpetrate ; be by my informant accurately remember , and freely relate , respect his near kindred , to any one who choose to hear he . it be a wonder how any of the family still live out of the hulk . I brother Tim have bring his fawther 's gree hair with sorrow to the greeve ; I brother Mick have rob the par'sh church repaytedly ; I sisther Annamaroia have jilt the Captain and run off with the ensign , forge her grandmother 's will , and steal the spoon , which Larry the knife - boy be hang for . the family of Atreus be as nothing compare to the race of O'What - d'ye - call-'em , from which my friend spring ; but no power on earth would , of course , induce I to name the country whence he come . 
 how great then use to be my naif astonishment to find these murderer , rogue , parricide , habitual forger of bill of exchange , and so forth , every now and then write to each other as " my dear brother , " " my dear sister , " and for month at a time live on the most amicable term ! with hand reek with the blood of his murder parent , Tim would mix a screech tumbler , and give Maria a glass from it . with lip black with the perjury he have swear in court respect his grandmother 's abstract testament , or the murder of his poor brother Thady 's helpless orphan , Mick would kiss his sister Julia 's bonny cheek , and they would have a jolly night , and cry as they talk about old time , and the dear old Castle what - d'ye - call-'em , where they be bear , and the fight Onetyoneth be quarthere there , and the major proposing for Cyaroloine , and the tomb of their seented mother   who have chayte they out of the propertee . Heaven bless her soul ! they use to weep and kiss so profusely at meeting and parting , that it be touch to behold they . at the sight of their embrace one forget those painful little story , and those repeat previous assurance that , do they tell all , they could hang each other all round . 
 what can there be fine than forgiveness ? what more rational than , after call a man by every bad name under the sun , to apologise , regret hasty expression , and so forth , withdraw the decanter   say   which you have fling at your enemy 's head , and be friend as before ? some folk possess this admirable , this angellike gift of forgiveness . it be beautiful , for instance , to see our two lady at Tunbridge Wells forgive one another , smile , joking , fondle almost in spite of the hard word of yesterday yes , and forget bygone , though they could not   help remember they perfectly well . I wonder , can you and I do as much ? let we strive , my friend , to acquire this pacable , christian spirit . my belief be that you may learn to forgive bad language employ to you ; but , then , you must have a deal of practice , and be accustom to hear and use it . you embrace after a quarrel and mutual bad language . Heaven bless we ! bad word be nothing when one be accustomed to they , and scarce need ruffle the temper on either side . 
 so the aunt and niece play card very amicably together , and drink to each other 's health , and each take a wing of the chicken , and pull a bone of the merry - thought , and   in conversation   scratch their neighbour ' , not each other 's , eye out . thus we have read how the Peninsular warrior , when the bugle sing truce , fraternise and exchange tobacco - pouch and wine , ready to seize their firelock and knock each other 's head off when the truce be over ; and thus our old soldier , skilful in war , but know the charm of a quiet life , lay their weapon down for the nonce , and hob - and - nobbed gaily together . of course , whilst drink with Jack Frenchman , you have your piece handy to blow his brain out if he make a hostile move : but , meanwhile , it be a votre sante , mon camarade ! here be to you , mounseer ! and everything be as pleasant as possible . regard Aunt Bernstein 's threatened gout ? the twinge have go off . Maria be so glad ! Maria 's fainting fit ? she have no return of they . a slight recurrence last night . the Baroness be so sorry ! her niece must see the good doctor , take everything to fortify she , continue to take the steel , even after she leave Tunbridge . how kind of Aunt Bernstein to offer to send some of the bottled water after she ! suppose Madame Bernstein say in confidence to her own woman , " fainting fit ! pooh ! epilepsy ! inherit from that horrible scrofulous german mother ! " what mean have we of know the private conversation of the old lady and her attendant ? suppose Lady Maria order Mrs. Betty , her ladyship 's maid , to taste every glass of medicinal water , first declare that her aunt be capable of poison she ? very likely such conversation take place . these be but precaution these be the firelock which our old soldier have at their side , load and cocked , but at present lying quiet on the grass . 
 have Harry 's bond in her pocket , the veteran Maria do not choose to press for payment . she know the world too well for that . he be bind to she , but she give he plenty of day - rule , and leave of absence on parole . it be not her object needlessly to chafe and anger her young slave . she know the difference of age , and that Harry must have his pleasure and diversion . " take your ease and amusement , cousin , " say Lady Maria . " Frisk about , pretty little mousekin , " say grey Grimalkin , purr in the corner , and keep watch with her green eye . about all that Harry be to see and do on his first visit to London , his female relative have of course talk and joke . both of the lady know perfectly what be a young gentleman 's ordinary amusement in those day , and speak of they with the frankness which characterise those easy time . 
 our wily Calypso console herself , then , perfectly , in the absence of her young wanderer , and take any diversion which come to hand . Mr. Jack Morris , the gentleman whom we have mention as rejoice in the company of Lord March and Mr. Warrington , be one of these diversion . to live with title personage be the delight of Jack Morris 's life ; and to lose money at card to an earl 's daughter be almost a pleasure to he . now , the Lady Maria Esmond be an earl 's daughter who be very glad to win money . she obtain permission to take Mr. Morris to the Countess of Yarmouth 's assembly , and play card with he and so everybody be pleased . 
 thus the first eight - and - forty hour after Mr. Warrington 's departure pass pretty cheerily at Tunbridge Wells , and Friday arrive , when the sermon be to be deliver which we have see Mr. Sampson prepare . the company at the Wells be ready enough to listen to it . Sampson have a reputation for be a most amusing and eloquent preacher ; and if there be no breakfast , conjurer , dancing bear , concert go on , the good Wells folk would put up with a sermon . he know Lady Yarmouth be come , and what a power she have in the giving of living and the dispensing of bishopric , the Defender of the Faith of that day have a remarkable confidence in her ladyship 's opinion upon these matter ; and so we may be sure that Mr. Sampson prepare his very good discourse for her hearing . when the Great Man be at home at the Castle , and walk over to the little country church , in the park , bring the Duke , the Marquis , and a couple of Cabinet Ministers with he , have it ever be your lot to sit among the congregation , and watch Mr. Trotter the curate and his sermon ? he look anxiously at the Great Pew ; he falter as he give out his text , and think , " ah ! perhaps his lordship may give I a living ! " Mrs. Trotter and the girl look anxiously at the Great Pew too , and watch the effect of papa 's discourse the well - know favourite discourse upon the big - wig assemble . Papa 's first nervousness be over : his noble voice clear , warm to his sermon : he kindle : he take his pocket - handkerchief out : he be come to that exquisite passage which have make they all cry at the parsonage : he have begin it ! ah ! what be that hum noise , which fill the edifice , and cause hob - nail Melibaeus to grin at smock - frock Tityrus ? it be the right honourable Lord Naseby snore in the pew by the fire ! and poor Trotter 's visionary mitre disappear with the music . 
 Sampson be the domestic chaplain of Madame Bernstein 's nephew . the two lady of the Esmond family patronise the preacher . on the day of the sermon , the Baroness have a little breakfast in his honour , at which Sampson make his appearance , rosy and handsome , with a fresh - flower wig , and a smart , rustle , new cassock , which he have on credit from some church - admire mercer at the Wells . by the side of his patroness , their ladyship ' lacquey walk behind they with their great gilt prayer - book , Mr. Sampson march from breakfast to church . every one remark how well the Baroness Bernstein look ; she laugh , and be particularly friendly with her niece ; she have a bow and a stately smile for all , as she move on , with her tortoiseshell cane . at the door there be a dazzling conflux of rank and fashion all the fine company of the Wells troop in ; and her ladyship of Yarmouth , conspicuous with vermilion cheek , and a robe of flame - colour taffeta . there be shabby people present , besides the fine company , though these latter be by far the most numerous . what an odd - look pair , for instance , be those in ragged coat , one of they with his carroty hair appear under his scratch - wig , and who enter the church just as the organ stop ! Nay , he could not have be a protestant , for he mechanically cross himself as he enter the place , say to his comrade , " Bedad , Tim , I forgawt ! " by which I conclude that the individual come from an island which have be mention at the commencement of this chapter . wherever they go a rich fragrance of whisky spread itself . a man may be a heretic , but possess genius : these catholic gentleman have come to pay homage to Mr. Sampson . 
 Nay , there be not only member of the old religion present , but disciple of a creed still old . who be those two individual with hooked nose and sallow countenance , who work into the church in spite of some little opposition on the part of the beadle ? see the greasy appearance of these hebrew stranger , Mr. Beadle be for deny they admission . but one whisper into his ear , " we want to be conwerte , gov'nor ! " another slip money into his hand , Mr. Beadle lift up the mace with which he be bar the doorway , and the Hebrew gentleman enter . there go the organ ! the door have close . shall we go in , and listen to Mr. Sampson 's sermon , or lie on the grass without ? 
 precede by that beadle in gold lace , Sampson walk up to the pulpit , as rosy and jolly a man as you could wish to see . presently , when he surge up out of his plump pulpit cushion , why do his Reverence turn as pale as death ? he look to the western church - door there , on each side of it , be those horrible Hebrew caryatide . he then look to the vestry - door , which be hard by the rector 's pew , in which Sampson have be sit during the service , alongside of their ladyship his patroness . suddenly a couple of perfume hibernian gentleman slip out of an adjacent seat , and place themselves on a bench close by that vestry - door and rector 's pew , and so sate till the conclusion of the sermon , with eye meekly cast down to the ground . how can we describe that sermon , if the preacher himself never know how it come to an end ? 
 nevertheless , it be consider an excellent sermon . when it be over , the fine lady buzz into one another 's ear over their pew , and utter their praise and comment . Madame Walmoden , who be in the next pew to our friend , say it be bewdiful , and make she dremble all over . Madame Bernstein say it be excellent . Lady Maria be pleased to think that the family chaplain should so distinguish himself . she look up at he , and strove to catch his reverence 's eye , as he still sate in his pulpit ; she greet he with a little wave of the hand and flutter of her handkerchief . he scarcely seem to note the compliment ; his face be pale , his eye be look yonder , towards the font , where those Hebrews still remain . the stream of people pass by they in a rush , when they be lose to sight , in a throng in a march of two and three in a dribble of one at a time . everybody be go . the two Hebrews be still there by the door . 
 the Baroness de Bernstein and her niece still linger in the rector 's pew , where the old lady be deep in conversation with that gentleman . 
 " who be those horrible man at the door ? and what a smell of spirit there be ! " cry Lady Maria , to Mrs. Brett , her aunt 's woman , who have attend the two lady . 
 " farewell , doctor ; you have a darling little boy : be he to be a clergyman , too ? " ask Madame de Bernstein . " be you ready , my dear ? " and the pew be throw open , and Madame Bernstein , whose father be only a viscount , insist that her niece , Lady Maria , who be an earl 's daughter , should go first out of the pew . 
 as she step forward , those individual whom her ladyship designate as two horrible man , advance . one of they pull a long strip of paper out of his pocket , and her ladyship start and turn pale . she make for the vestry , in a vague hope that she can clear the door and close it behind she . the two whiskified gentleman be up with she , however ; one of they actually lay his hand on her shoulder , and say : 
 " at the shuit of Misthress Pincott , of Kinsington , mercer , I have the honour of arrest your leedyship . I neem be Costigan , madam , a poor gentleman of Oireland , bind to circumstance and force to follow a disagrayable profession . will your leedyship walk , or shall I man go fetch a cheer ? " 
 for reply Lady Maria Esmond give three shriek , and fall swoon to the ground . " keep the door , Mick ! " shout Mr. Costigan . " well let in no one else , madam , " he say , very politely , to Madame de Bernstein . " her ladyship have fall in a feenting fit , and will recover here , at her aise . " 
 " Unlace she , Brett ! " cry the old lady , whose eye twinkle oddly ; and as soon as that operation be perform , Madame Bernstein seize a little bag suspend by a hair chain , which Lady Maria wear round her neck , and snip the necklace in twain . " dash some cold water over her face , it always recover she ! " say the Baroness . " you stay with she , Brett . how much be your suit gentleman ? " 
 Mr. Costigan say , " the deem we have against her leedyship for one hundred and thirty - two pound , in which she be indebted to Misthress Eliza Pincott " 
 meanwhile , where be the Reverend Mr. Sampson ? like the fabled opossum we have read of , who , when he spy the unerre gunner from his gum - tree , say : " it be no use Major , I will come down , " so Sampson give himself up to his pursuer . " at whose suit , simon ? " he sadly ask . Sampson know simon : they have meet many a time before . 
 " Buckleby Cordwainer , " say Mr. Simons . 
 " forty - eight pound and charge , I know , " say Mr. Sampson , with a sigh . " I have not   get the money . what officer be there here ? " Mr. Simons 's companion , Mr. Lyons , here step forward , and say his house be most convenient , and often use by gentleman , and he should be most happy and proud to accommodate his reverence . 
 two chair happen to be in wait outside the chapel . in those two chair my Lady Maria Esmond and Mr. Sampson place themselves , and go to Mr. Lyons 's residence , escort by the gentleman to whom we have just be introduce . 
 very soon after the capture the Baroness Bernstein send Mr. Case , her confidential servant , with a note to her niece , full of expression of the most ardent affection : but regret that her heavy loss at card render the payment of such a sum as that in which Lady Maria stand indebted quite impossible . she have write off to Mrs. Pincott , by that very post , however , to entreat she to grant time , and as soon as ever she have an answer , would not fail to acquaint her dear unhappy niece . 
 Mrs. Betty come over to console her mistress : and the two poor woman cast about for money enough to provide a horse and chaise for Mrs. Betty , who have very nearly come to misfortune , too . both my Lady Maria and her maid have be unlucky at card , and could not muster more than eighteen shilling between they : so it be agree that Betty should sell a gold chain belong to her lady , and with the money travel to London . now , Betty take the chain to the very toy - shop man who have sell it to Mr. Warrington , who have give it to his cousin ; and the toy - shop man , suppose that she have steal the chain , be for bring in a constable to Betty . hence , she have to make explanation , and to say how her mistress be in durance ; and , ere the night close , all Tunbridge Wells know that my Lady Maria Esmond be in the hand of bailiff . meanwhile , however , the money be find , and Mrs. Betty whisk up to London in search of the champion in whom the poor prisoner confide . 
 " do not   say anything about that paper be go ! oh , the wretch , the wretch ! she shall pay it I ! " I presume that Lady Maria mean her aunt by the word " wretch . " Mr. Sampson read a sermon to her ladyship , and they pass the evening over revenge and backgammon ; with well - ground hope that Harry Warrington would rush to their rescue as soon as ever he hear of their mishap . 
 though , ere the evening be over , every soul at the Wells know what have happen to Lady Maria , and a great deal more ; though they know she be take in execution , the house where she lay , the amount nay , ten time the amount for which she be capture , and that she be oblige to pawn her trinket to get a little money to keep she in jail ; though everybody say that old fiend of a Bernstein be at the bottom of the business , of course they be all civil and bland in society ; and , at my Lady Trumpington 's card that night , where Madame Bernstein appear , and as long as she be within hearing , not a word be say regard the morning 's transaction . Lady Yarmouth ask the Baroness news of her breddy nephew , and hear Mr. Warrington be in London . my Lady Maria be not come to Lady Trumpington 's that evening ? my Lady Maria be indispose , have faint at church that morning , and be oblige to keep her room . the card be deal , the fiddle sing , the wine go round , the gentlefolk talk , laugh , yawn , chatter , the footman waylay the supper , the chairman drink and swore , the star climb the sky , just as though no Lady Maria be imprison , and no poor Sampson arrest . ' tis certain , dearly beloved brother , that the little grief , sting , annoyance , which you and I feel acutely in our own person , do not   prevent our neighbour from sleep ; and that when we slip out of the world the world do not miss we . be this humiliating to our vanity ? so much the well . but , on the other hand , be it not a comfortable and consoling truth ? and may not   we be thankful for our humble condition ? if we be not selfish passez - moi le mot , s.v.p . and if we have to care for other people 's grief as much as our own , how intolerable human life would be ! if my neighbour 's tight boot pinch my corn ; if the calumny utter against Jones set Brown into fury ; if Mrs. A 's death plunge Messrs. B , C , D , E , F , into distraction , would there be any bearing of the world 's burthen ? do not let we be in the least angry or surprised if all the company play on , and be happy , although Lady Maria have come to grief . Countess , the deal be with you ! be you go to Stubblefield to shoot as usual , Sir John ? Captain , we shall have you run off to the bath after the widow ! so the clatter go on ; the light burn ; the beaux and the lady flirt , laugh , ogle ; the prisoner rag in his cell ; the sick man toss on his bed . 
 perhaps Madame de Bernstein stay at the assembly until the very last , not willing to allow the company the chance of speak of she as soon as her back should be turn . ah , what a comfort it be , I say again , that we have back , and that our ear do not   grow on they ! he that have ear to hear , let he stuff they with cotton . Madame Bernstein might have hear folk say it be heartless of she to come abroad , and play at card , and make merry when her niece be in trouble . as if she could help Maria by stay at home , indeed ! at her age , it be dangerous to disturb an old lady 's tranquillity . " do not   tell I ! " say Lady Yarmouth . " the Bernstein would play at card over her niece 's coffin . talk about her heart ! who ever say she have one ? that old spy lose it to the Chevalier a thousand year ago , and have live ever since perfectly well without one . for how much be the Maria put in prison ? if it be only a small sum we would pay it , it would vex her aunt so . find out , Fuchs , in the morning , for how much Lady Maria Esmond be put in prison . " and the faithful Fuchs bow , and promise to do her Excellency 's will . 
 meanwhile , about midnight , Madame de Bernstein go home , and presently fall into a sound sleep , from which she do not wake up until a late hour of the morning , when she summon her usual attendant , who arrive with her ladyship 's morning dish of tea . if I tell you she take a dram with it , you would be shock . some of our great - grandmother use to have cordial in their " closet . " have you not read of the fine lady in Walpole , who say , " if I drink more , I shall be ' muckibus ! ' ? " as surely as Mr. Gough be alive now , our ancestress be accustom to partake pretty freely of strong water . 
 so , have tip off the cordial , Madame Bernstein rous and ask Mrs. Brett the news . 
 " he can give it you , " say the waiting - woman , sulkily . 
 " he ? who ? " 
 Mrs. Brett name Harry , and say Mr. Warrington arrive about midnight yesterday and Betty , my Lady Maria 's maid , be with he . " and my Lady Maria send your ladyship her love and duty , and hop you sleep well , " say Brett . 
 " excellently , poor thing ! be Betty go to she ? " 
 " no ; she be here , " say Mrs. Brett . 
 " let I see she directly , " cry the old lady . 
 " I will tell she , " reply the obsequious Brett , and go away upon her mistress 's errand , leave the old lady placidly repose on her pillow . presently , two pair of high - heeled shoe be hear patter over the deal floor of the bedchamber . carpet be luxury scarcely know in bedroom of those day . 
 " so , Mrs. Betty , you be in London yesterday ? " call Bernstein from her curtain . 
 " it be not Betty it be I ! good morning , dear aunt ! I hope you sleep well ? " cry a voice which make old Bernstein start on her pillow . it be the voice of Lady Maria , who draw the curtain aside , and drop her aunt a low curtsey . Lady Maria look very pretty , rosy , and happy . and with the little surprise incident at her appearance through Madame Bernstein 's curtain , I think we may bring this chapter to a close . 




 CHAPTER XXXIX . Harry to the Rescue 

 " my dear Lord March "   write Mr. Warrington from Tunbridge Wells , on Saturday morning , the 25th August , 1756 : " this be to inform you   with satisfaction   that I have one all our three bett . I be at Bromley two minute within the hour ; my new horse kep a - go at a capital rate . I drive they myself , have the postilion by I to show I the way , and my black man inside with Mrs. Betty . Hope they find the drive very pleasant . we be not stop on Blackheath , though two fellow on horseback ride up to we , but not like the look of our countenantse , ride off again ; and we get into Tunbridge Wells   where I transact my business   at forty - five minute after eleven . this make I quitt with your lordship after yesterday 's piquet , which I shall be very happy to give your revenge , and be 
 " your most obliged , faithful servant , 

 " H. ESMOND WARRINGTON . " 
 and now , perhaps , the reader will understand by what mean Lady Maria Esmond be enable to surprise her dear aunt in her bed on Saturday morning , and walk out of the house of captivity . have despatch Mrs. Betty to London , she scarcely expect that her emissary would return on the day of her departure ; and she and the chaplain be play their card at midnight , after a small refection which the bailiff 's wife have provide for they , when the rapid whirling of wheel be hear approach their house , and cause the lady to lay her trump down , and her heart to beat with more than ordinary emotion . Whirr come the wheel the carriage stop at the very door : there be a parley at the gate : then appear Mrs. Betty , with a face radiant with joy , though her eye be full of tear ; and next , who be that tall young gentleman who enter ? can any of my reader guess ? will they be very angry if I say that the chaplain slap down his card with a huzzay , whilst Lady Maria , turn as white as a sheet , rise up from her chair , totter forward a step or two , and , with an hysterical shriek , fling herself in her cousin 's arm ? how many kiss do he give she ? if they be mille , deinde centum , dein mille altera , dein secunda centum , and so on , I be not go to cry out . he have come to rescue she . she know he would ; he be her champion , her preserver from bondage and ignominy . she weep a genuine flood of tear upon his shoulder , and as she recline there , give way to a hearty emotion , I protest I think she look handsomer than she have look during the whole course of this history . she do not faint this time ; she go home , lean lovingly on her cousin 's arm , and may have have one or two hysterical outbreak in the night ; but Madame Bernstein sleep soundly , and do not hear she . 
 " you be both free to go home , " be the first word Harry say . " get my lady 's hat and cardinal , Betty , and , Chaplain , we will smoke a pipe together at our lodging , it will refresh I after my ride . " the chaplain , who , too , have a great deal of available sensibility , be very much overcome ; he burst into tear as he seize Harry 's hand , and kiss it , and pray God to bless his dear , generous , young patron . Mr. Warrington feel a glow of pleasure thrill through his frame . it be good to be able to help the suffering and the poor ; it be good to be able to turn sorrow into joy . not a little proud and elated be our young champion , as , with his hat cock , he march by the side of his rescue princess . his feeling come out to meet he , as it be , and beautiful happiness with kind eye and smile dance before he , and clothe he in a robe of honour , and scatter flower on his path , and blow trumpet and shawm of sweet gratulation , call , " here come the conqueror ! make way for the champion ! " and so they lead he up to the king 's house , and seat he in the hall of complacency , upon the cushion of comfort . and yet it be not much he have do . only a kindness . he have but to put his hand in his pocket , and with an easy talisman , drive off the dragon which keep the gate , and cause the tyrant to lay down his axe , who have get Lady Maria in execution . never mind if his vanity be puff up ; he be very good - natured ; he have rescue two unfortunate people , and pump tear of goodwill and happiness out of their eye : and if he brag a little to - night , and swagger somewhat to the chaplain , and talk about London , and Lord March , and White 's , and Almack 's , with the air of a macaroni , I do not   think we need like he much the less . 
 Sampson continue to be prodigiously affect . this man have a nature most easily work upon , and extraordinarily quick to receive pain and pleasure , to tear , gratitude , laughter , hatred , like . in his preaching profession he have educate and train his sensibility so that they be of great use to he ; he be for the moment what he act . he weep quite genuine tear , find that he could produce they freely . he love you whilst he be with you ; he have a real pang of grief as he mingle his sorrow with the widow or orphan ; and , meet Jack as he come out of the door , go to the tavern opposite , and laugh and roar over the bottle . he give money very readily , but never repay when he borrow . he be on this night in a rapture of gratitude and flattery towards Harry Warrington . in all London , perhaps , the unlucky Fortunate Youth could not have find a more dangerous companion . 
 to - night he be in his grateful mood , and full of enthusiasm for the benefactor who have release he from durance . with each bumper his admiration grow strong . he exalt Harry as the good and noblest of man , and the complacent young simpleton , as we have say , be disposed to take these praise as very well deserve . " the young branch of our family , " say Mr. Harry , with a superb air , " have treat you scurvily ; but , by Jove , Sampson my boy , I will stand by you ! " at a certain period of burgundian excitement Mr. Warrington be always very eloquent respect the splendour of his family . " I be very glad I be enable to help you in your strait . count on I whenever you want I , Sampson . do you not say you have a sister at boarding - school ? you will want money for she , sir . here be a little bill which may help to pay her schooling . " and the liberal young fellow pass a bank - note across to the chaplain . 
 again the man be affect to tear . Harry 's generosity smite he . 
 " Mr. Warrington , " he say , put the bank - note a short distance from he , " I I do not   deserve your kindness by George , I do not ! " and he swear an oath to corroborate his passionate assertion . 
 " Psha ! " say Harry . " I have plenty more of them . there be no money in that confound pocket - book which I lose last week . " 
 " no , sir . there be no money ! " say Mr. Sampson , drop his head . 
 " Hallo ! how do you know , Mr. Chaplain ? " ask the young gentleman . 
 " I know because I be a villain , sir . I be not worthy of your kindness . I tell you so . I find the book , sir , that night , when you have too much wine at Barbeau 's . " 
 " and read the letter ? " ask Mr. Warrington , start up and turn very red . 
 " they tell I nothing I do not know , sir , " say the chaplain " you have have spy about you whom you little suspect from whom you be much too young and simple to be able to keep your secret . " 
 " be those story about Lady Fanny , and my cousin will and his doing , true then ? " inquire Harry . 
 " yes , they be true , " sigh the chaplain . " the house of Castlewood have not be fortunate , sir , since your honour 's branch , the eld branch , leave it . " 
 " Sir , you do not   dare for to breathe a word against my Lady Maria ? " Harry cry out . 
 " oh , not for world ! " say Mr. Sampson , with a queer look at his young friend . " I may think she be too old for your honour , and that ' tis a pity you should not have a wife well suit to your age , though I admit she look very young for her , and hath every virtue and accomplishment . " 
 " she be too old , Sampson , I know she be , " say Mr. Warrington , with much majesty ; " but she have my word , and you see , sir , how fond she be of I . go bring I the letter , sir , which you find , and let I try and forgive you for have seize upon they . " 
 " my benefactor , let I try and forgive myself ! " cry Mr. Sampson , and depart towards his chamber , leave his young patron alone over his wine . 
 Sampson return presently , look very pale . " what have happen , sir ? " say Harry , with an imperious air . 
 the chaplain hold out a pocket - book . " with your name in it , sir , " he say . 
 " my brother 's name in it , " say Harry ; " it be George who give it to I . " 
 " I keep it in a locked chest , sir , in which I leave it this morning before I be take by those people . here be the book , sir , but the letter be go . my trunk and valise have also be tamper with . and I be a miserable , guilty man , unable to make you the restitution which I owe you . " Sampson look the picture of woe as he utter these sentiment . he clasp his hand together , and almost knelt before Harry in an attitude the most pathetic . 
 who have be in the room in Mr. Sampson 's and Mr. Warrington 's absence ? the landlady be ready to go on her knee , and declare that nobody have come in : nor , indeed , be Mr. Warrington 's chamber in the least disturbed , nor anything abstract from Mr. Sampson 's scanty wardrobe and possession , except those paper of which he deplore the absence . 
 whose interest be it to seize they ? Lady Maria 's ? the poor woman have be a prisoner all day , and during the time when the capture be effect . 
 she certainly be guiltless of the rape of the letter . the sudden seizure of the two Case , the house - steward 's secret journey to London , Case , who know the shoemaker at whose house Sampson lodge in London , and all the secret affair of the Esmond family , these point , consider together and separately , might make Mr. Sampson think that the Baroness Bernstein be at the bottom of this mischief . but why arrest Lady Maria ? the chaplain know nothing as yet about that letter which her ladyship have lose ; for poor Maria have not think it necessary to confide her secret to he . 
 as for the pocket - book and its content , Mr. Harry be so swollen up with self - satisfaction that evening , at win his three bet , at rescue his two friend , at the capital premature cold supper of partridge and ancient Burgundy which obsequious Monsieur Barbeau have send over to the young gentleman 's lodging , that he accept Sampson 's vow of contrition , and solemn promise of future fidelity , and reach his gracious hand to the chaplain , and condone his offence . when the latter swear his great god , that henceforth he would be Harry 's true , humblest friend and follower , and at any moment would be ready to die for Mr. Warrington , Harry say , majestically , " I think , Sampson , you would ; I hope you would . my family the Esmond family have always be accustom to have faithful friend round about them and to reward them too . the wine be with you , Chaplain . what toast do you call , sir ? " 
 " I call a blessing on the house of Esmond - Warrington ! " cry the chaplain , with real tear in his eye . 
 " we be the elder branch , sir . my grandfather be the Marquis of Esmond , " say Mr. Harry , in a voice noble but somewhat indistinct . " here be to you , Chaplain and I forgive you , sir and God bless you , sir and if you have be take for three time as much , I would have pay it . why , what be that I see through the shutter ? I be blest if the sun have not   rise again ! we have no need of candle to go to bed , ha , ha ! " and once more extend his blessing to his chaplain , the young fellow go off to sleep . 
 about noon Madame de Bernstein send over a servant to say that she would be glad if her nephew would come over and drink a dish of chocolate with she , whereupon our young friend rise and walk to his aunt 's lodging . she remark , not without pleasure , some alteration in his toilette : in his brief sojourn in London he have visit a tailor or two , and have be introduce by my Lord March to some of his lordship 's purveyor and tradesman . 
 Aunt Bernstein call he " my dear child , " and thank he for his noble , his generous behaviour to dear Maria . what a shock that seizure in church have be to she ! a still great shock that she have lose three hundred only on the Wednesday night to Lady Yarmouth , and be quite a sec . " why , " say the Baroness , " I have to send case to London to my agent to get I money to pay I could not leave Tunbridge in her debt . " 
 " so case do go to London ? " say Mr. Harry . 
 " of course he do : the Baroness de Bernstein can not   afford to say she be court d'argent . Canst thou lend I some , child ? " 
 " I can give your ladyship twenty - two pound , " say Harry , blush very red : " I have but forty - four left till I get my virginian remittance . I have buy horse and clothe , and be very extravagant , aunt . " 
 " and rescue your poor relation in distress , you prodigal good boy . no , child , I do not want thy money . I can give thee some . here be a note upon my agent for fifty pound , vaurien ! go and spend it , and be merry ! I dare say thy mother will repay I , though she do not love I . " and she look quite affectionate , and hold out a pretty hand , which the youth kiss . 
 " your mother do not love I , but your mother 's father do once . Mind , sir , you always come to I when you have need of I . " 
 when bent on exhibit they , nothing could exceed Beatrix Bernstein 's grace or good - humour . " I can not   help love you , child , " she continue , " and yet I be so angry with you that I have scarce the patience to speak to you . so you have actually engage yourself to poor Maria , who be as old as your mother ? what will Madam Esmond say ? she may live three hundred year , and you will not have wherewithal to support yourself . " 
 " I have ten thousand pound from my father , of my own , now my poor brother be go , " say Harry , " that will go some way . " 
 " why , the interest will not keep you in card - money . " 
 " we must give up card , " say Harry . 
 " it be more than Maria be capable of . she will pawn the coat off your back to play . the rage for it run in all my brother 's family in I too , I own it . I warn you . I pray you not to play with they , and now a lad of twenty to engage himself to a woman of forty - two ! to write letter on his knee and sign with his heart 's blood   which he spell like hartshorn , and say that he will marry no other woman than his adorable cousin , Lady Maria Esmond . oh ! it be cruel cruel ! " 
 " Great heavens ! madam , who show you my letter ? " ask Harry , burn with a blush again . 
 " an accident . she faint when she be take by those bailiff . Brett cut her lace for she ; and when she be carry off , poor thing , we find a little sachet on the floor , which I open , not know in the least what it contain . and in it be Mr. Harry Warrington 's precious letter . and here , sir , be the case . " 
 a pang shoot through Harry 's heart . " great heaven ! why do not   she destroy it ? " he think . 
 " I I will give it back to Maria , " he say , stretch out his hand for the little locket . 
 " my dear , I have burn the foolish letter , " say the old lady . 
 " if you choose to betray I I must take the consequence . if you choose to write another , I can not help thee . but , in that case , Harry Esmond , I have rather never see thee again . will you keep my secret ? will you believe an old woman who love you and know the world well than you do ? I tell you , if you keep that foolish promise , misery and ruin be surely in store for you . what be a lad like you in the hand of a wily woman of the world , who make a toy of you ? she have entrap you into a promise , and your old aunt have cut the string and set you free . go back again ! betray I if you will , Harry . " 
 " I be not angry with you , aunt I wish I be , " say Mr. Warrington , with very great emotion . " I I shall not repeat what you tell I . " 
 " Maria never will , child mark my word ! " cry the old lady , eagerly . " she will never own that she have lose that paper . she will tell you that she have it . " 
 " but I be sure she she be very fond of I ; you should have see she last night , " falter Harry . 
 " must I tell more story against my own flesh and blood ? " sob out the Baroness . " child , you do not know her past life ! " 
 " and I must not , and I will not ! " cry Harry , start up . " write or say it do not matter which ! but my word be give ; they may play with such thing in England , but we gentleman of Virginia do not   break them . if she hold I to my word , she shall have I . if we be miserable , as I dare say we shall be , I will take a firelock , and go join the King of Prussia , or let a ball put an end to I . " 
 " I I have no more to say . will you be pleased to ring that bell ? I I wish you a good morning , Mr. Warrington , " and drop a very stately curtsey , the old lady rise on her tortoiseshell stick , and turn towards the door . but , as she make her first step , she put her hand to her heart , sink on the sofa again , an shed the first tear that have drop for long year from Beatrix Esmond 's eye . 
 Harry be greatly move , too . he kneel down by she . he seize her cold hand , and kiss it . he tell she , in his artless way , how very keenly he have feel her love for he , and how , with all his heart , he return it . " ah , aunt ! " say he , " you do not   know what a villain I feel myself . when you tell I , just now how that paper be burn oh ! I be ashamed to think how glad I be . " he bow his comely head over her hand . she feel hot drop from his eye rain on it . she have love this boy . for half a century past never , perhaps , in the course of her whole worldly life , have she feel a sensation so tender and so pure . the hard heart be wound now , soften , overcome . she put she two hand on his shoulder , and lightly kiss his forehead . 
 " you will not tell she what I have do , child ? " she say . 
 he declare never ! never ! and demure Mrs. Brett , enter at her mistress 's summon , find the nephew and aunt in this sentimental attitude . 




 CHAPTER XL . in which Harry pay off an Old Debt , and incur some New Ones 

 our Tunbridge friend be now weary of the Wells , and eager to take their departure . when the autumn should arrive , Bath be Madame de Bernstein 's mark . there be more card , company , life , there . she would reach it after pay a few visit to her country friend . Harry promise , with rather a bad grace , to ride with Lady Maria and the chaplain to Castlewood . again they pass by Oakhurst village , and the hospitable house where Harry have be so kindly entertain . Maria make so many keen remark about the young lady of Oakhurst , and their set their cap at Harry , and the mother 's evident desire to catch he for one of they , that , somewhat in a pet , Mr. Warrington say he would pass his friend ' door , as her ladyship dislike and abuse they ; and be very haughty and sulky that evening at the inn where they stop , some few mile far on the road . at supper , my Lady Maria 's smile bring no corresponding good - humour to Harry 's face ; her tear   which her ladyship have at command   do not seem to create the least sympathy from Mr. Warrington ; to her querulous remark he growl a surly reply ; and my lady be oblige to go to bed at length without get a single tete - a - tete with her cousin , that obstinate chaplain , as if by order , persist in stay in the room . have Harry give Sampson order to remain ? she depart with a sigh . he bow she to the door with an obstinate politeness , and consign she to the care of the landlady and she maid . 
 what horse be that which gallop out of the inn - yard ten minute after Lady Maria have go to her chamber ? an hour after her departure from their supper - room , Mrs. Betty come in for her lady 's bottle of smell - salt , and find Parson Sampson smoke a pipe alone . Mr. Warrington be go to bed be go to fetch a walk in the moonlight how should he know where Mr. Harry be ? Sampson answer , in reply to the maid 's interrogatory . Mr. Warrington be ready to set forward the next morning , and take his place by the side of Lady Maria 's carriage . but his brow be black the dark spirit be still on he . he hardly speak to she during the journey . " great heaven ! she must have tell he that she steal it ! " think Lady Maria within her own mind . 
 the fact be , that , as they be walk up that steep hill which lie about three mile from Oakhurst , on the Westerham road , Lady Maria Esmond , lean on her fond youth 's arm , and indeed very much in love with he , have warble into his ear the most sentimental vow , protest , and expression of affection . as she grow fonder , he grow cold . as she look up in his face , the sun shine down upon her , which , fresh and well - preserve as it be , yet show some of the line and wrinkle of twoscore year ; and poor Harry , with that arm lean on his , feel it intolerably weighty , and by no mean relish his walk up the hill . to think that all his life , that drag be to be upon he ! it be a dreary look forward and he curse the moonlight walk , and the hot evening , and the hot wine which have make he give that silly pledge by which he be fatally bind . 
 Maria 's praise and rapture annoy Harry beyond measure . the poor thing pour out scrap of the few play which she know that have reference to her case , and strove with her utmost power to charm her young companion . she call he , over and over again , her champion , her Henrico , her preserver , and vow that his Molinda would be ever , ever faithful to he . she cling to he . " ah , child ! have I not thy precious image , thy precious hair , thy precious writing here ? " she say , look in his face . " shall it not go with I to the grave ? it would , sir , be I to meet with unkindness from my Henrico ! " she sigh out . 
 here be a strange story ! Madame Bernstein have give he the little silken case she have burn the hair and the note which the case contain , and Maria have it still on her heart ! it be then , at the start which Harry give , as she be lean on his arm at the sudden movement as if he would drop hers that Lady Maria feel her first pang of remorse that she have tell a fib , or rather , that she be find out in tell a fib , which be a far more cogent reason for repentance . Heaven help we ! if some people be to do penance for tell lie , would they ever be out of sackcloth and ash ? 
 arrive at Castlewood , Mr. Harry 's good - humour be not increase . my lord be from home ; the lady also be away ; the only member of the family whom Harry find , be Mr. Will , who return from partridge - shooting just as the chaise and cavalcade reach the gate , and who turn very pale when he see his cousin , and receive a sulky scowl of recognition from the young Virginian . 
 nevertheless , he think to put a good face on the matter , and they meet at supper , where , before my Lady Maria , their conversation be at first civil , but not lively . Mr. Will have be to some race ? to several . he have be pretty successful in his bet ? Mr. Warrington hop . pretty well . " and you have bring back my horse sound ? " ask Mr. Warrington . 
 " your horse ! what horse ? " ask Mr. Will . 
 " what horse ? my horse ! " say Mr. Harry , curtly . 
 " protest I do not   understand you , " say will . 
 " the brown horse for which I play you , and which I win of you the night before you ride away upon it , " say Mr. Warrington , sternly . " you remember the horse , Mr. Esmond . " 
 " Mr. Warrington , I perfectly well remember play you for a horse , which my servant hand over to you on the day of your departure . " 
 " the chaplain be present at our play . Mr. Sampson , will you be umpire between we ? " Mr. Warrington say , with much gentleness . 
 " I be bind to decide that Mr. Warrington play for the brown horse , " say Mr. Sampson . 
 " well , he get the other one , " say sulky Mr. Will , with a grin . 
 " and sell it for thirty shilling ! " say Mr. Warrington , always preserve his calm tone . 
 will be waggish . " thirty shilling ? and a devilish good price , too , for the break - kneed old rip . ha , ha ! " 
 " not a word more . ' tis only a question about a bet , my dear Lady Maria . shall I serve you some more chicken ? " nothing could be more studiously courteous and gay than Mr. Warrington be , so long as the lady remain in the room . when she rise to go , Harry follow she to the door , and close it upon she with the most courtly bow of farewell . he stand at the closed door for a moment , and then he bid the servant retire . when those menial be go , Mr. Warrington lock the heavy door before they , and pocket the key . 
 as it click in the lock , Mr. Will , who have be sit over his punch , look now and then askance at his cousin , ask , with one of the oath which commonly garnish his conversation , what the Mr. Warrington mean by that ? 
 " I guess there be go to be a quarrel , " say Mr. Warrington , blandly , " and there be no use in have these fellow look on at row between their better . " 
 " who be go to quarrel here , I should like to know ? " ask Will , look very pale , and grasp a knife . 
 " Mr. Sampson , you be present when I play Mr. Will fifty guineas against his brown horse ? " 
 " against his horse ! " bawl out Mr. Will . 
 " I be not such a something fool as you take I for , " say Mr. Warrington , " although I do come from Virginia ! " and he repeat his question : " Mr. Sampson , you be here when I play the Honourable William Esmond , Esquire , fifty guineas against his brown horse ? " 
 " I must own it , sir , " say the chaplain , with a deprecatory look towards his lord 's brother . 
 " I do not   own no such a thing , " say Mr. Will , with rather a forced laugh . 
 " no , sir : because it cost you no more pain to lie than to cheat , " say Mr. Warrington , walk up to his cousin . " hand off , Mr. Chaplain , and see fair play ! because you be no well than a ha ! " 
 no well than a what we can not   say , and shall never know , for as Harry utter the exclamation , his dear cousin fling a wine bottle at Mr. Warrington 's head , who bob just in time , so that the missile fly across the room , and break against the wainscot opposite , break the face of a picture ancestor of the Esmond family , and then itself against the wall , whence it spirt a pint of good port wine over the chaplain 's face and flower wig . " great heaven , gentleman , I pray you to be quiet ! " cry the parson , drip with gore . 
 but gentleman be not incline at some moment to remember the command of the Church . the bottle have fail , Mr. Esmond seize the large silver - handle knife and drive at his cousin . but Harry catch up the other 's right hand with his left , as he have see the boxer do at Marybone ; and deliver a rapid blow upon Mr. Esmond 's nose , which send he reel up against the oak panel , and I dare say cause he to see ten thousand illumination . he drop his knife in his retreat against the wall , which his rapid antagonist kick under the table . 
 now will , too , have be at Marybone and Hockley - in - the - Hole , and after a gasp for breath and a glare over his bleed nose at his enemy , he dash forward his head as though it have be a battering - ram , intend to project it into Mr. Henry Warrington 's stomach . 
 this manoeuvre Harry have see , too , on his visit to Marybone , and amongst the negro upon the maternal estate , who would meet in combat like two concutient cannon - ball , each hard than the other . but Harry have see and mark the civilised practice of the white man . he skip aside , and , salute his advance enemy with a tremendous blow on the right ear , fell he , so that he strike his head against the heavy oak table and sink lifeless to the ground . 
 " Chaplain , you will bear witness that it have be a fair fight ! " say Mr. Warrington , still quiver with the excitement of the combat , but strive with all his might to restrain himself and look cool . and he draw the key from his pocket and open the door in the lobby , behind which three or four servant be gather . a crash of broken glass , a cry , a shout , an oath or two , have tell they that some violent scene be occur within , and they enter , and behold two victim bedabble with red the chaplain bleed port wine , and the Honourable William Esmond , Esquire , stretch in his own gore . 
 " Mr. Sampson will bear witness that I strike fair , and that Mr. Esmond hit the first blow , " say Mr. Warrington . " Undo his neckcloth , somebody he may be dead ; and get a fleam , Gumbo , and blee he . stop ! he be come to himself ! lift he up , you , and tell a maid to wash the floor . " 
 indeed , in a minute , Mr. Will do come to himself . first his eye roll about , or rather , I be ashamed to say , his eye , one have be close by Mr. Warrington 's first blow . first , then , his eye roll about ; then he gasp and utter an inarticulate moan or two , then he begin to swear and curse very freely and articulately . 
 " he be get well , " say Mr. Warrington . 
 " oh , praise be Mussy ! " sigh the sentimental Betty . 
 " ask he , Gumbo , whether he would like any more ? " say Mr. Warrington , with a stern humour . 
 " Massa Harry say , wool you like any maw ? " ask obedient Gumbo , bow over the prostrate gentleman . 
 " no , curse you , you black devil ! " say Mr. Will , hit up at the black object before he .   " so he nearly cut my tongue in to in my mouf ! " Gumbo explain to the pitying Betty .   " no , that be , yes ! you infernal Mohock ! why do not somebody kick he out of the place ? " 
 " because nobody dare , Mr. Esmond , " say Mr. Warrington , with great state , arrange his ruffle his ruffled ruffle . 
 " and nobody will not   neither , " growl the man . they have all grow to love Harry , whereas Mr. Will have nobody 's good word . 
 " we know all be fair , sir . it be not   the first time Master William have be serve so . " 
 " and I hope it will not   be the last , " cry shrill Betty . " to go for to strike a poor black gentleman so ! " 
 Mr. Will have gather himself up by this time , have wipe his bleeding face with a napkin , and be skulk off to bed . 
 " surely it be manner to say good night to the company . good night , Mr. Esmond , " say Mr. Warrington , whose joke , though few , be not very brilliant ; but the honest lad relish the brilliant sally and laugh at it inwardly . 
 " he be ad his zopper , and he go to baid ! " say Betty , in her native dialect , at which everybody laugh outright , except Mr. William , who go away leave a black fume of curse , as it be , roll out of that funnel , his mouth . 
 it must be own that Mr. Warrington continue to be witty the next morning . he send a note to Mr. Will beg to know whether he be for a ride to town or anywhere else . if he be for London , that he would friten the highwayman on Hounslow Heath , and look a very genteel figar at the Chocolate House . which letter , I fear , Mr. Will receive with his usual violence , request the writer to go to some place not hounslow . 
 and , besides the parley between Will and Harry , there come a maiden simper to Mr. Warrington 's door , and Gumbo advance , hold something white and triangular in his ebon finger . 
 Harry know what it be well enough . " of course it be a letter , " groan he . molinda greet her Enrico , etc . etc . etc . no sleep have she know that night , and so forth , and so forth , and so forth . have Enrico sleep well in the hall of his father ? und so weiter , und so weiter . he must never never quaril and be so cruel again . Kai ta loipa . and I protest I shall not   quote any more of this letter . ah , tablet , golden once , be ye now fade leaf ? where be the juggler who transmute you , and why be the glamour over ? 
 after the little scandal with cousin will , Harry 's dignity would not allow he to stay long at Castlewood : he write a majestic letter to the lord of the mansion , explain the circumstance which have occur , and , as he call in Parson Sampson to supervise the document , no doubt it contain none of those eccentricity in spelling which figure in his ordinary correspondence at this period . he represent to poor Maria , that after blacken the eye and damage the nose of a son of the house , he should remain in it with a very bad grace ; and she be force to acquiesce in the opinion that , for the present , his absence would well become he . of course , she weep plentiful tear at part with he . he would go to London , and see young beauty : he would find none , none who would love he like his fond Maria . I fear Mr. Warrington do not exhibit any profound emotion on leave she : nay , he cheer up immediately after he cross Castlewood Bridge , and make his horse whisk over the road at ten mile an hour : he sing to they to go along : he nod to the pretty girl by the roadside : he chuck my landlady under the chin : he certainly be not inconsolable . truth be , he long to be back in London again , to make a figure at St. James 's , at Newmarket , wherever the man of fashion congregate . all that petty Tunbridge society of woman and card - playing seem child's - play to he now he have taste the delight of London life . 
 by the time he reach London again , almost all the four - and - forty pound which we have see that he possess at Tunbridge have slip out of his pocket , and further supply be necessary . regard these he make himself presently easy . there be the two sum of 5000 pound in his own and his brother 's name , of which he be the master . he would take up a little money , and with a run or two of good luck at play he could easily replace it . meantime he must live in a manner become his station , and it must be explain to Madam Esmond that a gentleman of his rank can not keep fitting company , and appear as become he in society , upon a miserable pittance of two hundred a year .
