 Mr. Warrington sojourn at the Bedford Coffee - House as before , but only for a short while . he seek out proper lodging at the Court end of the town , and fix on some apartment in Bond Street , where he and Gumbo instal themselves , his horse stand at a neighbouring livery - stable . and now tailor , mercer , and shoemaker be put in requisition . not without a pang of remorse , he lay aside his mourning and figure in a laced hat and waistcoat . Gumbo be always dexterous in the art of dress hair , and with a little powder fling into his fair lock Mr. Warrington 's head be as modish as that of any gentleman in the Mall . he figure in the Ring in his phaeton . report of his great wealth have long since precede he to London , and not a little curiosity be excited about the fortunate Virginian . 
 until our young friend could be ballot for at the proper season , my Lord March have write down his name for the club at White 's Chocolate - House , as a distinguished gentleman from America . there be as yet but few person of fashion in London , but with a pocket full of money at one - and - twenty , a young fellow can make himself happy even out of the season ; and Mr. Harry be determined to enjoy . 
 he order Mr. Draper , then , to sell five hundred pound of his stock . what would his poor mother have say have she know that the young spendthrift be already begin to dissipate his patrimony ? he din at the tavern , he sup at the club , where Jack Morris introduce he , with immense eulogium , to such gentleman as be in town . life and youth and pleasure be before he , the wine be set a - running , and the eager lad be greedy to drink . do you see , far away in the west yonder , the pious widow at her prayer for her son ? behind the tree at Oakhurst a tender little heart , too , be beat for he , perhaps . when the Prodigal Son be away carouse , be not love and forgiveness still on the watch for he ? 
 amongst the inedited letter of the late Lord Orford , there be one which the present learn editor , Mr. Peter Cunningbam , have omit from his collection , doubt possibly the authenticity of the document . Nay , I myself have only see a copy of it in the Warrington paper in Madam Esmond 's prim handwriting , and note " Mr. H. Walpole 's account of my son Henry at London , and of Baroness Tusher , write to General Conway . " 
 " ARLINGTON STREET , Friday Night . 
 " I have come away , child , for a day or two from my devotion to our Lady of Strawberry . have I not be on my knee to she these three week , and be not   the poor old joint full of rheumatism ? a fit take I that I would pay London a visit , that I would go to Vauxhall and Ranelagh . Quoi ! may I not have my rattle as well as other elderly baby ? suppose , after be so long virtuous , I take a fancy to cake and ale , shall your reverence say nay to I ? George Selwyn and Tony Storer and your humble servant take boat at Westminster t'other night . be it Tuesday ? no , Tuesday I be with their Graces of Norfolk , who be just from Tunbridge it be Wednesday . how should I know ? be not   I dead drunk with a whole pint of lemonade I take at White 's ? 
 " the Norfolk folk have be entertain I on Tuesday with the account of a young savage Iroquois , Choctaw , or Virginian , who have lately be make a little noise in our quarter of the globe . he be an offshoot of that disreputable family of Esmond , Castlewood , of whom all the man be gambler and spendthrift , and all the woman well , I shall not   say the word , lest Lady Ailesbury should be look over your shoulder . both the late lord , my father tell I , be in his pay , and the last one , a beau of Queen Anne 's reign , from a viscount advanced to be an earl through the merit and intercession of his notorious old sister Bernstein , late Tusher , nee Esmond a great beauty , too , of her day , a favourite of the old Pretender . she sell his secret to my papa , who pay she for they ; and be nowise particular in her love for the Stuarts , come over to the august Hanoverian house at present reign over we . ' will Horace Walpole 's tongue never stop scandal ? ' say your wife over your shoulder . I kiss your ladyship 's hand . I be dumb . the Bernstein be a model of virtue . she have no good reason for marry her father 's chaplain . many of the nobility omit the marriage altogether . she be not   ashamed of be Mrs. Tusher , and do not   take a german Baroncino for a second husband , whom nobody out of Hanover ever see . the Yarmouth bear no malice . Esther and Vashti be very good friend , and have be cheat each other at Tunbridge at card all the summer . 
 " ' and what have all this to do with the Iroquois ? ' say your ladyship . the Iroquois have be at Tunbridge , too not cheat , perhaps , but win vastly . they say he have bleed Lord March of thousand Lord March , by whom so much blood hath be shed , that he have quarrel with everybody , fight with everybody , ride over everybody , be fall in love with by everybody 's wife except Mr. Conway 's , and not except her present Majesty , the Countess of England , Scotland , France and Ireland , queen of Walmoden and Yarmouth , whom Heaven preserve to we . 
 " you know an offensive little creature , de par le monde , one Jack Morris , who skip in and out of all the house of London . when we be at Vauxhall , Mr. Jack give we a nod under the shoulder of a pretty young fellow enough , on whose arm he be lean , and who appear hugely delighted with the enchantment of the garden . Lord , how he star at the firework ! god , how he huzzaye at the singing of a horrible paint wench who shriek the ear off my head ! a twopenny string of glass bead and a strip of tawdry cloth be treasure in Iroquois - land , and our savage value they accordingly . 
 " a buzz go about the place that this be the fortunate youth . he win three hundred at White 's last night very genteelly from Rockingham and my precious nephew , and here he be bellow and huzzaye over the music so as to do you good to hear . I do not love a puppet - show , but I love to treat child to one , Miss Conway ! I present your ladyship my compliment , and hope we shall go and see the doll together . 
 " when the singing woman come down from her throne , Jack Morris must introduce my Virginian to she . I see he blush up to the eye , and make she , upon my word , a very fine bow , such as I have no idea be practise in wigwam . ' there be a certain jenny squaw about she , and that be why the savage like she , ' George say a joke certainly not as brilliant as a firework . after which it seem to I that the savage and the savage retire together . 
 " have have a great deal too much to eat and drink three hour before , my partner must have chicken and rack - punch at Vauxhall , where George fall asleep straightway , and for my sin I must tell Tony Storer what I know about this Virginian 's amiable family , especially some of the Bernstein 's antecedent , and the history of another elderly beauty of the family , a certain Lady Maria , who be au mieux with the late Prince of Wales . what do I say ? I protest not half of what I know , and of course not a tenth part of what I be go to tell , for who should start out upon we but my savage , this time quite red in the face ; and in his war paint . the wretch have be drink fire - water in the next box ! 
 " he cock his hat , clap his hand to his sword , ask which of the gentleman be it that be malign his family ? so that I be oblige to entreat he not to make such a noise , lest he should wake my friend , Mr. George Selwyn . and I add , ' I assure you , sir , I have no idea that you be near I , and most sincerely apologise for give you pain . ' 
 " the Huron take his hand off his tomahawk at this pacific rejoinder , make a bow not ungraciously , say he could not , of course , ask more than an apology from a gentleman of my age   Merci , monsieur ! , and , hear the name of Mr. Selwyn , make another bow to George , and say he have a letter to he from Lord March , which he have have the ill - fortune to mislay . George have put he up for the club , it appear , in conjunction with March , and no doubt these three lamb will fleece each other . meanwhile , my pacified savage sate down with we , and bury the hatchet in another bowl of punch , for which these gentleman must call . Heaven help we ! ' tis eleven o'clock , and here come Bedson with my gruel ! H. W. 
 " to the Honourable . H. S. Conway . " 




 CHAPTER XLI . rake 's Progress 

 People be still very busy in Harry Warrington 's time   not that our young gentleman take much heed of the controversy   in determine the relative literary merit of the ancient and the modern ; and the learn , and the world with they , indeed , pretty generally pronounce in favour of the former . the modern of that day be the ancient of ours , and we speculate upon they in the present year of grace , as our grandchild , a hundred year hence , will give their judgment about we . as for your book - learning , o respectable ancestor   though , to be sure , you have the mighty Gibbon with you , I think you will own that you be beat , and could point to a couple of professor at Cambridge and Glasgow who know more greek than be to be have in your time in all the university of Europe , include that of Athens , if such an one exist . as for science , you be scarce more advanced than those heathen to whom in literature you own yourselves inferior . and in public and private morality ? which be the well , this actual year 1858 , or its predecessor a century back ? gentleman of Mr. Disraeli 's House of Commons ! have every one of you his price , as in Walpole 's or Newcastle 's time , or   and that be the delicate question   have you almost all of you have it ? lady , I do not say that you be a society of Vestals but the chronicle of a hundred year since contain such an amount of scandal , that you may be thankful you do not live in such dangerous time . no : on my conscience , I believe that man and woman be both well ; not only that the Susannas be more numerous , but that the Elders be not nearly so wicked . do you ever hear of such book as Clarissa , Tom Jones , Roderick Random ; painting by contemporary artist , of the man and woman , the life and society , of their day ? suppose we be to describe the doing of such a person as Mr. Lovelace or my Lady Bellaston , or that wonderful " Lady of Quality " who lend her memoir to the author of Peregrine Pickle . how the pure and outraged Nineteenth Century would blush , scream , run out of the room , call away the young lady , and order Mr. Mudie never to send one of that odious author 's book again ! you be fifty - eight year old , madam , and it may be that you be too squeamish , that you cry out before you be hurt , and when nobody have any intention of offend your ladyship . also , it may be that the novelist 's art be injure by the restraint put upon he as many an honest , harmless statue at St. Peter 's and the Vatican be spoil by the tin drapery in which ecclesiastical old woman have swaddle the fair limb of the marble . but in your prudery there be reason . so there be in the state censorship of the Press . the page may contain matter dangerous to bono more . out with your scissor , censor , and clip off the prurient paragraph ! we have nothing for it but to submit . Society , the despot , have give his imperial decree . we may think the statue have be see to great advantage without the tin drapery ; we may plead that the moral be well might we recite the whole fable . away with he not a word ! I never see the pianoforte in the United States with the frille muslin trouser on their leg ; but , depend on it , the muslin cover some of the note as well as the mahogany , muffle the music , and stop the player . 
 to what do this prelude introduce we ? I be think of Harry Warrington , Esquire , in his lodging in Bond Street , London , and of the life which he and many of the young buck of fashion lead in those time , and how I can no more take my faire young reader into they , than Lady Squeams can take her daughter to Cremorne Gardens on an ordinary evening . my dear Miss Diana   psha ! I know you be eight - and - thirty , although you be so wonderfully shy , and want to make we believe you have just leave off schoolroom dinner and a pinafore , when your grandfather be a young man about town , and a member of one of the club at White 's , and din at Pontac 's off the feast provide by Braund and Lebeck , and ride to Newmarket with March and Rockingham , and toast the good in England with Gilly Williams and George Selwyn   and do not   understand George 's joke , of which , indeed , the flavour have very much evaporate since the bottle   the old gentleman lead a life of which your noble aunt   author of Legends of the Squeams 's ; or , Fair Fruits of a Family Tree   have not give you the slight idea . 
 it be before your grandmother adopt those serious view for which she be distinguish during her last long residence at Bath , and after Colonel Tibbalt marry Miss Lye , the rich soap - boiler 's heiress , that her ladyship 's wild oat be sown . when she be young , she be as giddy as the rest of the genteel world . at her house in Hill Street , she have ten card - table on Wednesdays and Sunday evening , except for a short time when Ranelagh be open on Sundays . every night of her life she gamble for eight , nine , ten hour . everybody else in society do the like . she lose ; she win ; she cheat ; she pawn her jewel ; who know what else she be not ready to pawn , so as to find fund to supply her fury for play ? what be that after - supper duel at the Shakspeare 's Head in Covent Garden , between your grandfather and Colonel Tibbalt : where they draw sword and engage only in the presence of Sir John Screwby , who be drunk under the table ? they be interrupt by Mr. John Fielding 's people , and your grandfather be carry home to Hill Street wound in a chair . I tell you those gentleman in powder and ruffle , who turn out the toe of their buckle pump so delicately , be terrible fellow . sword be perpetually be draw ; bottle after bottle be drunk ; oath roar unceasingly in conversation ; tavern - drawer and watchman be pink and maim ; chairman belabour ; citizen insult by reel pleasure - hunter . you have be to Cremorne with proper " voucher " of course ? do you remember our great theatre thirty year ago ? you be too good to go to a play . well , you have no idea what the playhouse be , or what the green box be , when Garrick and Mrs. Pritchard be play before they ! and I , for my child 's sake , thank that good actor in his retirement who be the first to banish that shame from the theatre . no , madam , you be mistaken ; I do not plume myself on my superior virtue . I do not say you be naturally well than your ancestress in her wild , rouge , gambling , flare tearing day ; or even than poor Polly Fogle , who be just take up for shoplifting , and would have be hang for it a hundred year ago . only , I be heartily thankful that my temptation be less , have quite enough to do with those of the present century . 
 so , if Harry Warrington rid down to Newmarket to the October meeting , and lose or win his money there ; if he make one of a party at the Shakspeare or Bedford Head ; if he din at White 's ordinary , and sit down to macco and lansquenet afterwards ; if he box the watch , and make his appearance at the roundhouse ; if he turn out for a short space a wild dissipate , harum - scarum young Harry Warrington ; I , know the weakness of human nature , be not go to be surprised ; and , quite aware of my own shortcoming , do not   intend to be very savage at my neighbour 's . Mr. Sampson be : in his chapel in Long Acre he whip Vice tremendously ; give sin no quarter ; out - curse Blasphemy with superior Anathemas ; knock Drunkenness down , and trample on the prostrate brute wallowing in the gutter ; drag out conjugal infidelity , and pound she with endless stone of rhetoric and , after service , come to dinner at the Star and Garter , make a bowl of punch for Harry and his friend at the Bedford Head , or take a hand at whist at Mr. Warrington 's lodging or my Lord March 's , or wherever there be a supper and good company for he . 
 I often think , however , in respect of Mr. Warrington 's doing at this period of his come to London , that I may have take my usual degrading and uncharitable view of he for , you see , I have not utter a single word of virtuous indignation against his conduct , and if it be not reprehensible , have certainly judge he most cruelly . o the truthful , o the beautiful , o Modesty , O Benevolence , o Pudor , O Mores , o Blushing Shame , o Namby Pamby each with your respective capital letter to your honoured name ! o Niminy , O Piminy ! how shall I dare for to go for to say that a young man ever be a young man ? 
 no doubt , dear young lady , I be calumniate Mr. Warrington accord to my heartless custom . as a proof here be a letter out of the Warrington collection , from Harry to his mother in which there be not a single word that would lead you to suppose he be lead a wild life . and such a letter from an only son to a fond and exemplary parent , we know must be true : 
 " bond STREET , LONDON , October 25 , 1756 . 
 " HONORD MADAM I take up my pen to acknowledge your honored favor of 10 July per Lively Virginia packet , which have duly come to hand , forward by our Bristol agent , and rejoice to hear that the prospect of the crop be so good . ' tis Tully who say that agriculture be the noble pursuit ; how delightful when that pursuit be also prophetable ! 
 " since my last , date from Tunbridge Wells , one or two insadence have occur of which it be nessasery   this word have be much operate upon with the penknife , but be leave sic , no doubt to the writer 's satisfaction .   I should advise my honored mother . our party there break up end of August : the partridge - shoot commencing . Baroness Bernstein , whose kindness to I have be most invariable , have be to Bath , her usual winter resort , and have make I a welcome present of a fifty - pound bill . I ride back with Rev. Mr. Sampson , whose instruction I find most valluble , and my cousin , Lady Maria , to Castlewood .   could Parson Sampson have be dictate the above remark to Mr. Warrington ?   I pay a fly visit on the way to my dear kind friend Col . and Mrs. Lambert , Oakhurst House , who send my honored mother their most affectionate remembrance . the young Miss Lambert , I grieve to say , be dellicate ; and her parent in some anxiety . 
 " at Castlewood I lament to state my stay be short , owe to a quarrel with my cousin William . he be a young man of violent passion , and alas ! addict to liquor , when he have no controul over they . in a triffling dispute about a horse , high word arise between we , and he ayme a blow at I or its equivulent which my Grandfathers my honored mother child could not brook . I rejoyne , and feld he to the ground , whent he be carry almost sencelis to bed . I send to enquire after his health in the morning : but have no further news of he , come away to London where I have be ever since with brief intavle of absence . 
 " know you would wish I to see my dear Grandfathers University of Cambridge , I ride thither lately in company with some friend , pass through part of hart , and lie at the famous bed of Ware . the October meeting be just begin at Cambridge when I go . I see the student in their gownd and capp , and ride over to the famous Newmarket Heath , where there happen to be some race my friend Lord Marchs horse Marrowbones by Cleaver come off winner of a large steak . it be an amusing day the jockey , horse , etc . , very different to our poor race at home the bet awful the rich nobleman here mix with the jox , and bett all round . Cambridge please I : especially King 's College Chapel , of a rich but elegant Gothick . 
 " I have be out into the world , and be make member of the Club at White 's , where I meet gentleman of the first fashion . my Lords Rockingham , Carlisle , Orford , Bolingbroke , Coventry be of my friend , introduce to I by my Lord March , of whom I have often write before . Lady Coventry be a fine woman , but thinn . every lady paint here , old and young ; so , if you and Mountain and Fanny wish to be in fashion , I must send you out some roogepot : everybody play eight , ten , card - table at every house on every receive - night . I be sorry to say all do not play fair , and some do not pay fair . I have be oblige to sit down , and do as Rome do , and have actually see lady whom I could name take my counter from before my face ! 
 " one day , his regiment the 20th be parade in St. James 's Park , a friend of mine , Mr. Wolfe , do I the honour to present I to his Royal Highness the Captain - General , who be most gracious ; a fat , jolly Prince , if I may speak so without disrespect , remind I in his manner of that unhappy General Braddock ; whom we know to our sorrow last year . when he hear my name , and how dear George have serve and fall in Braddock 's unfortunate campaign , he talk a great deal with I ; ask why a young fellow like I do not serve too ; why I do not go to the King of Prussia , who be a great general , and see a campaign or two ; and whether that would not be well than dawdle about at rout and card - party in London ? I say , I would like to go with all my heart , but be an only son now , on leave from my mother , and belong to our estate in Virginia . his Royal Highness say , Mr. Braddock have write home account of Mrs. Esmond 's loyalty , and that he would gladly serve I . Mr. Wolfe and I have wait on he since , at his Royal Highness 's house in Pall Mall . the latter , who be still quite a young man , make the Scots campaign with his Highness , whom Mr. Dempster love so much at home . to be sure , he be too severe : if anything can be top severe against rebel in arm . 
 " Mr. Draper have have half the Stock , my late Papa 's property , transfer to my name . until there can be no doubt of that painful loss in our family which I would give my right hand to replace , the remain stock must remain in the trustee ' name in behalf of he who inherit it . ah , dear mother ! there be no day , scarce any hour , when I do not   think of he . I wish he be by I often . I feel like as if I be well when I be think of he , and would like , for the honour of my family , that he be represent of it here instead of honored madam , your dutiful and affectionate son , HENRY ESMOND WARRINGTON . " 
 " P.S. I be like your sex , who always , they say , put their chief news in a poscrip . I have something to tell you about a person to whom my heart be engage . I shall write more about it , which there be no hurry . safice she be a nobleman 's daughter , and her family as good as our own . " 
 " CLARGIS STREET , LONDON , October 23 , 1756 . 
 " I think , my good sister , we have be all our life a little more than kin and less than kind , to use the word of a poet whom your dear father love dearly . when you be bear in our Western Principallitie , my mother be not as old as Isaac 's ; but even then I be much more than old enough to be yours . and though she give you all she could leave or give , include the little portion of love that ought to have be my share , yet , if we can have good will for one another , we may learn to do without affection : and some little kindness you owe I , for your son 's sake ; as well as your father 's , whom I love and admire more than any man I think ever I know in this world : he be great than almost all , though he make no noyse in it . I have see very many who have , and , believe I , have find but few with such good head and good hart as Mr. Esmond . 
 " have we be well acquaint , I might have give you some advice regard your young gentleman 's introduction to Europe , which you would have take or not , as people do in this world . at least you would have se afterwards , ' what she counsel I be right , and have Harry do as Madam Beatrix wisht , it have be well for he . ' my good sister , it be not for you to know , or for I to whom you never write to tell you , but your boy in come to England and Castlewood find but ill friend there ; except one , an old aunt , of whom all kind of evil hath be speak and se these fifty year past and not without cawse too , perhaps . 
 " now , I must tell Harry 's mother what will doubtless scarce astonish she , that almost everybody who know he love he . he be prudent of his tongue , generous of his money , as bold as a lyon , with an imperious domineering way that set well upon he ; you know whether he be handsome or not : my dear , I like he none the less for not be over witty or wise , and never care for your sett - the - Thames afire gentleman , who be so much more clever than their neighbour . your father 's great friend , Mr. Addison , seem to I but a supercillious prig , and his follower , Sir Dick Steele , be not pleasant in his cupp , nor out of them . and   revenon a luy   your Master Harry will certainly , pot burn the river up with his wit . of book - learning he be as ignorant as any lord in England , and for this I hold he none the bad . if Heaven have not give he a turn that way , ' tis of no use try to bend he . 
 " consider the place he be to hold in his own colony when he return , and the stock he come from , let I tell you , that he hath not mean enough allow he to support his station , and be likely to make the more depence from the narrowness of his income from sheer despair break out of all bound , and become extravagant , which be not his turn . but he like to live as well as the rest of his company , and , between ourselves , have fall into some of the finist and most rakish in England . he think ' tis for the honour of the family not to go back , and many a time call for ortolan and champaign when he would as leaf dine with a stake and a mugg of beer . and in this kind of spirit I have no doubt from what he hath tell I in his talk   which be very naif , as the French say , that his mamma hath encourage he in his high opinion of himself . we woman like our belonging to have it , however little we love to pay the cost . will you have your ladd make a figar in London ? trebble his allowance at the very least , and his Aunt Bernstein   with his honored mamma 's permission   will add a little more on to whatever summ you give he . otherwise he will be spend the little capital I learn he have in this country , which , when a ladd once begin to manger , there be very soon an end to the loaf . please God , I shall be able to leave Henry Esmond 's grandson something at my death ; but my saving be small , and the pension with which my gracious Sovereign hath endow I die with I . as for feu M. de Bernstein , he leave only debt at his decease : the officer of his Majesty 's Electoral Court of Hannover be but scantily pay . 
 " a lady who be at present very high in his Majesty 's confidence hath take a great phancy to your ladd , and will take an early occasion to bring he to the Sovereign 's favorable notice . his Royal Highness the Duke he hath see . if live in America he must , why should not Mr. Esmond Warrington return as Governor of Virginia , and with a title to his name ? that be what I hope for he . 
 " meanwhile , I must be candid with you , and tell you I fear he hath entangle himself here in a very silly engagement . even to marry an old woman for money be scarce pardonable the game ne valant gueres la chandelle Mr. Bernstein , when alive , more than once assure I of this fact , and I believe he , poor gentleman ! to engage yourself to an old woman without money , and to marry she merely because you have promise she , this seem to I a follie which only very young lad fall into , and I fear Mr. Warrington be one . how , or for what consideration , I know not , but my niece Maria Esmond hath escamote a promise from Harry . he know nothing of her anteceden , which I do . she hath lay herself out for twenty husband these twenty year past . I care not how she hath get the promise from he . ' tis a sin and a shame that a woman more than forty year old should surprize the honour of a child like that , and hold he to his word . she be not the woman she pretend to be . a horse jockey   he saith   can not take he in but a woman ! 
 " I write this news to you advisedly , displeasant as it must be . perhaps ' twill bring you to England : but I would be very cautious , above all , very gentle , for the bitt will instantly make his high spirit restive . I fear the property be entail , so that threat of cut he off from it will not move Maria . otherwise I know she to be so mercenary that   though she really hath a great phancy for this handsome ladd   without money she would not hear of he . all I could , and more than I ought , I have do to prevent the match . what and more I will not say in writing ; but that I be , for Henry Esmond 's sake , his grandson 's sincere friend , and madam , your faithful sister and servant , BEATRIX BARONESS DE BERNSTEIN . 
 " to Mrs. Esmond Warrington of Castlewood , in Virginia . " 
 on the back of this letter be write , in Madam Esmond 's hand , " my sister Bernstein 's letter , receive with Henry 's December 24 on receipt of which it be determine my son should instantly go home . " 




 CHAPTER XLII . Fortunatus Nimium 

 though Harry Warrington persist in his determination to keep that dismal promise which his cousin have extract from he , we trust no benevolent reader will think so ill of he as to suppose that the engagement be to the young fellow 's taste , and that he would not be heartily glad to be rid of it . very likely the beating administer to poor Will be to this end ; and Harry may have think , " a boxing - match between we be sure to bring on a quarrel with the family ; in the quarrel with the family , Maria may take her brother 's side . I , of course , will make no retraction or apology . will , in that case , may call I to account , when I know which be the well man . in the midst of the feud , the agreement may come to an end , and I may be a free man once more . " 
 so honest Harry lay his train , and fire it : but , the explosion over , no harm be find to be do , except that William Esmond 's nose be swollen , and his eye black for a week . he do not send a challenge to his cousin , Harry Warrington ; and , in consequence , neither kill Harry , nor be kill by he . will be knock down , and he get up again . how many man of sense would do the same , could they get their little account settle in a private place , with nobody to tell how the score be pay ! Maria by no mean take her family 's side in the quarrel , but declare for her cousin , as do my lord , when advise of the disturbance . will have strike the first blow , Lord Castlewood say , by the chaplain 's showing . it be not the first or the tenth time he have be find quarrel in his cup . Mr. Warrington only show a proper spirit in resent the injury , and it be for Will , not for Harry , to ask pardon . 
 Harry say he would accept no apology as long as his horse be not return or his bet pay . the chronicler have not be able to find out , from any of the paper which have come under his view , how that affair of the bet be finally arrange ; but ' tis certain the cousin presently meet in the house of various friend , and without maul each other . 
 Maria 's eld brother have be at first quite willing that his sister , who have remain unmarried for so many year , and on the train of whose robe , in her long course over the path of life , so many briar , so much mud , so many rent and stain have naturally gather , should marry with any bridegroom who present himself , and if with a gentleman from Virginia , so much the well . she would retire to his wigwam in the forest , and there be dispose of . in the natural course of thing , Harry would survive his elderly bride , and might console himself or not , as he prefer , after her departure . 
 but , after an interview with Aunt Bernstein , which his lordship have on his come to London , he change his opinion : and even go so far as to try and dissuade Maria from the match ; and to profess a pity for the young fellow who be make to undergo a life of misery on account of a silly promise give at one - and - twenty ! 
 Misery , indeed ! Maria be at a loss to know why he be to be miserable . pity , forsooth ! my lord at Castlewood have think it be no pity at all . Maria know what pity mean . her brother have be with Aunt Bernstein : Aunt Bernstein have offer money to break this match off . she understand what my lord mean , but Mr. Warrington be a man of honour , and she could trust he . away , upon this , walk my lord to White 's , or to whatever haunt he frequent . it be probable that his sister have guess too accurately what the nature of his conversation wit Madame Bernstein have be . 
 " and so , " think he , " the end of my virtue be likely to be that the Mohock will fall a prey to other , and that there be no earthly use in my spar he . ' quem deus vult ' what be that schoolmaster 's adage ? if I do not   have he , somebody else will , that be clear . my brother have have a slice ; my dear sister want to swallow the whole of he bodily . here have I be at home respect his youth and innocence forsooth , decline to play beyond the value of a sixpence , and act guardian and Mentor to he . why , I be but a fool to fatten a goose for other people to feed off ! not many a good action have I do in this life , and here be this one , that serve to benefit whom ? other folk . talk of remorse ! by all the fire and fury , the remorse I have be for thing I have not   do and might have do ! why do I spare Lucretia ? she hat I ever after , and her husband go the way for which he be predestine . why have I let this lad off ? that March and the rest , who do not   want he , may pluck he ! and I have a bad repute ; and I be the man people point at , and call the wicked lord , and against whom woman warn their son ! Pardi , I be not a penny bad , only a great deal more unlucky than my neighbour , and ' tis only my cursed weakness that have be my great enemy ! " here , manifestly , in set down a speech which a gentleman only think , a chronicler overdraw his account with the patient reader , who have a right not to accept this draft on his credulity . but have not Livy , and Thucydides , and a score more of historian , make speech for their hero , which we know the latter never think of deliver ? how much more may we then , know my Lord Castlewood 's character so intimately as we do , declare what be pass in his mind , and transcribe his thought on this paper ? what ? a whole pack of the wolf be on the hunt after this lamb , and will make a meal of he presently , and one hungry old hunter be to stand by , and not have a single cutlet ? who have not admire that noble speech of my Lord Clive , when reproach on his return from India with make rather too free with jaghire , lakh , gold mohur , diamond , pearl , and what not ? " upon my life , " say the hero of Plassy , " when I think of my opportunity , I be surprised I take so little ! " 
 to tell disagreeable story of a gentleman , until one be in a manner force to impart they , be always painful to a feeling mind . hence , though I have know , before the very first page of this history be write , what sort of a person my Lord Castlewood be , and in what esteem he be hold by his contemporary , I have keep back much that be unpleasant about he , only allow the candid reader to perceive that he be a nobleman who ought not to be at all of our liking . it be true that my Lord March , and other gentleman of whom he complain , would have think no more of bet with Mr. Warrington for his last shilling , and take their winning , than they would scruple to pick the bone of a chicken ; that they would take any advantage of the game , or their superior skill in it , of the race , and their private knowledge of the horse engage ; in so far , they follow the practice of all gentleman : but when they play , they play fair ; and when they lose , they pay . 
 now Madame Bernstein be loth to tell her virginian nephew all she know to his family 's discredit ; she be even touch by my lord 's forbearance in regard to Harry on his first arrival in Europe ; and please with his lordship 's compliance with her wish in this particular . but in the conversation which she have with her nephew Castlewood regard Maria 's design on Harry , he have speak his mind out with his usual cynicism , vote himself a fool for have spar a lad whom no sparing would eventually keep from ruin ; point out Mr. Harry 's undeniable extravagance and spendthrift associate , his night at faro and hazard , and his ride to Newmarket , and ask why he alone should keep his hand from the young fellow ? in vain Madame Bernstein plead that Harry be poor . Bah ! he be heir to a principality which ought to have be his , Castlewood 's , and might have set up their ruin family .   indeed Madame Bernstein think Mr. Warrington 's virginian property much great than it be .   be there not money - lender in the town who would give he money on postobit in plenty ? Castlewood know as much to his cost : he have apply to they in his father 's lifetime , and the curse crew have eat up two - third of his miserable income . he speak with such desperate candour and ill - humour , that Madame Bernstein begin to be alarmed for her favourite , and determine to caution he at the first opportunity . 
 that evening she begin to pen a billet to Mr. Warrington : but all her life long she be slow with her pen , and dislike use it . " I never know any good come of write more than bon jour or business , " she use to say . " what be the use of write ill , when there be so many clever people who can do it well ? and even then it be well leave alone . " so she send one of her man to Mr. Harry 's lodging , bid he come and drink a dish of tea with her next day , when she propose to warn he . 
 but the next morning she be indispose , and could not receive Mr. Harry when he come : and she keep her chamber for a couple of day , and the next day there be a great engagement , and the next day Mr. Harry be off on some expedition of his own . in the whirl of London life , what man see his neighbour , what brother his sister , what schoolfellow his old friend ? ever so many day pass before Mr. Warrington and his aunt have that confidential conversation which the latter desire . 
 she begin by scold he mildly about his extravagance and madcap frolic   though , in truth , she be charm with he for both   he reply that young man will be young man , and that it be in dutifully wait in attendance on his aunt , he have make the acquaintance with whom he mostly live at present . she then with some prelude , begin to warn he regard his cousin , Lord Castlewood ; on which he break into a bitter laugh , and say the good - natured world have tell he plenty about Lord Castlewood already . " to say of a man of his lordship 's rank , or of any gentleman , ' do not   play with he , ' be more than I like to do , " continue the lady ; " but ... " 
 " oh , you may say on , aunt ! " say Harry , with something like an imprecation on his lip . 
 " and have you play with your cousin already ? " ask the young man 's worldly old monitress . 
 " and lose and win , madam ! " answer Harry , gallantly . " it do not   become I to say which . if we have a bout with a neighbour in Virginia , a bottle , or a pack of card , or a quarrel , we do not   go home and tell our mother . I mean no offence , aunt ! " and , blushing , the handsome young fellow go up and kiss the old lady . he look very brave and brilliant , with his rich lace , his fair face and hair , his fine new suit of velvet and gold . on take leave of his aunt he give his usual sumptuous benefaction to her servant , who crowd round he . it be a rainy wintry day , and my gentleman , to save his fine silk stocking , must come in a chair . " to White 's ! " he call out to the chairman , and away they carry he to the place where he pass a great deal of his time . 
 our Virginian 's friend might have wish that he have be a less sedulous frequenter of that house of entertainment ; but so much may be say in favour of Mr. Warrington that , have engage in play , he fight his battle like a hero . he be not fluster by good luck , and perfectly calm when the chance go against he . if Fortune be proverbially fickle to man at play , how many man be fickle to Fortune , run away frightened from her advance ; and desert she , who , perhaps , have never think of leave they but for their cowardice . " by George , Mr. Warrington , " say Mr. Selwyn , wake up in a rare fit of enthusiasm , " you deserve to win ! you treat your luck as a gentleman should , and as long as she remain with you , behave to she with the most perfect politeness . si celere quatit penna you know the rest no ? well , you be not much the bad off you will call her ladyship 's coach , and make she a bow at the step . look at Lord Castlewood yonder , pass the box . do you ever hear a fellow curse and swear so at lose five or six piece ? she must be a jade indeed , if she long give she favour to such a niggardly canaille as that ! " 
 " we do not   consider our family canaille , sir , " say Mr. Warrington , " and my Lord Castlewood be one of they . " 
 " I forget . I forget , and ask your pardon ! and I make you my compliment upon my lord , and Mr. Will Esmond , his brother , " say Harry 's neighbour at the hazard - table . " the box be with I . five be the main ! deuce Ace ! my usual luck . virtute mea I involvo ! " and he sink back in his chair . 
 whether it be upon this occasion of take the box , that Mr. Harry throw the fifteen main mention in one of those other letter of Mr. Walpole 's , which have not come into his present learn editor 's hand , I know not ; but certain it be , that on his first appearance at White 's , Harry have five or six evening of prodigious good luck , and seem more than ever the Fortunate Youth . the five hundred pound withdraw from his patrimonial inheritance have multiply into thousand . he buy fine clothe , purchase fine horse , give grand entertainment , make handsome present , live as if he have be as rich as Sir James Lowther , or his Grace of Bedford , and yet the five thousand pound never seem to diminish . no wonder that he give where giving be so easy ; no wonder that he be generous with Fortunatus 's purse in his pocket . I say no wonder that he give , for such be his nature . other Fortunati tie up the endless purse , drink small beer , and go to bed with a tallow candle . 
 during this vein of his luck , what must Mr. Harry do , but find out from Lady Maria what her ladyship 's debt be , and pay they off to the last shilling . her stepmother and half - sister , who do not love she , he treat to all sort of magnificent present . " have you not well get yourself arrest , will ? " my lord sardonically say to his brother . " although you bit he in that affair of the horse , the Mohock will certainly take you out of pawn . " it be then that Mr. William feel a true remorse , although not of that humble kind which send the repentant Prodigal to his knee . " confound it , " he groan , " to think that I have let this fellow slip for such a little matter as forty pound ! why , he be good for a thousand at least . " 
 as for Maria , that generous creature accept the good fortune send she with a grateful heart ; and be ready to accept as much more as you please . have pay off her debt to her various milliner , tradesman , and purveyor , she forthwith proceed to contract new one . Mrs. Betty , her ladyship 's maid , go round inform the tradespeople that her mistress be about to contract a matrimonial alliance with a young gentleman of immense fortune ; so that they might give my lady credit to any amount . have hear the same story twice or thrice before , the tradesfolk might not give it entire credit , but their bill be pay : even to Mrs. Pincott , of Kensington , my lady show no rancour , and affably order fresh supply from she : and when she drive about from the mercer to the toy - shop , and from the toy - shop to the jeweller in a coach , with she maid and Mr. Warrington inside , they think she a fortunate woman indeed , to have secure the Fortunate Youth , though they might wonder at the taste of this latter in have select so elderly a beauty . Mr. Sparks , of Tavistock Street , Covent Garden , take the liberty of wait upon Mr. Warrington at his lodging in Bond Street , with the pearl necklace and the gold etwee which he have buy in Lady Maria 's company the day before ; and ask whether he , Sparks , should leave they at his honour 's lodging , or send they to her ladyship with his honour 's compliment ? Harry add a ring out of the stock which the jeweller happen to bring with he , to the necklace and the etwee ; and sumptuously bid that individual to send he in the bill , take a majestic leave of Mr. Sparks , who retire , bow even to Gumbo , as he quit his honour 's presence . 
 nor do his bounty end here . Ere many day the pleased young fellow drive up in his phaeton to Mr. Sparks ' shop , and take a couple of trinket for two young lady , whose parent have be kind to he , and for whom he entertain a sincere regard . " ah ! " think he , " how I wish I have my poor George 's wit , and genius for poetry ! I would send these present with pretty verse to Hetty and Theo . I be sure , if goodwill and real regard could make a poet of I , I should have no difficulty in find rhyme . " and so he call in Parson Sampson , and they concoct a billet together . 




 CHAPTER XLIII . in which Harry fly high 

 so Mr. Harry Warrington , of Virginia , have his lodging in Bond Street , London , England , and live upon the fat of the land , and drink bumper of the good wine thereof . his title of Fortunate Youth be pretty generally recognise . be young , wealthy , good - look , and fortunate , the fashionable world take he by the hand and make he welcome . and do not , my dear brother , let we cry out too loudly against the selfishness of the world for be kind to the young , handsome , and fortunate , and frown upon you and I , who may be , for argument 's sake , old , ugly , and the miserable dog under the sun . if I have a right to choose my acquaintance , and at the club , let we say prefer the company of a lively , handsome , well - dress , gentleman like young man , who amuse I , to that of a slouching , ill - wash , misanthropic h - murderer , a ceaselessly prating coxcomb , or what not ; have not society the aggregate you and I a right to the same choice ? Harry be like because he be likeable ; because he be rich , handsome , jovial , well - bear , well - breed , brave ; because , with jolly toper , he like a jolly song and a bottle ; because , with gentleman sportsman , he love any game that be a - foot or a - horseback ; because , with lady , he have a modest blushing timidity which render the lad interesting ; because , to those humbler than himself in degree he be always magnificently liberal , and anxious to spare annoyance . our Virginian be very grand , and high and mighty , to be sure ; but , in those time , when the distinction of rank yet obtain , to be high and distant with his inferior , bring no unpopularity to a gentleman . remember that , in those day , the Secretary of State always knelt when he go to the king with his despatch of a morning , and the Under - Secretary never dare to sit down in his chief 's presence . if I be Secretary of State   and such there have be amongst man of letter since Addison 's day   I should not like to kneel when I go in to my audience with my despatch - bog . if I be under - Secretary , I should not like to have to stand , whilst the Right Honourable Benjamin or the Right Honourable Sir Edward look over the paper . but there be a modus in rebus : there be certain line which must be draw : and I be only half pleased for my part , when Bob Bowstreet , whose connection with letter be through Policeman X and Y , and Tom Garbage , who be an esteemed contributor to the Kennel Miscellany , propose to join fellowship as brother literary man , slap I on the back , and call I old boy , or by my christian name . 
 as much pleasure as the town could give in the winter season of 1756 - 57 , Mr. Warrington have for the asking . there be opera for he , in which he take but moderate delight .   a prodigious deal of satire be bring to bear against these italian opera , and they be assail for be foolish , Popish , unmanly , unmeaning ; but people go , nevertheless .   there be the theatre , with Mr. Garrick and Mrs. Pritchard at one house , and Mrs. Clive at another . there be masquerade and ridotto frequent by all the fine society ; there be their lordship ' and ladyship ' own private drum and assembly , which begin and end with card , and which Mr. Warrington do not like so well as White 's , because the play there be neither so high nor so fair as at the club - table . 
 one day his kinsman , Lord Castlewood , take he to court , and present Harry to his Majesty , who be now come to town from Kensington . but that gracious sovereign either do not like Harry 's introducer , or have other reason for be sulky . his Majesty only say , " oh , hear of you from Lady Yarmouth . the Earl of Castlewood "   turn to his lordship , and speak in German   ' shall tell he that he play too much ! " and so say , the Defender of the Faith turn his royal back . 
 Lord Castlewood shrink back quite frightened at this cold reception of his august master . 
 " what do he say ? " ask Harry . 
 " his Majesty think they play too high at White 's , and be displeased , " whisper the nobleman . 
 " if he do not want we , we have well not come again , that be all , " say Harry , simply . " I never , somehow , consider that german fellow a real king of England . " 
 " hush ! for Heaven 's sake , hold your confound colonial tongue ! " cry out my lord . " do not   you see the wall here have ear ! " 
 " and what then ? " ask Mr. Warrington . " why , look at the people ! hang I , if it be not quite a curiosity ! they be all shake hand with I , and bow to I , and flatter I just now ; and at present they avoid I as if I be the plague ! " 
 " shake hand , nephew , " say a broad - face , broad - shoulder gentleman , in a scarlet - laced waistcoat , and a great old - fashioned wig . " I hear what you say . I have ear like the wall , look you . and , now , if other people show you the cold shoulder , I will give you my hand ; " and so say , the gentleman put out a great brown hand , with which he grasp Harry 's . " something of my brother about your eye and face . though I suppose in your island you grow more wiry and thin like . I be thine uncle , child . my name be Sir Miles Warrington . my lord know I well enough . " 
 my lord look very frightened and yellow . " yes , my dear Harry . this be your paternal uncle , Sir Miles Warrington . " 
 " might as well have come to see we in Norfolk , as dangle about play the fool at Tunbridge Wells , Mr. Warrington , or Mr. Esmond , which do you call yourself ? " say the Baronet . " the old lady call herself Madam Esmond , do not   she ? " 
 " my mother be not ashamed of her father 's name , nor be I , uncle , " say Mr. Harry , rather proudly . 
 " well say , lad ! come home and eat a bit of mutton with Lady Warrington , at three , in Hill Street , that be if you can do without your White 's kickshaw . you need not look frightened , my Lord Castlewood ! I shall tell no tale out of school . " 
 " I I be sure Sir Miles Warrington will act as a gentleman ! " say my lord , in much perturbation . 
 " belike , he will , " growl the Baronet , turn on his heel . " and thou wilt come , young man , at three ; and mind , good roast mutton wait for nobody . Thou hast a great look of thy father . Lord bless we , how we use to beat each other ! he be small than I , and in course young ; but many a time he have the good of it . take it he be henpecke when he marry , and Madam Esmond take the spirit out of he when she get he in her island . Virginia be an island . ai not   it an island ? " 
 Harry laugh , and say " no ! " and the jolly Baronet , go off , say , " well , island or not , thou must come and tell all about it to my lady . she will know whether ' tis an island or not . " 
 " my dear Mr. Warrington , " say my lord , with an appealing look , " I need not tell you that , in this great city , every man have enemy , and that there be a great , great deal of detraction and scandal . I never speak to you about Sir Miles Warrington , precisely because I do know he , and because we have have difference together . should he permit himself remark to my disparagement , you will receive they cum grano , and remember that it be from an enemy they come . " and the pair walk out of the King 's apartment and into Saint James 's Street . Harry find the news of his cold reception at court have already precede he to White 's . the King have turn his back upon he . the King be jealous of Harry 's favour with the favourite . Harry be au mieux with Lady Yarmouth . a score of gentleman wish he a compliment upon his conquest . before night it be a settled matter that this be amongst the other victory of the Fortunate Youth . 
 Sir Miles tell his wife and Harry as much , when the young man appear at the appoint hour at the Baronet 's dinner - table , and he rally Harry in his simple rustic fashion . the lady , at first a grand and stately personage , tell Harry , on their further acquaintance , that the reputation which the world have make for he be so bad , that at first she have give he but a frigid welcome . with the young lady , Sir Miles 's daughter , it be " how d'ye do , cousin ? " and " no , thank you , cousin , " and a number of prim curtsey to the Virginian , as they greet he and take leave of he . the little boy , the heir of the house , din at table , under the care of his governor ; and , have his glass of port by papa after dinner , give a loose to his innocent tongue , and ask many question of his cousin . at last the innocent youth say , after look hard in Harry 's face , " be you wicked , cousin Harry ? you do not   look very wicked ! " 
 " my dear Master Miles ! " expostulate the tutor , turn very red . 
 " but you know you say he be wicked ! " cry the child . 
 " we be all miserable sinner , Miley , " explain papa . " have not   you hear the clergyman say so every Sunday ? " 
 " yes , but not so very wicked as cousin Harry . be it true that you gamble , cousin , and drink all night with wicked man , and frequent the company of wicked woman ? you know you say so , Mr. Walker and mamma say so , too , that Lady Yarmouth be a wicked woman . " 
 " and you be a little pitcher , " cry papa : " and my wife , nephew Harry , be a staunch Jacobite you will not   like she the bad for that . take Miles to his sister , Mr. Walker , and Topsham shall give thee a ride in the park , child , on thy little horse . " the idea of the little horse console Master Miles ; for , when his father order he away to his sister , he have begin to cry bitterly , bawl out that he would far rather stay with his wicked cousin . 
 " they have make you a sad reputation among them , nephew ! " say the jolly Baronet . " my wife , you must know , of late year , and since the death of my poor eld son , have take to , to , hum ! to Tottenham Court Road and Mr. Whitfield 's preaching : and we have have one Ward about the house , a friend of Mr. Walker 's yonder , who have recount sad story about you and your brother at home . " 
 " about I , Sir Miles , as much as he please , " cry Harry , warm with port : " but I will break any man 's bone who dare say a word against my brother ! why , sir , that fellow be not fit to buckle my dear George 's shoe ; and if I find he repeat at home what he dare to say in our house in Virginia , I promise he a second caning . " 
 " you seem to stand up for your friend , nephew Harry , " say the Baronet . " fill thy glass , lad , thou art not as bad as thou hast be paint . I always tell my lady so . I drink Madam Esmond Warrington 's health , of Virginia , and will have a full bumper for that toast . " 
 Harry , as in duty bind , empty his glass , fill again , and drank Lady Warrington and Master Miles . 
 " Thou wouldst be heir to four thousand acre in Norfolk , do he die , though , " say the Baronet . 
 " God forbid , sir , and be praise that I have acre enough in Virginia of my own ! " say Mr. Warrington . he go up presently and take a dish of coffee with Lady Warrington : he talk to the young lady of the house . he be quite easy , pleasant , and natural . there be one of they somewhat like Fanny Mountain , and this young lady become his special favourite . when he go away , they all agree their wicked cousin be not near so wicked as they have imagine he to be : at any rate , my lady have strong hope of rescue he from the pit . she send he a good book that evening , whilst Mr. Harry be at White 's ; with a pretty note , pray that Law 's Call might be of service to he : and , this despatch , she and her daughter go off to a rout at the house of a minister 's lady . but Harry , before he go to White 's , have drive to his friend Mr. Sparks , in Tavistock Street , and purchase more trinket for his female cousin " from their aunt in Virginia , " he say . you see , he be full of kindness : he kindle and warm with prosperity . there be man on whom wealth hath no such fortunate influence . it harden base heart : it make those who be mean and servile , mean and proud . if it should please the god to try I with ten thousand a year , I will , of course , meekly submit myself to their decree , but I will pray they to give I strength enough to bear the trial . all the girl in Hill Street be delighted at get the present from Aunt Warrington in Virginia and address a collective note , which must have astonish that good lady when she receive it in spring - time , when she and Mountain and Fanny be on a visit to grim desert Castlewood , when the snow have clear away and a thousand peach - tree flush with blossom . " poor boy ! " the mother think " this be some present he give his cousin in my name , in the time of his prosperity nay , of his extravagance and folly . how quickly his wealth have pass away ! but he ever have a kind heart for the poor mountain ; and we must not forget he in his need . it behove we to be more than ever careful of our own expense , my good people ! " and so , I dare say , they warm themselves by one log , and eat of one dish , and work by one candle . and the widow 's servant , whom the good soul begin to pinch more and more I fear , lie , stole , and cheat more and more : and what be save in one way , be steal in another . 
 one afternoon , Mr. Harry sate in his Bond Street lodging , array in his dressing - gown , sip his chocolate , surround by luxury , encase in satin , and yet envelop in care . a few week previously when the luck be with he , and he be scatter his benefaction to and fro , he have royally tell Parson Sampson to get together a list of his debt which he , Mr. Warrington , would pay . accordingly Sampson have go to work , and have get together a list , not of all his debt no man ever do set down all , but such a catalogue as he think sufficient to bring in to Mr. Warrington , at whose breakfast - table the divine have humbly wait until his honour should choose to attend it . 
 Harry appear at length , very pale and languid , in curl - paper , and scarce any appetite for his breakfast ; and the chaplain , fumble with his schedule in his pocket , humbly ask if his patron have have a bad night ? he have be bring home from White 's by two chairman at five o'clock in the morning ; have catch a confound cold , for one of the window of the chair would not shut , and the rain and snow come in , finally , be in such a bad humour , that all poor Sampson 's quirk and joke could scarcely extort a smile from he . 
 at last , to be sure , Mr. Warrington burst into a loud laugh . it be when the poor chaplain , after a sufficient discussion of muffin , egg , tea , the news , the theatre , and so forth , pull a paper out of his pocket and in a piteous tone say , " here be that schedule of debt which your honour ask for two hundred and forty - three pound every shilling I owe in the world , thank Heaven ! that be ahem ! every shilling of which the payment will in the least inconvenience I and I need not tell my dear patron that I shall consider he my saviour and benefactor ! " 
 it be then that Harry , take the paper and eye the chaplain with rather a wicked look , burst into a laugh , which be , however , anything but jovial . wicked execration , moreover , accompany this outbreak of humour , and the luckless chaplain feel that his petition have come at the wrong moment . 
 " confound it , why do not   you bring it on Monday ? " Harry ask . 
 " confound I , why do I not bring it on Monday ? " echo the chaplain 's timid soul . " it be my luck my usual luck . have the card be against you , Mr. Warrington ? " 
 " yes : a plague on they . Monday night , and last night , have both go against I . do not   be frightened , chaplain , there be money enough in the locker yet . but I must go into the City and get some . " 
 " what , sell out , sir ? " ask his reverence , with a voice that be reassure , though it intend to be alarm . 
 " sell out , sir ? yes ! I borrow a hundred off Mackreth in counter last night , and must pay he at dinner - time . I will do your business for you nevertheless , and never fear , my good Mr. Sampson . come to breakfast to - morrow , and we will see and deliver your reverence from the Philistines . " but though he laugh in Sampson 's presence , and strove to put a good face upon the matter , Harry 's head sink down on his chest when the parson quit he , and he sate over the fire , beat the coal about with the poker , and give utterance to many disjoint naughty word , which show , but do not relieve , the agitation of his spirit . 
 in this mood , the young fellow be interrupt by the appearance of a friend , who , on any other day even on that one when his conscience be so uneasy be welcome to Mr. Warrington . this be no other than Mr. Lambert , in his military dress , but with a cloak over he , who have come from the country , have be to the Captain - General 's levee that morning , and have come thence to visit his young friend in Bond Street . 
 Harry may have think Lambert 's greeting rather cold ; but be occupy with his own affair , he put away the notion . how be the lady of Oakhurst , and Miss Hetty , who be ail when he pass through in the autumn ? purely ? Mr. Warrington be very glad . they be come to stay a while in London with their friend , Lord Wrotham ? Mr. Harry be delighted though it must be confess his face do not exhibit any peculiar sign of pleasure when he hear the news . 
 " and so you live at White 's , and with the great folk ; and you fare sumptuously every day , and you pay your court at St. James 's , and make one at my Lady Yarmouth 's rout , and at all the card - party in the Court end of the town ? " ask the Colonel . 
 " my dear Colonel , I do what other folk do , " say Harry , with rather a high manner . 
 " other folk be rich folk than some folk , my dear lad . " 
 " Sir ! " say Mr. Warrington , " I would thank you to believe that I owe nothing for which I can not pay ! " 
 " I should never have speak about your affair , " say the other , not notice the young man 's haughty tone , " but that you yourself confide they to I . I hear all sort of story about the Fortunate Youth . only at his Royal Highness 's even today , they be say how rich you be already , and I do not undeceive they " 
 " Colonel Lambert , I can not help the world gossip about I ! " cry Mr. Warrington , more and more impatient . 
 " and what prodigious sum you have win . eighteen hundred one night two thousand another six or eight thousand in all ! oh ! there be gentleman from White 's at the levee too , I can assure you , and the army can fling a main as well as you civilian ! " 
 " I wish they would meddle with their own affair , " say Harry , scowl at his old friend . 
 " and I , too , you look as if you be go to say . well , my boy , it be my affair and you must let Theo 's father and Hetty 's father , and Harry Warrington 's father 's old friend say how it be my affair . " here the Colonel draw a packet out of his pocket , whereof the lappet and the coat - tail and the general pocket accommodation be much more ample than in the scant military garment of present warrior . " look you , Harry . these trinket which you send with the kind heart in the world to people who love you , and would cut off their little hand to spare you needless pain , could never be buy by a young fellow with two or three hundred a year . why , a nobleman might buy these thing , or a rich City banker , and send they to his to his daughter , let we say . " 
 " Sir , as you say , I mean only kindness , " say Harry , blush burning - red . 
 " but you must not give they to my girl , my boy . hester and Theodosia Lambert must not be dress up with the winning off the gaming - table , save your presence . it go to my heart to bring back the trinket . Mrs. Lambert will keep she present , which be of small value , and send you her love and a God bless you and so say I , Harry Warrington , with all my heart . " here the good Colonel 's voice be much move , and his face grow very red , and he pass his hand over his eye ere he hold it out . 
 but the spirit of rebellion be strong in Mr. Warrington . he rise up from his seat , never offer to take the hand which his senior hold out to he . " give I leave to tell Colonel Lambert , " he say , " that I have have somewhat too much advice from he . you be for ever volunteer it , sir , and when I do not   ask it . you make it your business to inquire about my gain at play , and about the company I keep . what right have you to control my amusement or my companion ? I strive to show my sense of your former kindness by little present to your family , and you fle you bring they back . " 
 " I can not   do otherwise , Mr. Warrington , " say the Colonel , with a very sad face . 
 " such a slight may mean nothing here , sir , but in our country it mean war , sir ! " cry Mr. Warrington . " God forbid I should talk of draw a sword against the father of lady who have be as mother and sister to I : but you have wound my heart , Colonel Lambert you have , I will not   say insult , but humiliate I , and this be a treatment I will bear from no man alive ! my servant will attend you to the door , sir ! " say which , and rustle in his brocade dressing - gown , Mr. Warrington , with much state , walk off to his bedroom .

 CHAPTER XLV . in which Harry find two uncle 

 we have all of we , no doubt , have a fine experience of the world , and a vast variety of character have pass under our eye ; but there be one sort of man not an uncommon object of satire in novel and play of whom I confess to have meet with scarce any specimen at all in my intercourse with this sinful mankind . I mean , mere religious hypocrite , preach for ever , and not believe a word of their own sermon ; infidel in broad brim and sable , expound , exhorting , comminating , blessing , without any faith in their own paradise , or fear about their pandemonium . look at those candid troop of hobnail clump to church on a Sunday evening ; those rustle maid - servant in their ribbon whom the young apprentice follow ; those little regiment of schoolboy ; those trim young maiden and staid matron , march with their glistening prayer - book , as the chapel bell chink yonder   pass Ebenezer , very likely , where the congregation of umbrella , great bonnet , and patten , be by this time assemble under the flare gas - lamp . look at those ! how many of they be hypocrite , think you ? very likely the maid - servant be think of her sweetheart : the grocer be cast about how he can buy that parcel of sugar , and whether the County Bank will take any more of his paper : the head - schoolboy be con Latin verse for Monday 's exercise : the young scapegrace remember that after his service and sermon , there will be papa 's exposition at home , but that there will be pie for supper : the clerk who call out the psalm have his daughter in trouble , and drone through his response scarcely aware of their meaning : the very moment the parson hide his face on his cushion , he may be think of that bill which be come due on Monday . these people be not heavenly - minded ; they be of the world , worldly , and have not yet get their foot off of it ; but they be not hypocrite , look you . folk have their religion in some handy mental lock - up , as it be a valuable medicine , to be take in ill health ; and a man administer his nostrum to his neighbour , and recommend his private cure for the other 's complaint . " my dear madam , you have spasm ? you will find these drop infallible ! " " you have be take too much wine , my good sir ? by this pill you may defy any evil consequence from too much wine , and take your bottle of port daily . " of spiritual and bodily physic , who be more fond and eager dispenser than woman ? and we know that , especially a hundred year ago , every lady in the country have her still - room , and her medicine chest , her pill , powder , potion , for all the village round . 
 my Lady Warrington take charge of the conscience and the digestion of her husband 's tenant and family . she have the faith and health of the servants'-hall in keeping . Heaven can tell whether she know how to doctor they rightly : but , be it pill or doctrine , she administer one or the other with equal belief in her own authority , and her disciple swallow both obediently . she believe herself to be one of the most virtuous , self - deny , wise , learn woman in the world ; and , din this opinion perpetually into the ear of all round about she , succeed in bring not few person to join in her persuasion . 
 at Sir Miles 's dinner there be so fine a sideboard of plate , and such a number of man in livery , that it require some presenter : of mind to perceive that the beer be of the small which the butler bring round in the splendid tankard , and that there be but one joint of mutton on the grand silver dish . when Sir Miles call the King 's health , and smack his jolly lip over his wine , he eye it and the company as if the liquor be ambrosia . he ask Harry Warrington whether they have port like that in Virginia ? he say that be nothing to the wine Harry should taste in Norfolk . he praise the wine so , that Harry almost believe that it be good , and wink into his own glass , try to see some of the merit which his uncle perceive in the ruby nectar . 
 just as we see in many a well - regulate family of this present century , the Warringtons have their two paragon . of the two grown daughter , the one be the great beauty , the other the great genius and angel of any young lady then alive , as Lady Warrington tell Harry . the eld , the Beauty , be engage to dear Tom Claypool , the fond mother inform her cousin Harry in confidence . but the second daughter , the Genius and Angel , be for ever set upon our young friend to improve his wit and moral . she sing to he at the harpsichord rather out of tune for an angel , Harry think ; she be ready with advice , instruction , conversation with almost too much instruction and advice , think Harry , who would have far prefer the society of the little cousin who remind he of Fanny Mountain at home . but the last - mention young maiden after dinner retire to her nursery commonly . Beauty go off on her own avocation ; mamma have to attend to her poor or write her voluminous letter ; papa doze in his arm - chair ; and the Genius remain to keep her young cousin company . 
 the calm of the house somehow please the young man , and he like to take refuge there away from the riot and dissipation in which he ordinarily live . certainly no welcome could be kind than that which he get . the door be open to he at all hour . if Flora be not at home , Dora be ready to receive he . Ere many day ' acquaintance , he and his little cousin Miles have be to have a galloping - match in the Park , and Harry , who be kind and generous to every man alive who come near he , have in view the purchase of a little horse for his cousin , far well than that which the boy ride , when the circumstance occur which bring all our poor Harry 's coach and horse to a sudden breakdown . 
 though Sir Miles Warrington have imagine Virginia to be an island , the lady be much well instruct in geography , and anxious to hear from Harry all about his home and his native country . he , on his part , be not averse to talk about it . he describe to they the length and breadth of his estate ; the river which it coast ; the produce which it bore . he have have with a friend a little practice of survey in his boyhood . he make a map of his county , with some fine town here and there , which , in truth , be but log - hut   but , for the honour of his country , he be desirous that they should wear as handsome a look as possible . here be Potomac ; here be James river ; here be the wharf whence his mother 's ship and tobacco be bring to the sea . in truth , the estate be as large as a county . he do not brag about the place overmuch . to see the handsome young fellow , in a fine suit of velvet and silver lace , make his draught , point out this hill and that forest or town , you might have imagine he a travel prince describe the realm of the queen his mother . he almost fancy himself to be so at time . he have mile where gentleman in England have acre . not only Dora listen but the beauteous Flora bow her fair head and hear he with attention . why , what be young Tom Claypool , their brother baronet 's son in Norfolk with his great boot , his great voice , and his heirdom to a poor five thousand acre , compare to this young american prince and charming stranger ? Angel as she be , Dora begin to lose her angelic temper , and to twit Flora for a flirt . Claypool in his red waistcoat , would sit dumb before the splendid Harry in his ruffle and lace , talk of March and Chesterfield , Selwyn and Bolingbroke , and the whole company of macaronis . Mamma begin to love Harry more and more as a son . she be anxious about the spiritual welfare of those poor Indians , of those poor negro in Virginia . what could she do to help dear Madam Esmond   a precious woman , she know !   in the good work ? she have a serious butler and housekeeper : they be delighted with the spiritual behaviour and sweet musical gift of Gumbo . 
 " ah ! Harry , Harry ! you have be a sad wild boy ! why do you not come soon to we , sir , and not lose your time amongst the spendthrift and the vain world ? but ' tis not yet too late . we must reclaim thee , dear Harry ! must not   we , Sir Miles ? must not   we Dora ? must not   we , Flora ? " 
 the three lady all look up to the ceiling . they will reclaim the dear prodigal . it be which shall reclaim he most . Dora sit by and watch Flora . as for mamma when the girl be away , she talk to he more and more seriously , more and more tenderly . she will be a mother to he in the absence of his own admirable parent . she give he a hymn - book . she kiss he on the forehead . she be actuate by the pure love , tenderness , religious regard , towards her dear , wayward , wild , amiable nephew . 
 whilst these sentimentality be go on , it be to be presume that Mr. Warrington keep his own counsel about his affair out - of - door , which we have see be in the very bad condition . he who have be favour by fortune for so many week be suddenly desert by she , and a few day have serve to kick down all his heap of winning . do we say that my Lord Castlewood , his own kinsman , have deal unfairly by the young Virginian , and in the course of a couple of afternoon ' closet practice have rob he ? we would insinuate nothing so disrespectful to his lordship 's character ; but he have win from Harry every shilling which properly belong to he , and would have play he for his reversion , but that the young man fling up his hand when he see himself so far beat , and declare that he must continue the battle no more . remember that there still remain a spar out of the wreck , as it be that portion which he have set aside for poor Sampson Harry venture it at the gaming - table ; but that last resource go down along with the rest of Harry 's possession , and Fortune flutter off in the storm , leave the luckless adventurer almost naked on the shore . 
 when a man be young and generous and hearty the loss of money scarce afflict he . Harry would sell his horse and carriage , and diminish his train of life . if he want immediate supply of money , would not his Aunt Bernstein be his banker , or his kinsman who have win so much from he , or his kind Uncle Warrington and Lady Warrington who be always talk virtue and benevolence , and declare that they love he as a son ? he would call upon these , or any one of they whom he might choose to favour , at his leisure ; meanwhile , Sampson 's story of his landlord 's distress touch the young gentleman , and , in order to raise a hasty supply for the clergyman , he carry off all his trinket to a certain pawnbroker 's shop in St. Martin 's Lane . 
 now this broker be a relative or partner of that very Mr. Sparks of Tavistock Street , from whom Harry have purchase purchase do we say ? no ; take the trinket which he have intend to present to his Oakhurst friend ; and it chance that Mr. Sparks come to visit his brother - tradesman very soon after Mr. Warrington have dispose of his good . recognise immediately the little enamel diamond - handle repeater which he have sell to the Fortunate Youth , the jeweller break out into expression regard Harry which I will not mention here , be already accuse of speak much too plainly . a gentleman who be acquaint with a pawnbroker , we may be sure have a bailiff or two amongst his acquaintance ; and those bailiff have follower who , at the bidding of the impartial Law , will touch with equal hand the fierce captain 's epaulet or the fine macaroni 's shoulder . the very gentleman who have seize upon Lady Maria at Tunbridge be set upon her cousin in London . they easily learn from the garrulous Gumbo that his honour be at Sir Miles Warrington 's house in Hill Street , and whilst the black be court Mrs. Lambert 's maid at the adjoining mansion , Mr. Costigan and his assistant lie in wait for poor Harry , who be enjoy the delight of intercourse with a virtuous family circle assemble round his aunt 's table . never have Uncle Miles be more cordial , never have Aunt Warrington be more gracious , gentle , and affectionate ; Flora look unusually lovely , Dora have be more than ordinarily amiable . at parting , my lady give he both her hand , and call benediction from the ceiling down upon he . Papa have say in his most jovial manner , " hang it , nephew ! when I be thy age I should have kiss two such fine girl as Do and Flo ere this , and my own flesh and blood too ! do not   tell I ! I should , my Lady Warrington ! odd - fish ! ' tis the boy blush , and not the girl ! I think I suppose they be use to it . he , he ! " 
 " Papa ! " cry the virgin . 
 " Sir Miles ! " say the august mother at the same instant .
