 " there , there ! " say papa . " a kiss will not   do no harm , and will not   tell no tale : will it , nephew Harry ? " I suppose , during the utterance of the above three brief phrase , the harmless little osculatory operation have take place , and blush cousin Harry have touch the damask cheek of cousin Flora and cousin Dora . 
 as he go downstairs with his uncle , mamma make a speech to the girl , look , as usual , up to the ceiling , and say , " what precious quality your poor dear cousin have ! what shrewdness mingle with his simplicity , and what a fine genteel manner , though upon mere worldly elegance I set little store . what a dreadful pity to think that such a vessel should ever be lose ! we must rescue he , my love . we must take he away from those wicked companion , and those horrible Castlewoods not that I would speak ill of my neighbour . but I shall hope , I shall pray , that he may be rescue from his evil course ! " and again Lady Warrington eye the cornice in a most determined manner , as the girl wistfully look towards the door behind which their interesting cousin have just vanish . 
 his uncle will go downstairs with he . he call " God bless you , my boy ! " most affectionately : he press Harry 's hand , and repeat his valuable benediction at the door . as it close , the light from the hall within have sufficiently illuminate Mr. Warrington 's face and figure , two gentleman , who have be stand on the opposite side of the way , advance rapidly , and one of they take a strip of paper out of his pocket , and put his hand upon Mr. Warrington 's shoulder , declare he his prisoner . a hackney - coach be in attendance , and poor Harry go to sleep in Chancery Lane . 
 oh , to think that a virginian prince 's back should be slap by a ragged bailiff follower ! that Madam Esmond 's son should be in a spunge - house in Cursitor Street ! I do not envy our young prodigal his rest on that dismal night . let we hit he now he be down , my beloved young friend . let we imagine the sting of remorse keep he wakeful on his dingy pillow ; the horrid jollification of other harden inmate of the place ring in his ear from the room hard by , where they sit boozing ; the rage and shame and discomfiture . no pity on he , I say , my honest young gentleman , for you , of course , have never indulge in extravagance or folly , or pay the reckoning of remorse . 




 CHAPTER XLVI . chain and Slavery 

 remorse for past misdeed and folly Harry sincerely feel , when he find himself a prisoner in that dismal lock - up house , and wrath and annoyance at the idea of be subject to the indignity of arrest ; but the present unpleasantry he feel sure could only be momentary . he have twenty friend who would release he from his confinement : to which of they should he apply , be the question . Mr. Draper , the man of business , who have be so obsequious to he : his kind uncle the Baronet , who have offer to make his house Harry 's home , who love he as a son : his cousin Castlewood , who have win such large sum from he : his noble friend at the Chocolate - House , his good Aunt Bernstein any one of these Harry feel sure would give he a help in his trouble , though some of the relative , perhaps , might administer to he a little scolding for his imprudence . the main point be , that the matter should be transact quietly , for Mr. Warrington be anxious that as few as possible of the public should know how a gentleman of his prodigious importance have be subject to such a vulgar process as an arrest . as if the public do not end by know everything it care to know . as if the dinner I shall have to - day , and the hole in the stocking which I wear at this present writing , can be keep a secret from some enemy or other who have a mind to pry it out though my boot be on , and my door be lock when I dress myself ! I mention that hole in the stocking for sake of example merely . the world can pry out everything about we which it have a mind to know . but then there be this consolation , which man will never accept in their own case , that the world do not   care . consider the amount of scandal it have be force to hear in its time , and how weary and blase it must be of that kind of intelligence . you be take to prison , and fancy yourself indelibly disgrace ? you be bankrupt under odd circumstance ? you drive a queer bargain with your friend and be find out , and imagine the world will punish you ? Psha ! your shame be only vanity . go and talk to the world as if nothing have happen , and nothing have happen . tumble down ; brush the mud off your clothe ; appear with a smile countenance , and nobody care . do you suppose Society be go to take out its pocket - handkerchief and be inconsolable when you die ? why should it care very much , then , whether your worship grace yourself or disgrace yourself ? whatever happen it talk , meet , joke , yawn , have its dinner , pretty much as before . therefore do not   be so conceited about yourself as to fancy your private affair of so much importance , mi fili . whereas Mr. Harry Warrington chafe and fume as though all the world be tingle with the touch of that hand which have be lay on his sublime shoulder . 
 " a pretty sensation my arrest must have create at the club ! " think Harry . " I suppose that Mr. Selwyn will be cut all sort of joke about my misfortune , plague take he ! everybody round the table will have hear of it . March will tremble about the bet I have with he ; and , faith , ' twill be difficult to pay he when I lose . they will all be set up a whoop of congratulation at the Savage , as they call I , be take prisoner . how shall I ever be able to appear in the world again ? whom shall I ask to come to my help ? no , " think he , with his mingled acuteness and simplicity , " I will not send in the first instance to any of my relation or my noble friend at White 's . I will have Sampson 's counsel . he have often be in a similar predicament , and will know how to advise I . " accordingly , as soon as the light of dawn appear , after an almost intolerable delay for it seem to Harry as if the sun have forget to visit Cursitor Street in his round that morning and as soon as the inmate of the house of bondage be stir , Mr. Warrington despatch a messenger to his friend in Long Acre , acquaint the chaplain with the calamity just befall he , and beseech his reverence to give he the benefit of his advice and consolation . 
 Mr. Warrington do not know , to be sure , that to send such a message to the parson be as if he say , " I be fall amongst the lion . come down , my dear friend , into the pit with I . " Harry very likely think Sampson 's difficulty be over ; or , more likely still , be so much engross with his own affair and perplexity , as to bestow little thought upon his neighbour 's . have send off his missive , the captive 's mind be somewhat more at ease , and he condescend to call for breakfast , which be bring to he presently . the attendant who serve he with his morning repast ask he whether he would order dinner , or take his meal at Mrs. Bailiff 's table with some other gentleman ? no . Mr. Warrington would not order dinner . he should quit the place before dinner - time , he inform the chamberlain who wait on he in that grim tavern . the man go away , think no doubt that this be not the first young gentleman who have announce that he be go away ere two hour be over . " well , if your honour do stay , there be good beef and carrot at two o'clock , " say the sceptic , and close the door on Mr. Harry and his solitary meditation . 
 Harry 's messenger to Mr. Sampson bring back a message from that gentleman to say that he would be with his patron as soon as might be : but ten o'clock come , eleven o'clock , noon , and no Sampson . no Sampson arrive , but about twelve Gumbo with a portmanteau of his master 's clothe , who fling himself , roar with grief , at Harry 's foot : and with a thousand vow of fidelity , express himself ready to die , to sell himself into slavery over again , to do anything to rescue his beloved Master Harry from this calamitous position . Harry be touch with the lad 's expression of affection , and tell he to get up from the ground where he be grovel on his knee , embrace his master 's . " all you have to do , sir , be to give I my clothe to dress , and to hold your tongue about this business . mind you , not a word , sir , about it to anybody ! " say Mr. Warrington , severely . 
 " oh no , sir , never to nobody ! " say Gumbo , look most solemnly , and proceed to dress his master carefully , who have need of a change and a toilette after his yesterday 's sudden capture , and night 's dismal rest . accordingly Gumbo fling a dash of powder in Harry 's hair , and array his master carefully and elegantly , so that he make Mr. Warrington look as fine and splendid as if he have be step into his chair to go to St. James 's . 
 indeed all that love and servility could do Mr. Gumbo faithfully do for his master , for whom he have an extreme regard and attachment . but there be certain thing beyond Gumbo 's power . he could not undo thing which be do already ; and he could not help lie and excuse himself when press upon point disagreeable to himself . the language of slave be lie   I mean black slave and white . the creature slink away and hide with subterfuge , as a hunted animal run to his covert at the sight of man , the tyrant and pursuer . strange relic of feudality , and consequence of our ever - so - old social life ! our domestic   be they not man , too , and brother ?   be all hypocrite before we . they never speak naturally to we , or the whole truth . we should be indignant : we should say , confound their impudence : we should turn they out of door if they do . but quo me rapis , o my unbridled hobby ? 
 well , the truth be , that as for swear not to say a word about his master 's arrest such an oath as that be impossible to keep for , with a heart full of grief , indeed , but with a tongue that never could cease wagging , bragging , joking , and lie , Mr. Gumbo have announce the woeful circumstance to a prodigious number of his acquaintance already , chiefly gentleman of the shoulder - knot and worst lace . we have see how he carry the news to Colonel Lambert 's and Lord Wrotham 's servant : he have proclaim it at the footman 's club to which he belong , and which be frequent by the gentleman of some of the first nobility . he have subsequently condescend to partake of a mug of ale in Sir Miles Warrington 's butler 's room , and there have repeat and embellish the story . then he have go off to Madame Bernstein 's people , with some of whom he be on term of affectionate intercourse , and have inform that domestic circle of his grief and , his master be capture , and there be no earthly call for his personal service that evening , Gumbo have step up to Lord Castlewood 's , and inform the gentry there of the incident which have just come to pass . so when , lay his hand on his heart , and with gush flood of tear , Gumbo say , in reply to his master 's injunction , " oh no , master ! nebber to nobody ! " we be in a condition to judge of the degree of credibility which ought to be give to the lad 's statement . 
 the black have long complete his master 's toilet : the dreary breakfast be over : slow as the hour go to the prisoner , still they be pass one after another , but no Sampson come in accordance with the promise send in the morning . at length , some time after noon , there arrive , not Sampson , but a billet from he , seal with a moist wafer , and with the ink almost yet wet . the unlucky divine 's letter run as follow : 
 " oh , sir , dear sir , I have do all that a man can at the command and in the behalf of his patron ! you do not know , sir , to what you be subject I , do you ? else , if I be to go to prison , why do I not share your , and why be I in a lock - up house three door off ? 
 " yes . such be the fact . as I be hasten to you , know full well the danger to which I be subject : but what danger will I not affront at the call of such a benefactor as Mr. Warrington hath be to I ? I be seize by two villain who have a writ against I , and who have lodge I at Naboth 's , hard by , and so close to your honour , that we could almost hear each other across the garden wall of the respective house where we be confine . 
 " I have much and of importance to say , which I do not care to write down on paper regard your affair . may they mend ! may my cursed fortune , too , well themselves , be the prayer of 
 " your honour 's afflict chaplain - in - Ordinary , J. S. " 
 and now , as Mr. Sampson refuse to speak , it will be our duty to acquaint the reader with those matter whereof the poor chaplain do not care to discourse on paper . 
 Gumbo 's loquacity have not reach so far as Long Acre , and Mr. Sampson be ignorant of the extent of his patron 's calamity until he receive Harry 's letter and messenger from Chancery Lane . the divine be still ardent with gratitude for the service Mr. Warrington have just confer on he , and eager to find some mean to succour his distressed patron . he know what a large sum Lord Castlewood have win from his cousin , have din in company with his lordship on the day before , and now run to Lord Castlewood 's house , with a hope of arouse he to some pity for Mr. Warrington . Sampson make a very eloquent and touching speech to Lord Castlewood about his kinsman 's misfortune , and speak with a real kindness and sympathy , which , however , fail to touch the nobleman to whom he address himself . 
 my lord peevishly and curtly put a stop to the chaplain 's passionate pleading . " do I not tell you , two day since , when you come for money , that I be as poor as a beggar , Sampson , " say his lordship , " and have anybody leave I a fortune since ? the little sum I win from my cousin be swallow up by other . I not only can not   help Mr. Warrington , but , as I pledge you my word , not be in the least aware of his calamity , I have positively write to he this morning to ask he to help I . " and a letter to this effect do actually reach Mr. Warrington from his lodging , whither it have be despatch by the penny post . 
 " I must get he money , my lord . I know he have scarcely anything leave in his pocket after relieve I . be I to pawn my cassock and band , he must have money , " cry the chaplain . 
 " amen . go and pawn your band , your cassock , anything you please . your enthusiasm do you credit , " say my lord ; and resume the reading of his paper , whilst , in the deep despondency , poor Sampson leave he . 
 my Lady Maria meanwhile have hear that the chaplain be with her brother , and conjecture what might be the subject on which they have be talk . she seize upon the parson as he issue from out his fruitless interview with my lord . she draw he into the dining - room : the strong mark of grief and sympathy be in her countenance . " tell I , what be this have happen to Mr. Warrington ? " she ask . 
 " your ladyship , then , know ? " ask the chaplain . 
 " have I not be in mortal anxiety ever since his servant bring the dreadful news last night ? " ask my lady . " we have it as we come from the opera from my Lady Yarmouth 's box my lord , my Lady Castlewood , and I. " 
 " his lordship , then , do know ? " continue Sampson . 
 " Benson tell the news when we come from the playhouse to our tea , " repeat Lady Maria . 
 the chaplain lose all patience and temper at such duplicity . " this be too bad , " he say , with an oath ; and he tell Lady Maria of the conversation which he have just have with Lord Castlewood , and of the latter 's refusal to succour his cousin , after win great sum of money from he , and with much eloquence and feeling , of Mr. Warrington 's most generous behaviour to himself . 
 then my Lady Maria break out with a series of remark regard her own family , which be by no mean complimentary to her own kith and kin . although not accustomed to tell truth commonly , yet , when certain family fall out , it be wonderful what a number of truth they will tell about one another . with tear , imprecation , I do not like to think how much strong language , Lady Maria burst into a furious and impassioned tirade , in which she touch upon the history of almost all her noble family . she compliment the man and the lady alike ; she shriek out interrogatory to Heaven , inquire why it have make such   never mind what name she call her brother , sister , uncle , aunt , parent ; and , embolden with wrath , she dash at her brother 's library door , so shrill in her outcry , so furious in her demeanour , that the alarmed chaplain , fear the scene which might ensue , make for the street . 
 my lord , look up from the book or other occupation which engage he , regard the furious woman with some surprise , and select a good strong oath to fle at she , as it be , and check her onset . 
 but , when rouse , we have see how courageous Maria could be . Afraid as she be ordinarily of her brother , she be not in a mood to be frighten now by any language of abuse or sarcasm at his command . 
 " so , my lord ! " she call out , " you sit down with he in private to card , and pigeon he ! you get the poor boy 's last shilling , and you will not   give he a guinea out of his own winning now he be penniless ! " 
 " so that infernal chaplain have be tell tale ! " say my lord . 
 " dismiss he : do ! pay he his wage , and let he go , he will be glad enough ! " cry Maria . 
 " I keep he to marry one of my sister , in case he be want , " say Castlewood , glare at she . 
 " what can the woman be in a family where there be such man ? " say the lady . 
 " Effectivement ! " say my lord , with a shrug of his shoulder . 
 " what can we be , when our father and brother be what they be ? we be bad enough , but what be you ? I say , you neither have courage no , nor honour , nor common feeling . as your equal will not   play with you , my Lord Castlewood , you must take this poor lad out of Virginia , your own kinsman , and pigeon he ! oh , it be a shame a shame ! " 
 " we be all play our own game , I suppose . have not   you play and win one , Maria ? be it you that be squeamish of a sudden about the poor lad from Virginia ? have Mr. Harry cry off , or have your ladyship get a well offer ? " cry my Lord . " if you will not   have he , one of the Warrington girl will , I promise you ; and the old Methodist woman in Hill Street will give he the choice of either . be you a fool , Maria Esmond ? a great fool , I mean , than in common ? " 
 " I should be a fool if I think that either of my brother could act like an honest man , Eugene ! " say Maria . " I be a fool to expect that you will be other than you be ; that if you find any relative in distress you will help he ; that if you can meet with a victim you will not   fleece he . " 
 " fleece he ! Psha ! what folly be you talk ! have you not see , from the course which the lad have be run for month past , how he would end ? if I have not win his money , some other would ? I never grudge thee thy little plan regard he . why shouldst thou fly in a passion , because I have just put out my hand to take what he be offer to all the world ? I reason with you , I do not   know why , Maria . you should be old enough to understand reason , at any rate . you think this money belong of right to Lady Maria Warrington and her child ? I tell you that in three month more every shilling would have find its way to White 's macco - table , and that it be much well spend in pay my debt . so much for your ladyship 's anger , and tear , and menace , and naughty language . see ! I be a good brother , and repay they with reason and kind word . " 
 " my good brother might have give a little more than kind word to the lad from whom he have just take hundred , " interpose the sister of this affectionate brother . 
 " great heaven , Maria ! do not   you see that even out of this affair , unpleasant as it seem , a clever woman may make her advantage , " cry my lord . Maria say she fail to comprehend . 
 " as thus . I name no name ; I meddle in no person 's business , have quite enough to do to manage my own cursed affair . but suppose I happen to know of a case in another family which may be applicable to ours . it be this . a green young lad of tolerable expectation , come up from the country to his friend in town never mind from what country : never mind to what town . an elderly female relative , who have be drag her spinsterhood about these how many year shall we say ? extort a promise of marriage from my young gentleman , never mind on what condition . " 
 " my lord , do you want to insult your sister as well as to injure your cousin ? " ask Maria . 
 " my good child , do I say a single word about fleece or cheat , or pigeone , or do I fly into a passion when you insult I ? I know the allowance that must be make for your temper , and the natural folly of your sex . I say I treat you with soft word I go on with my story . the elderly relative extract a promise of marriage from the young lad , which my gentleman be quite unwilling to keep . no , he will not   keep it . he be utterly tired of his elderly relative : he will plead his mother 's refusal : he will do anything to get out of his promise . " 
 " yes ; if he be one of we Esmonds , my Lord Castlewood . but this be a man of honour we be speak of , " cry Maria , who , I suppose , admired truth in other , however little she see it in her own family . 
 " I do not contradict either of my dear sister 's remark . one of we would fling the promise to the wind , especially as it do not exist in writing . " 
 " my lord ! " gasp out Maria . 
 " Bah ! I know all . that little coup of Tunbridge be play by the Aunt Bernstein with excellent skill . the old woman be the good man of our family . while you be arrest , your box be search for the Mohock 's letter to you . when you be let loose , the letter have disappear , and you say nothing , like a wise woman , as you be sometimes . you still hanker after your Cherokee . Soit . a woman of your mature experience know the value of a husband . what be this little loss of two or three hundred pound ? " 
 " not more than three hundred , my lord ? " interpose Maria . 
 " eh ! never mind a hundred or two , more or less . what be this loss at card ? a mere bagatelle ! you be play for a principality . you want your kingdom in Virginia ; and if you listen to my opinion , the little misfortune which have happen to your swain be a piece of great good - fortune to you . " 
 " I do not   understand you , my lord . " 
 " c'est possible ; but sit down , and I will explain what I mean in a manner suit to your capacity . " and so Maria Esmond , who have advance to her brother like a rag lion , now sate down at his foot like a gentle lamb . 
 Madame de Bernstein be not a little move at the news of her nephew 's arrest , which Mr. Gumbo bring to Clarges Street on the night of the calamity . she would have cros - examine the black , and have further particular respect Harry 's mishap ; but Mr. Gumbo , anxious to carry his intelligence to other quarter , have vanish when her ladyship send for he . her temper be not improve by the news , or by the sleepless night which she spend . I do not envy the dame de compagnie who play card with she , or the servant who have to lie in her chamber . an arrest be an everyday occurrence , as she know very well as a woman of the world . into what difficulty have her scapegrace of a nephew fall ? how much money should she be call upon to pay to release he ? and have he run through all his own ? provide he have not commit himself very deeply , she be quite disposed to aid he . she like even his extravagance and folly . he be the only be in the world on whom , for long , long year , that weary woman have be able to bestow a little natural affection . so , on their different bed , she and Harry be lie wakeful together ; and quite early in the morning the messenger which each send forth on the same business may have cross each other . 
 Madame Bernstein 's messenger be despatch to the chamber of her man of business , Mr. Draper , with an order that Mr. D. should ascertain for what sum Mr. Warrington have be arrest , and forthwith repair to the Baroness . Draper 's emissary speedily find out that Mr. Warrington be lock up close beside they , and the amount of detainer against he so far . be there other creditor , as no doubt there be , they would certainly close upon he when they be make acquainted with his imprisonment . 
 to Mr. Sparks , the jeweller , for those unlucky present , so much ; to the landlord in Bond Street , for board , fire , lodge , so much : these be at present the only claim against Mr. Warrington , Mr. Draper find . he be ready , at a signal from her ladyship , to settle they at a moment . the jeweller 's account ought especially to be pay , for Mr. Harry have act most imprudently in take good from Mr. Sparks on credit , and pledge they with a pawnbroker . he must have be under some immediate pressure for money ; intend to redeem the good immediately , mean nothing but what be honourable of course ; but the affair would have an ugly look , if make public , and have well be settle out of hand . " there can not be the least difficulty regard a thousand pound more or less , for a gentleman of Mr. Warrington 's rank and expectation , " say Madame de Bernstein . not the least : her ladyship know very well that there be fund belong to Mr. Warrington , on which money could be at once raise with her ladyship 's guarantee . 
 should he go that instant and settle the matter with Messrs. Amos ? Mr. Harry might be back to dine with she at two , and to confound the people at the club , " who be no doubt rejoice over his misfortune , " say the compassionate Mr. Draper . 
 but the Baroness have other view . " I think , my good Mr. Draper , " she say , " that my young gentleman have sow wild oats enough ; and when he come out of prison I should like he to come out clear , and without any liability at all . you be not aware of all his . " 
 " no gentleman ever do tell all his debt , madam , " say Mr. Draper ; " no one I ever have to deal with . " 
 " there be one which the silly boy have contract , and from which he ought to be release , Mr. Draper . you remember a little circumstance which occur at Tunbridge Wells in the autumn ? about which I send up my man case to you ? " 
 " when your ladyship please to recall it , I remember it not otherwise , " say Mr. Draper , with a bow . " a lawyer should be like a popish confessor , what be tell he be a secret for ever , and for everybody . " so we must not whisper Madame Bernstein 's secret to Mr. Draper ; but the reader may perhaps guess it from the lawyer 's conduct subsequently . 
 the lawyer feel pretty certain that ere long he would receive a summon from the poor young prisoner in Cursitor Street , and wait for that invitation before he visit Mr. Warrington . six - and - thirty hour pass ere the invitation come , during which period Harry pass the dreariest two day which he ever remember to have spend . 
 there be no want of company in the lock - up house , the bailiff 's room be nearly always full ; but Harry prefer the dingy solitude of his own room to the society round his landlady 's table , and it be only on the second day of his arrest , and when his purse be empty by the heavy charge of the place , that he make up his mind to apply to Mr. Draper . he despatch a letter then to the lawyer at the Temple , inform he of his plight , and desire he , in an emphatic postscript , not to say one word about the matter to his aunt , Madame de Bernstein . 
 he have make up his mind not to apply to the old lady except at the very last extremity . she have treat he with so much kindness that he revolt from the notion of trespassing on her bounty , and for a while try to please himself with the idea that he might get out of durance without she even know that any misfortune at all have befall he . there seem to he something humiliating in petition a woman for money . no ! he would apply first to his male friend , all of whom might help he if they would . it have be his intention to send Sampson to one or other of they as a negotiator , have not the poor fellow be capture on his way to succour his friend . 
 Sampson go , Harry be oblige to have recourse to his own negro servant , who be keep on the trot all day between Temple Bar and the Court end of the town with letter from his unlucky master . firstly , then , Harry send off a most private and confidential letter to his kinsman , the right Honourable the Earl of Castlewood , say how he have be cast into prison , and beg Castlewood to lend he the amount of the debt . " please to keep my application , and the cause of it , a profound secret from the dear lady , " write poor Harry . 
 " be ever anything so unfortunate ? " write back Lord Castlewood , in reply . " I suppose you have not get my note of yesterday ? it must be lie at your lodging , where I hope in heaven ! you will soon be , too . my dear Mr. Warrington , think you be as rich as Croesus otherwise I never should have sate down to card with you I write to you yesterday , beg you to lend I some money to appease some hungry dun whom I do not   know how else to pacify . my poor fellow ! every shilling of your money go to they , and but for my peer 's privilege I might be hob - and - nob with you now in your dungeon . may you soon escape from it , be the prayer of your sincere castlewood . " 
 this be the result of application number one : and we may imagine that Mr. Harry read the reply to his petition with rather a blank face . never mind ! there be kind , jolly Uncle Warrington . only last night his aunt have kiss he and love he like a son . his uncle have call down blessing on his head , and profess quite a paternal regard for he . with a feeling of shyness and modesty in presence of those virtuous parent and family . Harry have never say a word about his wild doing , or his horse - racing , or his gambling , or his extravagance . it must all out now . he must confess himself a Prodigal and a Sinner , and ask for their forgiveness and aid . so Prodigal sate down and compose a penitent letter to Uncle Warrington , and expose his sad case , and besought he to come to the rescue . be not that a bitter nut to crack for our haughty young Virginian ? hour of mortification and profound thought as to the pathos of the composition do Harry pass over that letter ; sheet after sheet of Mr. Amos 's sixpence - a - sheet letter - paper do he tear up before the missive be complete , with which poor blubber Gumbo   much vilify by the bailiff 's follower and parasite , whom he be rob , as they conceive , of their perquisite   go his way . 
 at evening the faithful negro bring back a thick letter in his aunt 's handwriting . Harry open the letter with a trembling hand . he think it be full of bank - note . ah I ! it contain a sermon   Daniel in the Lions ' Den   by Mr. Whitfield , and a letter from Lady Warrington say that , in Sir Miles 's absence from London , she be in the habit of open his letter , and hence , perforce , be become acquainted with a fact which she deplore from her inmost soul to learn , namely , that her nephew Warrington have be extravagant and be in debt . of course , in the absence of Sir Miles , she could not hope to have at command such a sum as that for which Mr. Warrington write , but she send he her heartfelt prayer , her deep commiseration , and a discourse by dear Mr. Whitfield , which would comfort he in his present   alas ! she fear not undeserved   calamity . she add profuse reference to particular Scriptural chapter which would do he good . if she might speak of thing worldly , she say , at such a moment , she would hint to Mr. Warrington that his epistolary orthography be anything but correct . she would not fail for her part to comply with his express desire that his dear cousin should know nothing of this most painful circumstance , and with every wish for his welfare here and elsewhere , she subscribe herself his love aunt , MARGARET WARRINGTON . 
 Poor Harry hide his face between his hand , and sate for a while with elbow on the greasy table blankly star into the candle before he . the bailiff 's servant , who be touch by his handsome face , suggest a mug of beer for his honour , but Harry could not drink , nor eat the meat that be place before he . Gumbo , however , could , whose grief do not deprive he of appetite , and who , blubber the while , finish all the beer , and all the bread and the meat . meanwhile , Harry have finish another letter , with which Gumbo be commission to start again , and away the faithful creature run upon his errand . 
 Gumbo run as far as White 's Club , to which house he be order in the first instance to carry the letter , and where he find the person to whom it be address . even the prisoner , for whom time pass so slowly , be surprised at the celerity with which his negro have perform his errand . 
 at least the letter which Harry expect have not take long to write . " my lord write it at the hall - porter 's desk , while I stand there then with Mr Mr. Morris , " say Gumbo , and the letter be to this effect : 
 " DEAR SIR I be sorry I can not comply with your wish , I be short of money at present , have pay large sum to you as well as to other gentleman . your obediently , MARCH and R. 
 " Henry Warrington , Esq . " 
 " do Lord March say anything ? " ask Mr. Warrington look very pale . 
 " he say it be the cool thing he ever know . so do Mr. Morris . he show he your letter , Master Harry . yes , Mr. Morris say , ' dam his imperence ! ' " add Gumbo . 
 Harry burst into such a yell of laughter that his landlord think he have good news , and run in in alarm lest he be about to lose his tenant . but by this time poor Harry 's laughter be over , and he be fling down in his chair gaze dismally in the fire . 
 " I I should like to smoke a pipe of Virginia " he groan . 
 Gumbo burst into tear : he fling himself at Harry 's knee . he kiss his knee and his hand . " oh , master , my dear master , what will they say at home ? " he sob out . 
 the jailor be touch at the sight of the black 's grief and fidelity , and at Harry 's pale face as he sink back in his chair quite overcome and beat by his calamity . 
 " your honour be not   eat anything these two day , " the man say , in a voice of rough pity . " pluck up a little , sir . you be not   the first gentleman who have be in and out of grief before this . let I go down and get you a glass of punch and a little supper . " 
 " my good friend , " say Harry , a sickly smile play over his white face , " you pay ready money for everything in this house , do not   you ? I must tell you that I have not   a shilling leave to buy a dish of meat . all the money I have I want for letter - paper . " 
 " oh , master , my master ! " roar out Gumbo . " look here , my dear Master Harry ! here be plenty of money here 's twenty - three five - guineas . here 's gold moidore from Virginia here no , not that that be keepsake the girl give I . take everything everything . I go sell myself to - morrow morning ; but here be plenty for to - night , master ! " 
 " God bless you , Gumbo ! " Harry say , lay his hand on the lad 's woolly head . " you be free if I be not , and Heaven forbid I should not take the offer help of such a friend as you . bring I some supper : but the pipe too , mind the pipe too ! " and Harry eat his supper with a relish : and even the turnkey and bailiff 's follower , when Gumbo go out of the house that night , shake hand with he , and ever after treat he well . 




 CHAPTER XLVII . visitor in Trouble 

 Mr. Gumbo 's generous and feel conduct soothe and soften the angry heart of his master , and Harry 's second night in the spunging - house be pass more pleasantly than the first . somebody at least there be to help and compassionate with he . still , though soften in that one particular spot , Harry 's heart be hard and proud towards almost all the rest of the world . they be selfish and ungenerous , he think . his pious Aunt Warrington , his lordly friend March , his cynical cousin Castlewood , all have be try , and be find want . not to avoid twenty year of prison would he stoop to ask a favour of one of they again . fool that he have be , to believe in their promise , and confide in their friendship ! there be no friendship in this cursed , cold , selfish country . he would leave it . he would trust no Englishman , great or small . he would go to Germany , and make a campaign with the king ; or he would go home to Virginia , bury himself in the wood there , and hunt all day ; become his mother 's factor and land - steward ; marry Polly Broadbent , or Fanny Mountain ; turn regular tobacco - grower and farmer ; do anything , rather than remain amongst these english fine gentleman . so he arise with an outwardly cheerful countenance , but an angry spirit ; and at an early hour in the morning the faithful Gumbo be in attendance in his master 's chamber , have come from Bond Street , and bring Mr. Harry 's letter thence . " I want to bring some more clothe , " honest Gumbo say ; " but Mr. Ruff , the landlord , he would not   let I bring no more . " 
 Harry do not care to look at the letter : he open one , two , three ; they be all bill . he open a fourth ; it be from the landlord , to say that he would allow no more of Mr. Warrington 's thing to go out of the house , that unless his bill be pay he should sell Mr. W. 's good and pay himself : and that his black man must go and sleep elsewhere . he would hardly let Gumbo take his own clothe and portmanteau away . the black say he have find refuge elsewhere with some friend at Lord Wrotham 's house . " with Colonel Lambert 's people , " say Mr. Gumbo , look very hard at his master . " and Miss Hetty she fall down in a faint , when she hear you take up ; and Mr. Lambert , he very good man , and he say to I this morning , he say , ' Gumbo , you tell your master if he want I he send to I , and I come to he . ' " 
 Harry be touch when he hear that Hetty have be afflict by his misfortune . he do not believe Gumbo 's story about her fainting ; he be accustom to translate his black 's language and to allow for exaggeration . but when Gumbo speak of the Colonel the young Virginian 's spirit be darken again . " I send to Lambert " he think , grind his tooth , " the man who insult I , and fling my present back in my face ! if I be starve I would not ask he for a crust ! " and presently , be dress , Mr. Warrington call for his breakfast , and despatch Gumbo with a brief note to Mr. Draper in the Temple , require that gentleman 's attendance . 
 " the note be as haughty as if he be write to one of his negro , and not to a freeborn english gentleman , " Draper say ; whom indeed Harry have always treat with insufferable condescension . " it be all very well for a fine gentleman to give himself air ; but for a fellow in a spunge - house ! hang he ! " say Draper , " I have   a great mind not to go ! " nevertheless , Mr. Draper do go , and find Mr. Warrington in his misfortune even more arrogant than he have ever be in the day of his utmost prosperity . Mr. W. sit on his bed , like a lord , in a splendid gown with his hair dress . he motion his black man to fetch he a chair . 
 " excuse I , madam , but such haughtiness and air I be not   accustom to ! " say the outraged attorney . 
 " take a chair and go on with your story , my good Mr. Draper ! " say Madame de Bernstein , smile , to whom he go to report proceeding . she be amuse at the lawyer 's anger . she like her nephew for be insolent in adversity . 
 the course which Draper be to pursue in his interview with Harry have be arrange between the Baroness and her man of business on the previous day . draper be an able man , and likely in most case to do a client good service : he fail in the present instance because he be pique and angry , or , more likely still , because he could not understand the gentleman with whom he have to deal . I presume that he who cast his eye on the present page be the most gentle of reader . Gentleman , as you unquestionably be , then , my dear sir , have you not remark in your dealing with people who be no gentleman , that you offend they not know the how or the why ? so the man who be no gentleman offend you in a thousand way of which the poor creature have no idea himself . he do or say something which provoke your scorn . he perceive that scorn   be always on the watch , and uneasy about himself , his manner and behaviour   and he rag . you speak to he naturally , and he fancy still that you be sneer at he . you have indifference towards he , but he hat you , and hat you the bad because you do not   care . " Gumbo , a chair to Mr. Draper ! " say Mr. Warrington , fold his brocaded dressing - gown round his leg as he sit on the dingy bed . " sit down , if you please , and let we talk my business over . much oblige to you for come so soon in reply to my message . have you hear of this piece of ill - luck before ? " 
 Mr. Draper have hear of the circumstance . " bad news travel quick , Mr. Warrington , " he say ; " and I be eager to offer my humble service as soon as ever you should require they . your friend , your family , will be much pain that a gentleman of your rank should be in such a position . " 
 " I have be very imprudent , Mr. Draper . I have live beyond my mean . "   Mr. Draper bow .   " I play in company with gentleman who be much rich than myself , and a cursed run of ill - luck have carry away all my ready money , leave I with liability to the amount of five hundred pound , and more . " 
 " five hundred now in the office , " say Mr. Draper . 
 " well , this be such a trifle that I think by send to one or two friend , yesterday , I could have pay my debt and go home without far to do . I have be mistake ; and will thank you to have the kindness to put I in the way of raise the money as soon as may be . " 
 Mr. Draper say " Hm ! " and pull a very grave and long face . 
 " why , sir , it can be do ! " say Mr. Warrington , star at the lawyer . 
 it not only could be do , but Mr. Draper have propose to Madame Bernstein on the day before instantly to pay the money , and release Mr. Warrington . that lady have declare she intend to make the young gentleman her heir . in common with the rest of the world , Draper believe Harry 's hereditary property in Virginia to be as great in money - value as in extent . he have note in his pocket , and Madame Bernstein 's order to pay they under certain condition : nevertheless , when Harry say , " it can be do ! " draper pull his long face , and say , " it can be do in time , sir ; but it will require a considerable time . to touch the property in England which be yours on Mr. George Warrington 's death , we must have the event prove , the trustee release : and who be to do either ? Lady Esmond Warrington in Virginia , of course , will not allow her son to remain in prison , but we must wait six month before we hear from she . have your Bristol agent any authority to honour your draft ? " 
 " he be only authorise to pay I two hundred pound a year , " say Mr. Warrington . " I suppose I have no resource , then , but to apply to my aunt , Madame de Bernstein . she will be my security . " 
 " her ladyship will do anything for you , sir ; she have say so to I , often and often , " say the lawyer ; " and , if she give the word at that moment you can walk out of this place . " 
 " go to she , then , from I , Mr. Draper . I do not want to have trouble my relation : but rather than continue in this horrible needless imprisonment , I must speak to she . say where I be , and what have befall I . disguise nothing ! and tell she , that I confide in her affection and kindness for I to release I from this this disgrace , " and Mr. Warrington 's voice shake a little , and he pass his hand across his eye . 
 " Sir , " say Mr. Draper , eye the young man , " I be with her ladyship yesterday , when we talk over the whole of this here most unpleasant I will not   say as you do , disgraceful business . " 
 " what do you mean , sir ? do Madame de Bernstein know of my misfortune ? " ask Harry . 
 " every circumstance , sir ; the pawn the watch , and all . " 
 Harry turn burn red . " it be an unfortunate business , the pawn they watch and thing which you have never pay for , " continue the lawyer . the young man start up from the bed , look so fierce that Draper feel a little alarmed . 
 " it may lead to litigation and unpleasant remark be make , in court , sir . they barrister respect nothing ; and when they get a feller in the box " 
 " Great Heaven , sir , you do not   suppose a gentleman of my rank can not   take a watch upon credit without intend to cheat the tradesman ? " cry Harry , in the great agitation . 
 " of course you mean everything that be honourable ; only , you see , the law may not   happen to think so , " say Mr. Draper , wink his eye .   " hang the supercilious beast ; I touch he there !   your aunt say it be the most imprudent thing ever she hear of to call it by no bad name . " 
 " you call it by no bad name yourself , Mr. Draper ? " say Harry , speak each word very slow , and evidently try to keep a command of himself . 
 Draper do not like his look . " Heaven forbid that I should say anything as between gentleman and gentleman , but between I and my client , it be my duty to say , ' Sir , you be in a very unpleasant scrape , ' just as a doctor would have to tell his patient , ' Sir , you be very ill . ' " 
 " and you can not   help I to pay this debt off , and you have come only to tell I that I may be accuse of roguery ? " say Harry . 
 " of obtain good under false pretence ? most undoubtedly , yes . I can not   help it , sir . do not   look as if you would knock I down .   curse he , I be make he wince , though .   a young gentleman , who have only two hundred a year from his ma ' , order diamond and watch , and take them to a pawnbroker . you ask I what people will think of such behaviour , and I tell you honestly . do not   be angry with I , Mr. Warrington . " 
 " go on , sir ! " say Harry , with a groan . 
 the lawyer think the day be his own . " but you ask if I can not   help to pay this debt off ? and I say yes and that here be the money in my pocket to do it now , if you like not mine , sir , my honoured client 's , your aunt , Lady Bernstein . but she have a right to impose her condition , and I have   bring them with I . " 
 " tell they , sir , " say Mr. Harry . 
 " they be not hard . they be only for your own good : and if you say yes , we can call a hackney - coach , and go to Clarges Street together , which I have promise to go there , whether you will or no . Mr. Warrington , I name no name , but there be a question of marriage between you and a certain party . " 
 " ah ! " say Harry ; and his countenance look more cheerful than it have yet do . 
 " to that marriage my noble client , the Baroness , be most averse have other view for you , and think it will be your ruin to marry a party , of noble birth and title it be true ; but , excuse I , not of first - rate character , and so much old than yourself . you have give an imprudent promise to that party . " 
 " yes ; and she have it still , " say Mr. Warrington . 
 " it have be recover . she drop it by an accident at Tunbridge , " say Mr. Draper , ' so my client inform I ; indeed her ladyship show it I , for the matter of that . it be write in bl " 
 " never mind , sir ! " cry Harry , turn almost as red as the ink which he have use to write his absurd promise , of which the madness and folly have smite he with shame a thousand time over . 
 " at the same time letter , write to you , and compromise a noble family , be recover , " continue the lawyer . " you have lose them . it be no fault of your . you be away when they be find again . you may say that that noble family , that you yourself , have a friend such as few young man have . well , sir , there be no earthly promise to bind you only so many idle word say over a bottle , which very likely any gentleman may forget . say you will not   go on with this marriage give I and my noble friend your word of honour . cry off , I say , Mr. W. ! do not   be such a d fool , save your presence , as to marry an old woman who have jilt score of man in her time . say the word , and I step downstairs , pay every shilling against you in the office , and put you down in my coach , either at your aunt 's or at White 's Club , if you like , with a couple of hundred in your pocket . say yes ; and give we your hand ! there be no use in sit grin behind these bar all day ! " 
 so far Mr. Draper have have the good of the talk . Harry only long himself to be rid of the engagement from which his aunt want to free he . his foolish flame for Maria Esmond have die out long since . if she would release he , how thankful would he be ! " come ! give we your hand , and say do ! " say the lawyer , with a knowing wink . " do not   stand shilly - shallying , sir . Law bless you , Mr. W. , if I have marry everybody I promise , I should be like the Grand Turk , or Captain Macheath in the play ! " 
 the lawyer 's familiarity disgust Harry , who shrink from Draper , scarcely know that he do so . he fold his dress gown round he , and step back from the other 's proffered hand . " give I a little time to think of the matter , if you please , Mr. Draper , " he say , " and have the goodness to come to I again in an hour . 
 " very good , sir , very good , sir ! " say the lawyer , bite his lip , and , as he seize up his hat , turn very red . " Most party would not want an hour to consider about such an offer as I make you : but I suppose my time must be your , and I will come again , and see whether you be to go or to stay . good morning , sir , good morning : " and he go his way , growl curse down the stair . " will not   take my hand , will not   he ? will tell I in an hour 's time ! hang his impudence ! I will show he what an hour be ! " 
 Mr. Draper go to his chamber in dudgeon then ; bully his clerk all round , send off a messenger to the Baroness , to say that he have wait on the young gentleman , who have demand a little time for consideration , which be for form 's sake , as he have no doubt ; the lawyer then see client , transact business , go out to his dinner in the most leisurely manner ; and then finally turn his step towards the neighbouring Cursitor Street . " he will be at home when I call , the haughty beast ! " say Draper , with a sneer . " the Fortunate Youth in his room ? " the lawyer ask of the sheriff 's officer 's aide - de - camp who come to open the double door . 
 " Mr. Warrington be in his apartment , " say the gentleman , " but " and here the gentleman wink at Mr. Draper , and lay his hand on his nose . 
 " but what , Mr. Paddy from Cork ? " say the lawyer . 
 " my name be Costigan ; I familee be noble , and I neetive place be the irish methrawpoli , Mr. Six - and - eightpence ! " say the janitor , scowl at Draper . a rich odour of spirituous liquor fill the little space between the double door where he hold the attorney in conversation . 
 " confound you , sir , let I pass ! " bawl out Mr. Draper . 
 " I can hear you perfectly well , six - and - eightpence , except your h 's , which you dthrop out of your conversation . I will thank ye not to call neem , I good friend , or I finger and your nose will have to make an intimate hic - quaintance . walk in , sir ! be polite for the future to your shuparior in birth and manner , though they may be your infarior in temporary station . confound the kay ! walk in , sir , I say ! Madam , I have the honour of salute ye most respectfully ! " 
 a lady with her face cover with a capuchin , and far hide by her handkerchief , utter a little exclamation as of alarm as she come down the stair at this instant and hurry past the lawyer . he be press forward to look at she for Mr. Draper be very cavalier in his manner to woman but the bailiff 's follower thrust his leg between Draper and the retreating lady , cry , " keep your own distance , if you plaise ! this way , madam ! I at once recognise your ladysh " here he close the door on Draper 's nose , and leave that attorney to find his own way to his client upstairs . 
 at six o'clock that evening the old Baroness de Bernstein be pace up and down her drawing - crutch , and for ever run to the window when the noise of a coach be hear pass in Clarges Street . she have delay her dinner from hour to hour : she who scold so fiercely , on ordinary occasion , if her cook be five minute after his time . she have order two cover to be lay , plate to be set out , and some extra dish to be prepare as if for a little fete . four five o'clock pass , and at six she look from the window , and a coach actually stop at her door . 
 " Mr. Draper " be announce , and enter bow profoundly . 
 the old lady tremble on her stick . " where be the boy ? " she say quickly . " I tell you to bring he , sir ! how dare you come without he ? " 
 " it be not my fault , madam , that Mr. Warrington refuse to come . " and Draper give his version of the interview which have just take place between himself and the young Virginian . 




 CHAPTER XLVIII . an apparition 

 go off in his wrath from his morning 's conversation with Harry , Mr. Draper think he hear the young prisoner speak behind he ; and , indeed , Harry have rise , and utter a half - exclamation to call the lawyer back . but he be proud , and the other offend : Harry check word , and Draper do not choose to stop . it wind Harry 's pride to be oblige to humble himself before the lawyer , and to have to yield from mere lack and desire of money . " an hour hence will do as well , " think Harry , and lapse sulkily on to the bed again . no , he do not care for Maria Esmond ! no : he be ashamed of the way in which he have be entrap into that engagement . a wily and experienced woman , she have cheat his boyish ardour . she have take unfair advantage of he , as her brother have at play . they be his own flesh and blood , and they ought to have spar he . instead , one and the other have make a prey of he , and have use he for their selfish end . he think how they have betray the right of hospitality : how they have make a victim of the young kinsman who come confide within their gate . his heart be sore wound : his head sink back on his pillow : bitter tear wet it . " have they come to Virginia , " he think , " I have give they a different welcome ! " 
 he be rouse from this mood of despondency by Gumbo 's grinning face at his door , who say a lady be come to see Master Harry , and behind the lad come the lady in the capuchin , of whom we have just make mention . Harry sit up , pale and haggard , on his bed . the lady , with a sob , and almost ere the servant - man withdraw , run towards the young prisoner , put her arm round his neck with real emotion and a maternal tenderness , sob over his pale cheek and kiss it in the midst of plentiful tear , and cry out 
 " oh , my Harry ! do I ever , ever think to see thee here ? " 
 he start back , scar as it seem at her presence , but she sink down at the bedside , and seize his feverish hand , and embrace his knee . she have a real regard and tenderness for he . the wretched place in which she find he , his wretched look , fill her heart with a sincere love and pity . 
 " I I think none of you would come ! " say poor Harry , with a groan . 
 More tear , more kiss of the hot young hand , more clasp and pressure with her , be the lady 's reply for a moment or two . 
 " oh , my dear ! my dear ! I can not bear to think of thee in misery , " she sob out . 
 harden though it might be , that heart be not all marble that dreary life not all desert . Harry 's mother could not have be fonder , nor her tone more tender than those of his kinswoman now kneel at his foot . 
 " some of the debt , I fear , be owe to my extravagance ! " she say   and this be true . " you buy trinket and jewel in order to give I pleasure . oh , how I hate they now ! I little think I ever could ! I have bring they all with I , and more trinket here ! and here ! and all the money I have in the world ! " 
 and she pour brooch , ring , a watch , and a score or so of guineas into Harry 's lap . the sight of which strangely agitated and immensely touch the young man . 
 " Dearest , kind cousin ! " he sob out . 
 his lip find no more word to utter , but yet , no doubt they serve to express his gratitude , his affection , his emotion . 
 he become quite gay presently , and smile as he put away some of the trinket , his present to Maria , and tell she into what danger he have fall by sell other good which he have purchase on credit ; and how a lawyer have insult he just now upon this very point . he would not have his dear Maria 's money he have enough , quite enough for the present : but he value she twenty guineas as much as if they have be twenty thousand . he would never forget her love and kindness : no , by all that be sacred he would not ! his mother should know of all her goodness . it have have cheer he when he be just on the point of break down under his disgrace and misery . might Heaven bless she for it ! there be no need to pursue beyond this , the cousin ' conversation . the dark day seem bright to Harry after Maria 's visit : the imprisonment not so hard to bear . the world be not all selfish and cold . here be a fond creature who really and truly love he . even Castlewood be not so bad as he have think . he have express the deep grief at not be able to assist his kinsman . he be hopelessly in debt . every shilling he have win from Harry he have lose on the next day to other . anything that lie in his power he would do . he would come soon and see Mr. Warrington : he be in wait to - day , and as much a prisoner as Harry himself . so the pair talk on cheerfully and affectionately until the darkness begin to close in , when Maria , with a sigh , bade Harry farewell . 
 the door scarcely close upon she , when it open to admit draper . 
 " your humble servant , sir , " say the attorney . his voice jar upon Harry 's ear , and his presence offend the young man . 
 " I have expect you some hour ago , sir , " he curtly say . 
 " a lawyer 's time be not always his own , sir , " say Mr. Draper , who have just be in consultation with a bottle of port at the Grecian . " never mind , I be at your order now . presume it be all right , Mr. Warrington . pack your trunk ? why , now there you be in your bedgown still . let I go down and settle whilst you call in your black man and titivate a bit . I have   a coach at the door , and we will be off and dine with the old lady . " 
 " be you go to dine with the Baroness de Bernstein , pray ? " 
 " not I no such honour . have my dinner already . it be you be a - go to dine with your aunt , I suppose ? " 
 " Mr. Draper , you suppose a great deal more than you know , " say Mr. Warrington , look very fierce and tall , as he fold his brocade dressing - gown round he . 
 " great goodness , sir , what do you mean ? " ask Draper . 
 " I mean , sir , that I have consider , and , that have give my word to a faithful and honourable lady , it do not become I to withdraw it . " 
 " confound it , sir ! " shriek the lawyer , " I tell you she have lose the paper . there be nothing to bind you nothing . why she be old enough to be " 
 " enough , sir , " say Mr. Warrington , with a stamp of his foot . " you seem to think you be talk to some other pettifogger . I take it , Mr. Draper , you be not accustomed to have dealing with man of honour . " 
 " Pettifogger , indeed ! " cry draper in a fury . " man of honour , indeed ! I would have you to know , Mr. Warrington , that I be as good a man of honour as you . I do not   know so many gambler and horse - jockey , perhaps . I have not   gamble away my patrimony , and live as if I be a nobleman on two hundred a year . I have not   buy watch on credit , and pawn touch I if you dare , sir , " and the lawyer spring to the door . 
 " that be the way out , sir . you can not   go through the window , because it be bar , " say Mr. Warrington . 
 " and the answer I take to my client be no , then ! " scream out Draper . 
 Harry step forward , with his two hand clench . " if you utter another word , " he say , " I will " the door be shut rapidly the sentence be never finish , and Draper go away furious to Madame de Bernstein , from whom , though he give she the good version of his story , he get still fiercer language than he have receive from Mr. Warrington himself . 
 " what ? shall she trust I , and I desert she ? " say Harry , stalk up and down his room in his flowing , rustle brocade . " dear , faithful , generous woman ! if I lie in prison for year , I will be true to she . " 
 her lawyer dismiss after a stormy interview , the desolate old woman be fain to sit down to the meal which she have hop to share with her nephew . the chair be before she which he be to have fill , the glass shin by the silver . one dish after another be lay before she by the silent major - domo , and taste and push away . the man press his mistress at last . " it be eight o'clock , " he say . " you have have nothing all day . it be good for you to eat . " she could not eat . she would have her coffee . let case go get her her coffee . the lacquey bear the dish off the table , leave their mistress sit at it before the vacant chair . 
 presently the old servant re - enter the room without his lady 's coffee and with a strange scared face , and say , " Mr. WARRINGTON ! " 
 the old woman utter an exclamation , get up from her armchair , but sink back in it tremble very much . " so you be come , sir , be you ? " she say , with a fond shake voice . " bring back the Ah ! " here she scream , " gracious God , who be it ? " her eye star wildly : her white face look ghastly through her rouge . she cling to the arm of her chair for support , as the visitor approach she . 
 a gentleman whose face and figure exactly resemble Harry Warrington and whose voice , when he speak , have tone strangely similar , have follow the servant into the room . he bow towards the Baroness . 
 " you expect my brother , madam ? " he say " I be but now arrive in London . I go to his house . I meet his servant at your door , who be bear this letter for you . I think I would bring it to your ladyship before go to he , " and the stranger lay down a letter before Madam Bernstein . 
 " be you " gasp out the Baroness " be you my nephew , that we suppose be " 
 " be kill and be alive ! I be George Warrington , madam and I ask his kinsfolk what have you do with my brother ? " 
 " look , George ! " say the bewildered old lady " I expect he here to - night that chair be set for he I have be wait for he , sir , till now till I be quite faint I do not   like I do not   like be alone . do stay an sup with I ! " 
 " Pardon I , madam . please God , my supper will be with Harry tonight ! " 
 " bring he back . bring he back here on any condition ! it be but five hundred pound ! here be the money , sir , if you need it ! " 
 " I have no want , madam . I have money with I that can not   be well employ than in my brother 's service . " 
 " and you will bring he to I , sir ! say you will bring he to I ! " 
 Mr. Warrington make a very stately bow for answer , and quit the room , pass by the amazed domestic , and call with an air of authority to Gumbo to follow he . 
 have Mr. Harry receive no letter from home ? Master Harry have not open all his letter the last day or two . have he receive no letter announce his brother 's escape from the french settlement and return to Virginia ? oh no ! no such letter have come , else Master Harry certainly tell Gumbo . quick , horse ! quick by Strand to Temple Bar ! here be the house of Captivity and the Deliverer come to the rescue ! 




 CHAPTER XLIX . friend in need 

 Quick , hackneycoach steed , and bear George Warrington through Strand and Fleet Street to his imprisoned brother 's rescue ! any one who remember Hogarth 's picture of a London hackneycoach and a London street road at that period , may fancy how weary the quick time be , and how long seem the journey : scarce any light , save those carry by link - boy ; badly hung coach ; bad pavement ; great hole in the road , and vast quagmire of winter mud . that drive from Piccadilly to Fleet Street seem almost as long to our young man , as the journey from Marlborough to London which he have perform in the morning . 
 he have write to Harry , announce his arrival at Bristol . he have previously write to his brother , give the great news of his existence and his return from captivity . there be war between England and France at that time ; the french privateer be for ever on the look - out for british merchant - ship , and seize they often within sight of port . the letter bear the intelligence of George 's restoration must have be on board one of the many american ship of which the French take possession . the letter tell of George 's arrival in England be never open by poor Harry ; it be lie at the latter 's apartment , which it reach on the third morning after Harry 's captivity , when the angry Mr. Ruff have refuse to give up any single item more of his lodger 's property . 
 to these apartment George first go on his arrival in London , and ask for his brother . scared at the likeness between they , the maid - servant who open the door scream , and run back to her mistress . the mistress not like to tell the truth , or to own that poor Harry be actually a prisoner at her husband 's suit , say Mr. Warrington have leave his lodging ; she do not know where Mr. Warrington be . George know that Clarges Street be close to Bond Street . often and often have he look over the London map . Aunt Bernstein would tell he where Harry be . he might be with she at that very moment . George have read in Harry 's letter to Virginia about Aunt Bernstein 's kindness to Harry . even Madam Esmond be soften by it   and especially touch by a letter which the Baroness write the letter which cause George to pack off post - haste for Europe , indeed . she heartily hop and trust that Madam Beatrix have find occasion to repent of her former bad way . it be time , indeed , at her age ; and Heaven know that she have plenty to repent of ! I have know a harmless , good old soul of eighty , still bepommelle and stone by irreproachable lady of the strait sect of the Pharisees , for a little slip which occur long before the present century be bear , or she herself be twenty year old . Rachel Esmond never mention her eld daughter : Madam Esmond Warrington never mention her sister . no . in spite of the order for remission of the sentence in spite of the handwriting on the floor of the Temple there be a crime which some folk never will pardon , and regard which female virtue , especially , be inexorable . 
 I suppose the Virginians ' agent at Bristol have tell George fearful story of his brother 's doing . Gumbo , whom he meet at his aunt 's door , as soon as the lad recover from his terror at the sudden reappearance of the master whom he suppose dead , have leisure to stammer out a word or two respect his young master 's whereabout , and present pitiable condition ; and hence Mr. George 's sternness of demeanour when he present himself to the old lady . it seem to he a matter of course that his brother in difficulty should be rescue by his relation . oh , George , how little you know about London and London way ! whenever you take your walk abroad how many poor you meet if a philanthropist be for rescue all of they , not all the wealth of all the province of America would suffice he ! 
 but the feeling and agitation display by the old lady touch her nephew 's heart when , jolt through the dark street towards the house of his brother 's captivity , George come to think of his aunt 's behaviour . " she do feel my poor Harry 's misfortune , " he think to himself , " I have be too hasty in judge she . " again and again , in the course of his life , Mr. George have to rebuke himself with the same crime of be too hasty . how many of we have not ? and , alas , the mischief do , there be no repentance will mend it . quick , coachman ! we be almost as slow as you be in get from Clarges Street to the Temple . poor Gumbo know the way to the bailiff 's house well enough . again the bell be set ring . the first door be open to George and his negro ; then that first door be lock warily upon they , and they find themselves in a little passage with a little jewish janitor ; then a second door be unlocked , and they enter into the house . the jewish janitor stare , as by his flare tallow - torch he see a second Mr. Warrington before he . come to see that gentleman ? yes . but wait a moment . this be Mr. Warrington 's brother from America . Gumbo must go and prepare his master first . step into this room . there be a gentleman already there about Mr. W. 's business   the porter say , and another upstairs with he now . there be no end of people have be about he . 
 the room into which George be introduce be a small apartment which go by the name of Mr. Amos 's office , and where , by a gutter candle , and talk to the bailiff , sit a stout gentleman in a cloak and a laced hat . the young porter carry his candle , too , precede Mr. George , so there be a sufficiency of light in the apartment . 
 " we be not angry any more , Harry ! " say the stout gentleman , in a cheery voice , get up and advance with an outstretched hand to the new - comer . " thank God , my boy ! Mr. Amos here say , there will be no difficulty about James and I be your bail , and we will do your business by breakfast - time in the morning . why ... angel and minister of grace ! who be you ? " and he start back as the other have hold of his hand . 
 but the stranger grasp it only the more strongly . " God bless you , sir ! " he say , " I know who you be . you must be Colonel Lambert , of whose kindness to he my poor Harry write . and I be the brother whom you have hear of , sir ; and who be leave for dead in Mr. Braddock 's action ; and come to life again after eighteen month amongst the French ; and live to thank God and thank you for your kindness to my Harry , " continue the lad with a falter voice . 
 " James ! James ! here be news ! " cry Mr. Lambert to a gentleman in red , who now enter the room . " here be the dead come alive ! here be Harry Scapegrace 's brother come back , and with his scalp on his head , too ! "   George have take his hat off , and be stand by the light .   " this be my brother - bail , Mr. Warrington ! this be Lieutenant - Colonel James Wolfe , at your service . you must know there have be a little difference between Harry and I , Mr. George . he be pacify , be he , James ? " 
 " he be full of gratitude , " say Mr. Wolfe , after make his bow to Mr. Warrington . 
 " Harry write home about Mr. Wolfe , too , sir , " say the young man , " and I hope my brother 's friend will be so kind as to be mine . " 
 " I wish he have none other but we , Mr. Warrington . Poor Harry 's fine folk have be too fine for he , and have end by land he here . " 
 " Nay , your honour , I have do my good to make the young gentleman comfortable ; and , know your honour before , when you come to bail Captain Watkins , and that your security be perfectly good , if your honour wish , the young gentleman can go out this very night , and I will make it all right with the lawyer in the morning , " say Harry 's landlord , who know the rank and respectability of the two gentleman who have come to offer bail for his young prisoner . 
 " the debt be five hundred and odd pound , I think ? " say Mr. Warrington . " with a hundred thank to these gentleman , I can pay the amount at this moment into the officer ' hand , take the usual acknowledgment and caution . but I can never forget , gentleman , that you help my brother at his need , and , for do so , I say thank you , and God bless you , in my mother 's name and mine . " 
 Gumbo have , meanwhile , go upstairs to his master 's apartment , where Harry would probably have scold the negro for return that night , but that the young gentleman be very much soothe and touch by the conversation he have have with the friend who have just leave he . he be sit over his pipe of Virginia in a sad mood   for , somehow , even Maria 's goodness and affection , as she have just exhibit they , have not altogether console he ; and he have think , with a little dismay , of certain consequence to which that very kindness and fidelity bind he , when Mr. Wolfe 's homely feature and eager outstretched hand come to cheer the prisoner , and he hear how Mr. Lambert be below , and the errand upon which the two officer have come . in spite of himself , Lambert would be kind to he . in spite of Harry 's ill - temper , and needless suspicion and anger , the good gentleman be determined to help he if he might to help he even against Mr. Wolfe 's own advice , as the latter frankly tell Harry , " for you be wrong , Mr. Warrington , " say the Colonel , " and you would not   be set right ; and you , a young man , use hard word and unkind behaviour to your senior , and what be more , one of the good gentleman who walk God 's earth . you see , sir , what his answer hath be to your wayward temper . you will bear with a friend who speak frankly with you ? Martin Lambert hath act in this as he always doth , as the good Christian , the good friend , the most kind and generous of man . Nay , if you want another proof of his goodness , here it be : he have convert I , who , as I do not   care to disguise , be angry with you for your treatment of he , and have absolutely bring I down here to be your bail . let we both cry Peccavimus ! Harry , and shake our friend by the hand ! he be sit in the room below . he would not come here till he know how you would receive he . " 
 " I think he be a good man ! " groan out Harry . " I be very angry and wild at the time when he and I meet last , Colonel Wolfe . Nay , perhaps he be right in send back those trinket , hurt as I be at his do so . go down to he , will you be so kind , sir ? and tell he I be sorry , and ask his pardon , and and , God bless he for his generous behaviour . " and here the young gentleman turn his head away , and rub his hand across his eye . 
 " tell he all this thyself , Harry ! " cry the Colonel , take the young fellow 's hand . " no deputy will ever say it half so well . come with I now . " 
 " you go first , and I will I will follow , on my word I will . see ! I be in my morning - gown ! I will but put on a coat and come to he . give he my message first . just just prepare he for I ! " say poor Harry , who know he must do it , but yet do not much like that process of eat of humble - pie . 
 Wolfe go out smile understand the lad 's scruple well enough , perhaps . as he open the door , Mr. Gumbo enter it ; almost forget to bow to the gentleman , profusely courteous as he be on ordinary occasion , his eye glare round , his great mouth grin himself in a state of such high excitement and delight that his master remark his condition . 
 " what , Gum ? what have happen to thee ? Hast thou get a new sweetheart ? " 
 no , Gum have not get no new sweetheart , master . 
 " give I my coat . what have bring thee back ? " 
 Gum grin prodigiously . " I have see a ghost , mas'r ! " he say . 
 " a ghost ! and whose , and where ? " 
 " Whar ? see he at Madame Bernstein 's house . come with he here in the coach ! he downstair now with Colonel Lambert ! " Whilst Gumbo be speak , as he be put on his master 's coat , his eye be roll , his head be wag , his hand be tremble , his lip be grin . 
 " ghost what ghost ? " say Harry , in a strange agitation . " be anybody be my mother come ? " 
 " no , sir ; no , Master Harry ! " Gumbo 's head roll nearly off its violent convolution , and his master , look oddly at he , fling the door open , and go rapidly down the stair . 
 he be at the foot of it , just as a voice within the little office , of which the door be open , be say , " and for do so , I say thank you , and God bless you , in my mother 's name and mine . " 
 " whose voice be that ? " call out Harry Warrington , with a strange cry in his own voice . 
 " it be the ghost 's , mas'r ! " say Gumbo , from behind ; and Harry run forward to the room , where , if you please , we will pause a little minute before we enter . the two gentleman who be there , turn their head away . the lost be find again . the dead be alive . the prodigal be on his brother 's heart , his own full of love , gratitude , repentance . 
 " come away , James ! I think we be not want any more here , " say the Colonel . " good - night , boy . some lady in Hill Street will not   be able to sleep for this strange news . or will you go home and sup with them , and tell they the story ? " 
 no , with many thank , the boy would not go and sup to - night . they have story of their own to tell . " quick , Gumbo , with the trunk ! good - bye , Mr. Amos ! " Harry feel almost unhappy when he go away . 




 CHAPTER L. contain a great deal of the Finest Morality 

 when first we have the honour to be present to Sir Miles Warrington at the King 's drawing - room , in St. James 's Palace , I confess that I , for one look at his jolly round face , his broad round waistcoat , his hearty country manner , expect that I have light upon a most eligible and agreeable acquaintance at last , and be about to become intimate with that noble speciman of the human race , the bepraised of song and man , the good old English country gentleman . in fact , to be a good old country gentleman be to hold a position nearest the god , and at the summit of earthly felicity . to have a large unencumbered rent - roll , and the rent regularly pay by adore farmer , who bless their star at have such a landlord as his honour ; to have no tenant hold back with his money , except just one , perhaps , who do so in order to give occasion to Good Old Country Gentleman to show his sublime charity and universal benevolence of soul ; to hunt three day a week , love the sport of all thing , and have perfect good health and good appetite in consequence ; to have not only good appetite , but a good dinner ; to sit down at church in the midst of a chorus of blessing from the villager , the first man in the parish , the benefactor of the parish , with a consciousness of consummate desert , say , " have mercy upon we , miserable sinner , " to be sure , but only for form 's sake , because the word be write in the book , and to give other folk an example a G. O. C. G. a miserable sinner ! so healthy , so wealthy , so jolly , so much respect by the vicar , so much honour by the tenant , so much beloved and admire by his family , amongst whom his story of grouse in the gunroom cause laughter from generation to generation ; this perfect be a miserable sinner ! allon donc ! give any man good health and temper , five thousand a year , the adoration of his parish , and the love and worship of his family , and I will defy you to make he so heartily dissatisfied with his spiritual condition as to set himself down a miserable anything . if you be a Royal Highness , and go to church in the most perfect health and comfort , the parson wait to begin the service until your R.
