H. come in , would you believe yourself to be a miserable , etc . ? you might when rack with gout , in solitude , the fear of death before your eye , the doctor have cut off your bottle of claret , and order arrowroot and a little sherry , you might then be humiliate , and acknowledge your own shortcoming , and the vanity of thing in general ; but , in high health , sunshine , spirit , that word miserable be only a form . you can not   think in your heart that you be to be pity much for the present . if you be to be miserable , what be Colin Ploughman , with the ague , seven child , two pound a year rent to pay for his cottage , and eight shilling a week ? no : a healthy , rich , jolly , country gentleman , if miserable , have a very supportable misery : if a sinner , have very few people to tell he so . 
 it may be he become somewhat selfish ; but at least he be satisfied with himself . except my lord at the castle , there be nobody for mile and mile round so good or so great . his admirable wife minister to he , and to the whole parish , indeed : his child bow before he : the vicar of the parish reverence he : he be respect at quarter - session : he cause poacher to tremble : off go all hat before he at market : and round about his great coach , in which his spotless daughter and sublime lady sit , all the country - town tradesman cringe , bareheade , and the farmeer ' woman drop innumerable curtsey . from their cushion in the great coach the lady look down beneficently , and smile on the poor folk . they buy a yard of ribbon with affability ; they condescend to purchase an ounce of salt , or a packet of flower - seed : they deign to cheapen a goose : their drive be like a royal progress ; a happy people be suppose to press round they and bless they . tradesman bow , farmer ' wife bob , town - boy , wave their ragged hat , cheer the red - faced coachman as he drive the fat bay , and cry , " Sir Miles for ever ! throw we a halfpenny , my lady ! " 
 but suppose the market - woman should hide her fat goose when Sir Miles 's coach come , out of terror lest my lady , spy the bird , should insist on purchase it a bargain ? suppose no copper ever be know to come out of the royal coach window ? suppose Sir Miles regale his tenant with notoriously small beer , and his poor with especially thin broth ? this may be our fine old english gentleman 's way . there have be not a few fine english gentleman and lady of this sort ; who patronise the poor without ever relieve they , who call out " amen ! " at church as loud as the clerk ; who go through all the form of piety , and discharge all the etiquette of old english gentlemanhood ; who buy virtue a bargain , as it be , and have no doubt they be honour she by the purchase . poor Harry in his distress ask help from his relation : his aunt send he a tract and her blessing ; his uncle have business out of town , and could not , of course , answer the poor boy 's petition . how much of this behaviour go on daily in respectable life , think you ? you can fancy Lord and Lady Macbeth concoct a murder , and come together with some little awkwardness , perhaps , when the transaction be do and over ; but my Lord and Lady Skinflint , when they consult in their bedroom about give their luckless nephew a help hand , and determine to refuse , and go down to family prayer , and meet their child and domestic , and discourse virtuously before they , and then remain together , and talk nose to nose , what can they think of one another ? and of the poor kinsman fall among the thief , and groan for help unheeded ? how can they go on with those virtuous air ? how can they dare look each other in the face ? 
 Dare ? do you suppose they think they have do wrong ? do you suppose skinflint be torture with remorse at the idea of the distress which call to he in vain , and of the hunger which he send empty away ? not he . he be indignant with Prodigal for be a fool : he be not ashamed of himself for be a curmudgeon . what ? a young man with such opportunity throw they away ? a fortune spend amongst gambler and spendthrift ? horrible , horrible ! take warning , my child , by this unfortunate young man 's behaviour , and see the consequence of extravagance . accord to the great and always establish Church of the Pharisees , here be an admirable opportunity for a moral discourse , and an assertion of virtue . " and to think of his deceive we so ! " cry out Lady Warrington . 
 " very sad , very sad , my dear ! " say Sir Miles , wag his head . 
 " to think of so much extravagance in one so young ! " cry Lady Warrington . " Cards , bet , feast at tavern of the most wicked profusion , carriage and rid horse , the company of the wealthy and profligate of his own sex , and , I fear , of the most iniquitous person of our . " 
 " Hush , my Lady Warrington ! " cry her husband , glance towards the spotless Dora and Flora , who hold down their blushing head , at the mention of the last naughty person . 
 " no wonder my poor child hide their face ! " mamma continue . " my dear , I wish even the existence of such creature could be keep from you ! " 
 " they can not   go to an opera , or the park , without see them , to be sure , " say Sir Miles . 
 " to think we should have introduce such a young serpent into the bosom of our family ! and have leave he in the company of that guileless darling ! " and she point to Master Miles . 
 " who be a serpent , mamma ? " inquire that youth . " first you say cousin Harry be bad : then he be good : now he be bad again . which be he , Sir Miles ? " 
 " he have fault , like all of we , Miley , my dear . your cousin have be wild , and you must take warning by he . " 
 " be not my eld brother , who die my naughty brother be not he wild too ? he be not kind to I when I be quite a little boy . he never give I money , nor toy , nor ride with I , nor why do you cry , mamma ? sure I remember how Hugh and you be always fight " 
 " Silence , sir ! " cry out papa and the girl in a breath . " do not   you know you be never to mention that name ? " 
 " I know I love Harry , and I do not   love Hugh , " say the sturdy little rebel . " and if cousin Harry be in prison , I will give he my half - guinea that my godpapa give I , and anything I have yes , anything , except except my little horse and my silver waistcoat and and Snowball and Sweetlips at home and and , yes , my custard after dinner . " this be in reply to a hint of sister Dora . " but I would give he some of it , " continue Miles , after a pause . 
 " shut thy mouth with it , child , and then go about thy business , " say papa , amuse . Sir Miles Warrington have a considerable fund of easy humour . 
 " who would have think he should ever be so wild ? " mamma go on . 
 " Nay . Youth be the season for wild oat , my dear . " 
 " that we should be so mislead in he ! " sigh the girl . 
 " that he should kiss we both ! " cry papa . 
 " Sir Miles Warrington , I have no patience with that sort of vulgarity ! " say the majestic matron . 
 " which of you be the favourite yesterday , girl ? " continue the father . 
 " Favourite , indeed ! I tell he over and over again of my engagement to dear Tom I do , Dora why do you sneer , if you please ? " say the handsome sister . 
 " Nay , to do her justice , so do Dora too , " say papa . 
 " because Flora seem to wish to forget her engagement with dear Tom sometimes , " remark the sister . 
 " I never , never , never wish to break with Tom ! it be wicked of you to say so , Dora ! it be you who be for ever sneer at he : it be you who be always envious because I happen at least , because gentleman imagine that I be not ill - looking , and prefer I to some folk , in spite of all their learning and wit ! " cry Flora , toss her head over her shoulder , and look at the glass . 
 " why be you always look there , sister ? " say the artless Miles junior . " sure , you must know your face well enough ! " 
 " some people look at it just as often , child , who have not   near such good reason , " say papa , gallantly . 
 " if you mean I , Sir Miles , I thank you , " cry Dora . " my face be as Heaven make it , and my father and mother give it I . ' tis not my fault if I resemble my papa 's family . if my head be homely , at least I have get some brain in it . I envious of Flora , indeed , because she have find favour in the sight of poor Tom Claypool ! I should as soon be proud of captivate a ploughboy ! " 
 " pray , miss , be your Mr. Harry , of Virginia , much wise than Tom Claypool ? you would have have he for the asking ! " exclaim Flora . 
 " and so would you , miss , and have drop Tom Claypool into the sea ! " cry Dora . 
 " I would not . " 
 " you would . " 
 " I would not ; " and da capo go the conversation the shuttlecock of wrath be briskly battle from one sister to another . 
 " oh , my child ! be this the way you dwell together in unity ? " exclaim their excellent female parent , lay down her embroidery . " what an example you set to this Innocent ! " 
 " like to see them fight , my lady ! " cry the Innocent , rub his hand . 
 " at she , Flora ! worry she , Dora ! to it again , you little rogue ! " say facetious papa . " ' tis good sport , be not   it , Miley ? " 
 " oh , Sir Miles ! oh , my child ! these dispute be unseemly . they tear a fond mother 's heart , " say mamma , with majestic action , though bear the laceration of her bosom with much seeming equanimity . " what cause for thankfulness ought we to have that watchful parent have prevent any idle engagement between you and your misguided cousin . if we have be mistake in he , be it not a mercy that we have find out our error in time ? if either of you have any preference for he , your excellent good sense , my love , will teach you to overcome , to eradicate , the vain feeling . that we cherish and be kind to he can never be a source of regret . ' tis a proof of our good - nature . what we have to regret , I fear , be , that your cousin should have prove unworthy of our kindness , and , come away from the society of gambler , play - actor , and the like , should have bring contamination pollution , I have almost say into this pure family ! " 
 " oh , bother mamma 's sermon ! " say Flora , as my lady pursue a harangue of which we only give the commencement here , but during which papa , whistle , gently quit the room on tiptoe , whilst the artless Miles junior wind his top and peg it under the robe of his sister . it have do hum , and stagger and tumble over , and expire in its usual tipsy manner , long ere Lady Warrington have finish her sermon . 
 " be you listen to I , my child ? " she ask , lay her hand on her darling 's head . 
 " yes , mother , " say he , with the whipcord in his mouth , and proceed to wind up his sportive engine . " you be a - say that Harry be very poor now , and that we ought not   to help he . that be what you be say ; be not   it , madam ? " 
 " my poor child , thou wilt understand I well when thou art old ! " say mamma , turn towards that ceiling to which her eye always have recourse . 
 " get out , you little wretch ! " cry one of the sister . the artless one have peg his top at Dora 's toe , and laugh with the glee of merry boyhood at his sister 's discomfiture . 
 but what be this ? who come here ? why do Sir Miles return to the drawing - room , and why do Tom Claypool , who stride after the Baronet , wear a countenance so disturbed ? 
 " here be a pretty business , my Lady Warrington ! " cry Sir Miles . " here be a wonderful wonder of wonder , girl ! " 
 " for goodness ' sake , gentleman , what be your intelligence ? " ask the virtuous matron . 
 " the whole town 's talk about it , my lady ! " say Tom Claypool puff for breath . 
 " Tom have see he , " continue Sir Miles . 
 " seen both of they , my Lady Warrington . they be at Ranelagh last night , with a regular mob after them . and so like , that but for their different ribbon you would hardly have tell one from the other . one be in blue , the other in brown ; but I be certain he have wear both the suit here . " 
 " what suit ? " 
 " what one , what other ? " call the girl . 
 " why , your fortunate youth , to be sure . " 
 " our precious Virginian , and heir to the principality ! " say Sir Miles . 
 " be my nephew , then , release from his incarceration ? " ask her ladyship . " and be he again plunge in the vortex of dissip " 
 " confound he ! " roar out the Baronet , with an expression which I fear be even strong . " what should you think , my Lady Warrington , if this precious nephew of mine should turn out to be an impostor ; by George ! no well than an adventurer ? " 
 " an inward monitor whisper I as much ! " cry the lady ; " but I dash from I the unworthy suspicion . speak , Sir Miles , we burn with impatience to listen to your intelligence . " 
 " I will speak , my love , when you have   do , " say Sir Miles . " well , what do you think of my gentleman , who come into my house , dine at my table , be treat as one of this family , kiss my " 
 " what ? " ask Tom Claypool , fire as red as his waistcoat . 
 " Hem ! kiss my wife 's hand , and be treat in the fond manner , by George ! what do you think of this fellow , who talk of his property and his principality , by Jupiter ! turn out to be a beggarly second son ! a beggar , my Lady Warrington , by " 
 " Sir Miles Warrington , no violence of language before these dear one ! I sink to the earth , confound by this unutterable hypocrisy . and do I entrust thee to a pretender , my blessed boy ? do I leave thee with an impostor , my innocent one ? " the matron cry , fondle her son . 
 " who be an impostor , my lady ? " ask the child . 
 " that confound young scamp of a Harry Warrington ! " bawl out papa ; on which the little Miles , after wear a puzzled look for a moment , and yield to I know not what hide emotion , burst out cry . 
 his admirable mother propose to clutch he to her heart , but he reject the pure caress , bawl only the loud , and kick frantically about the maternal gremium , as the butler announce " Mr. George Warrington , Mr. Henry Warrington ! " Miles be drop from his mother 's lap . Sir Miles 's face emulate Mr. Claypool 's waistcoat . the three lady rise up , and make three most frigid curtsey , as our two young man enter the room . 
 little Miles run towards they . he hold out a little hand . " oh , Harry ! no ! which be Harry ? you be my Harry , " and he choose rightly this time . " oh , you dear Harry ! I be so glad you be come ! and they have   be abuse you so ! " 
 " I be come to pay my duty to my uncle , " say the dark - haired Mr. Warrington ; " and to thank he for his hospitality to my brother Henry . " 
 " what , nephew George ? my brother 's face and eye ! boy both , I be delighted to see you ! " cry their uncle , grasp affectionately a hand of each , as his honest face radiate with pleasure . 
 " this indeed hath be a most mysterious and a most providential resuscitation , " say Lady Warrington . " only I wonder that my nephew Henry conceal the circumstance until now , " she add , with a sidelong glance at both young gentleman . 
 " he know it no more than your ladyship , " say Mr. Warrington . the young lady look at each other with downcast eye . 
 " indeed , sir ! a most singular circumstance , " say mamma , with another curtsey . " we have hear of it , sir ; and Mr. Claypool , our county neighbour , have just bring we the intelligence , and it even now form the subject of my conversation with my daughter . " 
 " yes , " cry out a little voice , " and do you know , Harry , father and mother say you be a a imp " 
 " Silence , my child ! screwby , convey Master Warrington to his own apartment ! these , Mr. Warrington or , I suppose I should say nephew George be your cousin . " two curtsey two cheese be make two hand be hold out . Mr. Esmond Warrington make a profound low bow , which embrace   and it be the only embrace which the gentleman offer   all three lady . he lay his hat to his heart . he say , " it be my duty , madam , to pay my respect to my uncle and cousin , and to thank your ladyship for such hospitality as you have be enable to show to my brother . " 
 " it be not much , nephew , but it be our good . ods bob ! " cry the hearty Sir Miles , " it be our good ! "

Thou wilt be of the party , Tom ? Thou know our brew , hey , my boy ? "

 " and thou shalt taste it , my boy , thou shalt taste it ! what be there for dinner , my Lady Warrington ? our food be plain , but plenty , lad plain , but plenty ! " 
 " we can not partake of it to - day , sir . we dine with a friend who occupy my Lord Wrotham 's house , your neighbour . Colonel Lambert Major - General Lambert he have just be make . " 
 " with two daughter , I think countrifie - look girl be they not ? " ask Flora . 
 " I think I have remark two little rather dowdy thing , " say Dora . 
 " they be as good girl as any in England ! " break out Harry , to whom no one have think of say a single word . his reign be over , you see . he be nobody . what wonder , then , that he should not be visible ? 
 " oh , indeed , cousin ! " say Dora , with a glance at the young man , who sate with burn cheek , chafe at the humiliation put upon he , but not know how or whether he should notice it . " oh , indeed , cousin ! you be very charitable or very lucky , I be sure ! you see angel where we only see ordinary little person . I be sure I could not imagine who be those odd - look people in Lord Wrotham 's coach , with his handsome livery . but if they be three angel , I have nothing to say . " 
 " my brother be an enthusiast , " interpose George . " he be often mistake about woman . " 
 " oh , really ! " say Dora , look a little uneasy . 
 " I fear my nephew Henry have indeed meet with some unfavourable specimen of our sex , " the matron remark , with a groan . 
 " we be so easily take in , madam we be both very young yet we shall grow old and learn well . " 
 " most sincerely , nephew George , I trust you may . you have my good wish , my prayer , for your brother 's welfare and your own . no effort of our have be want . at a painful moment , to which I will not far allude " 
 " and when my uncle Sir Miles be out of town , " say George , look towards the Baronet , who smile at he with affectionate approval . 
 " I send your brother a work which I think might comfort he , and I know might improve he . Nay , do not thank I ; I claim no credit ; I do but my duty a humble woman 's duty for what be this world 's good , nephew , compare to the welfare of a soul ? if I do good , I be thankful ; if I be useful , I rejoice . if , through my mean , you have be bring , Harry , to consider " 
 " oh ! the sermon , be it ? " break in downright Harry . " I have not   time to read a single syllable of it , aunt thank you . you see I do not   care much about that kind of thing but thank you all the same . "

" 
 " anything I could do , my dear boy , I be sure my brother 's son my own nephew od bobs ! you know that is , anything anything , you know ! " cry Sir Miles , bring his own hand into George 's with a generous smack . " you can not   stay and dine with we ? put off the Colonel the General do , now ! or name a day . my Lady Warrington , make my nephew name a day when he will sit under his grandfather 's picture , and drink some of his wine ! " 
 " his intellectual faculty seem more developed than those of his unlucky young brother , " remark my lady , when the young gentleman have take their leave . " the young must be reckless and extravagant about money indeed , for do you remark , Sir Miles , the loss of his reversion in Virginia the amount of which have , no doubt , be grossly exaggerated , but , nevertheless , must be something considerable do you , I say , remark that the ruin of Harry 's prospect scarcely seem to affect he ? " 
 " I should not   be at all surprised that the elder turn out to be as poor as the young one , " say Dora , toss her head .

 " do he take Flora to an oratorio ? " ask Mr. Claypool , fiercely . 
 " I be ill and could not   go , and my cousin go with she , " say Dora . 
 " far be it from I to object to any innocent amusement , much less to the music of Mr. Handel , dear Mr. Claypool , " say mamma . " music refine the soul , elevate the understanding , be hear in our church , and ' tis well know be practise by King David . your opera I shun as deleterious ; your ballet I would forbid to my child as most immoral ; but music , my dear ! may we enjoy it , like everything else in reason may we " 
 " there be the music of the dinner - bell , " say papa , rub his hand . " come , girl . screwby , go and fetch Master Miley . Tom take down my lady . " 
 " Nay , dear Thomas , I walk but slowly . go you with dear Flora downstair , " say Virtue . 
 but Dora take care to make the evening pleasant by talk of Handel and oratorio constantly during dinner . 




 CHAPTER LI . Conticuere Omnes 

 across the way , if the gracious reader will please to step over with we , he will find our young gentleman at Lord Wrotham 's house , which his lordship have lend to his friend the General , and that little family party assemble , with which we make acquaintance at Oakhurst and Tunbridge Wells . James Wolfe have promise to come to dinner ; but James be dance attendance upon Miss Lowther , and would rather have a glance from her eye than the fine kickshaw dress by Lord Wrotham 's cook , or the dessert which be promise for the entertainment at which you be just go to sit down . you will make the sixth . you may take Mr. Wolfe 's place . you may be sure he will not   come . as for I , I will stand at the sideboard and report the conversation . 
 note first , how happy the woman look ! when Harry Warrington be take by those bailiff , I have intend to tell you how the good Mrs. Lambert , hearing of the boy 's mishap , have fly to her husband , and have beg , implored , insist , that her Martin should help he . " never mind his rebeldom of the other day ; never mind about his be angry that his present be return of course anybody would be angry , much more such a high - spirited lad as Harry ! never mind about our be so poor , and want all our spare money for the boy at college ; there must be some way of get he out of the scrape . do you not get Charles Watkins out of the scrape two year ago ; and do he not pay you back every halfpenny ? yes ; and you make a whole family happy , bless be God ! and Mrs. Watkins pray for you and bless you to this very day , and I think everything have prosper with we since . and I have no doubt it have make you a major - general no earthly doubt , " say the fond wife . 
 now , as Martin Lambert require very little persuasion to do a kind action , he in this instance let himself be persuade easily enough , and have make up his mind to seek for friend James Wolfe , and give bail for Harry , he take his leave and his hat , and squeeze Theo 's hand , who seem to divine his errand   or perhaps that silly mamma have blab it , and kiss little Hetty 's flushed cheek , and away he go out of the apartment where the girl and their mother be sit , though he be follow out of the room by the latter . 
 when she be alone with he , that enthusiastic matron can not control her feeling any long . she fling her arm round her husband 's neck , kiss he a hundred and twenty - five time in an instant call God to bless he cry plentifully on his shoulder ; and in this sentimental attitude be discover by old Mrs. Quiggett , my lord 's housekeeper , who be bustle about the house , and , I suppose , be quite astounded at the conjugal phenomenon . 
 " we have have a tiff , and we be make it up ! do not   tell tale out of school , Mrs. Quiggett ! " say the gentleman , walk off . 
 " well , I never ! " say Mrs. Quiggett , with a shrill , strident laugh , like a venerable old cockatoo which white , hook - nose , long - live bird Mrs. Quiggett strongly resemble . " well , I never ! " say Quiggett , laugh and shake her old side till all her key , and , as one may fancy , her old rib clatter and jingle . 
 " oh , Quiggett ! " sob out Mrs. Lambert , " what a man that be ! " 
 " you have   be a - quarrelling , have you , mum , and make it up ? that be right . " 
 " quarrel with he ? he never tell a great story . my General be an angel , Quiggett . I should like to worship he . I should like to fall down at his boot and kiss them , I should ! there never be a man so good as my General . what have I do to have such a man ? how dare I have such a good husband ? " 
 " my dear , I think there be a pair of you , " say the old cockatoo ; " and what would you like for your supper ? " 
 when Lambert come back very late to that meal , and tell what have happen , how Harry be free , and how his brother have come to life , and rescue he , you may fancy what a commotion the whole of those people be in ! if Mrs. Lambert 's General be an angel before , what be he now ! if she want to embrace his boot in the morning , pray what further office of wallow degradation would she prefer in the evening ? little Hetty come and nestle up to her father quite silent , and drink a little drop out of his glass . Theo 's and mamma 's face beam with happiness , like two moon of brightness .... after supper , those four at a certain signal fall down on their knee glad homage pay in awful mirth - rejoicing , and with such pure joy as angel do , we read , for the sinner that repent . there come a great knocking at the door whilst they be so gather together . who can be there ? my lord be in the country mile off . it be past midnight now ; so late have they be , so long have they be talk ! I think Mrs. Lambert guess who be there . 
 " this be George , " say a young gentleman , lead in another . " we have be to Aunt Bernstein . we could not   go to bed , Aunt Lambert , without come to thank you too . you dear , dear , good " there be no more speech audible . Aunt Lambert be kiss Harry , Theo have snatch up Hetty who be as pale as death , and be hug she into life again . George Warrington stand with his hat off , and then   when Harry 's transaction be conclude   go up and kiss Mrs. Lambert 's hand : the General pass his across his eye . I protest they be all in a very tender and happy state . generous heart sometimes feel it , when Wrong be forgive , when Peace be restore , when love return that have be think lose . 
 " we come from Aunt Bernstein 's ; we see light here , you see ; we could not   go to sleep without say good - night to you all , " say Harry . " could we , George ? " 
 " ' tis certainly a famous nightcap you have bring we , boy , " say the General . " when be you to come and dine with we ? to - morrow ? " no , they must go to Madame Bernstein 's to - morrow . 
 the next day , then ? yes , they would come the next day and that be the very day we be write about : and this be the very dinner , at which , in the room of Lieutenant - Colonel James Wolfe , absent on private affair , my gracious reader have just be invite to sit down . 
 to sit down , and why , if you please ? not to a mere Barmecide dinner no , no but to hear MR . GEORGE ESMOND WARRINGTON 'S STATEMENT , which of course he be go to make . here they all sit not in my lord 's grand dining - room , you know , but in the snug study or parlour in front . the cloth have be withdraw , the General have give the King 's health , the servant have leave the room , the guest sit conticent , and so , after a little hemming and blushing , Mr. George proceed : 
 " I remember , at the table of our General , how the little Philadelphia agent , whose wit and shrewdness we have remark at home , make the very objection to the conduct of the campaign of which its disastrous issue show the justice . ' of course , ' say he , ' your Excellency 's troop once before Fort Duquesne , such a weak little place will never be able to resist such a general , such an army , such artillery , as will there be find attack it . but do you calculate , sir , on the difficulty of reach the place ? your Excellency 's march will be through wood almost untrodden , over road which you will have to make yourself , and your line will be some four mile long . this slender line , have to make its way through the forest , will be subject to endless attack in front , in rear , in flank , by enemy whom you will never see , and whose constant practice in war be the dexterous laying of ambuscade . ' ' Psha , sir ! ' say the General , ' the savage may frighten your raw american militia '   thank your Excellency for the compliment , Mr. Washington seem to say , who be sit at the table , ' but the Indians will never make any impression on his Majesty 's regular troop . ' ' I heartily hope not , sir , ' say Mr. Franklin , with a sigh ; and of course the gentleman of the General 's family sneer at the postmaster , as at a pert civilian who have no call to be give his opinion on matter entirely beyond his comprehension . 
 " we despise the Indians on our own side , and our commander make light of they and their service . our officer disgust the chief who be with we by outrageous behaviour to their woman . there be not above seven or eight who remain with our force . have we have a couple of hundred in our front on that fatal 9th of July , the event of the day must have be very different . they would have fling off the attack of the French Indians ; they would have prevent the surprise and panic which ensue . ' tis know now that the French have even get ready to give up their fort , never dream of the possibility of a defence , and that the French Indians themselves remonstrate against the audacity of attack such an overwhelming force as ours . 
 " I be with our General with the main body of the troop when the firing begin in front of we , and one aide - de - camp after another be send forwards . at first the enemy 's attack be answer briskly by our own advanced people , and our man huzzae and cheer with good heart . but very soon our fire grow slacker , whilst from behind every tree and bush round about we come single shot , which lay man after man low . we be march in orderly line , the skirmisher in front , the colour and two of our small gun in the centre , the baggage well guard bring up the rear , and be move over a ground which be open and clear for a mile or two , and for some half mile in breadth , a thick tangle covert of brushwood and tree on either side of we . after the firing have continue for some brief time in front , it open from both side of the environing wood on our advance column . the man drop rapidly , the officer in great number than the man . at first , as I say , these cheer and answer the enemy 's fire , our gun even open on the wood , and seem to silence the French in ambuscade there . but the hide rifle - firing begin again . our man halt , huddle up together , in spite of the shout and order of the General and officer to advance , and fire wildly into the brushwood of course make no impression . those in advance come run back on the main body frighten , and many of they wound . they report there be five thousand frenchman and a legion of yell indian devil in front , who be scalp our people as they fall . we could hear their cry from the wood around as our man drop under their rifle . there be no induce the people to go forward now . one aide - de - camp after another be send forward , and never return . at last it come to be my turn , and I be send with a message to Captain Fraser of Halkett 's in front , which he be never to receive nor I to deliver . 
 " I have not go thirty yard in advance when a rifle - ball strike my leg , and I fall straightway to the ground . I recollect a rush forward of Indians and Frenchmen after that , the former cry their fiendish war - cry , the latter as fierce as their savage ally . I be amazed and mortify to see how few of the whitecoat there be . not above a score pass I ; indeed there be not fifty in the accursed action in which two of the brave regiment of the british army be put to rout . 
 " one of they , who be half indian half Frenchman , with mocassin and a white uniform coat and cockade , see I prostrate on the ground , turn back and run towards I , his musket club over his head to dash my brain out and plunder I as I lay . I have my little fusil which my Harry give I when I go on the campaign ; it have fall by I and within my reach , luckily : I seize it , and down fall the Frenchman dead at six yard before I . I be save for that time , but bleed from my wound and very faint . I swoon almost in try to load my piece , and it drop from my hand , and the hand itself sink lifeless to the ground . 
 " I be scarcely in my sens , the yell and shot ring dimly in my ear , when I see an Indian before I , busy over the body of the Frenchman I have just shoot , but glance towards I as I lay on the ground bleed . he first rifle the Frenchman , tear open his coat , and feel in his pocket : he then scalp he , and with his bleed knife in his mouth advance towards I . I see he come as through a film , as in a dream I be powerless to move , or to resist he . 
 " he put his knee upon my chest : with one bloody hand he seize my long hair and lift my head from the ground , and as he lift it , he enable I to see a french officer rapidly advance behind he . 
 " good God ! it be young Florac , who be my second in the duel at Quebec . ' a moi , Florac ! ' I cry out . ' C'est Georges ! aide moi ! ' 
 " he start ; run up to I at the cry , lay his hand on the Indian 's shoulder , and call he to hold . but the savage do not understand French , or choose to understand it . he clutch my hair firm , and wave his dripping knife round it , motion to the french lad to leave he to his prey . I could only cry out again and piteously , ' a moi ! ' 
 " ' ah , canaille , tu veux du sing ? prend ! ' say Florac , with a curse ; and the next moment , and with an ugh , the Indian fall over my chest dead , with Florac 's sword through his body . 
 " my friend look round he . ' eh ! ' say he , ' la belle affaire ! where art thou wound ? in the leg ? ' he bind my leg tight round with his sash . ' the other will kill thee if they find thee here . ah , tien ! put I on this coat , and this hat with the white cockade . call out in French if any of our people pass . they will take thee for one of we . thou art Brunet of the Quebec Volunteers . God guard thee , Brunet ! I must go forward . ' tis a general debacle , and the whole of your redcoat be on the run , my poor boy . ' ah , what a rout it be ! what a day of disgrace for England ! 
 " Florac 's rough application stop the bleeding of my leg , and the kind creature help I to rest against a tree , and to load my fusil , which he place within reach of I , to protect I in case any other marauder should have a mind to attack I . and he give I the gourd of that unlucky french soldier , who have lose his own life in the deadly game which he have just play against I , and the drink the gourd contain serve greatly to refresh and invigorate I . take a mark of the tree against which I lay , and note the various bearing of the country , so as to be able again to find I , the young lad hasten on to the front . ' thou seest how much I love thee , George , ' he say , ' that I stay behind in a moment like this . ' I forget whether I tell thee Harry , that Florac be under some obligation to I . I have win money of he at card , at Quebec only play at his repeat entreaty and there be a difficulty about pay , and I remit his debt to I , and light my pipe with his note - of - hand . you see , sir , that you be not the only gambler in the family . 
 " at evening , when the dismal pursuit be over , the faithful fellow come back to I , with a couple of Indians , who have each reeking scalp at their belt , and whom he inform that I be a Frenchman , his brother , who have be wound early in the day , and must be carry back to the fort . they lay I in one of their blanket , and carry I , groan , with the trusty Florac by my side . have he leave I , they would assuredly have lay I down , plunder I , and add my hair to that of the wretch whose bleeding spoil hang at their girdle . he promise they brandy at the fort , if they bring I safely there : I have but a dim recollection of the journey : the anguish of my wound be extreme : I faint more than once . we come to the end of our march at last . I be take into the fort , and carry to the officer 's log - house , and lay upon Florac 's own bed . 
 " happy for I be my insensibility . I have be bring into the fort as a wound french soldier of the garrison . I hear afterwards , that during my delirium the few prisoner who have be make on the day of our disaster , have be bring under the wall of Duquesne by their savage captor , and there horribly burn , torture , and butcher by the Indians , under the eye of the garrison . " 
 as George speak , one may fancy a thrill of horror run through his sympathise audience . Theo take Hetty 's hand , and look at George in a very alarmed manner . Harry strike his fist upon the table , and cry , " the bloody , murderous , red - skin villain ! there will never be peace for we until they be all hunt down ! " 
 " they be offer a hundred and thirty dollar apiece for indian scalp in Pennsylvania , when I leave home , " say George , demurely , " and fifty for woman . " 
 " fifty for woman , my love ! do you hear that , Mrs. Lambert ? " cry the Colonel , lift up his wife 's hair . 
 " the murderous villain ! " say Harry , again . " hunt them down , sir ! hunt them down ! " 
 " I know not how long I lay in my fever , " George resume . " when I awake to my sens , my dear Florac be go . he and his company have be despatch on an enterprise against an english fort on the pennsylvanian territory , which the French claim , too . in Duquesne , when I come to be able to ask and understand what be say to I , there be not above thirty Europeans leave . the place might have be take over and over again , have any of our people have the courage to return after their disaster . 
 " my old enemy the ague - fever set in again upon I as I lie here by the river - side . ' tis a wonder how I ever survive . but for the goodness of a half - breed woman in the fort , who take pity on I , and tend I , I never should have recover , and my poor Harry would be what he fancy himself yesterday , our grandfather 's heir , our mother 's only son . 
 " I remember how , when Florac lay I in his bed , he put under my pillow my money , my watch , and a trinket or two which I have . when I wake to myself these be all go ; and a surly old sergeant , the only officer leave in the quarter , tell I , with a curse , that I be lucky enough to be leave with my life at all ; that it be only my white cockade and coat have save I from the fate which the other canaille of Rosbifs have deservedly meet with . 
 " at the time of my recovery the fort be almost empty of the garrison . the Indians have retire enrich with british plunder , and the chief part of the french regular be go upon expedition northward . my good Florac have leave I upon his service , consign I to the care of an invalided sergeant . Monsieur de Contrecoeur have accompany one of these expedition , leave an old lieutenant , Museau by name , in command at Duquesne . 
 " this man have long be out of France , and serve in the colony . his character , doubtless , have be indifferent at home ; and he know that , accord to the system pursue in France , where almost all promotion be give to the noblesse , he never would advance in rank . and he have make free with my guineas , I suppose , as he have with my watch , for I see it one day on his chest when I be sit with he in his quarter . 
 " Monsieur Museau and I manage to be pretty good friend . if I could be exchange , or send home , I tell he that my mother would pay liberally for my ransom ; and I suppose this idea excite the cupidity of the commandant , for a trapper come in the winter , whilst I still lay very ill with fever , Museau consent that I should write home to my mother , but that the letter should be in French , that he should see it , and that I should say I be in the hand of the Indians , and should not be ransom under ten thousand livre . 
 " in vain I say I be a prisoner to the troop of his Most christian Majesty , that I expect the treatment of a gentleman and an officer . Museau swear that letter should go , and no other ; that if I hesitate , he would fling I out of the fort , or hand I over to the tender mercy of his ruffian indian ally . he would not let the trapper communicate with I except in his presence . life and liberty be sweet . I resist for a while , but I be pull down with weakness , and shudder with fever ; I write such a letter as the rascal consent to let pass , and the trapper go away with my missive , which he promise , in three week , to deliver to my mother in Virginia . 
 " three week , six , twelve , pass . the messenger never return . the winter come and go , and all our little plantation round the fort , where the french soldier have clear corn - ground and plant garden and peach- and apple - tree down to the Monongahela , be in full blossom . Heaven know how I creep through the weary time ! when I be pretty well , I make drawing of the soldier of the garrison , and of the half - breed and her child   Museau 's child , and of Museau himself , whom , I be ashamed to say , I flatter outrageously ; and there be an old guitar leave in the fort , and I sing to it , and play on it some french air which I know , and ingratiate myself as good I could with my gaoler ; and so the weary month pass , but the messenger never return . 
 " at last news arrive that he have be shoot by some British Indians in Maryland : so there be an end of my hope of ransom for some month more . this make Museau very savage and surly towards I ; the more so as his sergeant inflame his rage by tell he that the indian woman be partial to I as I believe , poor thing , she be . I be always gentle with she , and grateful to she . my small accomplishment seem wonder in her eye ; I be ill and unhappy , too , and these be always claim to a woman 's affection . 
 " a captive pull down by malady , a ferocious gaoler , and a young woman touch by the prisoner 's misfortune sure you expect that , with these three prime character in a piece , some pathetic tragedy be go to be enact ? you , Miss Hetty , be about to guess that the woman save I ? " 
 " why , of course she do ! " cry mamma . 
 " what else be she good for ? " say Hetty . 
 " you , Miss Theo , have paint she already as a dark beauty be it not so ? a swift huntress " 
 " Diana with a baby , " say the Colonel . 
 " who scour the plain with her nymph , who bring down the game with her unerring bow , who be queen of the forest and I see by your look that you think I be madly in love with she ? " 
 " well , I suppose she be an interesting creature , Mr. George ? " say Theo , with a blush . 
 " what think you of a dark beauty , the colour of new mahogany with long straight black hair , which be usually dress with a hair - oil or pomade by no mean pleasant to approach , with little eye , with high cheek - bone , with a flat nose , sometimes ornament with a ring , with row of glass bead round her tawny throat , her cheek and forehead gracefully tattooed , a great love of finery , and inordinate passion for oh ! must I own it ? " 
 " for coquetry . I know you be go to say that ! " say Miss Hetty . 
 " for whisky , my dear Miss Hester in which appetite my gaoler partook ; so that I have often sate by , on the night when I be in favour with Monsieur Museau , and see he and his poor companion hob - and - nobbing together until they could scarce hold the noggin out of which they drank . in these evening entertainment , they would sing , they would dance , they would fondle , they would quarrel , and knock the can and furniture about ; and , when I be in favour , I be admit to share their society , for Museau , jealous of his dignity , or not willing that his man should witness his behaviour , would allow none of they to be familiar with he . 
 " whilst the result of the trapper 's mission to my home be yet uncertain , and Museau and I myself expect the payment of my ransom , I be treat kindly enough , allow to crawl about the fort , and even to go into the adjoining field and garden , always keep my parole , and duly return before gun - fire . and I exercise a piece of hypocrisy , for which , I hope , you will hold I excuse . when my leg be sound   the ball come out in the winter , after some pain and inflammation , and the wound heal up presently , I yet choose to walk as if I be disabled and a cripple ; I hobble on two stick , and cry ah ! and oh ! at every minute , hop that a day might come when I might treat my limb to a run . 
 " Museau be very savage when he begin to give up all hope of the first messenger . he fancy that the man might have get the ransom - money and flee with it himself . of course he be prepared to disown any part in the transaction , should my letter be discover . his treatment of I vary accord to his hope or fear , or even his mood for the time be . he would have I consign to my quarter for several day at a time ; then invite I to his tipsy supper - table , quarrel with I there , and abuse my nation ; or again break out into maudlin sentimentality about his native country of Normandy , where he long to spend his old age , to buy a field or two , and to die happy . 
 " ' eh , Monsieur Museau ! ' say I , ' ten thousand livre of your money would buy a pretty field or two in your native country ? you can have it for the ransom of I , if you will but let I go . in a few month you must be supersede in your command here , and then adieu the crown and the field in Normandy ! you have well trust a gentleman and a man of honour . let I go home , and I give you my word the ten thousand livre shall be pay to any agent you may appoint in France or in Quebec . ' 
 " ' ah , young traitor ! ' roar he , ' do you wish to tamper with my honour ? do you believe an officer of France will take a bribe ? I have a mind to consign thee to my black - hole , and to have thee shot in the morning . ' 
 " ' my poor body will never fetch ten thousand livre , ' say I ; ' and a pretty field in Normandy with a cottage ... ' 
 " ' and an orchard . ah , sacre bleu ! ' say Museau , whimpering , ' and a dish of tripe a la mode du pay ! ... " 
 " this talk happen between we again and again , and Museau would order I to my quarter , and then ask I to supper the next night , and return to the subject of Normandy , and cider , and trippe a la mode de Caen . my friend be dead now " 
 " he be hung , I trust ? " break in Colonel Lambert . 
 " and I need keep no secret about he . lady , I wish I have to offer you the account of a dreadful and tragical escape ; how I slay all the sentinel of the fort ; file through the prison window , destroy a score or so of watchful dragon , overcome a million of danger , and finally effect my freedom . but , in regard of that matter , I have no heroic deed to tell of , and own that , by bribery and no other mean , I be where I be . " 
 " but you would have fight , Georgy , if need be , " say Harry ; " and you could not   conquer a whole garrison , you know ! " and herewith Mr. Harry blush very much . 
 " see the woman , how disappointed they be ! " say Lambert . " Mrs. Lambert , you bloodthirsty woman , own that you be balk of a battle ; and look at Hetty , quite angry because Mr. George do not shoot the commandant . " 
 " you wish he be hang yourself , papa ! " cry Miss Hetty , " and I be sure I wish anything my papa wish . " 
 " Nay , lady , " say George , turn a little red , " to wink at a prisoner 's escape be not a very monstrous crime ; and to take money ? sure other folk besides frenchman have condescend to a bribe before now . although Monsieur Museau set I free , I be inclined , for my part , to forgive he . will it please you to hear how that business be do ? you see , Miss Hetty , I can not help be alive to tell it . " 
 " oh , George ! that be , I mean , Mr. Warrington ! that be , I mean , I beg your pardon ! " cry Hester . 
 " no pardon , my dear ! I never be angry yet or surprise that any one should like my Harry well than I . he deserve all the liking that any man or woman can give he . see , it be his turn to blush now , " say George . 
 " go on , Georgy , and tell they about the escape out of Duquesne ! " cry Harry , and he say to Mrs. Lambert afterwards in confidence , " you know he be always go on say that he ought never to have come to life again , and declare that I be well than he be . the idea of my be well than George , Mrs. Lambert ! a poor , extravagant fellow like I ! it be absurd ! " 




 CHAPTER LII . Intentique Ora tenebant 

 " we continue for month our weary life at the fort , and the commandant and I have our quarrel and reconciliation , our greasy game at card , our dismal duet with his asthmatic flute and my cracked guitar . the poor Fawn take her beating and her can of liquor as her lord and master choose to administer they ; and she nurse her papoose , or her master in the gout , or her prisoner in the ague ; and so matter go on until the beginning of the fall of last year , when we be visit by a hunter who have important news to deliver to the commandant , and such as set the little garrison in no little excitement . the Marquis de Montcalm have send a considerable detachment to garrison the fort already in the french hand , and to take up further position in the enemy 's that be , in the british possession . the troop have leave Quebec and Montreal , and be come up the St. Lawrence and the lake in bateaux , with artillery and large provision of warlike and other store . Museau would be supersede in his command by an officer of superior rank , who might exchange I , or who might give I up to the Indians in reprisal for cruelty practise by our own people on many and many an officer and soldier of the enemy . the man of the fort be eager for the reinforcement ; they would advance into Pennsylvania and New York ; they would seize upon Albany and Philadelphia ; they would drive the Rosbifs into the sea , and all America should be theirs from the Mississippi to Newfoundland . 
 " this be all very triumphant : but yet , somehow , the prospect of the french conquest do not add to Mr. Museau 's satisfaction . 
 " ' eh , commandant ! ' say I , " tis fort bien , but meanwhile your farm in Normandy , the pot of cider , and the trippe a la mode de Caen , where be they ? ' 
 " ' yes ; ' tis all very well , my garcon , ' say he . ' but where will you be when poor old Museau be supersede ? other officer be not good companion like I . very few man in the world have my humanity . when there be a great garrison here , will my successor give thee the indulgence which honest Museau have grant thee ? thou wilt be keep in a sty like a pig ready for kill . as sure as one of our officer fall into the hand of your brigand of frontier - man , and evil come to he , so surely wilt thou have to pay with thy skin for his . thou wilt be give up to our red ally to the brother of La Biche yonder . didst thou see , last year , what they do to thy countryman whom we take in the action with Braddock ? roasting be the very small punishment , ma foi be it not , La Biche ? ' 
 " and he enter into a variety of jocular description of torture inflict , eye burn out of their socket , tooth and nail wrench out , limb and body gash you turn pale , dear Miss Theo ! well , I will have pity , and will spare you the torture which honest Museau recount in his pleasant way as likely to befall I . 
 " La Biche be by no mean so affected as you seem to be , lady , by the recital of these horror . she have witness they in her time . she come from the Senecas , whose village lie near the great cataract between Ontario and Erie ; her people make war for the English , and against they : they have fight with other tribe ; and , in the battle between we and they , it be difficult to say whether whiteskin or redskin be most savage . 
 " ' they may chop I into cutlet and broil I , ' tis true , commandant , ' say I , coolly . ' but again , I say , you will never have the farm in Normandy . ' 
 " ' go get the whisky - bottle , La Biche , ' say Museau . 
 " ' and it be not too late , even now . I will give the guide who take I home a large reward . and again I say , I promise , as a man of honour , ten thousand livre to whom shall I say ? to one who shall bring I any token who shall bring I , say , my watch and seal with my grandfather 's arm which I have see in a chest somewhere in this fort . ' 
 " ' ah , scelerat ! ' roar out the commandant , with a hoarse yell of laughter . ' thou hast eye , thou ! all be good prize in war . ' 
 " ' think of a house in your village , of a fine field hard by with a half - dozen of cow of a fine orchard all cover with fruit . ' 
 " ' and Javotte at the door with her wheel , and a rascal of a child , or two , with cheek as red as the apple ! o my country ! o my mother ! " whimper out the commandant . ' quick , La Biche , the whisky ! ' 
 " all that night the commandant be deep in thought , and La Biche , too , silent and melancholy . she sate away from we , nurse her child , and whenever my eye turn towards she I see her be fix on I . the poor little infant begin to cry , and be order away by Museau , with his usual foul language , to the building which the luckless Biche occupy with her child . when she be go , we both of we speak our mind freely ; and I put such reason before monsieur as his cupidity could not resist . 
 " ' how do you know , ' he ask , ' that this hunter will serve you ? ' 
 " ' that be my secret , ' say I. but here , if you like , as we be not on honour , I may tell it . when they come into the settlement for their bargain , the hunter often stop a day or two for rest and drink and company , and our new friend love all these . he play at card with the man : he set his fur against their liquor : he enjoy himself at the fort , singing , dancing , and gamble with they . I think I say they like to listen to my song , and for want of well thing to do , I be often sing and guitar - scraping : and we would have many a concert , the man join in chorus , or dance to my homely music , until it be interrupt by the drum and the retraite . 
 " our guest the hunter be present at one or two of these concert , and I think I would try if possibly he understand English . after we have have our little stock of french song , I say , ' my lad , I will give you an english song , ' and to the tune of ' over the hill and far away , ' which my good old grandfather use to hum as a favourite air in Marlborough 's camp , I make some doggerel word : ' this long , long year , a prisoner drear ; ah , I ! I be tired of linger here : I will give a hundred guineas gay , to be over the hill and far away . ' 
 " ' what be it ? ' say the hunter . ' I do not   understand . ' 
 " " tis a girl to her lover , ' I answer ; but I see by the twinkle in the man 's eye that he understand I . 
 " the next day , when there be no man within hearing , the trapper show that I be right in my conjecture , for as he pass I he hum in a low tone , but in perfectly good English , ' over the hill and far away , ' the burden of my yesterday 's doggerel . 
 " ' if you be ready , ' say he , ' I be ready . I know who your people be , and the way to they . talk to the fawn , and she will tell you what to do . what ! you will not play with I ? ' here he pull out some card , and speak in French as two soldier come up . ' Milor est trop grand seigneur ? Bonjour , my lord ! ' 
 " and the man make I a mock bow , and walk away , shrug up his shoulder , to offer to play and drink elsewhere . 
 " I know now that the Biche be to be the agent in the affair , and that my offer to Museau be accept . the poor Fawn perform her part very faithfully and dexterously . I have not need of a word more with Museau ; the matter be understand between we . the Fawn have long be allow free communication with I . she have tend I during my wound and in my illness , help to do the work of my little chamber , my cooking , and so forth . she be free to go out of the fort , as I have say , and to the river and the field whence the corn and garden - stuff of the little garrison be bring in . 
 " have gamble away most of the money which he receive for his peltrie , the trapper now get together his store of flint , powder , and blanket , and take his leave . and , three day after his departure , the Fawn give I the signal that the time be come for I to make my little trial for freedom . 
 " when first wound , I have be take by my kind Florac and place on his bed in the officer ' room . when the fort be empty of all officer except the old lieutenant leave in command , I have be allow to remain in my quarter , sometimes be leave pretty free , sometimes be lock up and feed on prisoner ' ration , sometimes invite to share his mess by my tipsy gaoler . 
 " this officer ' house , or room , be of log like the half - dozen other within the fort , which mount only four gun of small calibre , of which one be on the bastion behind my cabin . look westward over this gun , you could see a small island at the confluence of the two river Ohio and Monongahela whereon Duquesne be situate . on the shore opposite this island be some tree . 
 " ' you see those tree ? ' my poor Biche say to I the day before , in her french jargon . ' he wait for you behind those tree . ' 
 " in the daytime the door of my quarter be open , and the Biche free to come and go . on the day before she come in from the field with a pick in her hand and a basketful of vegetable and potherb for soup . she sit down on a bench at my door , the pick rest against it , and the basket at her side . I stand talk to she for a while : but I believe I be so idiotic that I never should have think of put the pick to any use have she actually push it into my open door , so that it fall into my room . ' hide it ' she say ; ' want it soon . ' and that afternoon it be , she point out the tree to I . 
 " on the next day , she come , pretend to be very angry , and call out , ' my lord ! my lord ! why you not come to commandant 's dinner ? he very bad ! Entendez - vow ? ' and she peep into the room as she speak , and fling a coil of rope at I . 
 " ' I be come , La Biche , ' say I , and hobble after she on my crutch . as I go in to the commandant 's quarter she say , ' pour ce soir . ' and then I know the time be come . 
 " as for Museau , he know nothing about the matter . not he ! he growl at I , and say the soup be cold . he look I steadily in the face , and talk of this and that ; not only whilst his servant be present , but afterwards as we smoke our pipe and play our game at piquet ; whilst accord to she will not , the poor Biche sate cower in a corner . 
 " my friend 's whisky - bottle be empty ; and he say , with rather a know look , he must have another glass we must both have a glass that night . and rise from the table he stump to the inner room where he keep his fire - water under lock and key , and away from the poor Biche , who could not resist that temptation . 
 " as he turn his back the Biche raise herself ; and he be no soon go but she be at my foot , kiss my hand , press it to her heart , and burst into tear over my knee . I confess I be so troubled by this testimony of the poor creature 's silent attachment and fondness , the extent of which I scarce have suspect before , that when Museau return , I have not recover my equanimity , though the poor Fawn be back in her corner again and shroud in her blanket . 
 " he do not appear to remark anything strange in the behaviour of either . we sate down to our game , though my thought be so preoccupied that I scarcely know what card be before I . 
 " ' I gain everything from you to - night , milor , ' say he , grimly . ' we play upon parole . ' 
 " ' and you may count upon mine , ' I reply . 
 " ' eh ! ' tis all that you have ! ' say he . 
 " ' Monsieur , ' say I , ' my word be good for ten thousand livre ; ' and we continue our game . 
 " at last he say he have a headache , and would go to bed , and I understand the order too , that I be to retire . ' I wish you a good night , mon petit milor , ' say he , ' stay , you will fall without your crutch , ' and his eye twinkle at I , and his face wear a sarcastic grin . in the agitation of the moment I have quite forget that I be lame , and be walk away at a pace as good as a grenadier 's . 
 " ' what a vilain night ! ' say he , look out . in fact there be a tempest abroad , and a great roaring , and wind . ' bring a lanthorn , La Tulipe , and lock my lord comfortably into his quarter ! ' he stand a moment look at I from his own door , and I see a glimpse of the poor Biche behind he . 
 " the night be so rainy that the sentry prefer their box , and do not disturb I in my work . the log - house be build with upright post , deeply fix in the ground , and horizontal log lay upon it . I have to dig under these , and work a hole sufficient to admit my body to pass . I begin in the dark , soon after tattoo . it be some while after midnight before my work be do , when I lift my hand up under the log and feel the rain from without fall upon it . I have to work very cautiously for two hour after that , and then creep through to the parapet and silently fling my rope over the gun ; not without a little tremor of heart , lest the sentry should see I and send a charge of lead into my body . 
 " the wall be but twelve foot , and my fall into the ditch easy enough . I wait a while there , look steadily under the gun , and try to see the river and the island . I hear the sentry pace up above and hum a tune . the darkness become more clear to I ere long , and the moon rise , and I see the river shin before I , and the dark rock and tree of the island rise in the water . 
 " I make for this mark as swiftly as I could , and for the clump of tree to which I have be direct . oh , what a relief I have when I hear a low voice hum there , ' over the hill and far away ' ! " 
 when Mr. George come to this part of his narrative , Miss Theo , who be seat by a harpsichord , turn round and dash off the tune on the instrument , whilst all the little company break out into the merry chorus . 
 " our way , " the speaker go on , " lie through a level tract of forest with which my guide be familiar , upon the right bank of the Monongahela . by daylight we come to a clear country , and my trapper ask I Silverheels be the name by which he go have I ever see the spot before ? it be the fatal field where Braddock have fall , and whence I have be wonderfully rescue in the summer of the previous year . now , the leaf be begin to be tint with the magnificent hue of our autumn . " 
 " ah , brother ! " cry Harry , seize his brother 's hand . " I be gamble and make a fool of myself at the Wells and in London , when my George be fly for his life in the wilderness ! oh , what a miserable spendthrift I have be ! " 
 " but I think thou art not unworthy to be call thy mother 's son , " say Mrs. Lambert , very softly , and with moistened eye . indeed , if Harry have err , to mark his repentance , his love , his unselfish joy and generosity , be to feel that there be hope for the humbled and kind young sinner . 
 " we presently cross the river " George resume , " take our course along the base of the western slope of the Alleghanies ; and through a grand forest region of oak and maple , and enormous poplar that grow a hundred foot high without a branch . it be the Indians whom we have to avoid , besides the outlying party of French . always of doubtful loyalty , the savage have be specially against we , since our ill - treatment of they , and the french triumph over we two year ago . 
 " I be but weak still , and our journey through the wilderness last a fortnight or more . as we advance , the wood become red and redder . the frost nip sharply of night . we light fire at our foot , and sleep in our blanket as good we might . at this time of year the hunter who live in the mountain get their sugar from the maple . we come upon more than one such family , camp near their tree by the mountain stream ; and they welcome we at their fire , and give we of their venison . so we pass over the two range of the Laurel Hills and the Alleghanies . the last day 's march of my trusty guide and myself take we down that wild , magnificent pass of Will 's Creek , a valley lie between cliff near a thousand foot high bald , white , and break into tower like huge fortification , with eagle wheel round the summit of the rock , and watch their nest among the crag . 
 " and hence we descend to Cumberland , whence we have march in the year before , and where there be now a considerable garrison of our people . oh ! you may think it be a welcome day when I see English colour again on the bank of our native Potomac ! " 




 CHAPTER LIII . where we remain at the Court end of the Town 

 George Warrington have relate the same story , which we have just hear , to Madame de Bernstein on the previous evening a portion , that be , of the history ; for the old lady nod off to sleep many time during the narration , only wake up when George pause , say it be most interesting , and order he to continue . the young gentleman hem'd and ha'd , and stutter , and blush , and go on , much against his will , and do not speak half so well as he do to his friendly little auditory in Hill Street , where Hetty 's eye of wonder and Theo 's sympathise look , and mamma 's kind face , and papa 's funny look , be applause sufficient to cheer any modest youth who require encouragement for his eloquence . as for mamma 's behaviour , the General say , ' twa as good as Mr. Addison 's trunk - maker , and she would make the fortune of any tragedy by simply be engage to cry in the front box . that be why we choose my Lord Wrotham 's house as the theatre where George 's first piece should be perform , wish that he should speak to advantage , and not as when he be hear by that sleepy , cynical old lady , to whom he have to narrate his adventure . 
 " very good and most interesting , I be sure , my dear sir , " say Madame Bernstein , put up three pretty little finger cover with a lace mitten , to hide a convulsive movement of her mouth . " and your mother must have be delighted to see you . " 
 George shrug his shoulder ever so little , and make a low bow , as his aunt look up at he for a moment with her keen old eye . 
 " have be delighted to see you " she continue drily , " and kill the fat calf , and and that kind of thing . though why I say calf , I do not   know , nephew George , for you never be the prodigal . I may say calf to thee , my poor Harry ! Thou hast be amongst the swine sure enough . and evil companion have rob the money out of thy pocket and the coat off thy back . 
 " he come to his family in England , madam , " say George , with some heat , " and his friend be your ladyship 's . " 
 " he could not have come to bad adviser , nephew Warrington , and so I should have tell my sister early , have she condescend to write to I by he , as she have do by you , " say the old lady , toss up her head . " hey ! hey ! " she say , at night , as she arrange herself for the rout to which she be go , to her waiting - maid : " this young gentleman 's mother be half sorry that he have come to life again , I could see that in his face . she be half sorry , and I be perfectly furious ! why do not   he lie still when he drop there under the tree , and why do that young Florac carry he to the fort ? I know those florac when I be at Paris , in the time of Monsieur le Regent . they be of the Floracs of Ivry . no great house before Henri IV . his ancestor be the king 's favourite . his ancestor he ! he ! his ancestress ! Brett ! entendez - vous ? give I my card - purse . I do not   like the grand air of this Monsieur George ; and yet he resemble , very much , his grandfather the same look and sometimes the same tone . you have hear of Colonel Esmond when I be young ? this boy have his eye . I suppose I like the Colonel 's because he love I . " 
 be engage , then , to a card - party , an amusement which she never miss , week - day or Sabbath , as long as she have strength to hold trump or sit in a chair , very soon after George have end his narration the old lady dismiss her two nephew , give to the eld a couple of finger and a very stately curtsey ; but to Harry two hand and a kindly pat on the cheek . 
 " my poor child , now thou art disinherit , thou wilt see how differently the world will use thee ! " she say . " there be only , in all London , a wicked , heartless old woman who will treat thee as before . here be a pocket - book for you , child ! do not lose it at Ranelagh to - night . that suit of yours do not become your brother half so well as it sit upon you ! you will present your brother to everybody , and walk up and down the room for two hour at least , child . be I you , I would then go to the Chocolate - House , and play as if nothing have happen . whilst you be there , your brother may come back to I and eat a bit of chicken with I . my Lady Flint give wretched supper , and I want to talk his mother 's letter over with he . Au revoir , gentleman ! " and she go away to she toilette . her chairman and flambeaux be already wait at the door . 
 the gentleman go to Ranelagh , where but a few of Mr. Harry 's acquaintance chance to be present . they pace the round , and meet Mr. Tom Claypool with some of his country friend ; they hear the music ; they drink tea in a box ; Harry be master of ceremony , and introduce his brother to the curiosity of the place ; and George be even more excited than his brother have be on his first introduction to this palace of delight . George love music much more than Harry ever do ; he hear a full orchestra for the first time , and a piece of Mr. Handel satisfactorily perform ; and a not unpleasing instance of Harry 's humility and regard for his eld brother be , that he could even hold George 's love of music in respect at a time when fiddle be vote effeminate and unmanly in England , and Britons be , every day , call upon by the patriotic print to sneer at the frivolous accomplishment of your Squallinis , Monsieurs , and the like . nobody in Britain be proud of his ignorance now . there be no conceit leave among we . there be no such thing as dulness . Arrogance be entirely unknown ... well , at any rate , art have obtain her letter of naturalisation , and live here on term of almost equality . if Mrs. Thrale choose to marry a music - master now , I do not   think her friend would shudder at the mention of her name . if she have a good fortune and keep a good cook , people would even go and dine with she in spite of the misalliance , and actually treat Mr. Piozzi with civility . 
 after Ranelagh , and pursuant to Madam Bernstein 's advice , George return to her ladyship 's house , whilst Harry show himself at the club , where gentleman be accustom to assemble at night to sup , and then to gamble . no one , of course , allude to Mr. Warrington 's little temporary absence , and Mr. Ruff , his ex - landlord , wait upon he with the utmost gravity and civility , and as if there have never be any difference between they . Mr. Warrington have cause his trunk and habiliment to be convey away from Bond Street in the morning , and he and his brother be now establish in apartment elsewhere . 
 but when the supper be do , and the gentleman , as usual , be about to seek the macco - table upstairs , Harry say he be not go to play any more . he have burn his finger already , and could afford no more extravagance . 
 " why , " say Mr. Morris , in a rather flippant manner , " you must have win more than you have lose , Mr. Warrington , after all be say and do . " 
 " and of course I do not   know my own business as well as you do , Mr. Morris , " say Harry sternly , who have not forget the other 's behaviour on hearing of his arrest ; " but I have another reason . a few month or day ago , I be heir to a great estate , and could afford to lose a little money . now , thank God , I be heir to nothing . " and he look round , blush not a little , to the knot of gentleman , his gaming associate , who be lounge at the table or gather round the fire . 
 " how do you mean , Mr. Warrington ? " cry my Lord March , " have you lose Virginia , too ? who have win it ? I always have a fancy to play you myself for that stake . " 
 " and grow an improved breed of slave in the colony , " say another . 
 " the right owner have win it . you have hear I tell of my twin eld brother ? " 
 " who be kill in that affair of Braddock 's two year ago ! yes . gracious goodness , my dear sir , I hope in heaven he have not come to life again ? " 
 " he arrive in London two day since . he have be a prisoner in a french fort for eighteen month ; he only escape a few month ago , and leave our house in Virginia very soon after his release . " 
 " you have not   have time to order mourning , I suppose , Mr. Warrington ? " ask Mr. Selwyn very good - naturedly , and simple Harry hardly know the meaning of his joke until his brother interpret it to he . 
 " hang I , if I do not   believe the fellow be absolutely glad of the reappearance of his confounded brother ! " cry my Lord March , as they continue to talk of the matter when the young Virginian have take his leave . 
 " these savage practise the simple virtue of affection they be barely civilised in America yet , " yawn Selwyn . 
 " they love their kindre , and they scalp their enemy , " simper Mr. Walpole . " it be not Christian , but natural . should not   you like to be present at a scalping - match , George , and see a fellow skin alive ? " 
 " a man 's eld brother be his natural enemy , " say Mr. Selwyn , placidly range his money and counter before he . 
 " torture be like broiled bone and pepper . you would not   relish simple hang afterwards , George ! " continue Horry . 
 " I be hang if there be any man in England who would like to see his eld brother alive , " say my lord . 
 " no , nor his father either , my lord ! " cry Jack Morris . 
 " first time I ever know you have one , Jack . give I counter for five hundred . " 
 " I say , ' tis all mighty fine about dead brother come to life again , " continue Jack . " who be to know that it be not   a scheme arrange between these two fellow ? here come a young fellow who call himself the Fortunate Youth , who say he be a Virginian Prince and the deuce know what , and who get into our society " 
 a great laugh ensue at Jack 's phrase of " our society . " 
 " who be to know that it be not   a cross ? " Jack continue . " the young one be to come first . he be to marry an heiress , and , when he have get she , up be to rise the eld brother ! when do this eld brother show ? why , when the young 's scheme be blow , and all be up with he ! who shall tell I that the fellow have not   be live in Seven dial , or in a cellar dining off tripe and cow - heel until my young gentleman be dispose of ? Dammy , as gentleman , I think we ought to take notice of it : and that this Mr. Warrington have be take a most outrageous liberty with the whole club . " 
 " who put he up ? it be March , I think , put he up ? " ask a bystander . 
 " yes . but my lord think he be put up a very different person . do not   you , March ? " 
 " hold your confound tongue , and mind your game ! " say the nobleman address : but Jack Morris 's opinion find not a few supporter in the world . many person agree that it be most indecorous of Mr. Harry Warrington to have ever believe in his brother 's death : that there be something suspicious about the young man 's first appearance and subsequent action , and , in fine , that regard these foreigner , adventurer , and the like , we ought to be especially cautious . 
 though he be out of prison and difficulty ; though he have his aunt 's liberal donation of money in his pocket ; though his dear brother be restore to he , whose return to life Harry never once think of deploring , as his friend at White 's suppose he would do ; though Maria have show herself in such a favourable light by her behaviour during his misfortune : yet Harry , when alone , feel himself not particularly cheerful , and smoke his pipe of Virginia with a troubled mind . it be not that he be depose from his principality ; the loss of it never once vex he ; he know that his brother would share with he as he would have do with his brother ; but after all those struggle and doubt in his own mind , to find himself poor , and yet irrevocably bind to his elderly cousin ! yes , she be elderly , there be no doubt about it . when she come to that horrible den in Cursitor Street and the tear wash her rouge off , why , she look as old as his mother ! her face be all wrinkle and yellow , and as he think of she he feel just such a qualm as he have when she be take ill that day in the coach on their road to Tunbridge . what would his mother say when he bring her home , and , Lord , what battle there would be between they ! he would go and live on one of the plantation the far from home the well and have a few negro , and farm as good he might , and hunt a good deal ; but at Castlewood or in her own home , such as he could make it for she , what a life for poor Maria , who have be use to go to court and to card and ball and assembly every night ! if he could be but the overseer of the estate oh , he would be an honest factor , and try and make up for his useless life and extravagance in these past day ! five thousand pound , all his patrimony and the accumulation of his long minority squander in six month ! he a beggar , except for dear George 's kindness , with nothing in life leave to he but an old wife , a pretty beggar , dress out in velvet and silver lace forsooth the poor lad be array in his good clothe a pretty figure he have make in Europe , and a nice end he be come to ! with all his fine friend at White 's and Newmarket , with all his extravagance , have he be happy a single day since he have be in Europe ? yes , three day , four day , yesterday evening , when he have be with dear dear Mrs. Lambert , and those affectionate kind girl , and that brave good Colonel . and the Colonel be right when he rebuke he for his spendthrift folly , and he have be a brute to be angry as he have be , and God bless they all for their generous exertion in his behalf ! such be the thought which Harry put into his pipe , and he smoke they whilst he wait his brother 's return from Madame Bernstein . 




 CHAPTER LIV . during which Harry sit smoke his Pipe at home
