 the maternal grandfather of our Virginians , the Colonel Esmond of whom frequent mention have be make , and who have quit England to reside in the New World , have devote some portion of his long american leisure to the composition of the memoir of his early life . in these volume , Madame de Bernstein   Mrs. Beatrice Esmond be her name as a spinster   play a very considerable part ; and as George have read his grandfather 's manuscript many time over , he have learn to know his kinswoman long before he see she , to know , at least , the lady , young , beautiful , and wilful , of half a century since , with whom he now become acquaint in the decline of her day . when cheek be fade and eye be dim , be it sad or pleasant , I wonder , for the woman who be a beauty no more , to recall the period of her bloom ! when the heart be wither , do the old love to remember how it once be fresh and beat with warm emotion ? when the spirit be languid and weary , do we like to think how bright they be in other day , the hope how buoyant , the sympathy how ready , the enjoyment of life how keen and eager ? so they fall the bud of prime , the rose of beauty , the florid harvest of summer , fall and wither , and the naked branch shiver in the winter . 
 " and that be a beauty once ! " think George Warrington , as his aunt , in her rouge and diamond , come in from her rout , " and that ruin be a splendid palace . crowd of lover have sigh before those decrepit foot , and be bewilder by the brightness of those eye . " he remember a firework at home , at Williamsburg , on the King 's birthday , and afterwards look at the skeleton - wheel and the socket of the exploded roman candle . the dazzle and brilliancy of Aunt Beatrice 's early career pass before he , as he think over his grandsire 's journal . Honest Harry have see they , too , but Harry be no bookman , and have not read the manuscript very carefully : nay , if he have , he would probably not have reason about it as his brother do , be by no mean so much inclined to moralise as his melancholy senior . 
 Mr. Warrington think that there be no cause why he should tell his aunt how intimate he be with her early history , and accordingly hold his peace upon that point . when their meal be over , she point with her cane to her escritoire , and bade her attendant bring the letter which lie under the inkstand there ; and George , recognise the superscription , of course know the letter to be that of which he have be the bearer from home . 
 " it would appear by this letter , " say the old lady , look hard at her nephew , " that ever since your return , there have be some difference between you and my sister . " 
 " indeed ? I do not know that Madam Esmond have allude to they , " George say . 
 the Baroness put a great pair of glass upon eye which shoot fire and kindle who know how many passion in old day , and , after glance over the letter , hand it to George , who read as follow : 
 " RICHMOND , VIRGINIA , December 26th , 1756 . 
 " HONOURED MADAM ! and SISTER ! I have receive , and thankfully acknowledge , your ladyship 's favour , per Rose packet , of October 23 ult . ; and straightway answer you at a season which should be one of goodwill and peace to all man : but in which Heaven hath nevertheless decree we should still bear our portion of earthly sorrow and trouble . my reply will be bring to you by my eld son , Mr. Esmond Warrington , who return to we so miraculously out of the Valley of the Shadow of Death   as our previous letter have inform my poor Henry , and who be desirous , not without my consent to his wish , to visit Europe , though he have be amongst we so short a while . I grieve to think that my dear Harry should have appear at home I mean in England under false colour , as it be ; and should have be present to his Majesty , to our family , and his own , as his father 's heir , whilst my dear son George be still alive , though dead to we . ah , madam ! during the eighteen month of his captivity , what anguish have his mother 's , his brother 's , heart undergone ! my Harry 's be the tender of any man be now alive . in the joy of see Mr. Esmond Warrington return to life , he will forget the worldly misfortune which befall he . he will return to   comparative   poverty without a pang . the most generous , the most obedient of human being , of son , he will gladly give up to his eld brother that inheritance which have be his own but for the accident of birth , and for the providential return of my son George . 
 " your beneficent intention towards dear Harry will be more than ever welcome , now he be reduce to a young brother 's slender portion ! many year since , an advantageous opportunity occur of provide for he in this province , and he would by this time have be master of a noble estate and negro , and have be enable to make a figure with most here , could his mother 's wish have be comply with , and his father 's small portion , now lie at small interest in the british fund , have be invest in this most excellent purchase . but the form of the law , and , I grieve to own , my eld son 's scruple , prevail , and this admirable opportunity be lose to I ! Harry will find the saving of his income have be carefully accumulate long , long may he live to enjoy they ! may Heaven bless you , dear sister , for what your ladyship may add to his little store ! as I gather from your letter , that the sum which have be allow to he have not be sufficient for his expense in the fine company which he have keep   and the grandson of the Marquis of Esmond one who have so nearly be his lordship 's heir may sure claim equality with any other nobleman in Great Britain , and have a sum by I which I have always intend for the poor child 's establishment , I entrust it to my eld son , who , to do he justice , hath a most sincere regard for his brother , to lay it out for Harry 's good advantage . " 
 " it take he out of prison yesterday , madam . I think that be the good use to which we could put it , " interpose George , at this stage of his mother 's letter . 
 " Nay , sir , I do not   know any such thing ! why not have keep it to buy a pair of colour for he , or to help towards another estate and some negro , if he have a fancy for home ? " cry the old lady . " besides , I have a fancy to pay that debt myself . " 
 " I hope you will let his brother do that . I ask leave to be my brother 's banker in this matter , and consider I have borrow so much from my mother , to be pay back to my dear Harry . " 
 " do you say so , sir ? give I a glass of wine ! you be an extravagant fellow ! read on , and you will see your mother think so . I drink to your health , nephew George ! ' tis good Burgundy . your grandfather never love Burgundy . he love claret , the little he drank . " 
 and George proceed with the letter : 
 " this remittance will , I trust , amply cover any expense which , owe to the mistake respect his position , dear Harry may have incur . I wish I could trust his eld brother 's prudence as confidently as my Harry 's ! but I fear that , even in his captivity , Mr. Esmond W. have learn little of that humility which become all Christians , and which I have ever endeavour to teach to my child . should you by chance show he these line , when , by the blessing of Heaven on those who go down to the sea in ship , the Great Ocean divide we ! he will know that a fond mother 's blessing and prayer follow both her child , and that there be no act I have ever do , no desire I have ever express   however little he may have be incline to obey it !   but hath be dictate by the fond wish for my dear boy ' welfare . " 
 " there be a scratch with a penknife , and a great blot upon the letter there , as if water have fall on it . your mother write well , George . I suppose you and she have a difference ? " say George 's aunt , not unkindly . 
 " yes , ma'am , many , " answer the young man , sadly . " the last be about a question of money of ransom which I promise to the old lieutenant of the fort who aid I to make my escape . I tell you he have a mistress , a poor indian woman , who help I , and be kind to I . six week after my arrival at home , the poor thing make her appearance at Richmond , have find her way through the wood by pretty much the same track which I have follow , and bring I the token which Museau have promise to send I when he connive to my flight . a command officer and a considerable reinforcement have arrive at Duquesne . charge , I do not   know of what peculation   for his messenger could not express herself very clearly , have be bring against this Museau . he have be put under arrest , and have try to escape ; but , less fortunate than myself , he have be shoot on the rampart , and he send the indian woman to I , with my grandfather 's watch , and a line scrawl in his prison on his deathbed , beg I to send ce que je scavais to a notary at Havre de Grace in France to be transmit to his relative at Caen in Normandy . my friend silverheel , the hunter , have help my poor indian on her way . I do not   know how she would have escape scalping else . but at home they receive the poor thing sternly . they hardly give she a welcome . I will not   say what suspicion they have regard she and I . the poor wretch fall to drink whenever she could find mean . I order that she should have food and shelter , and she become the jest of our negro , and form the subject of the scandal and tittle - tattle of the old fool in our little town . our Governor be , luckily , a man of sense , and I make interest with he , and procure a pass to send she back to her people . her very grief at part with I only serve to confirm the suspicion against she . a fellow preach against I from the pulpit , I believe ; I have to treat another with a cane . and I have a violent dispute with Madam Esmond a difference which be not heal yet because I insist upon pay to the heir Museau point out the money I have promise for my deliverance . you see that scandal flourish at the border of the wilderness , and in the New World as well as the Old . " 
 " I have suffer from it myself , my dear ! " say Madame Bernstein , demurely . " fill thy glass , child ! a little tass of cherry - brandy ! ' twill do thee all the good in the world . " 
 " as for my poor Harry 's marriage , " Madam Esmond 's letter go on , " though I know too well , from sad experience , the danger to which youth be subject , and would keep my boy , at any price , from they , though I should wish he to marry a person of rank , as become his birth , yet my Lady Maria Esmond be out of the question . her age be almost the same as mine ; and I know my brother Castlewood leave his daughter with the very small portion . my Harry be so obedient that I know a desire from I will be sufficient to cause he to give up this imprudent match . some foolish people once suppose that I myself once think of a second union , and with a person of rank very different from ours . no ! I know what be due to my child . as succeed to this estate after I , Mr. Esmond W. be amply provide for . let my task now be to save for his less fortunate young brother : and , as I do not love to live quite alone , let he return without delay to his fond and love mother . 
 " the report which your ladyship hath give of my Harry fill my heart with warm gratitude . he be all indeed a mother may wish . a year in Europe will have give he a polish and refinement which he could not acquire in our homely Virginia . Mr. Stack , one of our invaluable minister in Richmond , hath a letter from Mr. Ward my darling ' tutor of early day who know my Lady Warrington and her excellent family , and saith that my Harry have live much with his cousin of late . I be grateful to think that my boy have the privilege of be with his good aunt . may he follow her counsel , and listen to those around he who will guide he on the way of his good welfare ! Adieu , dear madam and sister ! for your kindness to my boy accept the grateful thank of a mother 's heart . though we have be divide hitherto , may these kindly tie draw we near and nearer . I be thankful that you should speak of my dear father so . he be , indeed , one of the good of man ! he , too , thank you , I know , for the love you have bear to one of his child ; and his daughter subscribe herself , with sincere thank , your ladyship 's most dutiful and grateful sister and servant , RACHEL ESMOND WN . 
 " P.S. I have communicate with my Lady Maria ; but there will no need to tell she and dear Harry that his mother or your ladyship hope to be able to increase his small fortune . the match be altogether unsuitable . " 
 " as far as regard myself , madam , " George say , lay down the paper , " my mother 's letter convey no news to I . I always know that Harry be the favourite son with Madam Esmond , as he deserve indeed to be . he have a hundred good quality which I have not the good fortune to possess . he have well look " 
 " Nay , that be not your fault , " say the old lady , slily look at he ; " and , but that he be fair and you be brown , one might almost pass for the other . " 
 Mr. George bow , and a faint blush ting his pale cheek . 
 " his disposition be bright , and mine be dark , " he continue ; " Harry be cheerful , and I be otherwise , perhaps . he know how to make himself belove by every one , and it have be my lot to find but few friend . " 
 " my sister and you have pretty little quarrel . there be such in old day in our family , " the Baroness say ; " and if Madam Esmond take after our mother " 
 " my mother have always describe her as an angel upon earth , " interpose George . 
 " eh ! that be a common character for people when they be dead ! " cry the Baroness ; " and Rachel Castlewood be an angel , if you like at least your grandfather think so . but let I tell you , sir , that angel be sometimes not very commode a vivre . it may be they be too good to live with us sinner , and the air down below here do not   agree with they . my poor mother be so perfect that she never could forgive I for be otherwise . ah , mon Dieu ! how she use to oppress I with those angelical air ! " 
 George cast down his eye , and think of his own melancholy youth . he do not care to submit more of his family secret to the cynical inquisition of this old worldling , who seem , however , to understand he in spite of his reticence . 
 " I quite comprehend you , sir , though you hold your tongue , " the Baroness continue . " a sermon in the morning : a sermon at night : and two or three of a Sunday . that be what people call be good . every pleasure cry fie upon ; all we worldly people excommunicate ; a ball an abomination of desolation ; a play a forbid pastime ; and a game of card perdition ! what a life ! Mon Dieu , what a life ! " 
 " we play at card every night , if we be so inclined , " say George , smile ; " and my grandfather love Shakspeare so much , that my mother have not a word to say against her father 's favourite author . " 
 " I remember . he could say whole page by heart ; though , for my part , I like Mr. Congreve a great deal well . and then , there be that dreadful , dreary Milton , whom he and Mr. Addison pretend to admire ! " cry the old lady , tap her fan . 
 " if your ladyship do not like Shakspeare , you will not quarrel with my mother for be indifferent to he , too , " say George . " and indeed I think , and I be sure , that you do not   do her justice . wherever there be any poor she relieve they ; wherever there be any sick she " 
 " she dose they with her horrible purge and bolus ! " cry the Baroness . " of course , just as my mother do ! " 
 " she do she good to cure they ! she act for the good , and perform her duty as far as she know it . " 
 " I do not   blame you , sir , for do your , and keep your own counsel about Madam Esmond , " say the old lady . " but at least there be one point upon which we all three agree that this absurd marriage must be prevent . do you know how old the woman be ? I can tell you , though she have tear the first leaf out of the family Bible at Castlewood . " 
 " my mother have not forget her cousin 's age , and be shock at the disparity between she and my poor brother . indeed , a city - breed lady of her time of life , accustom to London gaiety and luxury , would find but a dismal home in our virginian plantation . besides , the house , such as it be , be not Harry 's . he be welcome there , Heaven know ; more welcome , perhaps , than I , to whom the property come in natural reversion ; but , as I tell he , I doubt how his wife would would like our colony , " George say , with a blush , and a hesitation in his sentence . 
 the old lady laugh shrilly . " he , he ! nephew Warrington ! " she say , " you need not scruple to speak your mind out . I shall tell no tale to your mother : though ' tis no news to I that she have a high temper , and love her own way . Harry have hold his tongue , too ; but it need no conjurer to see who be the mistress at home , and what sort of a life my sister lead you . I love my niece , my Lady Molly , so well , that I could wish her two or three year of Virginia , with your mother reign over she . you may well look alarmed , sir ! Harry have say quite enough to show I who govern the family . " 
 " Madam , " say George , smile , " I may say as much as this , that I do not   envy any woman come into our house against my mother 's will : and my poor brother know this perfectly well . " 
 " what ? you two have talk the matter over ? no doubt you have . and the foolish child consider himself bind in honour of course he do , the gaby ! " 
 " he say Lady Maria have behave most nobly to he . when he be send to prison , she bring he her trinket and jewel , and every guinea she have in the world . this behaviour have touch he so , that he feel more deeply than ever bind to her ladyship . but I own my brother seem bind by honour rather than love such at least be his present feeling . " 
 " my good creature , " cry Madame Bernstein , " do not   you see that Maria bring a few twopenny trinket and a half - dozen guineas to Mr. Esmond , the heir of the great estate in Virginia , not to the second son , who be a beggar , and have just squander away every shilling of his fortune ? I swear to you , on my credit as a gentlewoman , that , know Harry 's obstinacy , and the misery he have in store for himself , I try to bribe Maria to give up her engagement with he , and only fail because I could not bribe high enough ! when he be in prison , I send my lawyer to he , with order to pay his debt immediately , if he would but part from she , but Maria have be beforehand with we , and Mr. Harry choose not to go back from his stupid word . let I tell you what have pass in the last month ! " and here the old lady narrate at length the history which we know already , but in that cynical language which be common in her time , when the fine folk and the most delicate lady call thing and people by name which we never utter in good company nowadays . and so much the well on the whole . we may not   be more virtuous , but it be something to be more decent : perhaps we be not more pure , but of a surety we be more cleanly . 
 Madame Bernstein talk so much , so long , and so cleverly , that she be quite pleased with herself and her listener ; and when she put herself into the hand of Mrs. Brett to retire for the night , inform the waiting - maid that she have change her opinion about her eld nephew , and that Mr. George be handsome , that he be certainly much witty than poor Harry   whom Heaven , it must be confess , have not furnish with a very great supply of brain , and that he have quite the bel air a something melancholy a noble and distinguished je ne scais quoy which remind she of the Colonel . have she ever tell Brett about the Colonel ? score of time , no doubt . and now she tell Brett about the Colonel once more . meanwhile , perhaps , her new favourite be not quite so well pleased with she as she be with he . what a strange picture of life and manner have the old lady unveil to her nephew ! how she rail at all the world round about she ! how unconsciously do she paint her own family her own self ; how selfish , one and all ; pursue what mean end ; grasp and scramble frantically for what petty prize ; ambitious for what shabby recompense ; trample from life 's beginning to its close through what scene of stale dissipation and fade pleasure ! " be these the inheritor of noble blood ? " think George , as he go home quite late from his aunt 's house , pass by door whence the last guest of fashion be issue , and where the chairman be yawn over their expire torch . " be these the proud possessor of ancestral honour and ancient name , and be their forefather , when in life , no well ? we have our pedigree at home with noble coat - of - arm emblazon all over the branch , and title date back before the Conquest and the Crusaders . when a knight of old find a friend in want , do he turn his back upon he , or an unprotected damsel , do he delude she and leave she ? when a nobleman of the early time receive a young kinsman , do he get the well of he at dice , and do the ancient chivalry cheat in horseflesh ? can it be that this wily woman of the world , as my aunt have represent , have inveigle my poor Harry into an engagement , that her tear be false , and that as soon as she find he poor she will desert he ? have we not well pack the trunk and take a cabin in the next ship bind for home ? " George reach his own door revolve these thought , and Gumbo come up yawn with a candle , and Harry be asleep before the extinguished fire , with the ash of his emptied pipe on the table beside he . 
 he start up ; his eye , for a moment dull by sleep , lighten with pleasure as he see his dear George . he put his arm round his brother with a boyish laugh . 
 " there he be in flesh and blood , thank God ! " he say ; " I be dream of thee but now , George , and that Ward be hear we our lesson ! dost thou remember the ruler , georgy ? why , bless my soul , ' tis three o'clock ! where have you be a - gad , Mr. George ? Hast thou sup ? I sup at White 's , but I be hungry again . I do not play , sir , no , no ; no more of that for young brother ! and my Lord March pay I fifty he lose to I . I bet against his horse and on the Duke of Hamilton 's ! they both ride the match at Newmarket this morning , and he lose because he be under weight . and he pay I , and he be as sulky as a bear . let we have one pipe , Georgy ! just one . " 
 and after the smoke the young man go to bed , where I , for one , wish they a pleasant rest , for sure it be a good and pleasant thing to see brother who love one another . 




 CHAPTER LV . between brother 

 of course our young man have have their private talk about home , and all the people and doing there , and each have impart to the other full particular of his history since their last meeting . how be Harry 's dog , and little Dempster , and good old Nathan , and the rest of the household ? be Mountain well , and Fanny grow to be a pretty girl ? so Parson Broadbent 's daughter be engage to marry Tom Barker of Savannah , and they be to go and live in Georgia ! Harry own that at one period he be very sweet upon Parson Broadbent 's daughter , and lose a great deal of pocket - money at card , and drink a great quantity of strong - water with the father , in order to have a pretext for be near the girl . but , Heaven help we ! Madam Esmond would never have consent to his throw himself away upon Polly Broadbent . so Colonel G. Washington 's wife be a pretty woman , very good - natured and pleasant , and with a good fortune ? he have bring she into Richmond , and pay a visit of state to Madam Esmond . George describe , with much humour , the awful ceremonial at the interview between these two personage , and the kill politeness of his mother to Mr. Washington 's young wife . " never mind , George , my dear ! " say Mrs. Mountain . " the Colonel have take another wife , but I feel certain that at one time two young gentleman I know of run a very near chance of have a tall stepfather six foot two in his boot . " to be sure , Mountain be for ever match - making in her mind . two people could not play a game at card together , or sit down to a dish of tea , but she fancy their conjunction be for life . it be she the foolish tattler who have set the report abroad regard the poor indian woman . as for Madam Esmond , she have repel the insinuation with scorn when Parson Stack bring it to she , and say , " I should as soon fancy Mr. Esmond steal the spoon , or marry a negro woman out of the kitchen . " but , though she disdain to find the poor Biche guilty , and even thank she for attend her son in his illness , she treat she with such a chilling haughtiness of demeanour , that the indian slunk away into the servant ' quarter , and there try to drown her disappointment with drink . it be not a cheerful picture that which George give of his two month at home . " the birthright be mine , Harry , " he say , " but thou art the favourite , and God help I ! I think my mother almost grudge it to I . why should I have take the pas , and precede your worship into the world ? have you be the eider , you would have have the good cellar , and ride the good nag , and be the most popular man in the country , whereas I have not a word to say for myself , and frighten people by my glum face : I should have be second son , and set up as lawyer , or come to England and get my degree , and turn parson , and say grace at your honour 's table . the time be out of joint , sir . o curse spite , that ever I be bear to set it right ! " 
 " why , georgy , you be talk verse , I protest you be ! " say Harry . 
 " I think , my dear , some one else talk those verse before I , " say George , with a smile . 
 " it be out of one of your book . you know every book that ever be write , that I do believe ! " cry Harry , and then tell his brother how he have see the two author at Tunbridge , and how he have take off his hat to they . " not that I care much about their book , not be clever enough . but I remember how my dear old George use to speak of them , " say Harry , with a choke in his voice , " and that be why I like to see they . I say , dear , it be like a dream see you over again . think of that bloody Indian with his knife at my George 's head ! I should like to give that Monsieur de Florac something for save you but I have not   get much now , only my little gold knee - buckle , and they be not   worth two guineas . " 
 " you have get the half of what I have , child , and we will divide as soon as I have pay the Frenchman , " George say . 
 on which Harry break out not merely into blessing but actual imprecation , indicate his intense love and satisfaction ; and he swear that there never be such a brother in the world as his brother George . indeed , for some day after his brother 's arrival his eye follow George about : he would lay down his knife and fork , or his newspaper , when they be sit together , and begin to laugh to himself . when he walk with George on the Mall or in Hyde Park , he would gaze round at the company , as much as to say , " look here , gentleman ! this be he . this be my brother , that be dead and be alive again ! can any man in Christendom produce such a brother as this ? " 
 of course he be of opinion that George should pay to Museau 's heir the sum which he have promise for his ransom . this question have be the cause of no small unhappiness to poor George at home . Museau dead , Madam Esmond argue with much eagerness , and not a little rancour , the bargain fall to the ground , and her son be free . the man be a rogue in the first instance . she would not pay the wage of iniquity . Mr. Esmond have a small independence from his father , and might squander his patrimony if he choose . he be of age , and the money be in his power ; but she would be no party to such extravagance , as give twelve thousand livre to a parcel of peasant in Normandy with whom we be at war , and who would very likely give it all to the priest and the pope . she would not subscribe to any such wickedness . if George want to squander away his father 's money   she must say that formerly he have not be so eager , and when Harry 's benefit be in question have refuse to touch a penny of it !   if he wish to spend it now , why not give it to his own flesh and blood , to poor Harry , who be suddenly deprive of his inheritance , and not to a set of priest - ride peasant in France ? this dispute have rag between mother and son during the whole of the latter 's last day in Virginia . it have never be settle . on the morning of George 's departure , Madam Esmond have come to his bedside after a sleepless night , and ask he whether he still persist in his intention to fle away his father 's property ? 
 he reply in a depth of grief and perplexity , that his word be pass , and he must do as his honour bid he . she answer that she would continue to pray that Heaven might soften his proud heart , and enable she to bear her heavy trial : and the last view George have of his mother 's face be as she stand yet a moment by his bedside , pale and with tearless eye , before she turn away and slowly leave his chamber . 
 " where didst thou learn the art of win over everybody to thy side , Harry ? " continue George ; " and how be it that you and all the world begin by be friend ? teach I a few lesson in popularity , nay , I do not   know that I will have they ; and when I find and hear certain people hate I , I think I be rather pleased than angry . at first , at Richmond , Mr. Esmond Warrington , the only prisoner who have escape from Braddock 's field the victim of so much illness and hardship be a favourite with the town - folk , and receive privately and publicly with no little kindness . the parson glorify my escape in a sermon ; the neighbour come to visit the fugitive ; the family coach be order out , and Madam Esmond and I pay our visit in return . I think some pretty little cap be set at I . but these our mother rout off , and frighten with the prodigious haughtiness of her demeanour ; and my popularity be already at the decrease before the event occur which put the last finishing stroke to it . I be not jolly enough for the officer , and do not   care for their drinking - bout , dice - box , and swearing . I be too sarcastic for the lady , and their tea and tattle stupefy I almost as much as the man 's blustering and horse - talk . I can not tell thee , Harry , how lonely I feel in that place , amidst the scandal and squabble : I regret my prison almost , and find myself more than once wish for the freedom of thought , and the silent ease of Duquesne . I be very shy , I suppose : I can speak unreservedly to very few people . before most , I sit utterly silent . when we two be at home , it be thou who use to talk at table , and get a smile now and then from our mother . when she and I be together we have no subject in common , and we scarce speak at all until we begin to dispute about law and divinity . 
 " so the gentleman have determine I be supercilious , and a dull companion   and , indeed , I think their opinion be right , and the lady think I be cold and sarcastic , could never make out whether I be in earnest or no , and , I think , generally vote I be a disagreeable fellow , before my character be go quite away ; and that go with the appearance of the poor Biche . oh , a nice character they make for I , my dear ! " cry George , in a transport of wrath , " and a pretty life they lead I after Museau 's unlucky messenger have appear amongst we ! the boy hoot the poor woman if she appear in the street ; the lady drop I half - curtsey , and walk over to the other side . that precious clergyman go from one tea - table to another preaching on the horror of seduction , and the lax principle which young man learn in popish country and bring back thence . the poor fawn 's appearance at home a few week after my return home , be declare to be a scheme between she and I ; and the well inform agree that she have wait on the other side of the river until I give she the signal to come and join I in Richmond . the officer banter I at the coffee - house , and crack their clumsy joke about the woman I have select . oh , the world be a nice charitable world ! I be so enraged that I think of go to Castlewood and live alone there , for our mother find the place dull , and the great consolation in precious Mr. Stack 's ministry , when the news arrive of your female perplexity , and I think we be all glad that I should have a pretext for come to Europe . " 
 " I should like to see any of the infernal scoundrel who say word against you , and break their rascally bone , " roar out Harry , stride up and down the room . 
 " I have to do something like it for Bob Clubber . " 
 " what ! that little sneaking , backbiting , toad - eat wretch , who be always hang about my lord at Greenway Court , and spunge on every gentleman in the country ? if you whip he , I hope you whip he well , George ? " 
 " we be bind over to keep the peace ; and I offer to go into Maryland with he and settle our difference there , and of course the good folk say , that have make free with the seventh commandment I be inclined to break the sixth . so , by this and by that and be as innocent of the crime impute to I as you be I leave home , my dear Harry , with as awful a reputation as ever a young gentleman earn . " 
 ah , what an opportunity be there here to moralise ! if the esteemed reader and his humble servant could but know could but write down in a book could but publish , with illustration , a collection of the lie which have be tell regard each of we since we come to man 's estate , what a harrowing and thrilling work of fiction that romance would be ! not only be the world inform of everything about you , but of a great deal more . not long since the kind postman bring a paper contain a valuable piece of criticism , which state " this author state he be bear in such and such a year . it be a lie . he be bear in the year so and so . " the critic know well : of course he do . another   and both come from the country which give MULLIGAN birth   warn some friend , say , " do not   speak of New South Wales to he . he have a brother there , and the family never mention his name . " but this subject be too vast and noble for a mere paragraph . I shall prepare a memoir , or let we have rather , par une societe de gens de lettre , a series of biography , of life of gentleman , as tell by their dear friend whom they do not   know . 
 George have relate his exploit as champion and martyr , of course Harry have to unbosom himself to his brother , and lie before his eld an account of his private affair . he give up all the family of Castlewood my lord , not for get the well of he at play ; for Harry be a sporting man , and expect to pay when he lose , and receive when he win ; but for refuse to aid the chaplain in his necessity , and dismiss he with such false and heartless pretext . about Mr. will he have make up his mind , after the horse - deal matter , and freely mark his sense of the latter 's conduct upon Mr. Will 's eye and nose . respect the Countess and Lady Fanny , Harry speak in a manner more guard , but not very favourable . he have hear all sort of story about they . the Countess be a card - play old cat ; Lady Fanny be a desperate flirt . who tell he ? well , he have hear the story from a person who know they both very well indeed . in fact , in those day of confidence , of which we make mention in the last volume , Maria have freely impart to her cousin a number of anecdote respect her stepmother and her half - sister , which be by no mean in favour of those lady . 
 but in respect to Lady Maria herself , the young man be staunch and hearty . " it may be imprudent : I do not   say no , George . I may be a fool : I think I be . I know there will be a dreadful piece of work at home , and that Madam and she will fight . well ! we must live apart . our estate be big enough to live on without quarrel , and I can go elsewhere than to Richmond or Castlewood . when you come to the property , you will give I a bit at any rate , Madam will let I off at an easy rent or I will make a famous farmer or factor . I can not   and will not   part from Maria . she have act so nobly by I , that I should be a rascal to turn my back on she . think of she bring I every jewel she have in the world , dear brave creature ! and fling they into my lap with her last guineas , and and God bless she ! " here Harry dash his sleeve across his eye , with a stamp of his foot , and say , " no , brother , I will not   part with she not to be make Governor of Virginia tomorrow ; and my dear old George would never advise I to do so , I know that . " 
 " I be send here to advise you , " George reply . " I be send to break the marriage off , if I can : and a more unhappy one I can not   imagine . but I can not   counsel you to break your word , my boy . " 
 " I know you could not ! what be say be say , George . I have make my bed , and must lie on it , " say Mr. Harry , gloomily . 
 such have be the settlement between our two young worthy , when they first talk over Mr. Harry 's love affair . but after George 's conversation with his aunt , and the further knowledge of his family , which he acquire through the information of that keen old woman of the world , Mr. Warrington , who be naturally of a sceptical turn , begin to doubt about Lady Maria , as well as regard her brother and sister , and look at Harry 's engagement with increase distrust and alarm . be it for his wealth that Maria want Harry ? be it his handsome young person that she long after ? be those story true which Aunt Bernstein have tell of she ? certainly he could not advise Harry to break his word ; but he might cast about in his mind for some scheme for put Maria 's affection to the trial ; and his ensue conduct , which appear not very amiable , I suppose result from this deliberation . 




 CHAPTER LVI . Ariadne 

 my Lord Castlewood have a house in Kensington Square spacious enough to accommodate the several member of his noble family , and convenient for their service at the palace hard by , when his Majesty dwell there . her ladyship have her evening , and give her card - party here for such as would come ; but Kensington be a long way from London a hundred year since , and George Selwyn say he for one be afraid to go , for fear of be rob of a night , whether by footpad with crape over their face , or by lady in rouge at the quadrille - table , we have no mean of say . about noon on the day after Harry have make his reappearance at White 's , it chance that all his virtuous kinsfolk partook of breakfast together , even Mr. will be present , who be to go into wait in the afternoon . 
 the lady come first to their chocolate : they Mr. will join in his court suit ; finally , my lord appear , languid , in his bedgown and nightcap , have not yet assume his wig for the day . here be news which will have bring home from the Star and Garter last night , when he sup in company with some man who have hear it at White 's and see it at Ranelagh ! 
 " heard what ? see what ? " ask the head of the house , take up his Daily Advertiser . 
 " ask Maria ! " say Lady Fanny . my lord turn to his eld sister , who wear a face of portentous sadness , and look as pale as a tablecloth . 
 " ' tis one of Will 's usual elegant and polite invention , " say Maria . 
 " no , " swear Will , with several of his oath ; " it be no invention of his . Tom Claypool of Norfolk see them both at Ranelagh ; and Jack Morris come out of White 's , where he hear the story from Harry Warrington 's own lip . curse he , I be glad of it ! " roar Will , slap the table . " what do you think of your Fortunate Youth , your Virginian , whom your lordship make so much of , turn out to be a second son ? " 
 " the eld brother not dead ? " say my lord . 
 " no more dead than you be . never be . it be my belief that it be a cross between the two . " 
 " Mr. Warrington be incapable of such duplicity ! " cry Maria . 
 " I never encourage the fellow , I be sure you will do I justice there , " say my lady . " nor do fanny : not we , indeed ! " 
 " not we , indeed ! " echo my Lady Fanny . 
 " the fellow be only a beggar , and , I dare say , have not pay for the clothe on his back , " continue Will . " I be glad of it , for , hang he , I hate he ! " 
 " you do not   regard he with favourable eye ; especially since he black your , Will ! " grin my lord . " so the poor fellow have find his brother , and lose his estate ! " and here he turn towards his sister Maria , who , although she look the picture of woe , must have suggest something ludicrous to the humourist near whom she sate ; for his lordship , have gaze at she for a minute , burst into a shrill laugh , which cause the poor lady 's face to flush , and presently her eye to pour over with tear . " it be a shame ! it be a shame ! " she sob out , and hide her face in her handkerchief . Maria 's stepmother and sister look at each other . " we never quite understand your lordship 's humour , " the former lady remark , gravely . 
 " I do not   see there be the least reason why you should , " say my lord , coolly . " Maria , my dear , pray excuse I if I have say that is , do anything , to hurt your feeling . " 
 " done anything ! you pillage the poor lad in his prosperity , and laugh at he in his ruin ! " say Maria , rise from table , and glare round at all her family . 
 " excuse I , my dear sister , I be not laugh at he , " say my lord , gently . 
 " oh , never mind at what or whom else , my lord ! you have take from he all he have to lose . all the world point at you as the man who feed on his own flesh and blood . and now you have his all , you make merry over his misfortune ! " and away she rustle from the room , fling look of defiance at all the party there assemble . 
 " tell we what have happen , or what you have hear , will , and my sister 's grief will not interrupt we . " and will tell , at great length , and with immense exultation at Harry 's discomfiture , the story now buzz through all London , of George Warrington 's sudden apparition . Lord Castlewood be sorry for Harry : Harry be a good , brave lad , and his kinsman like he , as much as certain worldly folk like each other . to be sure he play Harry at card , and take the advantage of the market upon he ; but why not ? the peach which other man would certainly pluck , he might as well devour . eh ! if that be all my conscience have to reproach I with , I need not be very uneasy ! my lord think . " where do Mr. Warrington live ? " 
 will express himself ready to enter upon a state of reprobation if he know or care . 
 " he shall be invite here , and treat with every respect , " say my lord . 
 " include piquet , I suppose ! " growl Will . 
 " or will you take he to the stable , and sell he one of your bargain of horseflesh , will ? " ask Lord Castlewood . " you would have win of Harry Warrington fast enough , if you could ; but you cheat so clumsily at your game that you get pay with a cudgel . I desire , once more , that every attention may be pay to our cousin Warrington . " 
 " and that you be not to be disturb , when you sit down to play , of course , my lord ! " cry Lady Castlewood . 
 " Madam , I desire fair play , for Mr. Warrington , and for myself , and for every member of this amiable family , " retort Lord Castlewood , fiercely . 
 " heaven help the poor gentleman if your lordship be go to be kind to he , " say the stepmother , with a curtsey ; and there be no knowing how far this family dispute might have be carry , have not , at this moment , a phaeton drive up to the house , in which be seat the two young Virginians . 
 it be the carriage which our young Prodigal have purchase in the day of his prosperity . he drive it still : George sate in it by his side ; their negro be behind they . Harry have be for meekly give the whip and rein to his brother , and cede the whole property to he . " what business have a poor devil like I with horse and carriage , georgy ? " Harry have humbly say . " beyond the coat on my back , and the purse my aunt give I , I have nothing in the world . you take the driving - seat , brother ; it will ease my mind if you will take the driving - seat . " George laughingly say he do not know the way , and Harry do ; and that , as for the carriage , he would claim only a half of it , as he have already do with his brother 's wardrobe . " but a bargain be a bargain ; if I share thy coat , thou must divide my breech ' pocket , Harry ; that be but fair dealing ! " again and again Harry swear there never be such a brother on earth . how he rattle his horse over the road ! how pleased and proud he be to drive such a brother ! they come to Kensington in famous high spirit ; and Gumbo 's thunder upon Lord Castlewood 's door be worthy of the big footman in all St. James 's . 
 only my Lady Castlewood and her daughter Lady Fanny be in the room into which our young gentleman be usher . will have no particular fancy to face Harry , my lord be not dress , Maria have her reason for be away , at least till her eye be dry . when we drive up to friend ' house nowadays in our coach - and - six , when John carry up our noble name , when , finally , we enter the drawing - room with our good hat and good Sunday smile foremost , do it ever happen that we interrupt a family row ! that we come simper and smile in , and step over the delusive ash of a still burn domestic heat ? that in the interval between the hall - door and the drawing - room , Mrs. , Mr. , and the Misses Jones have group themselves in a family tableau ; this girl artlessly arrange flower in a vase , let we say ; that one recline over an illuminated work of devotion ; mamma on the sofa , with the butcher 's and grocer 's book push under the cushion , some elegant work in her hand , and a pretty little foot push out advantageously ; while honest Jones , far from say , " curse that Brown , he be always call here ! " hold out a kindly hand , show a pleased face , and exclaim , " what , Brown my boy , delight to see you ! hope you have   come to lunch ! " I say , do it ever happen to we to be make the victim of domestic artifice , the spectator of domestic comedy get up for our special amusement ? oh , let we be thankful , not only for face , but for mask ! not only for honest welcome , but for hypocrisy , which hide unwelcome thing from we ! whilst I be talk , for instance , in this easy , chatty way , what right have you , my good sir , to know what be really pass in my mind ? it may be that I be rack with gout , or that my eld son have just send I in a thousand pound ' worth of college - bill , or that I be writhe under an attack of the Stoke Pogis Sentinel , which have just be send I under cover , or that there be a dreadfully scrappy dinner , the evident remain of a party to which I do not   invite you , and yet I conceal my agony , I wear a merry smile ; I say , " what ! come to take pot - luck with we , Brown my boy ! Betsy ! put a knife and fork for Mr. Brown . eat ! welcome ! fall to ! it be my good ! " I say that humbug which I be perform be beautiful self - denial that hypocrisy be true virtue . oh , if every man speak his mind what an intolerable society our would be to live in ! 
 as the young gentleman be announce , Lady Castlewood advance towards they with perfect ease and good - humour . " we have hear , Harry , " she say , look at the latter with a special friendliness , " of this most extraordinary circumstance . my Lord Castlewood say at breakfast that he should wait on you this very day , Mr. Warrington , and , cousin Harry , we intend not to love you any the less because you be poor . " 
 " we shall be able to show now that it be not for your acre that we like you , Harry ! " say Lady Fanny , follow her mamma 's lead . 
 " and I to whom the acre have fall ? " say Mr. George , with a smile and a bow . 
 " oh , cousin , we shall like you for be like Harry ! " reply the arch Lady Fanny . 
 ah ! who that have see the world , have not admire that astonishing ease with which fine lady drop you and pick you up again ? both the lady now address themselves almost exclusively to the young brother . they be quite civil to Mr. George : but with Mr. Harry they be fond , they be softly familiar , they be gently kind , they be affectionately reproachful . why have Harry not be for day and day to see they ? 
 " well to have have a dish of tea and a game at piquet with they than with some other folk , " say Lady Castlewood . " if we have win enough to buy a paper of pin from you we should have be content ; but young gentleman do not   know what be for their own good , " say mamma . 
 " now you have no more money to play with , you can come and play with we , cousin ! " cry fond Lady Fanny , lift up a finger , " and so your misfortune will be good fortune to we . " 
 George be puzzle . this welcome of his brother be very different from that to which he have look . all these compliment and attention pay to the young brother , though he be without a guinea ! perhaps the people be not so bad as they be paint ? the Blackest of all Blacks be say not to be of quite so dark a complexion as some folk describe he . 
 this affectionate conversation continue for some twenty minute , at the end of which period my Lord Castlewood make his appearance , wig on head , and sword by side . he greet both the young man with much politeness : one not more than the other . " if you be to come to we and I , for one , cordially rejoice to see you what a pity it be you do not come a few month early ! a certain evening at piquet would then most likely never have take place . a young son would have be more prudent . " 
 " yes , indeed , " say Harry . 
 " or a kinsman more compassionate . but I fear that love of play run in the blood of all of we . I have it from my father , and it have make I the poor peer in England . those fair lady whom you see before you be not exempt . my poor brother will be a martyr to it ; and what I , for my part , win on one day , I lose on the next . ' tis shocking , positively , the rage for play in England . all my poor cousin 's bank - note part company from I within twenty - four hour after I get they . " 
 " I have play , like other gentleman , but never to hurt myself , and never indeed care much for the sport , " remark Mr. Warrington . 
 " when we hear that my lord have play with Harry , we do so scold he , " cry the lady . 
 " but if it have not be I , thou know , cousin Warrington , some other person would have have thy money . ' tis a poor consolation , but as such Harry must please to take it , and be glad that friend win his money , who wish he well , not stranger , who care nothing for he , and fleece he . " 
 " eh ! a tooth out be a tooth out , though it be your brother who pull it , my lord ! " say Mr. George , laugh . " Harry must bear the penalty of his fault , and pay his debt , like other man . " 
 " I be sure I have never say or think otherwise . ' tis not like an Englishman to be sulky because he be beat , " say Harry . 
 " your hand , cousin ! you speak like a man ! " cry my lord , with delight . the lady smile to each other . 
 " my sister , in Virginia , have know how to bring up her son as gentleman ! " exclaim Lady Castlewood , enthusiastically . 
 " I protest you must not be grow so amiable now you be poor , cousin Harry ! " cry cousin Fanny . " why , mamma , we do not know half his good quality when he be only Fortunate Youth and Prince of Virginia ! you be exactly like he , cousin George , but I vow you can not   be as amiable as your brother ! " 
 " I be the Prince of Virginia , but I fear I be not the Fortunate Youth , " say George , gravely . 
 Harry be begin , " by Jove , he be the good " when the noise of a harpsichord be hear from the upper room . the lad blush : the lady smile . 
 " ' Tis Maria , above , " say Lady Castlewood . " let some of we go up to she . " 
 the lady rise , and make way towards the door ; and Harry follow they , blush very much . George be about to join the party , but Lord Castlewood check he . " Nay , if all the lady follow your brother " his lordship say , " let I at least have the benefit of your company and conversation . I long to hear the account of your captivity and rescue , cousin George ! " 
 " oh , we must hear that too ! " cry one of the lady , linger . 
 " I be greedy , and should like it all by myself , " say Lord Castlewood , look at her very sternly ; and follow the woman to the door , and close it upon they with a low bow . 
 " your brother have no doubt acquaint you with the history of all that have happen to he in this house , cousin George ? " ask George 's kinsman . 
 " yes , include the quarrel with Mr. Will and the engagement to my Lady Maria , " reply George , with a bow . " I may be pardon for say that he hath meet with but ill fortune here , my lord . " 
 " which no one can deplore more cordially than myself . my brother live with horse jockey and trainer , and the wild blood of the town , and between we there be very little sympathy . we should not all live together , be we not so poor . this be the house which our grandmother occupy before she go to America and marry Colonel Esmond . much of the furniture belong to she . " George look round the wainscote parlour with some interest . " our house have not flourish in the last twenty year ; though we have a promotion of rank a score of year since , owe to some interest we have at court , then . but the malady of play have be the ruin of we all . I be a miserable victim to it : only too proud to sell myself and title to a roturiere , as many nobleman , less scrupulous , have do . Pride be my fault , my dear cousin . I remember how I be bear ! " and his lordship lay his hand on his shirt - frill , turn out his toe , and look his cousin nobly in the face . 
 Young George Warrington 's natural disposition be to believe everything which everybody say to he . when once deceive , however , or undeceive about the character of a person , he become utterly incredulous , and he salute this fine speech of my lord 's with a sardonical , inward laughter , preserve his gravity , however , and scarce allow any of his scorn to appear in his word . 
 " we have all our fault , my lord . that of play hath be condone over and over again in gentleman of our rank . have heartily forgive my brother , surely I can not presume to be your lordship 's judge in the matter ; and instead of play and losing , I wish sincerely that you have both play and win ! " 
 " so do I , with all my heart ! " say my lord with a sigh . " I augur well for your goodness when you can speak in this way , and for your experience and knowledge of the world , too , cousin , of which you seem to possess a great share than most young man of your age . your poor Harry hath the good heart in the world ; but I doubt whether his head be very strong . " 
 " not very strong , indeed . but he hath the art to make friend wherever he go , and in spite of all his imprudence most people love he . " 
 " I do we all do , I be sure ! as if he be our brother ! " cry my lord . 
 " he have often describe in his letter his welcome at your lordship 's house . my mother keep they all , you may be sure . Harry 's style be not very learn , but his heart be so good , that to read he be well than wit . " 
 " I may be mistake , but I fancy his brother possess a good heart and a good wit , too ! " say my lord , obstinately gracious . 
 " I be as Heaven make I , cousin ; and perhaps some more experience and sorrow than have fall to the lot of most young man . " 
 " this misfortune of your poor brother I mean this piece of good fortune , your sudden reappearance have not quite leave Harry without resource ? " continue Lord Castlewood , very gently . 
 " with nothing but what his mother can leave he , or I , at her death , can spare he . what be the usual portion here of a young brother , my lord ? " 
 " eh ! a young brother here be you know in fine , everybody know what a young brother be , " say my lord , and shrug his shoulder and look his guest in the face . 
 the other go on : " we be the good of friend , but we be flesh and blood : and I do not   pretend to do more for he than be usually do for young brother . why give he money ? that he should squander it at card or horse - racing ? my lord , we have card and jockey in Virginia , too ; and my poor Harry hath distinguish himself in his own country already , before he come to your . he inherit the family fail for dissipation . " 
 " poor fellow , poor fellow , I pity he ! " 
 " our estate , you see , be great , but our income be small . we have little more money than that which we get from England for our tobacco and very little of that too for our tobacco come back to we in the shape of good , clothe , leather , grocery , ironmongery , nay , wine and beer for our people and ourselves . Harry may come back and share all these : there be a nag in the stable for he , a piece of venison on the table , a little ready money to keep his pocket warm , and a coat or two every year . this will go on whilst my mother live , unless , which be far from improbable , he get into some quarrel with Madam Esmond . then , whilst I live he will have the run of the house and all it contain : then , if I die leave child , he will be less and less welcome . his future , my lord , be a dismal one , unless some strange piece of luck turn up on which we be fool to speculate . henceforth he be doom to dependence , and I know no bad lot than to be dependent on a self - willed woman like our mother . the mean he have to make himself respect at home he hath squander away here . he have fling his patrimony to the dog , and poverty and subserviency be now his only portion . " Mr. Warrington deliver this speech with considerable spirit and volubility , and his cousin hear he respectfully . 
 " you speak well , Mr. Warrington . have you ever think of public life ? " say my lord . 
 " of course I have think of public life like every man of my station every man , that is , who care for something beyond a dice - box or a stable , " reply George . " I hope , my lord , to be able to take my own place , and my unlucky brother must content himself with his . this I say advisedly , have hear from he of certain engagement which he have form , and which it would be misery to all party be he to attempt to execute now . " 
 " your logic be very strong , " say my lord . " shall we go up and see the lady ? there be a picture above - stair which your grandfather be say to have execute . before you go , my dear cousin , you will please to fix a day when our family may have the honour of receive you . Castlewood , you know , be always your home when we be there . it be something like your Virginian Castlewood , cousin , from your account . we have beef , and mutton , and ale , and wood , in plenty ; but money be woefully scarce amongst we . " 
 they ascend to the drawing - room , where , however , they find only one of the lady of the family . this be my Lady Maria , who come out of the embrasure of a window , where she and Harry Warrington have be engage in talk . 
 George make his good bow , Maria her low curtsey . " you be indeed wonderfully like your brother , " she say , give he her hand . " and from what he say , cousin George , I think you be as good as he be . " 
 at the sight of her swollen eye and tearful face George feel a pang of remorse . " poor thing ! " he think . " Harry have be vaunt my generosity and virtue to she , and I have beer , play the selfish eld brother downstairs ! how old she look ! how could he ever have a passion for such a woman as that ? " how ? because he do not see with your eye , Mr. George . he see rightly too now with his own , perhaps . I never know whether to pity or congratulate a man on come to his sens . 
 after the introduction a little talk take place , which for a while Lady Maria manage to carry on in an easy manner : but though lady in this matter of social hypocrisy be , I think , far more consummate performer than man , after a sentence or two the poor lady break out into a sob , and , motion Harry away with her hand , fairly flee from the room . 
 Harry be rush forward , but stop check by that sign . my lord say his poor sister be subject to these fit of nerve , and have already be ill that morning . after this event our young gentleman think it be needless to prolong their visit . Lord Castlewood follow they downstairs , accompany they to the door , admire their nag in the phaeton , and wave they a friendly farewell . 
 " and so we have be coax and cuddle in the window , and we part good friend , Harry ? be it not so ? " say George to his charioteer . 
 " oh , she be a good woman ! " cry Harry , lash the horse . " I know you will think so when you come to know she . " 
 " when you take her home to Virginia ? a pretty welcome our mother will give she . she will never forgive I for not break the match off , nor you for make it . " 
 " I can not   help it , George ! do not   you be pop your ugly head so close to my ear , Gumbo ! after what have pass between we , I be bind in honour to stand by she . if she see no objection , I must find none . I tell she all . I tell she that Madam would be very rusty at first ; but that she be very fond of I , and must end by relent . and when you come to the property , I tell she that I know my dearest George so well , that I might count upon share with he . " 
 " the deuce you do ! let I tell you , my dear , that I have be tell my Lord Castlewood quite a different story . that as an eld brother I intend to have all my right there , do not   flog that near horse so and that you can but look forward to poverty and dependence . " 
 " what ! you will not   help I ? " cry Harry , turn quite pale . 
 " George , I do not   believe it , though I hear it out of your own mouth ! there be a minute 's pause after this outbreak , during which Harry do not even look at his brother , but sate , gaze blindly before he , the picture of grief and gloom . he be drive so near to a road - post that the carriage might have be upset but for George 's pull the rein . 
 " you have well take the rein , sir , " say Harry . " I tell you you have well take they . " 
 " do you ever know I fail you , Harry ? " George ask . 
 " no , " say the other , " not till now " the tear be roll down his cheek as he speak . 
 " my dear , I think one day you will say I have do my duty . " 
 " what have you do ? ask Harry . 
 " I have say you be a young brother that you have spend all your patrimony , and that your portion at home must be very slender . be it not true ? " 
 " yes , but I would not have believe it , if ten thousand man have tell I , " say Harry . " whatever happen to I , I think I could trust you , George Warrington . " and in this frame of mind Harry remain during the rest of the drive . 
 their dinner be serve soon after their return to their lodging , of which Harry scarce eat any , though he drink freely of the wine before he . 
 " that wine be a bad consoler in trouble , Harry , " his brother remark . 
 " I have no other , sir , " say Harry , grimly ; and have drunk glass after glass in silence , he presently seize his hat , and leave the room . 
 he do not return for three hour . George , in much anxiety about his brother , have not leave home meanwhile , but read his book , and smoke the pipe of patience . " it be shabby to say I would not aid he , and , God help I , it be not true . I will not   leave he , though he marry a blackamoor , " think George " have I not do he harm enough already , by come to life again ? where have he go ; have he go to play ? " 
 " good God ! what have happen to thee ? " cry George Warrington , presently , when his brother come in , look ghastly pale . 
 he come up and take his brother 's hand . " I can take it now , Georgy , " he say . " perhaps what you do be right , though . I for one will never believe that you would throw your brother off in distress . I will tell you what . at dinner , I think suddenly , I will go back to she and speak to she . I will say to she , ' Maria , poor as I be , your conduct to I have be so noble , that , by heaven ! I be yours to take or to leave . if you will have I , here I be : I will enlist : I will work : I will try and make a livelihood for myself somehow , and my bro my relation will relent , and give we enough to live on . ' that be what I determine to tell she ; and I do , George . I run all the way to Kensington in the rain look , I be splash from head to foot , and find they all at dinner , all except Will , that be . I speak out that very moment to they all , sit round the table , over their wine . ' Maria , ' say I , ' a poor fellow want to redeem his promise which he make when he fancy he be rich . will you take he ? ' I find I have plenty of word , and do not   hem and stutter as I be do now . I speak ever so long , and I end by say I would do my good and my duty by she , so help I God ! 
 " when I have do , she come up to I quite kind . she take my hand , and kiss it before the rest . ' my dearest , good Harry ! ' she say   those be her word , I do not   want otherwise to be praise myself , ' you be a noble heart , and I thank you with all mine . but , my dear , I have long see it be only duty , and a foolish promise make by a young man to an old woman , that have hold you to your engagement . to keep it would make you miserable , my dear . I absolve you from it , thank you with all my heart for your fidelity , and blessing and love my dear cousin always . ' and she come up and kiss I before they all , and go out of the room quite stately , and without a single tear . they be all cry , especially my lord , who be sob quite loud . I do not   think he have so much feeling . and she , George ? oh , be not   she a noble creature ? " 
 " here be her health ! " cry George , fill one of the glass that still stand before he . 
 " Hip , hip , huzzay ! " say Harry . he be wild with delight at be free . 




 CHAPTER LVII . in which Mr. Harry 's Nose continue to be put out of joint 

 Madame de Bernstein be scarcely less pleased than her virginian nephew at the result of Harry 's final interview with Lady Maria . George inform the Baroness of what have pass , in a billet which he send to she the same evening ; and shortly afterwards her nephew Castlewood , whose visit to his aunt be very rare , come to pay his respect to she , and frankly speak about the circumstance which have take place ; for no man know well than my Lord Castlewood how to be frank upon occasion , and now that the business between Maria and Harry be end what need be there of reticence or hypocrisy ? the game have be play , and be over : he have no objection now to speak of its various move , stratagem , finesse . " she be my own sister , " say my lord , affectionately ; ' she will not   have many more chance many more such chance of marry and establish herself . I might not approve of the match in all respect , and I might pity your ladyship 's young virginian favourite : but of course such a piece of good fortune be not to be throw away , and I be bind to stand by my own flesh and blood . " 
 " your candour do your lordship honour , " say Madame de Bernstein , " and your love for your sister be quite edify ! " 
 " Nay , we have lose the game , and I be speak sans rancune . it be not for you , who have win , to bear malice , " say my lord , with a bow . 
 Madame de Bernstein protest she be never in her life in well humour . " Confess , now , Eugene , that visit of Maria to Harry at the spunging - house that touch give up of all his present to she , be a stroke of thy invention ? " 
 " pity for the young man , and a sense of what be due from Maria to her friend her affiance lover in misfortune , sure these be motif sufficient to make her act as she do , " reply Lord Castlewood , demurely . 
 " but ' twa you advise she , my good nephew ? " 
 Castlewood , with a shrug of his shoulder , own that he do advise his sister to see Mr. Henry Warrington . " but we should have win , in spite of your ladyship , " he continue , " have not the eld brother make his appearance . and I have be try to console my poor Maria by show she what a piece of good fortune it be after all , that we lose . " 
 " suppose she have marry Harry , and then cousin George have make his appearance ? " remark the Baroness . 
 " effectivement , " cry Eugene , take snuff . " as the grave be to give up its dead , let we be thankful to the grave for disgorge in time ! I be bind to say , that Mr. George Warrington seem to be a man of sense , and not more selfish than other eld son and man of the world . my poor molly fancy that he might be a what shall I say ? a greenhorn perhaps be the term like his young brother . she fondly hop that he might be incline to go share and share alike with Twin junior ; in which case , so infatuated be she about the young fellow , that I believe she would have take he . ' Harry Warrington , with half a loaf , might do very well , ' say I , ' but Harry Warrington with no bread , my dear ! ' " 
 " how no bread ? " ask the Baroness . 
 " well , no bread except at his brother 's side - table . the elder say as much . " 
 " what a hard - hearted wretch ! " cry Madame de Bernstein . 
 " ah , bah ! I play with you , aunt , carte sur table ! Mr. George only do what everybody else would do ; and we have no right to be angry with he , really we have not . molly herself acknowledge as much , after her first burst of grief be over , and I bring she to listen to reason . the silly old creature ! to be so wild about a young lad at her time of life ! " 
 " ' twas a real passion , I almost do believe , " say Madame de Bernstein . 
 " you should have hear she take leave of he . c'etait touchant , ma parole d'honneur ! I cry . before George , I could not help myself . the young fellow with muddy stocking , and his hair about his eye , fling himself amongst we when we be at dinner ; make his offer to Molly in a very frank and noble manner , and in good language too ; and she reply . Begad , it put I in mind of Mrs. Woffington in the new Scotch play , that Lord Bute 's man have write Douglas what d'ye call it ? she cling round the lad : she bid he adieu in heartrende accent . she step out of the room in a stately despair no more chocolate , thank you . if she have make a mauvais pas no one could retire from it with more dignity . ' twas a masterly retreat after a defeat . we be starve out of our position , but we retire with all the honour of war . " 
 " molly will not   die of the disappointment ! " say my lord 's aunt , sip her cup . 
 my lord snarl a grin , and show his yellow tooth . " he , he ! " he say , ' she hath once or twice before have the malady very severely , and recover perfectly . it do not   kill , as your ladyship know , at Molly 's age . " 
 how should her ladyship know ? she do not marry Doctor Tusher until she be advanced in life . she do not become Madame de Bernstein until still later . Old Dido , a poet remark , be not ignorant of misfortune , and hence learn to have compassion on the wretched . 
 People in the little world , as I have be tell , quarrel and fight , and go on abuse each other , and be not reconcile for ever so long . but people in the great world be surely wise in their generation . they have difference ; they cease see each other . they make it up and come together again , and no question be ask . a stray prodigal , or a stray puppy - dog , be thus bring in under the benefit of an amnesty , though you know he have be away in ugly company . for six month past , ever since the Castlewoods and Madame de Bernstein have be battle for possession of poor Harry Warrington , these two branch of the Esmond family have remain apart . now , the question be settle , they be free to meet again , as though no difference ever have separate they : and Madame de Bernstein drive in her great coach to Lady Castlewood 's rout , and the Esmond lady appear smile at Madame de Bernstein 's drum , and love each other just as much as they previously have do . 
 " so , sir , I hear you have act like a hard - hearted monster about your poor brother Harry ! " say the Baroness , delight , and menace George with her stick . 
 " I act but upon your ladyship 's hint , and desire to see whether it be for himself or his reputed money that his kinsfolk want to have he , " reply George , turn rather red . 
 " Nay , Maria could not marry a poor fellow who be utterly penniless , and whose eld brother say he would give he nothing ! " 
 " I do it for the good , madam , " say George , still blush . 
 " and so thou didst , o thou hypocrite ! " cry the old lady . 
 " Hypocrite , madam ! and why ? " ask Mr. Warrington , draw himself up in much state . 
 " I know all , my infant ! " say the Baroness in French . " thou art very like thy grandfather . come , that I embrace thee ! Harry have tell I all , and that thou hast divide thy little patrimony with he ! " 
 " it be but natural , madam . we have have common heart and purse since we be bear . I but feign hard - heartedness in order to try those people yonder , " say George , with fill eye . 
 " and thou wilt divide Virginia with he too ? " ask the Bernstein . 
 " I do not   say so . it be not just , " reply Mr. Warrington . " the land must go to the eld bear , and Harry would not have it otherwise : and it may be I shall die , or my mother outlive the pair of we . but half of what be mine be his : and he , it must be remember , only be extravagant because he be mistake as to his position . " 
 " but it be a knight of old , it be a Bayard , it be the grandfather come to life ! " cry Madame de Bernstein to her attendant , as she be retire for the night . and that evening , when the lad leave she , it be to poor Harry she give the two finger , and to George the rouged cheek , who blush , for his part , almost as deep as that often - dye rise , at such a mark of his old kinswoman 's favour . 
 although Harry Warrington be the least envious of man , and do honour to his brother as in all respect his chief , guide , and superior , yet no wonder a certain feeling of humiliation and disappointment oppress the young man after his deposition from his eminence as Fortunate Youth and heir to boundless virginian territory . our friend at Kensington might promise and vow that they would love he all the well after his fall ; Harry make a low bow and profess himself very thankful ; but he could not help perceive , when he go with his brother to the state entertainment with which my Lord Castlewood regale his new - find kinsman , that George be all in all to his cousin : have all the talk , compliment , and petit soin for himself , whilst of Harry no one take any notice save poor Maria , who follow he with wistful look , pursue he with eye convey dismal reproach , and , as it be , blame he because she have leave he . " ah ! " the eye seem to say , " ' tis mighty well of you , Harry , to have accept the freedom which I give you ; but I have no intention , sir , that you should be so pleased at be let off . " she give he up , but yet she do not quite forgive he for take she at her word . she would not have he , and yet she would . oh , my young friend , how delightful be the beginning of a love - business , and how undignified , sometimes , the end ! what a romantic vista be before young Damon and young Phillis   or middle - aged ditto ditto   when , their artless love make known to each other , they twine their arm round each other 's waist and survey that charming pay du tendre which lie at their foot ! into that country , so link together , they will wander from now until extreme old age . there may be rock and roar river , but will not Damon 's strong true love enable he to carry Sweetheart over they ? there may be dragon and danger in the path , but shall not his courageous sword cut they down ? then at eve , how they will rest cuddle together , like two pretty babe in the wood , the moss their couch , the star their canopy , their arm their mutual pillow ! this be the wise plan young folk make when they set out on the love journey ; and o I ! they have not get a mile when they come to a great wall and find they must walk back again . they be squabble with the post - boy at Barnet   the first stage on the Gretna Road , I mean , and , behold , perhaps Strephon have not get any money , or here be papa with a whacking horsewhip , who take Miss back again , and lock she up cry in the schoolroom . the parting be heart - breaking ; but , when she have marry the banker and have eight child , and he have become , it may be , a prosperous barrister , it may be , a seedy raff who have go twice or thrice into the Gazette ; when , I say , in after year Strephon and Delia meet again , be not the meeting ridiculous ? nevertheless , I hope no young man will fall in love , have any doubt in his mind as to the eternity of his passion . ' tis when a man have have a second or third amorous attack that he begin to grow doubtful ; but some woman be romantic to the end , and from eighteen to eight - and - fifty   for what I know   be always expect their heart to break . in fine , when you have be in love and be so no more , when the King of France , with twenty thousand man , with colour fly , music playing , and all the pomp of war , have march up the hill , then proceed to march down again , he and you be in an absurd position . 
 this be what Harry Warrington , no doubt , feel when he go to Kensington and encounter the melancholy , reproachful eye of his cousin . yes ! it be a foolish position to be in ; but it be also melancholy to look into a house you have once live in , and see black casement and emptiness where once shine the fire of welcome . melancholy ? yes ; but , ha ! how bitter , how melancholy , how absurd to look up as you pass sentimentally by No . 13 , and see somebody else grin out of window , and evidently on the good term with the landlady . I always feel hurt , even at an inn which I frequent , if I see other folk ' trunk and boot at the door of the room which be once mine . have those boot loll on the sofa which once I recline on ? I kick you from before I , you muddy , vulgar highlow ! 
 so consider that his period of occupation be over , and Maria 's room , if not give up to a new tenant , be , at any rate , to let , Harry do not feel very easy in his cousin 's company , nor she possibly in his . he find either that he have nothing to say to she , or that what she have to say to he be rather dull and commonplace , and that the red lip of a white - neck pipe of Virginia be decidedly more agreeable to he now than Maria 's soft accent and most melancholy moue . when George go to Kensington , then , Harry do not care much about go , and plead other engagement .
