 at his uncle 's house in Hill Street the poor lad be no well amuse , and , indeed , be treat by the virtuous people there with scarce any attention at all . the lady do not scruple to deny themselves when he come ; he could scarce have believe in such insincerity after their caress , their welcome , their repeat vow of affection ; but happen to sit with the lambert for an hour after he have call upon his aunt , he see her ladyship 's chairman arrive with an empty chair , and his aunt step out and enter the vehicle , and not even blush when he make she a bow from the opposite window . to be deny by his own relation to have that door which have open to he so kindly , slam in his face ! he would not have believe such a thing possible , poor simple Harry say . perhaps he think the door - knocker have a tender heart , and be not make of brass ; not more changed than the head of that knocker be my Lady Warrington 's virtuous face when she pass her nephew . 
 " my father 's own brother 's wife ! what have I do to offend she ? oh , Aunt Lambert , Aunt Lambert , do you ever see such cold - heartedness ? " cry out Harry , with his usual impetuosity . 
 " do we make any difference to you , my dear Harry ? " say Aunt Lambert , with a side look at her young daughter . " the world may look coldly at you , but we do not   belong to it : so you may come to we in safety . " 
 " in this house you be different from other people , " reply Harry . " I do not   know how , but I always feel quiet and happy somehow when I come to you . " 
  " Quis I uno vivit felicior ? aut magis hac est 
  Optandum vita dicere quis potuit ? " 
 call out General Lambert . " do you know where I get these verse , Mr. Gownsman ? " and he address his son from college , who be come to pass an Easter holiday with his parent . " you get they out of Catullus , sir , " say the scholar . 
 " I get they out of no such thing , sir . I get they out of my favourite Democritus Junior out of old Burton , who have provide many indifferent scholar with learning ; " and who and Montaigne , be favourite author with the good General . 




 CHAPTER LVIII . where we do what cat may do 

 we have say how our Virginians , with a wisdom not uncommon in youth , have choose to adopt strong Jacobite opinion , and to profess a prodigious affection for the exile royal family . the banished prince have recognise Madam Esmond 's father as Marquis of Esmond , and she do not choose to be very angry with an unfortunate race , that , after all , be so willing to acknowledge the merit of her family . as for any little scandal about her sister , Madame de Bernstein , and the Old Chevalier , she toss away from she with scorn the recollection of that odious circumstance , assert , with perfect truth , that the two first monarch of the House of Hanover be quite as bad as any stuart in regard to their domestic morality . but the king de facto be the king , as well as his Majesty de jure . De Facto have be solemnly crown and anoint at church , and have likewise utterly discomfited De Jure , when they come to battle for the kingdom together . Madam 's clear opinion be , then , that her son owe it to themselves as well as the sovereign to appear at his royal court . and if his Majesty should have be minded to confer a lucrative post , or a blue or red ribbon upon either of they , she , for her part , would not have be in the least surprised . she make no doubt but that the King know the Virginian Esmonds as well as any other member of his nobility . the lad be specially command , then , to present themselves at court , and , I dare say , their mother would have be very angry have she know that George take Harry 's laced coat on the day when he go to make his bow at Kensington . 
 a hundred year ago the King 's drawing - room be open almost every day to his nobility and gentry ; and loyalty especially since the war have begin could gratify itself a score of time in a month with the august sight of the sovereign . a wise avoidance of the enemy 's ship of war , a gracious acknowledgment of the inestimable loss the British Isles would suffer by the seizure of the royal person at sea , cause the monarch to forgo those visit to his native Hanover which be so dear to his royal heart , and compel he to remain , it must be own , unwillingly amongst his love Britons . a hanoverian lady , however , whose virtue have endear she to the prince , strove to console he for his enforced absence from Herrenhausen . and from the lip of the Countess of Walmoden   on whom the imperial beneficence have gracefully confer a high title of british honour   the revered Defender of the Faith could hear the accent of his native home . 
 to this beloved Sovereign , Mr. Warrington request his uncle , an assiduous courtier , to present he ; and as Mr. Lambert have to go to court likewise , and thank his Majesty for his promotion , the two gentleman make the journey to Kensington together , engage a hackney - coach for the purpose , as my Lord Wrotham 's carriage be now want by its rightful owner , who have return to his house in town . they alight at Kensington Palace Gate , where the sentry on duty know and salute the good General , and hence modestly make their way on foot to the summer residence of the sovereign . walk under the portico of the Palace , they enter the gallery which lead to the great black marble staircase   which hath be so richly decorate and paint by Mr. Kent , and then pass through several room , richly hang with tapestry and adorn with picture and busto , until they come to the King 's great drawing - room , where that famous " Venus " by Titian be , and , amongst other masterpiece , the picture of " St. Francis adore the infant saviour , " perform by Sir Peter Paul Rubens ; and here , with the rest of the visitor to the court , the gentleman wait until his Majesty issue from his private apartment , where he be in conference with certain personage who be call in the newspaper language of that day his M - j - ty 's M - n - st - rs . 
 George Warrington , who have never be in a palace before , have leisure to admire the place , and regard the people round he . he see fine picture for the first time too , and I dare say delighted in that charming piece of Sir Athony Vandyck , represent King Charles the First , his queen and Family , and the noble picture of " Esther before Ahasuerus , " paint by Tintoret , and in which all the figure be dress in the magnificent venetian habit . with the contemplation of these work he be so enraptured , that he scarce hear all the remark of his good friend the General , who be whisper into his young companion 's almost heedless ear the name of some of the personage round about they . 
 " yonder , " say Mr. Lambert , " be two of my Lords of the Admiralty , Mr. Gilbert Elliot and Admiral Boscawen : your Boscawen , whose fleet fire the first gun in your water two year ago . that stout gentleman all belate with gold be Mr. Fox , that be Minister , and be now content to be Paymaster with a great salary . 
 " he carry the auri fame on his person . why , his waistcoat be a perfect Potosi ! " say George . 
 " Aliena appeten how go the text ? he love to get money and to spend it , " continue General Lambert . " Yon be my Lord Chief Justice Willes , talk to my Lord of Salisbury , Doctor Headley , who , if he serve his God as he serve his king , will be translate to some very high promotion in Heaven . he belong to your grandfather 's time , and be love by Dick Steele and hat by the Dean . with they be my Lord of London , the learn Doctor Sherlock . my lord of the lawn sleeve have lose half their honour now . I remember when I be a boy in my mother 's hand , she make I go down on my knee to the Bishop of Rochester ; he who go over the water , and become Minister to somebody who shall be nameless Perkin 's Bishop . that handsome fair man be Admiral Smith . he be president of poor Byng 's court - martial , and strove in vain to get he off his penalty ; Tom of Ten Thousand they call he in the fleet . the french Ambassador have he break , when he be a lieutenant , for make a french man - of - war low topsail to he , and the King make Tom a captain the next day . that tall , haughty - look man be my Lord George Sackville , who , now I be a Major - General myself , will treat I somewhat well than a footman . I wish my stout old Blakeney be here ; he be the soldier 's darling , and as kind and brave as yonder poker of a nobleman be brave and I be your lordship 's very humble servant . this be a young gentleman who be just from America , and be in Braddock 's sad business two year ago . " 
 " oh , indeed ! " say the poker of a nobleman . " I have the honour of speak to Mr. ? " 
 " to Major - General Lambert , at your lordship 's service , and who be in his Majesty 's some time before you enter it . that , Mr. Warrington , be the first commoner in England , Mr. Speaker Onslow . where be your uncle ? I shall have to present you myself to his Majesty if Sir Miles delay much long . " as he speak , the worthy General address himself entirely to his young friend , make no sort of account of his colleague , who stalk away with a scared look as if amazed at the other 's audacity . a hundred year ago , a nobleman be a nobleman , and expect to be admire as such . 
 Sir Miles 's red waistcoat appear in sight presently , and many cordial greeting pass between he , his nephew , and General Lambert : for we have describe how Sir Miles be the most affectionate of man . so the General have quit my Lord Wrotham 's house ? it be time , as his lordship himself wish to occupy it ? very good ; but consider what a loss for the neighbour ! 
 " we miss you , we positively miss you , my dear General , " cry Sir Miles . " my daughter be in love with those lovely young lady upon my word , they be ; and my Lady Warrington and my girl be debate over and over again how they should find an opportunity of make the acquaintance of your charming family . we feel as if we be old friend already ; indeed we do , General , if you will permit I the liberty of say so ; and we love you , if I may be allow to speak frankly , on account of your friendship and kindness to our dear nephews : though we be a little jealous , I own a little jealous of they , because they go so often to see you . often and often have I say to my Lady Warrington , ' my dear , why do not   we make acquaintance with the General ? why do not   we ask he and his lady to come over in a family way and dine with some other plain country gentlefolk ? ' carry my most sincere respect to Mrs. Lambert , I pray , sir ; and thank she for her goodness to these young gentleman . my own flesh and blood , sir ; my dear , dear brother 's boy ! " he pass his hand across his manly eye : he be choke almost with generous and affectionate emotion . 
 whilst they be discourse George Warrington the while restrain his laughter with admirable gravity the door of the King 's apartment open , and the page enter , precede his Majesty . he be follow by his burly son , his Royal Highness the Duke , a very corpulent Prince , with a coat and face of blaze scarlet : behind they come various gentleman and officer of state ; among whom George at once recognise the famous Mr. Secretary Pitt , by his tall stature , his eagle eye and beak , his grave and majestic presence . as I see that solemn figure pass , even a hundred year off , I protest I feel a present awe , and a desire to take my hat off . I be not frightened at George the Second ; nor be my eye dazzle by the portentous appearance of his Royal Highness the Duke of Culloden and Fontenoy ; but the Great Commoner , the terrible Cornet of Horse ! his figure bestride our narrow isle of a century back like a Colossus ; and I hush as he pass in his gouty shoe , his thunderbolt hand wrap in flannel . perhaps as we see he now , issue with dark look from the royal closet , angry scene have be pass between he and his august master . he have be bore that old monarch for hour with prodigious long speech , full of eloquence , voluble with the noble phrase upon the commonest topic ; but , it must be confess , utterly repulsive to the little shrewd old gentleman , " at whose foot he lay himself , " as the phrase be , and who have the most thorough dislike for fine boedry and for fine brose too ! the sublime Minister pass solemnly through the crowd ; the company range itself respectfully round the wall ; and his Majesty walk round the circle , his royal son lag a little behind , and engage select individual in conversation for his own part . 
 the monarch be a little , keen , fresh - coloured old man , with very protrude eye , attire in plain , old - fashioned , snuff - colour clothe and brown stocking , his only ornament the blue ribbon of his order of the Garter . he speak in a german accent , but with ease , shrewdness , and simplicity , address those individual whom he have a mind to notice , or pass on with a bow . he know Mr. Lambert well , who have serve under his Majesty at Dettingen , and with his royal son in Scotland , and he congratulate he good - humouredly on his promotion . 
 " it be not always , " his Majesty be pleased to say , " that we can do as we like ; but I be glad when , for once , I could give myself that pleasure in your case , General ; for my army contain no well officer as you . " 
 the veteran blush and bow , deeply gratified at this speech . meanwhile , the Best of Monarchs be look at Sir Miles Warrington   whom his Majesty know perfectly , as the eager recipient of all favour from all Ministers , and at the young gentleman by his side . 
 " who be this ? " the Defender of the faith condescend to ask , point towards George Warrington , who stand before his sovereign in a respectful attitude , clothe in poor Harry 's good embroider suit . 
 with the deep reverence Sir Miles inform his king , that the young gentleman be his nephew , Mr. George Warrington , of Virginia , who ask leave to pay his humble duty . 
 " this , then , be the other brother ? " the Venerated Prince deign to observe . " he come in time , else the other brother would have spend all the money . my Lord Bishop of Salisbury , why do you come out in this bitter weather ? you have much well stay at home ! " and with this , the revered wielder of Britannia 's sceptre pass on to other lord and gentleman of his court . Sir Miles Warrington be deeply affect at the royal condescension . he clap his nephew 's hand . " God bless you , my boy , " he cry ; " I tell you that you would see the great monarch and the fine gentleman in the world . be he not so , my Lord Bishop ? " 
 " that , that he be ! " cry his lordship , clasp his ruffle hand , and turn his fine eye up to the sky , " the good of prince and of man . " 
 " that be Master Louis , my Lady Yarmouth 's favourite nephew , " say Lambert , point to a young gentleman who stand with a crowd round he ; and presently the stout Duke of Cumberland come up to our little group . 
 his Royal Highness hold out his hand to his old companion - in - arm . " congratulate you on your promotion , Lambert , " he say good - naturedly . Sir Miles Warrington 's eye be ready to burst out of his head with rapture . 
 " I owe it , sir , to your Royal Highness 's good office , " say the grateful General . 
 " not at all ; not at all : ought to have have it a long time before . always be a good officer ; perhaps there will be some employment for you soon . this be the gentleman whom James Wolfe introduce to I ? " 
 " his brother , sir . " 
 " oh , the real Fortunate Youth ! you be with poor Ned Braddock in America a prisoner , and lucky enough to escape . come and see I , sir , in Pall Mall . bring he to my levee , Lambert . " and the broad back of the Royal Prince be turn to our friend . 
 " it be rain ! you come on foot , General Lambert ? you and George must come home in my coach . you must and shall come home with I , I say . by George , you must ! I will have no denial , " cry the enthusiastic Baronet ; and he drive George and the General back to Hill Street , and present the latter to my Lady Warrington and his darling , Flora and Dora , and insist upon their partaking of a collation , as they must be hungry after their ride . " what , there be only cold mutton ? well , an old soldier can eat cold mutton . and a good glass of my Lady Warrington 's own cordial , prepare with her own hand , will keep the cold wind out . delicious cordial ! Capital mutton ! our own , my dear General , " say the hospitable Baronet , " our own from the country , six year old if a day . we keep a plain table ; but all the Warringtons since the Conqueror have be remarkable for their love of mutton ; and our meal may look a little scanty , and be , for we be plain people , and I be oblige to keep my rascal of servant on board - wage . can not   give they seven - year - old mutton , you know . " 
 Sir Miles , in his nephew 's presence and hearing , describe to his wife and daughter George 's reception at court in such flattering term that George hardly know himself , or the scene at which he have be present , or how to look his uncle in the face , or how to contradict he before his family in the midst of the astonishing narrative he be relate . Lambert sit by for a while with open eye . he , too , have be at Kensington . he have see none of the wonder which Sir Miles describe . 
 " we be proud of you , dear George . we love you , my dear nephew we all love you , we be all proud of you " 
 " yes ; but I like Harry well , " say a little voice . 
 " not because you be wealthy ! screwby , take Master Miles to his governor . go , dear child . not because you be blest with great estate and an ancient name ; but because , George , you have put to good use the talent with which Heaven have adorn you ; because you have fight and bleed in your country 's cause , in your monarch 's cause , and as such be indeed worthy of the favour of the good of sovereign . General Lambert , you have kindly condescend to look in on a country family , and partake of our unpretende meal . I hope we may see you some day when our hospitality be a little less homely . yes , by George , General , you must and shall name a day when you and Mrs. Lambert , and your dear girl , will dine with we . I will take no refusal now , by George I will not , " bawl the knight . 
 " you will accompany we , I trust , to my drawing - room ? " say my lady , rise . 
 Mr. Lambert plead to be excuse ; but the lady on no account would let dear George go away . no , positively , he should not go . they want to make acquaintance with their cousin . they must hear about that dreadful battle and escape from the Indians . Tom Claypool come in and hear some of the story . Flora be listen to it with her handkerchief to her eye , and little Miles have just say 
 " why do you take your handkerchief , Flora ? you be not cry a bit . " 
 be a man of great humour , Martin Lambert , when he go home , could not help entertain his wife with an account of the new family with which he have make acquaintance . a certain can not word call humbug have lately come into vogue . will it be believe that the General use it to designate the family of this virtuous country gentleman ? he describe the eager hospitality of the father , the pompous flattery of the mother , and the daughter ' look of admiration ; the toughness and security of the mutton , and the abominable taste and odour of the cordial ; and we may be sure Mrs. Lambert contrast Lady Warrington 's recent behaviour to poor Harry with her present conduct to George . 
 " be this Miss Warrington really handsome ? " ask Mrs Lambent . 
 " yes ; she be very handsome indeed , and the most astounding flirt I have ever set eye on , " reply the General . 
 " the hypocrite ! I have no patience with such people ! " cry the lady . 
 to which the General , strange to say , only reply by the monosyllable " Bo ! " 
 " why do you say ' Bo ! ' Martin ? " ask the lady . 
 " I say ' Bo ! ' to a goose , my dear , " answer the General . 
 and his wife vow she do not know what he mean , or of what he be think , and the General say 
 " of course not . " 




 CHAPTER LIX . in which we be treat to a play 

 the real business of life , I fancy , can form but little portion of the novelist 's budget . when he be speak of the profession of arm , in which man can show courage or the reverse , and in treating of which the writer naturally have to deal with interesting circumstance , action , and character , introduce recital of danger , devotedness , heroic death , and the like , the novelist may perhaps venture to deal with actual affair of life : but otherwise , they scarcely can enter into our story . the main part of Ficulnus 's life , for instance , be spend in sell sugar , spice and cheese ; of Causidicus 's in pore over musty volume of black - letter law ; of Sartorius 's in sit , cross - legged , on a board after measure gentleman for coat and breech . what can a story - teller say about the professional existence of these man ? would a real rustical history of hobnail and eighteenpence a day be endurable ? in the day whereof we be write , the poet of the time choose to represent a shepherd in pink breech and a chintz waistcoat , dance before his flock , and play a flageolet tie up with a blue satin ribbon . I say , in reply to some objection which have be urge by potent and friendly critic , that of the actual affair of life the novelist can not be expect to treat with the almost single exception of war before name . but law , stockbroking , polemical theology , linen - drapery , apothecary - business , and the like , how can writer manage fully to develop these in their story ? all author can do , be to depict man out of their business in their passion , love , laughter , amusement , hatred , and what not and describe these as well as they can , take the business part for grant , and leave it as it be for subaudition . 
 thus , in talk of the present or the past world , I know I be only dangle about the theatre - lobby , coffee - house , ridottos , pleasure - haunt , fair - booth , and feasting- and fiddle - room of life ; that , meanwhile , the great serious past or present world be plod in its chamber , toil at its humdrum loom , or jog on its accustomed labour , and we be only see our character away from their work . Corydon have to cart the litter and thresh the barley , as well as to make love to Phillis ; Ancillula have to dress and wash the nursery , to wait at breakfast and on her miss , to take the child out , etc . , before she can have her brief sweet interview through the area - railing with Boopis , the policeman . all day long have his heel to beat the stale pavement before he have the opportunity to snatch the hasty kiss or the furtive cold pie . it be only at moment , and away from these labour , that we can light upon one character or the other ; and hence , though most of the person of whom we be write have doubtless their grave employment and avocation , it be only when they be disengage and away from their work , that we can bring they and the equally disengage reader together . 
 the macaroni and fine gentleman at White 's and Arthur 's continue to show poor Harry Warrington such a very cold shoulder , that he seek their society less and less , and the Ring and the Mall and the gaming - table know he no more . Madame de Bernstein be for her nephew 's brave the indifference of the world , and vow that it would be conquer , if he would but have courage to face it ; but the young man be too honest to wear a smile face when he be discontent ; to disguise mortification or anger ; to parry slight by adroit flattery or cunning impudence ; as many gentleman and gentlewoman must and do who wish to succeed in society . 
 " you pull a long face , Harry , and complain of the world 's treatment of you , " the old lady say . " Fiddlededee , sir ! everybody have to put up with impertinence : and if you get a box on the ear now you be poor and cast down , you must say nothing about it , bear it with a smile , and if you can , revenge it ten year after . Moi qui vous parle , sir ! do you suppose I have have no humble - pie to eat ? all of we in our turn be call upon to swallow it : and , now you be no long the Fortunate Youth , be the Clever Youth , and win back the place you have lose by your ill luck . go about more than ever . go to all the rout and party to which you be ask , and to more still . be civil to everybody to all woman especially . only of course take care to show your spirit , of which you have plenty . with economy , and by your brother 's , I must say , admirable generosity , you can still make a genteel figure . with your handsome person , sir , you can not   fail to get a rich heiress . Tenez ! you should go amongst the merchant in the City , and look out there . they will not   know that you be out of fashion at the Court end of the town . with a little management , there be not the least reason , sir , why you should not make a good position for yourself still . when do you go to see my Lady Yarmouth , pray ? why do you not improve that connexion ? she take a great fancy to you . I desire you will be constant at her ladyship 's evening , and lose no opportunity of pay court to she . " 
 thus the old woman who have love Harry so on his first appearance in England , who have be so eager for his company , and please with his artless conversation , be take the side of the world , and turn against he . instead of the smile and kiss with which the fickle old creature use once to greet he , she receive he with coldness ; she become peevish and patronising ; she cast gibe and scorn at he before her guest , make his honest face flush with humiliation , and awake the keen pang of grief and amazement in his gentle , manly heart . Madame de Bernstein 's servant , who use to treat he with such eager respect , scarcely pay he now any attention . my lady be often indispose or engage when he call on she ; her people do not press he to wait ; do not volunteer to ask whether he would stay and dine , as they use in the day when he be the Fortunate Youth and companion of the wealthy and great . Harry carry his woe to Mrs. Lambert . in a passion of sorrow he tell she of his aunt 's cruel behaviour to he . he be stricken down and dismay by the fickleness and heartlessness of the world in its treatment of he . while the good lady and her daughter would move to and fro , and busy themselves with the care of the house , our poor lad would sit glum in a window - seat , heart - sick and silent . 
 " I know you be the good people alive , " he would say to the lady , " and the kind , and that I must be the dull company in the world yes , that I be . " 
 " well , you be not very lively , Harry , " say Miss Hetty , who begin to command he , and perhaps to ask herself , " what ? be this the gentleman whom I take to be such a hero ? " 
 " if he be unhappy , why should he be lively ? " ask Theo , gently . " he have a good heart , and be pain at his friend ' desertion of he . sure there be no harm in that ? " 
 " I would have too much spirit to show I be hurt , though , " cry Hetty , clench her little fist . " and I would smile , though that horrible old paint woman box my ear . she be horrible , mamma . you think so yourself , Theo ! Own , now , you think so yourself ! you say so last night , and act she come in on her crutch , and grin round to the company . " 
 " I may not   like she , " say Theo , turn very red . " but there be no reason why I should call Harry 's aunt name before Harry 's face . " 
 " you provoke thing ; you be always right ! " cry Hetty , " and that be what make I so angry . indeed , Harry , it be very wrong of I to make rude remark about any of your relation . " 
 " I do not   care about the other , Hetty ; but it seem hard that this one should turn upon I . I have get to be very fond of she ; and you see , it make I mad , somehow , when people I be very fond of turn away from I , or act unkind to I . " 
 " suppose George be to do so ? " ask Hetty . you see , it be George and Hetty , and Theo and Harry , amongst they now . 
 " you be very clever and very lively , and you may suppose a number of thing ; but not that , Hetty , if you please , " cry Harry , stand up and look very resolute and angry . " you do not   know my brother as I know he or you would not   take such a liberty as to suppose my brother George could do anything unkind or unworthy ! " Mr. Harry be quite in a flush as he speak . 
 Hetty turn very white . then she look up at Harry , and then she do not say a single word . 
 then Harry say , in his simple way , before take leave , " I be very sorry , and I beg your pardon , Hetty , if I say anything rough , or that seem unkind ; but I always fight up if anybody say anything against George . " 
 Hetty do not answer a word out of her pale lip , but give he her hand , and drop a prim little curtsey . 
 when she and Theo be together at night , make curl - paper confidence , " oh ! " say Hetty , " I think it would be so happy to see he every day , and be so glad when papa say we be to stay in London ! and now I do see he , you see , I go on offend he . I can not   help offend he ; and I know he be not clever , Theo . but oh ! be not   he good , and kind , and brave ? do not   he look handsome when he be angry ? " 
 " you silly little thing , you be always try to make he look handsome , " Theo reply . 
 it be Theo and Hetty , and Harry and George , among these young people , then ; and I dare say the reason why General Lambert choose to apply the monosyllable " Bo " to the mother of his daughter , be as a rebuke to that good woman for the inveterate love of sentiment and propensity to match - making which belong to she   and every other woman in the world whose heart be worth a fig ; and as a hint that Madam Lambert be a goose if she fancy the two virginian lad be go to fall in love with the young woman of the Lambert house . Little Het might have her fancy ; little girl will ; but they get it over : " and you know , Molly "   which dear , soft - hearted Mrs. Lambert could not deny , " you fancy somebody else before you fancy I , " say the General ; but Harry have evidently not be smite by Hetty ; and now he be supersede , as it be , by have an eld brother over he , and could not even call the coat upon his back his own , Master Harry be no great catch . 
 " oh yes : now he be poor we will show he the door , as all the rest of the world do , I suppose , " say Mrs. Lambert . 
 " that be what I always do , be not   it , molly ? turn my back on my friend in distress ? " ask the General . 
 " no , my dear ! I be a goose , now , and that I own , Martin ! " say the wife , have recourse to the usual pocket - handkerchief . 
 " let the poor boy come to we and welcome : ours be almost the only house in this selfish place where so much can be say for he . he be unhappy , and to be with we put he at ease ; in God 's name let he be with we ! " say the kind - hearted officer . accordingly , whenever poor crestfallen Hal want a dinner , or an evening 's entertainment , Mr. Lambert 's table have a corner for he . so be George welcome , too . he go among the lambert , not at first with the cordiality which Harry feel for these people , and inspire among they : for George be cold in his manner , and more mistrustful of himself and other than his twin - brother : but there be a goodness and friendliness about the family which touch almost all people who come into frequent contact with they ; and George soon learn to love they for their own sake , as well as for their constant regard and kindness to his brother . he could not but see and own how sad Harry be , and pity his brother 's depression . in his sarcastic way , George would often take himself to task before his brother for come to life again , and say , " Dear Harry , I be George the Unlucky , though you have cease to be Harry the Fortunate . Florac would have do much well not to pass his sword through that Indian 's body , and to have leave my scalp as an ornament for the fellow 's belt . I say he would , sir ! at White 's the people would have respect you . our mother would have weep over I , as a defunct angel , instead of be angry with I for again supplant her favourite you be her favourite , you deserve to be her favourite : everybody 's favourite : only , if I have not come back , your favourite , Maria , would have insist on marry you ; and that be how the god would have revenge themselves upon you for your prosperity . " 
 " I never know whether you be laugh at I or yourself , George " say the brother . I never know whether you be serious or jesting . 
 " precisely my own case , Harry , my dear ! " say George . 
 " but this I know , that there never be a well brother in the world ; and never well people than the lambert . " 
 " never be truer word say ! " cry George , take his brother 's hand . 
 " and if I be unhappy , ' tis not your fault nor their fault nor perhaps mine , George , " continue the young . " ' tis fate , you see , ' tis the have nothing to do . I must work ; and how , George ? that be the question . " 
 " we will see what our mother say . we must wait till we hear from she , " say George . 
 " I say , George ! do you know , I do not   think I should much like go back to Virginia ? " say Harry , in a low , alarmed voice . 
 " what ! in love with one of the lass here ? " 
 " love them like sister with all my heart , of course , dearest , good girl ! but , have come out of that business , thank to you , I do not   want to go back , you know . no ! no ! it be not for that I fancy stay in Europe well than go home . but , you see , I do not   fancy hunting , duck - shooting , tobacco - planting , whist - playing , and go to sermon , over and over and over again , for all my life , George . and what else be there to do at home ? what on earth be there for I to do at all , I say ? that be what make I miserable . it would not matter for you to be a young son you be so clever you would make your way anywhere ; but , for a poor fellow like I , what chance be there ? until I do something , George , I shall be miserable , that be what I shall ! " 
 " have I not always say so ? art thou not come round to my opinion ? " 
 " what opinion , George ? you know pretty much whatever you think , I think , George ! " say the dutiful junior . 
 " that Florac have well have leave the Indian to take my scalp , my dear ! " 
 at which Harry burst away with an angry exclamation ; and they continue to puff their pipe in friendly union . 
 they live together , each go his own gait ; and not much intercourse , save that of affection , be carry on between they . Harry never would venture to meddle with George 's book , and would sit as dumb as a mouse at the lodging whilst his brother be study . they remove presently from the Court end of the town , Madame de Bernstein pishing and pshae at their change of residence . but George take a great fancy to frequent Sir Hans Sloane 's new reading - room and museum , just set up in Montagu House , and he take cheerful lodging in Southampton Row , Bloomsbury , look over the delightful field towards Hampstead , at the back of the Duke of Bedford 's garden . and Lord Wrotham 's family come to Mayfair , and Mr. Lambert have business which detain he in London , have to change his house , too , and engage furnish apartment in Soho , not very far off from the dwelling of our young man ; and it be , as we have say , with the lambert that Harry , night after night , take refuge . 
 George be with they often , too ; and , as the acquaintance ripen , he frequent their house with increase assiduity , find their company more to his taste than that of Aunt Bernstein 's polite circle of gambler , than Sir Miles Warrington 's port and mutton , or the daily noise and clatter of the coffee - house . and as he and the Lambert lady be alike stranger in London , they partook of its pleasure together , and , no doubt , go to Vauxhall and Ranelagh , to Marybone Gardens , and the play , and the Tower , and wherever else there be honest amusement to be have in those day . Martin Lambert love that his child should have all the innocent pleasure which he could procure for they , and Mr. George , who be of a most generous , open - handed disposition , like to treat his friend likewise , especially those who have be so admirably kind to his brother . 
 with all the passion of his heart Mr. Warrington love a play . he have never enjoy this amusement in Virginia , and only once or twice at Quebec , when he visit Canada ; and when he come to London , where the two house be in their full glory , I believe he think he never could have enough of the delightful entertainment . anything he like himself , he naturally wish to share amongst his companion . no wonder that he be eager to take his friend to the theatre , and we may be sure our young countryfolk be not unwilling . shall it be Drury Lane or Covent Garden , lady ? there be Garrick and Shakspeare at Drury Lane . well , will it be believe , the lady want to hear the famous new author whose piece be be play at Covent Garden ? 
 at this time a star of genius have arise , and be blaze with quite a dazzle brilliancy . the great Mr. John Home , of Scotland , have produce a tragedy , than which , since the day of the ancient , there have be nothing more classic and elegant . what have Mr. Garrick mean by refuse such a masterpiece for his theatre ? say what you will about Shakspeare ; in the work of that undoubted great poet   who have begin to grow vastly more popular in England since Monsieur Voltaire attack he   there be many barbarism that could not but shock a polite auditory ; whereas , Mr. Home , the modern author , know how to be refine in the very midst of grief and passion ; to represent death , not merely as awful , but graceful and pathetic ; and never condescend to degrade the majesty of the Tragic Muse by the ludicrous apposition of buffoonery and familiar punning , such as the eld playwright certainly have resort to . besides , Mr. Home 's performance have be admire in quarter so high , and by personage whose taste be know to be as elevate as their rank , that all Britons could not but join in the plaudit for which august hand have give the signal . such , it be say , be the opinion of the very good company , in the coffee - house , and amongst the wit about town . why , the famous Mr. Gray , of Cambridge , say there have not be for a hundred year any dramatic dialogue of such a true style ; and as for the poet 's native capital of Edinburgh , where the piece be first bring out , it be even say that the triumphant scot call out from the pit   in their dialect , " where be Wully Shakspeare noo ? " 
 " I should like to see the man who could beat Willy Shakspeare ? " say the General , laugh . 
 " mere national prejudice , " say Mr. Warrington . 
 " Beat Shakspeare , indeed ! " cry Mrs. Lambert . 
 " Pooh , pooh ! you have cry more over Mr. Sam Richardson than ever you do over Mr. Shakspeare , Molly ! " remark the General . " I think few woman love to read Shakspeare : they say they love it , but they do not . " 
 " oh , papa ! " cry three lady , throw up three pair of hand . 
 " well , then , why do you all three prefer Douglas ? and you , boy , who be such Tories , will you go see a play which be write by a Whig Scotchman , who be actually make prisoner at Falkirk ? " 
 " Relicta non bene parmula , " say Mr. Jack the scholar . 
 " Nay ; it be relicta bene parmula , " cry the General . " it be the Highlanders who fling their targe down , and make fierce work among we redcoat . if they have fight all their field as well as that , and young Perkin have not turn back from Derby " 
 " I know which side would be rebel , and who would be call the Young Pretender , " interpose George . 
 " Hush ! you must please to remember my cloth , Mr. Warrington , " say the General , with some gravity ; " and that the cockade I wear be a black , not a white one ! well , if you will not love Mr. Home for his politic , there be , I think , another reason , George , why you should like he . " 
 " I may have Tory fancy , Mr. Lambert , but I think I know how to love and honour a good Whig , " say George , with a bow to the General : " but why should I like this Mr. Home , sir ? " 
 " because , be a presbyterian clergyman , he have commit the heinous crime of write a play , and his brother - parson have bark out an excommunication at he . they take the poor fellow 's mean of livelihood away from he for his performance ; and he would have starve , but that the young Pretender on our side of the water have give he a pension . " 
 " if he have be persecute by the parson , there be hope for he , " say George , smile . " and henceforth I declare myself ready to hear his sermon . " 
 " Mrs. Woffington be divine in it , though not generally famous in tragedy . Barry be draw tear from all eye ; and Garrick be wild at have refuse the piece . girl , you must bring each half a dozen handkerchief ! as for mamma , I can not trust she ; and she positively must be leave at home . " 
 but mamma persist she would go ; and , if need be to weep , she would sit and cry her eye out in a corner . they all go to Covent Garden , then ; the most of the party duly prepare to see one of the masterpiece of the age and drama . could they not all speak long page of Congreve ; have they not weep and kindle over Otway and Rowe ? o ye past literary glory , that be to be eternal , how long have you be dead ? who know much more now than where your graf be ? poor , neglect Muse of the bygone theatre ! she pip for we , and we will not dance ; she tear her hair , and we will not weep . and the Immortals of our time , how soon shall they be dead and bury , think you ? how many will survive ? how long shall it be ere Nox et Domus Plutonia shall overtake they ? 
 so away go the pleased party to Covent Garden to see the tragedy of the immortal John Home . the lady and the General be convey in a glass coach , and find the young man in wait to receive they at the theatre door . hence they elbow their way through a crowd of torch - boy , and a whole regiment of footman . little Hetty fall to Harry 's arm in this expedition , and the blushing Miss Theo be hand to the box by Mr. George . Gumbo have keep the place until his master arrive , when he retire , with many bow , to take his own seat in the footman 's gallery . they have good place in a front box , and there be luckily a pillar behind which mamma could weep in comfort . and opposite they they have the honour to see the august hope of the empire , his Royal Highness George Prince of Wales , with the Princess Dowager his mother , whom the people greet with loyal , but not very enthusiastic , plaudit . that handsome man stand behind his Royal Highness be my Lord Bute , the Prince 's Groom of the Stole , the patron of the poet whose performance they have come to see , and over whose work the Royal party have already weep more than once . 
 how can we help it , if during the course of the performance , Mr. Lambert would make his joke and mar the solemnity of the scene ? at first , as the reader of the tragedy well know , the character be occupy in make a number of explanation . Lady Randolph explain how it be that she be so melancholy . married to Lord Randolph somewhat late in life , she own , and his lordship perceive , that a dead lover yet occupy all her heart ; and her husband be fain to put up with this dismal , second - hand regard , which be all that my lady can bestow . hence , an invasion of Scotland by the Danes be rather a cause of excitement than disgust to my lord , who rush to meet the foe , and forget the dreariness of his domestic circumstance . welcome , Vikings and norseman ! blow , northern blast , the invader ' keel to Scotland 's shore ! Randolph and other hero will be on the beach to give the foeman a welcome ! his lordship have no soon disappear behind the tree of the forest , but Lady Randolph begin to explain to her confidante the circumstance of her early life . the fact be , she have make a private marriage , and what would the confidante say , if , in early youth , she , Lady Randolph , have lose a husband ? in the cold bosom of the earth be lodge the husband of her youth , and in some cavern of the ocean lie her child and his ! 
 up to this the General behave with as great gravity as any of his young companion to the play ; but when Lady Randolph proceed to say , " alas ! hereditary evil be the cause of my misfortune , " he nudge George Warrington , and look so droll , that the young man burst out laugh . 
 the magic of the scene be destroy after that . these two gentleman go on crack joke during the whole of the subsequent performance , to their own amusement , but the indignation of their company , and perhaps of the people in the adjacent box . Young Douglas , in those day , use to wear a white satin ' shape " slash at the leg and body , and when Mr. Barry appear in this droll costume , the General vow it be the exact dress of the Highlanders in the late war . the Chevalier 's guard , he declare , have all white satin slash breech , and red boot " only they leave they at home , my dear , " add this wag . not one pennyworth of sublimity would he or George allow henceforth to Mr. Home 's performance . as for Harry , he sate in very deep meditation over the scene ; and when Mrs. Lambert offer he a penny for his thought , he say , " that he think , Young Norval , Douglas , what - d'ye - call-'em , the fellow in white satin who look as old as his mother be very lucky to be able to distinguish himself so soon . I wish I could get a chance , Aunt Lambert , " say he , drum on his hat ; on which mamma sigh , and Theo , smile , say , " we must wait , and perhaps the Danes will land . " 
 " how do you mean ? " ask simple Harry . 
 " oh , the Danes always land , pour qui scait attendre ! " say kind Theo , who have hold of her sister 's little hand , and , I dare say , feel its pressure . 
 she do not behave unkindly that be not in Miss Theo 's nature but somewhat coldly to Mr. George , on whom she turn she back , address remark , from time to time , to Harry . in spite of the gentleman 's scorn , the woman choose to be affect . a mother and son , meet in love and parting in tear , will always awaken emotion in female heart . 
 " look , papa ! there be an answer to all your joke ! " say Theo , point towards the stage . 
 at a part of the dialogue between Lady Randolph and her son , one of the grenadier on guard on each side of the stage , as the custom of those day be , could not restrain his tear , and be visibly weep before the side - box . 
 " you be right , my dear , " say papa . 
 " do not   I tell you she always be ? " interpose Hetty . 
 " yonder sentry be a well critic than we be , and a touch of nature master we all . " 
 " taman usque recurrit ! " cry the young student from college . 
 George feel abash somehow , and interested too . he have be sneer , and theo sympathising . her kindness be well nay , wise than his scepticism , perhaps . nevertheless , when , at the beginning of the fifth act of the play , young Douglas , draw his sword and look up at the gallery , bawl out 
  " Ye glorious star ! high heaven 's resplendent host ! 
  to whom I oft have of my lot complain , 
  hear and record my soul 's unaltered wish 
  living or dead , let I but be renowne ! 
  may Heaven inspire some fierce gigantic dane 
  to give a bold defiance to our host ! 
  before he speak it out , I will accept , 
  like Douglas conquer , or like Douglas die ! " 
 the god , to whom Mr. Barry appeal , salute this heroic wish with immense applause , and the General clap his hand prodigiously . his daughter be rather disconcert . 
 " this Douglas be not only brave , but he be modest ! " say papa . 
 " I own I think he need not have ask for a gigantic dane , " say Theo , smile , as Lady Randolph enter in the midst of the gallery thunder . 
 when the applause have subside , Lady Randolph be make to say 
  " my son , I hear a voice ! " 
 " I think she do hear a voice ! " cry papa . " why , the fellow be bellow like a bull of Bashan . " and the General would scarcely behave himself from thenceforth to the end of the performance . he say he be heartily glad that the young gentleman be put to death behind the scene . when Lady Randolph 's friend describe how her mistress have " fly like lightning up the hill , and plunge herself into the empty air , " Mr. Lambert say he be delighted to be rid of she . " and as for that story of her early marriage , " say he , " I have my very strong doubt about it . " 
 " Nonsense , Martin ! look , child ! their Royal Highnesses be move . " 
 the tragedy over , the Princess Dowager and the Prince be , in fact , retire ; though , I dare say , the latter , who be always fond of a farce , would have be far well pleased with that which follow than he have be with Mr. Home 's dreary tragic masterpiece . 




 CHAPTER LX . which treat of Macbeth , a supper , and a Pretty Kettle of 

 fish 
 when the performance be conclude , our friend take coach for Mr. Warrington 's lodging , where the Virginians have provide an elegant supper . Mrs. Lambert must have be on George 's right hand , so that we have but to settle the three place of the General , his son , and the Templar . 
 Mr. Spencer have be at the other theatre , where , on a former day , he have actually introduce George to the greenroom . the conversation about the play be resume , and some of the party persist in be delighted with it . 
 " as for what our gentleman say , sir , " cry Mrs. Lambert to Mr. Spencer , " you must not believe a word of it . ' tis a delightful piece , and my husband and Mr. George behave as ill as possible . " 
 " we laugh in the wrong place , and when we ought to have cry , " the General own , " that be the truth . " 
 " you cause all the people in the box about we to look round and cry ' hush ! ' you make the pit folk say , ' silence in the box , yonder ! ' such behaviour I never know , and quite blush for you , Mr. Lambert ! "

I be sorry that the poor mother should find her child , and lose he . I be sorry , too , papa , if I ought not   to have be sorry ! " add the young lady , with a smile .

 " I wish we could have have the fight , " say General Lambert , " the fight between little Norval and the gigantic Norwegian that would have be rare sport : and you should write , Jack , and suggest it to Mr. Rich , the manager . " 
 " I have not see that : but I see Slack and Broughton at Marybone Gardens ! " say Harry , gravely ; and wonder if he have say something witty , as all the company laugh so ? " it would require no giant , " he add , " to knock over yonder little fellow in the red boot . I , for one , could throw he over my shoulder . " 
 " Mr. Garrick be a little man . but there be time when he look a giant , " say Mr. Spencer . " how grand he be in Macbeth , Mr. Warrington ! how awful that dagger - scene be ! you should have see our host , lady ! I present Mr. Warrington , in the greenroom , to Mr. Garrick and Mrs. Pritchard , and Lady Macbeth do he the honour to take a pinch out of his box . " 
 " do the wife of the Thane of Cawdor sneeze ? " ask the General , in an awful voice . 
 " she thank Mr. Warrington , in tone so hollow and tragic , that he start back , and must have upset some of his rappee , for Macbeth sneeze thrice . " 
 " Macbeth , Macbeth , Macbeth ! " cry the General . 
 " and the great philosopher who be stand by Mr. Johnson , say , ' you must mind , Davy , lest thy sneeze should awaken Duncan ! ' who , by the way , be talk with the three witch as they sit against the wall . " 
 " what ! have you be behind the scene at the play ? oh , I would give world to go behind the scene ! " cry Theo . 
 " and see the rope pull , and smell the tallow - candle , and look at the pasteboard gold , and the tinsel jewel , and the paint old woman , Theo ? no . do not look too close , " say the sceptical young host , demurely drink a glass of hock . " you be angry with your papa and I . " 
 " Nay , George ! " cry the girl . 
 " Nay ? I say , yes ! you be angry with we because we laugh when you be disposed to be cry . if I may speak for you , sir , as well as myself , " say George   with a bow to his guest , General Lambert , " I think we be not inclined to weep , like the lady , because we stand behind the author 's scene of the play , as it be . look close up to the young hero , we see how much of he be rant and tinsel ; and as for the pale , tragical mother , that her pallor be white chalk , and her grief her pocket - handkerchief . own now , Theo , you think I very unfeeling ? " 
 " if you find it out , sir , without my own it , what be the good of my confessing ? " say Theo . 
 " suppose I be to die ? " go on George , " and you see Harry in grief , you would be see a genuine affliction , a real tragedy ; you would grieve too . but you would not   be affect if you see the undertaker in weeper and a black cloak ! " 
 " indeed , but I should , sir ! " say Mrs. Lambert ; " and so , I promise you , would any daughter of mine . " 
 " perhaps we might find weeper of our own , Mr. Warrington , " say Theo , " in such a case . " 
 " would you ? " cry George , and his cheek and Theo 's simultaneously flush up with red ; I suppose because they both see Hetty 's bright young eye watch they . 
 " the elder writer understand but little of the pathetic , " remark Mr. Spencer , the Temple wit . 
 " what do you think of Sophocles and Antigone ? " call out Mr. John Lambert . 
 " Faith , our wit trouble themselves little about he , unless an Oxford gentleman come to remind we of he ! I do not mean to go back far than Mr. Shakspeare , who , as you will all agree , do not understand the elegant and pathetic as well as the modern . have he ever approach Belvidera , or Monimia , or Jane Shore ; or can you find in his comic female character the elegance of Congreve ? " and the Templar offer snuff to the right and left . 
 " I think Mr. Spencer himself must have try his hand ? " ask some one . 
 " many gentleman of leisure have . Mr. Garrick , I own , have have a piece of mine and return it . " 
 " and I confess that I have four act of a play in one of my box , " say George . 
 " I will be bind to say it be as good as any of them , " whisper Harry to his neighbour . 
 " be it a tragedy or a comedy ? " ask Mrs. Lambert . 
 " oh , a tragedy , and two or three dreadful murder at least ! " George reply . 
 " let we play it , and let the audience look to their eye ! yet my chief humour be for a tyrant , " say the General . 
 " the tragedy , the tragedy ! go and fetch the tragedy this moment , Gumbo ! " call Mrs. Lambert to the black . Gumbo make a low bow and say , " Tragedy ? yes , madam . " 
 " in the great cowskin trunk , Gumbo , " George say , gravely . 
 Gumbo bow and say , " yes , sir , " with still superior gravity . 
 " but my tragedy be at the bottom of I do not   know how much linen , package , book , and boot , Hetty . " 
 " never mind , let we have it , and fle the linen out of window ! " cry Miss Hetty .

 the lady groan a comical " oh ! " and papa , perhaps more seriously , say , " let we be thankful for the escape . let we be think of go home too . our young gentleman have treat we nobly , and we will all drink a parting bumper to Madam Esmond Warrington of Castlewood , in Virginia . suppose , boy , you be to find a tall , handsome stepfather when you get home ? lady as old as she have be know to marry before now . " 
 " to Madam Esmond Warrington , my old schoolfellow ! " cry Mrs. Lambert . " I shall write and tell she what a pretty supper her son have give we : and , Mr. George , I will not   say how ill you behave at the play ! " and , with this last toast , the company take leave ; the General 's coach and servant , with a flambeau , be in wait to carry his family home . 
 after such an entertainment as that which Mr. Warrington have give , what could be more natural or proper than a visit from he to his guest , to inquire how they have reach home and rest ? why , their coach might have take the open country behind Montague House , in the direction of Oxford Road , and be waylay by footpad in the field . the lady might have catch cold or sleep ill after the excitement of the tragedy . in a word , there be no reason why he should make any excuse at all to himself or they for visit his kind friend ; and he shut his book early at the Sloane Museum , and perhaps think , as he walk away thence , that he remember very little about what he have be read . 
 pray what be the meaning of this eagerness , this hesitation , this pshaing and shilly - shallying , these doubt , this tremor as he knock at the door of Mr. Lambert 's lodging in Dean Street , and survey the footman who come to his summon ? do any young man read ? do any old one remember ? do any weary , worn , disappointed pulseless heart recall the time of its full beat and early throbbing ? it be ever so many hundred year since some of we be young ; and we forget , but do not all forget . no , madam , we remember with advantage , as Shakspeare 's Harry promise his soldier they should do if they survive Agincourt and that day of St. Crispin . wear old charger turn out to grass , if the trumpet sound over the hedge , may we not kick up our old heel , and gallop a minute or so about the paddock , till we be bring up roar ? I do not care for clown and pantaloon now , and think the fairy ugly , and her verse insufferable : but I like to see child at a pantomime . I do not dance , or eat supper any more ; but I like to watch Eugenio and Flirtilla twirl round in a pretty waltz , or Lucinda and Ardentio pull a cracker . burn your little finger , child ! blaze out little kindly flame from each other 's eye ! and then draw close together and read the motto   that old namby - pamby motto , so stale and so new !   I say , let her lip read it , and his construe it ; and so divide the sweetmeat , young people , and crunch it between you . I have no tooth . bitter almond and sugar disagree with I , I tell you ; but , for all that , shall not bonbon melt in the mouth ? 
 we follow John upstairs to the General 's apartment , and enter with Mr. George Esmond Warrington , who make a prodigious fine bow . there be only one lady in the room , seat near a window : there be not often much sunshine in Dean Street : the young lady in the window be no especial beauty : but it be spring - time , and she be bloom vernally . a bunch of fresh rose be flush in her honest cheek . I suppose her eye be violet . if we live a hundred year ago , and write in the Gentleman 's or the London Magazine , we should tell Mr. Sylvanus Urban that her neck be the lily , and her shape the nymph 's : we should write an acrostic about she , and celebrate our Lambertella in an elegant poem , still to be read between a neat new engrave plan of the city of Prague and the King of Prussia 's camp , and a map of Maryland and the Delaware county . 
 here be Miss Theo blushing like a rose . what could mamma have mean an hour since by insist that she be very pale and tired , and have good not come out to - day with the rest of the party ? they be go to pay their compliment to my Lord Wrotham 's lady , and thank they for the house in their absence ; and papa be at the Horse Guards . he be in great spirit . I believe he expect some command , though mamma be in a sad tremor lest he should again be order abroad . 
 " your brother and mine be go to see our little brother at his school at the Chartreux . my brother be both to be clergyman , I think , " Miss Theo continue . she be assiduously hem at some article of boyish wear apparel as she talk . a hundred year ago , young lady be not afraid either to make shirt , or to name they . mind , I do not   say they be the bad or the well for that plain stitching or plain speaking : and have not the least desire , my dear young lady , that you should make pudding or I should black boot . 
 so Harry have be with they ? " he often come , almost every day , " Theo say , look up in George 's face . " poor fellow ! he like we well than the fine folk , who do not   care for he now now he be no long a fine folk himself , " add the girl , smile . " why have you not set up for the fashion , and frequent the chocolate - house and the racecourse , Mr. Warrington ? " 
 " have my brother get so much good out of his gay haunt or his grand friend , that I should imitate he ? " 
 " you might at least go to Sir Miles Warrington ; sure his arm be open to receive you . her ladyship be here this morning in her chair , and to hear her praise of you ! she declare you be in a certain way to preferment . she say his Royal Highness the Duke make much of you at court . when you be a great man will you forget we , Mr. Warrington ? " 
 " yes , when I be a great man I will , Miss Lambert . " 
 " well ! Mr. George , then " 
 " Mr. George ! " 
 " when papa and mamma be here , I suppose there need be no mistering , " say Theo , look out of the window , ever so little frightened . " and what have you be do , sir ? read book , or write more of your tragedy ? be it go to be a tragedy to make we cry , as we like they , or only to frighten we , as you like they ? " 
 " there be plenty of killing , but , I fear , not much cry . I have not meet many woman . I have not be very intimate with those . I daresay what I have write be only take out of book or parody from poem which I have read and imitate like other young man . woman do not speak to I , generally ; I be say to have a sarcastic way which displease they . " 
 " perhaps you never care to please they ? " inquire Miss Theo , with a blush . 
 " I displease you last night ; you know I do ? " 
 " yes ; only it can not   be call displeasure , and afterwards think I be wrong . " 
 " do you think about I at all when I be away , Theo ? " 
 " yes , George that be , Mr. well , George ! I think you and papa be right about the play ; and , as you say , that it be no real sorrow , only affectation , which be move we . I wonder whether it be good or ill fortune to see so clearly ? Hetty and I agree that we would be very careful , for the future , how we allow ourselves to enjoy a tragedy . so , be careful when yours come ! what be the name of it ? " 
 " he be not christen . will you be the godmother ? the name of the chief character be " but at this very moment mamma and Miss Hetty arrive from their walk ; and mamma straightway begin protest that she never expect to see Mr. Warrington at all that day that be , she think he might come that is , it be very good of he to come , and the play and the supper of yesterday be all charming , except that Theo have a little headache this morning . 
 " I dare say it be well now , mamma , " say Miss Hetty . 
 " indeed , my dear , it never be of any consequence ; and I tell mamma so , " say Miss Theo , with a toss of her head . 
 then they fall to talk about Harry . he be very low . he must have something to do . he be always go to the Military Coffee - House , and perpetually pore over the King of Prussia 's campaign . it be not fair upon he , to bid he remain in London , after his deposition , as it be . he say nothing , but you could see how he regret his previous useless life , and feel his present dependence , by the manner in which he avoid his former haunt and associate . pass by the guard at St. James 's , with John Lambert , he have say to brother Jack , " why may not   I be a soldier too ? I be as tall as yonder fellow , and can kill with a fowling - piece as well as any man I know . but I can not   earn so much as sixpence a day . I have squander my own bread , and now I be eat half my brother 's . he be the good of brother , but so much the more shame that I should live upon he . do not   tell my brother , Jack Lambert . " " and my boy promise he would not   tell , " say Mrs. Lambert . no doubt . the girl be both out of the room when their mother make this speech to George Warrington . he , for his part , say he have write home to his mother that half his little patrimony , the other half likewise , if want , be at Harry 's disposal , for purchase a commission , or for any other project which might bring he occupation or advancement . 
 " he have get a good brother , that be sure . let we hope for good time for he , " sigh the lady . 
 " the Danes always come pour qui scait attendre , " George say , in a low voice .

 now Mr. George have be revolve a fine private scheme , which he think might turn to his brother 's advantage . after George 's presentation to his Royal Highness at Kensington , more person than one , his friend General Lambert include , have tell he that the Duke have inquire regard he , and have ask why the young man do not come to his levee . importunity so august could not but be satisfied . a day be appoint between Mr. Lambert and his young friend , and they go to pay their duty to his Royal Highness at his house in Pall Mall . 
 when it come to George 's turn to make a bow , the Prince be especially gracious ; he speak to Mr. Warrington at some length about Braddock and the war , and be apparently pleased with the modesty and intelligence of the young gentleman 's answer . George ascribe the failure of the expedition to the panic and surprise certainly , but more especially to the delay occasion by the rapacity , selfishness , and unfair dealing of the people of the colony towards the King 's troop who be come to defend they . " could we have move , sir , a month soon , the fort be certainly ours , and the little army have never be defeat , " Mr. Warrington say ; in which observation his Royal Highness entirely concur . 
 " I be tell you save yourself , sir , mainly by your knowledge of the french language , " the Royal Duke then affably observe . Mr. Warrington modestly mention how he have be in the french colony in his youth , and have opportunity of acquire that tongue . 
 the Prince   who have a great urbanity when well please , and the fine sense of humour   condescend to ask who have teach Mr. Warrington the language ; and to express his opinion , that , for the pronunciation , the french lady be by far the good teacher . 
 the young virginian gentleman make a low bow , and say it be not for he to gainsay his Royal Highness ; upon which the Duke be good enough to say   in a jocose manner   that Mr. Warrington be a sly dog . 
 Mr. W. remain respectfully silent , the Prince continue , most kindly : " I take the field immediately against the French , who , as you know , be threaten his Majesty 's Electoral dominion , if you have a mind to make the campaign with I , your skill in the language may be useful , and I hope we shall be more fortunate than poor Braddock ! " every eye be fix on a young man to whom so great a Prince offer so signal a favour . 
 and now it be that Mr. George think he would make his very cleverest speech . " Sir , " he say , " your Royal Highness 's most kind proposal do I infinite honour , but " 
 " but what , sir ? " say the Prince , star at he . 
 " but I have enter myself of the Temple , to study our law , and to fit myself for my duty at home . if my have be wound in the service of my country be any claim on your kindness , I would humbly ask that my brother , who know the french language as well as myself , and have far more strength , courage , and military genius , might be allow to serve your Royal Highness ; in the place of " 
 " enough , enough , sir ! " cry out the justly irritated son of the monarch . " what ? I offer you a favour , and you hand it over to your brother ? Wait , sir , till I offer you another ! " and with this the Prince turn his back upon Mr. Warrington , just as abruptly as he turn it on the French a few month afterwards . 
 " oh , George ! oh , George ! here be a pretty kettle of fish ! " groan General Lambert , as he and his young friend walk home together . 




 CHAPTER LXI . in which the Prince march up the Hill and down again 

 we understand the respectful indignation of all loyal Britons when they come to read of Mr. George Warrington 's conduct towards a gallant and gracious Prince , the beloved son of the good of monarchs , and the Captain - General of the british army . what an inestimable favour have not the young man slight ! what a chance of promotion have he not throw away ! will Esmond , whose language be always rich in blasphemy , employ his very strong curse in speak of his cousin 's behaviour , and express his delight that the confound young Mohock be cut his own throat . Cousin Castlewood say that a savage gentleman have a right to scalp himself if he like ; or perhaps , he add charitably , our cousin Mr. Warrington hear enough of the war - whoop in Braddock 's affair , and have no more stomach for fight . Mr. Will rejoice that the young brother have go to the deuce , and he rejoice to think that the elder be follow he . the first time he meet the fellow , will say , he should take care to let Mr. George know what he think of he . 
 " if you intend to insult George , at least you have well take care that his brother Harry be out of hearing ! " cry Lady Maria on which we may fancy more curse utter by Mr. Will , with regard to his twin kinsfolk . 
 " Ta , ta , ta ! " say my lord . " no more of this squabbling ! we can not   be all warrior in the family ! " 
 " I never hear your lordship lay claim to be one ! " say Maria . 
 " never , my dear ; quite the contrary ! will be our champion , and one be quite enough in the house . so I dare say with the two mohock ; George be the student , and Harry be the fight man . when you intend to quarrel , will , what a pity it be you have not George , instead of t'other , to your hand ! " 
 " your lordship 's hand be famous at piquet , " say Will 's mother . 
 " it be a pretty one , " say my lord , survey his finger , with a simper . " my Lord Hervey 's glove and mine be of a size . yes , my hand , as you say , be more fit for card than for war . your , my Lady Castlewood , be pretty dexterous , too . how I bless the day when you bestow it on my lamented father ! " in this play of sarcasm , as in some other game of skill , his lordship be not sorry to engage , have a cool head , and be able to beat his family all round . 
 Madame de Bernstein , when she hear of Mr. Warrington 's bevue , be exceedingly angry , storm , and scold her immediate household ; and would have scold George but she be grow old , and have not the courage of her early day . moreover , she be a little afraid of her nephew , and respectful in her behaviour to he . " you will never make your fortune at court , nephew ! " she groan , when , soon after his discomfiture , the young gentleman go to wait upon she . 
 " it be never my wish , madam , " say Mr. George , in a very stately manner . 
 " your wish be to help Harry ? you might hereafter have be of service to your brother , have you accept the Duke 's offer . prince do not love to have their favour refuse , and I do not   wonder that his Royal Highness be offend . " 
 " General Lambert say the same thing , " George confess , turn rather red ; " and I see now that I be wrong . but you must please remember that I have never see a court before , and I suppose I be scarce likely to shine in one . " 
 " I think possibly not , my good nephew , " say the aunt , take snuff . 
 " and what then ? " ask George . " I never have ambition for that kind of glory , and can make myself quite easy without it . when his Royal Highness speak to I most kindly , as I own my thought be , I shall make a very bad soldier , and my brother would be a very good one . he have a hundred good quality for the profession , in which I be deficient ; and would have serve a Commanding Officer far well than I ever could . say the Duke be in battle , and his horse be shoot , as my poor chief 's be at home , would he not be well for a beast that have courage and strength to bear he anywhere , than with one that could not carry his weight ? " 
 " Au fait . his Royal Highness 's charger must be a strong one , my dear ! " say the old lady . 
 " Expende Hannibalem , " mutter George , with a shrug . " our Hannibal weigh no trifle . " 
 " I do not   quite follow you , sir , and your Hannibal , " the Baroness remark . 
 " when Mr. Wolfe and Mr. Lambert remonstrate with I as you have do , madam , " George rejoin , with a laugh , " I make this same defence which I be make to you . I say I offer to the Prince the good soldier in the family , and the two gentleman allow that my blunder at least have some excuse . who know but that they may set I right with his Royal Highness ? the taste I have have of battle have show I how little my genius incline that way . we see the Scotch play which everybody be talk about t'other night . and when the hero , young Norval , say how he long to follow to the field some warlike lord , I think to myself , ' how like my Harry be to he , except that he doth not brag . ' Harry be pin now for a red coat , and if we do not   mind , will take the shilling . he have the map of Germany for ever under his eye , and follow the King of Prussia everywhere . he be not afraid of man or god . as for I , I love my book and quiet good , and to read about battle in Homer or Lucan . " 
 " then what make a soldier of you at all , my dear ? and why do you not send Harry with Mr. Braddock , instead of go yourself ? " ask Madame de Bernstein . 
 " my mother love her young son the good , " say George , darkly . " besides , with the enemy invade our country , it be my duty , as the head of our family , to go on the campaign . have I be a Scotchman twelve year ago , I should have be a " 
 " Hush , sir ! or I shall be more angry than ever ! " say the old lady , with a perfectly pleased face . 
 George 's explanation might thus appease Madame de Bernstein , an old woman whose principle we fear be but loose : but to the loyal heart of Sir Miles Warrington and his lady , the young man 's conduct give a severe blow indeed ! " I should have think , " her ladyship say , " from my sister Esmond Warrington 's letter , that my brother 's widow be a woman of good sense and judgment , and that she have educate her son in a become manner . but what , Sir Miles , what , my dear Thomas Claypool , can we think of an education which have result so lamentably for both these young man ? " 
 " the elder seem to know a power of Latin , though , and speak the French and the German too . I hear he with the Hanover Envoy , at the Baroness 's rout , " say Mr. Claypool . " the French he jabber quite easy : and when he be at a loss for the High Dutch , he and the Envoy begin in Latin , and talk away till all the room star . " 
 " it be not language , but principle , Thomas Claypool ! " exclaim the virtuous matron . " what must Mr. Warrington 's principle be , when he could reject an offer make he by his Prince ? can he speak the High Dutch ? so much the more ought he to have accept his Royal Highness 's condescension , and make himself useful in the campaign ! look at our son , look at Miles ! " 
 " hold up thy head , Miley , my boy ! " say papa . 
 " I trust , Sir Miles , that , as a member of the House of Commons , as an english gentleman , you will attend his Royal Highness 's levee to - morrow , and say , if such an offer have be make to we for that child , we would have take it , though our boy be but ten year of age . " 
 " Faith , Miley , thou wouldst make a good little drummer or fifer ! " say papa . " shouldst like to be a little soldier , Miley ? " 
 " anything , sir , anything ! a Warrington ought to be ready at any moment to have himself cut in piece for his sovereign ! " cry the matron , point to the boy ; who , as soon as he comprehend his mother 's proposal , protest against it by a loud roar , in the midst of which he be remove by Screwby . in obedience to the conjugal order , Sir Miles go to his Royal Highness 's levee the next day , and make a protest of his love and duty , which the Prince deign to accept , say : 
 " nobody ever suppose that Sir Miles Warrington would ever refuse any place offer to he . " 
 a compliment gracious indeed , and repeat everywhere by Lady Warrington , as show how implicitly the august family on the throne could rely on the loyalty of the Warringtons . 
 accordingly , when this worthy couple see George , they receive he with a ghastly commiseration , such as our dear relative or friend will sometimes extend to we when we have do something fatal or clumsy in life ; when we have come badly out of our lawsuit ; when we enter the room just as the company have be abuse we ; when our banker have break ; or we for our sad part have have to figure in the commercial column of the London Gazette ; when , in a word , we be guilty of some notorious fault , or blunder , or misfortune . who do not know that face of pity ? whose dear relation have not so deplore he , not dead , but live ? not your ? then , sir , if you have never be in scrape ; if you have never sow a handful of wild oat or two ; if you have always be fortunate , and good , and careful , and butter have never melt in your mouth , and an imprudent word have never come out of it ; if you have never sin and repent , and be a fool and be sorry then , sir , you be a wiseacre who will not   waste your time over an idle novel , and it be not de te that the fable be narrate at all . 
 not that it be just on Sir Miles 's part to turn upon George , and be angry with his nephew for refuse the offer of promotion make by his Royal Highness , for Sir Miles himself have agree in George 's view of pursue quite other than a military career , and it be in respect to this plan of her son 's that Madam Esmond have write from Virginia to Sir Miles Warrington . George have announce to she his intention of enter at the Temple , and qualify himself for the magisterial and civil duty which , in the course of nature , he would be call to fulfil ; nor could any one applaud his resolution more cordially than his uncle Sir Miles , who introduce George to a lawyer of reputation , under whose guidance we may fancy the young gentleman read leisurely . Madam Esmond from home signify her approval of her son 's course , fully agree with Sir Miles   to whom and his lady she beg to send her grateful remembrance   that the British Constitution be the envy of the world , and the proper object of every english gentleman 's admire study . the chief point to which George 's mother object be the notion that Mr. Warrington should have to sit down in the Temple dinner - ball , and cut at a shoulder of mutton , and drink small - beer out of tin pannikin , by the side of rough student who wear gown like the parish - clerk . George 's loyal young brother share too this repugnance . anything be good enough for he , Harry say ; he be a young son , and prepare to rough it ; but George , in a gown , and dining in a mess with three nobody 's son off dirty pewter platter !
