Harry never could relish this condescension on his brother 's part , or fancy George in his proper place at any except the high table ; and be sorry that a plan Madam Esmond hint at in her letter be not feasible viz . , that an application should be make to the Master of the Temple , who should be inform that Mr. George Warrington be a gentleman of most noble birth , and of great property in America , and ought only to sit with the very good company in the Hall . rather to Harry 's discomfiture , when he communicate his own and his mother 's idea to the gentleman 's new coffee - house friend , Mr. Spencer , Mr. Spencer receive the proposal with roar of laughter ; and I can not learn , from the Warrington paper , that any application be make to the Master of the Temple on this subject . besides his literary and historical pursuit , which be those he most especially love , Mr. Warrington study the law of his country , attend the court at Westminster , where he hear a Henley , a Pratt , a Murray , and those other great famous school of eloquence and patriotism , the two house of parliament . 
 gradually Mr. Warrington make acquaintance with some of the member of the House and the Bar ; who , when they come to know he , speak of he as a young gentleman of good part and good breeding , and in term so generally complimentary , that his good uncle 's heart relent towards he , and Dora and Flora begin once more to smile upon he . this reconciliation date from the time when his Royal Highness the Duke , after have be defeat by the French , in the affair of Hastenbeck , conclude the famous capitulation with the French , which his Majesty George II . refuse to ratify . his Royal Highness , as ' tis well know , fling up his commission after this disgrace , lay down his commander 's baton which , it must be confess , he have not wield with much luck or dexterity and never again appear at the head of army or in public life . the stout warrior would not allow a word of complaint against his father and sovereign to escape his lip ; but , as he retire with his wound honour , and as he would have no interest or authority more , nor any place to give , it may be suppose that Sir Miles Warrington 's anger against his nephew diminish as his respect for his Royal Highness diminish . 
 as our two gentleman be walk in St. James 's Park , one day , with their friend Mr. Lambert , they meet his Royal Highness in plain clothe and without a star , and make profound bow to the Prince , who be pleased to stop and speak to they . 
 he ask Mr. Lambert how he like my Lord Ligonier , his new chief at the Horse Guards , and the new duty there in which he be engage ? and , recognise the young man , with that fidelity of memory for which his royal race hath ever be remarkable , he say to Mr. Warrington : 
 " you do well , sir , not to come with I when I ask you in the spring . " 
 " I be sorry , then , sir , " Mr. Warrington say , make a very low reverence , " but I be more sorry now . " 
 on which the Prince say , " thank you , sir , " and , touch his hat , walk away . and the circumstance of this interview , and the discourse which pass at it , be relate to Mrs. Esmond Warrington in a letter from her young son , create so deep an impression in that lady 's mind , that she narrate the anecdote many hundred of time until all her friend and acquaintance know and , perhaps , be tired of it . 
 our gentleman go through the Park , and so towards the Strand , where they have business . and Mr. Lambert , point to the lion on the top of the Earl of Northumberland 's house at Charing Cross , say : 
 " Harry Warrington ! your brother be like yonder lion . " 
 " because he be as brave as one , " say Harry . 
 " because I respect virgin ! " say George , laugh . 
 " because you be a stupid lion . because you turn your back on the East , and absolutely salute the set sun . why , child , what earthly good can you get by be civil to a man in hopeless dudgeon and disgrace ? your uncle will be more angry with you than ever and so be I , sir . " but Mr. Lambert be always laugh in his waggish way , and , indeed , he do not look the least angry . 




 CHAPTER LXII . Arma Virumque 

 indeed , if Harry Warrington have a passion for military pursuit and study , there be enough of war stir in Europe , and enough talk in all society which he frequent in London , to excite and inflame he . though our own gracious Prince of the house of Hanover have be beat , the Protestant Hero , the King of Prussia , be fill the world with his glory , and win those astonishing victory in which I deem it fortunate on my own account that my poor Harry take no part ; for then his veracious biographer would have have to narrate battle the description whereof have be undertake by another pen . I be glad , I say , that Harry Warrington be not at Rossbach on that famous Gunpowder Fete - day , on the 5th of November , in the year 1757 ; nor at that tremendous slaughtering - match of Leuthen , which the prussian king play a month afterwards ; for these prodigious action will presently be narrate in other volume , which I and all the world be eager to behold . would you have this history compete with yonder book ? could my jaunty , yellow park - phaeton run counter to that grim chariot of thunder war ? could my meek little jog - trot Pegasus meet the shock of yon steed of foam bit and flame nostril ? dear , kind reader   with whom I love to talk from time to time , step down from the stage where our figure be perform , attire in the habit and use the parlance of past age , my kind , patient reader ! it be a mercy for both of we that Harry Warrington do not follow the king of the Borussians , as he be minded to do , for then I should have have to describe battle which Carlyle be go to paint ; and I do not   wish you should make odious comparison between I and that master . 
 Harry Warrington not only do not join the King of the Borussians , but he pin and chafe at not go . he lead a sulky useless life , that be the fact . he dangle about the military coffee - house . he do not care for read anything save a newspaper . his turn be not literary . he even think novel be stupid ; and , as for the lady cry their eye out over Mr. Richardson , he could not imagine how they could be move by any such nonsense . he use to laugh in a very hearty jolly way , but a little late , and some time after the joke be over . pray , why should all gentleman have a literary turn ? and do we like some of our friend the bad because they never turn a couplet in their life ? ruin , perforce idle , dependent on his brother for supply , if he read a book fall asleep over it , with no fitting work for his great strong hand to do how lucky it be that he do not get into more trouble ! why , in the case of Achilles himself , when he be send by his mamma to the court of King what - d'ye - call-'em in order to be put out of harm 's reach , what happen to he amongst a parcel of woman with whom he be make to idle his life away ? and how do Pyrrhus come into the world ? a powerful mettlesome young Achilles ought not to be lead - string by woman too much ; be out of his place dawdle by distaff or hand coffee - cup ; and when he be not fight , depend on it , be likely to fall into much bad mischief . 
 those soft - hearted woman , the two elder lady of the Lambert family , with whom he mainly consort , have an untiring pity and kindness for Harry , such as woman only and only a few of those can give . if a man be in grief , who cheer he ; in trouble , who console he ; in wrath , who soothe he ; in joy , who make he doubly happy ; in prosperity , who rejoice ; in disgrace , who back he against the world , and dress with gentle unguent and warm poultice the rankle wound make by the sling and arrow of outrageous Fortune ? who but woman , if you please ? you who be ill and sore from the buffet of Fate , have you one or two of these sweet physician ? return thank to the god that they have leave you so much of consolation . what gentleman be not more or less a Prometheus ? who have not his rock   ai , ai , his chain   ea , ea , and his liver in a deuce of a condition ? but the sea - nymph come the gentle , the sympathising ; they kiss our writhing foot ; they moisten our parch lip with their tear ; they do their bless good to console we Titans ; they do not   turn their back upon we after our overthrow . 
 now Theo and her mother be full of pity for Harry ; but Hetty 's heart be rather hard and seemingly savage towards he . she chafe that his position be not more glorious ; she be angry that he be still dependent and idle . the whole world be in arm , and could he not carry a musket ? it be harvest - time , and hundred of thousand of reaper be out with their flash sickle ; could he not use his , and cut down his sheaf or two of glory ? 
 " why , how savage the little thing be with he ! " say papa , after a scene in which , accord to she will not , Miss Hetty have be fire little shot into that quivering target which come and set itself up in Mrs. Lambert 's drawing - room every day . 
 " her conduct be perfectly abominable ! " cry mamma ; ' she deserve to be whip , and send to bed . " 
 " perhaps , mother , it be because she like he well than any of we do , " say Theo , " and it be for his sake that Hetty be angry . if I be fond of of some one , I should like to be able to admire and respect he always to think everything he do right and my gentleman well than all the gentleman in the world . " 
 " the truth be , my dear , " answer Mrs. Lambert , " that your father be so much well than all the world , he have spoil we . do you ever see any one to compare with he ? " 
 " very few , indeed , " own Theo , with a blush . 
 " very few . who be so good - temper ? " 
 " I think nobody , mamma , " Theo acknowledge . 
 " or so brave ? " 
 " why , I dare say Mr. Wolfe , or Harry , or Mr. George , be very brave . " 
 " or so learn and witty ? " 
 " I be sure Mr. George seem very learn , and witty too , in his way , " say Theo ; " and his manner be very fine you own they be . Madame de Bernstein say they be , and she hath see the world . indeed , Mr. George have a lofty way with he , which I do not   see in other people ; and , in read book , I find he choose the fine noble thing always , and love they in spite of all his satire . he certainly be of a satirical turn , but then he be only bitter against mean thing and people . no gentleman hath a more tender heart I be sure ; and but yesterday , after he have be talk so bitterly as you say , I happen to look out of window , and see he stop and treat a whole crowd of little child to apple at the stall at the corner . and the day before yesterday , when he be come and bring I the Moliere , he stop and give money to a beggar , and how charmingly , sure , he read the French ! I agree with he though about Tartuffe , though ' tis so wonderfully clever and lively , that a mere villain and hypocrite be a figure too mean to be make the chief of a great piece . Iago , Mr. George say , be near as great a villain ; but then he be not the first character of the tragedy , which be Othello , with his noble weakness . but what fine lady and gentleman Moliere represent so Mr. George think and but oh , I do not   dare to repeat the verse after he . " 
 " but you know they by heart , my dear ? " ask Mrs. Lambert . 
 and Theo reply , " oh yes , mamma ! I know they by ... nonsense ! " 
 I here fancy osculation , palpitation , and exit Miss Theo , blush like a rose . why have she stop in her sentence ? because mamma be look at she so oddly . and why be mamma look at she so oddly ? and why have she look after Mr. George when he be go away , and look for he when he be come ? ah , and why do cheek blush , and why do rose bloom ? Old Time be still a - flying . old spring and bud time ; old summer and bloom time ; old autumn and seed time ; old winter time , when the cracking , shiver old tree - top be bald or cover with snow . 
 a few minute after George arrive , Theo would come downstairs with a flutter heart , may be , and a sweet nosegay in her cheek , just cull , as it be , fresh in his honour ; and I suppose she must have be constantly at that window which command the street , and whence she could espy his generosity to the sweep , or his purchase from the apple - woman . but if it be Harry who knock , she remain in her own apartment with her work or her book , send her sister to receive the young gentleman , or her brother when the elder be at home from college , or Doctor Crusius from the Chartreux give the young leave to go home . and what good eye Theo must have have and often in the evening , too to note the difference between Harry 's yellow hair and George 's dark lock and between their figure , though they be so like that people continually be mistake one for the other brother . now it be certain that Theo never mistook one or t'other ; and that Hetty , for her part , be not in the least excited , or rude , or pert , when she find the black - haired gentleman in her mother 's drawing - room . 
 our friend could come when they like to Mr. Lambert 's house , and stay as long as they choose ; and , one day , he of the golden lock be sit on a couch there , in an attitude of more than ordinary idleness and despondency , when who should come down to he but Miss Hetty ? I say it be a most curious thing   though the girl would have go to the rack rather than own any collusion , that when Harry call , Hetty appear ; when George arrive , Theo somehow come ; and so , accord to the usual dispensation , it be Miss Lambert , junior , who now arrive to entertain the young Virginian . 
 after usual ceremony and compliment we may imagine that the lady say to the gentleman : 
 " and pray , sir , what make your honour look so glum this morning ? " 
 " ah , Hetty ! " say he , " I have nothing else to do but to look glum . I remember when we be boy and I a rare idle one , you may be sure I would always be ask my tutor for a holiday , which I would pass very likely swing on a gate , or make duck and drake over the pond , and those do - nothing day be always the most melancholy . what have I get to do now from morning till night ? " 
 " Breakfast , walk dinner , walk tea , supper , I suppose ; and a pipe of your Virginia , " say Miss Hetty , toss her head . 
 " I tell you what , when I go back with Charley to the Chartreux , t'other night , I have a mind to say to the master , ' teach I , sir . here be a boy know a deal more latin and Greek , at thirteen , than I do , who be ten year old . I have nothing to do from morning till night , and I might as well go to my book again , and see if I can repair my idleness as a boy . ' why do you laugh , Hetty ? " 
 " I laugh to fancy you at the head of a class , and call up by the master ! " cry Hetty . 
 " I should not   be at the head of the class , " Harry say , humbly . " George might be at the head of any class , but I be not a bookman , you see ; and when I be young neglect myself , and be very idle . we would not let our tutor cane we much at home , but , if we have , it might have do I good . " 
 Hetty drub with her little foot , and look at the young man sit before her strong , idle , melancholy . 
 " upon my word , it might do you good now ! " she be minded to say . " what do Tom say about the caning at school ? do his account of it set you long for it , pray ? " she ask . 
 " his account of his school , " Harry answer simply , " make I see that I have be idle when I ought to have work , and that I have not a genius for book , and for what be I good ? only to spend my patrimony when I come abroad , or to lounge at coffee - house or racecourse , or to gallop behind dog when I be at home . I be good for nothing , I be . " 
 " what , such a great , brave , strong fellow as you good for nothing ? " cry Het . " I would not confess as much to any woman , if I be twice as good for nothing ! " 
 " what be I to do ? I ask for leave to go into the army , and Madam Esmond do not answer I . ' tis the only thing I be fit for . I have no money to buy . have spend all my own , and so much of my brother 's , I can not and will not   ask for more . if my mother would but send I to the army , you know I would jump to go . " 
 " eh ! a gentleman of spirit do not want a woman to buckle his sword on for he or to clean his firelock ! what be that our papa tell we of the young gentleman at court yesterday ? Sir John Armytage " 
 " Sir John Armytage ? I use to know he when I frequent White 's and the club - house a fine , noble young gentleman , of a great estate in the North . " 
 " and engage to be married to a famous beauty , too Miss Howe , my Lord Howe 's sister but that , I suppose , be not an obstacle to gentleman ? " 
 " an obstacle to what ? " ask the gentleman . 
 " an obstacle to glory ! " say Miss Hetty . " I think no woman of spirit would say ' stay ! ' though she adore her lover ever so much , when his country say ' go ! ' Sir John have volunteer for the expedition which be prepare , and be at court yesterday his Majesty ask he when he would be ready to go ? ' tomorrow , please your Majesty , ' reply Sir John , and the king say , that be a soldier 's answer . my father himself be long to go , though he have mamma and all we brat at home . oh dear , oh dear ! why be not   I a man myself ? both my brother be for the Church ; but , as for I , I know I should have make a famous little soldier ! " and , so speak , this young person stride about the room , wear a most courageous military aspect , and look as bold as Joan of Arc . 
 Harry behold she with a tender admiration . " I think , " say he , " I would hardly like to see a musket on that little shoulder , nor a wound on that pretty face , Hetty . " 
 " wound ! who fear wound ? " cry the little maid . " musket ? if I could carry one , I would use it . you man fancy that we woman be good for nothing but to make pudding or stitch sampler . why be not   I a man , I say ? George be read to we yesterday out of Tasso look , here it be , and I think the verse apply to I . see ! here be the book , with the mark in it where we leave off . " 
 " with the mark in it ? " say Harry dutifully . 
 " yes ! it be about a woman who be disappointed because because her brother do not go to war , and she say of herself 
  " ' alas ! why do not Heaven these member frail 
  with lively force and vigour strengthen , so 
  that I this silken gown ... ' " 
 " silken gown ? " say downright Harry , with a look of inquiry . 
 " well , sir , I know ' tis but Calimanco ; but so it be in the book 
  " ' ... this silken gown and slender veil 
  Might for a breastplate and a helm forgo ; 
  then should not heat , nor cold , nor rain , nor hail , 
  nor storm that fall , nor blust're wind that blow , 
  withhold I ; but I would , both day and night , 
  in pitch field or private combat , fight ' 
 " Fight ? yes , that I would ! why be both my brother to be parson , I say ? one of my papa 's child ought to be a soldier ! " 
 Harry laugh , a very gentle , kind laugh , as he look at she . he feel that he would not like much to hit such a tender little warrior as that . 
 " why , " say he , hold a finger out , " I think here be a finger nigh as big as your arm . how would you stand up before a great , strong man ? I should like to see a man try and injure you , though ; I should just like to see he ! you little , delicate , tender creature ! do you suppose any scoundrel would dare to do anything unkind to you ? " and , excite by this flight of his imagination , Harry fall to walk up and down the room , too , chafe at the idea of any rogue of a Frenchman dare to be rude to Miss Hester Lambert . 
 it be a belief in this silent courage of his which subjugate Hetty , and this quality which she suppose he to possess , which cause she specially to admire he . Miss Hetty be no more bold , in reality , than Madam Erminia , whose speech she have be read out of the book , and about whom Mr. Harry Warrington never hear one single word . he may have be in the room when brother George be read his poetry out to the lady , but his thought be busy with his own affair , and he be entirely bewilder with your Clotildas and Erminias , and giant , and enchanter , and nonsense . no , Miss Hetty , I say and believe , have nothing of the virago in her composition ; else , no doubt , she would have take a fancy to a soft young fellow with a literary turn , or a genius for play the flute , accord to the law of contrast and nature provide in those case ; and who have not hear how great , strong man have an affinity for frail , tender little woman ; how tender little woman be attract by great , honest , strong man ; and how your burly hero and champion of war be constantly henpecke ? if Mr. Harry Warrington fall in love with a woman who be like Miss Lambert in disposition , and if he marry she without be conjurer , I think we may all see what the end will be . 
 so , whilst Hetty be fire her little sarcasm into Harry , he for a while scarcely feel that they be sting he , and let she shoot on without so much as take the trouble to shake the little arrow out of his hide . do she mean by her sneer and innuendo to rouse he into action ? he be too magnanimous to understand such small hint . do she mean to shame he by say that she , a weak woman , would don the casque and breastplate ? the simple fellow either melt at the idea of she be in danger , or at the notion of her fighting fall a - laugh . 
 " pray what be the use of have a strong hand if you only use it to hold a skein of silk for my mother ? " cry Miss Hester ; " and what be the good of be ever so strong in a drawing - room ? nobody want you to throw anybody out of window , Harry ! a strong man , indeed ! I suppose there be a strong at Bartholomew Fair . James Wolfe be not a strong man . he seem quite weakly and ill . when he be here last he be cough the whole time , and as pale as if he have see a ghost . " 
 " I never could understand why a man should be frighten at a ghost , " say Harry . 
 " pray , have you see one , sir ? " ask the pert young lady . 
 " no . I think I do once at home when we be boy ; but it be only Nathan in his night - shirt ; but I be not   frighten when I think he be a ghost . I believe there be no such thing . our nurse tell a pack of lie about them , " say Harry , gravely . " George be a little frightened ; but then he be " here he pause . 
 " then George be what ? " ask Hetty . 
 " George be different from I , that be all . our mother be a bold woman as ever you see , but she scream at see a mouse always do can not   help it . it be her nature . so , you see , perhaps my brother can not   bear ghost . I do not   mind them . " 
 " George always say you would have make a well soldier than he . " 
 " so I think I should , if I have be allow to try . but he can do a thousand thing well than I , or anybody else in the world . why do not   he let I volunteer on Braddock 's expedition ? I might have get knock on the head , and then I should have be pretty much as useful as I be now , and then I should not   have ruin myself , and bring people to point at I and say that I have disgrace the name of Warrington . why may not   I go on this expedition , and volunteer like Sir John Armytage ? oh , Hetty ! I be a miserable fellow that be what I be , " and the miserable fellow pace the room at double quick time . " I wish I have never come to Europe , " he groan out . 
 " what a compliment to we ! thank you , Harry ! " but presently , on an appealing look from the gentleman , she add , " be you be you think of go home ? " 
 " and have all Virginia jeering at I ! there be not a gentleman there that would not , except one , and he my mother do not   like . I should be ashamed to go home now , I think . you do not   know my mother , Hetty . I be not   afraid of most thing ; but , somehow , I be of she . what shall I say to she , when she say , ' Harry , where be your patrimony ? ' ' spend , mother , ' I shall have to say . ' what have you do with it ? ' ' waste it , mother , and go to prison after . ' ' who take you out of prison ? ' ' Brother George , ma'am , he take I out of prison ; and now I be come back , have do no good for myself , with no profession , no prospect , no nothing only to look after negro , and be scold at home ; or to go to sleep at sermon ; or to play at card , and drink , and fight cock at the tavern about . ' how can I look the gentleman of the country in the face ? I be ashamed to go home in this way , I say . I must and will do something ! what shall I do , Hetty ? ah ! what shall I do ? " 
 " do ? what do Mr. Wolfe do at Louisbourg ? Ill as he be , and in love as we know he to be , he do not   stop to be nurse by his mother , Harry , or to dawdle with his sweetheart . he go on the King 's service , and hath come back cover with honour . if there be to be another great campaign in America , papa say he be sure of a great command . " 
 " I wish he would take I with he , and that a ball would knock I on the head and finish I , " groan Harry . " you speak to I , Hetty , as though it be my fault that I be not in the army , when you know I would give give , forsooth , what have I to give ? yes ! my life to go on service ! " 
 " life indeed ! " say Miss Hetty , with a shrug of her shoulder . 
 " you do not   seem to think that of much value , Hetty , " remark Harry , sadly . " no more it be to anybody . I be a poor useless fellow . I be not even free to throw it away as I would like , be under order here and at home . " 
 " order indeed ! why under order ? " cry Miss Hetty . " be not   you tall enough , and old enough , to act for yourself , and must you have George for a master here , and your mother for a schoolmistress at home ? if I be a man , I would do something famous before I be two - and - twenty year old , that I would ! I would have the world speak of I . I would not   dawdle at apron - string . I would not   curse my fortune I would make it . I vow and declare I would ! " 
 now , for the first time , Harry begin to wince at the word of his young lecturer . 
 " no negro on our estate be more a slave than I be , Hetty , " he say , turn very red as he address she ; " but then , Miss Lambert , we do not   reproach the poor fellow for not be free . that be not   generous . at least , that be not   the way I understand honour . perhaps with woman it be different , or I may be wrong , and have no right to be hurt at a young girl tell I what my fault be . perhaps my fault be not my fault only my cursed luck . you have be talk ever so long about this gentleman volunteering , and that man win glory , and crack up their courage as if I have none of my own . I suppose , for the matter of that , I be as well provide as other gentleman . I do not   brag but I be not afraid of Mr. Wolfe , nor of Sir John Armytage , nor of anybody else that ever I see . how can I buy a commission when I have   spend my last shilling , or ask my brother for more who have already halve with I ? a gentleman of my rank can not   go a common soldier else , by Jupiter , I would ! and if a ball finish I , I suppose Miss Hetty Lambert would not   be very sorry . it be not   kind , Hetty I do not   think it of you . " 
 " what be it I have say ? " ask the young lady . " I have only say Sir John Armytage have volunteer , and Mr. Wolfe have cover himself with honour , and you begin to scold I ! how can I help it if Mr. Wolfe be brave and famous ? be that any reason you should be angry , pray ? " 
 " I do not   say angry , " say Harry , gravely . " I say I be hurt . " 
 " oh , indeed ! I think such a little creature as I be could not   hurt anybody ! I be sure ' tis mighty complimentary to I to say that a young lady whose arm be no big than your little finger can hurt such a great strong man as you ! " 
 " I scarce think you would try , Hetty , " the young man say . " you see , I be not use to this kind of welcome in this house . " 
 " what be it , my poor boy ? " ask kind Mrs. Lambert , look in at the door at this juncture , and find the youth with a very woeworn countenance . 
 " oh , we have hear the story before , mamma ! " say Hetty , hurriedly . " Harry be make his old complaint of have nothing to do . and he be quite unhappy ; and he be tell we so over and over again , that be all . " 
 " so be you hungry over and over again , my dear ! be that a reason why your papa and I should leave off give you dinner ? " cry mamma , with some emotion . " will you stay and have our , Harry ? ' tis just three o'clock ! " Harry agree to stay , after a few faint negation . " my husband din abroad . we be but three woman , so you will have a dull dinner , " remark Mrs. Lambert . 
 " we shall have a gentleman to enliven we , mamma , I dare say ! " say Madam Pert , and then look in mamma 's face with that admirable gaze of blank innocence which Madam Pert know how to assume when she have be specially and successfully wicked . 
 when the dinner appear . Miss Hetty come downstairs , and be exceedingly chatty , lively , and entertaining . Theo do not know that any little difference have occur   such , alas , my christian friend , will happen in the most charming family , do not know , I say , that anything have happen until Hetty 's uncommon sprightliness and gaiety rouse her suspicion . Hetty would start a dozen subject of conversation the King of Prussia , and the news from America ; the last masquerade , and the highwayman shoot near Barnet ; and when her sister , admire this volubility , inquire the reason of it , with her eye , 
 " oh , my dear , you need not nod and wink at I ! " cry Hetty . " Mamma ask Harry on purpose to enliven we , and I be talk until he begin , just like the fiddle at the playhouse , you know , Theo ! first the fiddle . then the play . pray begin , Harry ! " 
 " hester ! " cry mamma . 
 " I merely ask Harry to entertain we . you say yourself , mother , that we be only three woman , and the dinner would be dull for a gentleman ; unless , indeed , he choose to be very lively . " 
 " I be not that on most day and , Heaven know , on this day less than most , " say poor Harry . 
 " why on this day less than another ? Tuesday be as good a day to be lively as Wednesday . the only day when we must not   be lively be Sunday . well , you know it be , ma'am ! we must not   sing , nor dance , nor do anything on Sunday . " 
 and in this naughty way the young woman go on for the rest of the evening , and be compliment by her mother and sister when poor Harry take his leave . he be not ready of wit , and could not fle back the taunt which Hetty cast against he . Nay , have he be able to retort , he would have be silent . he be too generous to engage in that small war , and choose to take all hester 's sarcasm without an attempt to parry or evade they . very likely the young lady watch and admire that magnanimity , while she try it so cruelly . and after one of her fit of ill - behaviour , her parent and friend have not the least need to scold she , as she candidly tell they , because she suffer a great deal more than they would ever have have she , and her conscience punish she a great deal more severely than her kind elder would have think of do . I suppose she lie awake all that night , and toss and tumble in her bed . I suppose she wet her pillow with tear , and should not mind about her sobbing : unless it keep her sister awake ; unless she be unwell the next day , and the doctor have to be fetch ; unless the whole family be to be put to discomfort ; mother to choke over her dinner in flurry and indignation ; father to eat his roast - beef in silence and with bitter sauce ; everybody to look at the door each time it open , with a vague hope that Harry be come in . if Harry do not come , why at least do not George come ? think Miss Theo . 
 some time in the course of the evening come a billet from George Warrington , with a large nosegay of lilacs , per Mr. Gumbo . " ' I send my good duty and regard to Mrs. Lambert and the lady , ' " George say , " ' and humbly beg to present to Miss Theo this nosegay of lilacs , which she say she love in the early spring . you must not thank I for they , please , but the gardener of Bedford House , with whom I have make great friend by present he with some dried specimen of a virginian plant which some lady do not   think as fragrant as lilacs . 
 " ' I have be in the garden almost all the day . it be alive with sunshine and spring ; and I have be compose two scene of you know what , and polish the verse which the Page sing in the fourth act , under Sybilla 's window , which she can not hear , poor thing , because she have just have her head off . ' " 
 " provoking ! I wish he would not always sneer and laugh ! the verse be beautiful , " say Theo . 
 " you really think so , my dear ? how very odd ! " remark papa . 
 Little Het look up from her dismal corner with a faint smile of humour . Theo 's secret be a secret for nobody in the house , it seem . can any young people guess what it be ? our young lady continue to read : 
 " ' Spencer have ask the famous Mr. Johnson to breakfast to - morrow , who condescend to hear the play , and who will not , I hope , be too angry because my heroine undergo the fate of his in Irene . I have hear he come up to London himself as a young man with only his tragedy in his wallet . shall I ever be able to get mine play ? can you fancy the catcall music begin , and the pit hiss at that perilous part of the fourth act , where my executioner come out from the closet with his great sword , at the awful moment when he be call upon to amputate ? they say Mr. Fielding , when the pit hiss at a part of one of his piece , about which Mr. Garrick have warn he , say , ' hang they , they have find it out , have they ? ' and finish his punch in tranquillity . I suppose his wife be not in the box . there be some woman to whom I would be very unwilling to give pain , and there be some to whom I would give the good I have . ' " 
 " whom can he mean ? the letter be to you , my dear . I protest he be make love to your mother before my face ! " cry papa to Hetty , who only give a little sigh , put her hand in her father 's hand , and then withdraw it . 
 " ' to whom I would give the good I have . to - day it be only a bunch of lilac . to - morrow it may be what ? a branch of rue a sprig of bay , perhaps anything , so it be my good and my all . 
 " ' I have have a fine long day , and all to myself . what do you think of Harry play truant ? ' "   here we may imagine , what they call in France , or what they use to call , when man dare to speak or citizen to hear , sensation dans l'auditoire . 
 " ' I suppose Carpezan weary the poor fellow 's existence out . certain it be he have be miserable for week past ; and a change of air and scene may do he good . this morning , quite early , he come to my room , and tell I he have take a seat in the Portsmouth machine , and propose to go to the Isle of Wight , to the army there . ' " 
 the army ! Hetty look very pale at this announcement , and her mother continue : 
 " ' and a little portion of it , namely , the thirty - second regiment , be command by Lieutenant - Colonel Richmond Webb the nephew of the famous old General under whom my grandfather Esmond serve in the great war of Marlborough . Mr. Webb meet we at our uncle 's , accost we very politely , and give we an invitation to visit he at his regiment . let my poor brother go and listen to his darling music of fife and drum ! he bid I tell the lady that they should hear from he . I kiss their hand , and go to dress for dinner , at the Star and Garter , in Pall Mall . we be to have Mr. Soame Jenyns , Mr. Cambridge , Mr. Walpole , possibly , if he be not too fine to dine in a tavern ; a young Irishman , a Mr. Bourke , who they say be a wonder of eloquence and learn in fine , all the wit of Mr. Dodsley 's shop . quick , Gumbo , a coach , and my french grey suit ! and if gentleman ask I , ' who give you that sprig of lilac you wear on your heart - side ? ' I shall call a bumper , and give Lilac for a toast . ' " 
 I fear there be no more rest for Hetty on this night than on the previous one , when she have behave so mutinously to poor Harry Warrington . some secret resolution must have inspire that gentleman , for , after leave Mr. Lambert 's table , he pace the street for a while , and appear at a late hour in the evening at Madame de Bernstein 's house in Clarges Street . her ladyship 's health have be somewhat ailing of late , so that even her favourite rout be deny she , and she be sit over a quiet game of ecarte , with a divine of whom our last news be from a lock - up house hard by that in which Harry Warrington have be himself confine . George , at Harry 's request , have pay the little debt under which Mr. Sampson have suffer temporarily . he have be at his living for a year . he may have pay and contract ever so many debt , have be in and out of jail many time since we see he . for some time past he have be back in London stout and hearty as usual , and ready for any invitation to card or claret . Madame de Bernstein do not care to have her game interrupt by her nephew , whose conversation have little interest now for the fickle old woman . next to the very young , I suppose the very old be the most selfish . alas , the heart harden as the blood cease to run . the cold snow strike down from the head , and check the glow of feeling . who want to survive into old age after abdicate all his faculty one by one , and be san tooth , san eye , san memory , san hope , san sympathy ? how fare it with those patriarch of old who live for their nine century , and when be life 's condition so change that , after threescore year and ten , it become but a vexation and a burden ? 
 get no reply but yes and no to his brief speech , poor Harry sit a while on a couch opposite his aunt , who shrug her shoulder , have she back to her nephew , and continue her game with the chaplain . Sampson sit opposite Mr. Warrington , and could see that something disturb he . his face be very pale , and his countenance disturb and full of gloom . " something have happen to he , ma'am , " he whisper to the Baroness . 
 " bah ! " she shrug her shoulder again , and continue to deal her card . " what be the matter with you , sir , " she at last say , at a pause in the game , " that you have such a dismal countenance ? Chaplain , that last game make we even , I think ! " 
 Harry get up from his place . " I be go on a journey : I be come to bid you good - bye , aunt , " he say , in a very tragical voice . 
 " on a journey ! be you go home to America ? I mark the king , Chaplain , and play he . " 
 no , Harry say : he be not go to America yet go to the Isle of Wight for the present . 
 " indeed ! a lovely spot ! " say the Baroness . " Bon jour , mon ami , et bon voyage ! " and she kiss a hand to her nephew . 
 " I may not   come back for some time , aunt , " he groan out . 
 " indeed ! we shall be inconsolable without you ! unless you have a spade , Mr. Sampson , the game be mine . good - bye , my child ! no more about your journey at present : tell we about it when you come back ! " and she gaily bid he farewell . he look for a moment piteously at she , and be go . 
 " something grave have happen , madam , " say the chaplain . 
 " oh ! the boy be always get into scrape ! I suppose he have be fall in love with one of those country girl what be their name , lambert ? with whom he be ever dawdle about . he have be do no good here for some time . I be disappoint in he , really quite grieve about he I will take two card , if you please again ? quite grieve . what do you think they say of his cousin the Miss Warrington who make eye at he when she think he be a prize they say the King have remark she , and the Yarmouth be creve with rage . he , be ! those methodistical warrington ! they be not a bit less worldly than their neighbour ; and , old as he be , if the Grand Seignior throw his pocket - handkerchief , they will jump to catch it ! " 
 " ah , madam ; how your ladyship know the world ! " sigh the chaplain . " I propose , if you please ! " 
 " I have live long enough in it , Mr. Sampson , to know something of it . ' tis sadly selfish , my dear sir , sadly selfish ; and everybody be struggle to pass his neighbour ! no , I can not   give you any more card . you have not   the king ? I play queen , knave , and a ten , a sadly selfish world , indeed . and here come my chocolate ! " 
 the more immediate interest of the card entirely absorb the old woman . the door shut out her nephew and his care . under his hat , he bear they into the street , and pace the dark town for a while . 
 " Good God ! " he think , " what a miserable fellow I be , and what a spendthrift of my life I have be ! I sit silent with George and his friend . I be not clever and witty as he be . I be only a burthen to he ; and , if I would help he ever so much , do not   know how . my dear Aunt Lambert 's kindness never tire , but I begin to be ashamed of try it . why , even Hetty can not   help turn on I ; and when she tell I I be idle and should be do something , ought I to be angry ? the rest have leave I . there be my cousin and uncle and my lady my aunt , they have show I the cold shoulder this long time . they do not   even ask I to Norfolk when they go down to the country , and offer I so much as a day 's partridge - shooting . I can not   go to Castlewood after what have happen ; I should break that scoundrel William 's bone ; and , faith , be well out of the place altogether . " 
 he laugh a fierce laugh as he recall his adventure since he have be in Europe . money , friend , pleasure , all have pass away , and he feel the past like a dream . he stroll into White 's Chocolate - House , where the waiter have scarce see he for a year . the parliament be up . gentleman be away ; there be not even any play go on : not that he would join it , if there be . 
 he have but a few piece in his pocket ; George 's drawer be open , and he may take what money he like thence ; but very , very sparingly will he avail himself of his brother 's repeat invitation . he sit and drink his glass in moody silence . two or three officer of the Guards enter from St. James 's . he know they in former day , and the young man , who have be already din and drink on guard , insist on more drink at the club . the other battalion of their regiment be at Winchester : it be go on this great expedition , no one know whither , which everybody be talk about . curse fate that they do not belong to the other battalion ; and must stay and do duty in London and at Kensington ! there be Webb , who be of their regiment : he do well to exchange his company in the Coldstreams for the lieutenant - colonelcy of the thirty - second . he will be of the expedition . why , everybody be go ; and the young gentleman mention a score of name of man of the first birth and fashion who have volunteer . " it be not   Hanoverians this time , command by the big Prince , " say one young gentleman   whose relative may have be Tories forty year ago   " it be Englishmen , with the Guards at the head of them , and a Marlborough for a leader ! will the Frenchmen ever stand against they ? no , by George , they be irresistible . " and a fresh bowl be call , and loud toast be drunk to the success of the expedition . 
 Mr. Warrington , who be a cup too low , the young Guardsmen say , walk away when they be not steady enough to be able to follow he , think over the matter on his way to his lodging , and lie think of it all through the night . 
 " what be it , my boy ? " ask George Warrington of his brother , when the latter enter his chamber very early on a blushing May morning . 
 " I want a little money out of the drawer , " say Harry , look at his brother . " I be sick and tired of London . " 
 " good heaven ! can anybody be tired of London ? " George ask , who have reason for think it the most delightful place in the world . 
 " I be for one . I be sick and ill , " say Harry . 
 " you and Hetty have be quarrel ? " 
 " she do not   care a penny - piece about I , nor I for she neither , " say Harry , nod his head . " but I be ill , and a little country air will do I good , " and he mention how he think of go to visit Mr. Webb in the Isle of Wight , and how a Portsmouth coach start from Holborn . 
 " there be the till , Harry , " say George , point from his bed . " put your hand in , and take what you will . what a lovely morning , and how fresh the Bedford House garden look ! " 
 " God bless you , brother ! " Harry say . 
 " have a good time , Harry ! " and down go George 's head on the pillow again , and he take his pencil and notebook from under his bolster , and fall to polish his verse , as Harry , with his cloak over his shoulder and a little valise in his hand , walk to the inn in Holborn whence the Portsmouth machine start . 




 CHAPTER LXIII . Melpomene 

 George Warrington by no mean allow his legal study to obstruct his comfort and pleasure , or interfere with his precious health . Madam Esmond have point out to he in her letter that though he wear a student 's gown , and sate down with a crowd of nameless people to hall - commons , he have himself a name , and a very ancient one , to support , and could take rank with the first person at home or in his own country ; and desire that he would study as a gentleman , not a mere professional drudge . with this injunction the young man comply obediently enough : so that he may be say not to have belong to the rank and file of the law , but may be consider to have be a volunteer in her service , like some young gentleman of whom we have just hear . though not so exact as she since have become though she allow her disciple much more leisure , much more pleasure , much more punch , much more frequenting of coffee - house and holiday - making , than she admit nowadays , when she scarce give her votary time for amusement , recreation , instruction , sleep , or dinner the law a hundred year ago be still a jealous mistress , and demand a pretty exclusive attention . Murray , we be tell , might have be an Ovid , but he prefer to be Lord Chief Justice , and to wear ermine instead of bay . perhaps Mr. Warrington might have rise to a peerage and the woolsack , have he study very long and assiduously , have he be a dexterous courtier , and a favourite of attorney : have he be other than he be , in a word . he behave to Themis with a very decent respect and attention ; but he love letter more than law always ; and the black - letter of Chaucer be infinitely more agreeable to he than the gothic page of Hale and Coke . 
 letter be love indeed in those quaint time , and author be actually authority . gentleman appeal to Virgil or Lucan in the Courts or the House of Commons . what say Statius , Juvenal let alone Tully or Tacitus on such and such a point ? their reign be over now , the good old Heathens : the worship of Jupiter and Juno be not more out of mode than the cultivation of Pagan poetry or ethic . the age of economist and calculator have succeed , and Tooke 's Pantheon be desert and ridiculous . now and then , perhaps , a Stanley kill a kid , a Gladstone bang up a wreath , a Lytton burn incense , in honour of the Olympians . but what do they care at Lambeth , Birmingham , the Tower Hamlets , for the ancient rite , divinity , worship ? who the plague be the Muses , and what be the use of all that greek and latin rubbish ? what be Elicon , and who care ? who be Thalia , pray , and what be the length of she I ? be Melpomene 's name in three syllable or four ? and do you know from whose design I steal that figure of Tragedy which adorn the initial g of this chapter ? 
 now , it have be say how Mr. George in his youth , and in the long leisure which he enjoy at home , and during his imprisonment in the french fort on the bank of Monongahela , have while away his idleness by pay court to Melpomene ; and the result of their union be a tragedy , which have be omit in Bell 's Theatre , though I dare say it be no bad than some of the piece print there . Most young man pay their respect to the Tragic Muse first , as they fall in love with woman who be a great deal old than themselves . let the candid reader own , if ever he have a literary turn , that his ambition be of the very high , and that however , in his riper age , he might come down in his pretension , and think that to translate an ode of Horace , or to turn a song of Waller or Prior into decent alcaic or sapphic , be about the utmost of his capability , tragedy and epic only do his green unknowe youth engage , and no prize but the high be fit for he . 
 George Warrington , then , on come to London , attend the theatrical performance at both house , frequent the theatrical coffee - house , and hear the opinion of the critic , and might be see at the Bedford between the play , or sup at the Cecil along with the wit and actor when the performance be over . here he gradually become acquainted with the player and such of the writer and poet as be know to the public . the tough old Macklin , the frolicsome Foote , the vivacious Hippisley , the sprightly Mr. Garrick himself , might occasionally be see at these house of entertainment ; and our gentleman , by his wit and modesty , as well , perhaps , as for the high character for wealth which he possess , come to be very much like in the coffee - house circle , and find that the actor would drink a bowl of punch with he , and the critic sup at his expense with great affability . to be on term of intimacy with an author or an actor have be an object of delight to many a young man ; actually to hob and nob with Bobadil or Henry the Fifth or Alexander the Great , to accept a pinch out of Aristarchus 's own box , to put Juliet into her coach , or hand Monimia to her chair , be privilege which would delight most young man of a poetic turn ; and no wonder George Warrington love the theatre . then he have the satisfaction of think that his mother only half approve of play and playhouse , and of feast on fruit forbid at home . he give more than one elegant entertainment to the player , and it be even say that one or two distinguished genius have condescend to borrow money of he . 
 and as he polish and add new beauty to his masterpiece , we may be sure that he take advice of certain friend of his , and that they give he applause and counsel . Mr. Spencer , his new acquaintance , of the Temple , give a breakfast at his chamber in Fig Tree Court , when Mr. Warrington read part of his play , and the gentleman present pronounce that it have uncommon merit . even the learn Mr. Johnson , who be invite , be good enough to say that the piece have show talent . it war against the unity , to be sure ; but these have be violate by other author , and Mr. Warrington might sacrifice they as well as another . there be in Mr. W. 's tragedy a something which remind he both of Coriolanus and Othello . " and two very good thing too , sir ! " the author plead . " well , well , there be no doubt on that point ; and ' tis certain your catastrophe be terrible , just , and be in part true , be not the less awful , " remark Mr. Spencer . 
 now the plot of Mr. Warrington 's tragedy be quite full indeed of battle and murder . a favourite book of his grandfather have be the life of old George Frundsberg of Mindelheim , a colonel of foot - folk in the imperial service at Pavia fight , and during the war of the Constable Bourbon : and one of Frundsberg 's military companion be a certain Carpzow , or Carpezan , whom our friend select as his tragedy hero . his first act , as it at present stand in Sir George Warrington 's manuscript , be suppose to take place before a convent on the Rhine , which the Lutherans , under Carpezan , be besiege . a godless gang these Lutherans be . they have pull the beard of roman friar , and tear the veil of hundred of religious woman . a score of these be tremble within the wall of the convent yonder , of which the garrison , unless the expect succour arrive before midday , have promise to surrender . meanwhile there be armistice , and the sentry within look on with hungry eye , as the soldier and camp people gamble on the grass before the gate . twelve o'clock , ding , ding , dong ! it sound upon the convent bell . no succour have arrive . open gate , warder ! and give admission to the famous protestant hero , the terror of Turks on the Danube , and Papists in the Lombard plain Colonel Carpezan ! see , here he come , clothe in complete steel , his hammer of battle over his shoulder , with which he have batter so many infidel sconce , his flag display , his trumpet blow . " no rudeness , my man , " say Carpezan ; " the wine be your , and the convent larder and cellar be good : the church plate shall be melt : any of the garrison who choose to take service with Gaspar Carpezan be welcome , and shall have good pay . no insult to the religious lady ! I have promise they a safe - conduct , and he who lay a finger on they , hang ! mind that Provost Marshal ! " the Provost Marshal , a huge fellow in a red doublet , nod his head . 
 " we shall see more of that Provost Marshal , or executioner , " Mr. Spencer explain to his guest . 
 " a very agreeable acquaintance , I be sure , shall be delighted to meet the gentleman again ! " say Mr. Johnson , wag his head over his tea . " this scene of the mercenary , the camp follower , and their wild sport , be novel and stirring , Mr. Warrington , and I make you my compliment on it . the Colonel have go into the convent , I think ? now let we hear what he be go to do there . " 
 the Abbess , and one or two of her old lady , make their appearance before the conqueror . conqueror as he be , they hear he in their sacred hall . they have hear of his violent behaviour in conventual establishment before . that hammer , which he always carry in action , have smash many sacred image in religious house . pound and pound of convent plate be he know to have melt , the sacrilegious plunderer ! no wonder the Abbess - Princess of St. Mary 's , a lady of violent prejudice , free language , and noble birth , have a dislike to the lowborn heretic who lord it in her convent , and tell Carpezan a bit of her mind , as the phrase be . this scene , in which the lady get somewhat well of the Colonel , be like not a little by Mr. Warrington 's audience at the Temple . terrible as he might be in war , Carpezan be shake at first by the Abbess 's brisk opening charge of word ; and , conqueror as he be , seem at first to be conquer by his actual prisoner . but such an old soldier be not to be beat ultimately by any woman . " pray , madam , " say he , " how many lady be there in your convent , for whom my people shall provide conveyance ? " the Abbess , with a look of much trouble and anger , say that , " besides herself , the noble sister of Saint Mary 's House be twenty twenty - three . " she be go to say twenty - four , and now say twenty - three ? " ha ! why this hesitation ? " ask Captain Ulric , one of Carpezan 's gay officer . 
 the dark chief pull a letter from his pocket . " I require from you , madam , " he say sternly to the Lady Abbess , " the body of the noble lady Sybilla of Hoya . her brother be my favourite captain , slay by my side , in the Milanese . by his death , she become heiress of his land . ' Tis say a greedy uncle bring her hither ; and fast immure the lady against her will . the damsel shall herself pronounce her fate to stay a cloister sister of Saint Mary 's , or to return to home and liberty , as Lady Sybil , Baroness of . " ha ! the Abbess be greatly disturb by this question . she say , haughtily : " there be no Lady Sybil in this house : of which every inmate be under your protection , and swear to go free . the Sister Agnes be a nun profess , and what be her land and wealth revert to this order . " 
 " give I straightway the body of the Lady Sybil of Hoya ! " roar Carpezan , in great wrath . " if not , I make a signal to my reiter , and give you and your convent up to war . " 
 " Faith , if I lead the storm , and have my right , ' tis not my Lady Abbess that I will choose , " say Captain Ulric , " but rather some plump , smile , red - lipped maid like like " here , as he , the sly fellow , be look under the veil of the two attendant nun , the stern abbess cry , " Silence , fellow , with thy ribald talk ! the lady , warrior , whom you ask of I be pass away from sin , temptation , vanity , and three day since our Sister Agnes die . " 
 at this announcement Carpezan be immensely agitated . the Abbess call upon the chaplain to confirm her statement . ghastly and pale , the old man have to own that three day since the wretched Sister Agnes be bury . 
 this be too much ! in the pocket of his coat of mail Carpezan have a letter from Sister Agnes herself , in which she announce that she be go to be bury indeed , but in an oubliette of the convent , where she may either be keep on water and bread , or die starve outright . he seize the unflinche Abbess by the arm , whilst Captain Ulric lay hold of the chaplain by the throat . the Colonel blow a blast upon his horn : in rush his furious Lanzknechts from without . Crash , bang ! they knock the convent wall about . and in the midst of flame , scream , and slaughter , who be presently bring in by Carpezan himself , and faint on his shoulder , but Sybilla herself ? a little sister nun   that gay one with the red lip   have point out to the Colonel and Ulric the way to Sister Agnes 's dungeon , and , indeed , have be the mean of make her situation know to the Lutheran chief . 
 " the convent be suppress with a vengeance , " say Mr. Warrington . " we end our first act with the burning of the place , the roar of triumph of the soldiery , and the outcry of the nun . they have well go change their dress immediately , for they will have to be court lady in the next act as you will see . " here the gentleman talk the matter over . if the piece be to be do at Drury Lane , Mrs. Pritchard would hardly like to be Lady Abbess , as she doth but appear in the first act . Miss Pritchard might make a pretty Sybilla , and Miss Gates the attendant nun . Mr. Garrick be scarce tall enough for Carpezan though , when he be excited , nobody ever think of he but as big as a grenadier . Mr. Johnson own Woodward will be a good Ulric , as he play the Mercutio part very gaily ; and so , by one and t'other , the audience fancy the play already on the board , and cast the character . 
 in act the second , Carpezan have marry Sybilla . he have enrich himself in the war , have be ennoble by the Emperor , and live at his castle on the Danube in state and splendour . 
 but , truth to say , though marry , rich , and ennoble , the Lord Carpezan be not happy . it may be that in his wild life , as leader of condottieri on both side , he have commit crime which agitate his mind with remorse . it may be that his rough soldier - manner consort ill with his imperious highborn bride . she lead he such a life I be narrate as it be the Warrington manuscript , which be too long to print in entire taunt he with his low birth , his vulgar companion , whom the old soldier love to see about he , and so forth that there be time when he rather wish that he have never rescue this lovely , quarrelsome , wayward vixen from the oubliette out of which he fish she . after the bustle of the first act this be a quiet one , and pass chiefly in quarrel between the Baron and Baroness Carpezan , until horn blow , and it be announce that the young king of Bohemia and Hungary be come bunt that way . 
 Act III . be pass at Prague , whither his Majesty have invite Lord Carpezan and his wife , with noble offer of preferment to the latter . from Baron he shall be promote to be Count , from Colonel he shall be general - in - chief . his wife be the most brilliant and fascinating of all the lady of the court and as for Carpzoff 
 " oh , stay I have it I know your story , sir , now , " say Mr. Johnson . " ' tis in ' Meteranus , ' in the Theatrum Universum . I read it in Oxford as a boy Carpezanus or Carpzoff " 
 " that be the fourth act , " say Mr. Warrington . in the fourth act the young King 's attention towards Sybilla grow more and more marked ; but her husband , battle against his jealousy , long refuse to yield to it , until his wife 's criminality be put beyond a doubt and here he read the act , which close with the terrible tragedy which actually happen . be convinced of his wife 's guilt , Carpezan cause the executioner who follow his regiment to slay she in her own palace . and the curtain of the act fall just after the dreadful deed be do , in a side - chamber illuminate by the moon shin through a great oriel window , under which the King come with his lute , and play the song which be to be the signal between he and his guilty victim . 
 this song   writ in the ancient style , and repeat in the piece , be sing in the third act previously at a great festival give by the King and Queen   be pronounce by Mr. Johnson to be a happy imitation of Mr. Waller 's manner , and its gay repetition at the moment of guilt , murder , and horror , very much deepen the tragic gloom of the scene . 
 " but whatever come afterwards ? " he ask . " I remember in the Theatrum , Carpezan be say to have be take into favour again by Count Mansfield , and doubtless to have murder other folk on the reform side . " 
 here our poet have depart from historic truth . in the fifth act of Carpezan King Louis of Hungary and Bohemia   sufficiently terror - stricken , no doubt , by the sanguinary termination of his intrigue   have receive word that the Emperor Solyman be invade his hungarian dominion . enter two nobleman who relate how , in the council which the King hold upon the news , the injure Carpezan rush infuriate into the royal presence , break his sword , and fling it at the King 's foot along with a glove which he dare he to wear , and which he swear he would one day claim . after that wild challenge the rebel flee from Prague , and have not since be hear of ; but it be report that he have join the turkish invader , assume the turban , and be now in the camp of the Sultan , whose white tent glance across the river yonder , and against whom the King be now on his march . then the King come to his tent with his general , prepare his order of battle ; and dismiss they to their post , keep by his side an aged and faithful knight , his master of the horse , to whom he express his repentance for his past crime , his esteem for his good and injured Queen , and his determination to meet the day 's battle like a man . 
 " what be this field call ? " 
 " Mohacz , my liege ! " say the old warrior , add the remark that " Ere set of sun , Mohacz will see a battle bravely win . " 
 trumpet and alarm now sound ; they be the cymbal and barbaric music of the Janissaries : we be in the turkish camp , and yonder , surround by turbane chief , walk the Sultan Solyman 's friend , the conqueror of Rhodes , the redoubte Grand Vizier . 
 who be that warrior in an eastern habit , but with a glove in his cap ? ' Tis Carpezan . even Solyman know his courage and ferocity as a soldier . he know ; the ordnance of the hungarian host ; in what arm King Louis be weak : how his cavalry , of which the shock be tremendous , should be receive , and inveigle into yonder morass , where certain death may await they he pray for a command in the front , and as near as possible to the place where the traitor King Louis will engage . " ' tis well , " say the grim Vizier , " our invincible Emperor survey the battle from yonder tower . at the end of the day , he will know how to reward your valour . " the signal - gun fire the trumpet blow the turkish captain retire , vow death to the infidel , and eternal fidelity to the Sultan . 
 and now the battle begin in earnest , and with those various incident which the lover of the theatre knoweth . christian knight and turkish warrior clash and skirmish over the stage . continue alarm be sound . troop on both side advance and retreat . Carpezan , with his glove in his cap , and his dreadful hammer smashing all before he , rage about the field , call for King Louis . the renegade be about to slay a warrior who face he , but recognise young Ulric , his ex - captain , he drop the uplifted hammer , and bid he fly , and think of Carpezan . he be soften at see his young friend , and thinking of former time when they fight and conquer together in the cause of Protestantism . ulric bid he to return , but of course that be now out of the question . they fight . Ulric will have it , and down he go under the hammer . the renegade melt in sight of his wound comrade , when who appear but King Louis , his plume tear , his sword hack , his shield dent with a thousand blow which he have receive and deliver during the day 's battle . ha ! who be this ? the guilty monarch would turn away   perhaps Macbeth may have do so before , but Carpezan be on he . all his softness be go . he rag like a fury . " an equal fight ! " he roar . " a traitor against a traitor ! Stand , King Louis ! false king , false knight , false friend by this glove in my helmet , I challenge you ! " and he tear the guilty token out of his cap , and fling it at the King . 
 of course they set to , and the monarch fall under the terrible arm of the man whom he have injure . he die , utter a few incoherent word of repentance , and Carpezan , lean upon his murderous mace , utter a heartbroken soliloquy over the royal corpse . the turkish warrior have gather meanwhile : the dreadful day be their own . yonder stand the dark Vizier , surround by his Janissaries , whose bow and sword be tired of drink death . he survey the renegade stand over the corpse of the King . 
 " christian renegade ! " he say , " Allah have give we a great victory . the arm of the Sublime Emperor be everywhere triumphant . the Christian King be slay by you . " 
 " peace to his soul ! he die like a good knight , " gasp Ulric , himself die on the field . 
 " in this day 's battle , " the grim Vizier continue , " no man hath comport himself more bravely than you . you be make Bassa of Transylvania ! Advance bowman Fire ! " 
 an arrow quiver in the breast of Carpezan . 
 " Bassa of Transylvania , you be a traitor to your king , who lie murder by your hand ! " continue grim Vizier . " you contribute more than any soldier to this day 's great victory . ' tis thus my sublime Emperor meetly reward you . sound trumpet ! we march for Vienna to - night ! " 
 and the curtain drop as Carpezan , crawl towards his die comrade , kiss his hand , and gasp 
 " forgive I , Ulric ! " 
 when Mr. Warrington have finish read his tragedy , he turn round to Mr. Johnson , modestly , and ask , 
 " what say you , sir ? be there any chance for I ? " 
 but the opinion of this most eminent critic be scarce to be give , for Mr. Johnson have be asleep for some time , and frankly own that he have lose the latter part of the play . 
 the little auditory begin to hum and stir as the noise of the speaker cease . George may have be very nervous when he first commence to read ; but everybody allow that he read the last two act uncommonly well , and make he a compliment upon his matter and manner . perhaps everybody be in good - humour because the piece have come to an end . Mr. Spencer 's servant hand about refreshing drink . the Templars speak out their various opinion whilst they sip the negus . they be a choice band of critic , familiar with the pit of the theatre , and they treat Mr. Warrington 's play with the gravity which such a subject demand . 
 Mr. Fountain suggest that the Vizier should not say " Fire ! " when he bid the archer kill Carpezan , as you certainly do not   fire with a bow and arrow . a note be take of the objection . 
 Mr. Figtree , who be of a sentimental turn , regret that Ulric could not be save , and marry to the comic heroine . 
 " Nay , sir , there be an utter annihilation of the hungarian army at Mohacz , " say Mr. Johnson , " and Ulric must take his knock on the head with the rest . he could only be save by flight , and you would not   have a hero run away ! pronounce sentence of death against Captain Ulric , but kill he with honour of war . " 
 Messrs. Essex and Tanfield wonder to one another who be this queer - look pert whom Spencer have invite , and who contradict everybody ; and suggest a boat up the river and a little fresh air after the fatigue of the tragedy . 
 the general opinion be decidedly favourable to Mr. Warrington 's performance ; and Mr. Johnson 's opinion , on which he set a special value , be the most favourable of all . perhaps Mr. Johnson be not sorry to compliment a young gentleman of fashion and figure like Mr. W. " up to the death of the heroine , " he say , " I be frankly with you , sir . and I may speak , as a playwright who have kill my own heroine , and have my share of the plausus in the atro . to hear your own line nobly deliver to an applauding house , be indeed a noble excitement . I like to see a young man of good name and lineage who condescend to think that the Tragic Muse be not below his advance . it be to a sordid roof that I invite she , and I ask she to rescue I from poverty and squalor . happy you , sir , who can meet she upon equal term , and can afford to marry she without a portion ! " 
 " I doubt whether the great genius be not debase who have to make a bargain with Poetry , " remark Mr. Spencer . 
 " Nay , sir , " Mr. Johnson answer , " I doubt if many a great genius would work at all without bribe and necessity ; and so a man have well marry a poor Muse for good and all , for well or bad , than dally with a rich one . I make you my compliment to your play , Mr. Warrington , and if you want an introduction to the stage , shall be very happy if I can induce my friend Mr. Garrick to present you . " 
 " Mr. Garrick shall be his sponsor , " cry the florid Mr. Figtree . " melpomene shall be his godmother , and he shall have the witch ' caldron in Macbeth for a christening font . " 
 " Sir , I neither say font nor godmother ! " remark the man of letter . " I would have no play contrary to moral or religion nor , as I conceive , be Mr. Warrington 's piece otherwise than friendly to they . Vice be chastise , as it should be , even in king , though perhaps we judge of their temptation too lightly . revenge be punish as not to be lightly exercise by our limited notion of justice . it may have be Carpezan 's wife who pervert the King , and not the King who lead the woman astray . at any rate , Louis be rightly humiliate for his crime , and the Renegade most justly execute for his . I wish you a good afternoon , gentleman ! " and with these remark , the great author take his leave of the company . 
 towards the close of the reading , General Lambert have make his appearance at Mr. Spencer 's chamber , and have listen to the latter part of the tragedy . the performance over , he and George take their way to the latter 's lodging in the first place , and subsequently to the General 's own house , where the young author be expect , in order to recount the reception which his play have meet from his Temple critic . 
 at Mr. Warrington 's apartment in Southampton Row , they find a letter await George , which the latter place in his pocket unread , so that he might proceed immediately with his companion to Soho . we may be sure the lady there be eager to know about the Carpezan 's fate in the morning 's small rehearsal . 
 Hetty say George be so shy , that perhaps it would be well for all party if some other person have read the play . Theo , on the contrary , cry out : 
 " read it , indeed ! who can read a poem well than the author who feel it in his heart ? and George have his whole heart in the piece ! " 
 Mr. Lambert very likely think that somebody else 's whole heart be in the piece too , but do not utter this opinion to Miss Theo . 
 " I think Harry would look very well in your figure of a Prince , " say the General . " that scene where he take leave of his wife before depart for the war remind I of your brother 's manner not a little . " 
 " oh , papa ! surely Mr. Warrington himself would act the Prince 's part good ! " cry Miss Theo . 
 " and be deservedly slain in battle at the end ? " ask the father of the house . 
 " I do not say that , only that Mr. George would make a very good Prince , papa ! " cry Miss Theo . 
 " in which case he would find a suitable Princess , I have no doubt . what news of your brother Harry ? " 
 George , who have be think about theatrical triumph ; about monumentum aere perennius ; about lilac ; about love whisper and tenderly accept , remember that he have a letter from Harry in his pocket , and gaily produce it . 
 " let we hear what Mr. Truant say for himself , Aunt Lambert ! " cry George , break the seal . 
 why be he so disturbed , as he read the content of his letter ? why do the woman look at he with alarmed eye ? and why , above all , be Hetty so pale ? 
 " here be the letter , " say George , and begin to read it : 
 " RYDE , June 1 , 1758 . 
 " I do not tell my dearest George what I hop and intend , when I leave home on Wednesday . ' twas to see Mr. Webb at Portsmouth or the Isle of Wight , wherever his regiment be , and if need be to go down on my knee to he to take I as volunteer on the Expedition . I take boat from Portsmouth , where I learn that he be with our regiment incampt at the village of Ryde . be receive by he most kindly , and my petition grant out of hand . that be why I say our regiment . we be eight gentleman volunteer with Mr. Webb , all man of birth , and good fortune except poor I , who do not   deserve one . we be to mess with the officer ; we take the right of the collumn , and have always the right to be in front , and in an hour we embark on board his Majesty 's Ship the Rochester of 60 gun , while our Commodore 's , Mr. Howe 's , be the Essex , 70 . his squadron be about 20 ship , and I should think 100 transport at least . though ' tis a secret expedition , we make no doubt France be our destination where I hope to see my friend the Monsieurs once more , and win my colour , a la point de mon epee , as we use to say in Canada . perhaps my service as interpreter may be useful ; I speak the language not so well as some one I know , but well than most here . 
 " I scarce venture to write to our mother to tell she of this step . will you , who have a coxing tongue will wheadle any one , write to she as soon as you have finisht the famous tradgedy ? will you give my affectionate respect to dear General Lambert and lady ? and if any accident should happen , I know you will take care of poor Gumbo as belong to my dearest good George 's most affectionate brother , HENRY E. WARRINGTON . 
 " P.S. Love to all at home when you write , include Dempster , Mountain , and Fanny M. and all the people , and duty to my honoured mother , wish I have please she well . and if I say anything unkind to dear Miss Hester Lambert , I know she will forgive I , and pray God bless all . H. E. W. " 
 " to G. Esmond Warrington , Esq . , at Mr. Scrace 's House in Southampton Row , Opposite Bedford House Gardens , London . " 
 he have not read the last word with a very steady voice . Mr. Lambert sit silent , though not a little move . theo and her mother look at one another ; but Hetty remain with a cold face and a stricken heart . she think , " he be go to danger , perhaps to death , and it be I send he ! " 




 CHAPTER LXIV . in which Harry live to fight another Day 

 the trusty Gumbo could not console himself for the departure of his beloved master : at least , to judge from his tear and howl on first hear the news of Mr. Harry 's enlistment , you would have think the negro 's heart must break at the separation . no wonder he go for sympathy to the maid - servant at Mr. Lambert 's lodging . wherever that dusky youth be , he seek comfort in the society of female . their fair and tender bosom know how to feel pity for the poor African , and the darkness of Gumbo 's complexion be no more repulsive to they than Othello 's to Desdemona . I believe Europe have never be so squeamish in regard to Africa , as a certain other respected Quarter . Nay , some Africans witness the Chevalier de St. Georges , for instance have be notorious favourite with the fair sex . 
 so , in his humbler walk , be Mr. Gumbo . the Lambert servant weep freely in his company ; the maid kindly consider he not only as Mr. Harry 's man , but their brother . Hetty could not help laugh when she find Gumbo roar because his master have go a volumteer , as he call it , and have not take he . he be ready to save Master Harry 's life any day , and would have do it and have himself cut in twenty thousand hundred piece for Master Harry , that he would ! meanwhile , Nature must be support , and he condescend to fortify she by large supply of beer and cold meat in the kitchen . that he be greedy , idle , and tell lie , be certain ; but yet Hetty give he half a crown , and be especially kind to he . her tongue , that be will not to wag so pertly , be so gentle now , that you might fancy it have never make a joke . she move about the house mum and meek . she be humble to mamma ; thankful to John and Betty when they wait at dinner ; patient to Polly when the latter pull her hair in comb it ; long - suffering when Charley from school trod on her toe , or derange her workbox ; silent in papa 's company , oh , such a transmogrify little Hetty ! if papa have order she to roast the leg of mutton , or walk to church arm - in - arm with Gumbo , she would have make a curtsey , and say , " yes , if you please , dear papa ! " Leg of mutton ! what sort of meal be some poor volunteer have , with the cannon - ball fly about their head ? church ! when it come to the prayer in time of war , oh , how her knee smite together as she kneel , and hide her head in the pew ! she hold down her head when the parson read out , " thou shalt do no murder , " from the communion - rail , and fancy he must be look at she . how she think of all traveller by land or by water ! how she sicken as she run to the paper to read if there be news of the Expedition ! how she watch papa when he come home from his Ordnance Office , and look in his face to see if there be good news or bad ! be he well ? be he make a General yet ? be he wound and make a prisoner ? ah I ! or , perhaps , be both his leg take off by one shot , like that pensioner they see in Chelsea Garden t'other day ? she would go on wooden leg all her life , if his can but bring he safe home ; at least , she ought never to get up off her knee until he be return . " have not   you hear of people , Theo , " say she , " whose hair have grow grey in a single night ? I should not   wonder if mine do , should not   wonder in the least . " and she look in the glass to ascertain that phenomenon . 
 " Hetty dear , you use not to be so nervous when papa be away in Minorca , " remark Theo . 
 " ah , Theo ! one may very well see that George be not with the army , but safe at home , " rejoin Hetty ; whereat the eld sister blush , and look very pensive . Au fait , if Mr. George have be in the army , that , you see , would have be another pair of boot . meanwhile , we do not   intend to harrow anybody 's kind feeling any long , but may as well state that Harry be , for the present , as safe as any officer of the Life Guards at Regent 's Park Barracks .
