 the first expedition in which our gallant volunteer be engage may be call successful , but certainly be not glorious . the British Lion , or any other lion , can not always have a worthy enemy to combat , or a battle - royal to deliver . suppose he go forth in quest of a tiger who will not   come , and lay his paw on a goose , and gobble he up ? lion , we know , must live like any other animal . but suppose , advance into the forest in search of the tiger aforesaid , and bellow his challenge of war , he espy not one but six tiger come towards he ? this manifestly be not his game at all . he put his tail between his royal leg , and retreat into his own snug den as quickly as he may . be he to attempt to go and fight six tiger , you might write that Lion down an Ass . 
 now , Harry Warrington 's first feat of war be in this wise . he and about 13,000 other fight man embark in various ship and transport on the 1st of June , from the Isle of Wight , and at daybreak on the 5th the fleet stand in to the Bay of Cancale in Brittany . for a while he and the gentleman volunteer have the pleasure of examine the french coast from their ship , whilst the Commander - in - Chief and the Commodore reconnoitre the bay in a cutter . Cattle be see , and some dragoon , who trot off into the distance ; and a little fort with a couple of gun have the audacity to fire at his Grace of Marlborough and the Commodore in the cutter . by two o'clock the whole british fleet be at anchor , and signal be make for all the grenadi company of eleven regiment to embark on board flat - bottom boat and assemble round the Commodore 's ship , the Essex . meanwhile , Mr. Howe , hoist his broad pennant on board the Success frigate , go in as near as possible to shore , follow by the other frigate , to protect the landing of the troop ; and , now , with Lord George Sackville and General Dury in command , the gentleman volunteer , the grenadi company , and three battalion of guard pull to shore . 
 the gentleman volunteer could not do any heroic deed upon this occasion , because the French , who should have stay to fight they , run away , and the frigate have silence the fire of the little fort which have disturb the reconnaissance of the Commander - in - Chief , the army presently assault it , take the whole garrison prisoner , and shoot he in the leg . indeed , he be but one old gentleman , who gallantly have fire his two gun , and who tell his conqueror , " if every Frenchman have act like I , you would not have land at Cancale at all . " 
 the advanced detachment of invader take possession of the village of Cancale , where they lie upon their arm all night ; and our volunteer be joke by his comrade about his eagerness to go out upon the war - path , and bring in two or three scalp of Frenchmen . none such , however , fall under his tomahawk ; the only person slay on the whole day be a french gentleman , who be rid with his servant , and be surprise by volunteer Lord Downe , march in the front with a company of Kingsley 's . my Lord Downe offer the gentleman quarter , which he foolishly refuse , whereupon he , his servant , and the two horse , be straightway shot . 
 next day the whole force be land , and advance from Cancale to St. Malo . all the village be empty through which the troop pass , and the road be so narrow in many place that the man have to march single file , and might have be shoot down from behind the tall leafy hedge have there be any enemy to disturb they . 
 at nightfall the army arrive before St. Malo , and be salute by a fire of artillery from that town , which do little damage in the darkness . under cover of this , the british set fire to the ship , wooden building , pitch and tar magazine in the harbour , and make a prodigious conflagration that last the whole night . 
 this feat be achieve without any attempt on the part of the French to molest the british force : but , as it be confidently assert that there be a considerable french force in the town of St. Malo , though they would not   come out , his grace the Duke of Marlborough and my Lord George Sackville determine not to disturb the garrison , march back to Cancale again , and and so get on board their ship . 
 if this be not a veracious history , do not   you see that it would have be easy to send our Virginian on a more glorious campaign ? exactly four week after his departure from England , Mr. Warrington find himself at Portsmouth again , and address a letter to his brother George , with which the latter run off to Dean Street so soon as ever he receive it . 
 " glorious news , lady ! " cry he , find the Lambert family all at breakfast . " our champion have come back . he have undergo all sort of danger , but have survive they all . he have see dragon upon my word , he say so . " 
 " dragon ! what do you mean , Mr. Warrington ? " 
 " but not kill any he say so , as you shall hear . he write : 
 " ' dearest BROTHER I think you will be glad to hear that I be return , without any commission as yet ; without any wound or glory ; but , at any rate , alive and harty . on board our ship , we be almost as crowded as poor Mr. Holwell and his friend in their Black Hole at Calicutta . we have rough weather , and some of the gentleman volunteer , who prefer smooth water , grumble not a little . my gentleman 's stomach be dainty ; and after Braund 's cookery and White 's kick - shaw , they do not   like plain sailor 's rum and bisket . but I , who have be at sea before , take my ration and can of flip very contentedly : be determined to put a good face on everything before our fine english macaronis , and show that a Virginia gentleman be as good as the good of them . I wish , for the honour of old Virginia , that I have more to brag about . but all I can say in truth be , that we have be to France and come back again . why , I do not   think even your tragick pen could make anything of such a campaign as ours have be . we land on the 6 at Cancalle Bay , we see a few dragon on a hill ... ' 
 " there ! do I not tell you there be dragon ? " ask George , laugh . 
 " Mercy ! what can he mean by dragon ? " cry Hetty . 
 " Immense , long - tail monster , with steel scale on their back , who vomit fire , and gobble up a virgin a day . have not   you read about they in the Seven Champions ? " say papa . " see St. George 's flag , I suppose , they slunk off . " 
 " I have read of them , " say the little boy from Chartreux , solemnly . " they like to eat woman . one be go to eat Andromeda , you know , papa ; and Jason kill another , who be guard the apple - tree . " 
 " ... a few dragon on a hill , " George resume , " who ride away from we without engage . we sleep under canvass . we march to St. Malo , and burn ever so many privateer there . and we go on board shipp again , without ever cross sword with an enemy or meeting any except a few poor devil whom the troop plunder . well luck next time ! this have not   be very much nor particular glorious : but I have like it for my part . I have smell powder , besides a good deal of rosn and pitch we burn . I have   see the enemy ; have sleppt under canvass , and be dredful crowdid and sick at sea . I like it . my good compliment to dear Aunt Lambert , and tell Miss Hetty I be not   very much fritene when I see the french horse . your most affectionate brother , H. E. WARRINGTON . " 
 we hope Miss Hetty 's qualm of conscience be allay by Harry 's announcement that his expedition be over , and that he have so far take no hurt . far otherwise . Mr. Lambert , in the course of his official duty , have occasion to visit the troop at Portsmouth and the Isle of Wight , and George Warrington bear he company . they find Harry vastly improve in spirit and health from the excitement produce by the little campaign , quite eager and pleased to learn his new military duty , active , cheerful , and healthy , and altogether a different person from the listless moping lad who have dawdle in London coffee - house and Mrs. Lambert 's drawing - room . the troop be under canvas ; the weather be glorious , and George find his brother a ready pupil in a fine brisk open - air school of war . not a little amuse , the eld brother , arm - in - arm with the young volunteer , pace the street of the warlike city , recall his own brief military experience of two year back , and see here a much great army than that ill - fat one of which he have share the disaster . the expedition , such as we have see it , be certainly not glorious , and yet the troop and the nation be in high spirit with it . we be say to have humiliate the proud Gaul . we should have vanquish as well as humble he have he dare to appear . what valour , after all , be like british valour ? I dare say some such expression have be hear in later time . not that I would hint that our people brag much more than any other , or more now than formerly . have not these eye beheld the battle - ground of Leipzig , Jena , Dresden , Waterloo , Blenheim , Bunker 's Hill , New Orleans ? what heroic nation have not fight , have not conquer , have not run away , have not brag in its turn ? well , the british nation be much excited by the glorious victory of St. Malo . capture treasure be send home and exhibit in London . the people be so excited , that more laurel and more victory be demand , and the enthusiastic army go forth to seek some . 
 with this new expedition go a volunteer so distinguished , that we must give he precedence of all other amateur soldier or sailor . this be our sailor Prince , H.R.H. Prince Edward , who be convey on board the Essex in the ship 's twelve - oare barge , the standard of England fly in the bow of the boat , the Admiral with his flag and boat follow the Prince 's , and all the captain follow in seniority . 
 Away sail the fleet , Harry , in high health and spirit , wave his hat to his friend as they cheer from the shore . he must and will have his commission before long . there can be no difficulty about that , George think . there be plenty of money in his little store to buy his brother 's ensigncy ; but if he can win it without purchase by gallantry and good conduct , that be good . the colonel of the regiment report highly of his recruit ; man and officer like he . it be easy to see that he be a young fellow of good promise and spirit . 
 Hip , hip , huzzay ! what famous news be these which arrive ten day after the expedition have sail ? on the 7th and 8th of August his Majesty 's troop have effect a landing in the Bay des Marais , two league westward of Cherbourg , in the face of a large body of the enemy . awe by the appearance of british valour , that large body of the enemy have disappear . Cherbourg have surrender at discretion ; and the english colour be hoist on the three outlying fort . seven - and - twenty ship have be burn in the harbour , and a prodigious number of fine brass cannon take . as for your common iron gun , we have destroy them , likewise the basin   about which the mounseer brag so , and the two pier at the entrance to the harbour . 
 there be no end of jubilation in London ; just as Mr. Howe 's gun arrive from Cherbourg , come Mr. Wolfe 's colour capture at Louisbourg . the colour be take from Kensington to St Paul 's , escort by fourscore life - guard and fourscore horse - grenadier with officer in proportion , their standard , kettle - drum , and trumpet . at St. Paul 's they be receive by the Dean and chapter at the West Gate , and at that minute bang , bong , bung the Tower and Park gun salute they ! next day be the turn of the Cherbourg cannon and mortar . these be the gun we take . look at they with their carving and flaunting emblem their lily , and crown , and motto ! here they be , the Teneraire , the Malfaisant , the Vainqueur   the Vainqueur , indeed ! a pretty vainqueur of Britons ! , and ever so many more . how the people shout as the piece be trail through the street in procession ! as for Hetty and Mrs. Lambert , I believe they be of opinion that Harry take every one of the gun himself , drag they out of the battery , and destroy the artilleryman . he have immensely rise in the general estimation in the last few day . Madame de Bernstein have ask about he . Lady Maria have beg her dear cousin George to see she , and , if possible , give her news of his brother . George , who be quite the head of the family a couple of month since , find himself depose , and of scarce any account , in Miss Hetty 's eye at least . your wit , and your learning , and your tragedy , may be all very well ; but what be these in comparison to victory and brass cannon ? George take his deposition very meekly . they be fifteen thousand briton . why should they not march and take Paris itself ? nothing more probable , think some of the lady . they embrace ; they congratulate each other ; they be in a high state of excitement . for once , they long that Sir Miles and Lady Warrington be in town , so that they might pay her ladyship a visit , and ask , " what do you say to your nephew now , pray ? have he not take twenty - one fine brass cannon ; fling a hundred and twenty iron gun into the water , seize twenty - seven ship in the harbour , and destroy the basin and the two pier at the entrance ? " as the whole town rejoice and illuminate , so these worthy folk display brilliant red hanging in their cheek , and light up candle of joy in their eye , in honour of their champion and conqueror . 
 but now , I grieve to say , come a cloudy day after the fair weather . the appetite of our commander , grow by what it feed on , lead they to think they have not feast enough on the plunder of St. Malo ; and thither , after stay a brief time at Portsmouth and the Wight , the conqueror of Cherbourg return . they be land in the Bay of St. Lunar , at a distance of a few mile from the place , and march towards it , intend to destroy it this time . meanwhile the harbour of St. Lunar be find insecure , and the fleet move up to St. Cas , keep up its communication with the invade army . 
 now the British Lion find that the town of St. Malo which he have propose to swallow at a single mouthful be guard by an army of French , which the Governor of Brittany have bring to the succour of his good town , and the meditate coup - de - main being thus impossible , our leader march for their ship again , which lie duly await our warrior in the Bay of St. Cas . 
 hide , blush glory , hide St. Cas 's day ! as our troop be march down to their ship they become aware of an army follow they , which the french governor of the province have send from Brest . two - third of the troop , and all the artillery , be already embark , when the Frenchmen come down upon the remainder . four company of the first regiment of guard and the grenadi company of the army , face about on the beach to await the enemy , whilst the remain troop be carry off in the boat . as the French descend from the height round the bay , these guard and grenadier march out to attack they , leave an excellent position which they have occupy a great dyke raise on the shore , and behind which they might have resist to advantage . and now , eleven hundred man be engage with six nay , ten time their number ; and , after a while , break and make for the boat with a sauve qui peut ! seven hundred out of the eleven be kill , drown , or take prisoner the General himself be kill and , ah ! where be the volunteer ? 
 a man of peace myself , and little intelligent of the practice or the detail of war , I own I think less of the engage troop than of the people they leave behind . Jack the Guardsman and La Tulipe of the Royal Bretagne be face to face , and strive to knock each other 's brain out . Bon ! it be their nature to like the bear and lion and we will not say Heaven , but some power or other have make they so to do . but the girl of Tower Hill , who hang on Jack 's neck before he depart ; and the lass at Quimper , who give the Frenchman his brule - gueule and tobacco - box before he depart on the noir trajet ? what have you do , poor little tender heart , that you should grieve so ? my business be not with the army , but with the people leave behind . what a fine state Miss Hetty Lambert must be in , when she hear of the disaster to the troop and the slaughter of the grenadi company ! what grief and doubt be in George Warrington 's breast ; what commiseration in Martin Lambert 's , as he look into his little girl 's face and read her piteous story there ! Howe , the brave Commodore , row in his barge under the enemy 's fire , have rescue with his boat score and score of our fly people . More be drown ; hundred be prisoner , or shoot on the beach . among these , where be our Virginian ? 




 CHAPTER LXV . soldier 's Return 

 Great Powers ! will the vainglory of man , especially of Frenchmen , never cease ? will it be believe , that after the action of St. Cas a mere affair of cut off a rearguard , as you be aware they be so unfeeling as to fire away I do not   know how much powder at the invalide at Paris , and brag and bluster over our misfortune ? be there any magnanimity in hallooing and huzzaying because five or six hundred brave fellow have be catch by ten thousand on a seashore , and that fate have overtake they which be say to befall the hindmost ? I have a mind to design an authentic picture of the rejoicing at London upon our glorious success at St. Malo . I fancy the polished gun drag in procession by our gallant tar ; the stout horse - grenadier prance by ; the mob wave hat , roar cheer , pick pocket , and our friend in a balcony in Fleet Street look on and bless this scene of british triumph . but now that the french Invalides have be so vulgar as to imitate the Tower , and set up their St. Cas against our St. Malo , I scorn to allude to the stale subject . I say Nolo , not Malo : content , for my part , if Harry have return from one expedition and t'other with a whole skin . and have I ever say he be so much as bruised ? have I not , for fear of excite my fair young reader , say that he be as well as ever he have be in his life ? the sea air have brown his cheek , and the ball whistle by his side - curl have spar it . the ocean have wet his gaiter and other garment , without swallow up his body . he have , it be true , show the lapel of his coat to the enemy ; but for as short a time as possible , withdraw out of their sight as quick as might be . and what , pray , be lapel but revers ? coat have they , as well as man ; and our duty be to wear they with courage and good - humour . 
 " I can tell you , " say Harry , " we all have to run for it ; and when our line break , it be he who could get to the boat who be most lucky . the french horse and foot pursue we down to the sea , and be mingle among we , cut our man down , and bayonet they on the ground . poor Armytage be shoot in advance of I , and fall ; and I take he up and stagger through the surf to a boat . it be lucky that the sailor in our boat be not   afraid ; for the shot be whistle about their ear , break the blade of their oar , and riddle their flag with shot ; but the officer in command be as cool as if he have be drink a bowl of punch at Portsmouth , which we have one on landing , I can promise you . poor Sir John be less lucky than I . he never live to reach the ship , and the service have lose a fine soldier , and Miss Howe a true gentleman to her husband . there must be these casualty , you see ; and his brother get the promotion the baronetcy . " 
 " it be of the poor lady I be think , " say Miss Hetty   to whom haply our volunteer be tell his story ; " and the king . why do the King encourage Sir John Armytage to go ? a gentleman could not refuse a command from such a quarter . and now the poor gentleman be dead ! oh , what a state his Majesty must be in ! " 
 " I have no doubt his Majesty will be in a deep state of grief , " say papa , wag his head . 
 " now you be laugh ! do you mean , sir , that when a gentleman die in his service , almost at his foot , the King of England will not   feel for he ? " Hetty ask . " if I think that , I vow I would be for the Pretender ! " 
 " the sauce - box would make a pretty little head for Temple Bar , " say the General , who could see Miss Hetty 's meaning behind her word , and be aware in what a tumult of remorse , of consternation , of gratitude that the danger be over , the little heart be beat . " no , " say he , " my dear . be king to weep for every soldier , what a life you would make for they ! I think well of his Majesty than to suppose he so weak ; and , if Miss Hester Lambert get her Pretender , I doubt whether she would be any the happy . that family be never famous for too much feeling . " 
 " but if the King send Harry I mean Sir John Armytage actually to the war in which he lose his life , ought not   his Majesty to repent very much ? " ask the young lady . 
 " if Harry have fall , no doubt the court would have go into mourning : as it be , gentleman and lady be in coloured clothe yesterday , " remark the General . 
 " why should we not make bonfire for a defeat , and put on sackcloth and ash after a victory ? " ask George . " I protest I do not   want to thank Heaven for help we to burn the ship at Cherbourg . " 
 " yes you do , George ! not that I have a right to speak , and you be not   ever so much cleverer . but when your country win you be glad I know I be . when I run away before Frenchmen I be ashamed I can not   help it , though I do it , " say Harry . " it do not   seem to I right somehow that Englishmen should have to do it , " he add , gravely . and George smile ; but do not choose to ask his brother what , on the other hand , be the Frenchman 's opinion . 
 " ' tis a bad business , " continue Harry , gravely ; " but ' tis lucky ' twa no bad . the story about the French be , that their Governor , the Duke of Aiguillon , be rather what you call a moistened chicken . our whole retreat might have be cut off , only , to be sure , we ourselves be in a mighty hurry to move . the french local militia behave famous , I be happy to say ; and there be ever so many gentleman volunteer with them , who show , as they ought to do , in the front . they say the Chevalier of Tour d'Auvergne engage in spite of the Duke of Aiguillon 's order . officer tell we , who come off with a list of our prisoner and wound to General Bligh and Lord Howe . he be a lord now , since the news come of his brother 's death to home , George . he be a brave fellow , whether lord or commoner . " 
 " and his sister , who be to have marry poor Sir John Armytage , think what her state must be ! " sigh Miss Hetty , who have grow of late so sentimental . 
 " and his mother ! " cry Mrs. Lambert . " have you see her ladyship 's address in the paper to the elector of Nottingham ? ' Lord Howe be now absent upon the publick service , and Lieutenant - Colonel Howe with his regiment at Louisbourg , it rest upon I to beg the favour of your vote and interest that Lieutenant - Colonel Howe may supply the place of his late brother as your representative in Parliament . ' be not   this a gallant woman ? " 
 " a laconic woman , " say George . 
 " how can son help be brave who have be nurse by such a mother as that ? " ask the General . 
 our two young man look at each other . 
 " if one of we be to fall in defence of his country , we have a mother in Sparta who would think and write so too , " say George . 
 " if Sparta be anywhere Virginia way , I reckon we have , " remark Mr. Harry . " and to think that we should both of we have meet the enemy , and both of we be whip by he , brother ! " he add pensively . 
 Hetty look at he , and think of he only as he be the other day , totter through the water towards the boat , his comrade bleed on his shoulder , the enemy in pursuit , the shot fly round . and it be she who drive he into the danger ! her word provoke he . he never rebuke she now he be return . except when ask , he scarcely speak about his adventure at all . he be very grave and courteous with Hetty ; with the rest of the family especially frank and tender . but those taunt of hers wound he . " little hand ! " his look and demeanour seem to say , " thou shouldst not have be lift against I ! it be ill to scorn any one , much more one who have be so devoted to you and all your . I may not be over quick of wit , but in as far as the heart go , I be the equal of the good , and the good of my heart your family have have . " 
 Harry 's wrong , and his magnanimous endurance of it , serve he to regain in Miss Hetty 's esteem that place which he have lose during the previous month ' inglorious idleness . the respect which the fair pay to the brave she give he . she be no long pert in her answer , or sarcastic in her observation regard his conduct . in a word , she be a humiliate , an altered , an improve Miss Hetty . 
 and all the world seem to change towards Harry , as he towards the world . he be no long sulky and indolent : he no more despond about himself , or defy his neighbour . the colonel of his regiment report his behaviour as exemplary , and recommend he for one of the commission vacate by the casualty during the expedition . Unlucky as its termination be , it at least be fortunate to he . his brother - volunteer , when they come back to St. James 's Street , report highly of his behaviour . these volunteer and their action be the theme of everybody 's praise . have he be a general commanding , and slay in the moment of victory , Sir John Armytage could scarce have have more sympathy than that which the nation show he . the paper teem with letter about he , and man of wit and sensibility vie with each other in compose epitaph in his honour . the fate of his affianced bride be bewail . she be , as we have say , the sister of the brave Commodore who have just return from this unfortunate expedition , and succeed to the title of his eld brother , an officer as gallant as himself , who have just fall in America . 
 my Lord Howe be hear to speak in special praise of Mr. Warrington , and so he have a handsome share of the fashion and favour which the town now bestow on the volunteer . doubtless there be thousand of man employ who be as good as they but the English ever love their gentleman , and love that they should distinguish themselves ; and these volunteer be vote Paladins and hero by common accord . as our young nobleman will , they accept their popularity very affably . White 's and Almack 's illuminate when they return , and St. James 's embrace its young knight . Harry be restore to full favour amongst they . their hand be hold out eagerly to he again . even his relation congratulate he ; and there come a letter from Castlewood , whither Aunt Bernstein have by this time betake herself , contain praise of his valour , and a pretty little bank - bill , as a token of his affectionate aunt 's approbation . this be under my Lord Castlewood 's frank , who send his regard to both his kinsman , and an offer of the hospitality of his country - house , if they be minded to come to he . and besides this , there come to he a private letter through the post not very well spelt , but in a handwriting which Harry smile to see again , in which his affeetionate cousin , Maria Esmond , tell he she always love to hear his praise   which be in everybody 's mouth now , and sympathise in his good or evil fortune ; and that , whatever occur to he , she beg to keep a little place in his heart . Parson Sampson , she write , have preach a beautiful sermon about the horror of war , and the noble action of man who volunteer to face battle and danger in the service of their country . indeed , the chaplain write himself , presently , a letter full of enthusiasm , in which he salute Mr. Harry as his friend , his benefactor , his glorious hero . even Sir Miles Warrington despatch a basket of game from Norfolk : and one bird   shoot sit , with love to my cousin , have a string and paper round the leg , and be send as the first victim of young Miles 's fowling - piece . 
 and presently , with joy beam in his countenance , Mr. Lambert come to visit his young friend at their lodging in Southampton Row , and announce to they that Mr. Henry Warrington be forthwith to be gazette as Ensign in the Second Battalion of Kingsley 's , the 20th Regiment , which have be engage in the campaign , and which now at this time be form into a separate regiment , the 67th . its colonel be not with his regiment during its expedition to Brittany . he be away at Cape Breton , and be engage in capture those gun at Louisbourg , of which the arrival in England have cause such exultation . 




 CHAPTER LXVI . in which we go a - court 

 some of my amiable reader no doubt be in the custom of visit that famous garden in the Regent 's Park , in which so many of our fin , feather , four - foot fellow - creature be accommodate with board and lodge , in return for which they exhibit themselves for our instruction and amusement : and there , as a man 's business and private thought follow he everywhere , and mix themselves with all life and nature round about he , I find myself , whilst look at some fish in the aquarium , still actually think of our friend the Virginians . 
 one of the most beautiful motion - master I ever beheld , sweep through his green bath in harmonious curve , now turn his black glistening back to I , now exhibit his fair white chest , in every movement active and graceful , turn out to be our old homely friend the flounder , whom we have all gobble up out of his bath of water souchy at Greenwich , without have the slight idea that he be a beauty . 
 as be the race of man , so be the race of flounder . if you can but see the latter in his right element , you may view he agile , healthy , and comely : put he out of his place , and behold his beauty be go , his motion be disgraceful : he flap the unfeele ground ridiculously with his tail , and will presently gasp his feeble life out . take he up tenderly , ere it be too late , and cast he into his native Thames again but stop : I believe there be a certain proverb about fish out of water , and that other profound naturalist have remark on they before I . now Harry Warrington have be flounder for ever so long a time past , and out of his proper element . as soon as he find it , health , strength , spirit , energy , return to he , and with the tap of the epaulet on his shoulder he spring up an altered being . he delight in his new profession ; he engage in all its detail , and master they with eager quickness . have I the skill of my friend Lorrequer , I would follow the other Harry into camp , and see he on the march , at the mess , on the parade - ground ; I would have many a carouse with he and his companion ; I would cheerfully live with he under the tent ; I would knowingly explain all the manoeuvre of war , and all the detail of the life military . as it be , the reader must please , out of his experience and imagination , to fill in the colour of the picture of which I can give but meagre hint and outline , and , above all , fancy Mr. Harry Warrington in his new red coat and yellow facing , very happy to bear the King 's colour , and please to learn and perform all the duty of his new profession . 
 as each young man delight in the excellence of the other , and cordially recognise his brother 's superior quality , George , we may be sure , be proud of Harry 's success , and rejoice in his return good fortune . he write an affectionate letter to his mother in Virginia , recount all the praise which he have hear of Harry , and which his brother 's modesty , George know , would never allow he to repeat . he describe how Harry have win his own first step in the army , and how he , George , would ask his mother leave to share with she the expense of purchase a high rank for he . 
 nothing , say George , would give he a great delight , than to be able to help his brother , and the more so , as , by his sudden return into life , as it be , he have deprive Harry of an inheritance which he have legitimately consider as his own . labour under that misconception , Harry have indulge in great expense than he ever would have think of incur as a young brother ; and George think it be but fair , and as it be , as a thank - offering for his own deliverance , that he should contribute liberally to any scheme for his brother 's advantage . 
 and now , have conclude his statement respect Harry 's affair , George take occasion to speak of his own , and address his honoured mother on a point which very deeply concerned himself . she be aware that the good friend he and his brother have find in England be the good Mr. and Mrs. Lambert , the latter Madam Esmond 's schoolfellow of early year . where their own blood relation have be worldly and unfeele , these true friend have ever be generous and kind . the General be respect by the whole army , and belove by all who know he . no mother 's affection could have be more touching than Mrs. Lambert 's for both Madam Esmond 's child ; and now , write Mr. George , he himself have form an attachment for the eld Miss Lambert , on which he think the happiness of his life depend , and which he besought his honoured mother to approve . he have make no precise offer to the young lady or her parent ; but he be bind to say that he have make little disguise of his sentiment , and that the young lady , as well as her parent , seem favourable to he . she have be so admirable and exemplary a daughter to her own mother , that he feel sure she would do her duty by his . in a word , Mr. Warrington describe the young lady as a model of perfection , and express his firm belief that the happiness or misery of his own future life depend upon possess or lose she . why do you not produce this letter ? haply ask some sentimental reader , of the present Editor , who have say how he have the whole Warrington correspondence in his hand . why not ? because ' tis cruel to babble the secret of a young man 's love ; to overhear his incoherent vow and wild rapture , and to note , in cold blood , the secret it may be , the folly of his passion . shall we play eavesdropper at twilight embrasure , count sigh and hand - shake , bottle hot tear : lay our stethoscope on delicate young breast , and feel their heart - throb ? I protest , for one , love be sacred . wherever I see it   as one sometimes may in this world   shoot suddenly out of two pair of eye ; or glance sadly even from one pair ; or look down from the mother to the baby in her lap ; or from papa at his girl 's happiness as she be whirl round the room with the captain ; or from John Anderson , as his old wife come into the room the bonne vieille , the ever peerless among woman ; wherever we see that signal , I say , let we salute it . it be not only wrong to kiss and tell , but to tell about kiss . everybody who have be admit to the mystery , hush about it . down with he qui Deae sacrum vulgarit arcanae . beware how you dine with he , he will print your private talk : as sure as you sail with he , he will throw you over . 
 Whilst Harry 's love of battle have lead he to smell powder to rush upon reluctante dracone , and to carry wounded comrade out of fire , George have be pursue an amusement much more peaceful and delightful to he ; pen sonnet to his mistress 's eyebrow , mayhap ; pace in the darkness under her window , and watch the little lamp which shine upon she in her chamber ; find all sort of pretext for send little note which do not   seem to require little answer , but get they ; cull bit out of his favourite poet , and flower out of Covent Garden for somebody 's special adornment and pleasure ; walk to St. James 's Church , sing very likely out of the same Prayer - book , and never hear one word of the sermon , so much do other thought engross he ; be prodigiously affectionate to all Miss Theo 's relation to her little brother and sister at school ; to the elder at college ; to Miss Hetty , with whom he engage in gay passage of wit ; and to mamma , who be half in love with he herself , Martin Lambert say ; for if father be sometimes sulky at the appearance of the destine son - in - law , be it not a fact that mother become sentimental and , as it be , love their own love over again ? 
 Gumbo and Sady be for ever on the trot between Southampton Row and Dean Street . in the summer month all sort of junketing and pleasure - party be devise ; and there be countless proposal to go to Ranelagh , to Hampstead , to Vauxhall , to Marylebone Gardens , and what not . George want the famous tragedy copy out fair for the stage , and who can write such a beautiful italian hand as Miss Theo ? as the sheet pass to and fro they be accompany by little note of thank , of interrogation , of admiration , always . see , here be the packet , mark in Warrington 's neat hand , " T 's letter , 1758 - 9 . " shall we open they and reveal their tender secret to the public gaze ? those virgin word be whisper for one ear alone . year after they be write , the husband read , no doubt , with sweet pang of remembrance , the fond line address to the lover . it be a sacrilege to show the pair to public eye : only let kind reader be pleased to take our word that the young lady 's letter be modest and pure , the gentleman 's most respectful and tender . in fine , you see , we have say very little about it ; but , in these few last month , Mr. George Warrington have make up his mind that he have find the woman of woman . she may not   be the most beautiful . why , there be Cousin Flora , there be Coelia , and Ardelia , and a hundred more , who be ever so much more handsome : but her sweet face please he well than any other in the world . she may not   be the most clever , but her voice be the dearest and pleasantest to hear ; and in her company he be so clever himself ; he have such fine thought ; he use such eloquent word ; he be so generous , noble , witty , that no wonder he delight in it . and , in regard to the young lady , as thank Heaven I never think so ill of woman as to suppose they to be just , we may be sure that there be no amount of wit , of wisdom , of beauty , of valour , of virtue with which she do not endow her young hero . 
 when George 's letter reach home , we may fancy that it create no small excitement in the little circle round Madam Esmond 's fireside . so he be in love , and wish to marry ! it be but natural , and would keep he out of harm 's way . if he propose to unite himself with a well - breed christian young woman , Madam see no harm . 
 " I know they would be set their cap at he , " say Mountain . " they fancy that his wealth be as great as his estate . he do not say whether the young lady have money . I fear otherwise . " 
 " People would set their cap at he here , I dare say , " say Madam Esmond , grimly look at her dependant , " and try and catch Mr. Esmond Warrington for their own daughter , who be no rich than Miss Lambert may be . " 
 " I suppose your ladyship mean I ! " say Mountain . " my Fanny be poor , as you say ; and ' tis kind of you to remind I of her poverty ! " 
 " I say people would set their cap at he . if the cap fit you , tant pis ! as my papa use to say . " 
 " you think , madam , I be scheme to keep George for my daughter ? I thank you , on my word ! a good opinion you seem to have of we after the year we have live together ! " 
 " my dear mountain , I know you much well than to suppose you could ever fancy your daughter would be a suitable match for a gentleman of Mr. Esmond 's rank and station , " say Madam , with much dignity . 
 " Fanny Parker be as good as Molly Benson at school , and Mr. Mountain 's daughter be as good as Mr. Lambert 's ! " Mrs. Mountain cry out . 
 " then you do think of marry she to my son ! I shall write to Mr. Esmond Warrington , and say how sorry I be that you should be disappoint ! " say the mistress of Castlewood . and we , for our part , may suppose that Mrs. Mountain be disappoint , and have some ambitious view respect her daughter else , why should she have be so angry at the notion of Mr. Warrington 's marriage ? 
 in reply to her son , Madam Esmond write back that she be pleased with the fraternal love George exhibit ; that it be indeed but right in some measure to compensate Harry , whose expectation have lead he to adopt a more costly mode of life than he would have enter on have he know he be only a young son . and with respect to purchase his promotion , she would gladly halve the expense with Harry 's eld brother , be thankful to think his own gallantry have win he his first step . this bestowal of George 's money , Madam Esmond add , be at least much more satisfactory than some other extravagance to which she would not advert . 
 the other extravagance to which Madam allude be the payment of the ransom to the french captain 's family , to which tax George 's mother never would choose to submit . she have a determined spirit of her own , which her son inherit . his persistence she call pride and obstinacy . what she think of her own pertinacity , her biographer , who live so far from her time , do not pretend to say . only I dare say people a hundred year ago pretty much resemble their grandchild of the present date , and love to have their own way , and to make other follow it . 
 now , after pay his own ransom , his brother 's debt , and half the price for his promotion , George calculate that no inconsiderable portion of his private patrimony would be swallow up : nevertheless he make the sacrifice with a perfect good heart . his good mother always enjoin he in her letter to remember who his grandfather be , and to support the dignity of his family accordingly . she give he various commission to purchase good in England , and though she as yet have send he very trifle remittance , she allude so constantly to the exalt rank of the Esmonds , to her desire that he should do nothing unworthy of that illustrious family ; she advise he so peremptorily and frequently to appear in the first society of the country , to frequent the court where his ancestor have be accustom to move , and to appear always in the world in a manner worthy of his name , that George make no doubt his mother 's money would be forthcoming when his own run short , and generously obey her injunction as to his style of life . I find in the Esmond paper of this period , bill for genteel entertainment , tailor ' bill for court suit supply , and livery for his honour 's negro servant and chairman , horse - dealer ' receipt , and so forth ; and be thus lead to believe that the elder of our Virginians be also after a while live at a considerable expense . 
 he be not wild or extravagant like his brother . there be no talk of gambling or racehorse against Mr. George ; his table be liberal , his equipage handsome , his purse always full , the estate to which he be heir be know to be immense . I mention these circumstance because they may probably have influence the conduct both of George and his friend in that very matter concern which , as I have say , he and his mother have be just corresponding . the young heir of Virginia be travel for his pleasure and improvement in foreign kingdom . the queen , his mother , be in daily correspondence with his Highness , and constantly enjoin he to act as become his lofty station . there could be no doubt from her letter that she desire he should live liberally and magnificently . he be perpetually make purchase at his parent 's order . she have not settle as yet ; on the contrary , she have write out by the last mail for twelve new set of waggon harness , and an organ that should play fourteen specify psalm - tune : which article George dutifully order . she have not pay as yet , and might not to - day or to - morrow , but eventually , of course , she would : and Mr. Warrington never think of trouble his friend about these calculation , or discuss with they his mother 's domestic affair . they , on their side , take for grant that he be in a state of competence and ease , and , without be mercenary folk , Mr. and Mrs. Lambert be no doubt pleased to see an attachment grow up between their daughter and a young gentleman of such good principle , talent , family , and expectation . there be honesty in all Mr. Esmond Warrington 's word and action , and in his behaviour to the world a certain grandeur and simplicity , which show he to be a true gentleman . somewhat cold and haughty in his demeanour to stranger , especially towards the great , he be not in the least supercilious : he be perfectly courteous towards woman , and with those people whom he love , especially kind , amiable , lively , and tender . 
 no wonder that one young woman we know of get to think he the good man in all the world alas ! not even except papa . a great love feel by a man towards a woman make he well , as regard she , than all other man . we have say that George use to wonder himself when he find how witty , how eloquent , how wise he be , when he talk with the fair young creature whose heart have become all his .... I say we will not again listen to their love whisper . those soft word do not bear be write down . if you please good sir , or madam , who be sentimentally inclined lie down the book and think over certain thing for yourself . you may be ever so old now ; but you remember . it may be all dead and bury ; but in a moment , up it spring out of its grave , and look , and smile , and whisper as of yore when it cling to your arm , and drop fresh tear on your heart . it be here , and alive , do I say ? o far , far away ! o lonely hearth and cold ash ! here be the vase , but the rose be go ; here be the shore , and yonder the ship be moor ; but the anchor be up , and it have sail away for ever . 
 Et cetera , et cetera , et cetera . this , however , be mere sentimentality ; and as regard George and Theo , be neither here nor there . what I mean to say be , that the young lady 's family be perfectly satisfied with the state of affair between she and Mr. Warrington ; and though he have not as yet ask the decisive question , everybody else know what the answer would be when it come . 
 Mamma perhaps think the question be a long time come . 
 " Psha ! my dear ! " say the General . " there be time enough in all conscience . Theo be not much more than seventeen ; George , if I mistake not , be under forty ; and , besides , he must have time to write to Virginia , and ask mamma . " 
 " but suppose she refuse ? " 
 " that will be a bad day for old and young , " say the General , " let we rather say , suppose she consent , my love ? I can not   fancy anybody in the world refuse Theo anything she have set her heart on , " add the father : " and I be sure ' tis bent upon this match . " 
 so they all wait with the utmost anxiety until an answer from Madam Esmond should arrive ; and tremble lest the french privateer should take the packet - ship by which the precious letter be convey . 




 CHAPTER LXVII . in which a Tragedy be act , and two more be begin 

 James Wolfe , Harry 's new Colonel , come back from America a few week after our Virginian have join his regiment . Wolfe have previously be Lieutenant - Colonel of Kingsley 's , and a second battalion of the regiment have be form and give to he in reward for his distinguished gallantry and service at Cape Breton . Harry go with quite unfeigned respect and cordiality to pay his duty to his new commander , on whom the eye of the world begin to be turn now , the common opinion be that he be likely to become a great general . in the late affair in France , several officer of great previous repute have be try and find lamentably want . the Duke of Marlborough have show himself no worthy descendant of his great ancestor . about my Lord George Sackville 's military genius there be doubt , even before his unhappy behaviour at Minden prevent a great victory . the nation be long for military glory , and the Minister be anxious to find a general who might gratify the eager desire of the people . Mr. Wolfe 's and Mr. Lambert 's business keep they both in London , the friendly intercourse between those officer be renew , no one be more delighted than Lambert at his young friend 's good fortune . 
 Harry , when he be away from his duty , be never tired of hear Mr. Wolfe 's detail of the military operation of the last year , about which Wolfe talk very freely and openly . whatever thought be in his mind , he appear to have speak it out generously . he have that heroic simplicity which distinguish Nelson afterwards : he talk frankly of his action . some of the fine gentleman at St. James 's might wonder and sneer at he ; but amongst our little circle of friend we may be sure he find admire listener . the young General have the romance of a boy on many matter . he delight in music and poetry . on the last day of his life he say he would rather have write Gray 's Elegy than have win a battle . we may be sure that with a gentleman of such literary taste our friend George would become familiar ; and as they be both in love , and both accept lover , and both eager for happiness , no doubt they must have have many sentimental conversation together which would be very interesting to report could we only have accurate account of they . in one of his later letter , Warrington write : 
 " I have the honour of know the famous General Wolfe , and see much of he during his last stay in London . we have a subject of conversation then which be of unfaile interest to both of we , and I could not but admire Mr. Wolfe 's simplicity , his frankness , and a sort of glorious bravery which characterise he . he be much in love , and he want heap and heap of laurel to take to his mistress . ' if it be a sin to covet honour , ' he use to say with Harry the Fifth   he be passionately fond of play and poetry , ' I be the most offending soul alive . ' surely on his last day he have a feast which be enough to satisfy the greedy appetite for glory . he hunger after it . he seem to I not merely like a soldier go resolutely to do his duty , but rather like a knight in quest of dragon and giant . my own country have furnish of late a chief of a very different order , and quite an opposite genius . I scarce know which to admire most . the Briton 's chivalrous ardour , or the more than roman constancy of our great Virginian . " 
 as Mr. Lambert 's official duty detain he in London , his family remain contentedly with he , and I suppose Mr. Warrington be so satisfied with the rural quiet of Southampton Row and the beautiful flower and tree of Bedford Gardens , that he do not care to quit London for any long period . he make his pilgrimage to Castlewood , and pass a few day there , occupy the chamber of which he have often hear his grandfather talk , and which Colonel Esmond have occupy as a boy and he be receive kindly enough by such member of the family as happen to be at home . but no doubt he love well to be in London by the side of a young person in whose society he find great pleasure than any which my Lord Castlewood 's circle could afford he , though all the lady be civil , and Lady Maria especially gracious , and enchant with the tragedy which George and Parson Sampson read out to the lady . the chaplain be enthusiastic in its praise , and indeed it be through his interest and not through Mr. Johnson 's after all , that Mr. Warrington 's piece ever come on the stage . Mr. Johnson , it be true , press the play on his friend Mr. Garrick for Drury Lane , but Garrick have just make an arrangement with the famous Mr. Home for a tragedy from the pen of the author of Douglas . accordingly , Carpezan be carry to Mr. Rich at Covent Garden , and accept by that manager . 
 on the night of the production of the piece , Mr. Warrington give an elegant entertainment to his friend at the Bedford Head , in Covent Garden , whence they adjourn in a body to the theatre ; leave only one or two with our young author , who remain at the coffee - house , where friend from time to time come to he with an account of the performance . the part of Carpezan be fill by Barry , Shuter be the old nobleman , Reddish , I need scarcely say , make an excellent ulric , and the King of Bohemia be by a young actor from Dublin , Mr. Geoghegan , or Hagan as he be call on the stage , and who look and perform the part to admiration . Mrs. Woffington look too old in the first act as the heroine , but her murder in the fourth act , about which great doubt be express , go off to the terror and delight of the audience . Miss Wayn sing the ballad which be suppose to be sing by the king 's page , just at the moment of the unhappy wife 's execution , and all agree that Barry be very terrible and pathetic as Carpezan , especially in the execution scene . the grace and elegance of the young actor , Hagan , win general applause . the piece be put very elegantly on the stage by Mr. Rich , though there be some doubt whether , in the march of Janissaries in the last , the manager be correct in introduce a favourite elephant , which have figure in various pantomime , and by which one of Mr. Warrington 's black servant march in a turkish habit . the other sate in the footman 's gallery , and uproariously weep and applaud at the proper interval . 
 the execution of Sybilla be the turning - point of the piece . her head off , George 's friend breathe freely , and one messenger after another come to he at the coffee - house , to announce the complete success of the tragedy . Mr. Barry , amidst general applause , announce the play for repetition , and that it be the work of a young gentleman of Virginia , his first attempt in the dramatic style . 
 we should like to have be in the box where all our friend be seat during the performance , to have watch Theo 's flutter and anxiety whilst the success of the play seem dubious , and have behold the blush and the sparkle in her eye , when the victory be assure . Harry , during the little trouble in the fourth act , be deadly pale whiter , Mrs. Lambert say , than Barry , with all his chalk . but if Briareus could have clap hand , he could scarcely have make more noise than Harry at the end of the piece . Mr. Wolfe and General Lambert huzzaye enthusiastically . Mrs. Lambert , of course , cry : and though Hetty say , " why do you cry , mamma ? I you do not   want any of they alive again ; you know it serve they all right " the girl be really as much delighted as any person present , include little Charley from the Chartreux , who have leave from Dr. Crusius for that evening , and Miss Lucy , who have be bring from boarding - school on purpose to be present on the great occasion . my Lord Castlewood and his sister , Lady Maria , be present ; and his lordship go from his box and compliment Mr. Barry and the other actor on the stage ; and Parson Sampson be invaluable in the pit , where he lead the applause , have , I believe , give previous instruction to Gumbo to keep an eye upon he from the gallery , and do as he do . 
 be sure there be a very jolly supper of Mr. Warrington 's friend that night much more jolly than Mr. Garrick 's , for example , who make but a very poor success with his Agis and its dreary chorus , and who must have again feel that he have miss a good chance , in prefer Mr. Home 's tragedy to our young author 's . a jolly supper , do we say ? many jolly supper . Mr. Gumbo give an entertainment to several gentleman of the shoulder - knot , who have concur in support his master 's masterpiece : Mr. Henry Warrington give a supper at the Star and Garter , in Pall Mall , to ten officer of his new regiment , who have come up for the express purpose of back Carpezan ; and finally , Mr. Warrington receive the three principal actor of the tragedy , our family party from the side box , Mr. Johnson and his ingenious friend , Mr. Reynolds the painter , my Lord Castlewood and his sister , and one or two more . my Lady Maria happen to sit next to the young actor who have perform the part of the King . Mr. Warrington somehow have Miss Theo for a neighbour , and no doubt pass a pleasant evening beside she . the great animation and cordiality prevail , and when toast be call , Lady Maria gaily give " the King of Hungary " for her . that gentleman , who have plenty of eloquence and fire , and excellent manner , on as well as off the stage , protest that he have already suffer death in the course of the evening , hop that he should die a hundred time more on the same field ; but , dead or living , vow he know whose humble servant he ever should be . ah , if he have but a real crown in place of his diadem of pasteboard and tinsel , with what joy would he lay it at her ladyship 's foot ! neither my lord nor Mr. Esmond be over well pleased with the gentleman 's exceed gallantry a part of which they attribute , no doubt justly , to the wine and punch , of which he have be partake very freely . theo and her sister , who be quite new to the world , be a little frightened by the exceed energy of Mr. Hagan 's manner but Lady Maria , much more experienced , take it in perfectly good part . at a late hour coach be call , to which the gentleman attend the lady , after whose departure some of they return to the supper - room , and the end be that Carpezan have to be carry away in a chair , and that the King of Hungary have a severe headache ; and that the Poet , though he remember make a great number of speech , be quite astounded when half a dozen of his guest appear at his house the next day , whom he have invite overnight to come and sup with he once more . 
 as he put Mrs. Lambert and her daughter into their coach on the night previous , all the lady be flurry , delighted , excited ; and you may be sure our gentleman be with they the next day , to talk of the play and the audience , and the actor , and the beauty of the piece , over and over again . Mrs. Lambert have hear that the lady of the theatre be dangerous company for young man . she hop George would have a care , and not frequent the greenroom too much . 
 George smile , and say he have a preventive against all greenroom temptation , of which he be not in the least afraid ; and as he speak he look in Theo 's face , as if in those eye lay the amulet which be to preserve he from all danger . 
 " why should he be afraid , mamma ? " ask the maiden simply . she have no idea of danger or of guile . 
 " no , my darling , I do not   think he need be afraid , " say the mother , kiss she . 
 " you do not   suppose Mr. George would fall in love with that paint old creature who perform the chief part ? " ask Miss Hetty , with a toss of her head . " she must be old enough to be his mother . " 
 " pray , do you suppose that at our age nobody can care for we , or that we have no heart leave ? " ask mamma , very tartly . " I believe , or I may say , I hope and trust , your father think otherwise . he be , I imagine , perfectly satisfied , miss . he do not sneer at age , whatever little girl out of the schoolroom may do . and they have much well be back there , and they have much well remember what the fifth commandment be that they have , Hetty ! " 
 " I do not   think I be break it by say that an actress be as old as George 's mother , " plead Hetty . 
 " George 's mother be as old as I be , miss ! at least she be when we be at school . and Fanny Parker Mrs. Mountain who now be be seven month old , and we be in the french class together ; and I have no idea that our age be to be make the subject of remark and ridicule by our child , and I will thank you to spare it , if you please ! do you consider your mother too old , George ? " 
 " I be glad my mother be of your age , Aunt Lambert , " say George , in the most sentimental manner . 
 strange infatuation of passion singular perversity of reason ! at some period before his marriage , it not unfrequently happen that a man actually be fond of his mother - in - law ! at this time our good General vow , and with some reason , that he be jealous . Mrs. Lambert make much more of George than of any other person in the family . she dress up Theo to the utmost advantage in order to meet he ; she be for ever caress she , and appeal to she when he speak . it be , " do not   you think he look well ? " " do not   you think he look pale , Theo , to - day ? " " do not   you think he have be sit up over his book too much at night ? " and so forth . if he have a cold , she would have like to make gruel for he and see his foot in hot water . she send he recipe of her own for his health . when he be away , she never cease talk about he to her daughter . I dare say Miss Theo like the subject well enough . when he come , she be sure to be want in some other part of the house , and would bid Theo take care of he till she return . why , before she return to the room , could you hear she talk outside the door to her young innocent child , to her servant in the upper region , and so forth ? when she reappear , be not Mr. George always stand or sit at a considerable distance from Miss Theo except , to be sure , on that one day when she have just happen to drop her scissor , and he have naturally stoop down to pick they up ? why be she blush ? be not youthful cheek make to blush , and rose to bloom in the spring ? not that mamma ever note the blush , but begin quite an artless conversation about this or that , as she sate down brimful of happiness to her worktable . 
 and at last there come a letter from Virginia in Madam Esmond 's neat , well - know hand , and over which George tremble and blush before he break the seal . it be in answer to the letter which he have send home , respect his brother 's commission and his own attachment to Miss Lambert . of his intention respect Harry , Madam Esmond fully approve . as for his marriage , she be not against early marriage . she would take his picture of Miss Lambert with the allowance that be to be make for lover ' portrait , and hope , for his sake , that the young lady be all he describe she to be . with money , as Madam Esmond gather from her son 's letter , she do not appear to be provide at all , which be a pity , as , though wealthy in land , their family have but little ready - money . however , by Heaven 's blessing , there be plenty at home for child and child 's child , and the wife of her son should share all she have . when she hear more at length from Mr. and Mrs. Lambert , she would reply for her part more fully . she do not pretend to say that she have not great hope for her son , as a gentleman of his name and prospect might pretend to the hand of the first lady of the land ; but as Heaven have will that her son 's choice should fall upon her old friend 's daughter , she acquiesce , and would welcome George 's wife as her own child . this letter be bring by Mr. Van den Bosch of Albany , who have lately buy a very large estate in Virginia , and who be bind for England to put his granddaughter to a boarding - school . she , Madam Esmond , be not mercenary , nor be it because this young lady be heiress of a very great fortune that she desire her son to pay Mr. Van d. B. every attention . their property lay close together , and could Harry find in the young lady those quality of person and mind suitable for a companion for life , at least she would have the satisfaction of see both her child near she in her decline year . Madam Esmond conclude by send her affectionate compliment to Mrs. Lambert , from whom she beg to hear far , and her blessing to the young lady who be to be her daughter - in - law . 
 the letter be not cordial , and the writer evidently but half satisfied ; but , such as it be , her consent be here formally announce . how eagerly George run away to Soho with the long - desire news in his pocket ! I suppose our worthy friend there must have read his news in his countenance else why should Mrs. Lambert take her daughter 's hand and kiss she with such uncommon warmth , when George announce that he have receive letter from home ? then , with a break in his voice , a pallid face , and a considerable tremor , turn to Mr. Lambert , he say : " Madam Esmond 's letter , sir , be in reply to one of mine , in which I acquaint she that I have form an attachment in England , for which I ask my mother 's approval . she give her consent , I be grateful to say , and I have to pray my dear friend to be equally kind to I . " 
 " God bless thee , my dear boy ! " say the good General , lay a hand on the young man 's head . " I be glad to have thee for a son , George . there , there , do not   go down on your knee , young folk ! George may , to be sure , and thank God for give he the good little wife in all England . yes , my dear , except when you be ill , you never cause I a heartache and happy be the man , I say , who win thee ! " 
 I have no doubt the young people knelt before their parent , as be the fashion in those day ; and be perfectly certain that Mrs. Lambert kiss both of they , and likewise bedew her pocket - handkerchief in the most plentiful manner . Hetty be not present at this sentimental scene , and when she hear of it , speak with considerable asperity , and a laugh that be by no means pleasant , say : " be this all the news you have to give I ? why , I have know it these month past . do you think I have no eye to see , and no ear to hear , indeed ? " but in private she be much more gentle . she fling herself on her sister 's neck , embrace she passionately , and vow that never , never would Theo find any one to love she like her sister . with Theo she become entirely mild and humble . she could not abstain from her joke and satire with George , but he be too happy to heed her much , and too generous not to see the cause of her jealousy . 
 when all party concern come to read Madam Esmond 's letter , that document , it be true , appear rather vague . it contain only a promise that she would receive the young people at her house , and no sort of proposal for a settlement . the General shake his head over the letter he do not think of examine it until some day after the engagement have be make between George and his daughter : but now he read Madam Esmond 's word , they give he but small encouragement . 
 " Bah ! " say George . " I shall have three hundred pound for my tragedy . I can easily write a play a year ; and if the bad come to the bad , we can live on that . " 
 " on that and your patrimony , " say Theo 's father . 
 George now have to explain , with some hesitation , that what with pay bill for his mother , and Harry 's commission and debt , and his own ransom George 's patrimony proper be well - nigh spend . 
 Mr. Lambert 's countenance look graver still at this announcement , but he see his girl 's eye turn towards he with an alarm so tender , that he take she in his arm and vow that , let the bad come to the bad , his darling should not be balk of her wish . 
 about the go back to Virginia , George frankly own that he little like the notion of return to be entirely dependent on his mother . he give General Lambert an idea of his life at home , and explain how little to his taste that slavery be . no . why should he not stay in England , write more tragedy , study for the bar , get a place , perhaps ? why , indeed ? he straightway begin to form a plan for another tragedy . he bring portion of his work , from time to time , to Miss Theo and her sister : Hetty yawn over the work , but Theo pronounce it to be still more beautiful and admirable than the last , which be perfect . 
 the engagement of our young friend be make known to the member of their respective family , and announce to Sir Miles Warrington , in a ceremonious letter from his nephew . for a while Sir Miles see no particular objection to the marriage ; though , to be sure , consider his name and prospect , Mr. Warrington might have look high . the truth be , that Sir Miles imagine that Madam Esmond have make some considerable settlement on her son , and that his circumstance be more than easy . but when he hear that George be entirely dependent on his mother , and that his own small patrimony be dissipate , as Harry 's have be before , Sir Miles 's indignation at his nephew 's imprudence know no bound ; he could not find word to express his horror and anger at the want of principle exhibit by both these unhappy young man : he think it his duty to speak his mind about they , and write his opinion to his sister Esmond in Virginia . as for General and Mrs. Lambert , who pass for respectable person , be it to be bear that such people should inveigle a penniless young man into a marriage with their penniless daughter ? regard they , and George 's behaviour , Sir Miles fully explain his view to Madam Esmond , give half a finger to George whenever his nephew call on he in town , and do not even invite he to partake of the famous family small - beer . towards Harry his uncle somewhat unbent ; Harry have do his duty in the campaign , and be mention with praise in high quarter . he have sow his wild oat , he at least be endeavour to amend ; but George be a young prodigal , fast career to ruin , and his name be only mention in the family with a groan . be there any poor fellow nowadays , I wonder , whose polite family fall on they and persecute they ; groan over they and stone they , and hand stone to their neighbour that they may do likewise ? all the patrimony spend ! gracious heaven ! Sir Miles turn pale when he see his nephew come . Lady Warrington pray for he as a dangerous reprobate ; and , in the meantime , George be walk the town , quite unconscious that he be occasion so much wrath and so much devotion . he take little Miley to the play and bring he back again . he send ticket to his aunt and cousin which they could not refuse , you know ; it would look too marked be they to break altogether . so they not only take the ticket , but whenever country constituent come to town they ask for more , take care to give the very bad motif to George 's intimacy with the theatre , and to suppose that he and the actress be on term of the most disgraceful intimacy . an august personage have be to the theatre , and express his approbation of Mr. Warrington 's drama to Sir Miles , when he attend his R - y - l h - ghn - ss 's levee at Saville House , Sir Miles , to be sure , modify his opinion regard the piece , and speak henceforth more respectfully of it . meanwhile , as we have say , George be pass his life entirely careless of the opinion of all the uncle , aunt , and cousin in the world . 
 Most of the Esmond cousin be at least more polite and cordial than George 's kinsfolk of the Warrington side . in spite of his behaviour over the card , Lord Castlewood , George always maintain , have a liking for our Virginians , and George be pleased enough to be in his company . he be a far abler man than many who succeed in life . he have a good name , and somehow only stain it ; a considerable wit , and nobody trust it ; and a very shrewd experience and knowledge of mankind , which make he mistrust they , and himself most of all , and which perhaps be the bar to his own advancement . my Lady Castlewood , a woman of the world , wear always a bland mask , and receive Mr. George with perfect civility , and welcome he to lose as many guineas as he like at her ladyship 's card - table . between Mr. William and the virginian brother there never be any love lose ; but , as for Lady Maria , though her love affair be over , she have no rancour ; she profess for her cousin a very great regard and affection , a part of which the young gentleman very gratefully return . she be charm to hear of Harry 's valour in the campaign ; she be delighted with George 's success at the theatre ; she be for ever go to the play , and have all the favourite passage of Carpezan by heart . one day , as Mr. George and Miss Theo be take a sentimental walk in Kensington Gardens , whom should they light upon but their cousin Maria in company with a gentleman in a smart suit and handsome laced hat , and who should the gentleman be but his Majesty King Louis of Hungary , Mr. Hagan ? he salute the party , and leave they presently . Lady Maria have only just happen to meet he . Mr. Hagan come sometimes , he say , for quiet , to study his part in Kensington Gardens , and George and the two lady walk together to Lord Castlewood 's door in Kensington Square , Lady Maria utter a thousand compliment to Theo upon her good look , upon her virtue , upon her future happiness , upon her papa and mamma , upon she destine husband , upon her paduasoy cloak and dear little foot and shoe - buckle . 
 Harry happen to come to London that evening , and sleep at his accustomed quarter . when George appear at breakfast , the Captain be already in the room   the custom of that day be to call all army gentleman captain , and look at the letter on the breakfast - table . 
 " why , George , " he cry , " there be a letter from Maria ! " 
 " little boy bring it from Common Garden last night Master George asleep , " say Gumbo . 
 " what can it be about ? " ask Harry , as George peruse his letter with a queer expression of face . 
 " about my play , to be sure , " George answer , tear up the paper , and still wear his queer look . 
 " what , she be not write love - letter to you , be she , georgy ? " 
 " no , certainly not to I , " reply the other . but he speak no word more about the letter ; and when at dinner in Dean Street Mrs. Lambert say , " so you meet somebody walk with the King of Hungary yesterday in Kensington Gardens ? " 
 " what little tell - tale tell you ? a mere casual rencontre the King go there to study his part , and Lady Maria happen to be cross the garden to visit some of the other King 's servant at Kensington Palace . " and so there be an end to that matter for the time be . 
 other event be at hand fraught with interest to our Virginians . one evening after Christmas , the two gentleman , with a few more friend , be meet round General Lambert 's supper - table ; and among the company be Harry 's new Colonel of the 67th , Major - General Wolfe . the young General be more than ordinarily grave . the conversation all relate to the war . event of great importance be pende . the great minister now in power be determined to carry on the war on a much more extended scale than have be attempt hitherto : an army be order to Germany to help Prince Ferdinand , another great expedition be prepare for America , and here , say Mr. Lambert , " I will give you the health of the Commander a glorious campaign , and a happy return to he ! " 
 " why do you not drink the toast , General James ! " ask the hostess of her guest . 
 " he must not drink his own toast , " say General Lambert ; " it be we must do that ! " 
 what ? be James appoint ? all the lady must drink such a toast as that , and they mingle their kind voice with the applause of the rest of the company . 
 why do he look so melancholy ? the lady ask of one another when they withdraw . in after day they remember his pale face . 
 " perhaps he have be part from his sweetheart , " suggest tender - hearted Mrs. Lambert . and at this sentimental notion , no doubt all the lady look sad . 
 the gentleman , meanwhile , continue their talk about the war and its chance . Mr. Wolfe do not contradict the speaker when they say that the expedition be to be direct against Canada . 
 " ah , sir , " say Harry , " I wish your regiment be go with you , and that I might pay another visit to my old friend at Quebec . " 
 what , have Harry be there ? yes . he describe his visit to the place five year before , and know the city , and the neighbourhood , well . he lay a number of bit of biscuit on the table before he , and make a couple of rivulet of punch on each side . " this fork be the Isle d'Orleans , " say he , " with the north and south branch of St. Lawrence on each side . here be the Low Town , with a battery how many gun be mount there in our time , brother ? but at long shot from the St. Joseph shore you might play the same game . here be what they call the little river , the St. Charles , and a bridge of boat with a tete du pont over to the place of arm . here be the citadel , and here 's convent ever so many convent and the cathedral ; and here , outside the line to the west and south , be what they call the Plains of Abraham where a certain little affair take place , do you remember , brother ? he and a young officer of the Rousillon regiment ca ca'd at each other for twenty minute , and George pink he , and then they jure'd each other an amitie eternelle . well it be for George : for his second save his life on that awful day of Braddock 's defeat . he be a fine little fellow , and I give his toast : Je bois a la sante du Chevalier de Florac ! " 
 " what , can you speak French , too , Harry ? " ask Mr. Wolfe . the young man look at the General with eager eye . 
 " yes , " say he , " I can speak , but not so well as George . " 
 " but he remember the city , and can place the battery , you see , and know the ground a thousand time well than I do ! " cry the eld brother . 
 the two eld officer exchange look with one another ; Mr. Lambert smile and nod , as if in reply to the mute query of his comrade : on which the other speak . " Mr. Harry , " he say , " if you have have enough of fine folk , and White 's , and horse - race " 
 " oh , sir ! " say the young man , turn very red . 
 " and if you have a mind to a sea voyage at a short notice , come and see I at my lodging to - morrow . " 
 what be that sudden uproar of cheer which the lady hear in their drawing - room ? it be the hurrah which Harry Warrington give when he leap up at hear the General 's invitation . 
 the woman see no more of the gentleman that night . General Lambert have to be away upon his business early next morning , before see any of his family ; nor have he mention a word of Harry 's outbreak on the previous evening . but when he rejoin his folk at dinner , a look at Miss Hetty 's face inform the worthy gentleman that she know what have pass on the night previous , and what be about to happen to the young Virginian . after dinner Mrs. Lambert sit demurely at her work , Miss Theo take her book of italian Poetry . neither of the General 's customary guest happen to be present that evening . 
 he take little Hetty 's hand in his , and begin to talk with she . he do not allude to the subject which he know be uppermost in her mind , except that by a more than ordinary gentleness and kindness he perhaps cause she to understand that her thought be know to he . 
 " I have breakfast , " say he , " with James Wolfe this morning , and our friend Harry be of the party . when he and the other guest be go , I remain and talk with James about the great expedition on which he be go to sail . would that his brave father have live a few month long to see he come back covered with honour from Louisbourg , and know that all England be look to he to achieve still great glory ! James be dreadfully ill in body so ill that I be frightened for he and not a little depressed in mind at have to part from the young lady whom he have love so long . a little rest , he think , might have set his shattered frame up ; and to call she his have be the object of his life . but , great as his love be   and he be as romantic as one of you young folk of seventeen , honour and duty be great , and he leave home , and wife , and ease , and health , at their bidding . every man of honour would do the like ; every woman who love he truly would buckle on his armour for he .
