James go to take leave of his mother to - night ; and though she love he devotedly , and be one of the tender woman in the world , I be sure she will show no sign of weakness at his go away . " 
 " when do he sail , papa ? " the girl ask . 
 " he will be on board in five day . " and Hetty know quite well who sail with he . 




 CHAPTER LXVIII . in which Harry go westward 

 our tender heart be averse to all idea and description of parting ; and I shall therefore say nothing of Harry Warrington 's feeling at take leave of his brother and friend . be not thousand of man in the same plight ? have not Mr. Wolfe his mother to kiss   his brave father have quit life during his son 's absence on the glorious Louisbourg campaign , and his sweetheart to clasp in a farewell embrace ? have not stout Admiral Holmes , before sail westward with his squadron , the Somerset , the terrible , the Northumberland , the Royal William , the Trident , the Diana , the Seahorse his own flag be hoist on board the Dublin to take leave of Mrs. and the Misses Holmes ? be Admiral Saunders , who sail the day after he , exempt from human feeling ? away go William and his crew of jovial sailor , plough through the tumble wave , and poor black - eyed Susan on shore watch the ship as it dwindle in the sunset . 
 it dwindle in the West . the night fall darkle over the ocean . they be go : but their heart be at home yet a while . in silence , with a heart inexpressibly soft and tender , how each man think of those he have leave ! what a chorus of pitiful prayer rise up to the Father , at sea and on shore , on that parting night at home by the vacant bedside , where the wife kneel in tear ; round the fire , where the mother and child together pour out their supplication : or on deck , where the seafarer look up to the star of heaven , as the ship cleave through the roar midnight water ! to - morrow the sun rise upon our common life again , and we commence our daily task of toil and duty . 
 George accompany his brother , and stay a while with he at Portsmouth whilst they be wait for a wind . he shake Mr. Wolfe 's hand , look at his pale face for the last time , and see the vessel depart amid the clangour of bell , and the thunder of cannon from the shore . next day he be back at his home , and at that business which be sure one of the most selfish and absorbing of the world 's occupation , to which almost every man who be thirty year old have serve ere this his apprenticeship . he have a pang of sadness , as he look in at the lodging to the little room which Harry use to occupy , and see his half - burn paper still in the grate . in a few minute he be on his way to Dean Street again , and whispering by the fitful firelight in the ear of the cling sweetheart . she be very happy oh , so happy ! at his return . she be ashamed of be so . be it not heartless to be so , when poor Hetty be so melancholy ? poor little Hetty ! indeed , it be selfish to be glad when she be in such a sad way . it make one quite wretched to see she . " do not , sir ! well , I ought to be wretche , and it be very , very wicked of I if I be not , " say Theo ; and one can understand her soft - hearted repentance . what she mean by " do not " who can tell ? I have say the room be dark , and the fire burn fitfully and " do not " be no doubt utter in one of the dark fit . enter servant with supper and light . the family arrive ; the conversation become general . the destination of the fleet be know everywhere now . the force on board be sufficient to beat all the French in Canada ; and , under such an officer as Wolfe , to repair the blunder and disaster of previous campaign . he look dreadfully ill , indeed . but he have a great soul in a feeble body . the minister , the country hope the utmost from he . after supper , accord to custom , Mr. Lambert assemble his modest household , of whom George Warrington may be say quite to form a part ; and as he pray for all traveller by land and water , Theo and her sister be kneel together . and so , as the ship speed far and far into the West , the fond thought pursue it ; and the night pass , and the sun rise . 
 a day or two more , and everybody be at his book or his usual work . as for George Warrington , that celebrate dramatist be busy about another composition . when the tragedy of Carpezan have run some thirty or twoscore night , other person of genius take possession of the theatre . 
 there may have be person who wonder how the town could be so fickle as ever to tire of such a masterpiece as the Tragedy who could not bear to see the actor dress in other habit , recite other man 's verse ; but George , of a sceptical turn of mind , take the fate of his Tragedy very philosophically , and pocket the proceed with much quiet satisfaction . from Mr. Dodsley , the bookseller , he have the usual complement of a hundred pound ; from the manager of the theatre two hundred or more ; and such praise from the critic and his friend , that he set to work to prepare another piece , with which he hop to achieve even great success than by his first performance . 
 over these study , and the other charming business which occupy he , month pass away . happy business ! happy time of youth and life , when love be first speak and return ; when the dear eye be daily shin welcome , and the fond lip never tire of whisper their sweet secret ; when the parting look that accompany " good night ! " give delightful warning of to - morrow ; when the heart be so overflow with love and happiness , that it have to spare for all the world ; when the day close with glad prayer , and open with joyful hope ; when doubt seem cowardice , misfortune impossible , poverty only a sweet trial of constancy ! Theo 's elder , thankfully remember their own prime , sit softly by and witness this pretty comedy perform by their young people . and in one of his later letter , dutifully write to his wife during a temporary absence from home , George Warrington record how he have be to look up at the window of the dear old house in Dean Street , and wonder who be sit in the chamber where he and Theo have be so happy . 
 meanwhile we can learn how the time pass , and our friend be engage , by some extract from George 's letter to his brother . 
 " from the old window opposite Bedford Gardens , this 20th August 1759 . 
 " why be you go back to rugged rock , bleak shore , burn summer , nipping winter , at home , when you might have be crop ever so many laurel in Germany ? Kingsley 's be come back as cover with them as Jack - a - green on May - day . our six regiment do wonder ; and our horse would have do if my Lord George Sackville only have let they . but when Prince Ferdinand say ' charge ! ' his lordship could not hear , or could not translate the german word for ' Forward ; ' and so we only beat the French , without utterly annihilate they , as we might , have Lord Granby or Mr. Warrington have the command . my lord be come back to town , and be shout for a Court - Martial . he hold his head high enough in prosperity : in misfortune he show such a constancy of arrogance that one almost admire he . he look as if he rather envy poor Mr. Byng , and the not shoot he be a manque d'egards towards he . 
 " the Duke have have notice to get himself in readiness for depart from this world of grandeur and victory , and downfall and disappointment . an attack of palsy have visit his Royal Highness ; and pallida mor have just peep in at his door , as it be , and say , ' I will call again . ' tyrant as he be , this prince have be noble in disgrace ; and no king have ever have a true servant than our have find in his son . why do I like the lose side always , and be I disposed to revolt against the winner ? your famous Mr. P , your chief 's patron and discoverer , I have be to hear in the House of Commons twice or thrice . I revolt against his magniloquence . I wish some little David would topple over that swell giant . his thought and his language be always attitudinising . I like Barry 's manner well , though the other be the more awful actor . 
 " Pocahontas get on apace . Barry like his part of Captain Smith ; and , though he will have he wear a red coat and blue facing and an epaulet , I have a fancy to dress he exactly like one of the picture of Queen Elizabeth 's gentleman at Hampton Court : with a ruff and a square beard and square shoe . ' and Pocahontas would you like she to be tattoo ? ' ask Uncle Lambert . Hagan 's part as the warrior who be in love with she , and , see her partiality for the captain , nobly rescue he from death , I trust will prove a hit . a strange fish be this Hagan : his mouth full of stage - play and rant , but good , honest , and brave , if I do not   err . he be angry at have be cast lately for Sir O'Brallaghan , in Mr. Macklin 's new farce of Love A - la - mode . he say that he do not keer to disgreece his tongue with imiteetion of that rascal brogue . as if there be any call for imiteetion , when he have such an admirable twang of his own ! 
 " shall I tell you ? shall I hide the circumstance ? shall I hurt your feeling ? shall I set you in a rage of jealousy , and cause you to ask for leave to return to Europe ? Know , then , that though Carpezan be long since dead , cousin Maria be for ever come to the playhouse . Tom Spencer have spy she out night after night in the gallery , and she come on the night when Hagan perform . quick , Burroughs , Mr. Warrington 's boot and portmanteau ! order a chaise and four for Portsmouth immediately ! the letter which I burn one morning when we be at breakfast   I may let the cat out of the bag , now puss have such a prodigious way to run   be from cousin M. , hint that she wish I to tell no tale about she : but I can not   help just whisper to you that Maria at this moment be busy console herself as fast as possible . shall I spoil sport ? shall I tell her brother ? be the affair any business of mine ? what have the Esmonds do for you and I but win our money at card ? yet I like our noble cousin . it seem to I that he would be good if he could or rather , he would have be once . he have be set on a wrong way of life , from which ' tis now probably too late to rescue he . o beati agricolae ! our Virginia be dull , but let we thank Heaven we be breed there . we be make little slave , but not slave to wickedness , gambling , bad male and female company . it be not until my poor Harry leave home that he fall among thief . I mean thief en grand , such as waylay he and strip he on english highroad . I consider you none the bad because you be the unlucky one , and have to deliver your purse up . and now you be go to retrieve , and make a good name for yourself ; and kill more ' french dragon , ' and become a great commander . and our mother will talk of her son the Captain , the Colonel , the General , and have his picture paint with all his star and epaulet , when poor I shall be but a dawdling poetaster , or , if we may hope for the good , a snug placeman , with a little box at Richmond or Kew , and a half - score of little picaninny , that will come and bob curtsey at the garden - gate when their uncle the General rid up on his great charger , with his aide - de - camp 's pocket fill with gingerbread for the nephew and niece . ' tis for you to brandish the sword of Mars . as for I , I look forward to a quiet life : a quiet little home , a quiet little library full of book , and a little some one dulce ridentem , dulce loquentem , on t'other side of the fire , as I scribble away at my paper . I be so pleased with this prospect , so utterly content and happy , that I feel afraid as I think of it , lest it should escape I ; and , even to my dear Hal , be shy of speak of my happiness . what be ambition to I , with this certainty ? what do I care for war , with this beatific peace smile near ? 
 " our mother 's friend , Mynheer Van den Bosch , have be away on a tour to discover his family in Holland , and , strange to say , have find one . Miss   who be intend by maternal solicitude to be a wife for your worship   have have six month at Kensington School , and be come out with a hundred pretty accomplishment , which be to complete she a perfect fine lady . her papa bring she to make a curtsey in Dean Street , and a mighty elegant curtsey she make . though she be scarce seventeen , no dowager of sixty can be more at her ease . she converse with Aunt Lambert on an equal footing ; she treat the girl as chit to Hetty 's wrath and Theo 's amusement . she talk politic with the General , and the last rout , dress , opera , fashion , scandal , with such perfect ease that , but for a blunder or two , you might have fancy Miss Lydia be bear in Mayfair . at the Court end of the town she will live , she say ; and have no patience with her father , who have a lodging in Monument Yard . for those who love a brown beauty , a pretty little mignonne creature can not be see . but my taste , you know , dear brother , and ... " 
 here follow a page of rapture and quotation of verse , which , out of a regard for the reader , and the writer 's memory , the editor of the present page decline to reprint . gentleman and lady of a certain age may remember the time when they indulge in these rapturous folly on their own account ; when the praise of the charmer be for ever warble from their lip or trickle from their pen ; when the flower of life be in full bloom , and all the bird of spring be sing . the twig be now bare , perhaps , and the leaf have fall ; but , for all that , shall we not , remember the vernal time ? as for you , young people , whose May   or April , be it ?   have not commence yet , you need not be detain over other folk ' love - rhapsody ; depend on it , when your spring - season arrive , kindly Nature will warm all your flower into bloom , and rouse your glad bosom to pour out their full song . 




 CHAPTER LXIX . a little Innocent 

 George Warrington have mention in the letter just quote , that in spite of my Lord Castlewood 's previous play transaction with Harry , my lord and George remain friend , and meet on term of good kinsmanship . do George want frank , or an introduction at court , or a place in the House of Lords to hear a debate , his cousin be always ready to serve he , be a pleasant and witty companion , and would do anything which might promote his relative 's interest , provide his own be not prejudiced . 
 now he even go so far as to promise that he would do his good with the people in power to provide a place for Mr. George Warrington , who daily show a great disinclination to return to his native country , and place himself once more under the maternal servitude . George have not merely a sentimental motive for remain in England : the pursuit and society of London please he infinitely well than any which he could have at home . a planter 's life of idleness might have suit he , could he have enjoy independence with it . but in Virginia he be only the first , and , as he think , the worst treat , of his mother 's subject . he dread to think of return with his young bride to his home , and of the life which she would be destine to lead there . well freedom and poverty in England , with congenial society , and a hope perchance of future distinction , than the wearisome routine of home life , the tedious subordination , the frequent bickering , the certain jealousy and difference of opinion , to which he must subject his wife so soon as they turn their face homeward . 
 so Lord Castlewood 's promise to provide for George be very eagerly accept by the Virginian . my lord have not provide very well for his own brother to be sure , and his own position , peer as he be , be anything but enviable ; but we believe what we wish to believe , and George Warrington choose to put great stress upon his kinsman 's offer of patronage . unlike the Warrington family , Lord Castlewood be quite gracious when he be make acquainted with George 's engagement to Miss Lambert ; come to wait upon her parent ; praise George to they and the young lady to George , and make himself so prodigiously agreeable in their company that these charitable folk forget his bad reputation , and think it must be a very wicked and scandalous world which malign he . he say , indeed , that he be improve in their society , as every man must be who come into it . among they he be witty , lively , good for the time be . he leave his wickedness and worldliness with his cloak in the hall , and only put they on again when he step into his chair . what worldle on life 's voyage do not know of some such harbour of rest and calm , some haven where he put in out of the storm ? very likely Lord Castlewood be actually well whilst he stay with those good people , and for the time be at least no hypocrite . 
 and , I dare say , the Lambert elder think no bad of his lordship for openly proclaim his admiration for Miss Theo . it be quite genuine , and he do not profess it be very deep . 
 " it do not   affect my sleep , and I be not go to break my heart because Miss Lambert prefer somebody else , " he remark . " only I wish when I be a young man , madam , I have have the good fortune to meet with somebody so innocent and good as your daughter . I might have be keep out of a deal of harm 's way : but innocent and good young woman do not fall into mine , or they would have make I well than I be . " 
 " sure , my lord , it be not too late ! " say Mrs. Lambert , very softly . 
 Castlewood start back , misunderstand she . 
 " not too late , madam ? " he inquire . 
 she blush . " it be too late to court my dear daughter , my lord , but not too late to repent . we read , ' tis never too late to do that . if other have be receive at the eleventh hour , be there any reason why you should give up hope ? " 
 " perhaps I know my own heart well than you , " he say in a plaintive tone . " I can speak french and German very well , and why ? because I be teach both in the nursery . a man who learn they late can never get the practice of they on his tongue . and so ' tis the case with goodness , I can not   learn it at my age . I can only see other practise it , and admire they . when I be on on the side opposite to Lazarus , will Miss Theo give I a drop of water ? do not   frown ! I know I shall be there , Mrs. Lambert . some folk be doom so ; and I think some of our family be amongst these . some people be vacillate , and one hardly know which way the scale will turn . whereas some be predestined angel , and fly heavenwards naturally , and do what they will . " 
 " oh , my lord , and why should you not be of the predestine ? whilst there be a day leave whilst there be an hour there be hope ! " say the fond matron . 
 " I know what be pass in your mind , my dear madam nay , I read your prayer in your look ; but how can they avail ? " Lord Castlewood ask sadly . " you do not   know all , my good lady . you do not   know what a life ours be of the world ; how early it begin ; how selfish Nature , and then necessity and education , have make we . it be Fate hold the rein of the chariot , and we can not   escape our doom . I know well : I see well people : I go my own way . my own ? no , not mine Fate 's : and it be not altogether without pity for we , since it allow we , from time to time , to see such people as you . " and he take her hand and look her full in the face , and bow with a melancholy grace . every word he say be true . no great error than to suppose that weak and bad man be stranger to good feeling , or deficient of sensibility . only the good feeling do not last nay , the tear be a kind of debauch of sentiment , as old libertine be say to find that the tear and grief of their victim add a z to their pleasure . but Mrs. Lambert know little of what be pass in this man 's mind   how should she ? , and so pray for he with the fond persistence of woman . he be much well yes , much well than he be suppose to be . he be a most interesting man . there be hope , why should there not be the most precious hope for he still ? 
 it remain to be see which of the two speaker form the correct estimate of my lord 's character . meanwhile , if the gentleman be right , the lady be mollify , and her kind wish and prayer for this experienced sinner 's repentance , if they be of no avail for his amendment , at least could do he no harm . kind - soule doctor   and what good woman be not of the faculty ?   look after a reprobate as physician after a perilous case . when the patient be convert to health their interest cease in he , and they drive to feel pulse and prescribe medicine elsewhere . 
 but , while the malady be under treatment , our kind lady could not see too much of her sick man . quite an intimacy spring up between my Lord Castlewood and the Lamberts . I be not sure that some worldly view might not suit even with good Mrs. Lambert 's spiritual plan   for who know into what pure Eden , though guard by flame - sworde angel , worldliness will not creep ? . her son be about to take order . my Lord Castlewood fear very much that his present chaplain 's , Mr. Sampson 's , careless life and heterodox conversation might lead he to give up his chaplaincy : in which case , my lord hint the little modest cure would be vacant , and at the service of some young divine of good principle and good manner , who would be content with a small stipend , and a small but friendly congregation . 
 thus an acquaintance be establish between the two family , and the lady of Castlewood , always on their good behaviour , come more than once to make their curtsey in Mrs. Lambert 's drawing - room . they be civil to the parent and the young lady . my Lady Castlewood 's card assembly be open to Mrs. Lambert and her family . there be play , certainly all the world play his Majesty , the Bishops , every peer and peeress in the land . but nobody need play who do not like ; and surely nobody need have scruple regard the practice , when such august and venerable personage be daily find to abet it . More than once Mrs. Lambert make her appearance at her ladyship 's rout , and be grateful for the welcome which she receive , and please with the admiration which her daughter excite . 
 Mention have be make , in a foregoing page and letter , of an american family of dutch extraction , who have come to England very strongly recommend by Madam Esmond , their virginian neighbour , to her son in Europe . the view express in Madam Esmond 's letter be so clear , that that arch match - maker , Mrs. Lambert , could not but understand they . as for George , he be engage already ; as for poor Hetty 's flame , Harry , he be go on service , for which circumstance Hetty 's mother be not very sorry perhaps . she laughingly tell George that he ought to obey his mamma 's injunction , break off his engagement with Theo , and make up to Miss Lydia , who be ten time ten time ! a hundred time as rich as her poor girl , and certainly much handsomer . " yes , indeed , " say George , " that I own : she be handsomer , and she be rich , and perhaps even cleverer . "   all which praise Mrs. Lambert but half like .   " but say she be all these ? so be Mr. Johnson much cleverer than I be : so be , whom shall we say ? so be Mr. Hagan the actor much tall and handsomer : so be Sir James Lowther much rich : yet pray , ma'am , do you suppose I be go to be jealous of any one of these three , or think my Theo would jilt I for their sake ? why should I not allow that Miss Lydia be handsomer , then ? and rich , and clever , too , and lively , and well breed , if you insist on it , and an angel if you will have it so ? Theo be not afraid : art thou , child ? " 
 " no , George , " say Theo , with such an honest look of the eye as would convince any scepticism , or shame any jealousy . and if , after this pair of speech , mamma take occasion to leave the room for a minute to fetch her scissor , or her thimble , or a bootjack and slipper , or the cross and ball on the top of St. Paul 's , or her pocket - handkerchief which she have forget in the parlour if , I say , Mrs. Lambert quit the room on any errand or pretext , natural or preposterous , I shall not be in the least surprised , if , at her return in a couple of minute , she find George in near proximity to Theo , who have a heighten colour , and whose hand George be just drop I shall not have the least idea of what they have be do . have you , madam ? have you any remembrance of what use to happen when Mr. Grundy come a - courting ? be you , who , after all , be not in the room with our young people , go to cry out fie and for shame ? then fie and for shame upon you , Mrs. Grundy ! 
 well , Harry be away , and Theo and George irrevocably engage , so that there be no possibility of bring Madam Esmond 's little plan to bear , why should not Mrs. Lambert have plan of her own ; and if a rich , handsome , beautiful little wife should fall in his way , why should not Jack Lambert from Oxford have she ? so think mamma , who be always think of marry and give in marriage , and so she prattle to General Lambert , who , as usual , call she a goose for her pain . at any rate , Mrs. Lambert say beauty and rich be no objection ; at any rate , Madam Esmond desire that this family should be hospitably entertain , and it be not her fault that Harry be go away to Canada . would the General wish he to come back ; leave the army and his reputation , perhaps ; yes , and come to England and marry this American , and break poor Hetty 's heart would her father wish that ? let we spare further argument , and not be so rude as to hint that Mr. Lambert be in the right in call a fond wife by the name of that absurd splay - foot bird , annually sacrifice at the Feast of St. Michael . 
 in those early day , there be vast distinction of rank draw between the court and city people : and Mr. Van den Bosch , when he first come to London , scarcely associate with any but the latter sort . he have a lodging near his agent 's in the city . when his pretty girl come from school for a holiday , he take she an airing to Islington or Highgate , or an occasional promenade in the Artillery Ground in Bunhill Fields . they go to that Baptist meeting - house in Finsbury Fields , and on the sly to see Mr. Garrick once or twice , or that funny rogue Mr. Foote , at the Little Theatre . to go to a Lord Mayor 's feast be a treat to the gentleman of the high order : and to dance with a young mercer at Hampstead Assembly give the utmost delight to the young lady . when George first go to wait upon his mother 's friend , he find our old acquaintance , Mr. Draper , of the Temple , sedulous in his attention to she ; and the lawyer , who be marry , tell Mr. Warrington to look out , as the young lady have a plumb to her fortune . Mr. Drabshaw , a young Quaker gentleman , and nephew of Mr. Trail , Madam Esmond 's Bristol agent , be also in constant attendance upon the young lady , and in dreadful alarm and suspicion when Mr. Warrington first make his appearance . wish to do honour to his mother 's neighbour , Mr. Warrington invite they to an entertainment at his own apartment ; and who should so naturally meet they as his friend from Soho ? not one of they but be force to own little Miss Lydia 's beauty . she have the foot of a fairy : the arm , neck , flash eye of a little brown huntress of Diana . she have bring a little plaintive accent from home with she of which I , moi qui vous parle , have hear a hundred gross Cockney imitation , and watch as many absurd disguise , and which I say   in moderation   be charming in the mouth of a charming woman . who set up to say no , forsooth ? you dear Miss Whittington , with whose h 's fate have deal so unkindly ? you lovely Miss Nicol Jarvie , with your northern burr ? you beautiful Miss Molony , with your Dame Street warble ? all accent be pretty from pretty lip , and who shall set the standard up ? shall it be a rose , or a thistle , or a shamrock , or a star and stripe ? as for Miss Lydia 's accent , I have no doubt it be not odious even from the first day when she set foot on these polite shore , otherwise Mr. Warrington , as a man of taste , have certainly disapprove of her manner of talking , and her schoolmistress at Kensington have not do her duty by her pupil . 
 after the six month be over , during which , accord to her father 's calculation , she be to learn all the accomplishment procurable at the Kensington Academy , Miss Lydia return nothing loth to her grandfather , and take her place in the world . a narrow world at first it be to she ; but she be a resolute little person , and resolve to enlarge her sphere in society ; and whither she choose to lead the way , the obedient grandfather follow she . he have be thwart himself in early life , he say , and little good come of the severity he underwent . he have thwart his own son , who have turn out but ill . as for little Lyddy , he be determined she should have as pleasant a life as be possible . do not Mr. George think he be right ? ' Twas say in Virginia he do not know with what reason that the young gentleman of Castlewood have be happy if Madam Esmond have allow they a little of their own way . George could not gainsay this public rumour , or think of induce the benevolent old gentleman to alter his plan respect his granddaughter . as for the Lambert family , how could they do otherwise than welcome the kind old man , the parent so tender and liberal , Madam Esmond 's good friend ? 
 when Miss come from school , grandpapa remove from Monument Yard to an elegant house in Bloomsbury ; whither they be follow at first by their city friend . there be merchant from Virginia Walk ; there be worthy tradesman , with whom the worthy old merchant have dealing ; there be their lady and daughter and son , who be all highly gracious to Miss Lyddy . it would be a long task to describe how these disappear one by one how there be no more junketing at Belsize , or trip to Highgate , or Saturday jaunt to Deputy Higgs ' villa , Highbury , or country - dance at honest Mr. Lutestring 's house at Hackney . even the Sunday practice be change ; and , oh , abomination of abomination ! Mr. Van den Bosch leave Bethesda Chapel in Bunhill Row , and actually take a pew in Queen Square Church ! 
 Queen Square Church , and Mr. George Warrington live hard by in Southampton Row ! ' twa easy to see at whom Miss Lyddy be set her cap , and Mr. Draper , who have be full of she and her grandfather 's praise before , now take occasion to warn Mr. George , and give he very different report regard Mr. Van den Bosch to those which have first be current . Mr. Van d. B. , for all he brag so of his dutch parentage , come from Albany , and be nobody 's son at all . he have make his money by land speculation , or by privateere   which be uncommonly like piracy , and by the Guinea trade . his son have marry if marriage it could be call , which be very doubtful an assign servant , and have be cut off by his father , and have take to bad course , and have die , luckily for himself , in his own bed . 
 " Mr. Draper have tell you bad tale about I , " say the placid old gentleman to George . " very likely we be all sinner , and some evil may be truly say of all of we , with a great deal more that be untrue . do he tell you that my son be unhappy with I ? I tell you so too . do he bring you wicked story about my family ? he like it so well that he want to marry my Lyddy to his brother . Heaven bless she ! I have have a many offer for she . and you be the young gentleman I should have choose for she , and I like you none the bad because you prefer somebody else ; though what you can see in your Miss , as compare to my Lyddy , beg your honour 's pardon , I be at a loss to understand . " 
 " there be no accounting for taste , my good sir , " say Mr. George , with his most superb air . 
 " no , sir ; ' tis a wonder of nature , and daily happen . when I keep store to Albany , there be one of your tiptop gentry there that might have marry my dear daughter that be alive then , and with a pretty piece of money , whereby for her father and I have quarrel Miss Lyddy would have be a pauper , you see : and in place of my beautiful Bella , my gentleman choose a little homely creature , no pretty than your Miss , and without a dollar to her fortune . the more fool he , save your presence , Mr. George . " 
 " pray do not   save my presence , my good sir , " say George , laugh . " I suppose the gentleman 's word be give to the other lady , and he have see she first , and hence be indifferent to your charming daughter . " 
 " I suppose when a young fellow give his word to perform a cursed piece of folly , he always stick to it , my dear sir , beg your pardon . but Lord , Lord , what be I speak of ? I be aspeake of twenty year ago . I be well - to - do then , but I may say Heaven have bless my store , and I be three time as well off now . ask my agent how much they will give for Joseph Van den Bosch 's bill at six month on New York or at sight may be for forty thousand pound ? I warrant they will discount the paper . " 
 " happy he who have the bill , sir ! " say George , with a bow , not a little amuse with the candour of the old gentleman . 
 " Lord , Lord , how mercenary you young man be ! " cry the elder , simply . " always think about money nowadays ! happy he who have the girl , I should say the money be not   the question , my dear sir , when it go along with such a lovely young thing as that though I humbly say it , who ought not , and who be her fond silly old grandfather . we be talk about you , Lyddy darling come , give I a kiss , my blessing ! we be talk about you , and Mr. George say he would not   take you with all the money your poor old grandfather can give you . " 
 " Nay , sir , " say George . 
 " well , you be right to say nay , for I do not   say all , that be the truth . my Blessing will have a deal more than that trifle I speak of , when it shall please Heaven to remove I out of this world to a well when poor old Gappy be go , Lyddy will be a rich little Lyddy , that she will . but she do not   wish I to go yet , do she ? " 
 " oh , you darle dear grandpapa ! " say Lyddy . 
 " this young gentleman will not   have you . "   Lyddy look an arch " thank you , sir , " from her brown eye .   " but at any rate he be honest , and that be more than we can say of some folk in this wicked London . oh , Lord , Lord , how mercenary they be ! do you know that yonder , in Monument Yard , they be all at my poor little blessing for her money ? there be Tom Lutestring ; there be Mr. Draper , your precious lawyer ; there be actually Mr. Tubbs , of Bethesda Chapel ; and they must all come buzz like fly round the honey - pot . that be why we come out of the quarter where my brother - tradesman live . " 
 " to avoid the fly , to be sure ! " say Miss Lydia , toss up her little head . 
 " where my brother - tradesman live , " continue the old gentleman . " else who be I to think of consort with your grandee and fine folk ? I do not   care for the fashion , Mr. George ; I do not   care for play and poetry , beg your honour 's pardon ; I never go to a play in my life , but to please this little minx . " 
 " oh , sir , ' twa lovely ! and I cry so , do not   I , grandpapa ? " say the child . 
 " at what , my dear ? " 
 " at at Mr. Warrington 's play , grandpapa . " 
 " do you , my dear ? I dare say ; I dare say ! it be mail day : and my letter have come in : and my ship the Lovely Lyddy have just come into Falmouth ; and Captain Joyce report how he have mercifully escape a french privateer ; and my head be so full of thank for that escape , which save I a deal of money , Mr. George for the rate at which ship be underwrite this war - time be so scandalous that I often prefer to venture than to insure that I confess I do not   listen much to the play , sir , and only go to please this little Lyddy . " 
 " and you do please I , dear Gappy ! " cry the young lady . 
 " bless you ! then it be all I want . what do a man want more here below than to please his child , Mr. George ? especially I , who know what be to be unhappy when I be young , and to repent of have treat this darling 's father too hard . " 
 " oh , grandpapa ! " cry the child , with more caress . 
 " yes , I be too hard with he , dear ; and that be why I spoil my little lydkin so ! " 
 more kiss ensue between Lyddy and Gappy . the little creature fling the pretty polished arm round the old man 's neck , press the dark red lip on his wither cheek , surround the venerable head with a halo of powder beat out of his wig by her caress ; and eye Mr. George the while , as much as to say , there , sir ! should you not like I to do as much for you ? 
 we confess ; but do we confess all ? George certainly tell the story of his interview with Lyddy and Gappy , and the old man 's news regard his granddaughter 's wealth ; but I do not   think he tell everything ; else Theo would scarce have be so much interested , or so entirely amused and good - humour with Lyddy when next the two young lady meet . 
 they meet now pretty frequently , especially after the old american gentleman take up his residence in Bloomsbury . Mr. Van den Bosch be in the city for the most part of the day , attend to his affair , and appear at his place upon ' Change . during his absence Lyddy have the command of the house , and receive her guest there like a lady , or ride abroad in a fine coach , which she order her grandpapa to keep for she , and into which he could very seldom be induce to set his foot . before long Miss Lyddy be as easy in the coach as if she have ride in one all her life . she order the domestic here and there ; she drive to the mercer 's and the jeweller 's , and she call upon her friend with the utmost stateliness , or ride abroad with they to take the air . Theo and Hetty be both greatly divert with she : but would the eld have be quite as well pleased have she know all Miss Lyddy 's doing ? not that Theo be of a jealous disposition , far otherwise ; but there be case when a lady have a right to a little jealousy , as I maintain , whatever my fair reader may say to the contrary . 
 it be because she know he be engage , very likely , that Miss Lyddy permit herself to speak so frankly in Mr. George 's praise . when they be alone and this bless chance occur pretty often at Mr. Van den Bosch 's house , for we have say he be constantly absent on one errand or the other it be wonderful how artlessly the little creature would show her enthusiasm , ask he all sort of simple question about himself , his genius , his way of life at home and in London , his project of marriage , and so forth . 
 " I be glad you be go to be marry , oh , so glad ! " she would say , heave the most piteous sigh the while ; " for I can talk to you frankly , quite frankly as a brother , and not be afraid of that odious politeness about which they be always scold I at boarding - school . I may speak to you frankly ; and if I like you , I may say so , may not   I , Mr. George ? " 
 " pray , say so , " say George , with a bow and a smile . " that be a kind of talk which most man delight to hear , especially from such pretty lip as Miss Lydia 's . " 
 " what do you know about my lip ? " say the girl , with a pout and an innocent look into his face . 
 " what , indeed ? " ask George . " perhaps I should like to know a great deal more . " 
 " they do not   tell nothing but truth , anyhow ! " say the girl ; " that be why some people do not   like they ! if I have anything on my mind , it must come out . I be a country - breed girl , I be with my heart in my mouth all honesty and simplicity ; not like your english girl , who have learn I do not   know what at their boarding - school , and from the man afterwards . " 
 " our girl be monstrous little hypocrite , indeed ! " cry George . 
 " you be think of Miss Lamberts ? and I might have think of they ; but I declare I do not then . they have be at boarding - school ; they have be in the world a great deal so much the great pity for they , for be certain they learn no good there . and now I have say so , of course you will go and tell Miss Theo , will not   you , sir ? " 
 " that she have learn no good in the world ? she have scarce speak to man at all , except her father , her brother , and I . which of we would teach she any wrong , think you ? " 
 " oh , not you ! though I can understand its be very dangerous to be with you ! " say the girl , with a sigh . 
 " indeed there be no danger , and I do not   bite ! " say George , laugh . 
 " I do not   say bite , " say the girl , softly . " there be other thing dangerous besides bite , I should think . be not   you very witty ? yes , and sarcastic , and clever , and always laugh at people ? have not   you a coax tongue ? if you be to look at I in that kind of way , I do not   know what would come to I . be your brother like you , as I be to have marry ? be he as clever and witty as you ? I have hear he be like you : but he have not   your coax tongue . Heigho ! ' tis well you be engage , Master George , that be all . do you think if you have see I first , you would have like Miss Theo well ? " 
 " they say marriage be make in Heaven , my dear , and let we trust that mine have be arrange there , " say George . 
 " I suppose there be no such thing never know , as a man have two sweetheart ? " ask the artless little maiden . " guess it be a pity . o I ! what nonsense I be a - talk ; there now ! I be like the little girl who cry for the moon ; and I can not   have it . ' tis too high for I too high and splendid and shin : can not   reach up to it nohow . well , what a foolish , wayward , little spoilt thing I be now ! but one thing you promise.-on your word and your honour , now , Mr. George ? " 
 " and what be that ? " 
 " that you will not   tell Miss Theo , else she will hate I . " 
 " why should she hate you ? " 
 " because I hate she , and wish she be dead ! " break out the young lady . and the eye that be look so gentle and lachrymose but now , flame with sudden wrath , and her cheek flush up . " for shame ! " she add , after a pause . " I be a little fool to speak ! but whatever be in my heart must come out . I be a girl of the wood , I be . I be breed where the sun be hot than in this foggy climate . and I be not like your cold english girl ; who , before they speak , or think , or feel , must wait for mamma to give leave . there , there ! I may be a little fool for say what I have . I know you will go and tell Miss Lambert . well , do ! " 
 but , as we have say , George do not   tell Miss Lambert . even from the beloved person there must be some thing keep secret ; even to himself , perhaps , he do not quite acknowledge what be the meaning of the little girl 's confession ; or , if he acknowledge it , do not act on it ; except in so far as this , perhaps , that my gentleman , in Miss Lydia 's presence , be particularly courteous and tender ; and in her absence think of she very kindly , and always with a certain pleasure . it be hard , indeed , if a man might not repay by a little kindness and gratitude the artless affection of such a warm young heart . 
 what be that story meanwhile which come round to our friend , of young Mr. Lutestring and young Mr. Drabshaw the Quaker have a boxing - match at a tavern in the city , and all about this young lady ? they fall out over their cup , and fight probably . why do Mr. Draper , who have praise she so at first , tell such story now against her grandfather ? " I suspect , " say Madame de Bernstein , " that he want the girl for some client or relation of his own ; and that he tell these tale in order to frighten all suitor from she . when she and her grandfather come to I , she behave perfectly well ; and I confess , sir , I think it be a great pity that you should prefer yonder red - cheeked countrifie little chit , without a halfpenny , to this pretty , wild , artless girl , with such a fortune as I hear she have . " 
 " oh , she have be with you , have she , aunt ? " ask George of his relative . 
 " of course she have be with I , " the other reply , curtly . " unless your brother have be so silly as to fall in love with that other little lambert girl " 
 " indeed , ma'am , I think I can say he have not , " George remark . 
 " why , then , when he come back with Mr. Wolfe , should he not take a fancy to this little person , as his mamma wish only , to do we justice , we esmond care very little for what our mamma wish and marry she , and set up beside you in Virginia ? she be to have a great fortune , which you will not   touch . pray , why should it go out of the family ? " 
 George now learn that Mr. Van den Bosch and his granddaughter have be often at Madame de Bernstein 's house . take his favourite walk with his favourite companion to Kensington Gardens , he see Mr. Van den Bosch 's chariot turn into Kensington Square . the Americans be go to visit Lady Castlewood , then ? he find , on some little inquiry , that they have be more than once with her ladyship . it be , perhaps , strange that they should have say nothing of their visit to George ; but , be little curious of other people 's affair , and have no intrigue or mystery of his own , George be quite slow to imagine they in other people . what matter to he how often Kensington entertain Bloomsbury , or Bloomsbury make its bow at Kensington ? 
 a number of thing be happen at both place , of which our Virginian have not the slight idea . indeed , do not thing happen under our eye , and we not see they ? be not comedy and tragedy daily perform before we of which we understand neither the fun nor the pathos ? very likely George go home thinking to himself , " I have make an impression on the heart of this young creature . she have almost confess as much . poor artless little maiden ! I wonder what there be in I that she should like I ? " can he be angry with she for this unlucky preference ? be ever a man angry at such a reason ? he would not have be so well pleased , perhaps , have he know all ; and that he be only one of the performer in the comedy , not the principal character by any mean ; Rosencrantz and Guildenstern in the Tragedy , the part of Hamlet by a gentleman unknown . how often be our little vanity shock in this way , and subject to wholesome humiliation ! have you not fancy that Lucinda 's eye beam on you with a special tenderness , and presently become aware that she ogle your neighbour with the very same killing glance ? have you not exchange exquisite whisper with lalage at the dinner - table   sweet murmur hear through the hum of the guest , and clatter of the banquet !   and then overheard her whisper the very same delicious phrase to old Surdus in the drawing - room ? the sun shin for everybody ; the flower smell sweet for all nose ; and the nightingale and Lalage warble for all ear not your long one only , good Brother ! 




 CHAPTER LXX . in which Cupid play a Considerable Part 

 we must now , however , and before we proceed with the history of Miss Lydia and her doing , perform the duty of explain that sentence in Mr. Warrington 's letter to his brother which refer to Lady Maria Esmond , and which , to some simple reader , may be still mysterious . for how , indeed , could well - regulate person divine such a secret ? how could innocent and respectable young people suppose that a woman of noble birth , of ancient family , of mature experience , a woman whom we have see exceedingly in love only a score of month ago , should so far forget herself as   oh , my very finger - tip blush as I write the sentence !   as not only to fall in love with a person of low origin , and very many year her junior , but actually to marry he in the face of the world ? that be , not exactly in the face , but behind the back of the world , so to speak ; for Parson Sampson privily tie the indissoluble knot for the pair at his chapel in Mayfair . 
 now stop before you condemn she utterly . because Lady Maria have have , and overcome , a foolish partiality for her young cousin , be that any reason why she should never fall in love with anybody else ? be man to have the sole privilege of change , and be woman to be rebuke for avail themselves now and again of their little chance of consolation ? no invective can be more rude , gross , and unphilosophical than , for instance , Hamlet 's to his mother about her second marriage . the truth , very likely , be , that that tender , parasitic creature want a something to cling to , and , Hamlet senior out of the way , twin herself round Claudius . Nay , we have know female so bent on attach themselves , that they can twine round two gentleman at once . why , forsooth , shall there not be marriage - table after funeral bake - meat ? if you say grace for your feast yesterday , be that any reason why you shall not be hungry to - day ? your natural fine appetite and relish for this evening 's feast , show that to - morrow evening at eight o'clock you will most probably be in want of your dinner . I , for my part , when Flirtilla or Jiltissa be partial to I   the kind reader will please to fancy that I be allude here to person of the most ravish beauty and lofty rank , always use to bear in mind that a time would come when they would be fond of somebody else . we be serve a la Russe , and gobble up a dish at a time , like the folk in Polyphemus 's cave . ' tis hodie mihi , cra tibi : there be some Anthropophagi who devour dozen of we , the old , the young , the tender , the tough , the plump , the lean , the ugly , the beautiful : there be no escape , and one after another , as our fate be , we disappear down their omnivorous maw . look at Lady Ogresham ! we all remember , last year , how she serve poor Tom Kydd : seize upon he , devour he , pick his bone , and fling they away . now it be Ned Suckling she have get into her den . he lie under her great eye , quivering and fascinated . look at the poor little trepid creature , pant and helpless under the great eye ! she trail towards he nearer and nearer ; he draw to she , close and close . presently there will be one or two feeble squeak for pity , and hobblegobble he will disappear ! ah I ! it be pity , too . I know , for instance , that Maria Esmond have lose her heart ever so many time before Harry Warrington find it ; but I like to fancy that he be go to keep it ; that , bewail mischance and time out of joint , she would yet have preserve her love , and fondle it in decorous celibacy . if , in some paroxysm of senile folly , I should fall in love to - morrow , I shall still try and think I have acquire the fee - simple of my charmer 's heart ; not that I be only a tenant , on a short lease , of an old battered furnished apartment , where the dingy old wine - glass have be cloud by score of pair of lip , and the tumbled old sofa be muddy with the last lodger 's boot . dear , dear nymph ! be beloved and beautiful ! suppose I have a little pass passion for Glycera   and her complexion really be as pure as splendent parian marble ; suppose you have a fancy for Telephus , and his low collar and absurd neck ; those folly be all over now , be not   they ? we love each other for good now , do not   we ? yes , for ever ; and Glycera may go to Bath , and Telephus take his cervicem roseam to Jack Ketch , n'est - ce pas ? 
 no . we never think of change , my dear . however wind blow , or time fly , or spoon stir , our potage , which be now so pip hot , will never get cold . pass fancy we may have allow ourselves in former day ; and really your infatuation for Telephus   do not   frown so , my darling creature ! and make the wrinkle in your forehead worse   I say , really it be the talk of the whole town ; and as for Glycera , she behave confoundedly ill to I . well , well , now that we understand each other , it be for ever that our heart be united , and we can look at Sir Cresswell Cresswell , and snap our finger at his wig . but this Maria of the last century be a woman of an ill - regulated mind . you , my love , who know the world , know that in the course of this lady 's career a great deal must have pass that would not bear the light , or edify in the telling . you know   not , my dear creature , that I mean you have any experience ; but you have hear people say you have hear your mother say   that an old flirt , when she have do play the fool with one passion , will play the fool with another ; that flirt be like drinking ; and the brandy be drunk up , you no , not you Glycera the brandy be drunk up , Glycera , who have take to drinking , will fall upon the gin . so , if Maria Esmond have find a successor for Harry Warrington , and set up a new sultan in the precious empire of her heart , what , after all , could you expect from she ? that territory be like the Low Countries , accustom to be conquer , and for ever open to invasion . 
 and Maria 's present enslaver be no other than Mr. Geoghegan or Hagan , the young actor who have perform in George 's tragedy . his tone be so thrilling , his eye so bright , his mien so noble , he look so beautiful in his gilt leather armour and large buckled periwig , give utterance to the poet 's glow verse , that the lady 's heart be yield up to he , even as Ariadne 's to Bacchus when her affair with Theseus be over . the young Irishman be not a little touch and elated by the highborn damsel 's partiality for he . he might have prefer a Lady Maria Hagan more tender in year , but one more tender in disposition it be difficult to discover . she cling to he closely , indeed . she retire to his humble lodging in Westminster with he , when it become necessary to disclose their marriage , and when her furious relative disown she . 
 General Lambert bring the news home from his office in Whitehall one day , and make merry over it with his family . in those homely time a joke be none the bad for be a little broad ; and a fine lady would laugh at a jolly page of Fielding , and weep over a letter of Clarissa , which would make your present ladyship 's eye start out of your head with horror . he utter all sort of waggery , do the merry General , upon the subject of this marriage ; upon George 's share in bring it about ; upon Barry 's jealousy when he should hear of it , he vow it be cruel that cousin Hagan have not select George as groomsman ; that the first child should be call Carpezan or Sybilla , after the tragedy , and so forth . they would not quite be able to keep a coach , but they might get a chariot and pasteboard dragon from Mr. Rich 's theatre . the baby might be christen in Macbeth 's caldron ; and Harry and harlequin ought certainly to be godfather . 
 " why should not   she marry he if she like he ? " ask little Hetty . " why should he not love she because she be a little old ? Mamma be a little old , and you love she none the bad . when you marry my mamma , sir , I have hear you say you be very poor ; and yet you be very happy , and nobody laugh at you ! " thus this impudent little person speak by reason of her tender age , not be aware of Lady Maria Esmond 's previous folly . 
 so her family have desert she ? George describe what wrath they be in ; how Lady Castlewood have go into mourning ; how Mr. will swore he would have the rascal 's ear ; how furious Madame de Bernstein be , the most angry of all . " it be an insult to the family , " say haughty little Miss Hett ; " and I can fancy how lady of that rank must be indignant at their relative 's marriage with a person of Mr. Hagan 's condition ; but to desert she be a very different matter . " 
 " indeed , my dear child , " cry mamma , " you be talk of what you do not   understand . after my Lady Maria 's conduct , no respectable person can go to see she . " 
 " what conduct , mamma ? " 
 " never mind , " cry mamma . " little girl can not   be expect to know , and ought not to be too curious to inquire , what Lady Maria 's conduct have be ! suffice it , miss , that I be shock her ladyship should ever have be here ; and I say again , no honest person should associate with she ! " 
 " then , Aunt Lambert , I must be whip and send to bed , " say George , with mock gravity . " I own to you   though I do not confess soon , see that the affair be not mine   that I have be to see my cousin the player , and her ladyship his wife . I find they in very dirty lodging in Westminster , where the wretch have the shabbiness to keep not only his wife , but his old mother , and a little brother , whom he put to school . I find Mr. Hagan , and come away with a liking , and almost a respect for he , although I own he have make a very improvident marriage . but how improvident some folk be about marriage , be not   they , Theo ? " 
 " Improvident , if they marry such spendthrift as you , " say the General . " Master George find his relation , and I will be bind to say he leave his purse behind he . " 
 " no , not the purse , sir , " say George , smile very tenderly . " Theo make that . but I be bind to own it come empty away . Mr. Rich be in great dudgeon . he say he hardly dare have Hagan on his stage , and be afraid of a riot , such as Mr. Garrick have about the foreign dancer . this be to be a fine gentleman 's riot . the macaroni be furious , and vow they will pelt Mr. Hagan , and have he cudgel afterwards . my cousin will , at Arthur 's , have take his oath he will have the actor 's ear . meanwhile , as the poor man do not play , they have cut off his salary ; and without his salary , this luckless pair of lover have no mean to buy bread and cheese . " 
 " and you take it to they , sir ? it be like you , George ! " say Theo , worship he with her eye . 
 " it be your purse take it , dear Theo ! " reply George . 
 " Mamma , I hope you will go and see they to - morrow ! " pray Theo . 
 " if she do not , I shall get a divorce , my dear ! " cry papa . " come and kiss I , you little wench that be , avec la bonne permission de monsieur mon beau - fil . " 
 " Monsieur mon beau fiddlestick , papa ! " say Miss Lambert , and I have no doubt complie with the paternal order . and this be the first time George Esmond Warrington , Esquire , be ever call a fiddlestick . 
 any man , even in our time , who make an imprudent marriage , know how he have to run the gauntlet of the family , and undergo the abuse , the scorn , the wrath , the pity of his relation . if your respectable family cry out because you marry the curate 's daughter , one in ten , let we say , of his charming child ; or because you engage yourself to the young barrister whose only present pecuniary resource come from the court which he report , and who will have to pay his Oxford bill out of your slender little fortune ; if your friend cry out for make such engagement as these , fancy the feeling of Lady Maria Hagan 's friend , and even those of Mr. Hagan 's , on the announcement of this marriage . 
 there be old Mrs. Hagan , in the first instance . her son have keep she dutifully and in tolerable comfort , ever since he leave Trinity College at his father 's death , and appear as Romeo at Crow Street Theatre . his salary have suffice of late year to keep the brother at school , to help the sister who have go out as companion , and to provide fire , clothing , tea , dinner , and comfort for the old clergyman 's widow . and now , forsooth , a fine lady , with all sort of extravagant habit , must come and take possession of the humble home , and share the scanty loaf and mutton ! be Hagan not a high - spirited fellow , and the old mother very much afraid of he , I doubt whether my lady 's life at the Westminster lodging would be very comfortable . it be very selfish perhaps to take a place at that small table , and in poor Hagan 's narrow bed . but love in some passionate and romantic disposition never regard consequence , or measure accommodation . who have not experience that frame of mind ; what thrifty wife have not see and lament her husband in that condition ; when , with rather a heighten colour and a deuce - may - care smile on his face , he come home and announce that he have ask twenty people to dinner next Saturday ? he do not   know whom exactly ; and he do know the dining - room will only hold sixteen . never mind ! two of the prettiest girl can sit upon young gentleman 's knee : other will not   come : there be sure to be plenty ! in the intoxication of love people venture upon this dangerous sort of housekeeping ; they do not   calculate the resource of their dining - table , or those inevitable butcher ' and fishmonger ' bill which will be bring to the ghastly housekeeper at the beginning of the month . 
 yes : it be rather selfish of my Lady Maria to seat herself at Hagan 's table and take the cream off the milk , and the wing of the chicken , and the good half of everything where there be only enough before ; and no wonder the poor old mamma - in - law be disposed to grumble . but what be her outcry compare to the clamour at Kensington among Lady Maria 's noble family ? think of the talk and scandal all over the town ! think of the titter and whisper of the lady in attendance at the Princess 's court , where Lady Fanny have a place ; of the joke of Mr. Will 's brother - officer at the usher 's table ; of the waggery in the daily print and magazine ; of the comment of outraged prude ; of the laughter of the club and the sneer of the ungodly ! at the receipt of the news Madame Bernstein have fit and run off to the solitude of her dear rock at Tunbridge Wells , where she do not see above forty people of a night at card . my lord refuse to see his sister ; and the Countess in mourning , as we have say , wait upon one of her patroness , a gracious Princess , who be pleased to condole with she upon the disgrace and calamity which have befall her house . for one , two , three whole day the town be excite and amuse by the scandal ; then there come other news a victory in Germany ; doubtful account from America ; a general officer come home to take his trial ; an exquisite new soprano singer from Italy ; and the public forget Lady Maria in her garret , eat the hard - earn meal of the actor 's family . 
 this be an extract from Mr. George Warrington 's letter to his brother , in which he describe other personal matter , as well as a visit he have pay to the newly marry pair : 
 " my dearest little Theo , " he write , " be eager to accompany her mamma upon this errand of charity ; but I think Aunt Lambert 's visit would be good under the circumstance , and without the attendance of her little spinster aide - de - camp . Cousin Hagan be out when we call ; we find her ladyship in a loose undress , and with her hair in not the neat paper , play at cribbage with a neighbour from the second floor , while good Mrs. Hagan sate on the other side of the fire with a glass of punch , and the Whole Duty of Man . 
 " Maria , your Maria once , cry a little when she see we ; and Aunt Lambert , you may be sure , be ready with her sympathy . while she bestow it on Lady Maria , I pay the good compliment I could invent to the old lady . when the conversation between Aunt L. and the bride begin to flag , I turn to the latter , and between we we do our good to make a dreary interview pleasant . our talk be about you , about Wolfe , about war ; you must be engage face to face with the Frenchmen by this time , and God send my dear brother safe and victorious out of the battle ! be sure we follow your step anxiously we fancy you at Cape Breton . we have plan of Quebec , and chart of the St. Lawrence . shall I ever forget your face of joy that day when you see I return safe and sound from the little combat with the little Frenchman ? so will my Harry , I know , return from his battle . I feel quite assured of it ; elated somehow with the prospect of your certain success and safety . and I have make all here share my cheerfulness . we talk of the campaign as over , and Captain Warrington 's promotion as secure . pray Heaven , all our hope may be fulfil one day ere long . 
 " how strange it be that you who be the mettlesome fellow   you know you be   should escape quarrel hitherto , and I , who be a peaceful youth , wish no harm to anybody , should have battle thrust upon I ! what do you think actually of my have have another affair upon my wicked hand , and with whom , think you ? with no less a personage than your old enemy , our kinsman , Mr. Will . 
 " what or who set he to quarrel with I , I can not think . Spencer   who act as second for I , for matter actually have go this length ; do not   be frighten ; it be all over , and nobody be a scratch the bad   think some one set Will on I , but who , I say ? his conduct have be most singular ; his behaviour quite unbearable . we have meet pretty frequently lately at the house of good Mr. Van den Bosch , whose pretty granddaughter be consign to both of we by our good mother . oh , dear mother ! do you know that the little thing be to be such a causa belli , and to cause sword to be draw , and precious life to be menace ? but so it have be . to show his own spirit , I suppose , or have some reasonable doubt about mine , whenever Will and I have meet at Mynheer 's house and he be for ever go there he have show such downright rudeness to I , that I have require more than ordinary patience to keep my temper . he have contradict I once , twice , thrice in the presence of the family , and out of sheer spite and rage , as it appear to I . be he pay his address to Miss Lydia , and her father 's ship , negro , and forty thousand pound ? I should guess so . the old gentleman be for ever talk about his money , and adore his granddaughter , and as she be a beautiful little creature , number of folk here be ready to adore she too . be will rascal enough to fancy that I would give up my Theo for a million of guineas , and negro , and Venus to boot ? could the thought of such baseness enter into the man 's mind ? I do not   know that he have accuse I of steal Van den Bosch 's spoon and tankard when we dine there , or of rob on the highway . but for one reason or the other he have choose to be jealous of I , and as I have parry his impertinence with little sarcastic speech   though perfectly civil before company , perhaps I have once or twice make he angry . our little Miss Lydia have unwittingly add fuel to the fire on more than one occasion , especially yesterday , when there be talk about your worship . 
 " ' ah ! ' say the heedless little thing , as we sit over our dessert , " tis lucky for you , Mr. Esmond , that Captain Harry be not here . ' 
 " ' why , miss ? ' ask he , with one of his usual conversational ornament . he must have offend some fairy in his youth , who have cause he to drop curse for ever out of his mouth , as she do the girl to spit out toad and serpent .   I know some one from whose gentle lip there only fall pure pearl and diamond .   ' why ? ' say will , with a cannonade of oath . 
 " ' o fie ! ' say she , put up the prettiest little finger to the prettiest little rosy ear in the world . ' o fie , sir ! to use such naughty word . ' tis lucky the Captain be not here , because he might quarrel with you ; and Mr. George be so peaceable and quiet , that he will not . have you hear from the Captain , Mr. George ? ' 
 " ' from Cape Breton , ' say I. ' he be very well , thank you ; that be ' I could not   finish the sentence , for I be in such a rage that I scarce could contain myself . 
 " ' from the Captain , as you call he , Miss Lyddy , ' say Will . ' he will distinguish himself as he do at Saint Cas ! Ho , ho ! ' 
 " ' so I apprehend he do , sir , ' say Will 's brother . 
 " ' do he ? ' say our dear cousin ; ' always think he run away ; take to his leg ; get a ducking , and run away as if a bailiff be after he . ' 
 " ' La ! ' say Miss , ' do the Captain ever have a bailiff after he ? ' 
 " ' do not   he ? Ho , ho ! ' laugh Mr. Will . 
 " I suppose I must have look very savage , for Spencer , who be din with we , trod on my foot under the table . ' do not   laugh so loud , cousin , ' I say , very gently ; ' you may wake good old Mr. Van den Bosch . ' the good old gentleman be asleep in his arm - chair , to which he commonly retire for a nap after dinner . 
 " ' oh , indeed , cousin , ' say will , and he turn and wink at a friend of his , Captain Deuceace , whose own and whose wife 's reputation I dare say you hear of when you frequent the club , and whom will have introduce into this simple family as a man of the high fashion . ' do not   be afraid , miss , ' say Mr. Will , ' nor my cousin need not   be . ' 
 " ' oh , what a comfort ! ' cry Miss Lyddy . ' keep quite quiet , gentleman , and do not   quarrel , and come up to I when I send to say the tea be ready . ' and with this she make a sweet little curtsey , and disappear . 
 " ' hang it , Jack , pass the bottle , and do not   wake the old gentleman ! ' continue Mr. Will . ' will not   you help yourself , cousin ? ' he continue ; be particularly facetious in the tone of that word cousin . 
 " ' I be go to help myself , ' I say ; ' but I be not go to drink the glass ; and I will tell you what I be go to do with it , if you will be quite quiet , cousin . '   desperate kick from Spencer all this time . 
 " ' and what the deuce do I care what you be go to do with it ? ' ask Will , look rather white . 
 " ' I be go to fle it into your face , cousin , ' say I , very rapidly perform that feat . 
 " ' by Jove , and no mistake ! ' cry Mr. Deuceace ; and as he and William roar out an oath together , good old Van den Bosch wake up , and , take the pocket - handkerchief off his face , ask what be the matter . 
 " I remark it be only a glass of wine go the wrong way and the old man say ; ' well , well , there be more where that come from ! let the butler bring you what you please , young gentleman ! ' and he sink back in his great chair , and begin to sleep again . 
 " ' from the back of Montagu House Gardens there be a beautiful view of Hampstead at six o'clock in the morning ; and the statue of the King on St. George 's Church be reckon elegant , cousin ! ' say I , resume the conversation . 
 " ' d the statue ! ' begin will ; but I say , ' do not , cousin ! or you will wake up the old gentleman . have we not well go upstairs to Miss Lyddy 's tea - table ? ' 
 " we arrange a little meeting for the next morning ; and a coroner might have be sit upon one or other , or both , of our body this afternoon ; but , would you believe it ? just as our engagement be about to take place , we be interrupt by three of Sir John Fielding 's man , and carry to Bow Street , and ignominiously bind over to keep the peace . 
 " who give the information ? not I , or Spencer , I can vow . though I own I be pleased when the constable come run to we ; bludgeon in hand : for I have no wish to take Will 's blood , or sacrifice my own to such a rascal . now , sir , have you such a battle as this to describe to I ? a battle of powder and no shot ? a battle of sword as bloody as any on the stage ? I have fill my paper , without finish the story of Maria and her Hagan . you must have it by the next ship . you see , the quarrel with will take place yesterday , very soon after I have write the first sentence or two of my letter . I have be dawdle till dinner - time   I look at the paper last night , when I be grimly make certain little account up , and wonder shall I ever finish this letter ? , and now the quarrel have be so much more interesting to I than poor Molly 's love - adventure , that behold my paper be full to the brim ! wherever my dear Harry read it , I know that there will be a heart full of love for his love brother , 
 " G. E. W. " 




 CHAPTER LXXI . White Favours 

 the little quarrel between George and his cousin cause the former to discontinue his visit to Bloomsbury in a great measure ; for Mr. Will be more than ever assiduous in his attention ; and , now that both be bind over to peace , so outrageous in his behaviour , that George find the great difficulty in keep his hand from his cousin . the artless little Lydia have certainly a queer way of receive her friend . but six week before madly jealous of George 's preference for another , she now take occasion repeatedly to compliment Theo in her conversation . Miss Theo be such a quiet , gentle creature , Lyddy be sure George be just the husband for she . how fortunate that horrible quarrel have be prevent ! the constable have come up just in time ; and it be quite ridiculous to hear Mr. Esmond curse and swearing , and the rage he be in at be disappoint of his duel ! " but the arrival of the constable save your valuable life , dear Mr. George , and I be sure Miss Theo ought to bless they forever , " say Lyddy , with a soft smile . " you will not   stop and meet Mr. Esmond at dinner to - day ? you do not   like be in his company ? he can not   do you any harm ; and I be sure you will do he none . " kind speech like these address by a little girl to a gentleman , and speak by a strange inadvertency in company , and when other gentleman and lady be present , be not likely to render Mr. Warrington very eager for the society of the young american lady . 
 George 's meeting with Mr. Will be not know for some day in Dean Street , for he do not wish to disturb those kind folk with his quarrel ; but when the lady be make aware of it , you may be sure there be a great flurry and to - do . " you be actually go to take a fellow - creature 's life , and you come to see we , and say not a word ! oh , George , it be shocking ! " say Theo . 
 " my dear , he have insult I and my brother , " plead George . " could I let he call we both coward , and sit by and say , thank you ? " 
 the General sate by and look very grave . 
 " you know you think , papa , it be a wicked and un - christian practice ; and have often say you wish gentleman would have the courage to refuse ! " 
 " to refuse ? yes , " say Mr. Lambert , still very glum . 
 " it must require a prodigious strength of mind to refuse , " say Jack Lambert , look as gloomy as his father ; " and I think if any man be to call I a coward , I should be apt to forget my order . " 
 " you see brother Jack be with I ! " cry George . 
 " I must not be against you , Mr. Warrington , " say Jack Lambert . 
 " Mr. Warrington ! " cry George , turn very red . 
 " would you , a clergyman , have George break the Commandments , and commit murder , John ? " ask Theo , aghast . 
 " I be a soldier 's son , sister , " say the young divine , drily . " besides , Mr. Warrington have commit no murder at all . we must soon be hear from Canada , father . the great question of the supremacy of the two race must be try there ere long ! " he turn his back on George as he speak , and the latter eye he with wonder . 
 Hetty , look rather pale at this original remark of brother Jack , be call out of the room by some artful pretext of her sister . George start up and follow the retreat girl to the door . 
 " great power , gentleman ! " say he , come back , " I believe , on my honour , you be give I the credit of shirk this affair with Mr. Esmond ! " the clergyman and his father look at one another . 
 " a man 's near and dearest be always the first to insult he , " say George , flash out . 
 " you mean to say , ' not guilty ? ' God bless thee , my boy ! " cry the General . " I tell thee so , Jack . " and he rub his hand across his eye , and blush , and wring George 's hand with all his might . 
 " not guilty of what , in heaven 's name ? " ask Mr. Warrington . 
 " Nay , " say the General , " Mr. Jack , here , bring the story . let he tell it . I believe ' tis a lie , with all my heart . " and utter this wicked expression , the General fairly walk out of the room . 
 the Rev. J. Lambert look uncommonly foolish . 
 " and what be this this d d lie , sir , that somebody have be tell of I ? " ask George , grin at the young clergyman . 
 " to question the courage of any man be always an offence to he , " say Mr. Lambert , " and I rejoice that yours have be belie . " 
 " who tell the falsehood , sir , which you repeat ? " bawl out Mr. Warrington . " I insist on the man 's name ! " 
 " you forget you be bind over to keep the peace , " say Jack . 
 " curse the peace , sir ! we can go and fight in Holland . tell I the man 's name , I say ! " 
 " fair and softly , Mr. Warrington ! " cry the young parson ; " my hearing be perfectly good . it be not a man who tell I the story which , I confess , I impart to my father . " 
 " what ? " ask George , the truth suddenly occur . " be it that artful , wicked little vixen in Bloomsbury Square ? " 
 " Vixen be not the word to apply to any young lady , George Warrington ! " exclaim Lambert , " much less to the charming Miss Lydia . she artful the most innocent of Heaven 's creature ! she wicke that angel ! with unfeigned delight that the quarrel should be over with devout gratitude to think that blood consanguineous should not be shed she speak in term of the high praise of you for decline this quarrel , and of the deep sympathy with you for take the painful but only method of avert it . " 
 " what method ? " demand George , stamp his foot . 
 " why , of lay an information , to be sure ! " say Mr. Jack ; on which George burst forth into language much too violent for we to repeat here , and highly uncomplimentary to Miss Lydia . 
 " do not   utter such word , sir ! " cry the parson , who , as it seem , now take his turn to be angry . " do not insult , in my hearing , the most charming , the most innocent of her sex ! if she have be mistake in her information regard you , and doubt your willingness to commit what , after all , be a crime for a crime homicide be , and of the most awful description you , sir , have no right to blacken that angel 's character with foul word : and , innocent yourself , should respect the most innocent as she be the most lovely of woman ! oh , George , be you to be my brother ? " 
 " I hope to have that honour , " answer George , smile . he begin to perceive the other 's drift . 
 " what , then , what though ' tis too much bliss to be hop for by sinful man what , if she should one day be your sister ? who could see her charm without be subjugate by they ? I own that I be a slave . I own that those Latin Sapphics in the September number of the Gentleman 's magazine , begin Lydicae quondam cecinit venustae   with an english version by my friend Hickson of Corpus , be mine . I have tell my mother what hath pass between we , and Mrs. Lambert also think that the most lovely of her sex have deign to look favourably on I . I have compose a letter she another . she propose to wait on Miss Lydia 's grandpapa this very day , and to bring I the answer , which shall make I the happy or the most wretched of man ! it be in the unrestrained intercourse of family conversation that I chance to impart to my father the sentiment which my dear girl have utter . perhaps I speak slightingly of your courage , which I do not   doubt by Heaven , I do not   doubt : it may be , she have err , too , regard you . it may be that the fiend jealousy have be gnaw at my bosom , and horrible suspicion ! that I think my sister 's lover find too much favour with she I would have all my own . ah , dear George , who know his fault ? I be as one distract with passion . confound it , sir ! what right have you to laugh at I ? I would have you to know that risu inepto . " 
 " what , have you two boy make it up ? " cry the General , enter at this moment , in the midst of a roar of laughter from George . 
 " I be give my opinion to Mr. Warrington upon laughter , and upon his laughter in particular , " say Jack Lambert , in a fume .
