 " George be bind over to keep the peace , Jack ! thou canst not fight he for two year ; and between now and then , let we trust you will have make up your quarrel . here be dinner , boy ! we will drink absent friend , and an end to the war , and no fighting out of the profession ! " 
 George plead an engagement , as a reason for run away early from his dinner ; and Jack must have speedily follow he , for when the former , after transact some brief business at his own lodging , come to Mr. Van den Bosch 's door , in Bloomsbury Square , he find the young parson already in parley with a servant there . " his master and mistress have leave town yesterday , " the servant say . 
 " poor Jack ! and you have the decisive letter in your pocket ? " George ask of his future brother - in - law . 
 " well , yes , " Jack own he have the document " and my mother have order a chair , and be come to wait on Miss Lyddy , " he whisper piteously , as the young man linger on the step . 
 George have a note , too , in his pocket for the young lady , which he have not care to mention to Jack . in truth , his business at home have be to write a smart note to Miss Lyddy , with a message for the gentleman who have bring she that funny story of his give information regard the duel ! the family be absent , George , too , do not choose to leave his note . " if cousin will have be the slander - bearer , I will go and make he recant , " think George . " will the family soon be back ? " he blandly ask . 
 " they be go to visit the quality , " the servant reply . " here be the address on this paper ; " and George read , in Miss Lydia 's hand , " the box from Madam Hocquet 's to be send by the Farnham Flying Coach ; address to Miss Van den Bosch , at the Right Honourable the Earl of Castlewood 's , Castlewood , hant . " 
 " where ? " cry poor Jack , aghast . 
 " his lordship and their ladyship have be here often , " the servant say , with much importance . " the family be quite intimate . " 
 this be very strange ; for , in the course of their conversation , Lyddy have own but to one single visit from Lady Castlewood . 
 " and they must be a - go to stay there some time , for Miss have take a power of box and gown with she ! " the man add . and the young man walk away , each crumple his letter in his pocket . 
 " what be that remark you make ? " ask George of Jack , at some exclamation of the latter . " I think you say " 
 " Distraction ! I be beside myself , George ! I I scarce know what I be say , " groan the clergyman . " she be go to Hampshire , and Mr. Esmond be go with she ! " 
 " Othello could not have speak well ! and she have a pretty scoundrel in her company ! " say Mr. George . " ha ! here be your mother 's chair ! " indeed , at this moment poor Aunt Lambert come swing down Great Russell Street , precede by her footman . " ' tis no use go far , Aunt Lambert ! " cry George . " our little bird have fly . " 
 " what little bird ? " 
 " the bird Jack wish to pair with : the Lyddy bird , aunt . why , Jack , I protest you be swear again ! this morning ' twa the Sixth Commandment you want to break ; and now " 
 " confound it ! leave I alone , Mr. Warrington , do you hear ? " growl Jack , look very savage ; and away he stride far out of the reach of his mother 's bearer . 
 " what be the matter , George ? " ask the lady . 
 George , who have not be very well pleased with brother Jack 's behaviour all day , say : " Brother Jack have not a fine temper , Aunt Lambert . he inform you all that I be a coward , and remonstrate with I for be angry . he find his mistress go to the country , and he bawl , and stamp , and swear . o fie ! oh , Aunt Lambert , beware of jealousy ! do the General ever make you jealous ? " 
 " you will make I very angry if you speak to I in this way , " say poor Aunt Lambert from her chair . 
 " I be respectfully dumb . I make my bow . I withdraw , " say George , with a low bow , and turn towards Holborn . his soul be wrath within he . he be bent on quarrel with somebody . have he meet cousin will that night , it have go ill with his surety . 
 he seek Will at all his haunt , at Arthur 's , at his own house . there Lady Castlewood 's servant inform he that they believe Mr. Esmond have go to join the family in hant . he write a letter to his cousin : 
 " my dear , kind cousin William , " he say , " you know I be bind over , and would not quarrel with any one , much less with a dear , truth - telling , affectionate kinsman , whom my brother insult by can . but if you can find any one who say that I prevent a meeting the other day by give information , will you tell your informant that I think it be not I but somebody else be the coward ? and I write to Mr. Van den Bosch by the same post , to inform he and Miss Lyddy that I find some rascal have be tell they lie to my discredit , and to beg they to have a care of such person . " and , these neat letter be despatch , Mr. Warrington dress himself , show himself at the play , and take supper cheerfully at the Bedford . 
 in a few day George find a letter on his breakfast - table frank " Castlewood , " and , indeed , write by that nobleman . 
 " Dear Cousin , " my lord write , " there have be so much annoyance in our family of late , that I be sure ' tis time our quarrel should cease . two day since my brother William bring I a very angry letter , sign G. Warrington , and at the same time , to my great grief and pain , acquaint I with a quarrel that have take place between you , in which , to say the least , your conduct be violent . ' tis an ill use to put good wine to that to which you apply good Mr. Van den Bosch 's . sure , before an old man , young one should be more respectful . I do not deny that Wm . 's language and behaviour be often irritate . I know he have often try my temper , and that within the 24 hour . 
 " ah ! why should we not all live happily together ? you know , cousin , I have ever profess a sincere regard for you that I be a sincere admirer of the admirable young lady to whom you be engage , and to whom I offer my most cordial compliment and remembrance . I would live in harmony with all my family where ' tis possible the more because I hope to introduce to it a Countess of Castlewood . 
 " at my mature age , ' tis not uncommon for a man to choose a young wife . my Lydia   you will divine that I be happy in be able to call mine the elegant Miss Van den Bosch   will naturally survive I . after soothe my decline year , I shall not be jealous if at their close she should select some happy man to succeed I ; though I shall envy he the possession of so much perfection and beauty . though of a noble dutch family , her rank , the dear girl declare , be not equal to mine , which she confess that she be pleased to share . I , on the other hand , shall not be sorry to see descendant to my house , and to have it , through my Lady Castlewood 's mean , restore to something of the splendour which it know before two or three improvident predecessor impair it . my Lydia , who be by my side , send you and the charming Lambert family her warm remembrance . 
 " the marriage will take place very speedily here . may I hope to see you at church ? my brother will not be present to quarrel with you . when I and dear Lydia announce the match to he yesterday , he take the intelligence in bad part , utter language that I know he will one day regret , and be at present on a visit to some neighbour . the Dowager Lady Castlewood retain the house at Kensington ; we have our own establishment , where you will ever be welcome , dear cousin , by your affectionate humble servant , CASTLEWOOD . " 
 from the London Magazine of November 1759 : 
 " Saturday , October 13th , marry , at his seat , Castlewood , Hants , the Right Honourable Eugene , Earl of Castlewood , to the beautiful Miss Van den Bosch , of Virginia . 70,000 pound . " 




 CHAPTER LXXII .   from the Warrington MS .   in which my Lady be on the Top 

 of the Ladder 
 look across the fire , towards her accustomed chair , who have be the beloved partner of my hearth during the last half of my life , I often ask   for middle aged gentleman have the privilege of repeat their joke , their question , their story   whether two young people ever be more foolish and imprudent than we be when we marry , as we do , in the year of the old King 's death ? my son , who have take some prodigious leap in the heat of his fox - hunting , say he survey the gap and river which he cross so safely over with terror afterwards , and astonishment at his own foolhardiness in make such desperate venture ; and yet there be no more eager sportsman in the two county than Miles . he love his amusement so much that he care for no other . he have break his collar - bone , and have a hundred tumble   to his mother 's terror ; but so have his father   thinking , perhaps , of a copy of verse , or his speech at Quarter Sessions   be throw over his old mare 's head , who have slip on a stone as they be both dream along a park road at four mile an hour ; and Miles 's reckless sport have be the delight of his life , as my marriage have be the blessing of mine ; and I never think of it but to thank Heaven . mind , I do not   set up my worship as an example . I do not   say to all young folk , " go and marry upon twopence a year ; " or people would look very black at I at our vestry - meeting ; but my wife be know to be a desperate match - maker ; and when Hodge and Susan appear in my justice - room with a talk of allowance , we urge they to spend their half - crown a week at home , add a little contribution of our own , and send for the vicar . 
 now , when I ask a question of my dear oracle , I know what the answer will be ; and hence , no doubt , the reason why I so often consult she . I have but to wear a particular expression of face and my Diana take her reflection from it . suppose I say , " my dear , do not   you think the moon be make of cream cheese to - night ? " she will say , " well , papa , it do look very like cream cheese , indeed there be nobody like you for droll simile . " or , suppose I say , " my love , Mr. Pitt 's speech be very fine , but I do not   think he be equal to what I remember his father . " " nobody be equal to my Lord Chatham , " say my wife . and then one of the girl cry , " why , I have often hear our papa say Lord Chatham be a charlatan ! " on which mamma say , " how like she be to her Aunt Hetty ! " 
 as for Miles , Tros Tyriusve be all one to he . he only read the sporting announcement in the Norwich paper . so long as there be good scent , he do not care about the state of the country . I believe the rascal have never read my poem , much more my tragedy   for I mention Pocahontas to he the other day , and the dunce think she be a river in Virginia ; and with respect to my Latin verse , how can he understand they when I know he can not   construe Corderius ? why , this notebook lie publicly on the little table at my corner of the fireside , and any one may read in it who will take the trouble of lift my spectacle off the cover : but Miles never hath . I insert in the loose page caricature of Miles : joke against he : but he never know nor heed they . only once , in place of a neat drawing of mine , in China - ink , represent Miles asleep after dinner , and which my friend Bunbury would not disown , I find a rude picture of myself go over my mare Sultana 's head , and entitle " the squire on Horseback , or fish out of Water . " and the fellow to roar with laughter , and all the girl to titter , when I come upon the page ! my wife say she never be in such a fright as when I go to my book : but I can bear a joke against myself , and have hear many , though   strange to say , for one who have live among some of the chief wit of the age   I never hear a good one in my life . never mind , Miles , though thou art not a wit , I love thee none the bad   there never be any love lose between two wit in a family ; though thou hast no great beauty , thy mother think thee as handsome as Apollo , or his Royal Highness the Prince of Wales , who be bear in the very same year with thee . indeed , she always think Coates 's picture of the Prince be very like her eld boy , and have the print in her dressing - room to this very day . 
  note , in a female hand : " my son be not a spendthrift , nor a breaker of woman 's heart , as some gentleman be ; but that he be exceed like H.R.H. when they be both baby , be most certain , the Duchess of Aneaster have herself remark he in St. James 's Park , where Gumbo and my poor molly use often to take he for an airing . Th . W. " 
 in that same year , with what different prospect ! my Lord Esmond , Lord Castlewood 's son , likewise appear to adorn the world . my Lord C. and his humble servant have already come to a coolness at that time , and , heaven know ! my honest Miles 's godmother , at his entrance into life , bring no gold pap - boat to his christening ! matter have mend since , laus Deo laus Deo , indeed ! for I suspect neither Miles nor his father would ever have be able to do much for themselves , and by their own wit . 
 Castlewood House have quite a different face now from that venerable one which it wear in the day of my youth , when it be cover with the wrinkle of time , the scar of old war , the crack and blemish which year have mark on its hoary feature . I love good to remember it in its old shape , as I see it when young Mr. George Warrington go down at the owner 's invitation , to be present at his lordship 's marriage with Miss Lydia Van den Bosch " an american lady of noble family of Holland , " as the county paper announce her ladyship to be . then the tower stand as Warrington 's grandfather the Colonel   the Marquis , as Madam Esmond would like to call her father   have see they . the wood   thin not a little to be sure   stand , nay , some of the self - same rook may have caw over they , which the Colonel have see threescore year back . his picture hang in the hall which might have be his , have he not prefer love and gratitude to wealth and worldly honour ; and Mr. George Esmond Warrington   that be , Egomet Ipse who write this page down , as he walk the old place , pace the long corridor , the smooth dew - spangled terrace and cool darkle avenue , feel a while as if he be one of Mr. Walpole 's cavalier with ruff , rapier , buff - coat , and gorget , and as if an Old Pretender , or a Jesuit emissary in disguise , might appear from behind any tall tree - trunk round about the mansion , or antique carve cupboard within it . I have the strange , saddest , pleasantest , old - world fancy as I walk the place ; I imagine tragedy , intrigue , serenade , escaladoe , Oliver 's roundhead batter the tower , or bluff Hal 's Beefeaters prick over the plain before the castle . I be then court a certain young lady   madam , your ladyship 's eye have no need of spectacle then , and on the brow above they there be never a wrinkle or a silver hair , and I remember I write a ream of romantic description , under my Lord Castlewood 's frank , to the lady who never tired of read my letter then . she say I only send she three line now , when I be away in London or elsewhere . ' tis that I may not fatigue your old eye , my dear ! 
 Mr. Warrington think himself authorise to order a genteel new suit of clothe for my lord 's marriage , and with mon . gumbo in attendance , make his appearance at Castlewood a few day before the ceremony . I may mention that it have be find expedient to send my faithful Sady home on board a Virginia ship . a great inflammation attack the throat and lung , and prove fatal in very many case , in that year of Wolfe 's expedition , have seize and well - nigh kill my poor lad , for whom his native air be pronounce to be the good cure . we part with an abundance of tear , and Gumbo shed as many when his master go to Quebec : but he have attraction in this country and none for the military life , so he remain attached to my service . we find Castlewood House full of friend , relation , and visitor . Lady Fanny be there upon compulsion , a sulky bridesmaid . some of the virgin of the neighbourhood also attend the young Countess . a bishop 's widow herself , the Baroness Beatrix bring a holy brother - in - law of the bench from London to tie the holy knot of matrimony between Eugene Earl of Castlewood and Lydia Van den Bosch , spinster ; and for some time before and after the nuptial the old house in Hampshire wear an appearance of gaiety to which it have long be unaccustomed . the country family come gladly to pay their compliment to the newly married couple . the lady 's wealth be the subject of everybody 's talk , and no doubt do not decrease in the telling . those naughty story which be rife in town , and spread by her disappointed suitor there , take some little time to travel into Hampshire ; and when they reach the country find it disposed to treat Lord Castlewood 's wife with civility , and not inclined to be too curious about her behaviour in town . suppose she have jilt this man , and laugh at the other ? it be her money they be anxious about , and she be no more mercenary than they . the Hampshire folk be determined that it be a great benefit to the country to have Castlewood House once more open , with beer in the cellar , horse in the stable , and spit turn before the kitchen fire . the new lady take her place with great dignity , and ' twa certain she have uncommon accomplishment and wit . be it not write , in the marriage advertisement , that her ladyship bring her noble husband seventy thousand pound ? on a beaucoup d'esprit with seventy thousand pound . the Hampshire people say this be only a small portion of her wealth . when the grandfather should fall , ever so many plum would be find on that old tree . 
 that quiet old man , and keen reckoner , begin quickly to put the dilapidated Castlewood account in order , of which long neglect , poverty , and improvidence have hasten the ruin . the business of the old gentleman 's life now , and for some time henceforth , be to advance , improve , mend my lord 's finance ; to screw the rent up where practicable , to pare the expense of the establishment down . he could , somehow , look to every yard of worsted lace on the footman 's coat , and every pound of beef that go to their dinner . a watchful old eye note every flagon of beer which be fetch from the buttery , and mark that no waste occur in the larder . the people be few , but more regularly pay ; the livery be not so ragged , and yet the tailor have no need to dun for his money ; the gardener and groom grumble , though their wage be no long overdue : but the horse fatten on less corn , and the fruit and vegetable be ever so much more plentiful so keenly do my lady 's old grandfather keep a watch over the household affair , from his lonely little chamber in the turret . 
 these improvement , though here tell in a paragraph or two , be the affair of month and year at Castlewood ; where , with thrift , order , and judicious outlay of money   however , upon some press occasion , my lord might say he have none , the estate and household increase in prosperity . that it be a flourishing and economical household no one could deny : not even the dowager lady and her two child , who now seldom enter within Castlewood gate , my lady consider they in the light of enemy for who , indeed , would like a stepmother - in - law ? the little reign Countess give the dowager battle , and rout she utterly and speedily . though educate in the colony , and ignorant of polite life during her early year , the Countess Lydia have a power of language and a strength of will that all have to acknowledge who quarrel with she . the dowager and my Lady Fanny be no match for the young American : they flee from before she to their jointure house in Kensington , and no wonder their absence be not regret by my lord , who be in the habit of regret no one whose back be turn . could cousin Warrington , whose hand his lordship press so affectionately on come and parting , with whom cousin Eugene be so gay and frank and pleasant when they be together , expect or hope that his lordship would grieve at his departure , at his death , at any misfortune which could happen to he , or any soul alive ? Cousin Warrington know well . always of a sceptical turn , Mr. W. take a grim delight in watch the peculiarity of his neighbour , and could like this one even though he have no courage and no heart . courage ? heart ? what be these to you and I in the world ? a man may have private virtue as he may have half a million in the fund . what we du monde expect be , that he should be lively , agreeable , keep a decent figure , and pay his way . Colonel Esmond Warrington 's grandfather   in whose history and dwelling - place Mr. W. take an extraordinary interest , might once have be owner of this house of Castlewood , and of the title which belong to its possessor . the gentleman often look at the Colonel 's grave picture as it still hang in the saloon , a copy or replica of which piece Mr. Warrington fondly remember in Virginia . 
 " he must have be a little touch here , " my lord say , tap his own tall , placid forehead . 
 there be certain action , simple and common with some man , which other can not understand , and deny as utter lie , or deride as act of madness . 
 " I do you the justice to think , cousin , " say Mr. Warrington to his lordship , " that you would not give up any advantage for any friend in the world . " 
 " eh ! I be selfish : but be I more selfish than the rest of the world ? " ask my lord , with a french shrug of his shoulder , and a pinch out of his box . once , in their walk in the field , his lordship happen to wear a fine scarlet coat , a cow run towards he ; and the ordinarily languid nobleman spring over a stile with the agility of a schoolboy . he do not conceal his tremor , or his natural want of courage . " I dare say you respect I no more than I respect myself , George , " he would say , in his candid way , and begin a very pleasant sardonical discourse upon the fall of man , and his fault , and shortcoming ; and wonder why Heaven have not make we all brave and tall , and handsome and rich ? as for Mr. Warrington , who very likely love to be king of his company   as some people do , he could not help like this kinsman of his , so witty , graceful , polished , high - place in the world so utterly his inferior . like the animal in Mr. Sterne 's famous book , " do not beat I , " his lordship 's look seem to say , " but , if you will , you may . " no man , save a bully and coward himself , deal hardly with a creature so spiritless . 




 CHAPTER LXXIII . we keep Christmas at Castlewood . 1759 

 we know , my dear child , from our favourite fairy story - book , how at all christening and marriage some one be invariably disappoint , and vow vengeance ; and so need not wonder that good cousin will should curse and rage energetically at the news of his brother 's engagement with the colonial heiress . at first , will flee the house , in his wrath , swear he would never return . but nobody , include the swearer , believe much in Master Will 's oath ; and this unrepentant prodigal , after a day or two , come back to the paternal house . the fume of the marriage - feast allure he : he could not afford to resign his knife and fork at Castlewood table . he return , and drank and eat there in token of revenge . he pledge the young bride in a bumper , and drank perdition to she under his breath . he make response of smothered malediction as her father give she away in the chapel , and my lord vow to love , honour and cherish she . he be not the only grumbler respect that marriage , as Mr. Warrington know : he hear , then and afterwards , no end of abuse of my lady and her grandfather . the old gentleman 's City friend , his legal adviser , the dissent clergyman at whose chapel they attend on their first arrival in England , and poor Jack Lambert , the orthodox young divine , whose eloquence he have fondly hop have be exert over she in private , be bitter against the little lady 's treachery , and each have a story to tell of his have be enslave , encouraged , jilt , by the young American . the lawyer , who have have such an accurate list of all her property , estate , money , slave , ship , expectation , be ready to vow and swear that he believe the whole account be false ; that there be no such place as New York or Virginia ; or at any rate , that Mr. Van den Bosch have no land there ; that there be no such thing as a Guinea trade , and that the negro be so many black falsehood invent by the wily old planter . the dissent pastor moan over his stray lambling if such a little , wily , mischievous monster could be call a lamb at all . poor Jack Lambert ruefully acknowledge to his mamma the possession of a lock of black hair , which he bedew with tear and apostrophise in quite unclerical language : and , as for Mr. William Esmond , he , with the shriek and curse in which he always freely indulge , even at Castlewood , under his sister - in - law 's own pretty little nose , when under any strong emotion , call Acheron to witness , that out of that region there do not exist such an artful young devil as Miss Lydia . he swear that she be an infernal female Cerberus , and call down all the wrath of this world and the next upon his swindle rascal of a brother , who have cajole he with fair word , and filch his prize from he . 
 " why , " say Mr. Warrington   when will expatiate on these matter with he , " if the girl be such a she - devil as you describe she , you be all the well for lose she . if she intend to deceive her husband , and to give he a dose of poison , as you say , how lucky for you , you be not the man ! you ought to thank the god , will , instead of curse they , for rob you of such a fury , and can not   be well revenge on Castlewood than by allow he her sole possession . " 
 " all this be very well , " will Esmond say ; but not unjustly , perhaps , remark that his brother be not the less a scoundrel for have cheat he out of the fortune which he expect to get , and which he have risk his life to win , too . 
 George Warrington be at a loss to know how his cousin have be make so to risk his precious existence   for which , perhaps , a rope 's end have be a fitting termination , on which will Esmond , with the utmost candour , tell his kinsman how the little Cerbera have actually cause the meeting between they , which be interrupt somehow by Sir John Fielding 's man ; how she be always say that George Warrington be a coward for ever sneer at Mr. Will , and the latter doubly a poltroon for not take notice of his kinsman 's taunt ; how George have run away and nearly die of fright in Braddock 's expedition ; and " deuce take I , " say will , " I never be more surprised , cousin , than when you stand to your ground so coolly in Tottenham Court Fields yonder , for I and my second offer to wager that you would never come ! " 
 Mr. Warrington laugh , and thank Mr. Will for this opinion of he . 
 " though , " say he , " cousin , ' twa lucky for I the constable come up , or you would have whip your sword through my body in another minute . do not   you see how clumsy I be as I stand before you ? and you actually turn white and shake with anger ! " 
 " yes , curse I , " say Mr. Will   who turn very red this time , " that be my way of show my rage ; and I be confoundedly angry with you , cousin ! but now ' tis my brother I hate , and that little devil of a Countess a countess ! a pretty countess , indeed ! " and with another rumble cannonade of oath , will salute the reign member of his family . 
 " well , cousin , " say George , look he queerly in the face , " you let I off easily , and I dare say I owe my life to you , or at any rate a whole waistcoat , and I admire your forbearance and spirit . what a pity that a courage like yours should be waste as a mere court usher ! you be a loss to his Majesty 's army . you positively be ! " 
 " I never know whether you be joke or serious , Mr. Warrington , " growl Will . 
 " I should think very few gentleman would dare to joke with you , cousin , if they have a regard for their own life or ear ! cry Mr. Warrington , who love this grave way of deal with his noble kinsman , and use to watch , with a droll interest , the other choke his curse , grind his tooth because afraid to bite , and smother his cowardly anger . 
 " and you should moderate your expression , cousin , regard the dear Countess and my lord your brother , " Mr. Warrington resume . " of you they always speak most tenderly . her ladyship have tell I everything . " 
 " what everything ? " cry will , aghast . 
 " as much as woman ever do tell , cousin . she own that she think you have be a little epris with she . what woman can help like a man who have admire she ? " 
 " why , she hat you , and say you be wild about she , Mr. Warrington ! " say Mr. Esmond . 
 " Spretae injuria formae , cousin ! " 
 " for I what be for I ? " ask the other . 
 " I never do care for she , and hence , perhaps , she do not love I . do not   you remember that case of the wife of the Captain of the Guard ? " 
 " which guard ? " ask Will . 
 " my Lord Potiphar , " say Mr. Warrington . 
 " Lord who ? my Lord Falmouth be Captain of the Yeomen of the Guard , and my Lord Berkeley of the Pensioners . my Lord Hobart have them before . suppose you have not   be long enough in England to know who be who , cousin ! " remark Mr. William . 
 but Mr. Warrington explain that he be speak of a Captain of the Guard of the King of Egypt , whose wife have persecute one Joseph for not return her affection for he . on which will say that , as for Egypt , he believe it be a confounded long way off ; and that if Lord What - d'ye - call 's wife tell lie about he , it be like her sex , who he suppose be the same everywhere . 
 now the truth be , that when he pay his marriage - visit to Castlewood , Mr. Warrington have hear from the little Countess her version of the story of difference between Will Esmond and herself . and this tale differ , in some respect , though he be far from say it be more authentic than the ingenuous narrative of Mr. Will . the lady be grieve to think how she have be deceive in her brother - in - law . she fear that his life about the court and town have injure those high principle which all the Esmonds be know to be bear with ; that Mr. Will 's word be not altogether to be trust ; that a loose life and pecuniary difficulty have make he mercenary , blunt his honour , perhaps even impair the high chivalrous courage " which we Esmonds , cousin , " the little lady say , toss her head , " which we esmond must always possess leastway , you and I , and my lord , and my cousin Harry have it , I know ! " say the Countess . " oh , cousin George ! and must I confess that I be lead to doubt of your , without which a man of ancient and noble family like ours be not   worthy to be call a man ! I shall try , George , as a christian lady , and the head of one of the first family in this kingdom and the whole world , to forgive my brother William for have speak ill of a member of our family , though a young branch and by the female side , and make I for a moment doubt of you . he do so . perhaps he tell I ever so many bad thing you have say of I . " 
 " I , my dear lady ! " cry Mr. Warrington . 
 " which he say you say of I , cousin , and I hope you do not , and heartily pray you do not ; and I can afford to despise them . and he pay I his court , that be a fact ; and so have other , and that I be use to ; and he might have prosper well than he do perhaps   for I do not know my dear lord , nor come to vally his great and eminent quality , as I do out of the fulness of this grateful heart now ! , but , oh ! I find William be deficient in courage , and no man as want that can ever have the esteem of Lydia , Countess of Castlewood , no more he can ! he say ' twa you that want for spirit , cousin , and anger I by tell I that you be always abuse of I . but I forgive you , George , that I do ! and when I tell you that it be he be afraid the mean skunk ! and actually send for they constable to prevent the match between you and he , you will not   wonder I would not   vally a feller like that no , not that much ! " and her ladyship snap her little finger . " I say , noblesse oblige , and a man of our family who have not   get courage , I do not   care not this pinch of snuff for he there , now , I do not ! look at our ancestor , George , round these wall ! have not   the esmond always fight for their country and king ? be there one of we that , when the moment arrive , be not   ready to show that he be an Esmond and a nobleman ? if my eld son be to show the white feather , ' my Lord Esmond ! ' I would say to he   for that be the second title in our family , ' I disown your lordship ! ' " and so say , the intrepid little woman look round at her ancestor , whose effigy , depict by Lely and Kneller , figure round the wall of her drawing - room at Castlewood . 
 over that apartment , and the whole house , domain , and village , the new Countess speedily begin to rule with an unlimited sway . it be surprising how quickly she learn the way of command ; and , if she do not adopt those method of precedence usual in England among great lady , invent regulation for herself , and promulgate they , and make other submit . have be breed a Dissenter , and not be over - familiar with the Established Church service , Mr. Warrington remark that she make a blunder or two during the office   not know , for example , when she be to turn her face towards the east , a custom not adopt , I believe , in other reform church besides the English ; but between Warrington 's first bridal visit to Castlewood and his second , my lady have get to be quite perfect in that part of her duty , and sail into chapel on her cousin 's arm , her two footman bear her ladyship 's great prayer - book behind she , as demurely as that delightful old devotee with her lackey , in Mr. Hogarth 's famous picture of " Morning , " and as if my Lady Lydia have be accustom to have a chaplain all her life . she seem to patronise not only the new chaplain , but the service and the church itself , as if she have never in her own country hear a Ranter in a barn . she make the old established family in the country grave baronet and their wife worthy squire of twenty descent , who ride over to Castlewood to pay the bride and bridegroom honour know their distance , as the phrase be , and give she the pas . she get an old heraldry book ; and a surprising old maiden lady from Winton , learn in politeness and genealogy , from whom she learn the court etiquette   as the old Winton lady have know it in Queen Anne 's time ; and ere long she jabber gule and sable , bend and saltire , not with correctness always , but with a wonderful volubility and perseverance . she make little progress to the neighbouring town in her gilt coach - and - six , or to the village in her chair , and assert a quasi - regal right of homage from her tenant and other clodpole . she lecture the parson on his divinity ; the bailiff on his farming ; instruct the astonished housekeeper how to preserve and pickle ; would have teach the great London footman to jump behind the carriage , only it be too high for her little ladyship to mount ; give the village gossip instruction how to nurse and take care of their child long before she have one herself ; and as for physic , Madam Esmond in Virginia be not more resolute about her pill and draught than Miss Lydia , the earl 's new bride . do you remember the story of the Fisherman and the Genie , in the Arabian Nights ? so one wonder with regard to this lady , how such a prodigious genius could have be cork down into such a little bottle as her body . when Mr. Warrington return to London after his first nuptial visit , she bring he a little present for her young friend in Dean Street , as she call they   theo be old , and Hetty scarce young than herself , and send a trinket to one and a book to the other G. Warrington always vow that Theo 's present be a doll , while Hetty 's share be a nursery - book with word of one syllable . as for Mr. Will , her young brother - in - law , she treat he with a maternal gravity and tenderness , and be in the habit of speak of and to he with a protect air , which be infinitely divert to Warrington , although Will be usual curse and blasphemy be sorely increase by her behaviour . 
 as for old age , my Lady Lydia have little respect for that accident in the life of some gentleman and gentlewoman ; and , once the settlement be make in her behalf , treat the ancient Van den Bosch and his large periwig with no more ceremony than Dinah her black attendant , whose great ear she would pinch , and whose woolly pate she would pull without scruple , upon offence give so at least Dinah tell Gumbo , who tell his master . all the household tremble before my lady the Countess : the housekeeper , of whom even my lord and the dowager have be in awe ; the pamper London footman , who use to quarrel if they be disturb at their card , and grumble as they swill the endless beer , now step nimbly about their business when they hear her ladyship 's call ; even old Lockwood , who have be gate - porter for half a century or more , try to rally his poor old wandering wit when she come into his lodge to open his window , inspect his wood - closet , and turn his old dog out of door . Lockwood bar his old bald head before his new mistress , turn an appealing look towards his niece , and vaguely tremble before her little ladyship 's authority . Gumbo , dress his master for dinner , talk about Elisha   of whom he have hear the chaplain read in the morning , " and his bald head and de boy who call um name , and de bar eat em up , and serve um right , " say Gumbo . but as for my lady , when discourse with her cousin about the old porter , " Pooh , pooh ! stupid old man ! " say she ; " past his work , he and his dirty old dog ! they be as old and ugly as those old fish in the pond ! "   here she point to two old monster of carp that have be in a pond in Castlewood garden for century , accord to tradition , and have their back all cover with a hideous grey mould .   " Lockwood must pack off ; the workhouse be the place for he ; and I shall have a smart , good - look , tall fellow in the lodge that will do credit to our livery . " 
 " he be my grandfather 's man , and serve he in the war of Queen Anne , " interpose Mr. Warrington . on which my lady cry , petulantly , " o Lord ! Queen Anne 's dead , I suppose , and we be not   a - go into mourning for she . " 
 this matter of Lockwood be discuss at the family dinner , when her ladyship announce her intention of get rid of the old man . 
 " I be tell , " demurely remark Mr. Van den Bosch , " that , by the law , poor servant and poor folk of all kind be admirably provide in their old age here in England . I be sure I wish we have such an asylum for our folk at home , and that we be ease of the expense of keep our old hand . " 
 " if a man can not   work he ought to go ! " cry her ladyship . 
 " yes , indeed , and that be a fact ! " say grandpapa . 
 " what ! an old servant ? " ask my lord . 
 " Mr. Van den Bosch possibly be independent of servant when he be young , " remark Mr. Warrington . 
 " grease my own boot , open my own shutter , sand and water my own " 
 " Sugar , sir ? " say my lord . 
 " no ; floor , son - in - law ! " say the old man , with a laugh ; " though there be such trick , in grocery store , save your ladyship 's presence . " 
 " La , pa ! what should I know about store and grocery ? " cry her ladyship . 
 " he ! remember steal the sugar , and what come on it , my dear ladyship ? " say grandpapa . 
 " at any rate , a handsome , well - grow man in our livery will look well than that shrivel old porter creature ! " cry my lady . 
 " no livery be so become as old age , madam , and no lace as handsome as silver hair , " say Mr. Warrington . " what will the county say if you banish old Lockwood ? " 
 " oh ! if you plead for he , sir , I suppose he must stay . had not   I well order a couch for he out of my drawing - room , and send he some of the good wine from the cellar ? " 
 " indeed your ladyship could not   do well , " Mr. Warrington remark , very gravely . 
 and my lord say , yawn , " Cousin George be perfectly right , my dear . to turn away such an old servant as Lockwood would have an ill look . " 
 " you see those mouldy old carp be , after all , a curiosity , and attract visitor , " continue Mr. Warrington , gravely . " your ladyship must allow this old wretch to remain . it will not   be for long . and you may then engage the tall porter . it be very hard on we , Mr. Van den Bosch , that we be oblige to keep our old negro when they be past work . I shall sell that rascal Gumbo in eight or ten year . " 
 " do not   tink you will , master ! " say Gumbo , grin . 
 " hold your tongue , sir ! he do not   know english way , you see , and perhaps think an old servant have a claim on his master 's kindness , " say Mr. Warrington . 
 the next day , to Warrington 's surprise , my lady absolutely do send a basket of good wine to Lockwood , and a cushion for his armchair . 
 " I think of what you say , yesterday , at night when I go to bed ; and guess you know the world well than I do , cousin ; and that it be good to keep the old man , as you say . " 
 and so this affair of the porter 's lodge end , Mr. Warrington wonder within himself at this strange little character out of the West , with her naivete and simplicity , and a heartlessness would have do credit to the most battered old dowager who ever turn trump in St. James 's . 
 " you tell I to respect old people . why ? I do not   see nothing to respect in the old people , I know , " she say to Warrington . " they be not   so funny , and I be sure they be not   so handsome . look at grandfather ; look at Aunt Bernstein . they say she be a beauty once ! that picture paint from she ! I do not   believe it , nohow . no one shall tell I that I shall ever be as bad as that ! when they come to that , people ought not   to live . no , that they ought not . " 
 now , at Christmas , Aunt Bernstein come to pay her nephew and niece a visit , in company with Mr. Warrington . they travel at their leisure in the Baroness 's own landau ; the old lady be in particular good health and spirit , the weather delightfully fresh and not too cold ; and , as they approach her paternal home , Aunt Beatrice tell her companion a hundred story regard it and old day . though often lethargic , and not seldom , it must be confess , out of temper , the old lady would light up at time , when her conversation become wonderfully lively , her wit and malice be brilliant , and her memory supply she with a hundred anecdote of a bygone age and society . sure , ' tis hard with respect to Beauty , that its possessor should not have even a life - enjoyment of it , but be compel to resign it after , at the most , some forty year ' lease . as the old woman prattle of her former lover and admirer   her auditor have much more information regard her past career than her ladyship know of , I would look in her face , and , out of the ruin , try to build up in my fancy a notion of her beauty in its prime . what a homily I read there ! how the court be grow with grass , the tower break , the door ajar , the fine gilt saloon tarnish , and the tapestry cobwebbe and tear ! yonder dilapidated palace be all alive once with splendour and music , and those dim window be dazzle and blaze with light ! what ball and feast be once here , what splendour and laughter ! I could see lover in waiting , crowd in admiration , rival furious . I could imagine twilight assignation , and detect intrigue , though the curtain be close and draw . I be often minded to say to the old woman as she talk , " Madam , I know the story be not as you tell it , but so and so "   I have read at home the history of her life , as my dear old grandfather have write it : and my fancy wander about in she , amused and solitary , as I have walk about our father 's house at Castlewood , meditate on departed glory , and imagine ancient time . 
 when Aunt Bernstein come to Castlewood , her relative there , more , I think , on account of her own force of character , imperiousness , and sarcastic wit , than from their desire to possess her money , be accustom to pay she a great deal of respect and deference , which she accept as her due . she expect the same treatment from the new Countess , whom she be prepared to greet with special good - humour . the match have be of her making . " as you , you silly creature , would not have the heiress , " she say , " I be determined she should not go out of the family , " and she laughingly tell of many little scheme for bring the marriage about . she have give the girl a coronet and her nephew a hundred thousand pound . of course she should be welcome to both of they . she be delighted with the little Countess 's courage and spirit in rout the Dowager and Lady Fanny . almost always pleased with pretty people on her first introduction to they , Madame Bernstein raffle of her niece Lydia 's bright eye and lovely little figure . the marriage be altogether desirable . the old man be an obstacle , to be sure , and his talk and appearance somewhat too homely . but he will be get rid of . he be old and in delicate health . " he will want to go to America , or perhaps far , " say the Baroness , with a shrug . " as for the child , she have great fire and liveliness , and a Cherokee manner which be not without its charm , " say the pleased old Baroness . " your brother have it so have you , Master George ! nous la formeron , cette petite . Eugene want character and vigour , but he be a finished gentleman , and between we we shall make the little savage perfectly presentable . " in this way we discourse on the second afternoon as we journey towards Castlewood . we lie at the King 's arm at Bagshot the first night , where the Baroness be always receive with profound respect , and thence drive post to Hexton , where she have write to have my lord 's horse in wait for she ; but these be not forthcoming at the inn , and after a couple of hour we be oblige to proceed with our Bagshot horse to Castlewood . 
 during this last stage of the journey , I be bind to say the old aunt 's testy humour return , and she scarce speak a single word for three hour . as for her companion ; be prodigiously in love at the time , no doubt he do not press his aunt for conversation , but think unceasingly about his Dulcinea , until the coach actually reach Castlewood Common , and roll over the bridge before the house . 
 the housekeeper be ready to conduct her ladyship to her apartment . my lord and lady be both absent . she do not know what have keep they , the housekeeper say , head the way . 
 " not that door , my lady ! " cry the woman , as Madame de Bernstein put her hand upon the door of the room which she have always occupy . " that be her ladyship 's room now . this way , " and our aunt follow , by no mean in increase good - humour . I do not envy her maid when their mistress be displeased . but she have clear her brow before she join the family , and appear in the drawing - room before supper - time with a countenance of tolerable serenity . 
 " how d'ye do , aunt ? " be the Countess 's salutation . " I declare now , I be take a nap when your ladyship arrive ! hope you find your room fix to your liking ! " 
 have address three brief sentence to the astonished old lady , the Countess now turn to her other guest , and direct her conversation to they . Mr. Warrington be not a little divert by her behaviour , and by the appearance of surprise and wrath which begin to gather over Madame Bernstein 's face . " La petite , " whom the Baroness propose to " form , " be rather a rebellious subject , apparently , and propose to take a form of her own . look once or twice rather anxiously towards his wife , my lord try to atone for her pertness towards his aunt by profuse civility on his own part ; indeed , when he so wish , no man could be more courteous or pleasing . he find a score of agreeable thing to say to Madame Bernstein . he warmly congratulate Mr. Warrington on the glorious news which have come from America , and on his brother 's safety . he drink a toast at supper to Captain Warrington . " our family be distinguish itself , cousin , " he say ; and add , look with fond significance towards his Countess , " I hope the happy day be in store for we all . " 
 " yes , George ! " say the little lady . " you will write and tell Harry that we be all very much pleased with he . this action at Quebec be a most glorious action ; and now we have turn the french king out of the country , should not   be at all surprised if we set up for ourselves in America . " 
 " my love , you be talk treason ! " cry Lord Castlewood . 
 " I be talk reason , anyhow , my lord . I have   no notion of folk be keep down , and treat as child for ever ! " 
 George ! Harry ! I protest I be almost as much astonish as amuse . " when my brother hear that your ladyship be satisfied with his conduct , his happiness will be complete , " I say gravely . 
 next day , when talk beside her sofa , where she choose to lie in state , the little Countess no long call her cousin " George , " but " Mr. George , " as before ; on which Mr. George laughingly say she have change her language since the previous day . 
 " guess I do it to tease old Madam Buzwig , " say her ladyship . " she want to treat I as a child , and do the grandmother over I . I do not   want no grandmother , I do not . I be the head of this house , and I intend to let she know it . and I have   bring she all the way from London in order to tell it she , too ! La ! how she do look when I call you George ! I might have call you George only you have see that little Theo first , and like she good , I suppose . " 
 " yes , I suppose I like she good , " say Mr. George . 
 " well , I like you because you tell the truth . because you be the only one of them in London who do not   seem to care for my money , though I be downright mad and angry with you once , and with myself too , and with that little sweetheart of your , who be not   to be compare to I , I know she be not . " 
 " do not   let we make the comparison , then ! " I say , laugh . 
 " I suppose people must lie on their bed as they make them , " say she , with a little sigh . " dare say Miss Theo be very good , and you will marry she and go to Virginia , and be as dull as we be here . we be talk of Miss Lambert , my lord , and I be wish my cousin joy . how be old Goody to - day ? what a supper she do eat last night , and drink ! drink like a dragoon ! no wonder she have get a headache , and keep her room . guess it take she ever so long to dress herself . " 
 " you , too , may be feeble when you be old , and require rest and wine to warm you ! " say Mr. Warrington . 
 " Hope I shall not   be like she when I be old , anyhow ! " say the lady . " can not   see why I be to respect an old woman , because she hobble on a stick , and have shaky hand , and false tooth ! " and the little heathen sink back on her couch , and show twenty - four pearl of her own . 
 " Law ! " she add , after gaze at both her hearer through the curl lash of her brilliant dark eye . " how frighten you both look ! my lord have already give I ever so many sermon about old Goody . you be both afraid of she : and I be not , that be all . do not   look so scared at one another ! I be not   a - go to bite her head off . we shall have a battle , and I intend to win . how do I serve the Dowager , if you please , and my Lady Fanny , with their high and mighty air , when they try to put down the Countess of Castlewood in her own house , and laugh at the poor american girl ? we have a fight , and which get the good of it , pray ? I and Goody will have another , and when it be over , you will see that we shall both be perfect friend ! " 
 when at this point of our conversation the door open , and Madame Beatrix , elaborately dress accord to she will not , actually make her appearance , I , for my part , be not ashamed to own that I feel as great a panic as ever coward experienced . my lord , with his profound bow and blandest courtesy , greet his aunt and lead she to the fire , by which my lady   who be already hop for an heir to Castlewood   lie recline on her sofa . she do not attempt to rise , but smile a greeting to her venerable guest . and then , after a brief talk , in which she show a perfect self - possession , while the two gentleman blunder and hesitate with the most dastardly tremor , my lord say : 
 " if we be to look for those pheasant , cousin , we have well go now . " 
 " and I and aunt will have a cosy afternoon . and you will tell I about Castlewood in the old time , will not   you , Baroness ? " say the new mistress of the mansion . 
 o les lache que les homme ! I be so frightened , that I scarce see anything , but vaguely feel that Lady Castlewood 's dark eye be follow I . my lord grip my arm in the corridor , we quicken our pace till our retreat become a disgraceful run . we do not breathe freely till we be in the open air in the courtyard , where the keeper and the dog be wait . 
 and what happen ? I protest , child , I do not   know . but this be certain : if your mother have be a woman of the least spirit , or have know how to scold for five minute during as many consecutive day of her early married life , there would have be no more humble , henpecke wretch in Christendom than your father . when Parson Blake come to dinner , do not   you see how at a glance from his little wife he put his glass down and say , " no , thank you , Mr. Gumbo , " when old Gum bring he wine ? Blake wear a red coat before he take to black , and walk up Breeds Hill with a thousand bullet whistle round his ear , before ever he see our Bunker Hill in Suffolk . and the fire - eater of the 43rd now dare not face a glass of old port wine ! ' tis his wife have subdue his courage . the woman can master we , and do they know their own strength , be invincible . 
 well , then , what happen I know not on that disgraceful day of panic when your father flee the field , nor dare to see the heroine engage ; but when we return from our shooting , the battle be over . America have revolt , and conquer the mother country . 




 CHAPTER LXXIV . news from Canada 

 our Castlewood relative keep we with they till the commencement of the new year , and after a fortnight 's absence   which seem like an age to the absurd and infatuated young man   he return to the side of his charmer . Madame de Bernstein be not sorry to leave the home of her father . she begin to talk more freely as we get away from the place . what pass during that interview in which the battle - royal between she and her niece occur , she never reveal . but the old lady talk no more of form cette petite , and , indeed , when she allude to she , speak in a nervous , laugh way , but without any hostility towards the young Countess . her nephew eugene , she say , be doom to be henpecke for the rest of his day that she see clearly . a little order bring into the house would do it all the good possible . the little old vulgar american gentleman seem to be a shrewd person , and would act advantageously as a steward . the Countess 's mother be a convict , she have hear , send out from England , where no doubt she have beat hemp in most of the gaol ; but this news need not be carry to the town - crier ; and , after all , in respect to certain kind of people , what matter what their birth be ? the young woman would be honest for her own sake now : be shrewd enough , and would learn English presently ; and the name to which she have a right be great enough to get she into any society . a grocer , a smuggler , a slave - dealer , what matter Mr. Van den Bosch 's pursuit or previous profession ? the Countess of Castlewood could afford to be anybody 's daughter , and as soon as my nephew produce she , say the old lady , it be our duty to stand by she . 
 the tie of relationship bind Madame de Bernstein strongly to her nephew , Mr. Warrington hop that she would be dispose to be equally affectionate to her niece ; and speak of his visit to Mr. Hagan and his wife , for whom he entreat her aunt 's favour . but the old lady be obdurate regard Lady Maria ; beg that her name might never be mention , and immediately go on for two hour talk about no one else . she relate a series of anecdote regard her niece , which , as this book lie open virginibus puerisque , to all the young people of the family , I shall not choose to record . but this I will say of the kind creature , that if she sin , she be not the only sinner of the family , and if she repent , that other will do well to follow her example . Hagan , ' tis know , after he leave the stage , lead an exemplary life , and be remarkable for elegance and eloquence in the pulpit . his lady adopt extreme view , but be greatly respect in the sect which she join ; and when I see she last , talk to I of possess a peculiar spiritual illumination , which I strongly suspect at the time to be occasion by the too free use of liquor : but I remember when she and her husband be good to I and mine , at a period when sympathy be needful , and many a Pharisee turn away . 
 I have tell how easy it be to rise and fall in my fickle aunt 's favour , and how each of us brother , by turn , be embrace and neglect . my turn of glory have be after the success of my play . I be introduce to the town - wit ; hold my place in their company tolerably well ; be pronounce to be pretty well breed by the macaroni and people of fashion , and might have run a career amongst they have my purse be long enough ; have I choose to follow that life ; have I not love at that time a pair of kind eye well than the bright orb of the Gunnings or Chudleighs , or all the paint beauty of the Ranelagh ring . because I be fond of your mother , will it be believe , child , that my taste be say to be low , and deplore by my genteel family ? so it be , and I know that my godly Lady Warrington and my worldly Madame Bernstein both lay their elderly head together and lament my way of life . " why , with his name , he might marry anybody , " say meek Religion , who have ever one eye on Heaven and one on the main chance . " I meddle with no man 's affair , and admire genius , " say uncle , " but it be a pity you consort with those poet and author , and that sort of people , and that , when you might have have a lovely creature , with a hundred thousand pound , you let she slip and make up to a country girl without a penny - piece . " 
 " but if I have promise she , uncle ? " say I. 
 " promise , promise ! these thing be matter of arrangement and prudence , and demand a careful look - out . when you first commit yourself with little Miss Lambert , you have not see the lovely american lady whom your mother wish you to marry , as a good mother naturally would . and your duty to your mother , nephew , your duty to the Fifth Commandment , would have warrant your breaking with Miss L. , and fulfil your excellent mother 's intention regard Miss what be the Countess 's dutch name ? never mind . a name be nothing ; but a plumb , Master George , be something to look at ! why , I have my dear little Miley at a dancing - school with Miss Barwell , Nabob Barwell 's daughter , and I do not   disguise my wish that the child may contract an attachment which may endure through their life ! I tell the Nabob so . we go from the House of Commons one dancing - day and see they . ' twa beautiful to see the young thing walk a minuet together ! it bring tear into my eye , for I have a feeling heart , George , and I love my boy ! " 
 " but if I prefer Miss Lambert , uncle , with twopence to her fortune , to the Countess , with her hundred thousand pound ? " 
 " why then , sir , you have a singular taste , that be all , " say the old gentleman , turn on his heel and leave I . and I could perfectly understand his vexation at my not be able to see the world as he view it . 
 nor do my Aunt Bernstein much like the engagement which I have make , or the family with which I pass so much of my time . their simple way weary , and perhaps annoy , the old woman of the world , and she no more relish their company than a certain person   who be not so black as he be paint   like holy water . the old lady chafe at my for ever dangle at my sweetheart 's lap . have rise mightily in her favour , I begin to fall again : and once more Harry be the favourite , and his brother , Heaven know , not jealous . 
 he be now our family hero . he write we brief letter from the seat of war where he be engage ; Madame Bernstein care little at first about the letter or the writer , for they be simple , and the fact he narrate not over interesting . we have early learn in London the news of the action on the glorious first of August at Minden , where Wolfe 's old regiment be one of the british six which help to achieve the victory on that famous day . at the same hour , the young General lie in his bed , in sight of Quebec , stricken down by fever , and perhaps rage and disappointment at the check which his troop have just receive . 
 arrive in the St. Lawrence in June , the fleet which bring Wolfe and his army have land they on the last day of the month on the Island of Orleans , opposite which rise the great cliff of Quebec . after the great action in which his General fall , the dear brother who accompany the chief , write home to I one of his simple letter , describe his modest share in that glorious day , but add nothing to the many description already write of the action of the 13th of September , save only I remember he write , from the testimony of a brother aide - de - camp who be by his side , that the General never speak at all after receive his death - wound , so that the phrase which have be put into the mouth of the die hero may be consider as no more authentic than an oration of Livy or Thucydides . 
 from his position on the island , which lie in the great channel of the river to the north of the town , the General be ever hungrily on the look - out for a chance to meet and attack his enemy . above the city and below it he land , now here and now there ; he be bent upon attack wherever he see an opening . ' twas surely a prodigious fault on the part of the Marquis of Montcalm , to accept a battle from Wolfe on equal term , for the British General have no artillery , and when we have make our famous scalade of the height , and be on the Plains of Abraham , we be a little nearer the city , certainly , but as far off as ever from be within it . 
 the game that be play between the brave chief of those two gallant little army , and which last from July until Mr. Wolfe win the crown hazard in September , must have be as interesting a match as ever eager player engage in . on the very first night after the landing   as my brother have narrate it   the sport begin . at midnight the French send a flame squadron of fireship down upon the british ship which be discharge their store at Orleans . our seaman think it be good sport to tow the fireship clear of the fleet , and grind they on the shore , where they burn out . 
 as soon as the french commander hear that our ship have enter the river , he march to Beauport in advance of the city and there take up a strong position . when our store and hospital be establish , our General cross over from his island to the left shore , and draw near to his enemy . he have the ship in the river behind he , but the whole country in face of he be in arm . the Indians in the forest seize our advanced party as they strove to clear it , and murder they with horrible torture . the French be as savage as their indian friend . the Montmorenci River rush between Wolfe and the enemy . he could neither attack these nor the city behind they . 
 bent on see whether there be no other point at which his foe might be assailable , the General pass round the town of Quebec and skirt the left shore beyond . everywhere it be guard , as well as in his immediate front , and have run the gauntlet of the battery up and down the river , he return to his post at Montmorenci . on the right of the french position , across the Montmorenci River , which be fordable at low tide , be a redoubt of the enemy . he would have that . perhaps , to defend it the french chief would be force out from his line , and a battle be bring on . Wolfe determine to play these odd . he would fetch over the body of his army from the Island of Orleans , and attack from the St. Lawrence . he would time his attack , so that , at shallow water , his lieutenant , Murray and Townsend , might cross the Montmorenci , and , at the last day of July , he play this desperate game . 
 he first , and General Monckton , his second in command   set out from Point Levi , which he occupy , cross over the St. Lawrence from their respective station , be receive with a storm of shot and artillery as they row to the shore . no soon be the troop land than they rush at the french redoubt without order , be shoot down before it in great number , and be oblige to fall back . at the preconcerted signal the troop on the other side of the Montmorenci avance across the river in perfect order . the enemy even evacuate the redoubt and fall back to their line ; but from these the assailant be receive with so severe a fire that an impression on they be hopeless , and the General have to retreat . 
 the battle of Montmorenci   which my brother Harry and I have fight again many a time over our wine   form the dismal burthen of the first despatch from Mr. Wolfe which reach England and plunge we all in gloom . what more might one expect of a commander so rash ? what disaster might one not foretell ? be ever scheme so wild as to bring three great body of man , across broad river , in the face of murderous battery , merely on the chance of induce an enemy , strongly entrench and guard , to leave his position and come out and engage we ? ' twas the talk of the town . no wonder grave people shake their head , and prophesy fresh disaster . the General , who take to his bed after this failure , shudder with fever , be to live barely six week long , and die immortal ! how be it , and by what , and whom , that Greatness be achieve ? be Merit be Madness the patron ? be it Frolic or Fortune ? be it fate that award success and defeat ? be it the just Cause that ever win ? how do the french gain Canada from the savage , and we from the French , and after which of the conquest be the right time to sing Te deum ? we be always for implicate Heaven in our quarrel , and cause the god to intervene whatever the nodus may be . do Broughton , after pummel and beat Slack , lift up a black eye to Jove and thank he for the victory ? and if ten thousand boxer be to be so heard , why not one ? and if Broughton be to be grateful , what be Slack to be ? 
 " by the list of disabled officer   many of whom be of rank   you may perceive , sir , that the army be much weaken . by the nature of this river the most formidable part of the armament be deprive of the power of acting , yet we have almost the whole force of Canada to oppose . in this situation there be such a choice of difficulty , that I own myself at a loss how to determine . the affair of Great Britain , I know , require the most vigorous measure ; but then the courage of a handful of brave man should be exert only where there be some hope of a favourable event . the admiral and I have examine the town with a view to a general assault : and he would readily join in this or any other measure for the public service ; but I can not propose to he an undertaking of so dangerous a nature , and promise so little success .... I find myself so ill , and be still so weak , that I beg the general officer to consult together for the public utility . they be of opinion that they should try by convey up a corps of 4000 or 5000 man   which be nearly the whole strength of the army , after the point of Levi and Orleans be put in a proper state of defence   to draw the enemy from their present position , and bring they to an action . I have acquiesce in their proposal , and we be prepare to put it into execution . " 
 so write the General   of whose noble letter it be clear our dear scribe be not the author or secretary   from his headquarters at Montmorenci Falls on 2nd day of September ; and on the 14th of October following , the Rodney cutter arrive with the sad news in England . the attack have fail , the chief be sick , the army dwindle , the menace city so strong that assault be almost impossible ; " the only chance be to fight the Marquis of Montcalm upon term of less disadvantage than attack his entrenchment , and , if possible , to draw he from his present position . " would the french chief , whose great military genius be know in Europe , fall into such a snare ? no wonder there be pale look in the City at the news , and doubt and gloom wheresoever it be know . 
 three day after this first melancholy intelligence , come the famous letter announce that wonderful consummation of fortune with which Mr. Wolfe 's wonderful career end . if no man be to be style happy till his death , what shall we say of this one ? his end be so glorious , that I protest not even his mother nor his mistress ought to have deplore it , or at any rate have wish he alive again . I know it be a hero we speak of ; and yet I vow I scarce know whether in the last act of his life I admire the result of genius , invention , and daring , or the boldness of a gambler win surprising odd . suppose his ascent discover a half - hour soon , and his people , as they would have be assuredly , beat back ? suppose the Marquis of Montcalm not to quit his entrenched line to accept that strange challenge ? suppose these point and none of they depend upon Mr. Wolfe at all and what become of the glory of the young hero , of the great minister who discover he , of the intoxicated nation which rise up frantic with self - gratulation at the victory ? I say , what fate be it that shape our end , or those of nation ? in the many hazardous game which my Lord Chatham play , he win this prodigious one . and as the greedy british hand seize the Canadas , it let fall the United States out of its grasp . 
 to be sure this wisdom d'apres coup be easy . we wonder at this man 's rashness now the deed be do , and marvel at the other 's fault . what general some of we be upon paper ! what repartee come to our mind when the talk be finish ! and , the game over , how well we see how it should have be play ! writing of an event at a distance of thirty year , ' tis not difficult now to criticise and find fault . but at the time when we first hear of Wolfe 's glorious deed upon the Plains of Abraham of that army marshal in darkness and carry silently up the midnight river of those rock scale by the intrepid leader and his troop of that miraculous security of the enemy , of his present acceptance of our challenge to battle , and of his defeat on the open plain by the sheer valour of his conqueror we be all intoxicate in England by the news . the whole nation rise up and feel itself the strong for Wolfe 's victory . not merely all man engage in the battle , but those at home who have condemn its rashness , feel themselves hero . our spirit rise as that of our enemy falter . friend embrace each other when they meet . coffee - house and public place be throng with people eager to talk the news . courtier rush to the King and the great Minister by whose wisdom the campaign have be decree . when he show himself , the people follow he with shout and blessing . People do not deplore the dead warrior , but admire his euthanasia . should James Wolfe 's friend weep and wear mourning , because a chariot have come from the sky to fetch he away ? let they watch with wonder , and see he depart , radiant ; rise above we superior . to have a friend who have be near or about he be to be distinguish . every soldier who fight with he be a hero . in our fond little circle I know ' twas a distinction to be Harry 's brother . we should not in the least wonder but that he , from his previous knowledge of the place , have find the way up the height which the british army take , and point it out to his general . his promotion would follow as a matter of course . why , even our Uncle Warrington write letter to bless Heaven and congratulate I and himself upon the share Harry have have in the glorious achievement . our Aunt Beatrix open her house and receive company upon the strength of the victory . I become a hero from my likeness to my brother . as for Parson Sampson , he preach such a sermon that his auditor   some of whom have be warn by his reverence of the come discourse   be with difficulty restrain from huzzae the orator , and be mob as they leave the chapel . " do not   talk to I , madam , about grief , " say General Lambert to his wife , who , dear soul , be for allow herself some small indulgence of her favourite sorrow on the day when Wolfe 's remain be gloriously bury at Greenwich . " if our boy could come by such death as James 's , you know you would not   prevent they from be shoot , but would scale the Abraham height to see the thing do ! wouldst thou mind die in the arm of victory , Charley ? " he ask of the little hero from the Chartreux . " that I would not , " say the little man ; " and the doctor give we a holiday , too . " 
 our Harry 's promotion be insure after his share in the famous battle , and our aunt announce her intention of purchase a company for he . 




 CHAPTER LXXV . the course of True Love 

 have your father , young folk , possess the commonest share of prudence , not only would this chapter of his history never have be write , but you yourselves would never have appear in the world to plague he in a hundred way to shout and laugh in the passage when he want to be quiet at his book ; to wake he when he be doze after dinner , as a healthy country gentleman should : to mislay his spectacle for he , and steal away his newspaper when he want to read it ; to ruin he with tailor ' bill , mantua - maker ' bill , tutor ' bill , as you all of you do : to break his rest of night when you have the impudence to fall ill , and when he would sleep undisturbed but that your silly mother will never be quiet for half an hour ; and when Joan can not   sleep , what use , pray , be there in Darby put on his nightcap ? every trifling ailment that any one of you have have , have scar she so that I protest I have never be tranquil ; and , be I not the most long - suffer creature in the world , would have like to be rid of the whole pack of you . and now , forsooth , that you have grow out of childhood , long petticoat , chicken - pox , small - pox , whooping - cough , scarlet fever , and the other delectable accident of puerile life , what must that unconscionable woman propose but to arrange the south room as a nursery for possible grandchild , and set up the Captain with a wife , and make he marry early because we do ! he be too fond , she say , of Brookes 's and Goosetree 's when he be in London . she have the perversity to hint that , though an entree to Carlton House may be very pleasant , ' tis very dangerous for a young gentleman : and she would have Miles live away from temptation , and sow his wild oat , and marry , as we do . Marry ! my dear creature , we have no business to marry at all ! by the law of common prudence and duty , I ought to have back out of my little engagement with Miss Theo   who would have marry somebody else , and take a rich wife . your Uncle John be a parson and could not   fight , poor Charley be a boy at school , and your grandfather be too old a man to call I to account with sword and pistol . I repeat there never be a more foolish match in the world than ours , and our relation be perfectly right in be angry with we . what be relation make for , indeed , but to be angry and find fault ? when Hester marrie , do you mind , Master George , to quarrel with she if she do not take a husband of your selecting . when George have get his living , after be senior wrangler and fellow of his college , Miss Hester , do you toss up your little nose at the young lady he shall fancy . as for you , my little Theo , I can not   part with your . you must not quit your old father ; for he like you to play Haydn to he , and peel his walnut after dinner . 
  on the blank leaf opposite this paragraph be write , in a large , girlish hand : 
 " I never intend to go . THEODOSIA . " 
 " nor i. HESTER . " 
 they both marry , as I see by the note in the family Bible Miss Theodosia Warrington to Joseph Clinton , son of the Rev. Joseph Blake , and himself subsequently Master of Rodwell Regis Grammar School ; and Miss Hester Mary , in 1804 , to Captain F. Handyman , R.N. ED . 
 whilst they have the blessing   forsooth !   of meeting , and billing and coo every day , the two young people , your parent , go on in a fool 's paradise , little heed the world round about they , and all its tattling and meddling . Rinaldo be as brave a warrior as ever slew Turk , but you know he love dangle in Armida 's garden . Pray , my Lady Armida , what do you mean by fling your spell over I in youth , so that not glory , not fashion , not gaming - table , not the society of man of wit in whose way I fall , could keep I long from your apron - string , or out of reach of your dear simple prattle ? pray , my dear , what use we to say to each other during those endless hour of meeting ? I never go to sleep after dinner then . which of we be so witty ? be it I or you ? and how come it our conversation be so delightful ? I remember that year I do not even care to go and see my Lord Ferrers try and hung , when all the world be run after his lordship . the King of Prussia 's capital be take ; have the Austrians and Russians be encamp round the Tower there could scarce have be more stir in London : yet Miss Theo and her young gentleman feel no inordinate emotion of pity or indignation . what to we be the fate of Leipzig or Berlin ? the truth be , that dear old house in Dean Street be an enchanted garden of delight . I have be as idle since , but never as happy . shall we order the postchaise , my dear , leave the child to keep house ; and drive up to London and see if the old lodging be still to be let ? and you shall sit at your old place in the window , and wave a little handkerchief as I walk up the street . say what we do be imprudent . would we not do it over again ? my good folk , if Venus have walk into the room and challenge the apple , I be so infatuated , I would have give it your mother . and have she have the choice , she would have prefer her humble servant in a threadbare coat to my Lord Clive with all his diamond .
