 once , to be sure , and for a brief time in that year , I have a notion of go on the highway in order to be catch and hang as my Lord Ferrers : or of join the King of Prussia , and request some of his Majesty 's enemy to knock my brain out ; or of enlist for the India service , and perform some desperate exploit which should end in my bodily destruction . ah I ! that be indeed a dreadful time ! your mother scarce dare speak of it now , save in a whisper of terror ; or think of it it be such cruel pain . she be unhappy year after on the anniversary of the day , until one of you be bear on it . suppose we have be part : what have come to we ? what have my lot be without she ? as I think of that possibility , the whole world be a blank . I do not say be we part now . it have please God to give we thirty year of union . we have reach the autumn season . our successor be appoint and ready ; and that one of we who be first call away , know the survivor will follow ere long . but we be actually part in our youth ; and I tremble to think what might have be , have not a dear friend bring we together . 
 unknown to myself , and very likely mean only my advantage , my relative in England have choose to write to Madam Esmond in Virginia , and represent what they be pleased to call the folly of the engagement I have contract . every one of they sing the same song : and I see the letter , and burn the whole cursed pack of they year afterwards when my mother show they to I at home in Virginia . Aunt Bernstein be forward with her advice . a young person , with no wonderful good look , of no family , with no money ; be ever such an imprudent connexion , and ought it not for dear George 's sake to be break off ? she have several eligible match in view for I . with my name and prospect , ' twas a shame I should throw myself away on this young lady ; her sister ought to interpose and so forth . 
 my Lady Warrington must write , too , and in her peculiar manner . her ladyship 's letter be garnish with scripture text . 
 she dress her worldliness out in phylactery . she point out how I be live in an unworthy society of player - folk , and the like people , who she could not say be absolutely without religion   Heaven forbid ! , but who be deplorably worldly . she would not say an artful woman have inveigle I for her daughter , have in vain try to captivate my young brother . she be far from say any harm of the young woman I have select ; but at least this be certain , Miss L. have no fortune or expectation , and her parent might naturally be anxious to compromise I . she have take counsel , etc . etc . she have seek for guidance where it be , etc . feel what her duty be , she have determine to speak . Sir Miles , a man of excellent judgment in the affair of this world   though he know and seek a well , fully agree with she in opinion , nay , desire she to write , and entreat her sister to interfere , that the ill - advise match should not take place . 
 and who besides must put a little finger into the pie but the new Countess of Castlewood ? she write a majestic letter to Madam Esmond , and state , that have be place by Providence at the head of the Esmond family , it be her duty to communicate with her kinswoman and warn she to break off this marriage . I believe the three woman lay their head together previously ; and , packet after packet , send off their warning to the Virginian lady . 
 one raw April morning , as Corydon go to pay his usual duty to Phillis , he find , not his charmer with her dear smile as usual ready to welcome he , but Mrs. Lambert , with very red eye , and the General as pale as death . " read this , George Warrington ! " say he , as his wife 's head drop between her hand ; and he put a letter before I , of which I recognise the handwriting . I can hear now the sob of the good Aunt Lambert , and to this day the noise of fire - iron stir a fire in a room overhead give I a tremor . I hear such a noise that day in the girl ' room where the sister be together . poor , gentle child ! Poor Theo ! 
 " what can I do after this , George , my poor boy ? " ask the General , pace the room with desperation in his face . 
 I do not quite read the whole of Madam Esmond 's letter , for a kind of sickness and faintness come over I ; but I fear I could say some of it now by heart . its style be good , and its actual word temperate enough , though they only imply that Mr. and Mrs. Lambert have inveigle I into the marriage ; that they know such an union be unworthy of I ; that   as Madam E. understand   they have desire a similar union for her young son , which project , not unluckily for he , perhaps , be give up when it be find that Mr. Henry Warrington be not the inheritor of the virginian property . if Mr. Lambert be a man of spirit and honour , as he be represent to be , Madam Esmond scarcely suppose that , after her representation , he would persist in desire this match . she would not lay command upon her son , whose temper she know ; but for the sake of Miss Lambert 's own reputation and comfort , she urge that the dissolution of the engagement should come from her family , and not from the just unwillingness of Rachel Esmond Warrington of Virginia . 
 " God help we , George ! " the General say , " and give we all strength to bear this grief , and these charge which it have please your mother to bring ! they be hard , but they do not   matter now . what be of most importance , be to spare as much sorrow as we can to my poor girl . I know you love she so well , that you will help I and her mother to make the blow as tolerable as we may to that poor gentle heart . since she be bear she have never give pain to a soul alive , and ' tis cruel that she should be make to suffer . " and as he speak he pass his hand across his dry eye . 
 " it be my fault , Martin ! it be my fault ! " weep the poor mother . 
 " your mother speak we fair , and give her promise , " say the father . 
 " and do you think I will withdraw mine ? " cry I ; and protest , with a thousand frantic vow , what they know full well , that I be bind to Theo before Heaven , and that nothing should part I from she . 
 " she herself will demand the parting . she be a good girl , God help I ! and a dutiful . she will not have her father and mother call schemer , and treat with scorn . your mother know not , very likely , what she be do , but ' tis do . you may see the child , and she will tell you as much . be Theo dress , molly ? I bring the letter home from my office last evening after you be go . the woman have have a bad night . she know at once by my face that there be bad news from America . she read the letter quite firmly . she say she would like to see you and say good - bye . of course , George , you will give I your word of honour not to try and see she afterwards . as soon as my business will let I we will get away from this , but mother and I think we be good all together . ' tis you , perhaps , have good go . but give I your word , at any rate , that you will not try and see she . we must spare her pain , sir ! we must spare her pain ! " and the good man sate down in such deep anguish himself that I , who be not yet under the full pressure of my own grief , actually feel his , and pity it . it could not be that the dear lip I have kiss yesterday be to speak to I only once more . we be all here together ; love each other , sit in the room where we meet every day ; my drawing on the table by her little workbox ; she be in the chamber upstairs ; she must come down presently . 
 who be this open the door ? I see her sweet face . it be like our little Mary 's when we think she would die of the fever . there be even a smile upon her lip . she come up and kiss I . " good - bye , dear George ! " she say . Great Heaven ! an old man sit in this room , with my wife 's workbox opposite , and she but five minute away , my eye grow so dim and full that I can not   see the book before I . I be three - and - twenty year old again . I go through every stage of that agony . I once have it sit in my own postchaise , with my wife actually by my side . who dare to sully her sweet love with suspicion ? who have a right to stab such a soft bosom ? do not   you see my lady get their knife ready , and the poor child bar it ? my wife come in . she have be serve out tea or tobacco to some of her pensioner . " what be it make you look so angry , papa ? " she say . " my love ! " I say , " it be the thirteenth of April . " a pang of pain shoot across her face , follow by a tender smile . she have undergo the martyrdom , and in the midst of the pang come a halo of forgiveness . I can not   forgive ; not until my day of dotage come , and I cease remember anything . " Hal will be home for Easter ; he will bring two or three of his friend with he from Cambridge , " she say . and straightway she fall to devise scheme for amuse the boy . when be she ever occupy , but with plan for make other happy ? 
 a gentleman sit in spectacle before an old ledger , and write down pitiful remembrance of his own condition , be a quaint and ridiculous object . my corn hurt I , I know , but I suspect my neighbour 's shoe pinch he too . I be not go to howl much over my own grief , or enlarge at any great length on this one . many another man , I dare say , have have the light of his day suddenly put out , the joy of his life extinguish , and have be leave to darkness and vague torture . I have a book I try to read at this time of grief Howel 's Letters and when I come to the part about Prince Charles in Spain , up start the whole tragedy alive again . I go to Brighthelmstone , and there , at the inn , have a room face the east , and see the sun get up ever so many morning , after blank night of wakefulness , and smoke my pipe of Virginia in his face . when I be in that place by chance , and see the sun rise now , I shake my fist at he , thinking , o orient Phoebus , what horrible grief and savage wrath have you not see I suffer ! though my wife be mine ever so long , I say I be angry just the same . who dare , I want to know , to make we suffer so ? I be forbid to see she . I keep my promise , and remain away from the house : that be , after that horrible meeting and parting . but at night I would go and look at her window , and watch the lamp burn there ; I would go to the Chartreux   where I know another boy , and call for her brother , and gorge he with cake and half - crown . I would meanly have her eld brother to dine , and almost kiss he when he go away . I use to breakfast at a coffee - house in Whitehall , in order to see Lambert go to his office ; and we would salute each other sadly , and pass on without speak . why do not the woman come out ? they never do . they be practise on she , and persuade she to try and forget I . oh , the weary , weary day ! oh , the madden time ! at last a doctor 's chariot use to draw up before the General 's house every day . be she ill ? I fear I be rather glad she be ill . my own suffering be so infernal , that I greedily want she to share my pain . and would she not ? what grief of mine have it not feel , that gentle and most compassionate of heart ? what pain would it not suffer to spare mine a pang ? 
 I seek that doctor out . I have an interview with he . I tell my story , and lay bare my heart to he , with an outburst of passionate sincerity , which win his sympathy . my confession enable he to understand his young patient 's malady ; for which his drug have no remedy or anodyne . I have promise not to see she , or to go to she : I have keep my promise . I have promise to leave London : I have go away . twice , thrice I go back and tell my suffering to he . he would take my fee now and again , and always receive I kindly , and let I speak . ah , how I cling to he ! I suspect he must have be unhappy once in his own life , he know so well and gently how to succour the miserable . 
 he do not tell I how dangerously , though he do not disguise from I how gravely and seriously , my dear girl have be ill . I tell he everything that I would marry she and brave every chance and danger ; that , without she , I be a man utterly wreck and ruin , and care not what become of I . my mother have once consent , and have now choose to withdraw her consent , when the tie between we have be , as I hold , draw so closely together , as to be paramount to all filial duty . 
 " I think , sir , if your mother hear you , and see Miss Lambert , she would relent , " say the doctor . who be my mother to hold I in bondage ; to claim a right of misery over I ; and to take this angel out of my arm ? 
 " he could not , " he say , " be a message - carrier between young lady who be pin and young lover on whom the sweetheart ' gate be shut : but so much he would venture to say , that he have see I , and be prescribe for I , too . " yes , he must have be unhappy once , himself . I see he , you may be sure , on the very day when he have keep his promise to I . he say she seem to be comfort by hear news of I . 
 " she bear her suffering with an angelical sweetness . I prescribe Jesuit 's bark , which she take ; but I be not sure the hearing of you have not do more good than the medicine . " the woman own afterwards that they have never tell the General of the doctor 's new patient . 
 I know not what wild expression of gratitude I pour out to the good doctor for the comfort he bring I . his treatment be cure two unhappy sick person . ' Twas but a drop of water , to be sure ; but then a drop of water to a man rag in torment . I love the ground he trod upon , bless the hand that take mine , and have feel her pulse . I have a ring with a pretty cameo head of a Hercules on it . ' twas too small for his finger , nor do the good old man wear such ornament . I make he hang it to his watch - chain , in hope that she might see it , and recognise that the token come from I . how I fasten upon Spencer at this time   my friend of the Temple who also have an unfortunate love - match , and walk with he from my apartment to the Temple , and he back with I to Bedford Gardens , and our talk be for ever about our woman ! I dare say I tell everybody my grief . my good landlady and Betty the housemaid pity I . my son Miles , who , for a wonder , have be read in my MS . , say , " by Jove , sir , I do not   know you and my mother be take in this kind of way . the year I join , I be hit very bad myself . an infernal little jilt that throw I over for Sir Craven Oaks of our regiment . I think I should have go crazy . " and he give a melancholy whistle , and walk away . 
 the General have to leave London presently on one of his military inspection , as the doctor casually tell I ; but , have give my word that I would not seek to present myself at his house , I keep it , avail myself , however , as you may be sure , of the good physician 's leave to visit he , and have news of his dear patient . his account of she be , far from encourage . " she do not rally , " he say . " we must get she back to Kent again , or to the sea . " I do not know then that the poor child have beg and pray so piteously not to be move , that her parent , divine , perhaps , the reason of her desire to linger in London , and feel that it might be dangerous not to humour she , have yield to her entreaty , and consent to remain in town . 
 at last one morning I come , pretty much as usual , and take my place in my doctor 's front parlour , whence his patient be call in their turn to his consulting - room . here I remain , look heedlessly over the book on the table and take no notice of any person in the room , which speedily empty itself of all , save I and one lady who sate with her veil down . I use to stay till the last , for Osborn , the doctor 's man , know my business , and that it be not my own illness I come for . 
 when the room be empty of all save I and the lady , she put out two little hand , cry in a voice which make I start " do not   you know I , George ? " and the next minute I have my arm round she , and kiss she as heartily as ever I kiss in my life , and give way to a passionate outgush of emotion the most refreshing , for my parched soul have be in rage and torture for six week past , and this be a glimpse of Heaven . 
 who be it , child ? you think it be your mother whom the doctor have bring to I ? no . it be Hetty . 




 CHAPTER LXXVI . inform we how Mr. Warrington jump into a Landau 

 the emotion at the first surprise and greet over , the little maiden begin at once . 
 " so you be come at last to ask after Theo , and you feel sorry that your neglect have make she so ill ? for six week she have be unwell , and you have never ask a word about she ! very kind of you , Mr. George , I be sure ! " 
 " Kind ! " gasp out Mr. Warrington . 
 " I suppose you call it kind to be with she every day and all day for a year , and then to leave she without a word ? " 
 " my dear , you know my promise to your father ? " I reply . 
 " promise ! " say Miss Hetty , shrug her shoulder . " a very fine promise , indeed , to make my darling ill , and then suddenly , one fine day , to say , ' good - bye , Theo , ' and walk away for ever . I suppose gentleman make these promise , because they wish to keep them . I would not   trifle with a poor child 's heart , and leave she afterwards , if I be a man . what have she ever do to you , but be a fool and too fond of you ? pray , sir , by what right do you take she away from all of we , and then desert she , because an old woman in America do not   approve of she ? she be happy with we before you come . she love her sister there never be such a sister until she see you . and now , because your mamma think her young gentleman might do well , you must leave her forsooth ! " 
 " great power , child ! " I cry , exasperate at this wrongheadedness . " be it I that draw back ? be it not I that be forbid your house ? and do not your father require , on my honour , that I should not see she ? " 
 " Honour ! and you be the man who pretend to be our superior ; and it be we who be to respect you and admire you ! I declare , George Warrington , you ought to go back to your schoolroom in Virginia again ; have your black nurse to tuck you up in bed , and ask leave from your mamma when you might walk out . oh , George ! I little think that my sister be give her heart away to a man who have not   the spirit to stand by she ; but , at the first difficulty , leave she ! when Doctor Heberden say he be attend you , I determine to come and see you , and you do look very ill , that I be glad to see ; and I suppose it be your mother you be frightened of . but I shall not   tell Theo that you be unwell . she have not   leave off care for you . she can not   walk out of a room , break her solemn engagement , and go into the world the next day as if nothing have happen ! that be leave for man , our superior in courage and wisdom ; and to desert an angel yes , an angel ten thousand time too good for you ; an angel who use to love I till she see you , and who be the blessing of life and of all of we be what you call honour ? do not   tell I , sir ! I despise you all ! you be our better , be you ? we be to worship and wait on you , I suppose ? I do not   care about your wit , and your tragedy , and your verse ; and I think they be often very stupid . I will not   set up of night copy your manuscript , nor watch hour after hour at a window waste my time and neglect everybody because I want to see your worship walk down the street with your hat cock ! if you be go away , and welcome , give I back my sister , I say ! give I back my darling of old day , who love every one of we , till she see you . and you leave she because your mamma think she can find somebody rich for you ! oh , you brave gentleman ! go and marry the person your mother choose , and let my dear die here desert ! " 
 " great heaven , Hetty ! " I cry , amazed at the logic of the little woman . " be it I who wish to leave your sister ? do I not offer to keep my promise , and be it not your father who refuse I , and make I promise never to try and see she again ? what have I but my word , and my honour ? " 
 " Honour , indeed ! you keep your word to he , and you break it to she ! pretty honour ! if I be a man , I would soon let you know what I think of your honour ! only I forget you be bind to keep the peace and must not ... oh , George , George ! do not   you see the grief I be in ? I be distract , and scarce know what I say . you must not leave my darling . they do not   know it at home . they do not   think so but I know she good of all , and she will die if you leave she . say you will not ! have pity upon I , Mr. Warrington , and give I my dearest back ! " thus the warm - hearted , distract creature run from anger to entreaty , from scorn to tear . be my little doctor right in thus speak of the case of her dear patient ? be there no other remedy than that which Hetty cry for ? have not other feel the same cruel pain of amputation , undergo the same exhaustion and fever afterwards , lain hopeless of anything save death , and yet recover after all , and limp through life subsequently ? why , but that love be selfish , and do not heed other people 's grief and passion , or that our be so intense and special that we deem no other lover could suffer like ourselves ; here in the passionate young pleader for her sister , we might have show an instance that a fond heart could be stricken with the love malady and silently suffer it , live under it , recover from it . what have happen in Hetty 's own case ? her sister and I , in our easy triumph and fond confidential prattle , have many a time talk over that matter , and , egotist as we be , perhaps draw a secret zest and security out of she less fortunate attachment . ' twa like sit by the fireside and hear the winter howl without ; ' twa like walk by the maxi magno , and see the ship toss at sea . we cling to each other only the more closely , and , wrap in our own happiness , view other ' misfortune with complacent pity . be the truth as it may . grant that we might have be sunder , and after a while survive the separation , so much my sceptical old age may be dispose to admit . yet , at that time , I be eager enough to share my ardent little Hetty 's terror and apprehension , and willingly choose to believe that the life dear to I in the world would be sacrifice if separate from mine . be I wrong ? I would not say as much now . I may doubt about myself   or not doubt , I know , but of she , never ; and Hetty find in her quite a willing sharer in her alarm and terror . I be for impart some of these to our doctor ; but the good gentleman shut my mouth . " hush , " say he , with a comical look of fright . " I must hear none of this . if two people who happen to know each other chance to meet and talk in my patient ' room , I can not help myself ; but as for match - making and love - making , I be your humble servant ! what will the General do when he come back to town ? he will have I behind Montagu House as sure as I be a live doctor , and alive I wish to remain , my good sir ! " and he skip into his carriage , and leave I there meditate . " and you and Miss Hetty must have no meeting here again , mind you that , " he have say previously . 
 oh no ! of course we would have none ! we be gentleman of honour , and so forth , and our word be our word . besides , to have see Hetty , be not that an inestimable boon , and would we not be for ever grateful ? I be so refresh with that drop of water I have have , that I think I can hold out for ever so long a time now . I walk away with Hetty to Soho , and never once think of arrange a new meeting with she . but the little emissary be more thoughtful , and she ask I whether I go to the Museum now to read ? and I say , " oh yes , sometimes , my dear ; but I be too wretched for read now ; I can not see what be on the paper . I do not care about my book . even Pocahontas be wearisome to I . I ... " I might have continue ever so much far , when , " Nonsense ! " she say , stamp her little foot . " why , I declare , George , you be more stupid than Harry ! " 
 " how do you mean , my dear child ? " I ask . 
 " when do you go ? you go away at three o'clock . you strike across on the road to Tottenham Court . you walk through the village , and return by the Green Lane that lead back towards the new hospital . you know you do ! if you walk for a week there , it can not   do you any harm . good morning , sir ! you will please not follow I any far . " and she drop I a curtsey , and walk away with a veil over her face . 
 that Green Lane , which lie to the north of the new hospital , be build all over with house now . in my time , when good old George II . be yet king , ' twa a shabby rural outlet of London ; so dangerous , that the City folk who go to their villa and junket house at Hampstead and the outlying village , would return in party of night , and escort by waiter with lanthorn , to defend they from the footpad who prowl about the town outskirt . Hampstead and highgate church , each crown its hill , fill up the background of the view which you see as you turn your back to London ; and one , two , three day Mr. George Warrington have the pleasure of look upon this landscape , and walk back in the direction of the new hospital . 
 along the lane be sundry small house of entertainment ; and I remember at one place , where they sell cake and beer , at the sign of the Protestant Hero , a decent woman smile at I on the third or fourth day , and curtsey in her clean apron , as she say , " it appear the lady do not   come , sir ! your honour have good step in , and take a can of my cool beer . " 
 at length , as I be come back through Tottenham Road , on the 25th of May o day to be mark with the white stone ! a little way beyond Mr. Whitefield 's Tabernacle , I see a landau before I , and on the box - seat by the driver be my young friend Charley , who wave his hat to I and call out , " George ! George ! " I run up to the carriage , my knee knock together so that I think I should fall by the wheel ; and inside I see Hetty , and by she my dear Theo , prop with a pillow . how thin the little hand have become since last it be lay in mine ! the cheek be flush and waste , the eye strangely bright , and the thrill of the voice when she speak a word or two , smote I with a pang , I know not of grief or joy be it , so intimately be they blend . 
 " I be take she an airing to Hampstead , " say Hetty , demurely . " the doctor say the air will do she good . " 
 " I have be ill , but I be well now , George , " say Theo . there come a great burst of music from the people in the chapel hard by , as she be speak . I hold her hand in mine . her eye be look into mine once more . it seem as if we have never be part . 
 I can never forget the tune of that psalm . I have hear it all through my life . my wife have touch it on her harpsichord , and her little one have warble it . now , do you understand , young people , why I love it so ? because ' twa the music play at our amoris redintegratio . because it sing hope to I , at the period of my existence the most miserable . yes , the most miserable : for that dreary confinement of Duquesne have its tenderness and kindly association connect with it ; and many a time in after day I have think with fondness of the poor Biche and my tipsy jailor , and the reveille of the forest bird and the military music of my prison . 
 Master Charley look down from his box - seat upon his sister and I engage in beatific contemplation , and Hetty listen too , to the music . " I think I should like to go and hear it . and that famous Mr. Whitfield , perhaps he be go to preach this very day ! come in with I , Charley and George can drive for half an hour with dear Theo towards Hampstead and back . " 
 Charley do not seem to have any very strong desire for witness the devotional exercise of good Mr. Whitfield and his congregation , and propose that George Warrington should take Hetty in ; but Het be not to be deny . " I will never help you in another exercise as long as you live , sir , " cry Miss Hetty , " if you do not   come on , " while the youth clamber down from his box - seat , and they enter the temple together . 
 can any moralist , bear my previous promise in mind , excuse I for jump into the carriage and sit down once more by my dear Theo ? suppose I do break them ? will he blame I much ? reverend sir , you be welcome . I break my promise ; and if you would not do as much , good friend , you be welcome to your virtue . not that I for a moment suspect my own child will ever be so bold as to think of have heart of their own , and bestow they accord to their liking . no , my young people , you will let papa choose for you ; be hungry when he tell you ; be thirsty when he order ; and settle your child 's marriage afterwards . 
 and now of course you be anxious to hear what take place when papa jump into the landau by the side of poor little mamma , prop up by her pillow . " I be come to your part of the story , my dear , " say I , look over to my wife as she be ply her needle . 
 " to what , pray ? " say my lady . " you should skip all that part , and come to the grand battle , and your heroic defence of " 
 " of Fort Fiddlededee in the year 1778 , when I pull off Mr. Washington 's epaulet , gouge General Gates 's eye , cut off Charles Lee 's head , and paste it on again ! " 
 " let we hear all about the fighting , " say the boy . even the Captain condescend to own he will listen to any military detail , though only from a militia officer . 
 " fair and softly , young people ! everything in its turn . I be not yet arrive at the war . I be only a young gentleman , just step into a landau , by the side of a young lady whom I promise to avoid . I be take her hand , which , after a little ado , she leave in mine . do you remember how hot it be , the little thing , how it tremble , and how it throb and jump a hundred and twenty in a minute ? and as we trot on towards Hampstead , I address Miss Lambert in the following term " 
 " ah , ah , ah ! " say the girl in a chorus with mademoiselle , their french governess , who cry , " nous ecouton maintenant . La parole est a vous , Monsieur le Chevalier ! " 
 here we have they all in a circle : mamma be at her side of the fire , papa at his ; Mademoiselle Eleonore , at whom the Captain look rather sweetly   eye off , Captain ! ; the two girl , listen like like nymphas discente to Apollo , let we say ; and John and Tummas   with obtuse ear , who be bring in the tea - tray and urn . 
 " very good , " say the Squire , pull out the MS . , and wave it before he . " we be go to tell your mother 's secret and mine . " 
 " I be sure you may , papa , " cry the house matron . " there be nothing to be ashamed of . " and a blush rise over her kind face . 
 " but before I begin , young folk , permit I two or three question . " 
 " allon , toujour des question ! " say mademoiselle , with a shrug of her pretty shoulder .   Florac have recommend she to we , and I suspect the little Chevalier have himself an eye upon this pretty Mademoiselle de Blois . 
 to the question , then . 




 CHAPTER LXXVII . and how everybody get out again 

 " you , Captain Miles Warrington , have the honour of win the good grace of a lady of ever so many lady of the Duchess of Devonshire , let we say , of Mrs. Crew , of Mrs. Fitzherbert , of the Queen of Prussia , of the Goddess Venus , of Mademoiselle Hillisberg of the Opera never mind of whom , in fine . if you win a lady 's good grace , do you always go to the mess and tell what happen ? " 
 " not such a fool , Squire ! " say the Captain , survey his side curl in the glass . 
 " have you , Miss Theo , tell your mother every word you say to Mr. Joe Blake , junior , in the shrubbery this morning ? " 
 " Joe Blake , indeed ! " cry Theo junior . 
 " and you , mademoiselle ? that scented billet which come to you under Sir Thomas 's frank , have you tell we all the letter contain ? look how she blush ! as red as the curtain , on my word ! no , mademoiselle , we all have our secret "   say the Squire , here make his good french bow . " no , Theo , there be nothing in the shrubbery only nut , my child ! no , Miles , my son , we do not   tell all , even to the most indulgent of father and if I tell what happen in a landau on the Hampstead Road , on the 25th of May , 1760 , may the Chevalier Ruspini pull out every tooth in my head ! " 
 " pray tell , papa ! " cry mamma : " or , as Jobson , who drive we , be in your service now , perhaps you will have he in from the stable ! I insist upon your telling ! " 
 " what be , then , this mystery ? " ask mademoiselle , in her pretty french accent , of my wife . 
 " eh , ma fille ! " whisper the lady . " Thou wouldst ask I what I say ? I say ' yes ! ' behold all I say . " and so ' tis my wife have peach , and not I ; and this be the sum of our conversation , as the carriage , all too swiftly as I think , gallop towards Hampstead , and fly back again . Theo have not agree to fly in the face of her honoured parent no such thing . but we would marry no other person ; no , not if we live to be as old as Methuselah ; no , not the Prince of Wales himself would she take . her heart she have give away with her papa 's consent nay , order it be not her to resume . so kind a father must relent one of these day ; and , if George would keep his promise be it now , or be it in twenty year , or be it in another world , she know she should never break her . 
 Hetty 's face beam with delight when , my little interview over , she see Theo 's countenance wear a sweet tranquillity . all the doctor 's medicine have not do she so much good , the fond sister say . the girl go home after their act of disobedience . I give up the place which I have hold during a brief period of happiness by my dear invalid 's side . Hetty skip back into her seat , and Charley on to his box . he tell I in after day , that it be a very dull , stupid sermon he have hear . the little chap be too orthodox to love dissent preacher ' sermon . 
 Hetty be not the only one of the family who remark her sister 's altered countenance and improved spirit . I be tell that on the girl ' return home their mother embrace both of they , especially the invalid , with more than common ardour of affection . " there be nothing like a country ride , " Aunt Lambert say , " for do her dear Theo good . she have be on the road to Hampstead , have she ? she must have another ride to - morrow . Heaven be bless , my Lord Wrotham 's horse be at their order three or four time a week , and the sweet child might have the advantage of they ! " as for the idea that Mr. Warrington might have happen to meet the child on their drive , Aunt Lambert never once entertain it , at least speak of it . I leave anybody who be interested in the matter to guess whether Mrs. Lambert could by any possibility have suppose that her daughter and her sweetheart could ever have come together again . do woman help each other in love perplexity ? do woman scheme , intrigue , make little plan , tell little fib , provide little amorous opportunity , hang up the rope - ladder , coax , wheedle , mystify the guardian or Abigail , and turn their attention away while Strephon and Chloe be bill and coo in the twilight , or whisk off in the postchaise to Gretna Green ? my dear young folk , some people there be of this nature ; and some kind soul who have love tenderly and truly in their own time , continue ever after to be kindly and tenderly disposed towards their young successor , when they begin to play the same pretty game . 
 Miss Prim do not . if she hear of two young person attach to each other , it be to snarl at they for fool , or to imagine of they all conceivable evil . because she have a hump - back herself , she be for bite everybody else 's . I believe if she see a pair of turtle coo in a wood , she would turn her eye down , or fle a stone to frighten they ; but I be speak , you see , young lady , of your grandmother , Aunt Lambert , who be one great syllabub of human kindness ; and , besides , about the affair at present under discussion , how be I ever to tell whether she know anything regard it or not ? 
 so , all she say to Theo on her return home be , " my child , the country air have do you all the good in the world , and I hope you will take another drive to - morrow , and another , and another , and so on . " 
 " do not   you think , papa , the ride have do the child most wonderful good , and must not she be make to go out in the air ? " Aunt Lambert ask of the General , when he come in for supper . 
 " yes , sure , if a coach - and - six will do his little Theo good , she shall have it , " Lambert say , " or he will drag the landau up Hampstead Hill himself , if there be no horse ; " and so the good man would have spend , freely , his guineas , or his breath , or his blood , to give his child pleasure . he be charm at his girl 's altered countenance ; she pick a bit of chicken with appetite : she drink a little negus , which he make for she : indeed it do seem to be well than the kind doctor 's good medicine , which hitherto , God wot , have be of little benefit . Mamma be gracious and happy . Hetty be radiant and rident . it be quite like an evening at home at Oakhurst . never for month past , never since that fatal , cruel day , that no one speak of , have they spend an evening so delightful . 
 but , if the other woman choose to coax and cajole the good , simple father , Theo herself be too honest to continue for long even that sweet and fond delusion . when , for the third or fourth time , he come back to the delightful theme of his daughter 's improved health , and ask , " what have do it ? be it the country air ? be it the Jesuit 's bark ? be it the new medicine ? " 
 " can not   you think , dear , what it be ? " she say , lay a hand upon her father 's , with a tremor in her voice , perhaps , but eye that be quite open and bright . 
 " and what be it , my child ? " ask the General . 
 " it be because I have see he again , papa ! " she say . 
 the other two woman turn pale , and Theo 's heart too begin to palpitate , and her cheek to whiten , as she continue to look in her father 's scared face . 
 " it be not wrong to see he , " she continue , more quickly ; " it would have be wrong not to tell you . " 
 " Great God ! " groan the father , draw his hand back , and with such a dreadful grief in his countenance , that Hetty run to she almost swoon sister , clasp she to her heart , and cry out , rapidly , " Theo know nothing of it , sir ! it be my do it be all my doing ! " 
 theo lie on her sister 's neck , and kiss it twenty , fifty time . 
 " woman , woman ! be you play with my honour ? " cry the father , burst out with a fierce exclamation . 
 Aunt Lambert sob , wildly , " Martin ! Martin ! do not   say a word to she ! " again call out Hetty , and fall back herself staggering towards the wall , for Theo have faint on her shoulder . 
 I be take my breakfast next morning , with what appetite I might , when my door open , and my faithful black announce , " General Lambert . " at once I see , by the General 's face , that the yesterday 's transaction be know to he . " your accomplice do not confess , " the General say , as soon as my servant have leave we , " but side with you against their father a proof how desirable clandestine meeting be . it be from Theo herself I hear that she have see you . " 
 " accomplice , sir ! " I say   perhaps not unwilling to turn the conversation from the real point at issue . " you know how fondly and dutifully your young people regard their father . if they side against you in this instance , it must be because justice be against you . a man like you be not go to set up sic volo sic jubeo as the sole law in his family ! " 
 " Psha , George ! " cry the General . " for though we be part , God forbid I should desire that we should cease to love each other . I have your promise that you would not seek to see she . " 
 " nor do I go to she , sir , " I say , turn red , no doubt ; for though this be truth , I own it be untrue . 
 " you mean she be bring to you ? " say Theo 's father , in great agitation . " be it behind Hester 's petticoat that you will shelter yourself ? what a fine defence for a gentleman ! " 
 " well , I will not   screen myself behind the poor child , " I reply . " to speak as I do be to make an attempt at evasion , and I be ill - accustomed to dissemble . I do not infringe the letter of my agreement , but I act against the spirit of it . from this moment I annul it altogether . " 
 " you break your word give to I ! " cry Mr. Lambert . 
 " I recall a hasty promise make on a sudden at a moment of extreme excitement and perturbation . no man can be for ever bind by word utter at such a time ; and , what be more , no man of honour or humanity , Mr. Lambert , would try to bind he . " 
 " Dishonour to I ! sir , " exclaim the General . 
 " yes , if the phrase be to be shuttlecock between we ! " I answer , hotly . " there can be no question about love , or mutual regard , or difference of age , when that word be use : and be you my own father and I love you well than a father , Uncle Lambert , I would not bear it ! what have I do ? I have see the woman whom I consider my wife before God and man , and if she call I I will see she again . if she come to I , here be my home for she , and the half of the little I have . ' tis you , who have no right , have make I the gift , to resume it . because my mother taunt you unjustly , be you to visit Mrs. Esmond 's wrong upon this tender , innocent creature ? you profess to love your daughter , and you can not   bear a little wound pride for her sake . well she should perish away in misery , than an old woman in Virginia should say that Mr. Lambert have scheme to marry one of his daughter . say that to satisfy what you call honour and I call selfishness , we part , we break our heart well nigh , we rally , we try to forget each other , we marry elsewhere ? can any man be to my dear as I have be ? God forbid ! can any woman be to I what she be ? you shall marry she to the Prince of Wales to - morrow , and it be a cowardice and treason . how can we , how can you , undo the promise we have make to each other before Heaven ? you may part we : and she will die as surely as if she be Jephthah 's daughter . have you make any vow to Heaven to compass her murder ? kill she if you conceive your promise so bind you : but this I swear , that I be glad you have come , so that I may here formally recall a hasty pledge which I give , and that , call I when she will , I will come to she ! " 
 no doubt this speech be make with the flurry and agitation belong to Mr. Warrington 's youth , and with the firm conviction that death would infallibly carry off one or both of the party , in case their worldly separation be inevitably decreed . who do not believe his first passion eternal ? have watch the world since , and see the rise , progress , and alas , that I must say it ! decay of other amour , I may smile now as I think of my own youthful error and ardour ; but , if it be a superstition , I have rather hold it ; I have rather think that neither of we could have live with any other mate , and that , of all its innumerable creature , Heaven decree these special two should be join together . 
 " we must come , then , to what I have fain have spar myself , " say the General , in reply to my outbreak ; " to an unfriendly separation . when I meet you , Mr. Warrington , I must know you no more . I must order and they will not do other than obey I my family and child not to recognise you when they see you , since you will not recognise in your intercourse with I the respect due to my age , the courtesy of gentleman . I have hop so far from your sense of honour , and the idea I have form of you , that , in my present great grief and perplexity , I should have find you willing to soothe and help I as far as you might for , God know , I have need of everybody 's sympathy . but , instead of help , you fle obstacle in my way . instead of a friend a gracious Heaven pardon I ! I find in you an enemy ! an enemy to the peace of my home and the honour of my child , sir ! and as such I shall treat you , and know how to deal with you , when you molest I ! " 
 and , wave his hand to I , and put on his hat , Mr. Lambert hastily quit my apartment . 
 I be confound , and believe , indeed , there be war between we . the brief happiness of yesterday be cloud over and go , and I think that never since the day of the first separation have I feel so exquisitely unhappy as now , when the bitterness of quarrel be add to the pang of parting , and I stand not only alone but friendless . in the course of one year 's constant intimacy I have come to regard Lambert with a reverence and affection which I have never before feel for any mortal man except my dear Harry . that his face should be turn from I in anger be as if the sun have go out of my sphere , and all be dark around I . and yet I feel sure that in withdraw the hasty promise I have make not to see Theo , I be act rightly that my fidelity to she , as hers now to I , be paramount to all other tie of duty or obedience , and that , ceremony or none , I be her , first and before all . promise be pass between we , from which no parent could absolve either ; and all the priest in Christendom could no more than attest and confirm the sacred contract which have tacitly be ratify between we . 
 I see Jack Lambert by chance that day , as I go mechanically to my not unusual haunt , the library of the new Museum ; and with the impetuousness of youth , and eager to impart my sorrow to some one , I take he out of the room and lead he about the garden , and pour out my grief to he . I do not much care for Jack   who in truth be somewhat of a prig , and not a little pompous and wearisome with his latin quotation   except in the time of my own sorrow , when I would fasten upon he or any one ; and have suffer himself in his affair with the little american , be haud ignarus mali   as I know he would say , I find the college gentleman ready to compassionate another 's misery . I tell he , what have here be represent at great length , of my yesterday 's meeting with his sister ; of my interview with his father in the morning ; of my determination at all hazard never to part with Theo . when I find from the various quotation from the greek and latin author which he utter that he lean to my side in the dispute , I think he a man of great sense , clung eagerly to his elbow , and bestow upon he much more affection than he be accustom at other time to have from I . I walk with he up to his father 's lodging in Dean Street ; see he enter at the dear door ; survey the house from without with a sickening desire to know from its exterior appearance how my beloved fare within ; and call for a bottle at the coffee - house where I wait Jack 's return . I call he Brother when I send he away . I fondle he as the condemn wretch at Newgate hang about the jailor or the parson , or any one who be kind to he in his misery . I drink a whole bottle of wine at the coffee - house by the way , Jack 's Coffee - House be its name call another . I think Jack would never come back . 
 he appear at length with rather a scared face ; and , come to my box , pour out for himself two or three bumper from my second bottle , and then fall to his story , which , to I at least , be not a little interesting . my poor Theo be keep her room , it appear , be much agitated by the occurrence of yesterday ; and Jack have come home in time to find dinner on table ; after which his good father hold forth upon the occurrence of the morning , be anxious and able to speak more freely , he say , because his eld son be present and Theodosia be not in the room . the General state what have happen at my lodging between I and he . he bid hester be silent , who indeed be as dumb as a mouse , poor thing ! he tell Aunt Lambert   who be indulge in that madefaction of pocket - handkerchief which I have before describe , and with something like an imprecation , that the woman be all against he , and pimp   he call they   for one another ; and frantically turn round to Jack , ask what be his view in the matter ? 
 to his father 's surprise and his mother 's and sister 's delight , Jack make a speech on my side . he rule with I   cite what ancient authority I do not   know , that the matter have go out of the hand of the parent on either side ; that have give their consent , some month previously , the elder have put themselves out of court . though he do not hold with a great , a respectable , he might say a host of divine , those sacramental view of the marriage - ceremony for which there be a great deal to be say yet he hold it , if possible , even more sacredly than they ; conceive that though marriage be make before the civil magistrate , and without the priest , yet they be , before Heaven , bind and indissoluble . 
 " it be not merely , sir , " say Jack , turn to his father , " those whom I , John Lambert , Priest , have join , let no man put asunder ; it be those whom God have join let no man separate . "   here he take off his hat , as he tell the story to I .   " my view be clear upon the point , and surely these young people be join , or permit to plight themselves to each other by the consent of you , the priest of your own family . my view , I say , be clear , and I will lay they down at length in a series of two or three discourse which , no doubt , will satisfy you . upon which , " say Jack , " my father say , ' I be satisfied already , my dear boy , ' and my lively little Het   who have much archness   whisper to I , ' Jack , mother and I will make you a dozen shirt , as sure as egg be egg . ' " 
 " whilst we be talk , " Mr. Lambert resume , " my sister Theodosia make her appearance , I must say very much agitated and pale , kiss our father , and sate down at his side , and take a sippet of toast   my dear George , this port be excellent , and I drink your health   and take a sippet of toast and dip it in his negus . 
 " ' you should have be here to hear Jack 's sermon ! ' say Hester . ' he have be preach most beautifully . ' 
 " ' has he ? ' ask Theodosia , who be too languid and weak , poor thing , much to care for the exercise of eloquence , or the display of authority , such as I must own , " say Jack , " it be give to I this afternoon to bring forward . 
 " ' he have talk for three quarter of an hour by Shrewsbury clock , ' say my father , though I certainly have not talk so long or half so long by my own watch . ' and his discourse have be you , my dear , ' say papa , play with Theodosia 's hand . 
 " ' I , papa ? ' 
 " ' you and and Mr. Warrington and and George , my love , ' say papa . upon which "   say Mr. Jack . " my sister come close to the General , and lay her head upon he , and weep upon his shoulder . 
 " ' this be different , sir , ' say I , ' to a passage I remember in Pausanias . ' 
 " ' in Pausanias ? indeed ! ' say the General . ' and pray who be he ? ' 
 " I smile at my father 's simplicity in expose his ignorance before his child . ' when Ulysses be take away Penelope from her father , the king hasten after his daughter and bridegroom , and besought his darling to return . Whereupon , it be relate , Ulysses offer she her choice , whether she would return , or go on with he ? upon which the daughter of Icarius cover her face with her veil . for want of a veil my sister have take refuge in your waistcoat , sir , ' I say , and we all laugh ; though my mother vow that if such a proposal have be make to she , or Penelope have be a girl of spirit , she would have go home with her father that instant . 
 " ' but I be not a girl of any spirit , dear mother ! ' say Theodosia , still in gremio patris . I do not remember that this habit of caressing be frequent in my own youth , " continue Jack . " but after some more discourse , Brother Warrington ! bethink I of you , and leave my parent insist upon Theodosia return to bed . the late transaction have , it appear , weaken and agitate she much . I myself have experience , in my own case , how full of solliciti timoris be a certain passion ; how it rack the spirit ; and I make no doubt , if carry far enough , or indulge to the extent to which woman who have little philosophy will permit it to go I make no doubt , I say , be ultimately injurious to the health . my service to you , brother ! " 
 from grief to hope , how rapid the change be ! what a flood of happiness pour into my soul , and glow in my whole being ! Landlord , more port ! would honest Jack have drink a binful I would have treat he ; and , to say truth , Jack 's sympathy be large in this case , and it have be generous all day . I decline to score the bottle of port : and place to the fabulous computation of interested waiter , the amount score against I in the reckoning . Jack be my dearest , good of brother . my friendship for he I swear should be eternal . if I could do he any service , be it a bishopric , by George ! he should have it . he say I be interrupt by the watchman rhapsodise verse beneath the love one 's window . I know not . I know I awake joyfully and rapturously , in spite of a rack headache the next morning . 
 nor do I know the extent of my happiness quite , or the entire conversion of my dear noble enemy of the previous morning . it must have be gall to the pride of an eld man to have to yield to representation and objection couch in language so little dutiful as that I have use towards Mr. Lambert . but the true christian gentleman , retire from his talk with I , mortify and wound by my asperity of remonstrance , as well as by the pain which he see his beloved daughter suffer , go thoughtfully and sadly to his business , as he subsequently tell I , and in the afternoon   as his custom not unfrequently be   into a church which be open for prayer . and it be here , on his knee , submit his case in the quarter whither he frequently , though privately , come for guidance and comfort , that it seem to he that his child be right in her persistent fidelity to I , and himself wrong in demand her utter submission . hence Jack 's cause be win almost before he begin to plead it ; and the brave , gentle heart , which could bear no rancour , which bleed at inflict pain on those it love , which even shrink from assert authority or demand submission , be only too glad to return to its natural pulse of love and affection . 




 CHAPTER LXXVIII . Pyramus and Thisbe 

 in examine the old paper at home , year afterwards , I find , docket and label with my mother 's well - know neat handwriting , " from London , April , 1760 . my son 's dreadful letter . " when it come to be mine I burn the document , not choose that that story of domestic grief and disunion should remain amongst our family annal for future Warringtons to gaze on , mayhap , and disobedient son to hold up as example of foregone domestic rebellion . for similar reason , I have destroy the paper which my mother despatch to I at this time of tyranny , revolt , annoyance , and irritation . 
 madden by the pang of separation from my mistress , and not unrightly consider that Mrs. Esmond be the prime cause of the great grief and misery which have ever befall I in the world , I write home to Virginia a letter , which might have be more temperate , it be true , but in which I endeavour to maintain the extreme respect and reticence . I say I do not know by what motif she have be influence , but that I hold her answerable for the misery of my future life , which she have choose wilfully to mar and render wretche . she have occasion a separation between I and a virtuous and innocent young creature , whose own hope , health , and happiness be cast down for ever by Mrs. Esmond 's interference . the deed be do , as I fear , and I would offer no comment upon the conduct of the perpetrator , who be answerable to God alone ; but I do not disguise from my mother that the injury which she have do I be so dreadful and mortal , that her life or mine could never repair it ; that the tie of my allegiance be break towards she , and that I never could be , as heretofore , her dutiful and respectful son . 
 Madam Esmond reply to I in a letter of very great dignity   her style and correspondence be extraordinarily elegant and fine . she utter not a single reproach or hard word , but coldly give I to understand that it be before that awful tribunal of God she have refer the case between we , and ask for counsel ; that , in respect of her own conduct , as a mother , she be ready , in all humility , to face it . might I , as a son , be equally able to answer for myself , and to show , when the Great Judge demand the question of I , whether I have do my own duty , and honour my father and mother ! o popoi ! my grandfather have quote in his memoir a line of Homer , show how in our trouble and grief the god be always call in question . when our pride , our avarice , our interest , our desire to domineer , be work upon , be we not for ever pester Heaven to decide in their favour ? in our great american quarrel , do we not on both side appeal to the sky as to the justice of our cause , sing Te Deum for victory , and boldly express our confidence that the right should prevail ? be America right because she be victorious ? then I suppose Poland be wrong because she be defeat ? how be I wander into this digression about Poland , America , and what not , and all the while thinking of a little woman now no more , who appeal to Heaven and confront it with a thousand text out of its own book , because her son want to make a marriage not of her like ? we appeal , we imprecate , we go down on our knee , we demand blessing , we shriek out for sentence accord to law ; the great course of the great world move on ; we pant , and strive , and struggle ; we hate ; we rage ; we weep passionate tear ; we reconcile ; we race and win ; we race and lose ; we pass away , and other little struggler succeed ; our day be spend ; our night come , and another morning rise , which shin on we no more . 
 my letter to Madam Esmond , announce my revolt and disobedience   perhaps I myself be a little proud of the composition of that document , I show in duplicate to Mr. Lambert , because I wish he to understand what my relation to my mother be , and how I be determine , whatever of threat or quarrel the future might bring , never for my own part to consider my separation from Theo as other than a force one . whenever I could see she again I would . my word give to she be in secula seculorum , or bind at least as long as my life should endure . I imply that the girl be similarly bind to I , and her poor father know indeed as much . he might separate we ; as he might give she a dose of poison , and the gentle , obedient creature would take it and die ; but the death or separation would be his do : let he answer they . now he be tender about his child to weakness , and could not have the heart to submit any one of they this one especially to torture . we have try to part : we could not . he have endeavour to separate we : it be more than be in his power . the bar be up , but the young couple the maid within and the knight without be love each other all the same . the wall be build , but Pyramus and Thisbe be whisper on either side . in the midst of all his grief and perplexity , Uncle Lambert have plenty of humour , and could not but see that his role be rather a sorry one . light be begin to show through that lime and rough plaster of the wall : the lover be get their hand through , then their head through indeed , it be wall 's good business to retire . 
 I forget what happen stage by stage and day by day ; nor , for the instruction of future age , do it much matter . when my descendant have love scrape of their own , they will find their own mean of get out of they . I believe I do not go back to Dean Street , but that practice of drive in the open air be consider most healthful for Miss Lambert . I get a fine horse , and ride by the side of her carriage . the old woman at Tottenham Court come to know both of we quite well , and nod and wink in the most friendly manner when we pass by . I fancy the old goody be not unaccustomed to interest herself in young couple , and have dispense the hospitality of her roadside cottage to more than one pair . 
 the doctor and the country air effect a prodigious cure upon Miss Lambert . Hetty always attend as duenna , and sometimes of his holiday , Master Charley ride my horse when I get into the carriage . what a deal of love - make Miss Hetty hear ! with what exemplary patience she listen to it ! I do not say she go to hear the Methodist sermon any more , but ' tis certain that when we have a closed carriage she would very kindly and considerately look out of the window . then , what heap of letter there be ! what run to and fro ! Gumbo 's bandy leg be for ever on the trot from my quarter to Dean Street ; and , on my account or her own , Mrs. Molly , the girl 's maid , be for ever bring back answer to Bloomsbury . by the time when the autumn leaf begin to turn pale , Miss Theo 's rose be in full bloom again , and my good Doctor Heberden 's cure be pronounce to be complete . what else happen during this blessed period ? Mr. Warrington complete his great tragedy of Pocahontas , which be not only accept by Mr. Garrick this time   his friend Dr. Johnson have speak not unfavourably of the work , but my friend and cousin , Hagan , be engage by the manager to perform the part of the hero , Captain Smith . Hagan 's engagement be not make before it be want . I have help he and his family with mean disproportione , perhaps , to my power , especially consider my feud with Madam Esmond , whose answer to my angry missive of April come to I towards autumn , and who write back from Virginia with war for war , controlment for controlment . these menace , however , frighten I little : my poor mother 's thunder could not reach I ; and my conscience , or casuistry , supply I with other interpretation for her text of scripture , so that her oracle have not the least weight with I in frighten I from my purpose . how my new love speed I neither inform she , nor any other member of my maternal or paternal family , who , on both side , have be bitter against my marriage . of what use wrangle with they ? it be well to carpere diem and its sweet love and pleasure , and to leave the railer to grumble , or the senior to advise , at their ease . 
 besides Madam Esmond I have , it must be own , in the frantic rage of my temporary separation , address note of wondrous sarcasm to my Uncle Warrington , to my Aunt Madame de Bernstein , and to my Lord or Lady of Castlewood   I forget to which individually , thank they for the trouble which they have take in prevent the dear happiness of my life , and promise they a corresponding gratitude from their oblige relative . business bring the jovial Baronet and his family to London somewhat early than usual , and Madame de Bernstein be never sorry to get back to Clarges Street and her card . I see they . they find I perfectly well . they conclude the match be break off , and I do not choose to undeceive they . the Baroness take heart at see how cheerful I be , and make many sly joke about my philosophy , and my prudent behaviour as a man of the world . she be , as ever , bent upon find a rich match for I : and I fear I pay many compliment at her house to a rich young soap - boiler 's daughter from Mile End , whom the worthy Baroness wish to place in my arm . 
 " you court she with infinite wit and esprit , my dear , " say my pleased kinswoman , " but she do not understand half you say , and the other half , I think , frighten she . this ton de persiflage be very well in our society , but you must be spar of it , my dear nephew , amongst these roturier . " 
 Miss Badge marry a young gentleman of royal dignity , though shatter fortune , from a neighbouring island ; and I trust Mrs. Mackshane have ere this pardon my levity . there be another person besides Miss at my aunt 's house , who do not understand my persiflage much well than Miss herself ; and that be a lady who have see James the Second 's reign , and who be alive and as worldly as ever in King George 's . I love to be with she : but that my little folk have access to this volume , I could put down a hundred story of the great old folk whom she have know in the great old day of George the First and his lady , of St. John and Marlborough , of his reign Majesty and the late Prince of Wales , and the cause of the quarrel between they but my modest muse pipe for boy and virgin . Son Miles do not care about court story , or if he doth , have a fresh budget from Carlton House , quite as bad as the bad of our old Baroness . no , my dear wife , thou hast no need to shake thy powdered lock at I ! papa be not go to scandalise his nursery with old - world gossip , nor bring a blush over our chaste bread - and - butter . 
 but this piece of scandal I can not help . my aunt use to tell it with infinite gusto ; for , to do her justice , she hat your would - be good people , and snigger over the fault of the self - style righteous with uncommon satisfaction . in her later day she have no hypocrisy , at least ; and in so far be well than some whitewash ... well , to the story . my Lady Warrington , one of the tall and the most virtuous of her sex , who have goodness for ever on her lip and " Heaven in her eye , " like the woman in Mr. Addison 's tedious tragedy   which have keep the stage , from which some other , which shall be nameless , have disappear , have the world in her other eye , and an exceedingly shrewd desire of push herself in it . what do she do , when my marriage with your ladyship yonder be suppose to be break off , but attempt to play off on I those art which she have try on my poor Harry with such signal ill success , and which fail with I likewise ! it be not the Beauty Miss Flora be for my master   and what a master ! I protest I take off my hat at the idea of such an illustrious connexion !   it be Dora , the Muse , be set upon I to languish at I and to pity I , and to read even my godless tragedy , and applaud I and console I . meanwhile , how be the Beauty occupy ? will it be believe that my severe aunt give a great entertainment to my Lady Yarmouth , present her boy to she , and place poor little mile under her ladyship 's august protection ? that , so far , be certain ; but can it be that she send her daughter to stay at my lady 's house , which our gracious lord and master daily visit , and with the view which old Aunt Bernstein attribute to she ? " but for that fit of apoplexy , my dear , " Bernstein say , " that aunt of yours intend there should have be a Countess in her own right in the Warrington family ! "   Compare Walpole 's letter in Mr. Cunningham 's excellent new edition . see the story of the supper at N. House , to show what great nobleman would do for a king 's mistress , and the pleasant account of the waiting for the Prince of Wales before Holland House.-EDITOR .   my neighbour and kinswoman , my Lady Claypole , be dead and bury . grow white , ye daisy , upon Flora 's tomb ! I can see my pretty Miles , in a gay little uniform of the Norfolk Militia , lead up by his parent to the lady whom the King delight to honour , and the good - natured old Jezebel lay her hand upon the boy 's curly pate . I be accuse of be but a lukewarm royalist ; but sure I can contrast those time with ours , and acknowledge the difference between the late sovereign and the present , who , bear a Briton , have give to every family in the empire an example of decorum and virtuous life .   the Warrington MS . be date 1793.-ED . 
 thus my life speed in the pleasantest of all occupation ; and , be so happy myself , I could afford to be reconcile to those who , after all , have do I no injury , but rather add to the zest of my happiness by the brief obstacle which they have place in my way . no specific plan be form , but Theo and I know that a day would come when we need say farewell no more . should the day befall a year hence ten year hence we be ready to wait . day after day we discuss our little plan , with Hetty for our confidante . on our drive we spy out pretty cottage that we think might suit young people of small mean ; we devise all sort of delightful scheme and childish economy . we be Strephon and Chloe to be sure . a cot and a brown loaf should content we ! Gumbo and Molly should wait upon we   as indeed they have do from that day until this . at twenty , who be afraid of be poor ? our trial would only confirm our attachment . the ' sweet sorrow " of every day 's parting but make the morrow 's meeting more delightful ; and when we separate we run home and write each other those precious letter which we and other young gentleman and lady write under such circumstance ; but though my wife have they all in a great tin sugar - box in the closet in her bedroom , and , I own , I myself have look at they once , and even think some of they pretty , I hereby desire my heir and executor to burn they all , unread , at our demise ; specially desire my son the Captain   to whom I know the perusal of MSS . be not pleasant   to perform this duty . those secret whisper to the penny - post , or deliver between Molly and Gumbo , be intend for we alone , and no ear of our descendant shall overhear they . 
 we hear in successive brief letter how our dear Harry continue with the army , as Mr. General Amherst 's aide - de - camp , after the death of his own glorious general . by the middle of October there come news of the Capitulation of Montreal and the whole of Canada , and a brief postscript in which Hal say he would ask for leave now , and must go and see the old lady at home , who write as sulky as a bare , Captain Warrington remark . I could guess why , though the claw could not reach I . I have write pretty fully to my brother how affair be stand with I in England . 
 then , on the 25th October , come the news that his Majesty have fall down dead at Kensington , and that George III . reign over we . I fear we grieve but little . what do those care for the Atridae whose heart be string only to erota mounon ? a modest , handsome , brave new Prince , we gladly accept the common report that he be endow with every virtue ; and we cry huzzay with the loyal crowd that hail his accession : it could make little difference to we , as we think , simple young sweetheart , whisper our little love - story in our corner . 
 but who can say how great event affect he ? do not our little Charley , at the Chartreux , wish impiously for a new king immediately , because on his gracious Majesty 's accession Doctor Crusius give his boy a holiday ? he and I , and Hetty , and Theo   Miss Theo be strong enough to walk many a delightful mile now , hear the Heralds proclaim his new Majesty before Savile House in Leicester Fields , and a pickpocket get the watch and chain of a gentleman hard by we , and be catch and carry to Bridewell , all on account of his Majesty 's accession . have the king not die , the gentleman would not have be in the crowd ; the chain would not have be seize ; the thief would not have be catch and soundly whip : in this way many of we , more or less remotely , be implicate in the great change which ensue , and even we humble folk be affect by it presently . 
 as thus . my Lord Wrotham be a great friend of the august family of Savile House , who know and esteem his many virtue . now , of all live man , my Lord Wrotham know and love good his neighbour and old fellow - soldier , Martin Lambert , declare that the world contain few well gentleman . and my Lord Bute , be all potent , at first , with his Majesty , and a nobleman , as I believe , very eager at the commencement of his brief and luckless tenure of power , to patronise merit wherever he could find it , be strongly prejudiced in Mr. Lambert 's favour by the latter 's old and constant friend . 
 my   and Harry 's   old friend Parson Sampson , who have be in and out of gaol I do not   know how many time of late year , and retain an ever - endure hatred for the Esmonds of Castlewood , and as last a regard for I and my brother , be occupy poor Hal 's vacant bed at my lodging at this time   be , in truth , hunt out of his own by the bailiff . I like to have Sampson near I , for a more amusing Jack - friar never walk in cassock ; and , besides , he enter into all my rhapsody about Miss Theo ; be never tired   so he vow   of hear I talk of she ; admire Pocahontas and Carpezan with , I do believe , an honest enthusiasm ; and could repeat whole passage of those tragedy with an emphasis and effect that Barry or cousin Hagan himself could not surpass . Sampson be the go - between between Lady Maria and such of her relation as have not disown she ; and , always in debt himself , be never more happy than in drink a pot , or mingle his tear with his friend in similar poverty . his acquaintance with pawnbroker ' shop be prodigious . he could procure more money , he boast , on an article than any gentleman of his cloth . he never pay his own debt , to be sure , but he be ready to forgive his debtor . poor as he be , he always find mean to love and help his needy little sister , and a more prodigal , kindly , amiable rogue never probably grin behind bar . they say that I love to have parasite about I . I own to have have a great liking for Sampson , and to have esteem he much well than probably much well man . 
 when he hear how my Lord Bute be admit into the cabinet , Sampson vow and declare that his lordship a great lover of the drama , who have be to see Carpezan , who have admire it , and who would act the part of the king very finely in it he vow , by George ! that my lord must give I a place worthy of my birth and merit . he insist upon it that I should attend his lordship 's levee . I would not ? the Esmonds be all as proud as Lucifer ; and , to be sure , my birth be as good as that of any man in Europe . Demmy ! where be my lord himself when the Esmonds be lord of great county , warrior , and Crusaders ? where be they ? beggarly Scotchmen , without a rag to their back by George ! tear raw fish in their island . but now the time be change . the Scotchmen be in luck . Mum 's the word ! " I do not   envy he , " say Sampson , " but he shall provide for you and my dearest , noblest , heroic captain ! he shall , by George ! " would my worthy parson roar out . and when , in the month after his accession , his Majesty order the play of Richard III . at Drury Lane , my chaplain curse , vow , swore , but he would have he to Covent Garden to see Carpezan too . and now , one morning , he burst into my apartment , where I happen to lie rather late , wave the newspaper in his hand , and sing " Huzza ! " with all his might . 
 " what be it , Sampson ? " say I. " have my brother get his promotion ? " 
 " no , in truth : but some one else have . Huzzay ! huzzay ! his Majesty have appoint Major - General Martin Lambert to be Governor and Commander - in - chief of the Island of Jamaica . " 
 I start up . here be news , indeed ! Mr. Lambert would go to his government : and who would go with he ? I have be sup with some genteel young fellow at the Cocoa - Tree . the rascal Gumbo have a note for I from my dear mistress on the night previous , convey the same news to I , and have delay to deliver it . Theo beg I to see she at the old place at midday the next day without fail .   in the Warrington MS . there be not a word to say what the " old place " be . perhaps some oblige reader of Notes and Queries will be able to inform I , and who Mrs. Goodison was.-ED . 
 there be no little trepidation in our little council when we reach our place of meeting . Papa have announce his acceptance of the appointment , and his speedy departure . he would have a frigate give he , and take his family with he . merciful power ! and be we to be part ? my Theo 's old deathly paleness return to she . Aunt Lambert think she would have swoon ; one of Mrs. Goodison 's girl have a bottle of salt , and run up with it from the workroom . " go away ? go away in a frigate , Aunt Lambert ? go to tear she away from I ? great God ! Aunt Lambert , I shall die ! " she be well when mamma come up from the workroom with the young lady 's bottle of salt . you see the woman use to meet I : know dear Theo 's delicate state , how could they refrain from compassionate she ! but the General be so busy with his levee and his wait on Ministers , and his outfit , and the settlement of his affair at home , that they never happen to tell he about our little walk and meeting ; and even when order for the outfit of the lady be give , Mrs. Goodison , who have know and work for Miss Molly Benson as a schoolgirl   she remember Miss Esmond of Virginia perfectly , the worthy lady tell I , and a dress she make for the young lady to be present at her Majesty 's Ball   " even when the outfit be order for the three lady , " say Mrs. Goodison , demurely , " why , I think I could do no harm in complete the order . " 
 now I need not say in what perturbation of mind Mr. Warrington go home in the evening to his lodging , after the discussion with the lady of the above news . no , or at least a very few , more walk ; no more ride to dear , dear Hampstead or beloved Islington ; no more fetching and carrying of letter for Gumbo and Molly ! the former blubber so , that Mr. Warrington be quite touch by his fidelity , and give he a crown - piece to go to supper with the poor girl , who turn out to be his sweetheart . what , you too unhappy , Gumbo , and tear from the maid you love ? I be ready to mingle with he tear for tear . 
 what a solemn conference I have with Sampson that evening ! he know my affair , my expectation , my mother 's anger . Psha ! that be far off , and he know some excellent liberal people   of the order of Melchizedek   who would discount the other . the General would not give his consent ? Sampson shrug his broad shoulder and swear a great roaring oath . my mother would not relent ? what then ? a man be a man , and to make his own way in the world ? he suppose . he be only a churl who will not   play for such a stake as that , and lose or win , by George ! shout the chaplain , over a bottle of Burgundy at the Bedford Head , where he din . I need not put down our conversation . we be two of we , and I think there be only one mind between we . our talk be of a Saturday night ....
