 I do not tell Theo , nor any relative of her , what be be do . but when the dear child falter and talk , tremble , of the come departure , I bid her bear up , and vow all would be well , so confidently , that she , who ever have take her alarm and joy from my face   I wish , my dear , it be sometimes not so gloomy , could not but feel confidence ; and place   with many fond word that need not here be repeat   her entire trust in I murmur those sweet word of Ruth that must have comfort myriad of tender heart in my dearest maiden 's plight ; that whither I would go she would go , and that my people should be her . at last , one day , the General 's preparation be make , the trunk encumber the passage of the dear old Dean Street lodging , which I shall love as long as I shall remember at all one day , almost the last of his stay , when the good man   his Excellency we call he now   come home to his dinner a comfortless meal enough it be in the present condition of the family he look round the table at the place where I have use to sit in happy old day , and sigh out : " I wish , Molly , George be here . " 
 " do you , Martin ? " say Aunt Lambert , fling into his arm . 
 " yes , I do ; but I do not   wish you to choke I , Molly , " he say . " I love he dearly . I may go away and never see he again , and take his foolish little sweetheart along with I . I suppose you will write to each other , child ? I can not   prevent that , you know ; and until he change his mind , I suppose Miss Theo will not   obey papa 's order , and get he out of her foolish little head . Wilt thou , Theo ? " 
 " no , dearest , dearest , good papa ! " 
 " what ! more embrace and kiss ! what do all this mean ? " 
 " it mean that that George be in the drawing - room , " say mamma . 
 " be he ! my dear boy ! " cry the General . " come to I come in ! " and when I enter he hold I to his heart , and kiss I . 
 I confess at this I be so overcome that I fall down on my knee before the dear , good man , and sob on his own . 
 " God bless you , my dear boy ! " he mutter hurriedly . " always love you as a son have not   I , molly ? break my heart nearly when I quarrel with you about this little what ! odd marrowbone ! all down on your knee ! Mrs. Lambert , pray what be the meaning of all this ? " 
 " Dearest , dearest papa ! I will go with you all the same ! " whimper one of the kneeling party . " and I will wait oh ! as long as ever my dear father want I ! " 
 " in Heaven 's name ! " roar the General , " tell I what have happen ? " 
 what have happen be , that George Esmond Warrington and Theodosia Lambert have be marry in Southwark that morning , their bann have be duly call in the church of a certain friend of the Reverend Mr. Sampson . 




 CHAPTER LXXIX . contain both Comedy and Tragedy 

 we , who have be active in the guilty scene of the morning , feel trebly guilty when we see the effect which our conduct have produce upon he , who , of all other , we love and respect . the shock to the good man be strange , and pitiful to we to witness who have administer it . the child of his heart have deceive and disobey he I declare I think , my dear , now , we would not or could not do it over again ; his whole family have enter into a league against he . dear , kind friend and father ! we know thou hast pardon our wrong in the Heaven where thou dwellest amongst purify spirit who learn on earth how to love and pardon ! to love and forgive be easy duty with that man . Beneficence be natural to he , and a sweet , smile humility ; and to wound either be to be savage and brutal , as to torture a child , or strike blow at a nursing woman . the deed do , all we guilty one grovel in the earth , before the man we have injure . I pass over the scene of forgiveness , of reconciliation , of common worship together , of final separation when the good man depart to his government , and the ship sail away before we , leave I and Theo on the shore . we stand there hand in hand , horribly abash , silent , and guilty . my wife do not come to I till her father go : in the interval between the ceremony of our marriage and his departure , she have remain at home , occupy her old place by her father , and bed by her sister 's side : he as kind as ever , but the woman almost speechless among themselves ; Aunt Lambert , for once , unkind and fretful in her temper ; and little Hetty feverish and strange , and say , " I wish we be go . I wish we be go . " Though admit to the house , and forgive , I slunk away during those last day , and only see my wife for a minute or two in the street , or with her family . she be not mine till they be go . we go to Winchester and Hampton for what may be call our wedding . it be but a dismal business . for a while we feel utterly lonely : and of our dear father as if we have bury he , or drive he to the grave by our undutifulness . 
 I make Sampson announce our marriage in the paper .   my wife use to hang down her head before the poor fellow afterwards .   I take Mrs. Warrington back to my old lodging in Bloomsbury , where there be plenty of room for we , and our modest married life begin . I write home a letter to my mother in Virginia , inform she of no particular , but only that Mr. Lambert be about to depart for his government , I consider myself bind in honour to fulfil my promise towards his dear daughter ; and state that I intend to carry out my intention of complete my study for the Bar , and qualify myself for employment at home , or in our own or any other colony . my good Mrs. Mountain answer this letter , by desire of Madam Esmond , she say , who think that for the sake of peace my communication have well be conduct that way . I find my relative in a fury which be perfectly amusing to witness . the butler 's face , as he say , " not at home , " at my uncle 's house in Hill Street , be a blank tragedy that might have be study by Garrick when he see Banque . my poor little wife be on my arm , and we be trip away , laugh at the fellow 's accueil , when we come upon my lady in a street stoppage in her chair . I take off my hat and make she the low possible bow . I affectionately ask after my dear cousin . " I I wonder you dare look I in the face ! " Lady Warrington gasp out . " Nay , do not   deprive I of that precious privilege ! " say I. " move on , Peter , " she scream to her chairman . " your ladyship would not impale your own husband 's flesh and blood ! " say I. she rattle up the glass of her chair in a fury . I kiss my hand , take off my hat , and perform another of my very fine bow . 
 walk shortly afterwards in Hyde Park with my dear companion , I meet my little cousin exercise on horseback with a groom behind he . as soon as he see we , he gallop up to we , the groom powdering afterwards and bawl out , " stop , Master Miles , stop ! " 
 " I be not to speak to my cousin , " say Miles , " but tell you to send my love to Harry be not speak to you , be it ? be that my new cousin ? I be not tell not to speak to she . I be Miles , cousin , Sir Miles Warrington Baronet 's son , and you be very pretty ! " " now , duee now , Master Miles , " say the groom , touch his hat to we ; and the boy trot away laugh and look at we over his shoulder . " you see how my relation have determine to treat I , " I say to my partner . " as if I marry you for your relation ! " say Theo , her eye beam joy and love into mine . ah , how happy we be ! how brisk and pleasant the winter ! how snug the kettle by the fire   where the abashed Sampson sometimes come and make the punch ; how delightful the night at the theatre , for which our friend bring we ticket of admission , and where we daily expect our new play of Pocahontas would rival the success of all former tragedy . 
 the fickle old aunt of Clarges Street , who receive I , on my first come to London with my wife , with a burst of scorn , mollify presently , and as soon as she come to know Theo   who she have pronounce to be an insignificant little country - face chit , fall utterly in love with she , and would have she to tea and supper every day when there be no other company . " as for company , my dear , " she would say , " I do not   ask you . you be no long du monde . your marriage have put that entirely out of the question . " so she would have have we come to amuse she , and go in and out by the back - stair . my wife be fine lady enough to feel only amuse at this reception ; and , I must do the Baroness 's domestic the justice to say that , have we be duke and duchess , we could not have be receive with more respect . Madame de Bernstein be very much tickle and amuse with my story of Lady Warrington and the chair . I act it for she , and give her anecdote of the pious Baronet 's lady and her daughter , which please the mischievous , lively old woman . 
 the Dowager Countess of Castlewood , now establish in her house at Kensington , give we that kind of welcome which genteel lady extend to their poor relative . we go once or twice to her ladyship 's drum at Kensington ; but , lose more money at card , and spend more money in coach - hire than I like to afford , we speedily give up those entertainment , and , I dare say , be no more miss or regret than other people in the fashionable world , who be carry by death , debt , or other accident out of the polite sphere . my Theo do not in the least regret this exclusion . she have make her appearance at one of these drum , attire in some little ornament which her mother leave behind she , and by which the good lady set some store ; but I think her own white neck be a great deal pretty than these poor twinkle stone ; and there be dowager , whose wrinkle old bone blaze with ruby and diamond , which , I be sure , they would gladly have exchange for her modest parure of beauty and freshness . not a soul speak to she except , to be sure , Beau Lothair , a friend of Mr. Will 's , who prowl about Bloomsbury afterwards , and even send my wife a billet . I meet he in Covent Garden shortly after , and promise to break his ugly face if ever I see it in the neighbourhood of my lodging , and Madam Theo be molest no far . 
 the only one of our relative who come to see we   Madame de Bernstein never come ; she send her coach for we sometimes , or make inquiry regard we by her woman or her major - domo   be our poor Maria , who , with her husband , Mr. Hagan , often take a share of our homely dinner . then we have friend Spencer from the Temple , who admire our arcadian felicity , and gently ask our sympathy for his less fortunate love ; and twice or thrice the famous Doctor Johnson come in for a dish of Theo 's tea . a dish ? a pailful ! " and a pail the good thing to feed he , sar ! " say Mr. Gumbo , indignantly : for the Doctor 's appearance be not pleasant , nor his linen particularly white . he snort , he grow red , and sputter in feeding ; he fling his meat about , and bawl out in contradict people : and annoy my Theo , whom he profess to admire greatly , by say , every time he see she , " Madam , you do not love I ; I see by your manner you do not love I ; though I admire you , and come here for your sake . here be my friend Mr. Reynolds that shall paint you : he have no ceruse in his paint - box that be as brilliant as your complexion . " and so Mr. Reynolds , a most perfect and agreeable gentleman , would have paint my wife ; but I know what his price be , and do not choose to incur that expense . I wish I have now , for the sake of the child , that they might see what yonder face be like some five - and - thirty year ago . to I , madam , ' tis the same now as ever ; and your ladyship be always young ! 
 what annoy Mrs. Warrington with Dr. Johnson more than his contradiction , his sputtering , and his dirty nail , be , I think , an unfavourable opinion which he form of my new tragedy . Hagan once propose that he should read some scene from it after tea . 
 " Nay , sir , conversation be well , " say the Doctor . " I can read for myself , or hear you at the theatre . I have rather hear Mrs. Warrington 's artless prattle than your declamation of Mr. Warrington 's decasyllable . tell we about your household affair , madam , and whether his Excellency your father be well , and whether you make the pudden and the butter sauce . the butter sauce be delicious ! "   he love it so well that he have keep a large quantity in the bosom of a very dingy shirt .   " you make it as though you love I . you help I as though you love I , though you do not . " 
 " Faith , sir , you be take some of the present away with you in your waistcoat , " say Hagan , with much spirit . 
 " Sir , you be rude ! " bawl the Doctor . " you be unacquainted with the first principle of politeness , which be courtesy before lady . have receive an university education , I be surprised that you have not learn the rudiment of politeness . I respect Mrs. Warrington . I should never think of make personal remark about her guest before she ! " 
 " then , sir , " say Hagan , fiercely , " why do you speak of my theatre ? " 
 " Sir , you be saucy ! " roar the Doctor . 
 " de te fabula , " say the actor . " I think it be your waistcoat that be saucy . Madam , shall I make some punch in the way we make it in Ireland ? " 
 the Doctor , puffing , and purple in the face , be wipe the dingy shirt with a still more dubious pocket - handkerchief , which he then apply to his forehead . after this exercise , he blow a hyperborean whistle , as if to blow his wrath away . " it be de I , sir though , as a young man , perhaps you need not have tell I so . " 
 " I drop my point , sir ! if you have be wrong , I be sure I be bind to ask your pardon for set you so ! " say Mr. Hagan , with a fine bow . 
 " do not   he look like a god ? " say Maria , clutch my wife 's hand : and indeed Mr. Hagan do look like a handsome young gentleman . his colour have rise ; he have put his hand to his breast with a noble air : Chamont or Castalio could not present himself well . 
 " let I make you some lemonade , sir ; my papa have send we a box of fresh lime . may we send you some to the Temple ? " 
 " Madam , if they stay in your house , they will lose their quality and turn sweet , " say the Doctor . " Mr. Hagan , you be a young sauce - box , that be what you be ! Ho ! ho ! it be I have be wrong . " 
 " oh , my lord , my Polidore ! " bleat Lady Maria , when she be alone in my wife 's drawing - room : 
  " ' oh , I could hear thee talk for ever thus , 
  eternally admiring , fix and gaze 
  on those dear eye , for every glance they send 
  dart through my soul , and fill my heart with rapture ! ' 
 " Thou know not , my Theo , what a pearl and paragon of a man my castalio be ; my Chamont , my oh , dear I , child , what a pity it be that in your husband 's tragedy he should have to take the horrid name of Captain Smith ! " 
 upon this tragedy not only my literary hope , but much of my financial prospect be found . my brother 's debt discharge , my mother 's draft from home duly honour , my own expense pay , which , though moderate , be not inconsiderable , pretty nearly the whole of my patrimony have be spend , and this auspicious moment I must choose for my marriage ! I could raise money on my inheritance : that be not impossible , though certainly costly . my mother could not leave her eld son without a maintenance , whatever our quarrel might be . I have health , strength , good wit , some friend , and reputation above all , my famous tragedy , which the manager have promise to perform , and upon the proceed of this I count for my present support . what become of the arithmetic of youth ? how do we then calculate that a hundred pound be a maintenance , and a thousand a fortune ? how do I dare play against Fortune with such odd ? I succeed , I remember , in convince my dear General , and he leave home convince that his son - in - law have for the present necessity at least a score of hundred pound at his command . he and his dear Molly have begin life with less , and the raven have somehow always feed they . as for the woman , the question of poverty be one of pleasure to those sentimental soul , and Aunt Lambert , for her part , declare it would be wicked and irreligious to doubt of a provision be make for her child . be the righteous ever forsake ? do the just man ever have to beg his bread ? she know well than that ! " no , no , my dear ! I be not go to be afraid on that account , I warrant you ! look at I and my General ! " 
 Theo believe all I say and wish to believe myself . so we actually begin life upon a capital of five act , and about three hundred pound of ready money in hand ! 
 well , the time of the appearance of the famous tragedy draw near , and my friend canvass the town to get a body of supporter for the opening night . I be ill at ask favour from the great ; but when my Lord Wrotham come to London , I go , with Theo in my hand , to wait on his lordship , who receive we kindly , out of regard for his old friend , her father though he good - naturedly shake a finger at I   at which my little wife hang down her head , for have steal a march on the good General . however , he would do his good for her father 's daughter ; hop for a success ; say he have hear great thing of the piece ; and engage a number of place for himself and his friend . but this patron secure , I have no other . " Mon cher , at my age , " say the Baroness , " I should bore myself to death at a tragedy : but I will do my good ; and I will certainly send my people to the box . yes ! case in his good black look like a nobleman ; and Brett in one of my gown have a faux air de moi which be quite distinguish . put down my name for two in the front box . good - bye , my dear . Bonne chance ! " the Dowager Countess present compliment   on the back of the nine of club , have a card - party that night , and be quite sorry she and Fanny could not go to my tragedy . as for my uncle and Lady Warrington , they be out of the question . after the affair of the sedan - chair I might as well have ask Queen Elizabeth to go to Drury Lane . these be all my friend that host of aristocratic connexion about whom poor Sampson have brag ; and on the strength of whom , the manager , as he say , have give Mr. Hagan his engagement ! " where be my Lord Bute ? have I not promise his lordship should come ? " he ask , snappishly , take snuff   how different from the brisk , and engaging , and obsequious little manager of six month ago !   " I promise Lord Bute should come ? " 
 " yes , " say Mr. Garrick , " and her Royal Highness the Princess of Wales , and his Majesty too . " 
 Poor Sampson own that he , buoy up by vain hope , have promise the appearance of these august personage . 
 the next day , at rehearsal , matter be bad still , and the manager in a fury . 
 " great heaven , sir ! " say he , " into what a pretty guet - a - pen have you lead I ! look at that letter , sir ! read that letter ! " and he hand I one : 
 " MY DEAR SIR "   say the letter   " I have see his lordship , and convey to he Mr. Warrington 's request that he would honour the tragedy of Pocahontas by his presence . his lordship be a patron of the drama , and a magnificent friend of all the liberal art ; but he desire I to say that he can not think of attend himself , much less of ask his Gracious Master to witness the performance of a play , a principal part in which be give to an actor who have make a clandestine marriage with a daughter of one of his Majesty 's nobility . your well - wisher , saunder MCDUFF . " 
 " Mr. D. Garrick , at the Theatre Royal in Drury Lane . " 
 my poor Theo have a nice dinner wait for I after the rehearsal . I plead fatigue as the reason for look so pale : I do not dare to convey to she this dreadful news . 




 CHAPTER LXXX . Pocahontas 

 the english public not be so well acquainted with the history of Pocahontas as we of Virginia , who still love the memory of that simple and kindly creature , Mr. Warrington , at the suggestion of his friend , make a little ballad about this indian princess , which be print in the magazine a few day before the appearance of the tragedy . this proceed Sampson and I consider to be very artful and ingenious . " it be like ground - bait , sir , " say the enthusiastic parson , " and you will see the fish rise in multitude , on the great day ! " he and Spencer declare that the poem be discuss and admire at several coffee - house in their hearing , and that it have be attribute to Mr. Mason , Mr. Cowper of the Temple , and even to the famous Mr. Gray . I believe poor Sam have himself set abroad these report ; and , if Shakspeare have be name as the author of the tragedy , would have declare Pocahontas to be one of the poet 's good performance . I make acquaintance with brave Captain Smith , as a boy in my grandfather 's library at home , where I remember how I would sit at the good old man 's knee , with my favourite volume on my own , spell out the exploit of our virginian hero . I love to read of Smith 's travel , suffering , captivity , escape , not only in America but Europe . I become a child again almost as I take from the shelf before I in England the familiar volume , and all sort of recollection of my early home come crowd over my mind . the old grandfather would make picture for I of Smith do battle with the Turks on the Danube , or lead out by our indian savage to death . ah , what a terrific fight be that in which he be engage with the three turkish champion , and how I use to delight over the story of his combat with Bonny Molgro , the last and most dreadful of the three ! what a name Bonny Molgro be , and with what a prodigious turban , scimitar , and whisker we represent he ! have slay and take off the head of his first two enemy , Smith and Bonny Molgro meet , fall to   say my favourite old book   " with their battle - axis , whose pierce bill make sometimes the one , sometimes the other , to have scarce sense to keep their saddle : especially the Christian receive such a wound that he lose his battle - axe , whereat the suppose conquer Turke have a great shout from the rampire . yet , by the readinesse of his horse , and his great judgment and dexteritie , he not only avoid the Turke 's blow , but , have draw his falchion , so pierce the Turke under the cutlet , through back and body , that though hee alight from his horse , he stand not long ere hee lose his head as the rest have do . in reward for which deed , Duke Segismundus give he 3 Turke 's head in a shield for arme and 300 duckat yeerely for a pension . " disdain time and place   with that daring which be the privilege of poet   in my tragedy , Smith be make to perform similar exploit on the bank of our Potomac and James 's river . our " ground - bait " verse , run thus : 
  " POCAHONTAS 

  " wearied arm and broken sword 
  wage in vain the desperate fight 
  Round he press the countless horde , 
  he be but a single knight . 
  Hark ! a cry of triumph shrill 
  through the wilderness resound , 
  as , with twenty bleed wound , 
  sink the warrior , fight still . 

  " now they heap the fatal pyre , 
  and the torch of death they light 
  ah ! ' tis hard to die of fire ! 
  who will shield the captive knight ? 
  round the stake with fiendish cry 
  Wheel and dance the savage crowd , 
  cold the victim 's mien and proud , 
  and his breast be bared to die . 

  " who will shield the fearless heart ? 
  who avert the murderous blade ? 
  from the throng , with sudden start , 
  see , there spring an Indian maid . 
  Quick she stand before the knight , 
  ' loose the chain , unbind the ring , 
  I be daughter of the king , 
  and I claim the indian right ! ' 

  " dauntlessly aside she fling 
  lift axe and thirsty knife ; 
  fondly to his heart she cling , 
  and her bosom guard his life ! 
  in the wood of Powhattan , 
  still ' tis tell , by indian fire , 
  how a daughter of their sire 
  save the captive Englishman . " 
 I need not describe at length the plot of my tragedy , as my child can take it down from the shelf any day and peruse it for themselves . nor shall I , let I add , be in a hurry to offer to read it again to my young folk , since Captain Miles and the parson both choose to fall asleep last Christmas , when , at mamma 's request , I read aloud a couple of act . but any person have a moderate acquaintance with play and novel can soon , out of the above sketch , fill out a picture to his liking . an indian king ; a love princess , and her attendant , in love with the british captain 's servant ; a traitor in the English fort ; a brave indian warrior , himself entertain an unhappy passion for Pocahontas ; a medicine - man and priest of the Indians   very well play by Palmer , capable of every treason , stratagem , and crime , and bend upon the torture and death of the English prisoner ; these , with the accident of the wilderness , the war - dance and cry   which Gumbo have learn to mimic very accurately from the red people at home , and the arrival of the english fleet , with allusion to the late glorious victory in Canada , and the determination of Britons ever to rule and conquer in America , some of we not unnaturally think might contribute to the success of our tragedy . 
 but I have mention the ill omen which precede the day : the difficulty which a peevish , and jealous , and timid management throw in the way of the piece , and the violent prejudice which be feel against it in certain high quarter . what wonder then , I ask , that Pocahontas should have turn out not to be a victory ? I laugh to scorn the malignity of the critic who find fault with the performance . pretty critic , forsooth , who say that Carpezan be a masterpiece , whilst a far superior and more elaborate work receive only their sneer ! I insist on it that Hagan act his part so admirably that a certain actor and manager of the theatre might well be jealous of he ; and that , but for the cabal make outside , the piece would have succeed . the order have be give that the play should not succeed ; so at least Sampson declare to I . " the house swarm with Macs , by George , and they should have the gallery wash with brimstone , " the honest fellow swore , and always vow that Mr. Garrick himself would not have have the piece succeed for the world ; and be never in such a rage as during that grand scene in the second act , where Smith   poor Hagan   be bind to the stake , Pocahontas come and save he , and when the whole house be thrill with applause and sympathy . 
 anybody who have curiosity sufficient , may refer to the publish tragedy   in the octavo form , or in the subsequent splendid quarto edition of my Collected Works , and Poems Original and Translated , and say whether the scene be without merit , whether the verse be not elegant , the language rich and noble ? one of the cause of the failure be my actual fidelity to history . I have copy myself at the Museum , and tint neatly , a figure of Sir Walter Raleigh in a frill and beard ; and   my dear Theo give some of her mother 's good lace for the ruff   we dress Hagan accurately after this drawing , and no man could look well . Miss Pritchard as Pocahontas , I dress too as a Red Indian , have see enough of that costume in my own experience at home . will it be believe the house titter when she first appear ? they get use to she , however , but just at the moment when she rush into the prisoner 's arm , and a number of people be actually in tear , a fellow in the pit bawl out , " Bedad ! here be the Belle Savage kiss the saracen 's head ; " on which an impertinent roar of laughter spring up in the pit , break out with fitful explosion during the remainder of the performance . as the wag in Mr. Sheridan 's amusing Critic admirably say about the morning gun , the playwright be not content with one of they , but must fire two or three ; so with this wretched pothouse joke of the Belle Savage   the ignorant people not know that Pocahontas herself be the very Belle Sauvage from whom the tavern take its name ! . my friend of the pit repeat it ad nauseam during the performance , and as each new character appear , salute he by the name of some tavern for instance , the english governor   with a long beard   he call the Goat and Boots ; his lieutenant   Barker , whose face certainly be broad , the Bull and Mouth , and so on ! and the curtain descend amidst a shrill storm of whistle and hiss , which especially assail poor Hagan every time he open his lip . Sampson see Master Will in the green box , with some pretty acquaintance of his , and have no doubt that the treacherous scoundrel be one of the ringleader in the conspiracy . " I would have fling he over into the pit , " the faithful fellow say   and Sampson be man enough to execute his threat , " but I see a couple of Mr. Nadab 's follower prowl about the lobby , and be oblige to sheer off . " and so the egg we have count on sell at market be break , and our poor hope lie shatter before we ! 
 I look in at the house from the stage before the curtain be lift , and see it pretty well fill , especially remark Mr. Johnson in the front box , in a laced waistcoat , have his friend Mr. Reynolds by his side ; the latter could not hear , and the former could not see , and so they come good - naturedly a deux to form an opinion of my poor tragedy . I could see Lady Maria   I know the hood she wear   in the low gallery , where she once more have the opportunity of sit and look at her beloved actor perform a principal character in a piece . as for Theo , she fairly own that , unless I order she , she have rather not be present , nor have I any such command to give , for , if thing go wrong , I know that to see her suffer would be intolerable pain to myself , and so acquiesced in her desire to keep away . 
 be of a pretty equanimous disposition , and , as I flatter myself , able to bear good or evil fortune without disturbance , I myself , after take a light dinner at the Bedford , go to the theatre a short while before the commencement of the play , and propose to remain there , until the defeat or victory be decide . I own now , I could not help see which way the fate of the day be likely to turn . there be something gloomy and disastrous in the general aspect of all thing around . Miss Pritchard have the headache : the barber who bring home Hagan 's wig have powder it like a wretch : amongst the gentleman and lady in the greenroom , I see none but doubtful face : and the manager   a very flippant , not to say impertinent gentleman , in my opinion , and who himself on that night look as dismal as a mute at a funeral   have the insolence to say to I , " for Heaven 's sake , Mr. Warrington , go and get a glass of punch at the Bedford , and do not   frighten we all here by your dismal countenance ! " 
 " Sir , " say I , " I have a right , for five shilling , to comment upon your face , but I never give you any authority to make remark upon mine . " " Sir , " say he in a pet , " I most heartily wish I have never see your face at all ! " " your , sir ! " say I , " have often amuse I greatly ; and when paint for Abel Drugger be exceedingly comic " and indeed I have always do Mr. G. the justice to think that in low comedy he be unrivalle . I make he a bow , and walk off to the coffee - house , and for five year after never speak a word to the gentleman , when he apologise to I , at a nobleman 's house where we chance to meet . I say I have utterly forget the circumstance to which he allude , and that , on the first night of a play , no doubt author and manager be flurry alike . and add , " after all , there be no shame in not be make for the theatre . Mr. Garrick you be . " a compliment with which he appear to be as well pleased as I intend he should . 
 Fidus Achates run over to I at the end of the first act to say that all thing be go pretty well ; though he confess to the titter in the house upon Miss Pritchard 's first appearance , dress exactly like an indian princess . 
 " I can not help it , Sampson , " say I   fill he a bumper of good punch , " if Indians be dress so . " 
 " why , " say he , " would you have have Caractacus paint blue like an ancient Briton , or Bonduca with nothing but a cow - skin ? " and indeed it may be that the fidelity to history be the cause of the ridicule cast on my tragedy , in which case I , for one , be not ashamed of its defeat . 
 after the second act , my aide - de - camp come from the field with dismal news indeed . I do not   know how it be that , nervous before action , in disaster I become pretty cool and cheerful .   the writer seem to contradict himself here , have just boast of possess a pretty equanimous disposition . he be probably mistake in his own estimate of himself , as other folk have be besides.-ED .   " be thing go ill ? " say I. I call for my reckoning , put on my hat , and march to the theatre as calmly as if I be go to dine at the Temple ; fidus achate walk by my side , press my elbow , kick the link - boy out of the way , and cry , " by George , Mr. Warrington , you be a man of spirit a Trojan , sir ! " so , there be man of spirit in Troy ; but alas ! fate be too strong for they . 
 at any rate , no man can say that I do not bear my misfortune with calmness : I could no more help the clamour and noise of the audience than a captain can help the howling and hiss of the storm in which his ship go down . but I be determined that the rush wave and broken mast should impavidum ferient , and flatter myself that I bear my calamity without flinch . " not Regulus , my dear madam , could step into his barrel more coolly , " Sampson say to my wife . ' tis unjust to say of man of the parasitic nature that they be unfaithful in misfortune . whether I be prosperous or poor , the wild parson be equally true and friendly , and share our crust as eagerly as ever he have partake of our well fortune . 
 I take my place on the stage , whence I could see the actor of my poor piece , and a portion of the audience who condemn I . I suppose the performer give I a wide berth out of pity for I . I must say that I think I be as little move as any spectator ; and that no one would have judge from my mien that I be the unlucky hero of the night . 
 but my dear Theo , when I go home , look so pale and white , that I see from the dear creature 's countenance that the knowledge of my disaster have precede my return . Spencer , Sampson , cousin Hagan , and Lady Maria be to come after the play , and congratulate the author , God wot !   Poor Miss Pritchard be engage to we likewise , but send word that I must understand that she be a great deal too unwell to sup that night .   my friend the gardener of Bedford House have give my wife his good flower to decorate her little table . there they be ; the poor little paint standard and the battle lose ! I have bear the defeat well enough , but as I look at the sweet pale face of the wife across the table , and those artless trophy of welcome which she have set up for her hero , I confess my courage give way , and my heart feel a pang almost as keen as any that ever have smite it . 
 our meal , it may be imagine , be dismal enough , nor be it render much gayer by the talk we strove to carry on . Old Mrs. Hagan be , luckily , very ill at this time ; and her disease , and the incident connect with it , a great blessing to we . then we have his Majesty 's approach marriage , about which there be a talk .   how well I remember the most futile incident of the day down to a tune which a carpenter be whistle by my side at the playhouse , just before the dreary curtain fall !   then we talk about the death of good Mr. Richardson , the author of Pamela and Clarissa , whose work we all admire exceedingly . and as we talk about Clarissa , my wife take on herself to wipe her eye once or twice , and say , faintly , " you know , my love , mamma and I could never help cry over that dear book . oh , my dearest , dear mother "   she add , " how I wish she could be with I now ! " this be an occasion for more open tear , for of course a young lady may naturally weep for her absent mother . and then we mix a gloomy bowl with Jamaica lime , and drink to the health of his Excellency the Governor : and then , for a second toast , I fill a bumper , and , with a smile face , drink to " our well fortune ! " 
 this be too much . the two woman fling themselves into each other 's arm , and irrigate each other 's neck - handkerchief with tear . " oh , Maria ! be not be not my George good and kind ? " sob Theo . " look at my Hagan how great , how godlike he be in his part ! " gasp Maria . " it be a beastly cabal which throw he over and I could plunge this knife into Mr. Garrick 's black heart the odious little wretch ! " and she grasp a weapon at her side . but throw it presently down , the enthusiastic creature rush up to her lord and master , fling her arm round he , and embrace he in the presence of the little company . 
 I be not sure whether some one else do not do likewise . we be all in a state of extreme excitement and enthusiasm . in the midst of grief , Love the consoler appear amongst we , and soothe we with such fond blandishment and tender caress , that one scarce wish the calamity away . two or three day afterwards , on our birthday , a letter be bring I in my study , which contain the follow line : 
  " from POCAHONTAS 

  " return from the cruel fight 
  how pale and faint appear my knight ! 
  he see I anxious at his side ; 
  ' why seek , my love , your wound to hide ? 
  or deem your english girl afraid 
  to emulate the Indian maid ? ' 

  " be mine my husband 's grief to cheer , 
  in peril to be ever near ; 
  Whate'er of ill or woe betide , 
  to bear it cling at his side ; 
  the poisoned stroke of fate to ward , 
  his bosom with my own to guard ; 
  ah ! could it spare a pang to his , 
  it could not know a purer bliss ! 
  ' twould gladden as it feel the smart , 
  and thank the hand that fling the dart ! " 
 I do not say the verse be very good , but that I like they as well as if they be and that the face of the writer   whose sweet young voice I fancy I can hear as I hum the line , when I go into her drawing - room after get the letter , and when I see her blushing and blessing I seem to I more beautiful than any I can fancy out of Heaven . 




 CHAPTER LXXXI . Res Angusta Domi 

 I have already describe my present feeling as an elderly gentleman , regard that rash jump into matrimony , which I persuade my dear partner to take with I when we be both scarce out of our teen . as a man and a father with a due sense of the necessity of mutton chop , and the importance of pay the baker with a pack of rash child round about we who might be run off to Scotland to - morrow , and plead papa 's and mamma 's example for their impertinence , I know that I ought to be very cautious in narrate this early part of the married life of George Warrington , Esquire , and Theodosia his wife to call out mea culpa , and put on a demure air , and , sit in my comfortable easy - chair here , profess to be in a white sheet and on the stool of repentance , offer myself up as a warning to imprudent and hot - head youth . 
 but , truth to say , that married life , regard which my dear relative prophesy so gloomily , have disappoint all those prudent and respectable people . it have have its trial ; but I can remember they without bitterness its passionate grief , of which time , by God 's kind ordinance , have be the benign consoler its day of poverty , which we bore , who endure it , to the wonder of our sympathise relative look on its precious reward and blessing , so great that I scarce dare to whisper they to this page ; to speak of they , save with awful respect and to one ear , to which be offer up the prayer and thank of all man . to marry without a competence be wrong and dangerous , no doubt , and a crime against our social code ; but do not score of thousand of our fellow - being commit the crime every year with no other trust but in , Heaven , health , and their labour ? be young people enter into the married life not to take hope into account , nor dare to begin their housekeeping until the cottage be completely furnish , the cellar and larder stock , the cupboard full of plate , and the strong - box of money ? the increase and multiplication of the world would stop , be the law which regulate the genteel part of it to be make universal . our gentlefolk tremble at the brink in their silk stocking and pump , and wait for whole year , until they find a bridge or a gilt barge to carry they across ; our poor do not fear to wet their bare foot , plant they in the brook , and trust to fate and strength to bear they over . who would like to consign his daughter to poverty ? who would counsel his son to undergo the countless risk of poor married life , to remove the beloved girl from comfort and competence , and subject she to debt , misery , privation , friendlessness , sickness , and the hundred gloomy consequence of the res angusta domi ? I look at my own wife and ask her pardon for have impose a task so fraught with pain and danger upon one so gentle . I think of the trial she endure , and be thankful for they and for that unfailing love and constancy with which God bless she and strengthen she to bear they all . on this question of marriage , I be not a fair judge : my own be so imprudent and have be so happy , that I must not dare to give young people counsel . I have endure poverty , but scarcely ever find it otherwise than tolerable : have I not undergone it , I never could have know the kindness of friend , the delight of gratitude , the surprising joy and consolation which sometimes accompany the scanty meal and narrow fire , and cheer the long day 's labour . this at least be certain , in respect of the lot of the decent poor , that a great deal of superfluous pity be often throw away upon it . good - natured fine folk , who sometimes step out of the sunshine of their rich into a narrow obscurity , be blind as it be , whilst we could see quite cheerfully and clearly : they stumble over obstacle which be none to we : they be surprised at the resignation with which we drink small beer , and that we could heartily say grace over such very cold mutton . 
 the good General , my father - in - law , have marry his Molly , when he be a subaltern of a foot regiment , and have a purse scarce well fill than my own . they have have their up and down of fortune . I think   though my wife will never confess to this point   they have marry , as people could do in their young time , without previously ask papa 's and mamma 's leave .   the Editor have look through Burn 's Registers of Fleet Marriages without find the name of Martin Lambert and Mary Benson .   at all event , they be so well pleased with their own good luck in matrimony , that they do not grudge their child 's , and be by no mean frighten at the idea of any little hardship which we in the course of our married life might be call upon to undergo . and I suppose when I make my own pecuniary statement to Mr. Lambert , I be anxious to deceive both of we . believe I to be master of a couple of thousand pound , he go to Jamaica quite easy in his mind as to his darling daughter 's comfort and maintenance , at least for some year to come . after pay the expense of his family 's outfit , the worthy man go away not much rich than his son - in - law ; and a few trinket , and some lace of Aunt Lambert 's , with twenty new guineas in a purse which her mother and sister make for she , be my Theo 's marriage portion . but in value my stock , I choose to count as a good debt a sum which my honoured mother never could be get to acknowledge up to the day when the resolute old lady be call to pay the last debt of all . the sum I have disburse for she , she argue , be spend for the improvement and maintenance of the estate which be to be mine at her decease . what money she could spare be to be for my poor brother , who have nothing , who would never have spend his own mean have he not imagine himself to be sole heir of the virginian property , as he would have be the good lady take care to emphasise this point in many of her letter but for a half - hour 's accident of birth . he be now distinguish himself in the service of his king and country . to purchase his promotion be his mother 's , she should suppose his brother 's duty ! when I have finish my bar - study and my dramatic amusement , Madam Esmond inform I that I be welcome to return home and take that place in our colony to which my birth entitle I . this statement she communicate to I more than once through Mountain , and before the news of my marriage have reach she . 
 there be no need to recall her expression of maternal indignation when she be inform of the step I have take . on the pacification of Canada , my dear Harry ask for leave of absence , and dutifully pay a visit to Virginia . he write , describe his reception at home , and the splendid entertainment which my mother make in honour of her son . Castlewood , which she have not inhabit since our departure for Europe , be throw open again to our friend of the colony ; and the friend of Wolfe , and the soldier of Quebec , be receive by all our acquaintance with every become honour . some dismal quarrel , to be sure , ensue , because my brother persist in maintain his friendship with Colonel Washington , of Mount Vernon , whose praise Harry never be tired of singing . indeed I allow the gentleman every virtue ; and in the struggle which terminate so fatally for England a few year since , I can admire as well as his warm friend , General Washington 's glorious constancy and success . 
 if these battle between Harry and our mother be frequent , as , in his letter , he describe they to be , I wonder , for my part , why he should continue at home ? one reason naturally suggest itself to my mind , which I scarcely like to communicate to Mrs. Warrington ; for we have both talk over our dear little Hetty 's romantic attachment for my brother , and wonder that he have never discover it . I need not say , I suppose , that my gentleman have find some young lady at home more to his taste than our dear hester , and hence account for his prolonged stay in Virginia . 
 presently there come , in a letter from he , not a full confession but an admission of this interesting fact . a person be describe , not name a be all beauty and perfection , like other young lady under similar circumstance . my wife ask to see the letter : I could not help show it , and hand it to she , with a very sad face . to my surprise she read it , without exhibit any corresponding sorrow of her own . 
 " I have think of this before , my love , " I say . " I feel with you for your disappointment regard poor Hetty . " 
 " ah ! poor Hetty , " say Theo , look down at the carpet . 
 " it would never have do , " say I. 
 " no they would not have be happy , " sigh Theo . 
 " how strange he never should have find out her secret ! " I continue . 
 she look I full in the face with an odd expression . " pray , what do that look mean ? " I ask . 
 " nothing , my dear nothing ! only I be not surprised ! " say Theo , blush . 
 " what , " I ask , " can there be another ? " 
 " I be sure I never say so , George , " say the lady , hurriedly . " but if Hetty have overcome her childish folly , ought we not all to be glad ? do you gentleman suppose that you only be to fall in love and grow tired , indeed ? " 
 " what ! " I say , with a strange commotion of my mind . " do you mean to tell I , Theo , that you ever care for any one but I ? " 
 " oh , George , " she whimper , " when I be at school , there be there be one of the boy of Doctor Backhouse 's school , who sate in the loft next to we ; and I think he have lovely eye , and I be so shocked when I recognise he behind the counter at Mr. Grigg 's the mercer 's , when I go to buy a cloak for baby , and I want to tell you , my dear , and I do not   know how ! " 
 I go to see this creature with the lovely eye , have make my wife describe the fellow 's dress to I , and I see a little bandy - legged wretch in a blue camlet coat , with his red hair tie with a dirty ribbon , about whom I forbear generously even to reproach my wife ; nor will she ever know that I have look at the fellow , until she read the confession in this page . if our wife see we as we be , I think , would they love we as they do ? be we as much mistaken in they , as they in we ? I look into one candid face at least , and think it never have deceive I . 
 lest I should encourage my young people to an imitation of my own imprudence , I will not tell they with how small a capital Mrs. Theo and I commence life . the unfortunate tragedy bring we nothing ; though the reviewer , since its publication of late , have speak not unfavourably as to its merit , and Mr. Kemble himself have do I the honour to commend it . our kind friend Lord Wrotham be for have the piece publish by subscription , and send I a bank - note , with a request that I would let he have a hundred copy for his friend ; but I be always averse to that method of levy money , and , prefer my poverty sine dote , lock up my manuscript , with my poor girl 's verse insert at the first page . I know not why the piece should have give such offence at court , except for the fact that an actor who have run off with an earl 's daughter , perform a principal part in the play ; but I be tell that sentiment which I have put into the mouth of some of the indian character   who be make to declaim against ambition , the british desire of rule , and so forth , be pronounce dangerous and unconstitutional ; so that the little hope of royal favour , which I might have have , be quite take away from I . 
 what be to be do ? a few month after the failure of the tragedy , as I count up the remain of my fortune   the calculation be not long or difficult , I come to the conclusion that I must beat a retreat out of my pretty apartment in Bloomsbury , and so give warning to our good landlady , inform she that my wife 's health require that we should have lodging in the country . but we go no far than Lambeth , our faithful Gumbo and molly follow we ; and here , though as poor as might be , we be wait on by a maid and a lackey in livery , like any folk of condition . you may be sure kind relative cry out against our extravagance ; indeed , be they not the people who find our fault out for we , and proclaim they to the rest of the world ? 
 return home from London one day , whither I have be on a visit to some bookseller , I recognise the family arm and livery on a grand gilt chariot which stand before a public - house near to our lodging . a few loitering inhabitant be gather round the splendid vehicle , and look with awe at the footman , resplendent in the sun , and quaff blaze pot of beer . I find my Lady Castlewood seat opposite to my wife in our little apartment   whence we have a very bright , pleasant prospect of the river , cover with barge and wherry , and the ancient tower and tree of the Archbishop 's palace and garden , and Mrs. Theo , who have a very droll way of describe person and scene , narrate to I all the particular of her ladyship 's conversation , when she take she leave . 
 " I have be here this ever - so - long , " say the Countess , " gossip with cousin Theo , while you have be away at the coffee - house , I dare say , make merry with your friend , and drink your punch and coffee . guess she must find it rather lonely here , with nothing to do but work they little cap and hem they frock . never mind , dear ; reckon you will soon have a companion who will amuse you when cousin George be away at his coffee - house ! what a nice lodging you have get here , I do declare ! our new house which we have take be twenty time as big , and cover with gold from top to bottom ; but I like this quite as well . bless you be rich be no well than be poor . when we live to Albany , and I do most all the work myself , scour the room , bile the kettle , help the wash , and all , I be just as happy as I be now . we only have one old negro to keep the store . why do not   you sell Gumbo , cousin George ? he be not   no use here idle and dawdle about , and make love to the servant - girl . Fogh ! guess they be not   particular , these english people ! " so she talk , rattle on with perfect good - humour , until her hour for departure come ; when she produce a fine repeat watch , and say it be time for she to pay a call upon her Majesty at Buckingham House . " and mind you come to we , George , " say her ladyship , wave a little parting hand out of the gilt coach . " Theo and I have settle all about it . " 
 " here , at least , " say I , when the laced footman have clamber up behind the carriage , and our magnificent little patroness have leave we ; " here be one who be not afraid of our poverty , nor ashamed to remember she own . " 
 " Ashamed ! " say Theo , resume her lilliputian needlework . " to do her justice , she would make herself at home in any kitchen or palace in the world . she have give I and molly twenty lesson in housekeeping . she say , when she be at home to Albany , she roast , bake , sweep the house , and milk the cow . "   Madam Theo pronounce the word cow archly in our american way , and imitate her ladyship 's accent very divertingly . 
 " and she have no pride , " I add . " it be good - natured of she to ask we to dine with she and my lord . when will Uncle Warrington ever think of offer we a crust again , or a glass of his famous beer ? " 
 " yes , it be not ill - natured to invite we , " say Theo , slily . " but , my dear , you do not   know all the condition ! " and then my wife , still imitate the Countess 's manner , laughingly inform I what these condition be . " she take out her pocket - book , and tell I , " say Theo , " what day she be engage abroad and at home . on Monday she receive a Duke and a Duchess , with several other member of my lord 's house , and their lady . on Tuesday come more earl , two bishop , and an ambassador . ' of course you will not   come on they day ? ' say the Countess . ' now you be so poor , you know , that fine company be not   no good for you . Lord bless you ! father never dine on our company day ! he do not   like it ; he take a bit of cold meat anyways . ' on which , " say Theo , laugh , " I tell she that Mr. Warrington do not care for any but the good of company , and propose that she should ask we on some day when the Archbishop of Canterbury din with she , and his grace must give we a lift home in his coach to Lambeth . and she be an economical little person , too , " continue Theo . " ' I think of bring with I some of my baby 's cap and thing , which his lordship have outgrow them , but they may be want again , you know , my dear . ' and so we lose that addition to our wardrobe , " say Theo , smile , " and Molly and I must do our good without her ladyship 's charity . ' when people be poor , they be poor , ' the Countess say , with her usual outspokenness , ' and must get on the good they can . what we shall do for that poor Maria , goodness only know ! we can not   ask she to see we as we can you , though you be so poor : but an earl 's daughter to marry a play - actor ! La , my dear , it be dreadful : his Majesty and the Princess have both speak of it ! every other noble family in this kingdom as have ever hear of it pity we ; though I have a plan for help those poor unhappy people , and have send down simon , my groom of the chamber , to tell they on it . ' this plan be , that Hagan , who have keep almost all his term at Dublin College , should return thither and take his degree , and enter into holy order , ' when we will provide he with a chaplaincy at home , you know , ' Lady Castlewood add . " and I may mention here , that this benevolent plan be execute a score of month later ; when I be enable myself to be of service to Mr. Hagan , who be one of the kind and good of our friend during our own time of want and distress . Castlewood then execute his promise loyally enough , get order and a colonial appointment for Hagan , who distinguish himself both as soldier and preacher , as we shall presently hear ; but not a guinea do his lordship spare to aid either his sister or his kinsman in their trouble . I never ask he , thank Heaven , to assist I in my own ; though , to do he justice , no man could express himself more amiably , and with a joy which I believe be quite genuine , when my day of poverty be end . 
 as for my Uncle Warrington , and his virtuous wife and daughter , let I do they justice likewise , and declare that throughout my period of trial , their sorrow at my poverty be consistent and unvarying . I still have a few acquaintance who see they , and of course   as friend will   bring I a report of their opinion and conversation ; and I never could hear that my relative have utter one single good word about I or my wife . they speak even of my tragedy as a crime I be accustomed to hear that sufficiently malign of the author as a miserable reprobate , for ever reel about Grub Street , in rag and squalor . they hold I out no hand of help . my poor wife might cry in her pain , but they have no twopence to bestow upon she . they go to church a half - dozen time in the week . they subscribe to many public charity . their tribe be know eighteen hundred year ago , and will flourish as long as man endure . they will still thank Heaven that they be not as other folk be ; and leave the wounded and miserable to other succour . 
 I do not   care to recall the dreadful doubt and anxiety which begin to beset I ; the plan after plan which I try , and in which I fail , for procure work and add to our dwindle stock of money . I bethink I of my friend Mr. Johnson , and when I think of the eager kindness with which he receive I , be ashamed of some pert speech which I own to have make regard his manner and behaviour . I tell my story and difficulty to he , the circumstance of my marriage , and the prospect before I . he would not for a moment admit they be gloomy , or , si male nunc , that they would continue to be so . I have before I the chance , certainly very slender , of a place in England ; the inheritance which must be mine in the course of nature , or at any rate would fall to the heir I be expect . I have a small stock of money for present actual necessity a possibility , " though , to be free with you , sir "   say he , " after the performance of your tragedy , I doubt whether nature have endow you with those peculiar quality which be necessary for achieve a remarkable literary success " and finally a submission to the maternal rule , and a return to Virginia , where plenty and a home be always ready for I . " why , sir ! " he cry , ' such a sum as you mention would have be a fortune to I when I begin the world , and my friend Mr. Goldsmith would set up a coach - and - six on it . with youth , hope , to - day , and a couple of hundred pound in cash no young fellow need despair . think , sir , you have a year at least before you , and who know what may chance between now and then . why , sir , your relative here may provide for you , or you may succeed to your virginian property , or you may come into a fortune ! " I do not in the course of that year , but he do . my Lord Bute give Mr. Johnson a pension , which set all Grub Street in a fury against the recipient , who , to be sure , have publish his own not very flattering opinion upon pension and pensioner . 
 nevertheless , he do not altogether discourage my literary project , promise to procure I work from the bookseller , and faithfully perform that kind promise . " but , " say he , ' sir , you must not appear amongst they in forma pauperis . have you never a friend 's coach , in which we can ride to see they ? you must put on your well laced hat and waistcoat ; and we must appear , sir , as if we be do they a favour . " this stratagem answer , and procure I respect enough at the first visit or two ; but when the bookseller know that I want to be pay for my work , their back refuse to bend any more , and they treat I with a familiarity which I could ill stomach . I overhear one of they , who have be a footman , say , " oh , it be Pocahontas , be it ? let he wait . " and he tell his boy to say as much to I . " wait , sir ? " say I , fume with rage and put my head into his parlour , " I be not accustomed to waiting , but I have hear you be . " and I stride out of the shop into Pall Mall in a mighty fluster . 
 and yet Mr. D. be in the right . I come to he , if not to ask a favour , at any rate to propose a bargain , and surely it be my business to wait his time and convenience . in more fortunate day I ask the gentleman 's pardon , and the kind author of the Muse in Livery be instantly appease . 
 I be more prudent , or Mr. Johnson more fortunate , in an application elsewhere , and Mr. Johnson procure I a little work from the bookseller in translate from foreign language , of which I happen to know two or three . by a hard day 's labour I could earn a few shilling ; so few that a week 's work would hardly bring I a guinea : and that be fling to I with insolent patronage by the low huckster who employ I . I can put my finger upon two or three magazine article write at this period , and pay for with a few wretched shilling , which paper as I read they awaken in I the keen pang of bitter remembrance .   Mr. George Warrington , of the Upper Temple , say he remember a book , contain his grandfather 's book - plate , in which be paste various extract from review and newspaper in an old type , and letter outside Les Chains de l'Esclavage . these be no doubt the contribution above mention ; but the volume have not be find , either in the town - house or in the library at Warrington Manor . the Editor , by the way , be not answerable for a certain inconsistency , which may be remark in the narrative . the writer say early , that he speak without bitterness of past time , and presently fall into a fury with they . the same manner of forgive our enemy be not uncommon in the present century .   I recall the doubt and fear which agitate I , see the dear wife nurse her infant and look up into my face with hypocritical smile that vainly try to mask her alarm : the struggle of pride be fight over again : the wound under which I smart re - open . there be some act of injustice commit against I which I do not   know how to forgive ; and which , whenever I think of they , awaken in I the same feeling of revolt and indignation . the gloom and darkness gather over I till they be relieve by a reminiscence of that love and tenderness which through all gloom and darkness have be my light and consolation . 




 CHAPTER LXXXII . Miles 's Moidore 

 Little Miles make his appearance in this world within a few day of the gracious Prince who command his regiment . illumination and cannonading salute the Royal George 's birth , multitude be admit to see he as he lie behind a gilt rail at the Palace with noble nurse watch over he . few nurse guard the cradle of our little Prince ; no courtier , no faithful retainer salute it , except our trusty Gumbo and kind Molly , who to be sure love and admire the little heir of my poverty as loyally as our heart could desire . why be our boy not name George like the other paragon just mention , and like his father ? I give he the name of a little scapegrace of my family , a name which many generation of Warringtons have bear likewise ; but my poor little Miles 's love and kindness touch I at a time when kindness and love be rare from those of my own blood , and Theo and I agree that our child should be call after that single little friend of my paternal race . 
 we write to acquaint our royal parent with the auspicious event , and bravely insert the child 's birth in the Daily Advertiser , and the place , Church Street , Lambeth , where he be bear . " my dear , " say Aunt Bernstein , write to I in reply to my announcement , " how could you point out to all the world that you live in such a trou as that in which you have bury yourself ? I kiss the little mamma , and send a remembrance for the child . " this remembrance be a fine silk coverlid , with a lace edge fit for a prince . it be not very useful : the price of the lace would have serve we much well , but Theo and Molly be delighted with the present , and my eld son 's cradle have a cover as fine as any nobleman 's . 
 Good Dr. Heberden come over several time to visit my wife , and see that all thing go well . he know and recommend to we a surgeon in the vicinage , who take charge of she ; luckily , my dear patient need little care , beyond that which our landlady and her own trusty attendant could readily afford she . again our humble precinct be adorn with the gild apparition of Lady Castlewood 's chariot wheel ; she bring a pot of jelly , which she think Theo might like , and which , no doubt , have be serve at one of her ladyship 's banquet on a previous day . and she tell we of all the ceremony at court , and of the splendour and festivity attend the birth of the august heir to the crown ; our good Mr. Johnson happen to pay I a visit on one of those day when my lady countess 's carriage flame up to our little gate . he be not a little strike by her magnificence , and make she some bow , which be more respectful than graceful . she call I cousin very affably , and help to transfer the present of jelly from her silver dish into our crockery pan with much benignity . the Doctor taste the sweetmeat , and pronounce it to be excellent . " the great , sir , " say he , " be fortunate in every way . they can engage the most skilful practitioner of the culinary art , as they can assemble the most amiable wit round their table . if , as you think , sir , and , from the appearance of the dish , your suggestion at least be plausible , this sweetmeat may have appear already at his lordship 's table , it have be there in good company . it have quiver under the eye of celebrated beauty , it have be taste by ruby lip , it have divide the attention of the distinguished company , with fruit , tart , and cream , which I make no doubt be like itself delicious . " and so say , the good Doctor absorb a considerable portion of Lady Castlewood 's benefaction ; though as regard the epithet delicious I be bind to say , that my poor wife , after taste the jelly , put it away from she as not to she liking ; and Molly , fling up her head , declare it be mouldy . 
 my boy enjoy at least the privilege of have an earl 's daughter for his godmother ; for this office be perform by his cousin , our poor Lady Maria , whose kindness and attention to the mother and the infant be beyond all praise ; and who , have lose her own solitary chance for maternal happiness , yearn over our child in a manner not a little touching to behold . Captain Miles be a mighty fine gentleman , and his uniform of the Prince 's Hussars as splendid as any that ever bedizen a soldier of fashion ; but he hath too good a heart , and be too true a gentleman , let we trust , not to be thankful when he remember that his own infant limb be dress in some of the little garment which have be prepare for the poor player 's child . Sampson christen he in that very chapel in Southwark , where our marriage ceremony have be perform . never be the word of the Prayer - book more beautifully and impressively read than by the celebrant of the service ; except at its end , when his voice fail he , and he and the rest of the little congregation be fain to wipe their eye . " Mr. Garrick himself , sir , " say Hagan , " could not have read those word so nobly . I be sure little innocent never enter the world accompany by wish and benediction more tender and sincere . " 
 and now I have not tell how it chance that the Captain come by his name of Miles . a couple of day before his christening , when as yet I believe it be intend that our firstborn should bear his father 's name , a little patter of horse 's hoof come gallop up to our gate ; and who should pull at the bell but young Miles , our cousin ? I fear he have disobey his parent when he gallop away on that undutiful journey . 
 " you know , " say he , " cousin Harry give I my little horse ; and I can not   help like you , because you be so like Harry , and because they be always say thing of you at home , and it be a shame ; and I have bring my whistle and coral that my godmamma Lady Suckling give I , for your little boy ; and if you be so poor , cousin George , here be my gold moidore , and it be worth ever so much , and it be no use to I , because I may not   spend it , you know . " 
 we take the boy up to Theo in her room   he mount the stair in his little tramping boot , of which he be very proud ; and Theo kiss he , and thank he ; and his moidore have be in her purse from that day . 
 my mother , write through her ambassador as usual , inform I of her royal surprise and displeasure on learn that my son have be christen Miles a name not know , at least in the Esmond family . I do not care to tell the reason at the time ; but when , in after year , I tell Madam Esmond how my boy come by his name , I see a tear roll down her wrinkle cheek , and I hear afterwards that she have ask Gumbo many question about the boy who give his name to our Miles our Miles Gloriosus of Pall Mall , Valenciennes , Almack 's , Brighton . 




 CHAPTER LXXXIII . trouble and Consolations 

 in our early day at home , when Harry and I use to be so undutiful to our tutor , who would have think that Mr. Esmond Warrington of Virginia would turn Bearleader himself ? my mother   when we come together again   never could be get to speak directly of this period of my life ; but would allude to it as " that terrible time , my love , which I can not   bear to think of , " " those dreadful year when there be difference between we , " and so forth ; and though my pupil , a worthy and grateful man , send I out to Jamestown several barrel of that liquor by which his great fortune be make , Madam Esmond speak of he as " your friend in England , " " your wealthy Lambeth friend , " etc . , but never by his name ; nor do she ever taste a drop of his beer . we brew our own too at Warrington Manor , but our good Mr. Foker never fail to ship to Ipswich every year a couple of butt of his entire . his son be a young sprig of fashion , and have marry an earl 's daughter ; the father be a very worthy and kind gentleman , and it be to the luck of make his acquaintance that I owe the receipt of some of the most welcome guineas that ever I receive in my life . 
 it be not so much the sum , as the occupation and hope give I by the office of Governor , which I take on myself , which be then so precious to I . Mr. F. 's Brewery   the site have since be change   then stand near to Pedlar 's acre in Lambeth and the surgeon who attend my wife in her confinement , likewise take care of the wealthy brewer 's family . he be a Bavarian , originally name Voelker . Mr. Lance , the surgeon , I suppose , make he acquainted with my name and history . the worthy doctor would smoke many a pipe of Virginia in my garden , and have conceive an attachment for I and my family . he bring his patron to my house ; and when Mr. F. find that I have a smattering of his language , and could sing " Prinz Eugen the noble Ritter "   a song that my grandfather have bring home from the Marlborough war , the german conceive a great friendship for I : his lady put her chair and her chariot at Mrs. Warrington 's service : his little daughter take a prodigious fancy to our baby   and to do he justice , the Captain , who be as ugly a fellow now as ever wear a queue , be beautiful as an infant    the very image of the Squire at 30 , everybody say so . M. W.   Note in the MS .   : and his son and heir , Master Foker , be much maltreat at Westminster School because of his father 's profession of brewer , the parent ask if I would take charge of he ; and pay I a not insufficient sum for superintend his education . 
 Mr. F. be a shrewd man of business , and as he and his family really interest themselves in I and mine , I lay all my pecuniary affair pretty unreservedly before he ; and my statement , he be pleased to say , augment the respect and regard which he feel for I . he laugh at our story of the aid which my noble relative have give I my aunt 's coverlid , my Lady Castlewood 's mouldy jelly , Lady Warrington 's contemptuous treatment of we . but he weep many tear over the story of little Miles 's moidore ; and as for Sampson and Hagan , " I wow , " say he , " dey shall have so much beer al ever dey can drink . " he send his wife to call upon Lady Maria , and treat she with the utmost respect and obsequiousness , whenever she come to visit he . it be with Mr. Foker that Lady Maria stay when Hagan go to Dublin to complete his college term ; and the good brewer 's purse also minister to our friend 's want and supply his outfit . 
 when Mr. Foker come fully to know my own affair and position , he be pleased to speak of I with term of enthusiasm , and as if my conduct show some extraordinary virtue . I have say how my mother save money for Harry , and how the two be in my debt . but when Harry spend money , he spend it fancy it to be his ; Madam Esmond never could be make to understand she be deal hardly with I the money be pay and go , and there be an end of it . now , at the end of ' 62 , I remember Harry send over a considerable remittance for the purchase of his promotion , beg I at the same time to remember that he be in my debt , and to draw on his agent if I have any need . he do not know how great the need be , or how my little capital have be swallow . 
 well , to take my brother 's money would delay his promotion , and I naturally do not draw on he , though I own I be tempt ; nor , know my dear General Lambert 's small mean , do I care to impoverish he by ask for supply . these simple act of forbearance my worthy brewer must choose to consider as instance of exalt virtue . and what do my gentleman do but write privately to my brother in America , laud I and my wife as the most admirable of human being , and call upon Madame de Bernstein , who never tell I of his visit indeed , but who , I perceive , about this time treat we with singular respect and gentleness , that surprise I in one whom I could not but consider as selfish and worldly . in after day I remember ask he how he have gain admission to the Baroness ? he laugh : " De Baroness ! " say he . " I know de Baron when he be a walet at Munich , and I be a brewer - apprentice . " I think our family have good not be too curious about our uncle the Baron . 
 thus , the part of my life which ought to have be most melancholy be in truth make pleasant by many friend , happy circumstance , and stroke of lucky fortune . the bear I lead be a docile little cub , and dance to my piping very readily . well to lead he about , than to hang round bookseller ' door , or wait the pleasure or caprice of manager ! my wife and I , during our exile , as we may call it , spend very many pleasant evening with these kind friend and benefactor . nor be we without intellectual enjoyment ; Mrs. Foker and Mrs. Warrington sing finely together ; and sometimes when I be in the mood , I read my own play of Pocahontas , to this friendly audience , in a manner well than Hagan 's own , Mr. Foker be pleased to say . 
 after that little escapade of Miles Warrington , junior , I see nothing of he , and hear of my paternal relative but rarely . Sir Miles be assiduous at court   as I believe he would have be at Nero 's , and I laugh one day when Mr. Foker tell I that he have hear on ' change " that they be go to make my uncle a Beer . " " a beer ? " say I in wonder . " can not   you understand de vort , ven I say it ? " say the testy old gentleman . " Vell , veil , a Lort ! " Sir , Miles indeed be the obedient humble servant of the Minister , whoever he might be . I be surprised he do not speak English with a Scotch accent during the first favourite 's brief reign . I see he and his wife come from court , when Mrs. Claypool be present to her Majesty on her marriage . I have my little boy on my shoulder . my uncle and aunt star resolutely at I from their gilt coach window . the footman look blank over their nosegay . have I wear the Fairy 's cap and be invisible , my father 's brother could not have pass I with less notice . 
 we do not avail ourselves much , or often , of that queer invitation of Lady Castlewood , to go and drink tea and sup with her ladyship when there be no other company . Old Van den Bosch , however shrewd his intellect , and great his skill in make a fortune , be not amusing in conversation , except to his daughter , who talk household and City matter , bull and bear , raise and sell farming - stock , and so forth , quite as keenly and shrewdly as her father . nor be my Lord Castlewood often at home , or much miss by his wife when absent , or very much at ease in the old father 's company . the Countess tell all this to my wife in her simple way . " guess , " say she , " my lord and father do not   pull well together nohow . guess my lord be always want money , and father keep the key of the box and quite right , too . if he could have the fingering of all our money , my lord would soon make away with it , and then what be to become of our noble family ? we pay everything , my dear   except play - debt , and they we will not   have nohow . we pay cook , horse , wine - merchant , tailor , and everybody and lucky for they too reckon my lord would not   pay them ! and we always take care that he have a guinea in his pocket , and go out like a real nobleman . what that man do owe to we : what he do before we come gracious goodness only know ! I and father do our good to make he respectable : but it be no easy job , my dear . law ! he would melt the plate , only father keep the key of the strong - room ; and when we go to Castlewood , my father travel with I , and papa be arm too , as well as the people . " 
 " gracious heaven ! " cry my wife , " your ladyship do not mean to say you suspect your own husband of a desire to " 
 " to what ? oh no , nothing , of course ! and I would trust our brother will with untold money , would not   I ? as much as I would trust the cat with the cream - pan ! I tell you , my dear , it be not all pleasure be a woman of rank and fashion : and if I have buy a countess 's coronet , I have pay a good price for it that I have ! " 
 and so have my Lord Castlewood pay a large price for have his estate free from incumbrance , his house and stable furnish , and his debt discharge . he be the slave of the little wife and her father . no wonder the old man 's society be not pleasant to the poor victim , and that he gladly slunk away from his own fine house , to feast at the club when he have money , or at least to any society save that which he find at home . to lead a bear , as I do , be no very pleasant business , to be sure : to wait in a bookseller 's anteroom until it should please his honour to finish his dinner and give I audience , be sometimes a hard task for a man of my name and with my pride ; but would I have exchange my poverty against Castlewood 's ignominy , or prefer his miserable dependence to my own ? at least I earn my wage , such as it be ; and no man can say that I ever flatter my patron , or be servile to they ; or indeed , in my dealing with they , be otherwise than sulky , overbearing , and , in a word , intolerable .
