 now there be a certain person with whom Fate have throw I into a life - partnership , who bear her poverty with such a smile sweetness and easy grace , that niggard Fortune relent before she , and , like some savage Ogre in the fairy tale , melt at the constant goodness and cheerfulness of that uncomplaining , artless , innocent creature . however poor she be , all who know she see that here be a fine lady ; and the little tradesman and humble folk round about we treat she with as much respect as the rich of our neighbour . " I think , my dear , " say good - natured Mrs. Foker , when they ride out in the latter 's chariot , " you look like the mistress of the carriage , and I only as your maid . " our landlady adore she ; the tradesfolk execute her little order as eagerly as if a duchess give they , or they be to make a fortune by wait on she . I have think often of the lady in Comus , and how , through all the rout and rabble , she move , entirely serene and pure . 
 several time , as often as we choose indeed , the good - natured parent of my young bear lend we their chariot to drive abroad or to call on the few friend we have . if I must tell the truth , we drive once to the Protestant Hero and have a syllabub in the garden there : and the hostess would insist upon call my wife her ladyship during the whole afternoon . we also visit Mr. Johnson , and take tea with he   the ingenious Mr. Goldsmith be of the company ; the doctor wait upon my wife to her coach . but our most frequent visit be to Aunt Bernstein , and I promise you I be not at all jealous because my aunt presently profess to have a wonderful liking for Theo . 
 this liking grow so that she would have she most day in the week , or to stay altogether with she , and think that Theo 's child and husband be only plague to be sure , and hat we in the most amusing way for keep she favourite from she . not that my wife be unworthy of anybody 's favour ; but her many forced absence , and the constant difficulty of intercourse with she , raise my aunt 's liking for a while to a sort of passion . she pour in note like love - letter ; and her people be ever about our kitchen . if my wife do not go to she , she write heartrende appeal , and scold I severely when I see she ; and , the child be ill once   it hath pleased Fate to spare our Captain to be a prodigious trouble to we , and a wholesome trial for our temper , Madame Bernstein come three day run to Lambeth ; vow there be nothing the matter with the baby ; nothing at all ; and that we only pretend his illness , in order to vex she . 
 the reign Countess of Castlewood be just as easy and affable with her old aunt , as with other folk great and small . " what air you all about , scrap and bow to that old woman , I can not   tell , noway ! " her ladyship would say . " she a fine lady ! nonsense ! she be not   no more fine than any other lady : and I guess I be as good as any of them with their high heel and their grand air ! she a beauty once ! take away her wig , and her rouge , and her tooth ; and what become of your beauty , I would like to know ? guess you would put it all in a bandbox , and there would be nothing leave but a shrivelled old woman ! " and indeed the little homilist only speak too truly . all beauty must at last come to this complexion ; and decay , either underground or on the tree . here be old age , I fear , without reverence . here be grey hair , that be hide or paint . the world be still here , and she totter on it , and cling to it with her crutch . for fourscore year she have move on it , and eat of the tree , forbid and permit . she have have beauty , pleasure , flattery : but what secret rage , disappointment , defeat , humiliation ! what thorn under the rose ! what sting bee in the fruit ! " you be not a beauty , my dear , " she would say to my wife : " and may thank your star that you be not . "   if she contradict herself in her talk , I suppose the rest of we occasionally do the like .   " do not   tell I that your husband be pleased with your face , and you want no one else 's admiration ! we all do . every woman would rather be beautiful than be anything else in the world ever so rich , or ever so good , or have all the gift of the fairy ! look at that picture , though I know ' tis but a bad one , and that stupid vapouring Kneller could not paint my eye , nor my hair , nor my complexion . what a shape I have then and look at I now , and this wrinkle old neck ! why have we such a short time of our beauty ? I remember Mademoiselle de l'Enclos at a much great age than mine , quite fresh and well - conserved . we can not   hide our age . they be write in Mr. Collins 's book for we . I be bear in the last year of King James 's reign . I be not old yet . I be but seventy - six . but what a wreck , my dear : and be not   it cruel that our time should be so short ? " 
 here my wife have to state the incontrovertible proposition , that the time of all of we be short here below . 
 " ha ! " cry the Baroness . " do not Adam live near a thousand year , and be not Eve beautiful all the time ? I use to perplex Mr. Tusher with that poor creature ! what have we do since , that our life be so much lessen , I say ? " 
 " have your life be so happy that you would prolong it ever so much more ? " ask the Baroness 's auditor . " have you , who love wit , never read Dean Swift 's famous description of the deathless people in Gulliver ? my papa and my husband say ' tis one of the fine and most awful sermon ever write . it be well not to live at all , than to live without love ; and I be sure , " say my wife , put her handkerchief to her eye , ' should anything happen to my dear George , I would wish to go to Heaven that moment . " 
 " who love I in Heaven ? I be quite alone , child that be why I have rather stay here , " say the Baroness , in a frightened and rather piteous tone . " you be kind to I , God bless your sweet face ! though I scold , and have a frightful temper , my servant will do anything to make I comfortable , and get up at any hour of the night , and never say a cross word in answer . I like my card still . indeed , life would be a blank without them . almost everything be go except that . I can not   eat my dinner now , since I lose those last two tooth . everything go away from we in old age . but I still have my card thank Heaven , I still have my card ! " and here she would begin to doze : wake up , however , if my wife stir or rise , and imagine that Theo be about to leave she . " do not   go away , I can not   bear to be alone . I do not   want you to talk . but I like to see your face , my dear ! it be much pleasanter than that horrid old Brett 's , that I have have scowl about my bedroom these ever so long year . " 
 " well , Baroness ! still at your cribbage ? "   we may fancy a noble Countess interrupt a game at card between Theo and Aunt Bernstein .   " I and my Lord Esmond have come to see you ! go and shake hand with grandaunt , Esmond ! and tell her ladyship that your lordship be a good boy ! " 
 " my lordship be a good boy , " say the child .   Madam Theo use to act these scene for I in a very lively way . 
 " and if he be , I guess he do not   take after his father , " shriek out Lady Castlewood . she choose to fancy that Aunt Bernstein be deaf , and always bawl at the old lady . 
 " your ladyship choose my nephew for well or for bad , " say Aunt Bernstein , who be now always very much flurry in the presence of the young Countess . 
 " but he be a precious deal bad than ever I think he be . I be speak of your Pa , Ezzy . if it be not   for your mother , my son , Lord know what would become of you ! we be a - go to see his little Royal Highness . sorry to see your ladyship not look quite so well to - day . we can not   always remain young and law ! how we do change as we grow old ! go up and kiss that lady , Ezzy . she have get a little boy , too . why , bless we ! have you get the child downstairs ? " indeed , Master Miles be down below , for special reason accompany his mother on her visit to Aunt Bernstein sometimes ; and our aunt desire the mother 's company so much , that she be actually fain to put up with the child . " so you have get the child here ? oh , you slyboot ! " say the Countess . " guess you come after the old lady 's money ! Law bless you ! do not   look so frightened . she can not   hear a single word I say . come , Ezzy . good - bye , aunt ! " and my lady Countess rustle out of the room . 
 do Aunt Bernstein hear she or not ? where be the wit for which the old lady have be long famous ? and be that fire put out , as well as the brilliancy of her eye ? with other people she be still ready enough , and unsparing of her sarcasm . when the Dowager of Castlewood and Lady Fanny visit she   these exalt lady treat my wife with perfect indifference and charming good breeding , the Baroness , in their society , be stately , easy , and even command . she would mischievously caress Mrs. Warrington before they ; in her absence , vaunt my wife 's good breeding ; say that her nephew have make a foolish match , perhaps , but that I certainly have take a charming wife . " in a word , I praise you so to they , my dear , " say she , " that I think they would like to tear your eye out . " but , before the little american , ' tis certain that she be uneasy and tremble . she be so afraid , that she actually do not dare to deny her door ; and , the Countess 's back turn , do not even abuse she . however much they might dislike she , my lady do not tear out Theo 's eye . once they drive to our cottage at Lambeth , where my wife happen to be sit at the open window , hold her child on her knee , and in full view of her visitor . a gigantic footman strut through our little garden , and deliver their ladyship ' visit ticket at our door . their hatred hurt we no more than their visit please we . when next we have the loan of our friend the Brewer 's carriage Mrs. Warrington drive to Kensington , and Gumbo hand over to the giant our card in return for those which his noble mistress have bestow on we . 
 the Baroness have a coach , but seldom thought of give it to we : and would let Theo and her maid and baby start from Clarges Street in the rain , with a faint excuse that she be afraid to ask her coachman to take his horse out . but , twice on her return home , my wife be frighten by rude fellow on the other side of Westminster Bridge ; and I fairly tell my aunt that I should forbid Mrs. Warrington to go to she , unless she could be bring home in safety ; so grumble Jehu have to drive his horse through the darkness . he grumble at my shilling : he do not know how few I have . our poverty wear a pretty decent face . my relative never think of relieve it , nor I of complain before they . I do not   know how Sampson get a windfall of guineas ; but , I remember , he bring I six once ; and they be more welcome than any money I ever have in my life . he have be look into Mr. Miles 's crib , as the child lie asleep ; and , when the parson go away , I find the money in the baby 's little rosy hand . yes , Love be good of all . I have many such benefaction register in my heart precious welcome fountain spring up in desert place , kind , friendly light cheer our despondency and gloom . 
 this worthy divine be willing enough to give as much of his company as she choose to Madame de Bernstein , whether for card or theology . have know her ladyship for many year now , Sampson could see , and aver to we , that she be break fast ; and as he speak of she evidently increase infirmity , and of the probability of their fatal termination , Mr. S. would discourse to we in a very feeling manner of the necessity for prepare for a future world ; of the vanity of this , and of the hope that in another there might be happiness for all repentant sinner . 
 " I have be a sinner for one , " say the chaplain , bow his head . " God knoweth , and I pray he to pardon I . I fear , sir , your aunt , the Lady Baroness , be not in such a state of mind as will fit she very well for the change which be imminent . I be but a poor weak wretch , and no prisoner in Newgate could confess that more humbly and heartily . once or twice of late , I have seek to speak on this matter with her ladyship , but she have receive I very roughly . ' parson , ' say she , ' if you come for card , ' tis mighty well , but I will thank you to spare I your sermon . ' what can I do , sir ? I have call more than once of late , and Mr. Case hath tell I his lady be unable to see I . " in fact Madame Bernstein tell my wife , whom she never refuse , as I say , that the poor chaplain 's ton be unendurable , and as for his theology , " have not   I be a Bishop 's wife ? " say she , " and do I want this creature to teach I ? " 
 the old lady be as impatient of doctor as of divine ; pretend that my wife be ail , and that it be more convenient for our good Doctor Heberden to visit she in Clarges Street than to travel all the way to our Lambeth lodging , we get Dr. H. to see Theo at our aunt 's house , and pray he if possible to offer his advice to the baroness : we make Mrs. Brett , her woman , describe her ailment , and the doctor confirm our opinion that they be most serious , and might speedily end . she would rally briskly enough of some evening , and entertain a little company ; but of late she scarcely go abroad at all . a somnolence , which we have remark in she , be attributable in part to opiate which she be in the habit of taking ; and she use these narcotic to smother habitual pain . one night , as we two sit with her   Mr. Miles be wean by this time , and his mother could leave he to the charge of our faithful Molly , she fall asleep over her card . we hush the servant who come to lay out the supper - table   she would always have this luxurious , nor could any injunction of our or the Doctor 's teach her abstinence , and we sit a while as we have often do before , wait in silence till she should arouse from her doze . 
 when she awake , she look fixedly at I for a while , fumble with the card , and dropt they again in her lap , and say , " Henry , have I be long asleep ? " I think at first that it be for my brother she mistake I ; but she go on quickly , and with eye fix as upon some very far distant object , and say , " my dear , ' tis of no use , I be not good enough for you . I love card , and play , and court ; and oh , Harry , you do not   know all ! " here her voice change , and she fling her head up . " his father marry Anne Hyde , and sure the Esmond blood be as good as any that be not royal . Mamma , you must please to treat I with more respect . vos sermon I fatiguent ; entendez - vous ? faite place a mon Altesse royale : mesdames , I connaissez - vous ? je suis la " here she break out into frightful hysterical shriek and laughter , and as we run up to she , alarmed , " Oui , Henri , " she say , " il a jure de m'epouser et les prince tiennent parole n'est - ce pas ? o oui ! il tiennent parole ; si non , tu le tueras , cousin ; tu le ah ! que je suis folle ! " and the pitiful shriek and laughter recommence . Ere her frightened people have come up to her summon , the poor thing have pass out of this mood into another ; but always labour under the same delusion that I be the Henry of past time , who have love she and have be forsake by she , whose bone be lie far away by the bank of the Potomac . 
 my wife and the woman put the poor lady to bed as I run myself for medical aid . she ramble , still talk wildly , through the night , with her nurse and the surgeon sit by she . then she fall into a sleep , bring on by more opiate . when she awake , her mind do not actually wander ; but her speech be change , and one arm and side be paralyse . 
 ' tis needless to relate the progress and termination of her malady , or watch that expire flame of life as it gasp and flicker . her sens would remain with she for a while   and then she be never satisfied unless Theo be by her bedside , or again her mind would wander , and the poor decrepit creature , lie upon her bed , would imagine herself young again , and speak incoherently of the scene and incident of her early day . then she would address I as Henry again , and call upon I to revenge some insult or slight , of which   whatever my suspicion might be   the only record lie in her insane memory . " they have always be so , " she would murmur : " they never love man or woman but they forsook they . Je I vengerai , o oui , je I vengerai ! I know they all : I know they all : and I will go to my Lord Stair with the list . do not   tell I ! his religion can not   be the right one . I will go back to my mother 's though she do not love I . she never do . why do not   you , mother ? be it because I be too wicked ? ah ! Pitie , pitie . o mon pere ! I will make my confession " and here the unhappy paralysed lady make as if she would move in her bed . 
 let we draw the curtain round it . I think with awe still , of those rapid word , utter in the shadow of the canopy , as my pallid wife sit by she , her Prayer - book on her knee ; as the attendant move to and fro noiselessly ; as the clock tick without , and strike the fleeting hour ; as the sun fall upon the Kneller picture of beatrix in her beauty , with the blushing cheek , the smile lip , the wave auburn tress , and the eye which seem to look towards the dim figure moan in the bed . I could not for a while understand why our aunt 's attendant be so anxious that we should quit it . but towards evening , a servant steal in , and whisper her woman ; and then Brett , look rather disturbed , beg we to go downstairs , as the as the Doctor be come to visit the Baroness . I do not tell my wife , at the time , who " the Doctor " be ; but as the gentleman slide by we , and pass upstairs , I see at once that he be a catholic ecclesiastic . when Theo next see our poor lady , she be speechless ; she never recognise any one about she , and so pass unconsciously out of life . during her illness her relative have call assiduously enough , though she would see none of they save we . but when she be go , and we descend to the low room after all be over , we find Castlewood with his white face , and my lady from Kensington , and Mr. will already assemble in the parlour . they look greedily at we as we appear . they be hungry for the prey . 
 when our aunt 's will be open , we find it date five year back , and everything she have be leave to her dear nephew , Henry Esmond Warrington , of Castlewood , in Virginia , " in affectionate love and remembrance of the name which he bore . " the property be not great . her revenue have be derive from pension from the Crown as it appear   for what service I can not say , but the pension of course die with she , and there be only a few hundred pound , besides jewel , trinket , and the furniture of the house in Clarges Street , of which all London come to the sale . Mr. Walpole bid for her portrait , but I make free with Harry 's money so far as to buy the picture in : and it now hang over the mantelpiece of the chamber in which I write . what with jewel , lace , trinket , and old china which she have gather Harry become possessed of more than four thousand pound by his aunt 's legacy . I make so free as to lay my hand upon a hundred , which come , just as my stock be reduce to twenty pound ; and I procure bill for the remainder , which I forward to Captain Henry Esmond in Virginia . nor should I have scruple to take more   for my brother be indebted to I in a much great sum , but he write I there be another wonderful opportunity for buy an estate and negro in our neighbourhood at home ; and Theo and I be only too glad to forgo our little claim , so as to establish our brother 's fortune . as to mine , poor Harry at this time do not know the state of it . my mother have never inform he that she have cease remit to I . she help he with a considerable sum , the result of her saving , for the purchase of his new estate ; and Theo and I be most heartily thankful at his prosperity . 
 and how strange our be ! by what curious good fortune , as our purse be empty , be it fill again ! I have actually come to the end of our stock , when poor Sampson bring I his six piece and with these I be enable to carry on , until my half - year 's salary , as young Mr. Foker 's Governor , be due : then Harry 's hundred , on which I lay main basse , help we over three month   we be behindhand with our rent , or the money would have last six good week long : and when this be pretty near expended , what should arrive but a bill of exchange for a couple of hundred pound from Jamaica , with ten thousand blessing , from the dear friend there , and fond scolding from the General that we have not soon tell he of our necessity of which he have only hear through our friend , Mr. Foker , who speak in such term of Theo and myself as to make our parent more than ever proud of their child . be my quarrel with my mother irreparable ? let I go to Jamaica . there be plenty there for all , and employment which his Excellency as Governor would immediately procure for I . " come to we ! " write Hetty . " come to we ! " write Aunt Lambert . " have my child be suffer poverty , and we roll in our Excellency 's coach , with guard to turn out whenever we pass ? have Charley be home to you for ever so many holiday , from the Chartreux , and have ever so many of my poor George 's half - crown in his pocket , I dare say ? "   this be indeed the truth , for where be he to go for holiday but to his sister ? and be there any use in tell the child how scarce half - crown be with we ? . " and you always treat he with such goodness , as his letter tell I , which be brimful of love for George and little Miles ! oh , how we long to see Miles ! " write Hetty and her mother ; " and as for his godfather "   write Het , " who have be good to my dearest and her child , I promise he a kiss whenever I see he ! " 
 our young benefactor be never to hear of our family 's love and gratitude to he . that glimpse of his bright face over the railing before our house at Lambeth , as he ride away on his little horse , be the last we ever be to have of he . at Christmas a basket come to we , contain a great turkey , and three brace of partridge , with a card , and ' shoot by M. W. " write on one of they . and on receipt of this present , we write to thank the child and give he our sister 's message . 
 to this letter , there come a reply from Lady Warrington , who say she be bind to inform I , that in visit I her child have be guilty of disobedience , and that she learn his visit to I now for the first time . know my view regard duty to my parent   which I have exemplify in my marriage , she could not wish her son to adopt they . and fervently hop that I might be bring to see the error of my present course , she take leave of this most unpleasant subject , subscribe herself , etc . etc . and we get this pretty missive as sauce for poor Miles 's turkey , which be our family feast for New Year 's day . my Lady Warrington 's letter choke our meal , though Sampson and Charley rejoice over it . 
 ah I ! Ere the month be over , our little friend be go from amongst we . go out shooting , and drag his gun through a hedge after he , the trigger catch in a bush , and the poor little man be bring home to his father 's house , only to live a few day and expire in pain and torture . under the yew - tree yonder , I can see the vault which cover he , and where my bone one day no doubt will be lay . and over our pew at church , my child have often wistfully spelt the touching epitaph in which Miles 's heartbroken father have inscribe his grief and love for his only son . 




 CHAPTER LXXXIV . in which Harry submit to the Common Lot 

 hard time be now over with I , and I have to battle with poverty no more . my little kinsman 's death make a vast difference in my worldly prospect . I become next heir to a good estate . my uncle and his wife be not likely to have more child . " the woman be capable of commit any crime to disappoint you , " Sampson vow ; but , in truth , my Lady Warrington be guilty of no such treachery . cruelly smite by the stroke which fall upon they , Lady Warrington be teach by her religious adviser to consider it as a chastisement of Heaven , and submit to the Divine Will . " whilst your son live , your heart be turn away from the well world "   her clergyman tell she , " and your ladyship think too much of this . for your son 's advantage you desire rank and title . you ask and might have obtain an earthly coronet . of what avail be it now , to one who have but a few year to pass upon earth of what importance compare to the heavenly crown , for which you be an assured candidate ? " the accident cause no little sensation . in the chapel of that enthusiastic sect , towards which , after her son 's death , she now more than ever inclined , many sermon be preach bear reference to the event . far be it from I to question the course which the bereaved mother pursue , or to regard with other than respect and sympathy any unhappy soul seek that refuge whither sin and grief and disappointment fly for consolation . Lady Warrington even try a reconciliation with myself . a year after her loss , be in London , she signify that she would see I , and I wait on she ; and she give I , in her usual didactic way , a homily upon my position and her own . she marvel at the decree of Heaven , which have permit , and how dreadfully punish ! her poor child 's disobedience to she a disobedience by which I be to profit .   it appear my poor little man have disobey order , and go out with his gun , unknown to his mother .   she hop that , should I ever succeed to the property , though the Warringtons be , thank Heaven , a long - live family , except in my own father 's case , whose life have be curtail by the excess of a very ill - regulate youth , but should I ever succeed to the family estate and honour , she hop , she pray , that my present course of life might be alter ; that I should part from my unworthy associate ; that I should discontinue all connexion with the horrid theatre and its licentious frequenter ; that I should turn to that quarter where only peace be to be have ; and to those sacred duty which she fear she very much fear that I have neglect . she fill her exhortation with scripture language , which I do not care to imitate . when I take my leave she give I a packet of sermon for Mrs. Warrington , and a little book of hymn by Miss Dora , who have be eminent in that society of which she and her mother become avow professor subsequently , and who , after the dowager 's death , at Bath , three year since , marry young Mr. Juffles , a celebrated preacher . the poor lady forgave I then , but she could not bear the sight of our boy . we lose our second child , and then my aunt and her daughter come eagerly enough to the poor suffering mother , and even invite we hither . but my uncle be now almost every day in our house . he would sit for hour look at our boy . he bring he endless toy and sweetmeat . he beg that the child might call he Godpapa . when we feel our own grief   which at time still , and after the lapse of five - and - twenty year , strike I as keenly as on the day when we first lose our little one   when I feel my own grief , I know how to commiserate his . but my wife could pity he before she know what it be to lose a child of her own . the mother 's anxious heart have already divine the pang which be feel by the sorrow - stricken father ; mine , more selfish , have only learn pity from experience , and I be reconcile to my uncle by my little baby 's coffin . 
 the poor man send his coach to follow the humble funeral , and afterwards take out little Miles , who prattle to he unceasingly , and forget any grief he might have feel in the delight of his new black clothe , and the pleasure of the airing . how the innocent talk of the child stab the mother 's heart ! would we ever wish that it should heal of that wound ? I know her face so well that , to this day , I can tell when , sometimes , she be think of the loss of that little one . it be not a grief for a parting so long ago ; it be a communion with a soul we love in Heaven . 
 we come back to our bright lodging in Bloomsbury soon afterwards , and my young bear , whom I could no long lead , and who have take a prodigious friendship for Charley , go to the Chartreux School , where his friend take care that he have no more beat than be good for he , and where   in consequence of the excellence of his private tutor , no doubt   he take and keep a good place . and he like the school so much , that he say , if ever he have a son , he shall be send to that seminary . 
 now , I could no long lead my bear , for this reason , that I have other business to follow . be fully reconcile to we , I do believe , for Mr. Miles 's sake , my uncle   who be such an obsequious supporter of Government , that I wonder the Minister ever give he anything , be perfectly sure of his vote   use his influence in behalf of his nephew and heir ; and I have the honour to be gazette as one of his Majesty 's Commissioners for license hackney - coach , a post I fill , I trust , with credit , until a quarrel with the Minister   to be mention in its proper place   deprive I of that one . I take my degree also at the Temple , and appear in Westminster Hall in my gown and wig . and , this year , my good friend , Mr. Foker , have business at Paris , I have the pleasure of accompany he thither , where I be receive a bras ouvert by my dear american preserver , Monsieur de Florac , who introduce I to his noble family , and to even more of the polite society of the capital than I have leisure to frequent ; for I have too much spirit to desert my kind patron Foker , whose acquaintance lie chiefly amongst the bourgeoisie , especially with Monsieur Santerre , a great brewer of Paris , a scoundrel who hath since distinguish himself in blood and not beer . Mr. F. have need of my service as interpreter , and I be too glad that he should command they , and to be able to pay back some of the kindness which he have render to I . our lady , meanwhile , be reside at Mr. Foker 's new villa at Wimbledon , and be pleased to say that they be amuse with the " parisian letter " which I send to they , through my distinguished friend Mr. Hume , then of the Embassy , and which subsequently have be publish in a neat volume . 
 whilst I be tranquilly discharge my small official duty in London , those trouble be commence which be to end in the great separation between our colony and the mother country . when Mr. Grenville propose his stamp - duty , I say to my wife that the bill would create a mighty discontent at home , for we be ever anxious to get as much as we could from England , and pay back as little ; but assuredly I never anticipate the prodigious anger which the scheme create . it be with we as with family or individual . a pretext be give for a quarrel : the real cause lie in long bickering and previous animosity . many foolish exaction and petty tyranny , the habitual insolence of Englishmen towards all foreigner , all colonist , all folk who dare to think their river as good as our Abana and Pharpar , the natural spirit of man outrage by our imperious domineering spirit , set Britain and her colony to quarrel ; and the astonishing blunder of the system adopt in England bring the quarrel to an issue , which I , for one , be not go to deplore . have I be in Virginia instead of London , ' tis very possible I should have take the provincial side , if out of mere opposition to that resolute mistress of Castlewood , who might have drive I into revolt , as England do the colony . be the Stamp Act the cause of the revolution ? a tax no great than that cheerfully pay in England . ten year early , when the French be within our territory , and we be implore succour from home , would the colony have rebel at the payment of this tax ? do not most people consider the tax - gatherer the natural enemy ? against the British in America there be array thousand and thousand of the high - spirited and brave , but there be thousand more who find their profit in the quarrel , or have their private reason for engage in it . I protest I do not   know now whether mine be selfish or patriotic , or which side be in the right , or whether both be not . I be sure we in England have nothing to do but to fight the battle out ; and , have lose the game , I do vow and believe that , after the first natural soreness , the loser feel no rancour . 
 what make brother Hal write home from Virginia , which he seem exceedingly loth to quit , such flame patriotic letter ? my kind , good brother be always lead by somebody ; by I when we be together   he have such an idea of my wit and wisdom , that if I say the day be fine , he would ponder over the observation as though it be one of the saying of the Seven Sages , by some other wiseacre when I be away . who inspire these flaming letter , this boisterous patriotism , which he send to we in London ? " he be rebel against Madam Esmond , " say i. " he be lead by some colonial person by that lady , perhaps , " hint my wife . who " that lady " be Hal never have tell we ; and , indeed , besought I never to allude to the delicate subject in my letter to he ; " for Madam wish to see them all , and I wish to say nothing about you know what until the proper moment , " he write . no affection could be great than that which his letter show . when he hear   from the informant whom I have mention   that in the midst of my own extreme strait I have retain no more than a hundred pound out of his aunt 's legacy , he be for mortgage the estate which he have just buy ; and have more than one quarrel with his mother in my behalf , and speak his mind with a great deal more frankness than I should ever have venture to show . until her angry recrimination   when she charge he with ingratitude , after have toil and save so much and so long for he , the poor fellow do not know that our mother have cut off my supply to advance his interest ; and by the time this news come to he his bargain be make , and I be fortunately quite out of want . 
 every scrap of paper which we ever write , our thrifty parent at Castlewood tap and docket and put away . we boy be more careless about our letter to one another : I especially , who perhaps choose rather to look down upon my young brother 's literary performance ; but my wife be not so supercilious , and hath keep no small number of Harry 's letter , as well as those of the angelic be whom we be presently to call sister . 
 " to think whom he have choose , and whom he might have have ! oh , ' tis cruel ! " cry my wife , when we get that notable letter in which Harry first make we acquaint with the name of his charmer . 
 " she be a very pretty little maid when I leave home , she may be a perfect beauty now , " I remark , as I read over the long letter Harry ever write on private affair . 
 " but be she to compare to my Hetty ? " say Mrs. Warrington . 
 " we agree that Hetty and Harry be not to be happy together , my love , " say I. 
 Theo give her husband a kiss . " my dear , I wish they have try , " she say with a sigh . " I be afraid lest lest Hetty should have lead he , you see ; and I think she hath the well head . but , from read this , it appear that the new lady have take command of poor Harry , " and she hand I the letter : 
 " my dearest George hath be prepare by previous letter to understand how a certain lady have make a conquest of my heart , which I have give away in exchange for something infinitely more valuable , namely , her own . she be at my side as I write this letter , and if there be no bad spelling , such as you often use to laugh at , ' tis because I have my pretty dictionary at hand , which make no fault in the long word , nor in anything else I know of : being of opinion that she be perfection . 
 " as Madam Esmond see all your letter , I writ you not to give any hint of a certain delicate matter but now ' tis no secret , and be know to all the country . Mr. George be not the only one of our family who have make a secret marriage , and be scold by his mother . as a dutiful young brother I have follow his example ; and now I may tell you how this mighty event come about . 
 " I have not be at home long before I see my fate be accomplisht . I will not tell you how beautiful Miss Fanny Mountain have grow since I have be away in Europe . she saith , ' you never will think so , ' and I be glad , as she be the only thing in life I would grudge to my dear brother . 
 " that neither Madam Esmond nor my other mother   as Mountain be now   should have see our mutual attachment , be a wonder only to be account for by suppose that love make other folk blind . mine for my Fanny be increase by see what the treatment be she have from Madam Esmond , who indeed be very rough and haughty with she , which my love bear with a sweetness perfectly angelic   this I will say , though she will order I not to write any such nonsense . she be scarce well treat than a servant of the house indeed our negro can talk much more free before Madam Esmond than ever my Fanny could . 
 " and yet my Fanny say she doth not regret Madam 's unkindness , as without it I possibly never should have be what I be to she . oh , dear brother ! when I remember how great your goodness hath be , how , in my own want , you pay my debt , and rescue I out of prison ; how you have be live in poverty which never need have occur but for my fault ; how you might have pay yourself back my just debt to you and would not , prefer my advantage to your own comfort , indeed I be lose at the thought of such goodness ; and ought I not to be thankful to Heaven that hath give I such a wife and such a brother ? 
 " when I writ to you request you to send I my aunt 's legacy money , for which indeed I have the most profitable and urgent occasion , I have no idea that you be yourself suffer poverty . that you , the head of our family , should condescend to be governor to a brewer 's son ! that you should have to write for bookseller   except in so far as your own genius might prompt you , never once enter my mind , until Mr. Foker 's letter come to we , and this would never have be show for Madam keep it secret have it not be for the difference which spring up between we . 
 " Poor Tom Diggle 's estate and negro be for sale , owe to Tom 's loss and extravagance at play , and his father 's debt before he Madam Esmond see here be a great opportunity of make a provision for I , and that with six thousand pound for the farm and stock , I should be put in possession of as pretty a property as fall to most young son in this country . it lie handy enough to Richmond , between Kent and Hanover Court House the mansion nothing for elegance compare to our at Castlewood , but the land excellent and the people extraordinary healthy . 
 " here be a second opportunity , Madam Esmond say , such as never might again befall . by the sale of my commission and her own saving I might pay more than half of the price of the property , and get the rest of the money on mortgage ; though here , where money be scarce to procure , it would have be difficult and dear . at this juncture , with our new relative , Mr. Van den Bosch , bid against we   his agent be wild that we should have buy the property over he , my aunt 's legacy most opportunely fall in . and now I be owner of a good house and negro in my native country , shall be call , no doubt , to our House of Burgesses , and hope to see my dear brother and family under my own roof - tree . to sit at my own fireside , to ride my own horse to my own hound , be well than go a - soldiering , now war be over , and there be no french to fight . indeed , Madam Esmond make a condition that I should leave the army , and live at home , when she bring I her 1750 pound of saving . she have lose one son , she say , who choose to write play - book , and live in England let the other stay with she at home . 
 " but , after the purchase of the estate be make , and my paper for sell out be send home , my mother would have have I marry a person of her choosing , but by no mean of mine . you remember Miss Betsy Pitts at Williamsburgh ? she be in no wise improve by have have her face dreadfully scar with small - pock , and though Madam Esmond saith the young lady hath every virtue , I own her virtue do not suit I . her eye do not look straight ; she hath one leg short than another ; and oh , brother ! didst thou never remark Fanny 's ankle when we be boy ? Neater I never see at the Opera . 
 " now , when ' twa agree that I should leave the army , a certain dear girl   canst thou guess her name ?   one day , when we be private , burst into tear of such happiness , that I could not but feel immensely touch by her sympathy . 
 " ' ah ! ' say she , ' do you think , sir , that the idea of the son of my revered benefactress go to battle doth not inspire I with terror ? ah , Mr. Henry ! do you imagine I have no heart ? when Mr. George be with Braddock , do you fancy we do not pray for he ? and when you be with Mr. Wolfe oh ! ' 
 " here the dear creature hide her eye in her handkerchief , and have hard work to prevent her mama , who come in , from see that she be cry . but my dear Mountain declare that , though she might have fancy , might have pray in secret for such a thing   she own to that now , she never imagine it for one moment . nor , indeed , do my good mother , who suppose that Sam Lintot , the apothecary 's lad at Richmond , be Fanny 's flame an absurd fellow that I near kick into James River . 
 " but when the commission be sell , and the estate buy , what do fanny do but fall into a deep melancholy ? I find she cry one day , in her mother 's room , where the two lady have be at work trim hat for my negro . 
 " ' what ! cry , miss ? ' say I. ' has my mother be scold you ? ' 
 " ' no , ' say the dear creature . ' Madam Esmond have be kind to - day . ' 
 " and her tear drop down on a cockade which she be sew on to a hat for Sady , who be to be head - groom . 
 " ' then , why , miss , be those dear eye so red ? ' say I. 
 " ' because I have the toothache , ' she say , ' or because because I be a fool . ' here she fairly burst out . ' oh , Mr. Harry ! oh , Mr. Warrington ! you be go to leave we , and ' tis as well . you will take your place in your country , as become you . you will leave we poor woman in our solitude and dependence . you will come to visit we from time to time . and when you be happy and honour , and among your gay companion , you will remember your ' 
 " here she could say no more , and hide her face with one hand as I , I confess , seize the other . 
 " ' Dearest , sweetest Miss Mountain ! ' say I. ' oh , could I think that the parting from I have bring tear to those lovely eye ! indeed , I fear , I should be almost happy ! let they look upon your ' 
 " ' oh , sir ! ' cry my charmer . ' oh , Mr. Warrington ! consider who I be , sir , and who you be ! remember the difference between we ! release my hand , sir ! what would Madam Esmond say if if ' 
 " if what , I do not   know , for here our mother be in the room . 
 " ' what would Madam Esmond say ? ' she cry out . ' she would say that you be an ungrateful , artful , false , little ' 
 " ' Madam ! ' say I. 
 " ' yes , an ungrateful , artful , false , little wretch ! ' cry out my mother . ' for shame , miss ! what would Mr. Lintot say if he see you make eye at the Captain ? and for you , Harry , I will have you bring none of your garrison manner hither . this be a christian family , sir , and you will please to know that my house be not intend for captain and their miss ! ' 
 " ' Misses , mother ! ' say I. ' gracious power , do you ever venture for to call Miss Mountain by such a name ? Miss Mountain , the purest of her sex ! ' 
 " ' the purest of her sex ! can I trust my own ear ? ' ask Madam , turn very pale . 
 " ' I mean that if a man would question her honour , I would fling he out of window , ' say I. 
 " ' you mean that you your mother 's son be actually pay honourable attention to this young person ? ' 
 " ' he would never dare to offer any other , ' cry my fanny ; ' nor any woman but you , madam , to think so ! ' 
 " ' oh , I do not   know , miss ! ' say mother , drop she a fine curtsey , ' I do not   know the honour you be do our family ! you propose to marry with we , do you ? do I understand Captain Warrington aright , that he intend to offer I Miss Mountain as a daughter - in - law ? ' 
 " " tis to be see , madam , that I have no protector , or you would not insult I so ! ' cry my poor victim . 
 " ' I should think the apothecary protection sufficient ! ' say our mother . 
 " ' I do not , mother ! ' I bawl out , for I be very angry ; ' and if Lintot offer she any liberty , I will brain he with his own pestle ! ' 
 " ' oh ! if Lintot have withdraw , sir , I suppose I must be silent . but I do not know of the circumstance . he come hither , as I suppose , to pay court to Miss : and we all think the match equal , and I encourage it . ' 
 " ' he come because I have the toothache ! ' cry my darling   and indeed she have a dreadful bad tooth . and he take it out for she , and there be no end to the suspicion and calumny of woman . 
 " ' what more natural than that he should marry my housekeeper 's daughter ' twas a very suitable match ! ' continue Madam , take snuff . ' but I confess , ' she add , go on , ' I be not aware that you intend to jilt the apothecary for my son ! ' 
 " ' peace , for Heaven 's sake , peace , Mr. Warrington ! ' cry my angel . 
 " ' pray , sir , before you fully make up your mind , have you not well look round the rest of my family ? ' say Madam . ' Dinah be a fine tall girl , and not very black ; Cleopatra be promise to ajax the blacksmith , to be sure ; but then we could break the marriage , you know . if with an apothecary , why not with a blacksmith ? Martha 's husband have run away , and ' 
 " here , dear brother , I own I break out a - swearing . I can not   help it ; but at time , when a man be angry , it do relieve he immensely . I be blest , but I should have go wild , if it have not   be for they oath . 
 " ' Curses , blasphemy , ingratitude , disobedience , ' say mother , lean now on her tortoiseshell stick , and then wave it something like a queen in a play . ' these be my reward ! ' say she . ' o Heaven , what have I do , that I should merit this awful punishment ? and do it please you to visit the sin of my father upon I ? where do my child inherit their pride ? when I be young , have I any ? when my papa bid I marry , do I refuse ? do I ever think of disobey ? no , sir . my fault hath be , and I own it , that my love be centre upon you , perhaps to the neglect of your eld brother . '   indeed , brother , there be some truth in what Madam say .   ' I turn from Esau , and I clung to Jacob . and now I have my reward , I have my reward ! I fix my vain thought on this world , and its distinction . to see my son advanced in worldly rank be my ambition . I toil , and spar , that I might bring he worldly wealth . I take unjustly from my eld son 's portion , that my young might profit . and oh ! that I should see he seduce the daughter of my own housekeeper under my own roof , and reply to my just anger with oath and blasphemy ! ' 
 " ' I try to seduce no one , madam , ' I cry out . ' if I utter oath and blasphemy , I beg your pardon ; but you be enough to provoke a saint to speak them . I will not   have this young lady 's character assail no , not by own mother nor any mortal alive . no , dear Miss Mountain ! if Madam Esmond choose to say that my design on you be dishonourable , let this undeceive she ! ' and , as I speak , I go down on my knee , seize my adorable Fanny 's hand . ' and if you will accept this heart and hand , miss , ' say I , ' they be yours for ever . ' 
 " ' you , at least , I know , sir , ' say Fanny , with a noble curtsey , ' never say a word that be disrespectful to I , or entertain any doubt of my honour . and I trust it be only Madam Esmond , in the world , who can have such an opinion of I . after what your ladyship hath say of I , of course I can stay no long in your house . ' 
 " ' of course , madam , I never intend you should ; and the soon you leave it the well , ' cry our mother . 
 " ' if you be drive from my mother 's house , mine , miss , be at your service , ' say I , make she a low bow . ' it be nearly ready now . if you will take it and stay in it for ever , it be yours ! and as Madam Esmond insult your honour , at least let I do all in my power to make a reparation ! ' I do not   know what more I exactly say , for you may fancy I be not a little flustered and excite by the scene . but here Mountain come in , and my dear Fanny , fling herself into her mother 's arm , weep upon her shoulder ; whilst Madam Esmond , sit down in her chair , look at we as pale as a stone . whilst I be tell my story to Mountain   who , poor thing , have not the least idea , not she , that Miss Fanny and I have the slight inclination for one another , I could hear our mother once or twice still say , ' I be punish for my crime ! ' 
 " now , what our mother mean by her crime I do not know at first , or indeed take much heed of what she say ; for you know her way , and how , when she be angry , she always talk sermon . but Mountain tell I afterwards , when we have some talk together , as we do at the tavern , whither the lady presently remove with their bag and baggage for not only would they not stay at Madam 's house after the language she use , but my mother determine to go away likewise . she call her servant together , and announce her intention of go home instantly to Castlewood ; and I own to you ' twa with a horrible pain I see the family coach roll by , with six horse , and ever so many of the servant on mule and on horseback , as I and Fanny look through the blind of the Tavern . 
 " after the word Madam use to my spotless fanny , ' twa impossible that the poor child or her mother should remain in our house : and indeed M. say that she would go back to her relation in England : and a ship bind homeward lie in James River , she go and bargain with the captain about a passage , so bent be she upon quit the country , and so little do she think of make a match between I and my angel . but the cabin be mercifully engage by a North Carolina gentleman and his family , and before the next ship sail   which bear this letter to my dearest George   they have agree to stop with I . almost all the lady in this neighbourhood have wait on they . when the marriage take place , I hope Madam Esmond will be reconcile . my Fanny 's father be a british officer ; and sure , our be no more . some day , please Heaven , we shall visit Europe , and the place where my wild oat be sown , and where I commit so many extravagance from which my dear brother rescue I . 
 " the lady send you their affection and duty , and to my sister . we hear his Excellency General Lambert be much beloved in Jamaica : and I shall write to our dear friend there announce my happiness . my dear brother will participate in it , and I be ever his grateful and affectionate H. E. W. 
 " P.S. Till Mountain tell I , I have no more notion than the ded that Madam E. have actially stopt your allowance ; besides make you pay for ever so much near upon 1000 pound Mountain say for good , etc . , provide for the virginian proparty . then there be all the charge of I out of prison , which I. O. U. with all my hart . draw upon I , please , dearest brother to any amount adresse I to care of Messrs. Horn and Sandon , Williamsburg , privit ; who remitt by present occasion a bill for 225 pound , payable by their London agent on demand . please do not   acknolledge this in answer ; as there be no good in bother woman with account and with the extra 5 pound by a capp or what she like for my dear sister , and a toy for my nephew from Uncle Hal . " 
 the conclusion to which we come on the perusal of this document be , that the lady have superintend the style and spelling of my poor Hal 's letter , but that the postscript be add without their knowledge . and I be afraid we argue that the Virginian Squire be under female domination as Hercules , Samson , and fortis multi have be before he . 




 CHAPTER LXXXV . Inveni Portum 

 when my mother hear of my acceptance of a place at home , I think she be scarcely well pleased . she may have withdraw her supply , in order to starve I into a surrender , and force I to return with my family to Virginia , and to dependence under she . we never , up to her die day , have any explanation on the pecuniary dispute between we . she cut off my allowance : I utter not a word ; but manage to live without her aid . I never hear that she repent of her injustice , or acknowledge it , except from Harry 's private communication to I . in after day , when we meet , by a great gentleness in her behaviour , and an uncommon respect and affection show to my wife , Madam Esmond may have intend I should understand her tacit admission that she have be wrong ; but she make no apology , nor do I ask one . Harry be provide for   whose welfare I could not grudge , all my mother 's saving and economical scheme go to my advantage , who be her heir . time be when a few guineas would have be more useful to I than hundred which might come to I when I have no need ; but when Madam Esmond and I meet , the period of necessity be long pass away ; I have no need to scheme ignoble saving , or to grudge the doctor his fee : I have plenty , and she could but bring I more . no doubt she suffer in her own mind to think that my child have be hungry , and she have offer they no food ; and that stranger have relieve the necessity from which her proud heart have cause she to turn aside . proud ? be she prouder than I ? a soft word of explanation between we might have bring about a reconciliation year before it come but I would never speak , nor do she . when I commit a wrong , and know it subsequently , I love to ask pardon ; but ' tis as a satisfaction to my own pride , and to myself I be apologise for have be want to myself . and hence , I think   out of regard to that personage of ego , I scarce ever could degrade myself to do a meanness . how do man feel whose whole life   and many man 's life be   be lie , scheme , and subterfuge ? what sort of company do they keep when they be alone ? daily in life I watch man whose every smile be an artifice , and every wink be an hypocrisy . Doth such a fellow wear a mask in his own privacy , and to his own conscience ? if I choose to pass over an injury , I fear ' tis not from a christian and forgiving spirit : ' tis because I can afford to remit the debt , and disdain to ask a settlement of it . one or two sweet soul I have know in my life   and perhaps try   to whom forgiveness be no trouble a plant that grow naturally , as it be , in the soil . I know how to remit , I say , not forgive . I wonder be we proud man proud of be proud ? 
 so I show not the least sign of submission towards my parent in Virginia yonder , and we continue for year to live in estrangement , with occasionally a brief word or two   such as the announcement of the birth of a child , or what not   pass between my wife and she . after our first trouble in America about the Stamp Act , trouble fall on I in London likewise . though I have be on the Tory side in our quarrel   as indeed upon the lose side in most controversy , have no doubt that the Imperial Government have a full right to levy taxis in the colony , yet at the time of the dispute I must publish a pert letter to a member of the House of Burgesses in Virginia , in which the question of the habitual insolence of the mother country to the colony be so freely handle , and sentiment be utter so disagreeable to person in power , that I be deprive of my place as hackney - coach licenser , to the terror and horror of my uncle , who never could be bring to love people in disgrace . he have grow to have an extreme affection for my wife as well as my little boy ; but towards myself , personally , entertain a kind of pity contempt which always infinitely amuse I . he have a natural scorn and dislike for poverty , and a corresponding love for success and good fortune . any opinion depart at all from the regular track shock and frighten he , and all truth - telling make he turn pale . he must have have originally some warmth of heart and genuine love of kindre : for , spite of the dreadful shock I give he , he continue to see Theo and the child   and I too , give I a mournful recognition when we meet ; and though break - hearted by my free - spokenness , he do not refuse to speak to I as he have do at the time of our first difference , but look upon I as a melancholy lose creature , who be past all worldly help or hope . never mind , I must cast about for some new scheme of life ; and the repayment of Harry 's debt to I at this juncture enable I to live at least for some month even , or year to come . o strange fatuity of youth ! I often say . how be it that we dare to be so poor and so little cast down ? 
 at this time his Majesty 's royal uncle of Cumberland fall down and perish in a fit ; and , strange to say , his death occasion a remarkable change in my fortune . my poor Sir Miles Warrington never miss any court ceremony to which he could introduce himself . he be at all the drawing - room , christening , ball , funeral of the court . if ever a prince or princess be ail , his coach be at their door : Leicester Fields , Carlton House , Gunnersbury , be all the same to he , and nothing must satisfy he now but go to the stout duke 's funeral . he catch a great cold and an inflammation of the throat from stand bareheade at this funeral in the rain ; and one morning , before almost I have hear of his illness , a lawyer wait upon I at my lodging in Bloomsbury , and salute I by the name of Sir George Warrington . 
 Party and fear of the future be over now . we lay the poor gentleman by the side of his little son , in the family churchyard where so many of his race repose . little Miles and I be the chief mourner . an obsequious tenantry bow and curtsey before we , and do their utmost to conciliate my honour and my worship . the dowager and her daughter withdraw to Bath presently ; and I and my family take possession of the house , of which I have be master for thirty year . be not too eager , o my son ! have but a little patience , and I too shall sleep under yonder yew - tree , and the people will be toss up their cap for Sir Miles . 
 the record of a prosperous country life be easily and briefly tell . the steward 's book show what rent be pay and forgive , what crop be raise , and in what rotation . what visitor come to we , and how long they stay : what pensioner my wife have , and how they be doctor and relieve , and how they die : what year I be sheriff , and how often the hound meet near we ; all these be narrate in our house journal , which any of my heir may read who choose to take the trouble . we could not afford the fine mansion in Hill Street , which my predecessor have occupy ; but we take a small house , in which , however , we spend more money . we make not half the show   with livery , equipage , and plate   for which my uncle have be famous ; but our beer be strong , and my wife 's charity be perhaps more costly than those of the Dowager Lady Warrington . no doubt she think there be no harm in spoil the Philistines ; for she make we pay unconscionably for the good she leave behind she in our country - house , and I submit to most of her extortion with unutterable good - humour . what a value she imagine the potted plant in her greenhouse bore ! what a price she set upon that horrible old spinet she leave in her drawing - room ! and the frame piece of worst - work , perform by the accomplished Dora and the lovely Flora , have they be masterpiece of Titian or Vandyck , to be sure my lady dowager could hardly have value they at a high price . but though we pay so generously , though we be , I may say without boast , far kind to our poor than ever she have be , for a while we have the very bad reputation in the county , where all sort of story have be tell to my discredit . I think I might perhaps succeed to my uncle 's seat in Parliament , as well as to his land property ; but I find , I know not how , that I be vote to be a person of very dangerous opinion . I would not bribe : I would not coerce my own tenant to vote for I in the election of ' 68 . a gentleman come down from Whitehall with a pocket - book full of bank - note ; and I find that I have no chance against my competitor . 
 Bon Dieu ! now that we be at ease in respect of worldly mean , now that obedient tenant bow and curtsey as we go to church ; that we drive to visit our friend , or to the neighbouring town , in the great family coach with the four fat horse ; do we not often regret poverty , and the dear little cottage at Lambeth , where Want be ever prowl at the door ? do I not long to be bear - lead again , and vow that translate for bookseller be not such very hard drudgery ? when we go to London , we make sentimental pilgrimage to all our old haunt . I dare say my wife embrace all her landlady . you may be sure we ask all the friend of those old time to share the comfort of our new home with we . the Reverend Mr. Hagan and his lady visit we more than once . his appearance in the pulpit at b   where he preach very finely , as we think   cause an awful scandal there . Sampson come too , another unlucky Levite , and be welcome as long as he would stay among we . Mr. Johnson talk of come , but he put we off once or twice . I suppose our house be dull . I know that I myself would be silent for day , and fear that my moodiness must often have try the sweetest - temper woman in the world who live with I . I do not care for field sport . the kill one partridge be so like kill another , that I wonder how man could pass day after day in the pursuit of that kind of slaughter . their fox - hunt story would begin at four o'clock , when the tablecloth be remove , and last till supper - time . I sate silent , and listened : day after day I fall asleep : no wonder I be not popular with my company . 
 what admission be this I be make ? here be the storm over , the rock avoid , the ship in port and the sailor not overcontente ? be Susan I have be sigh for during the voyage , not the beauty I expect to find she ? in the first place , Susan and all the family can look in her William 's logbook , and so , madam , I be not go to put my secret down there . no , Susan , I never have secret from thee . I never care for another woman . I have see more beautiful , but none that suit I as well as your ladyship . I have meet Mrs. Carter and Miss Mulso , and Mrs. Thrale and Madam Kaufmann , and the angelical Gunnings , and her Grace of Devonshire , and a host of beauty who be not angelic , by any mean : and I be not dazzle by they . Nay , young folk , I may have lead your mother a weary life , and be a very Bluebeard over she , but then I have no other head in the closet . only , the first pleasure of take possession of our kingdom over , I own I begin to be quickly tired of the crown . when the captain wear it his Majesty will be a very different Prince . he can ride a - hunt five day in the week , and find the sport amusing . I believe he would hear the same sermon at church fifty time , and not yawn more than I do at the first delivery . but sweet Joan , beloved Baucis ! be thy faithful husband and true lover always , thy Darby be rather ashamed of have be testy so often ! and , be arrive at the consummation of happiness , Philemon ask pardon for fall asleep so frequently after dinner . there come a period of my life , when have reach the summit of felicity I be quite tired of the prospect I have there : I yawn in Eden , and say , " be this all ? what , no lion to bite ? no rain to fall ? no thorn to prick you in the rose - bush when you sit down ? only Eve , for ever sweet and tender , and fig for breakfast , dinner , supper , from week 's end to week 's end ! " shall I make my confession ? Hearken ! well , then , if I must make a clean breast of it . 
            
 here three page be tear out of Sir George Warrington 's MS . book , for which the editor be sincerely sorry . 
 I know the theory and practice of the Roman Church ; but , be breed of another persuasion   and sceptical and heterodox regard that , I can not   help doubt the other , too , and wonder whether Catholics , in their confession , confess all ? do we Protestants ever do so ; and have education render those other fellow - man so different from we ? at least , amongst we , we be not accustomed to suppose catholic priest or layman more frank and open than ourselves . which bring I back to my question , do any man confess all ? do yonder dear creature know all my life , who have be the partner of it for thirty year ; who , whenever I have tell she a sorrow , have be ready with the good of her gentle power to soothe it ; who have watch when I do not speak , and when I be silent have be silent herself , or with the charming hypocrisy of woman have wear smile and an easy appearance so as to make I imagine she feel no care , or would not even ask to disturb her lord 's secret when he seem to indicate a desire to keep it private ? oh , the dear hypocrite ! have I not watch she hide the boy ' peccadillo from papa 's anger ? have I not know her cheat out of her housekeeping to pay off their little extravagance ; and talk to I with an artless face , as if she do not know that our revered captain have have dealing with the gentleman of Duke 's Place , and our learn collegian , at the end of his term , have very press reason for sport his oak   as the phrase be   against some of the University tradesman ? why , from the very early day , thou wise woman , thou wert for ever conceal something from I , this one steal jam from the cupboard ; that one get into disgrace at school ; that naughty rebel   put on the cap , young folk , accord to the fit   flinging an inkstand at mamma in a rage , whilst I be tell the gown and the carpet be spoil by accident . we all hide from one another . we have all secret . we be all alone . we sin by ourselves , and , let we trust , repent too . yonder dear woman would give her foot to spare mine a twinge of the gout ; but , when I have the fit , the pain be in my slipper . at the end of the novel or the play , the hero and heroine marry or die , and so there be an end of they as far as the poet be concern , who huzzas for his young couple till the postchaise turn the corner ; or fetch the hearse and plume , and shovel they underground . but when Mr. Random and Mr. Thomas Jones be marry , be all over ? be there no quarrel at home ? be there no Lady Bellastons abroad ? be there no constable to be outrun ? no temptation to conquer we , or be conquer by we ? the Sirens sing after Ulysses long after his marriage , and the suitor whisper in Penelope 's ear , and he and she have many a weary day of doubt and care , and so have we all . as regard money I be put out of trouble by the inheritance I make : but do not Atra Cura sit behind baronet as well as equite ? my friend in London use to congratulate I on my happiness . who would not like to be master of a good house and a good estate ? but can Gumbo shut the hall - door upon blue devil , or lie they always in a red sea of claret ? do a man sleep the well who have four - and - twenty hour to doze in ? do his intellect brighten after a sermon from the dull old vicar ; a ten minute ' cackle and flattery from the village apothecary ; or the conversation of Sir John and Sir Thomas with their lady , who come ten moonlight muddy mile to eat a haunch , and play a rubber ? ' tis all very well to have tradesman bow to your carriage - door , room make for you at quarter - session , and my lady wife take down the second or the third to dinner : but these pleasure fade nay , have their inconvenience . in our part of the country , for seven year after we come to Warrington Manor , our two what they call good neighbour be my Lord Tutbury and Sir John Mudbrook . we be of an old date than the Mudbrooks ; consequently , my Lady Tutbury always fall to my lot , when we din together , who be deaf and fall asleep after dinner ; or if I have Lady Mudbrook , she chatter with a folly so incessant and intense , that even my wife could hardly keep her complacency   consummate hypocrite as her ladyship be , know the rage with which I be fume at the other 's clatter . I come to London . I show my tongue to Dr. Heberden . I pour out my catalogue of complaint . " Psha , my dear Sir George ! " say the unfeele physician . " Headaches , languor , bad sleep , bad temper "   " not bad temper : Sir George have the sweetest temper in the world , only he be sometimes a little melancholy , " say my wife .   " bad sleep , bad temper , " continue the implacable doctor . " my dear lady , his inheritance have be his ruin , and a little poverty and a great deal of occupation would do he all the good in life . " 
 no , my brother Harry ought to have be the squire , with remainder to my son Miles , of course . Harry 's letter be full of gaiety and good spirit . his estate prosper : his negro multiply ; his crop be large ; he be a member of our House of Burgesses ; he adore his wife ; could he but have a child his happiness would be complete . have Hal be master of Warrington Manor - house , in my place , he would have be belove through the whole country ; he would have be steward at all the race , the gay of all the jolly huntsman , the bien venu at all the mansion round about , where people scarce care to perform the ceremony of welcome at sight of my glum face . as for my wife , all the world like she , and agree in pity she . I do not   know how the report get abroad , but ' twa generally agree that I treat she with awful cruelty , and that for jealousy I be a perfect Bluebeard . ah I ! and so it be true that I have have many dark hour ; that I pass day in long silence ; that the conversation of fool and whipper - snapper make I rebellious and peevish , and that , when I feel contempt , I sometimes do not   know how to conceal it , or I should say do not . I hope as I grow old I grow more charitable . because I do not love bawl and gallop after a fox , like the captain yonder , I be not his superior ; but , in this respect , humbly own that he be mine . he have perception which be deny I ; enjoyment which I can not understand . because I be blind the world be not dark . I try now and listen with respect when Squire Codgers talk of the day 's run . I do my good to laugh when Captain Rattleton tell his garrison story . I step up to the harpsichord with old Miss Humby   our neighbour from Beccles   and try and listen as she warble her ancient ditty . I play whist laboriously . be I not try to do the duty of life ? and I have a right to be garrulous and egotistical , because I have be read Montaigne all the morning . 
 I be not surprised , know by what influence my brother be lead , to find his name in the list of Virginia burgess who declare that the sole right of impose taxis on the inhabitant of this colony be now , and ever hath be , legally and constitutionally vest in the House of Burgesses , and call upon the other colony to pray for the Royal interposition in favour of the violate right of America . and it be now , after we have be some three year settle in our english home , that a correspondence between we and Madam Esmond begin to take place . it be my wife who   upon some pretext such as woman always know how to find   re - establish the relation between we . Mr. Miles must need have the small - pox , from which he miraculously recover without lose any portion of his beauty ; and on his recovery the mother write her prettiest little wheedle letter to the grandmother of the fortunate babe . she coax she with all sort of modest phrase and humble offering of respect and goodwill . she narrate anecdote of the precocious genius of the lad   what hath subsequently happen , I wonder , to stop the growth of that gallant young officer 's brain ? , and she must have send over to his grandmother a lock of the darling boy 's hair , for the old lady , in her reply , acknowledge the receipt of some such present . I wonder , as it come from England , they allow it to pass our custom - house at Williamsburg . in return for these peace - offering and smuggled token of submission , come a tolerably gracious letter from my Lady of Castlewood . she inveigh against the dangerous spirit pervade the colony : she lament to think that her unhappy son be consort with people who , she fear , will be no well than rebel and traitor . she do not wonder , consider who his friend and adviser be . how can a wife take from an almost menial situation be expect to sympathise with person of rank and dignity who have the honour of the Crown at heart ? if evil time be come for the monarchy   for the folk in America appear to be disinclined to pay taxis , and require that everything should be do for they without cost , she remember how to monarchs in misfortune , the Esmonds her father the Marquis especially have ever be faithful . she know not what opinion   though she might judge from my newfangled Lord Chatham   be in fashion in England . she pray , at least , she might hear that one of her son be not on the side of rebellion . when we come , in after day , to look over old family paper in Virginia , we find " letter from my daughter Lady Warrington , " neatly tie up with a ribbon . my Lady Theo insist I should not open they ; and the truth , I believe , be , that they be so full of praise of her husband that she think my vanity would suffer from read they . 
 when Madam begin to write , she give we brief notice of Harry and his wife . " the two woman , " she write , ' still govern everything with my poor boy at Fannystown   as he choose to call his house . they must save money there , for I hear but a shabby account of their manner of entertaining . the Mount Vernon gentleman continue to be his great friend , and he vote in the House of Burgesses very much as his guide advise he . why he should be so sparing of his money I can not understand : I hear , of five negro who go with his equipage to my Lord Bottetourt 's , only two have shoe to their foot . I have reason to save , have son for whom I wish to provide , but he hath no child , wherein he certainly be spar from much grief , though , no doubt , Heaven in its wisdom mean our good by the trial which , through our child , it cause we to endure . his mother - in - law , " she add in one of her letter , " have be ail . ever since his marriage , my poor Henry have be the creature of these two artful woman , and they rule he entirely . nothing , my dear daughter , be more contrary to common sense and to Holy Scripture than this . be we not tell , wife , be obedient to your husband ? have Mr. Warrington live , I should have endeavour to follow up that sacred precept , hold that nothing so become a woman as humility and obedience . " 
 presently we have a letter seal with black , and announce the death of our dear good mountain , for whom I have a hearty regret and affection , remember her sincere love for we as child . Harry deplore the event in his honest way , and with tear which actually blot his paper . and Madam Esmond , allude to the circumstance , say : " my late housekeeper , Mrs. Mountain , as soon as she find her illness be fatal , send to I request a last interview on her deathbed , intending , doubtless , to pray my forgiveness for her treachery towards I . I send her word that I could forgive she as a Christian , and heartily hope   though I confess I doubt it   that she have a due sense of her crime towards I . but our meeting , I consider , be of no use , and could only occasion unpleasantness between we . if she repent , though at the eleventh hour , it be not too late , and I sincerely trust that she be now do so . and , would you believe her lamentable and hardened condition ? she send I word through Dinah , my woman , whom I dispatch to she with medicine for her soul 's and her body 's health , that she have nothing to repent of as far as regard her conduct to I , and she want to be leave alone ! Poor Dinah distribute the medicine to my negro , and our people take it eagerly whilst Mrs. Mountain , leave to herself , succumb to the fever . oh , the perversity of human kind ! this poor creature be too proud to take my remedy , and be now beyond the reach of cure and physician . you tell I your little Miles be subject to fit of cholic . my remedy , and I will beg you to let I know if effectual , be , " etc . etc . and here follow the prescription , which thou didst not take , o my son , my heir , and my pride ! because thy fond mother have her mother 's favourite powder , on which in his infantine trouble our firstborn be dutifully nurture . do word not exactly consonant with truth pass between the lady in their correspondence ? I fear my Lady Theo be not altogether candid : else how to account for a phrase in one of Madam Esmond 's letter , who say : " I be glad to hear the powder have do the dear child good . they be , if not on a first , on a second or third application , almost infallible , and have be the blessed mean of relieve many person round I , both infant and adult , white and colour . I send my grandson an indian bow and arrow . shall these old eye never behold he at Castlewood , I wonder , and be Sir George so busy with his book and his politic that he can not   afford a few month to his mother in Virginia ? I be much alone now . my son 's chamber be just as he leave it : the same book be in the press : his little hanger and fowling - piece over the bed , and my father 's picture over the mantelpiece . I never allow anything to be alter in his room or his brother 's . I fancy the child play near I sometimes , and that I can see my dear father 's head as he doze in his chair . mine be grow almost as white as my father 's . be I never to behold my child ere I go hence ? the Lord 's will be do . " 




 CHAPTER LXXXVI . at home 

 such an appeal as this of our mother would have soften heart much less obdurate than our ; and we talk of a speedy visit to Virginia , and of hire all the Young Rachel 's cabin accommodation . but our child must fall ill , for whom the voyage would be dangerous , and from whom the mother of course could not part ; and the Young Rachel make her voyage without we that year . another year there be another difficulty , in my worship 's first attack of the gout   which occupy I a good deal , and afterwards certainly clear my wit and enliven my spirit ; and now come another much sad cause for delay in the sad news we receive from Jamaica . some two year after our establishment at the Manor , our dear General return from his government , a little rich in the world 's good than when he go away , but have undergo a loss for which no wealth could console he , and after which , indeed , he do not care to remain in the West Indies . my Theo 's poor mother the most tender and affectionate friend   save one   I have ever have die abroad of the fever . her last regret be that she should not be allow to live to see our child and ourselves in prosperity .
