 " she see we , though we do not see she ; and she thank you , George , for have be good to her child , " her husband say . 
 he , we think , would not be long ere he join she . his love for she have be the happiness and business of his whole life . to be away from she seem live no more . it be pitiable to watch the good man as he sate with we . my wife , in her air and in many tone and gesture , constantly recall her mother to the bereaved widower 's heart . what cheer we could give he in his calamity we offer ; but , especially , little Hetty be now , under Heaven , his chief support and consolation . she have refuse more than one advantageous match in the Island , the General tell we ; and on her return to England , my Lord Wrotham 's heir lay himself at her foot . but she love well to stay with her father , Hetty say . as long as he be not tired of she she care for no husband . 
 " Nay , " say we , when this last great match be propose , " let the General stay six month with we at the Manor here , and you can have he at Oakhurst for the other six . " 
 but Hetty declare her father never could bear Oakhurst again now that her mother be go ; and she would marry no man for his coronet and money not she ! the General , when we talk this matter over , say gravely that the child have no desire for marry , owe possibly to some disappointment in early life , of which she never speak ; and we , respect her feeling , be for our part equally silent . my brother Lambert have by this time a college live near to Winchester , and a wife of course to adorn his parsonage . we profess but a moderate degree of like for this lady , though we make she welcome when she come to we . her idea regard our poor Hetty 's determined celibacy be different to that which I have . this Mrs. Jack be a chatterbox of a woman , in the habit of speak her mind very freely , and of pride herself excessively on her skill in give pain to her friend . 
 " my dear Sir George , " she be pleased to say , " I have often and often tell our dear Theo that I would not   have a pretty sister in my house to make tea for Jack when I be upstairs , and always to be at hand when I be want in the kitchen or nursery , and always to be dress neat and in she good when I be very likely make pie or pudding or look to the child . I have every confidence in Jack , of course . I should like to see he look at another woman , indeed ! and so I have in Jemima but they do not   come together in my house when I be upstairs that I promise you ! and so I tell my sister Warrington . " 
 " be I to understand , " say the General , " that you have do my Lady Warrington the favour to warn she against her sister , my daughter Miss Hester ? " 
 " yes , pa , of course I have . a duty be a duty , and a woman be a woman , and a man 's a man , as I know very well . do not   tell I ! he be a man . every man be a man , with all his sanctify air ! " 
 " you yourself have a married sister , with whom you be stay when my son Jack first have the happiness of make your acquaintance ? " remark the General . 
 " yes , of course I have a married sister ; every one know that and I have be as good as a mother to her child , that I have ! " 
 " and be I to gather from your conversation that your attraction prove a powerful temptation for your sister 's husband ? " 
 " Law , General ! I do not   know how you can go for to say I ever say any such a thing ! " cry Mrs. Jack , red and voluble . 
 " do not   you perceive , my dear madam , that it be you who have insinuate as much , not only regard yourself , but regard my own two daughter ? " 
 " never , never , never , as I be a christian woman ! and it be most cruel of you to say so , sir . and I do say a sister be good out of the house , that I do ! and as Theo 's time be come , I warn she , that be all . " 
 " have you discover , my good madam , whether my poor Hetty have steal any of the spoon ? when I come to breakfast this morning , my daughter be alone , and there must have be a score of piece of silver on the table . " 
 " Law , sir ! who ever say a word about spoon ? do I ever accuse the poor dear ? if I do , may I drop down dead at this moment on this hearth - rug ! and I be not   use to be speak to in this way . and I and Jack have both remark it ; and I have   do my duty , that I have . " and here Mrs. Jack flounce out of the room , in tear . 
 " and have the woman have the impudence to tell you this , my child ? " ask the General , when Theo   who be a little delicate   come to the tea - table . 
 " she have tell I every day since she have be here . she come into my dressing - room to tell I . she come to my nursery , and say , ' ah , I would not   have a sister prowl about my nursery , that I would not . ' ah , how pleasant it be to have amiable and well - breed relative , say I. " 
 " Thy poor mother have be spar this woman , " groan the General . 
 " our mother would have make she well , papa , " say Theo , kiss he . 
 " yes , dear . " and I see that both of they be at their prayer . 
 but this must be own , that to love one 's relative be not always an easy task ; to live with one 's neighbour be sometimes not amusing . from Jack Lambert 's demeanour next day , I could see that his wife have give he her version of the conversation . Jack be sulky , but not dignified . he be angry , but his anger do not prevent his appetite . he preach a sermon for we which be entirely stupid . and little Miles , once more in sable , sate at his grandfather 's side , his little hand place in that of the kind old man . 
 would he stay and keep house for we during our virginian trip ? the housekeeper should be put under the full domination of Hetty . the butler 's key should be hand over to he ; for Gumbo , not I think with an over good grace , be to come with we to Virginia : have , it must be premise , unite himself with Mrs. Molly in the bond of matrimony , and people a cottage in my park with sundry tawny Gumbos . under the care of our good General and his daughter we leave our house , then ; we travel to London , and thence to Bristol , and our obsequious agent there have the opportunity of declare that he should offer up prayer for our prosperity , and of vow that child so beautiful as ours   we have an infant by this time to accompany Miles   be never see on any ship before . we make a voyage without accident . how strange the feeling be as we land from our boat at Richmond ! a coach and a host of negro be there in wait to receive we ; and hard by a gentleman on horseback , with negro in our livery , too , who spring from his horse and rush up to embrace we . not a little charm be both of we to see our dear Hal . he ride with we to our mother 's door . yonder she stand on the step to welcome we ; and Theo kneel down to ask her blessing . 
 Harry ride in the coach with we as far as our mother 's house ; but would not , as he say , spoil sport by enter with we . " she see I , " he own , " and we be pretty good friend ; but Fanny and she be well apart ; and there be no love lose between them , I can promise you . come over to I at the Tavern , George , when thou art free . and to - morrow I shall have the honour to present her sister to Theo . ' twas only from happen to be in town yesterday that I hear the ship be signal , and wait to see you . I have send a negro boy home to my wife , and she will be here to pay her respect to my Lady Warrington . " and Harry , after this brief greeting , jump out of the carriage , and leave we to meet our mother alone . 
 since I part from she I have see a great deal of fine company , and Theo and I have pay our respect to the King and Queen at St. James 's ; but we have see no more stately person than this who welcome we , and raise my wife from her knee , embrace she and lead she into the house . ' twas a plain , wood - build place , with a gallery round , as our virginian house be ; but if it have be a palace , with a little empress inside , our reception could not have be more courteous . there be old Nathan , still the major - domo , a score of kind black face of black , grin welcome . some whose name I remember as child be grow out of remembrance , to be sure , to be buxom lad and lass ; and some I have leave with black pate be grizzle now with snowy poll : and some who be bear since my time be peer at doorway with their great eye and little naked foot . it be , " I be little Sip , Master George ! " and " I be Dinah , Sir George ! " and " I be Master Miles 's boy ! " say a little chap in a new livery and boot of nature 's black . Ere the day be over the whole household have find a pretext for pass before we , and grin and bow and make we welcome . I do not   know how many repast be serve to we . in the evening my Lady Warrington have to receive all the gentry of the little town , which she do with perfect grace and good - humour , and I have to shake hand with a few old acquaintance old enemy I be go to say ; but I have come into a fortune and be no long a naughty prodigal . why , a drove of fat calf be kill in my honour ! my poor Hal be of the entertainment , but gloomy and crestfallen . his mother speak to he , but it be as a queen to a rebellious prince , her son who be not yet forgive . we two slip away from the company , and go up to the room assign to I : but there , as we begin a free conversation , our mother , taper in hand , appear with her pale face . do I want anything ? be everything quite as I wish it ? she have peep in at the dear child , who be sleep like cherub . how she do caress they , and delight over they ! how she be charm with Miles 's dominating air , and the little Theo 's smile and dimple ! " supper be just come on the table , Sir George . if you like our cookery well than the tavern , Henry , I beg you to stay . " what a different welcome there be in the word and tone address to each of we ! Hal hang down his head , and follow to the low room . a clergyman beg a blessing on the meal . he touch with not a little art and eloquence upon our arrival at home , upon our safe passage across the stormy water , upon the love and forgiveness which await we in the mansion of the Heavenly Parent when the storm of life be over . 
 here be a new clergyman , quite unlike some whom I remember about we in early day , and I praise he , but Madam Esmond shake her head . she be afraid his principle be very dangerous : she be afraid other have adopt those dangerous principle . have I not see the paper sign by the burgess and merchant at Williamsburg the year before the Lees , Randolphs , Bassets , Washingtons , and the like , and oh , my dear , that I should have to say it , our name , that is , your brother 's   by what influence I do not like to say , and this unhappy Mr. Belman 's who beg a blessing last night ? 
 if there have be quarrel in our little colonial society when I leave home , what be these to the feud I find rag on my return ? we have send the Stamp Act to America , and be force to repeal it . then we must try a new set of duty on glass , paper , and what not , and repeal that Act too , with the exception of a duty on tea . from Boston to Charleston the tea be confiscate . even my mother , loyal as she be , give up her favourite drink ; and my poor wife would have have to forgo her , but we have bring a quantity for our private drinking on board ship , which have pay four time as much duty at home . not that I for my part would have hesitate about pay duty . the home Government must have some mean of revenue , or its pretension to authority be idle . they say the colony be try and tyrannise over ; I say the home Government be try and tyrannise over .   ' Tis but an affair of argument and history , now ; we try the question , and be beat ; and the matter be settle as completely as the conquest of Britain by the Normans .   and all along , from conviction I trust , I own to have take the british side of the quarrel . in that brief and unfortunate experience of war which I have have in my early life , the universal cry of the army and well - affect person be , that Mr. Braddock 's expedition have fail , and defeat and disaster have fall upon we in consequence of the remissness , the selfishness , and the rapacity of many of the very people for whose defence against the french arm have be take up . the colonist be for have all do for they , and for do nothing , they make extortionate bargain with the champion who come to defend they ; they fail in contract ; they furnish niggardly supply ; they multiply delay until the hour for beneficial action be past , and until the catastrophe come which never need have occur but for their ill - will . what shout of joy be there , and what ovation for the great british Minister who have devise and effect the conquest of Canada ! Monsieur de Vaudreuil say justly that that conquest be the signal for the defection of the north american colony from their allegiance to Great Britain ; and my Lord Chatham , have do his good to achieve the first part of the scheme , contribute more than any man in England towards the completion of it . the colony be insurgent , and he applaud their rebellion . what score of thousand of waverer must he have encourage into resistance ! it be a general who say to an army in revolt , " God save the king ! my man , you have a right to mutiny ! " no wonder they set up his statue in this town , and his picture in t'other ; whilst here and there they hang Ministers and Governors in effigy . to our virginian town of Williamsburg , some wiseacre must subscribe to bring over a portrait of my lord , in the habit of a roman orator speak in the Forum , to be sure , and point to the palace of Whitehall , and the special window out of which Charles I. be behead ! here be a neat allegory , and a pretty compliment to a british statesman ! I hear , however , that my lord 's head be paint from a bust , and so be take off without his knowledge . 
 now my country be England , not America or Virginia ; and I take , or rather take , the english side of the dispute . my sympathy have always be with home , where I be now a squire and a citizen : but have my lot be to plant tobacco , and live on the bank of James River or Potomac , no doubt my opinion have be alter . when , for instance , I visit my brother at his new house and plantation , I find he and his wife as staunch Americans as we be british . we have some word upon the matter in dispute , who have not in those troublesome time ? but our argument be carry on without rancour ; even my new sister could not bring we to that , though she do she good when we be together , and in the curtain lecture which I have no doubt she inflict on her spouse , like a notable housewife as she be . but we trust in each other so entirely that even Harry 's duty towards his wife would not make he quarrel with his brother . he love I from old time , when my word be law with he ; he still protest that he and every virginian gentleman of his side be loyal to the Crown . War be not declare as yet , and gentleman of different opinion be courteous enough to one another . Nay , at our public dinner and festival , the health of the King be still ostentatiously drunk ; and the assembly of every colony , though prepare for Congress , though resist all attempt at taxation on the part of the home authority , be loud in its expression of regard for the King our Father , and pathetic in its appeal to that paternal sovereign to put away evil counsellor from he , and listen to the voice of moderation and reason . up to the last , our virginian gentry be a grave , orderly , aristocratic folk , with the strong sense of their own dignity and station . in later day , and nearer home , we have hear of fraternisation and equality . amongst the great folk of our Old World I have never see a gentleman stand more on his dignity and maintain it well than Mr. Washington : no not the King against whom he take arm . in the eye of all the gentry of the french court , who gaily join in the crusade against we , and so take their revenge for Canada , the great american chief always appear as anax andron , and they allow that his well could not be see in Versailles itself . though they be quarrel with the Governor , the gentleman of the House of Burgesses still maintain amicable relation with he , and exchange dignified courtesy . when my Lord Bottetourt arrive , and hold his court at Williamsburg in no small splendour and state , all the gentry wait upon he , Madam Esmond include . and at his death , Lord Dunmore , who succeed he , and bring a fine family with he , be treat with the utmost respect by our gentry privately , though publicly the House of Assembly and the Governor be at war . 
 their quarrel be a matter of history , and concern I personally only so far as this , that our burgess be convene for the 1st of March in the year after my arrival in Virginia , it be agree that we should all pay a visit to our capital , and our duty to the Governor . since Harry 's unfortunate marriage Madam Esmond have not perform this duty , though always previously accustom to pay it ; but now that her eld son be arrive in the colony , my mother opine that we must certainly wait upon his Excellency the Governor , nor be we sorry , perhaps , to get away from our little Richmond to enjoy the gaiety of the provincial capital . Madam engage , and at a great price , the good house to be have at Richmond for herself and her family . now I be rich , her generosity be curious . I have more than once to interpose   her old servant likewise wonder at her new way of life , and beg she not to be so lavish . but she gently say , in former day she have occasion to save , which now exist no more . Harry have enough , sure , with such a wife as he have take out of the housekeeper 's room . if she choose to be a little extravagant now , why should she hesitate ? she have not her dear daughter and grandchild with she every day   she fall in love with all three of they , and spoil they as much as they be capable of be spoil . besides , in former day I could not accuse she of too much extravagance , and this I think be almost the only allusion she make to the pecuniary difference between we . so she have her people dress in their good , and her good wine , plate , and furniture from Castlewood by sea at no small charge , and her dress in which she have be marry in George II . 's reign , and we all flatter ourselves that our coach make the great figure of any except his Excellency 's , and we engage Signor Formicalo , his Excellency 's major - domo , to superintend the series of feast that be give in my honour ; and more fleshpot be set a - stew in our kitchen in one month , our servant say , than have be know in the family since the young gentleman go away . so great be Theo 's influence over my mother , that she actually persuade she , that year , to receive our sister Fanny , Hal 's wife , who would have stay upon the plantation rather than face Madam Esmond . but , trust to Theo 's promise of amnesty , Fanny   to whose house we have pay more than one visit   come up to town , and make her curtsey to Madam Esmond , and be forgive . and rather than be forgive in that way , I own , for my part , that I would prefer perdition or utter persecution . 
 " you know these , my dear ? " say Madam Esmond , point to her fine silver sconce . " Fanny hath often clean they when she be with I at Castlewood . and this dress , too , Fanny know , I dare say ? her poor mother have the care of it . I always have the great confidence in she . " 
 here there be wrath flash from Fanny 's eye , which our mother , who have forgive she , do not perceive not she ! 
 " oh , she be a treasure to I ! " Madam resume . " I never should have nurse my boy through their illness but for your mother 's admirable care of they . Colonel Lee , permit I to present you to my daughter , my Lady Warrington . her ladyship be a neighbour of your relative the Bunburys at home . here come his Excellency . welcome , my lord ! " 
 and our princess perform before his lordship one of those curtsey of which she be not a little proud ; and I fancy I see some of the company venture to smile . 
 " by George ! madam , " say Mr. Lee , ' since Count Borulawski , I have not see a bow so elegant as your ladyship 's . " 
 " and pray , sir , who be Count Borulawski ? " ask Madam . 
 " he be a nobleman high in favour with his polish Majesty , " reply Mr. Lee . " may I ask you , madam , to present I to your distinguished son ? " 
 " this be Sir George Warrington , " say my mother , point to I . 
 " Pardon I , madam . I mean Captain Warrington , who be by Mr. Wolfe 's side when he die . I have be content to share his fate , so I have be near he . " 
 and the ardent Lee swagger up to Harry , and take his hand with respect , and pay he a compliment or two , which make I , at least , pardon he for his late impertinence ; for my dear Hal walk gloomily through his mother 's room in his old uniform of the famous corps which he have quit . 
 we have have many meeting , which the stern mother could not interrupt , and in which that instinctive love which bind we to one another , and which nothing could destroy , have opportunity to speak . entirely unlike each other in our pursuit , our taste , our opinion his life be one of eager exercise , active sport , and all the amusement of the field , while mine be to dawdle over book and spend my time in languid self - contemplation we have , nevertheless , have such a sympathy as almost pass the love of woman . my poor Hal confess as much to I , for his part , in his artless manner , when we go away without wife or womankind , except a few negro leave in the place , and pass a week at Castlewood together . 
 the lady do not love each other . I know enough of my Lady Theo , to see after a very few glance whether or not she take a liking to another of her amiable sex . all my power of persuasion or command fail to change the stubborn creature 's opinion . have she ever say a word against Mrs. this or Miss that ? not she ! have she be otherwise than civil ? no , assuredly ! my Lady Theo be polite to a beggar - woman , treat her kitchenmaid like duchess , and murmur a compliment to the dentist for his elegant manner of pull her tooth out . she would black my boot , or clean the grate , if I ordain it   always look like a duchess the while ; but as soon as I say to she , " my dear creature , be fond of this lady , or t'other ! " all obedience cease ; she execute the most refined curtsey ; smile and kiss even to order ; but perform that mysterious undefinable freemasonic signal , which pass between woman , by which each know that the other hat she . so , with regard to Fanny , we have meet at her house , and at other . I remember she affectionately from old day , I fully credit poor Hal 's violent protest and tearful oath , that , by George , it be our mother 's persecution which make he marry she . he could not   stand by and see a poor thing torture as she be , without come to her rescue ; no , by heaven , he could not ! I say I believe all this ; and have for my sister - in - law a genuine compassion , as well as an early regard ; and yet I have no love to give she ; and , in reply to Hal 's passionate outbreak in praise of her beauty and worth , and eager query to I whether I do not think she a perfect paragon ? I could only answer with faint compliment or vague approval , feel all the while that I be disappoint my poor ardent fellow , and curse inwardly that revolt against flattery and falsehood into which I sometimes frantically rush . why should I not say , " yes dear Hal , thy wife be a paragon ; her singing be delightful , her hair and shape be beautiful ; " as I might have say by a little common stretch of politeness ? why could I not cajole this or that stupid neighbour or relative , as I have hear Theo do a thousand time , find all sort of lively prattle to amuse they , whilst I sit before they dumb and gloomy ? I say it be a sin not to have more word to say in praise of Fanny . we ought to have praise she , we ought to have like she . my Lady Warrington certainly ought to have like she , for she can play the hypocrite , and I can not . and there be this young creature pretty , graceful , shape like a nymph , with beautiful black eye and we care for they no more than for two gooseberry ! at Warrington my wife and I , when we pretend to compare note , elaborately compliment each other on our new sister 's beauty . what lovely eye ! oh yes ! what a sweet little dimple on her chin ! ah oui ! what wonderful little foot ! perfectly Chinese ! where should we in London get slipper small enough for she ? and , these compliment exhaust , we know that we do not like Fanny the value of one penny - piece ; we know that we dislike she ; we know that we ha ... well , what hypocrite woman be ! we hear from many quarter how eagerly my brother have take up the new anti - english opinion , and what a champion he be of so - call american right and freedom . " it be she do , my dear , " say I to my wife . " if I have say so much , I be sure you would have scold I , " say my Lady Warrington , laugh : and I do straightway begin to scold she , and say it be most cruel of she to suspect our new sister ; and what earthly right have we to do so ? but I say again , I know Madam Theo so well , that when once she have get a prejudice against a person in her little head , not all the king 's horse nor all the king 's man will get it out again . I vow nothing would induce she to believe that Harry be not henpecke nothing . 
 well , we go to Castlewood together without the woman , and stay at the dreary , dear old place , where we have be so happy , and I , at least , so gloomy . it be winter , and duck - time , and Harry go away to the river , and shoot dozen and score and bushel of canvasback , whilst I remain in my grandfather 's library amongst the old mouldering book which I love in my childhood which I see in a dim vision still rest on a little boy 's lap , as he sit by an old white - head gentleman 's knee . I read my book ; I sleep in my own bed and room religiously keep , as my mother tell I , and leave as on the day when I go to Europe . Hal 's cheery voice would wake I , as of old . like all man who love to go a - field , he be an early riser : he would come and wake I , and sit on the foot of the bed and perfume the air with his morning pipe , as the house negro lay great log on the fire . it be a happy time ! Old Nathan have tell I of cunning crypt where ancestral rum and claret be deposit . we have have care , struggle , battle , bitter grief , and disappointment ; we be boy again as we sit there together . I be a boy now even as I think of the time . 
 that unlucky tea - tax , which alone of the taxis lately impose upon the colony , the home Government be determine to retain , be meet with defiance throughout America . ' tis true we pay a shilling in the pound at home , and ask only threepence from Boston or Charleston ; but as a question of principle , the impost be refuse by the province , which indeed ever show a most spirited determination to pay as little as they could help . in Charleston the tea - ship be unload , and the cargo store in cellar . from New York and Philadelphia , the vessel be turn back to London . in Boston   where there be an armed force , whom the inhabitant be perpetually mobbing , certain patriot , paint and disguise as Indians , board the ship , and fling the obnoxious cargoe into the water . the wrath of our white Father be kindle against this city of Mohocks in masquerade . the notable Boston Port Bill be bring forward in the British House of Commons ; the port be closed , and the Custom House remove to Salem . the Massachusetts Charter be annul ; and , in just apprehension that riot might ensue , in deal with the perpetrator of which the colonial court might be lead to act partially , Parliament decreed that person indict for act of violence and armed resistance , might be send home , or to another colony , for trial . if such act set all America in a flame , they certainly drive all wellwisber of our country into a fury . I might have sentence Master Miles Warrington , at five year old , to a whipping , and he would have cry , take down his little small - clothe and submit : but suppose I offer   and he richly deserve it   to chastise Captain Miles of the Prince 's dragoon ? he would whirl my paternal cane out of my hand , box my hair - powder out of my ear . Lord a - mercy ! I tremble at the very idea of the controversy ? he would assert his independence in a word ; and if , I say , I think the home Parliament have a right to levy taxis in the colony , I own that we take mean most captious , most insolent , most irritate , and , above all , most impotent , to assert our claim . 
 my Lord Dunmore , our Governor of Virginia , upon Lord Bottetourt 's death , receive I into some intimacy soon after my arrival in the colony , be willing to live on good term with all our gentry . my mother 's severe loyalty be no secret to he ; indeed , she wave the king 's banner in all company , and talk so loudly and resolutely , that Randolph and Patrick Henry himself be strike dumb before she . it be Madam Esmond 's celebrated reputation for loyalty   his Excellency laughingly tell I   which induce he to receive her eld son to grace . 
 " I have have the bad character of you from home , " his lordship say . " little bird whisper to I , Sir George , that you be a man of the most dangerous principle . you be a friend of Mr. Wilkes and Alderman Beckford . I be not sure you have not be at Medmenham Abbey . you have live with player , poet , and all sort of wild people . I have be warn against you , sir , and I find you " 
 " not so black as I have be paint , " I interrupt his lordship , with a smile . 
 " faith , " say my lord , " if I tell Sir George Warrington that he seem to I a very harmless , quiet gentleman , and that ' tis a great relief to I to talk to he amidst these loud politician ; these lawyer with their perpetual noise about Greece and Rome ; these virginian squire who be for ever profess their loyalty and respect , whilst they be shake their fist in my face I hope nobody overhear we , " say my lord , with an arch smile , " and nobody will carry my opinion home . " 
 his lordship 's ill opinion have be remove by a well knowledge of I , our acquaintance daily grow more intimate ; and , especially between the lady of his family and my own , a close friendship arise between they and my wife at least . Hal 's wife , receive kindly at the little provincial court , as all lady be , make herself by no mean popular there by the hot and eager political tone which she adopt . she assail all the Government measure with indiscriminate acrimony . be they lenient ? she say the perfidious British Government be only prepare a snare , and bid its time until it could forge heavy chain for unhappy America . be they angry ? why do not every american citizen rise , assert his right as a freeman , and serve every british governor , officer , soldier , as they have treat the East India Company 's tea ? my mother , on the other hand , be pleased to express her opinion with equal frankness , and , indeed , to press her advice upon his Excellency with a volubility which may have fatigue that representative of the Sovereign . call out the militia ; send for fresh troop from New York , from home , from anywhere ; lock up the Capitol !   this advice be follow , it must be own   and send every one of the ringleader amongst those wicked burgess to prison ! be Madam Esmond 's daily counsel to the Governor by word and letter . and if not only the burgess , but the burgess ' wife could have be lead off to punishment and captivity , I think this Brutus of a woman would scarce have appeal against the sentence . 




 CHAPTER LXXXVII . the last of God save the king 

 what perverse law of Fate be it that ever place I in a minority ? should a law be propose to hand over this realm to the Pretender of Rome , or the Grand Turk , and submit it to the new sovereign 's religion , it might pass , as I should certainly be vote against it . at home in Virginia , I find myself disagree with everybody as usual . by the Patriots I be vote   as indeed I profess myself to be   a tory ; by the Tories I be presently declare to be a dangerous Republican . the time be utterly out of joint . o curse spite ! Ere I have be a year in Virginia , how I wish myself back by the bank of the Waveney ! but the aspect of affair be so troublous , that I could not leave my mother , a lone lady , to face possible war and disaster , nor would she quit the country at such a juncture , nor should a man of spirit leave it . at his Excellency 's table , and over his Excellency 's plentiful claret , that point be agree on by number of the well - affect , that vow be vow over countless brimming bumper . no : it be statue signum , signifer ! we Cavaliers would all rally round it ; and at these time , our Governor talk like the brave of the brave . 
 now , I will say , of all my virginian acquaintance , Madam Esmond be the most consistent . our gentlefolk have come in number to Williamsburg ; and a great number of they propose to treat her Excellency , the Governor 's lady , to a ball , when the news reach we of the Boston Port Bill . Straightway the House of Burgesses adopt an indignant protest against this measure of the British Parliament , and decree a solemn day of fast and humiliation throughout the country , and of solemn prayer to Heaven to avert the calamity of Civil War . meanwhile , the invitation to my Lady Dunmore have be already give and accept , the gentleman agree that their ball should take place on the appoint evening , and then sackcloth and ash should be assume some day afterwards . 
 " a ball ! " say Madam Esmond . " I go to a ball which be give by a set of rebel who be go publicly to insult his Majesty a week afterwards ! I will die soon ! " and she write to the gentleman who be steward for the occasion to say , that view the dangerous state of the country , she , for her part , could not think of attend a ball . 
 what be her surprise then , the next time she go abroad in her chair , to be cheer by a hundred person , white and black , and shout of " Huzzah , Madam ! " " Heaven bless your ladyship ! " they evidently think her patriotism have cause her determination not to go to the ball . 
 Madam , that there should be no mistake , put her head out of the chair , and cry out " God save the king " as loud as she can . the people cry " God save the king , " too . everybody cry " God save the king " in those day . on the night of that entertainment , my poor Harry , as a Burgess of the House , and one of the giver of the feast , don his uniform red coat of Wolfe 's   which he so soon be to exchange for another colour , and go off with Madam Fanny to the ball . my Lady Warrington and her humble servant , as be stranger in the country , and english people as it be , be permit by Madam to attend the assembly from which she of course absent herself . I have the honour to dance a country - dance with the lady of Mount Vernon , whom I find a most lively , pretty , and amiable partner ; but be bind to say that my wife 's praise of she be receive with a very grim acceptance by my mother , when Lady Warrington come to recount the event of the evening . could not Sir George Warrington have dance with my Lady Dunmore or her daughter , or with anybody but Mrs. Washington ; to be sure the Colonel think so well of himself and his wife , that no doubt he consider she the grand lady in the room ; and she who remember he a road - surveyor at a guinea a day ! well , indeed ! there be no measure the pride of these provincial upstart , and as for this gentleman , my Lord Dunmore 's partiality for he have evidently turn his head . I do not know about Mr. Washington 's pride , I know that my good mother never could be get to love he or anything that be his . 
 she be no well pleased with he for go to the ball , than with his conduct three day afterwards , when the day of fast and humiliation be appoint , and when he attend the service which our new clergyman perform . she invite Mr. Belman to dinner that day , and sundry colonial authority . the clergyman excuse himself . Madam Esmond toss up her head , and say he might do as he like . she make a parade of a dinner ; she light her house up at night , when all the rest of the city be in darkness and gloom ; she beg Mr. Hardy , one of his Excellency 's aide - de - camp , to sing " God save the king , " to which the people in the street outside listened , think that it might be a part of some religious service which Madam be celebrate ; but then she call for " Britons , strike home ! " which the simple young gentleman just from Europe begin to perform , when a great yell arise in the street , and a large stone , fling from some rebellious hand , plump into the punch - bowl before I , and scatter it and its content about our dining - room . 
 my mother go to the window nothing daunt . I can see her rigid little figure now , as she stand with a toss - up head , outstretche frille arm , and the twinkle star for a background , and sing in chorus , " Britons , strike home ! strike home ! " the crowd in front of the paling shout and roar , " Silence ! for shame ! go back ! " but she will not go back , not she . " fling more stone , if you dare ! " say the brave little lady ; and more might have come , but some gentleman issue out of the Raley Tavern interpose with the crowd . " you must not   insult a lady , " say a voice I think I know . " Huzza , Colonel ! Hurrah , Captain ! God bless your honour ! " say the people in the street . and thus the enemy be pacify . 
 my mother , protest that the whole disturbance be over , would have have Mr. Hardy sing another song , but he give a sickly grin , and say , " he really do not like to sing to such accompaniment , " and the concert for that evening be end ; though I be bind to say that some scoundrel return at night , frighten my poor wife almost out of wit , and break every single window in the front of our tenement . " Britons , strike home ! " be a little too much ; Madam should have content herself with " God save the king . " Militia be drill , bullet be cast , supply of ammunition get ready , cunning plan for disappoint the royal ordinance devise and carry out ; but , to be sure , " God save the king " be the cry everywhere , and in reply to my objection to the gentleman - patriot , " why , you be scheme for a separation ; you be bring down upon you the inevitable wrath of the great power in the world ! " the answer to I always be , " we mean no separation at all ; we yield to no man in loyalty ; we glory in the name of Britons , " and so forth , and so forth . the powder - barrel be heap in the cellar , the train be lay , but Mr. Fawkes be persistent in his dutiful petition to King and Parliament and mean no harm , not he ! ' tis true when I speak of the power of our country , I imagine she would exert it ; that she would not expect to overcome three million of fellow - Britons on their own soil with a few battalion , a half - dozen general from Bond Street , and a few thousand bravo hire out of Germany . as if we want to insult the thirteen colony as well as to subdue they , we must set upon they these horde of Hessians , and the murderer out of the indian wigwam . be our great quarrel not to be fight without tali auxilio and istis defensoribus ? ah ! ' tis easy , now we be worst , to look over the map of the great empire wrest from we , and show how we ought not to have lose it . Long Island ought to have exterminate Washington 's army ; he ought never to have come out of Valley Forge except as a prisoner . the South be ours after the battle of Camden , but for the inconceivable meddling of the Commander - in - chief at New York , who paralyse the exertion of the only capable British General who appear during the war , and send he into that miserable cul - de - sac at York Town , whence he could only issue defeat and a prisoner . oh , for a week more ! a day more , an hour more of darkness or light ! in read over our american campaign from their unhappy commencement to their inglorious end , now that we be able to see the enemy 's movement and condition as well as our own , I fancy we can see how an advance , a march , might have put enemy into our power who have no means to withstand it , and change the entire issue of the struggle . but it be ordain by Heaven , and for the good , as we can now have no doubt , of both empire , that the great Western Republic should separate from we : and the gallant soldier who fight on her side , their indomitable and heroic Chief above all , have the glory of face and overcome , not only veteran soldier amply provide and inure to war , but wretchedness , cold , hunger , dissension , treason within their own camp , where all must have go to rack , but for the pure unquenchable flame of patriotism that be for ever burn in the bosom of the heroic leader . what a constancy , what a magnanimity , what a surprising persistence against fortune ! Washington before the enemy be no well nor braver than hundred that fight with he or against he   who have not hear the repeat sneer against " Fabius " in which his factious captain be accustom to indulge ? , but Washington the Chief of a nation in arm , do battle with distracted party ; calm in the midst of conspiracy ; serene against the open foe before he and the dark enemy at his back ; Washington inspire order and spirit into troop hungry and in rag ; sting by ingratitude , but betray no anger , and ever ready to forgive ; in defeat invincible , magnanimous in conquest , and never so sublime as on that day when he lay down his victorious sword and seek his noble retirement : here indeed be a character to admire and revere ; a life without a stain , a fame without a flaw . quando invenie parem ? in that more extensive work , which I have plan and partly write on the subject of this great war , I hope I have do justice to the character of its great leader .   and I trust that in the opinion I have record regard he , I have show that I also can be just and magnanimous towards those who view I personally with no favour . for my brother Hal be at Mount Vernon , and always eager to bring I and his beloved Chief on good term , show his Excellency some of the early sheet of my history . General Washington   who read but few book , and have not the slight pretension to literary taste   remark , " if you will have my opinion , my dear General , I think Sir George 's project work , from the speciman I have of it , be certain to offend both party . " G. E. W. . and this from the sheer force of respect which his eminent virtue extort . with the young Mr. Washington of my own early day I have not the honour to enjoy much sympathy : though my brother , whose character be much more frank and affectionate than mine , be always his fast friend in early time , when they be equal , as in latter day when the General , as I do own and think , be all mankind 's superior . 
 I have mention that contrariety in my disposition , and , perhaps , in my brother 's , which somehow place we on wrong side in the quarrel which ensue , and which from this time forth rag for five year , until the mother country be fain to acknowledge her defeat . Harry should have be the Tory , and I the Whig . theoretically my opinion be very much more liberal than those of my brother , who , especially after his marriage , become what our indian nabob call a Bahadoor a person ceremonious , stately , and exact respect . when my Lord Dunmore , for instance , talk about liberate the negro , so as to induce they to join the King 's standard , Hal be for hang the Governor and the Black Guards   as he call they   whom his Excellency have crimp . " if you , gentleman be fight for freedom , " say I , ' sure the negro may fight , too . " on which Harry roar out , shake his fist , " infernal villain , if I meet any of them , they shall die by this hand ! " and my mother agree that this idea of a negro insurrection be the most abominable and parricidal notion which have ever spring up in her unhappy country . she at least be more consistent than brother Hal . she would have black and white obedient to the power that be : whereas Hal only could admit that freedom be the right of the latter colour . 
 as a proof of her argument , Madam Esmond and Harry too would point to an instance in our own family in the person of Mr. Gumbo . have get his freedom from I , as a reward for his admirable love and fidelity to I when time be hard , Gumbo , on his return to Virginia , be scarce a welcome guest in his old quarter , amongst my mother 's servant . he be free , and they be not : he be , as it be , a centre of insurrection . he give himself no small air of protection and consequence amongst they ; brag of his friend in Europe   " at home , " as he call it , and his doing there ; and for a while bring the household round about he to listen to he and admire he , like the monkey who have see the world . now , Sady , Hal 's boy , who go to America of his own desire , be not free . hence jealousy between he and Mr. Gum ; and battle , in which they both practise the noble art of boxing and butting , which they have learn at Marybone Gardens and Hockley - in - the - Hole . nor be Sady the only jealous person : almost all my mother 's servant hat Signor Gumbo for the air which he give himself ; and I be sorry to say , that our faithful Molly , his wife , be as jealous as his old fellow - servant . the black could not pardon she for have demean herself so far as to marry one of their kind . she meet with no respect , could exercise no authority , come to her mistress with ceaseless complaint of the idleness , knavery , lie , steal of the black people ; and finally with a story of jealousy against a certain Dinah , or Diana , who , I heartily trust , be as innocent as her namesake the moonlight visitant of endymion . now , on the article of morality Madam Esmond be a very draconess ; and a person accuse be a person guilty . she make charge against Mr. Gumbo to which he reply with asperity . forget that he be a free gentleman , my mother now order Gumbo to be whip , on which molly fly at her ladyship , all her wrath at her husband 's infidelity vanishing at the idea of the indignity put upon he ; there be a rebellion in our house at Castlewood . a quarrel take place between I and my mother , as I take my man 's side . Hal and Fanny side with she , on the contrary ; and in so far the difference do good , as it bring about some little intimacy between Madam and her young child . this little difference be speedily heal ; but it be clear that the Standard of Insurrection must be remove out of our house ; and we determine that Mr. Gumbo and his lady should return to Europe . 
 my wife and I would willingly have go with they , God wot , for our boy sicken and lose his strength , and catch the fever in our swampy country ; but at this time she be expect to lie in   of our son Henry , and she know , too , that I have promise to stay in Virginia . it be agree that we should send the two back ; but when I offer Theo to go , she say her place be with her husband ; her father and Hetty at home would take care of our child ; and she scarce would allow I to see a tear in her eye whilst she be make her preparation for the departure of her little one . dost thou remember the time , madam , and the silence round the worktable , as the pile of little shirt be make ready for the voyage ? and the stealthy visit to the child 's chamber whilst they be asleep and yet with you ? and the terrible time of parting , as our barge with the servant and child row to the ship , and you stand on the shore ? have the Prince of Wales be go on that voyage , he could not have be well provide . where , sirrah , be the tompion watch your grandmother give you ? and how do you survive the box of cake which the good lady stow away in your cabin ? 
 the ship which take out my poor Theo 's child , return with the Reverend Mr. Hagan and my Lady Maria on board , who meekly choose to resign her rank , and be know in the colony   which be not to be a colony very long   only as Mrs. Hagan . at the time when I be in favour with my Lord Dunmore , a live fall vacant in Westmoreland county , he give it to our kinsman , who arrive in Virginia time enough to christen our boy Henry , and to preach some sermon on the then gloomy state of affair , which Madam Esmond pronounce to be prodigious fine . I think my Lady Maria win Madam 's heart by insist on go out of the room after she . " my father , your brother , be an earl , ' tis true , " say she , " but you know your ladyship be a marquis 's daughter , and I never can think of take precedence of you ! " so fond do Madam become of her niece , that she even allow Hagan to read play my own humble composition amongst other and be fairly force to own that there be merit in the tragedy of Pocahontas , which our parson deliver with uncommon energy and fire . 
 Hal and his wife come but rarely to Castlewood and Richmond when the chaplain and his lady be with we . Fanny be very curt and rude with Maria , use to giggle and laugh strangely in her company , and repeatedly remind she of her age , to our mother 's astonishment , who would often ask , be there any cause of quarrel between her niece and her daughter - in - law ? I keep my own counsel on these occasion , and be often not a little touch by the meekness with which the eld lady bear her persecution . Fanny love to torture she in her husband 's presence   who , poor fellow , be also in happy ignorance about his wife 's early history , and the other bear her agony , wince as little as might be . I sometimes would remonstrate with Madam Harry , and ask she be she a Red Indian , that she torture her victim so ? " have not I have torture enough in my time ? " say the young lady , and look as though she be determined to pay back the injury inflict on she . 
 " Nay , " say I , " you be breed in our wigwam , and I do not   remember anything but kindness ! " 
 " kindness ! " cry she . " no slave be ever treat as I be . the blow which wound most , often be those which never be aim . the people who hate we be not those we have injure . " 
 I think of little Fanny in our early day , silent , smile , willing to run and do all our bidding for we , and I grieve for my poor brother , who have take this sly creature into his bosom . 




 CHAPTER LXXXVIII . Yankee Doodle come to Town 

 one of the us to which we put America in the day of our british dominion be to make it a refuge for our sinner . besides convict and assign servant whom we transport to our colony , we discharge on their shore scapegrace and young son , for whom dissipation , despair , and bailiff make the old country uninhabitable . and as Mr. Cook , in his voyage , make his newly discover islander present of english animal   and other specimen of european civilisation , we use to take care to send sample of our black sheep over to the colony , there to browse as well they might , and propagate their precious breed . I myself be perhaps a little guilty in this matter , in busy myself to find a living in America for the worthy Hagan , husband of my kinswoman , at least be guilty in so far as this , that as we could get he no employment in England , we be glad to ship he to Virginia , and give he a colonial pulpit - cushion to thump . he demean himself there as a brave honest gentleman , to be sure ; he do his duty thoroughly by his congregation , and his king too ; and in so far do credit to my small patronage . Madam Theo use to urge this when I confide to she my scruple of conscience on this subject , and show , as her custom be and be , that my conduct in this , as in all other matter , be dictate by the high principle of morality and honour . but would I have give Hagan our living at home , and select he and his wife to minister to our parish ? I fear not . I never have a doubt of our cousin 's sincere repentance ; but I think I be secretly glad when she go to work it out in the wilderness . and I say this , acknowledge my pride and my error . twice , when I want they most , this kind Maria aid I with her sympathy and friendship . she bear her own distress courageously , and soothe those of other with admirable affection and devotion . and yet I , and some of mine   not Theo , would look down upon she . oh , for shame , for shame on our pride ! 
 my poor Lady Maria be not the only one of our family who be to be send out of the way to american wilderness . have borrow , steal , cheat at home , until he could cheat , borrow , and steal no more , the Honourable William Esmond , Esquire , be accommodate with a place at New York ; and his noble brother and royal master heartily desire that they might see he no more . when the trouble begin , we hear of the fellow and his doing in his new habitation . lie and mischief be his avant - courier wherever he travel . my Lord Dunmore inform I that Mr. Will declare publicly , that our estate of Castlewood be only our during his brother 's pleasure ; that his father , out of consideration for Madam Esmond , his lordship 's half - sister , have give she the place for life , and that he , William , be in negotiation with his brother , the present Lord Castlewood , for the purchase of the reversion of the estate ! we have the deed of gift in our strongroom at Castlewood , and it be furthermore register in due form at Williamsburg ; so that we be easy on that score . but the intention be everything ; and Hal and I promise , as soon as ever we meet Mr. William , to get from he a confirmation of this pretty story . what Madam Esmond 's feeling and expression be when she hear it , I need scarcely here particularise . " what ! my father , the Marquis of Esmond , be a liar , and I be a cheat , be I ? " cry my mother . " he will take my son 's property at my death , will he ? " and she be for writing , not only to Lord Castlewood in England , but to his Majesty himself at St. James 's , and be only prevent by my assurance that Mr. Will 's lie be notorious amongst all his acquaintance , and that we could not expect , in our own case , that he should be so inconsistent as to tell the truth . we hear of he presently as one of the loud amongst the Loyalists in New York , as Captain , and presently Major of a corps of volunteer who be send their address to the well - dispose in all the other colony , and announce their perfect readiness to die for the mother country . 
 we could not lie in a house without a whole window , and close the shutter of that unlucky mansion we have hire at Williamsburg , Madam Esmond leave our little capital , and my family return to Richmond , which also be desert by the member of the   dissolve   Assembly . Captain Hal and his wife return pretty early to their plantation ; and I , not a little annoyed at the course which event be take , divide my time pretty much between my own family and that of our Governor , who profess himself very eager to have my advice and company . there be the strong political difference , but as yet no actual personal quarrel . even after the dissolution of our House of Assembly   the member of which adjourn to a tavern , and there hold that famous meeting where , I believe , the idea of a congress of all the colony be first propose , the gentleman who be strong in opposition remain good friend with his Excellency , partook of his hospitality , and join he in excursion of pleasure . the session over , the gentry go home and have meeting in their respective county ; and the Assemblies in most of the other province have be also abruptly dissolve , it be agree everywhere that a general congress should be hold . Philadelphia , as the large and most important city on our continent , be select as the place of meeting ; and those celebrated conference begin , which be but the angry preface of war . we be still at God save the king ; we be still present our humble petition to the throne ; but when I go to visit my brother Harry at Fanny 's Mount   his new plantation lie not far from our , but with Rappahannock between we , and towards Mattaponey River , he ride out on business one morning , and I in the afternoon happen to ride too , and be tell by one of the groom that master be go towards Willis 's Ordinary ; in which direction , think no harm , I follow . and upon a clear place not far from Willis 's , as I advance out of the wood , I come on Captain Hal on horseback , with three- or four - and - thirty countryman round about he , arm with every sort of weapon , pike , scythe , fowling - piece , and musket ; and the Captain , with two or three likely young fellow as officer under he , put the man through their exercise . as I ride up a queer expression come over Hal 's face . " present arm ! " say he   and the army try to perform the salute as well they could . " Captain Cade , this be my brother , Sir George Warrington . " 
 " as a relation of your , Colonel , " say the individual address as captain , " the gentleman be welcome , " and he hold out a hand accordingly . 
 " and and a true friend to Virginia , " say Hal , with a redden face . 
 " yes , please God ! gentleman , " say I , on which the regiment give a hearty huzzay for the Colonel and his brother . the drill over , the officer , and the man too , be for adjourn to Willis 's and take some refreshment , but Colonel Hal say he could not drink with they that afternoon , and we trot homeward together . 
 " so , Hal , the cat be out of the bag ! " I say . 
 he give I a hard look . " I guess there be wilder cat in it . it must come to this , George . I say , you must not   tell Madam , " he add . 
 " Good God ! " I cry , " do you mean that with fellow such as those I see yonder , you and your friend be go to make fight against the great nation and the good army in the world ? " 
 " I guess we shall get an awful whipping , " say Hal , " and that be the fact . but then , George , " he add , with his sweet kind smile , " we be young , and a whipping or two may do we good . will not   it do we good , dolly , you old slut ? " and he give a playful touch with his whip to an old dog of all trade , that be run by he . 
 I do not try to urge upon he   I have do so in vain many time previously   our british side of the question , the side which appear to I to be the good . he be accustom to put off my reason by say , " all mighty well , brother , you speak as an Englishman , and have cast in your lot with your country , as I have with mine . " to this argument I own there be no answer , and all that remain for the disputant be to fight the matter out , when the strong be in the right . which have the right in the war of the last century ? the king or the parliament ? the side that be uppermost be the right , and on the whole much more humane in their victory than the Cavaliers would have be have they win . Nay , suppose we Tories have win the day in America ; how frightful and bloody that triumph would have be ! what rope and scaffold one imago , what noble head lay low ! a strange feeling this , I own ; I be on the Loyalist side , and yet want the whig to win . my brother Hal , on the other hand , who distinguish himself greatly with his regiment , never allow a word of disrespect against the enemy whom he oppose . " the officer of the british army , " he use to say , " be gentleman : at least , I have not hear that they be very much change since my time . there may be scoundrel and ruffian amongst the enemy 's troop ; I dare say we could find some such amongst our own . our business be to beat his Majesty 's force , not call they name ; any rascal can do that . " and from a name which Mr. Lee give my brother , and many of his rough horseman do not understand , Harry be often call " Chevaleer Baird " in the Continental army . he be a knight , indeed , without fear and without reproach . 
 as for the argument , " what could such people as those you be drill do against the british army ? " Hal have as confident answer . 
 " they can beat they , " say he , " Mr. George , that be what they can do . " 
 " great heaven ! " I cry , " do you mean with your company of Wolfe 's you would hesitate to attack five hundred such ? " 
 " with my company of the 67th , I would go anywhere . and , agree with you , that at this present moment I know more of soldiering than they ; but place I on that open ground where you find we , arm as you please , and half a dozen of my friend , with rifle , in the wood round about I ; which would get the well ? you know well , Mr. Braddock 's aide - de - camp ! " 
 there be no arguing with such a determination as this . " Thou know my way of thinking , Hal , " I say ; " and have surprise you at your work , I must tell my lord what I have see . " 
 " tell he , of course . you have see our county militia exercise . you will see as much in every colony from here to the Saint Lawrence or Georgia . as I be an old soldier , they have elect I colonel . what more natural ? Come , brother , let we trot on ; dinner will be ready , and Mrs. Fan do not like I to keep it wait . " and so we make for his house , which be open like all the house of our virginian gentleman , and where not only every friend and neighbour , but every stranger and traveller , be sure to find a welcome . 
 " so , Mrs. Fan , " I say , " I have find out what game my brother have be play . " 
 " I trust the Colonel will have plenty of sport ere long , " say she , with a toss of her head . 
 my wife think Harry have be hunt , and I do not care to undeceive she , though what I have see and he have tell I , make I naturally very anxious . 




 CHAPTER lxxxix . a Colonel without a regiment 

 when my visit to my brother be conclude , and my wife and young child have return to our maternal house at Richmond , I make it my business to go over to our Governor , then at his country house , near Williamsburg , and confer with he regard these open preparation for war , which be be make not only in our own province , but in every one of the colony as far as we could learn . gentleman , with whose name history have since make all the world familiar , be appoint from Virginia as delegate to the General Congress about to be hold in Philadelphia . in Massachusetts the people and the royal troop be face each other almost in open hostility : in Maryland and Pennsylvania we flatter ourselves that a much more loyal spirit be prevalent : in the Carolinas and Georgia the mother country could reckon upon staunch adherent , and a great majority of the inhabitant : and it never be to be suppose that our own Virginia would forgo its ancient loyalty . we have but few troop in the province , but its gentry be proud of their descent from the Cavaliers of the old time : and round about our Governor be swarm of loud and confident loyalist who be only eager for the moment when they might draw the sword , and scatter the rascally rebel before they . of course , in these meeting I be force to hear many a hard word against my poor Harry . his wife , all agree   and not without good reason , perhaps , have lead he to adopt these extreme anti - british opinion which he have of late declare ; and he be infatuate by his attachment to the gentleman of Mount Vernon , it be far say , whose opinion my brother always follow , and who , day by day , be commit himself far in the dreadful and desperate course of resistance . " this be your friend , " the people about his Excellency say , " this be the man you favour , who have have your special confidence , and who have repeatedly share your hospitality ! " it could not but be own much of this be true : though what some of our eager Loyalists call treachery be indeed rather a proof of the longing desire Mr. Washington and other gentleman have , not to withdraw from their allegiance to the Crown , but to remain faithful , and exhaust the very last chance of reconciliation , before they risk the other terrible alternative of revolt and separation . let traitor arm , and villain draw the parricidal sword ! we at least would remain faithful ; the unconquerable power of England would be exert , and the misguided and ungrateful province punish and bring back to their obedience . with what cheer we drink his Majesty 's health after our banquet ! we would die in defence of his right ; we would have a Prince of his Royal house to come and govern his ancient dominion ! in consideration of my own and my excellent mother 's loyalty , my brother 's benighted conduct should be forgive . be it yet too late to secure he by offer he a good command ? would I not intercede with he , who , it be know , have a great influence over he ? in our Williamsburg council we be alternately in every state of exaltation and triumph , of hope , of fury against the rebel , of anxious expectancy of home succour , of doubt , distrust , and gloom . 
 I promise to intercede with my brother ; and write to he , I own , with but little hope of success , repeat , and try to strengthen the argument which I have many a time use in our conversation . my mother , too , use her authority ; but from this , I own , I expect little advantage . she assail he , as her habit be , with such text of scripture as she think bear out her own opinion , and threaten punishment to he . she menace he with the penalty which must fall upon those who be disobedient to the power that be . she point to his eld brother 's example ; and hint , I fear , at his subjection to his wife , the very bad argument she could use in such a controversy . she do not show I her own letter to he ; possibly she know I might find fault with the energy of some of the expression she think proper to employ ; but she show I his answer , from which I gather what the style and tenor of her argument have be . and if Madam Esmond bring Scripture to her aid , Mr. Hal , to my surprise , bring score of text to bear upon she in reply , and address she in a very neat , temperate , and even elegant composition , which I think his wife herself be scarcely capable of pen . indeed , I find he have enlist the service of Mr. Belman , the New Richmond clergyman , who have take up strong opinion on the Whig side , and who preach and print sermon against Hagan   who , as I have say , be of our faction , in which I fear Belman have the good of the dispute . 
 my exhortation to Hal have no more success than our mother 's . he do not answer my letter . be still far press by the friend of the Government , I write over most imprudently to say I would visit he at the end of the week at Fanny 's Mount ; but on arrive , I only find my sister , who receive I with perfect cordiality , but inform I that Hal be go into the country , ever so far towards the Blue Mountains to look at some horse , and be to be away she do not know how long he be to be away ! 
 I know then there be no hope . " my dear , " I say , " as far as I can judge from the sign of the time , the train that have be lay these year must have a match put to it before long . Harry be rid away . God know to what end . " 
 " the Lord prosper the righteous cause , Sir George , " say she . 
 " amen , with all my heart . you and he speak as Americans ; I as an Englishman . tell he from I , that when anything in the course of nature shall happen to our mother , I have enough for I and mine in England , and shall resign all our land here in Virginia to he . " 
 " you do not   mean that , George ? " she cry , with brighten eye . " well , to be sure , it be but right and fair , " she presently add . " why should you , who be the eld but by an hour , have everything ? a palace and land in England the plantation here the title and child and my poor Harry none ? but ' tis generous of you all the same leastway handsome and proper , and I do not   expect it of you ; and you do not   take after your mother in this , Sir George , that you do not , nohow . give my love to sister Theo ! " and she offer I a cheek to kiss , ere I ride away from her door . with such a woman as Fanny to guide he , how could I hope to make a convert of my brother ? 
 have meet with this poor success in my enterprise , I ride back to our Governor , with whom I agree that it be time to arm in earnest , and prepare ourselves against the shock that certainly be at hand . he and his whole Court of Officials be not a little agitated and excited ; needlessly savage , I think , in their abuse of the wicked Whigs , and loud in their shout of Old England for ever ; but they be all eager for the day when the contend party could meet hand to hand , and they could have an opportunity of rid those wicked whig down . and I leave my lord , have receive the thank of his Excellency in Council , and engage to do my good endeavour to raise a body of man in defence of the Crown . hence the corps , call afterwards the Westmoreland Defenders , have its rise , of which I have the honour to be appoint Colonel , and which I be to command when it appear in the field . and that fortunate event must straightway take place , so soon as the county know that a gentleman of my station and name would take the command of the force . the announcement be duly make in the Government Gazette , and we fill in our officer readily enough ; but the recruit , it must be own , be slow to come in , and quick to disappear . nevertheless , friend Hagan eagerly come forward to offer himself as chaplain . Madam Esmond give we our colour , and progress about the country engage volunteer ; but the most eager recruiter of all be my good old tutor , little Mr. Dempster , who have be out as a boy on the Jacobite side in Scotland , and who go specially into the Carolinas , among the child of his banish old comrade , who have wear the white cockade of Prince Charles , and who most of all show themselves in this contest still loyal to the Crown . 
 Hal 's expedition in search of horse lead he not only so far as the Blue Mountains in our colony , but thence on a long journey to Annapolis and Baltimore ; and from Baltimore to Philadelphia , to be sure ; where a second General Congress be now sit , attend by our virginian gentleman of the last year . meanwhile , all the almanac tell what have happen . Lexington have happen , and the first shot be fire in the war which be to end in the independence of our native country . we still protest of our loyalty to his Majesty ; but we state our determination to die or be free ; and some twenty thousand of our loyal petitioner assemble round about Boston with arm in their hand and cannon , to which they have help themselves out of the Government store . Mr. Arnold have begin that career which be to end so brilliantly , by the daring and burglarious capture of two fort , of which he force the door . three general from Bond Street , with a large reinforcement , be on their way to help Mr. Gage out of his ugly position at Boston . presently the army be actually engage ; and our british general commence their career of conquest and pacification in the colony by the glorious blunder of Breed 's Hill . here they fortify themselves , feel themselves not strong enough for the moment to win any more glorious victory over the rebel ; and the two army lie watch each other whilst Congress be deliberate at Philadelphia who should command the force of the confederate colony . 
 we all know on whom the most fortunate choice of the nation fall . of the virginian regiment which march to join the new General - in - Chief , one be command by Henry Esmond Warrington , Esq . , late a Captain in his Majesty 's service ; and by his side ride his little wife , of whose bravery we often subsequently hear . I be glad , for one , that she have quit Virginia ; for , have she remain after her husband 's departure , our mother would infallibly have go over to give her battle ; and I be thankful , at least , that that terrific incident of civil war be spar to our family and history . 
 the rush of our farmer and country - folk be almost all direct towards the new northern army ; and our people be not a little flatter at the selection of a virginian gentleman for the principal command . with a thrill of wrath and fury the province hear of the blood draw at Lexington ; and man yell denunciation against the cruelty and wantonness of the bloody british invader . the invader be but do his duty , and be meet and resist by man in arm , who wish to prevent he from help himself to his own ; but people do not stay to weigh their word when they mean to be angry ; the colonist have take their side ; and , with what I own to be a natural spirit and ardour , be determined to have a trial of strength with the braggart domineering mother country . Breed 's Hill become a mountain , as it be , which all man of the American Continent might behold , with Liberty , Victory , Glory , on its flaming summit . these dreaded troop could be withstood , then , by farmer and plowman . these famous officer could be outgeneral by doctor , lawyer , and civilian ! grant that Britons could conquer all the world ; here be their child who could match and conquer Britons ! indeed , I do not   know which of the two deserve the palm , either for bravery or vainglory . we be in the habit of laugh at our french neighbour for boasting , gasconading , and so forth ; but for a steady self - esteem and indomitable confidence in our own courage , greatness , magnanimity ; who can compare with Britons , except their child across the Atlantic ? 
 the people round about we take the people 's side for the most part in the struggle , and , truth to say , Sir George Warrington find his regiment of Westmoreland Defenders but very thinly manned at the commencement , and woefully diminish in number presently , not only after the news of battle from the north , but in consequence of the behaviour of my Lord our Governor , whose conduct enrage no one more than his own immediate partisan , and the loyal adherent of the Crown throughout the colony . that he would plant the King 's standard , and summon all loyal gentleman to rally round it , have be a measure agree in countless meeting , and applaud over thousand of bumper . I have a pretty good memory , and could mention the name of many a gentleman , now a smug officer of the United States Government , whom I have hear hiccup out a prayer that he might be allow to perish under the fold of his country 's flag ; or roar a challenge to the bloody traitor absent with the rebel army . but let bygone be bygone . this , however , be matter of public history , that his lordship , our Governor , a peer of Scotland , the Sovereign 's representative in his Old Dominion , who so loudly invite all the liege to join the King 's standard , be the first to put it in his pocket , and fly to his ship out of reach of danger . he would not leave they , save as a pirate at midnight to burn and destroy . meanwhile , we loyal gentry remain on shore , commit to our cause , and only subject to great danger in consequence of the weakness and cruelty of he who ought to have be our leader . it be the beginning of June , our orchard and garden be all bloom with plenty and summer ; a week before I have be over at Williamsburg , exchange compliment with his Excellency , devise plan for future movement by which we should be able to make good head against rebellion , shake hand heartily at parting , and vincere aut mori the very last word upon all our lip . our little family be gather at Richmond , talk over , as we do daily , the prospect of affair in the north , the quarrel between our own Assembly and his Excellency , by whom they have be afresh convene , when our ghostly Hagan rush into our parlour , and ask , " have we hear the news of the Governor ? " 
 " have he dissolve the Assembly again , and put that scoundrel Patrick Henry in iron ? " ask Madam Esmond . 
 " no such thing ! his lordship with his lady and family have leave their palace privately at night . they be on board a man - of - war off York , whence my lord have send a despatch to the Assembly , beg they to continue their sitting , and announce that he himself have only quit his Government House out of fear of the fury of the people . " 
 what be to become of the sheep , now the shepherd have run away ? no entreaty could be more pathetic than those of the gentleman of the House of Assembly , who guarantee their Governor security if he would but land , and implore he to appear amongst they , if but to pass bill and transact the necessary business . no : the man - of - war be his seat of government , and my lord desire his House of Commons to wait upon he there . this be erect the King 's standard with a vengeance . our Governor have leave we ; our Assembly perforce rule in his stead ; a rabble of people follow the fugitive Viceroy on board his ship . a mob of negro desert out of the plantation to join this other deserter . he and his black ally land here and there in darkness , and emulate the most lawless of our opponent in their alacrity at seize and burn . he not only invite runaway negro , but he send an ambassador to Indians with entreaty to join his standard . when he come on shore it be to burn and destroy : when the people resist , as at Norfolk and Hampton , he retreat and betook himself to his ship again . 
 even my mother , after that miserable flight of our chief , be scared at the aspect of affair , and doubt of the speedy put down of the rebellion . the arming of the negro be , in her opinion , the most cowardly blow of all . the loyal gentry be ruin , and rob , many of they , of their only property . a score of our bad hand desert from Richmond and Castlewood , and flee to our courageous Governor 's fleet ; not all of they , though some of they , be slain , and a couple hang by the enemy for plunder and robbery perpetrate whilst with his lordship 's precious army . because her property be wantonly injure , and his Majesty 's chief officer an imbecile , would Madam Esmond desert the cause of Royalty and Honour ? my good mother be never so prodigiously dignified , and loudly and enthusiastically loyal , as after she hear of our Governor 's lamentable defection . the people round about she , though most of they of quite a different way of thinking , listen to her speech without unkindness . her oddity be know far and wide through our province ; where , I be afraid , many of the wag amongst our young man be accustom to smoke she , as the phrase then be , and draw out her story about the Marquis her father , about the splendour of her family , and so forth . but along with her oddity , her charity and kindness be remember , and many a rebel , as she call they , have a sneaking regard for the pompous little Tory lady .
