 as for the Colonel of the Westmoreland Defenders , though that gentleman 's command dwindle utterly away after the outrageous conduct of his chief , yet I escape from some very serious danger which might have befall I and mine in consequence of some dispute which I be know to have have with my Lord Dunmore . go on board his ship after he have burn the store at Hampton , and issue the proclamation call the negro to his standard , I make so free as to remonstrate with he in regard to both measure ; I implore he to return to Williamsburg , where hundred of we , thousand , I hop , would be ready to defend he to the last extremity ; and in my remonstrance use term so free , or rather , as I suspect , indicate my contempt for his conduct so clearly by my behaviour , that his lordship fly into a rage , say I be a rebel like all the rest of they , and order I under arrest there on board his own ship . in my quality of militia officer   since the breaking out of the trouble I commonly use a red coat , to show that I wear the King 's colour   I beg for a court - martial immediately ; and turn round to two officer who have be present during our altercation , desire they to remember all that have pass between his lordship and I . these gentleman be no doubt of my way of thinking as to the chief 's behaviour , and our interview end in my going ashore unaccompanie by a guard . the story get wind amongst the Whig gentry , and be improve in the telling . I have speak out my mind manfully to the Governor ; no Whig could have utter sentiment more liberal . when riot take place in Richmond , and of the Loyalists remain there , many be in peril of life and betook themselves to the ship , my mother 's property and house be never endanger , nor her family insult . we be still at the stage when a reconciliation be fondly think possible . " ah ! if all the Tories be like you , " a distinguished Whig have say to I , " we and the people at home should soon come together again . " this , of course , be before the famous Fourth of July , and that Declaration which render reconcilement impossible . afterwards , when party grow more rancorous , motif much less creditable be assign for my conduct , and it be say I choose to be a Liberal Tory because I be a cunning fox , and wish to keep my estate whatever way thing go . and this , I be bind to say , be the opinion regard my humble self which have obtain in very high quarter at home , where a profound regard for my own interest have be suppose not uncommonly to have occasion my conduct during the late unhappy trouble . 
 there be two or three person in the world   for I have not tell my mother how I be resolve to cede to my brother all my life - interest in our american property   who know that I have no mercenary motif in regard to the conduct I pursue . it be not worth while to undeceive other ; what be life worth , if a man be force to feel himself a la piste of all the calumny utter against he ? and I do not quite know to this present day , how it happen that my mother , that notorious Loyalist , be leave for several year quite undisturbed in her house at Castlewood , a stray troop or company of Continentals be occasionally quarter upon she . I do not know for certain , I say , how this piece of good fortune happen , though I can give a pretty shrewd guess as to the cause of it . Madam Fanny , after a campaign before Boston , come back to Fanny 's Mount , leave her Colonel . my modest Hal , until the conclusion of the war , would accept no high rank , believe that in command of a regiment he could be more useful than in charge of a division . Madam Fanny , I say , come back , and it be remarkable after her return how her old asperity towards my mother seem to be remove , and what an affection she show for she and all the property . she be great friend with the Governor and some of the most influential gentleman of the new Assembly : Madam Esmond be harmless , and for her son 's sake , who be bravely battle for his country , her error should be lightly visit : I know not how it be , but for year she remain unharmed , except in respect of heavy Government requisition , which of course she have to pay , and it be not until the redcoat appear about our house , that much serious evil come to it . 




 CHAPTER XC 

 in which we both fight and run away 

 what be the use of a Colonel without a regiment ? the Governor and Council who have make such a parade of thank in endow I with mine , be away out of sight , skulk on board ship , with an occasional piracy and arson on shore . my Lord Dunmore 's black ally frighten away those of his own blood ; and besides these negro whom he have summon round he in arm , we hear that he have send an envoy among the Indians of the South , and that they be to come down in number and tomahawk our people into good behaviour . " and these be to be our ally ! " I say to my mother , exchange ominous look with she , and remembering , with a ghastly distinctness , that savage whose face glare over mine , and whose knife be at my throat when Florac strike he down on Braddock 's Field . we put our house of Castlewood into as good a state of defence as we could devise ; but , in truth , it be more of the red man and the black than of the rebel we be afraid . I never see my mother lose courage but once , and then when she be recount to we the particular of our father 's death in a foray of Indians more than forty year ago . see some figure one night move in front of our house , nothing could persuade the good lady but that they be savage , and she sink on her knee cry out , " the Lord have mercy upon we ! the Indians the Indians ! " 
 my lord 's negro ally vanish on board his ship , or where they could find pay and plunder ; but the paint hero from the South never make their appearance , though I own to have look at my mother 's grey head , my wife 's brown hair , and our little one 's golden ringlet , with a horrible pang of doubt lest these should fall the victim of ruffian war . and it be we who fight with such weapon , and enlist these ally ! but that I dare not   so to speak   be set myself up as interpreter of Providence , and point out the special finger of Heaven   as many people be will not to do , I would say our employment of these Indians , and of the german mercenary , bring their own retribution with they in this war . in the field , where the mercenary be attack by the Provincials , they yield , and it be triumph over they that so raise the spirit of the Continental army ; and the murder of one woman   Miss McCrea   by a half - dozen drunken Indians , do more harm to the Royal cause than the loss of a battle or the destruction of regiment . 
 now , the indian panic over , Madam Esmond 's courage return : and she begin to be seriously and not unjustly uneasy at the danger which I run myself , and which I bring upon other , by remain in Virginia . 
 " what harm can they do I , " say she , " a poor woman ? if I have one son a colonel without a regiment , I have another with a couple of hundred continental behind he in Mr. Washington 's camp . if the Royalists come , they will let I off for your sake ; if the rebel appear , I shall have Harry 's passport . I do not   wish , sir , I do not   like that your delicate wife and this dear little baby should be here , and only increase the risk of all of we ! we must have they away to Boston or New York . do not   talk about defend I ! who will think of hurt a poor , harmless , old woman ? if the rebel come , I shall shelter behind Mrs. Fanny 's petticoat , and shall be much safe without you in the house than in it . " this she say in part , perhaps , because ' twa reasonable ; more so because she would have I and my family out of the danger ; and danger or not , for her part feel that she be determined to remain in the land where her father be bury , and she be bear . she be live backwards , so to speak . she have see the new generation , and bless they , and bid they farewell . she belong to the past , and old day and memory . 
 while we be debate about the Boston scheme , come the news that the British have evacuate that luckless city altogether , never have venture to attack Mr. Washington in his camp at Cambridge   though he lie there for many month without powder at our mercy ; but wait until he procure ammunition , and seize and fortify Dorchester height , which command the town , out of which the whole british army and colony be oblige to beat a retreat . that the King 's troop win the battle at Bunker 's Hill , there be no more doubt than that they beat the French at Blenheim ; but through the war their chief seem constantly to have be afraid of assault entrenched continental afterwards ; else why , from July to March , hesitate to strike an almost defenceless enemy ? why the hesitation at Long Island , when the Continental army be in our hand ? why that astonishing timorousness of Howe before Valley Forge , where the relic of a force starve , sickening , and in rag , could scarcely man the line , which they hold before a great , victorious , and perfectly appoint army ? 
 as the hope and fear of the contend party rise and fall , it be curious to mark the altered tone of the partisan of either . when the news come to we in the country of the evacuation of Boston , every little Whig in the neighbourhood make his bow to Madam , and advise she to a speedy submission . she do not carry her loyalty quite so openly as heretofore , and flaunt her flag in the face of the public , but she never swerve . every night and morning in private poor Hagan pray for the Royal Family in our own household , and on Sundays any neighbour be welcome to attend the service , where my mother act as a very emphatic clerk , and the prayer for the High Court of Parliament under our most religious and gracious King be very stoutly deliver . the brave Hagan be a parson without a living , as I be a Militia Colonel without a regiment . Hagan have continue to pray stoutly for King George in Williamsburg , long after his Excellency our Governor have run away : but on come to church one Sunday to perform his duty , he find a corporal 's guard at the church - door , who tell he that the Committee of Safety have put another divine in his place , and he be request to keep a quiet tongue in his head . he tell the man to " lead he before their chief "   our honest friend always love tall word and tragic attitude ; and accordingly be march through the street to the Capitol , with a chorus of white and coloured blackguard at the skirt of his gown ; and have an interview with Messrs. Henry and the new State officer , and confront the robber , as he say , in their den . of course he be for make an heroic speech before these gentleman   and be one of many man who perhaps would have no objection to be make martyr , so that they might be roast coram populo , or torture in a full house , but Mr. Henry be determined to give he no such chance . after keep Hagan three or four hour wait in an anteroom in the company of negro , when the worthy divine enter the new chief magistrate 's room with an undaunted mien , and begin a prepared speech with " Sir , by what authority be I , a minister of the " " Mr. Hagan , " say the other , interrupt he , " I be too busy to listen to speech . and as for King George , he have henceforth no more authority in this country than King Nebuchadnezzar . mind you that , and hold your tongue , if you please ! Stick to King John , sir , and King Macbeth ; and if you will send round your benefit - ticket , all the Assembly shall come and hear you . do you ever see Mr. Hagan on the board , when you be in London , General ? " and , so say , Henry turn round upon Mr. Washington 's second in command , General Lee , who be now come into Virginia upon State affair , and our shamefaced good Hagan be bustle out of the room , redden , and almost cry with shame . after this event we think that Hagan 's ministration be well confine to we in the country , and remove the worthy pastor from his restive lamb in the city . 
 the selection of Virginians to the very high civil and military appointment of the new government bribe and flatter many of our lead people , who , otherwise , and but for the outrageous conduct of our government , might have remain faithful to the Crown , and make good head against the rise rebellion . but , although we Loyalists be gag and muzzle , though the Capitol be in the hand of the whig , and our vaunted levy of loyal recruit so many Falstaff 's regiment for the most part , the faithful still keep intelligence with one another in the colony , and with our neighbour ; and though we do not rise , and though we run away , and though , in examination before committee , justice , and so forth , some of our frightened people give themselves republican air , and vow perdition to king and noble ; yet we know each other pretty well , and accord as the chance be more or less favourable to we , the master more or less hard we conceal our colour , show our colour , half show our colour , or downright apostatise for the nonce , and cry , " down with King George ! " our negro bear about , from house to house , all sort of message and token . endless underhand plot and scheme be engage in by those who could not afford the light . the battle over , the neutral come and join the win side , and shout as loudly as the patriot . the runaway be not count . will any man tell I that the signer and ardent well - wisher of the Declaration of Independence be not in a minority of the nation , and that the minority do not win ? we know that apart of the defeat army of Massachusetts be about to make an important expedition southward , upon the success of which the very great hope be found ; and I , for one , be anxious to make a movement as soon as there be any chance of activity , have put myself in communication with the ex - Governor Martin , of North Carolina , whom I propose to join , with three or four of our virginian gentleman , officer of that notable corp of which we only want private . we make no particular mystery about our departure from Castlewood ; the affair of Congress be not go so well yet that the new government could afford to lay any particular stress or tyranny upon person of a doubtful way of thinking . gentleman 's house be still open ; and in our southern fashion we would visit our friend for month at a time . my wife and I , with our infant and a fitting suite of servant , take leave of Madam Esmond on a visit to a neighbouring plantation . we go thence to another friend 's house , and then to another , till finally we reach Wilmington , in North Carolina , which be the point at which we expect to stretch a hand to the succour which be come to meet we . 
 Ere our arrival , our brother Carolinian Royalists have show themselves in some force . their encounter with the Whigs have be unlucky . the poor highlander have be no more fortunate in their present contest in favour of King George , than when they have draw their sword against he in their own country . we do not reach Wilmington until the end of May , by which time we find Admiral Parker 's squadron there , with General Clinton and five british regiment on board , whose object be a descent upon Charleston . 
 the General , to whom I immediately make myself know , see that my regiment consist of Lady Warrington , our infant , whom she be nurse , and three negro servant , receive we at first with a very grim welcome . but Captain Horner of the Sphinx frigate , who have be on the Jamaica station , and receive , like all the rest of the world , many kindness from our dear Governor there , when he hear that my wife be General Lambert 's daughter , eagerly receive she on board , and give up his good cabin to our service ; and so we be refugee , too , like my Lord Dunmore , have wave our flag , to be sure , and pocket it , and slip out at the back door . from Wilmington we bear away quickly to Charleston , and in the course of the voyage and our delay in the river , previous to our assault on the place , I make some acquaintance with Mr. Clinton , which increase to a further intimacy . it be the King 's birthday when we appear in the river : we determine it be a glorious day for the commencement of the expedition . 
 it do not take place for some day after , and I leave out , purposely , all description of my Penelope parting from her Hector , go forth on this expedition . in the first place , Hector be perfectly well   though a little gouty , nor have any rascal of a Pyrrhus make a prize of his widow : and in time of war and commotion , be not such scene of woe and terror , and parting , occur every hour ? I can see the gentle face yet over the bulwark , as we descend the ship 's side into the boat , and the smile of the infant on her arm . what old story , to be sure ! Captain Miles , have no natural taste for poetry , you have forget the verse , no doubt , in Mr. Pope 's Homer , in which you be describe as part with your heroic father ; but your mother often read they to you as a boy , and keep the gorget I wear on that day somewhere amongst her dressing - box now . 
 my second venture at fighting be no more lucky than my first . we come back to our ship that evening thoroughly beat . the madcap Lee , whom Clinton have face at Boston , now meet he at Charleston . Lee , and the gallant garrison there , make a brilliant and most successful resistance . the fort on Sullivan 's Island , which we attack , be a nut we could not crack . the fire of all our frigate be not strong enough to pound its shell ; the passage by which we move up to the assault of the place be not fordable , as those officer find Sir Henry at the head of they , who be always the first to charge who attempt to wade it . death by shot , by drown , by catch my death of cold , I have brave before I return to my wife ; and our frigate be aground for a time and get off with difficulty , be agreeably cannonade by the enemy until she get off her bank . 
 a small incident in the midst of this unlucky struggle be the occasion of a subsequent intimacy which arise between I and Sir Harry Clinton , and bind I to that most gallant officer during the Period in which it be my fortune to follow the war . of his qualification as a leader there may be many opinion : I fear to say , regard a man I heartily respect and admire , there ought only to be one . of his personal bearing and his courage there can be no doubt ; he be always eager to show it ; and whether at the final charge on Breed 's Hill , when at the head of the rally troop he carry the continental line , or here before Sullivan 's Fort , or a year later at Fort Washington , when , standard in hand , he sweep up the height , and enter the fort at the head of the storm column , Clinton be always foremost in the race of battle , and the King 's service know no more admirable soldier . 
 we be take to the water from our boat , with the intention of force a column to the fort , through a way which our own gun have render practicable , when a shot strike a boat alongside of we , so well aim , as actually to put three - fourth of the boat 's crew hor de combat , and knock down the officer steering , and the flag behind he . I could not help cry out , " Bravo ! well aim ! " for no ninepin ever go down more helplessly than these poor fellow before the round shot . then the General , turn round to I , say , rather grimly , " Sir , the behaviour of the enemy seem to please you ! " " I be pleased , sir , " say I , " that my countryman , yonder , should fight as become our nation . " we flounder on towards the fort in the midst of the same amiable attention from small arm and great , until we find the water be up to our breast and deepen at every step , when we be fain to take to our boat again and pull out of harm 's way . Sir Henry wait upon my Lady Warrington on board the Sphinx after this , and be very gracious to she , and mighty facetious regard the character of the humble writer of the present memoir , whom his Excellency always describe as a rebel at heart . I pray my child may live to see or engage in no great revolution , such as that , for instance , rag in the country of our miserable french neighbour . save a very , very few indeed , the actor in those great tragedy do not bear to be scan too closely ; the chief be often no well than rant quack ; the hero ignoble puppet ; the heroine anything but pure . the prize be not always to the brave . in our revolution it certainly do fall , for once and for a wonder , to the most deserving : but who know his enemy now ? his great and surprising triumph be not in those rare engagement with the enemy where he obtain a trifling mastery ; but over Congress ; over hunger and disease ; over lukewarm friend , or smile foe in his own camp , whom his great spirit have to meet and master . when the struggle be over , and our important chief who have conduct it begin to squabble and accuse each other in their own defence before the nation what charge and counter - charge be bring ; what pretext of delay be urge ; what piteous excuse be put forward that this fleet arrive too late ; that that regiment mistook its order ; that these cannon - ball would not fit those gun ; and so to the end of the chapter ! here be a general who beat we with no shot at time , and no powder , and no money ; and he never think of a convention ; his courage never capitulate ! through all the doubt and darkness , the danger and long tempest of the war , I think it be only the american leader 's indomitable soul that remain entirely steady . 
 of course our Charleston expedition be make the most of , and pronounce a prodigious victory by the enemy , who have learn   from their parent , perhaps   to cry victory if a corporal 's guard be surprised , as loud as if we have win a pitched battle . Mr. Lee rush back to New York , the conqueror of conqueror , trumpet his glory , and by no man receive with more eager delight than by the Commander - in - chief of the American Army . it be my dear Lee and my dear General between they , then ; and it hath always touch I in the history of our early Revolution to note that simple confidence and admiration with which the General - in - Chief be will not to regard officer under he , who have happen previously to serve with the King 's army . so the Mexicans of old look and wonder when they first see an armed spanish horseman ! and this mad , flashy braggart   and another Continental general , whose name and whose luck afterwards be sufficiently notorious   you may be sure take advantage of the modesty of the Commander - in - chief , and advise , and bluster , and sneer , and disobey order ; daily present fresh obstacle   as if he have not enough otherwise !   in the path over which only Mr. Washington 's astonishing endurance could have enable he to march . 
 whilst we be away on our South Carolina expedition , the famous Fourth of July have take place , and we and the thirteen United States be part for ever . my own native state of Virginia have also distinguish itself by announce that all man be equally free ; that all power be vest in the people , who have an inalienable right to alter , reform , or abolish their form of government at pleasure , and that the idea of an hereditary first magistrate be unnatural and absurd ! our General present I with this document fresh from Williamsburg , as we be sail northward by the Virginia cape , and , amidst not a little amusement and laughter , point out to I the faith to which , from the Fourth inst . inclusive , I be bind . there be no help for it ; I be a Virginian my godfather have promise and vow , in my name , that all man be equally free   include , of course , the race of poor Gumbo , that the idea of a monarchy be absurd , and that I have the right to alter my form of government at pleasure . I think of Madam Esmond at home , and how she would look when these article of faith be bring she to subscribe ; how would Hagan receive they ? he demolish they in a sermon , in which all the logic be on his side , but the U.S. Government have not , somehow , be affect by the discourse ; and when he come to touch upon the point that all man be free , therefore Gumbo and Sady , and Nathan , have assuredly a right to go to Congress : " Tut , tut ! my good Mr. Hagan , " say my mother , " let we hear no more of this nonsense ; but leave such wickedness and folly to the rebel ! " 
 by the middle of August we be before New York , whither Mr. Howe have bring his army that have betake itself to Halifax after its inglorious expulsion from Boston . the American Commander - in - Chief be at New York , and a great battle inevitable ; and I look forward to it with an inexpressible feeling of doubt and anxiety , know that my dear brother and his regiment form part of the troop whom we must attack , and could not but overpower . almost the whole of the american army come over to fight on a small island , where every officer on both side know that they be to be beat , and whence they have not a chance of escape . two frigate , out of a hundred we have place so as to command the enemy 's entrenched camp and point of retreat across East River to New York , would have destroy every bark in which he seek to fly , and compel he to lay down his arm on shore . he fight : his hasty levy be utterly overthrow ; some of his general , his good troop , his artillery take ; the remnant huddle into their entrench camp after their rout , the pursuer enter it with they . the victor be call back ; the enemy be then pen up in a corner of the island , and could not escape . " they be at our mercy , and be ours to - morrow , " say the gentle General . not a ship be set to watch the american force ; not a sentinel of our could see a movement in their camp . a whole army cross under our eye in one single night to the mainland without the loss of a single man ; and General Howe be suffer to remain in command after this feat , and to complete his glory of Long Island and Breed 's Hill , at Philadelphia ! a friend , to be sure , cross in the night to say the enemy 's army be be ferry over , but he fall upon a picket of Germans : they could not understand he : their commander be booze or asleep . in the morning , when the spy be bring to some one who could comprehend the american language , the whole continental force have cross the East River , and the empire over thirteen colony have slip away . 
 the opinion I have about our chief be by no mean uncommon in the army ; though , perhaps , wisely keep secret by gentleman under Mr. Howe 's immediate command . be I more unlucky than other folk , I wonder ? or why be my imprudent saying carry about more than my neighbour ' ? my rage that such a use be make of such a victory be no great than that of score of gentleman with the army . why must my name forsooth be give up to the Commander - in - Chief as that of the most guilty of the grumbler ? personally , General Howe be perfectly brave , amiable , and good - humour . 
 " so , Sir George , " say he , " you find fault with I , as a military man , because there be a fog after the battle on Long Island , and your friend , the Continentals , give I the slip ! surely we take and kill enough of they ; but there be no satisfy you gentleman amateur ! " and he turn his back on I , and shrug his shoulder , and talk to some one else . Amateur I might be , and he the most amiable of man ; but if King George have say to he , " never more be officer of mine , " yonder agreeable and pleasant Cassio would most certainly have have his desert . 
 I soon find how our Chief have come in possession of his information regard myself . my admirable cousin , Mr. William Esmond who of course have forsake New York and his post , when all the Royal authority flee out of the place , and Washington occupy it , return along with our troop and fleet ; and , be a gentleman of good birth and name , and well acquaint with the city , make himself agreeable to the newcomer of the Royal army , the young blood , merry fellow , and macaronis , by introduce they to play - table , tavern , and yet bad place , with which the worthy gentleman continue to be familiar in the New World as in the Old . Coelum non animum . however will have change his air , or whithersoever he transport his carcase , he carry a rascal in his skin . 
 I have hear a dozen story of his saying regard my family , and be determine neither to avoid he nor seek he ; but to call he to account whensoever we meet ; and , chance one day to be at a coffee - house in a friend 's company , my worthy kinsman swagger in with a couple of young lad of the army , whom he find it be his pleasure and profit now to lead into every kind of dissipation . I happen to know one of Mr. Will 's young companion , an aide - de - camp of General Clinton 's , who have be in my close company both at Charleston , before Sullivan 's Island , and in the action of Brooklyn , where our General gloriously lead the right wing of the english army . they take a box without notice we at first , though I hear my name three or four time mention by my brawling kinsman , who end some drunken speech he be make by slap his fist on the table , and swearing , " by , I will do for he , and the bloody rebel , his brother ! " 
 " ah ! Mr. Esmond , " say I , come forward with my hat on .   he look a little pale behind his punch - bowl .   " I have long want to see you , to set some little matter right about which there have be a difference between we . " 
 " and what may those be , sir ? " say he , with a volley of oath . 
 " you have choose to cast a doubt upon my courage , and say that I shirk a meeting with you when we be young man . our relationship and our age ought to prevent we from have recourse to such murderous folly "   Mr. Will start up , look fierce and relieve , " but I give you notice , that though I can afford to overlook lie against myself , if I hear from you a word in disparagement of my brother , Colonel Warrington , of the Continental Army , I will hold you accountable . " 
 " indeed , gentleman ! mighty fine , indeed ! you take notice of Sir George Warrington 's word ! " cry Mr. Will over his punch - bowl . 
 " you have be pleased to say , " I continue , grow angry as I speak , and be a fool therefore for my pain , " that the very estate we hold in this country be not ours , but of right revert to your family ! " 
 " so they be ours ! by George , they be ours ! I have   hear my brother Castlewood say so a score of time ! " swear Mr. Will . 
 " in that case , sir , " say I , hotly , " your brother , my Lord Castlewood , tell no more truth than yourself . we have the title at hone in Virginia . they be register in the court there ; and if ever I hear one word more of this impertinence , I shall call you to account where no constable will be at hand to interfere ! " 
 " I wonder , " cry will , in a choking voice , " that I do not   cut he into twenty thousand piece as he stand there before I with his confound yellow face . it be my brother Castlewood win his money no , it be his brother ; d you , which be you , the rebel or the other ? I hate the ugly face of both of you , and , hic ! if you be for the king , show you be for the king , and drink his health ! " and he sink down into his box with a hiccup and a wild laugh , which he repeat a dozen time , with a hundred more oath and vociferous outcry that I should drink the King 's health . 
 to reason with a creature in this condition , or ask explanation or apology from he , be absurd . I leave Mr. Will to reel to his lodging under the care of his young friend who be surprised to find an old toper so suddenly affected and so utterly prostrate by liquor and limp home to my wife , whom I find happy in possession of a brief letter from Hal , which a countryman have bring in ; and who say not a word about the affair of the Continentals with whom he be engage , but write a couple of page of rapturous eulogium upon his brother 's behaviour in the field , which my dear Hal be pleased to admire , as he admire everything I say and do . 
 I rather look for a messenger from my amiable kinsman in consequence of the speech which have pass between we the night before , and do not know but that I might be call by Will to make my word good ; and when accordingly Mr. Lacy   our companion of the previous evening   make his appearance at an early hour of the forenoon , I be beckon my Lady Warrington to leave we , when , with a laugh and a cry of " oh dear , no ! " Mr. Lacy beg her ladyship not to disturb herself . 
 " I have see , " say he , " a gentleman who beg to send you his apology if he utter a word last night which could offend you . " 
 " what apology ? what word ? " ask the anxious wife . 
 I explain that roaring Will Esmond have meet I in a coffee - house on the previous evening , and quarrel with I , as he have do with hundred before . " it appear the fellow be constantly abusive , and invariably plead drunkenness , and apologise the next morning , unless he be can over - night , " remark Captain Lacy . and my lady , I dare say , make a little sermon , and ask why we gentleman will go to idle coffee - house and run the risk of meeting roar , roystere Will Esmonds ? 
 our sojourn in New York be enliven by a project for burn the city which some ardent patriot entertain and partially execute . several such scheme be lay in the course of the war , and each one of the principal city be doom to fire ; though , in the interest of peace and goodwill , I hope it will be remember that these plan never originate with the cruel government of a tyrant king , but be always propose by gentleman on the Continental side , who vow that , rather than remain under the ignominious despotism of the ruffian of Brunswick , the fair town of America should burn . I presume that the sage who be for burn down Boston be not actual proprietor in that place , and the New York burner might come from other part of the country from Philadelphia , or what not . Howbeit , the British spar you , gentleman , and we pray you give we credit for this act of moderation . 
 I have not the fortune to be present in the action on the White Plains , be detain by the hurt which I have receive at Long Island , and which break out again and again , and take some time in the healing . the tender of nurse watch I through my tedious malady , and be eager for the day when I should doff my militia coat and return to the quiet English home where Hetty and our good General be tend our child . indeed I do not   know that I have yet forgive myself for the pain and terror that I must have cause my poor wife , by keep she separate from her young one , and away from her home , because , forsooth , I wish to see a little more of the war then go on . our grand tour in Europe have be all very well . we have behold St. Peter 's at Rome , and the Bishop thereof ; the Dauphiness of France   alas , to think that glorious head should ever have be bring so low !   at Paris ; and the rightful king of England at Florence . I have dip my gout in a half - dozen bath and spa , and play card in a hundred court , as my Travels in Europe   which I propose to publish after my completion of the history of the American War   will testify .   neither of these two project work of Sir George Warrington be bring , as it appear , to a completion .   and , during our peregrination , my hypochondria diminish   which plague I woefully at home ; and my health and spirit visibly improve . perhaps it be because she see the evident benefit I have from excitement and change , that my wife be reconcile to my continue to enjoy they ; and though secretly suffer pang at be away from her nursery and her eld boy   for whom she ever have have an absurd infatuation , the dear hypocrite scarce allow a look of anxiety to appear on her face ; encourage I with smile ; profess herself eager to follow I ; ask why it should be a sin in I to covet honour ? and , in a word , be ready to stay , to go , to smile , to be sad ; to scale mountain , or to go down to the sea in ship ; to say that cold be pleasant , heat tolerable , hunger good sport , dirty lodging delightful ; though she be wretched sailor , very delicate about the little she eat , and an extreme sufferer both of cold and heat . hence , as I will to stay on yet a while on my native continent , she be certain nothing be so good for I ; and when I be minded to return home oh , how she brighten , and kiss her infant , and tell he how he should see the beautiful garden at home , and Aunt Theo , and grandpapa , and his sister , and Miles . " Miles ! " cry the little parrot , mock its mother and crowing ; as if there be any mighty privilege in see Mr. Miles , forsooth , who be under Doctor Sumner 's care at Harrow - on - the - Hill , where , to do the gentleman justice , he show that he could eat more tart than any boy in the school , and take most creditable prize at football and hare - and - hound . 




 CHAPTER XCI . Satis Pugnae 

 it have always seem to I   I speak under the correction of military gentleman   that the entrenchment of Breed 's Hill serve the Continental army throughout the whole of our american war . the slaughter inflict upon we from behind those line be so severe , and the behaviour of the enemy so resolute , that the british chief respect the barricade of the Americans hereafter ; and be they fire from behind a row of blanket , certain of our general rather hesitate to force they . in the affair of the White Plains , when , for a second time , Mr. Washington 's army be quite at the mercy of the victor , we subsequently hear that our conquering troop be hold back before a barricade actually compose of cornstalk and straw . another opportunity be give we , and last during a whole winter , during which the dwindle and dismayed troop of Congress lie starve and unarmed under our grasp , and the magnanimous Mr. Howe leave the famous camp of Valley Forge untouched , whilst his great , brave , and perfectly appoint army fiddle and gamble and feast in Philadelphia . and , by Byng 's countryman , triumphal arch be erect , tournament be hold in pleasant mockery of the middle age , and wreath and garland offer by beautiful lady to this clement chief , with fantastical motto and posy announce that his laurel should be immortal ! why have my ungrateful countryman in America never erect statue to this general ? they have not in all their army an officer who fight their battle well ; who enable they to retrieve their error with such adroitness ; who take care that their defeat should be so little hurtful to themselves ; and when , in the course of event , the strong force naturally get the uppermost , who show such an untiring tenderness , patience , and complacency in help the poor disabled opponent on to his leg again . ah ! think of eighteen year before and the fiery young warrior whom England have send out to fight her adversary on the american continent . Fancy he for ever pace round the defence behind which the foe lie shelter ; by night and by day alike sleepless and eager ; consume away in his fierce wrath and longing , and never close his eye , so intent be it in watch ; wind the track with untiring scent that pant and hunger for blood and battle ; prowl through midnight forest , or climb silent over precipice before dawn ; and watch till his great heart be almost wear out , until the foe show himself at last , when he spring on he and grapple with he , and , dying , slay he ! think of Wolfe at Quebec , and hearken to Howe 's fiddle as he sit smile amongst the dancer at Philadelphia ! 
 a favourite scheme with our minister at home and some of our general in America , be to establish a communication between Canada and New York , by which mean it be hop New England might be cut off from the neighbouring colony , overpower in detail , and force into submission . Burgoyne be entrust with the conduct of the plan , and he set forth from Quebec , confidently promise to bring it to a successful issue . his march begin in military state : the trumpet of his proclamation blow before he ; he bid the colonist to remember the immense power of England ; and summon the misguided rebel to lay down their arm . he bring with he a formidable english force , an army of german veteran not less powerful , a dreadful band of indian warrior , and a brilliant train of artillery . it be suppose that the people round his march would rally to the Royal cause and standard . the Continental force in front of he be small at first , and Washington 's army be weaken by the withdrawal of troop who be hurry forward to meet this canadian invasion . a british detachment from New York be to force its way up the Hudson , sweep away the enemy on the route , and make a junction with Burgoyne at Albany . then be the time when Washington 's weakened army should have be strike too ; but a great power willed otherwise : nor be I , for one , even go to regret the termination of the war . as we look over the game now , how clear seem the blunder which be make by the lose side ! from the beginning to the end we be for ever arrive too late . our supply and reinforcement from home be too late . our troop be in difficulty , and our succour reach they too late . our fleet appear off York Town just too late , after Cornwallis have surrender . a way of escape be open to Burgoyne , but he resolve upon retreat too late . I have hear discomfited officer in after day prove infallibly how a different wind would have save America to we ; how we must have destroy the french fleet but for a tempest or two ; how once , twice , thrice , but for nightfall , Mr. Washington and his army be in our power . who have not speculate , in the course of his reading of history , upon the " have be " and the " might have be " in the world ? I take my tattered old map - book from the shelf , and see the board on which the great contest be play ; I wonder at the curious chance which lose it : and , put aside any idle talk about the respective bravery of the two nation , can not   but see that we have the good card , and that we lose the game . 
 I own the sport have a considerable fascination for I , and stir up my languid blood . my brother Hal , when settle on his plantation in Virginia , be perfectly satisfied with the sport and occupation he find there . the company of the country neighbour suffice he ; he never tired of look after his crop and people , take his fish , shoot his duck , hunt in his wood , or enjoy his rubber and his supper . Happy Hal , in his great barn of a house , under his roomy porch , his dog lie round his foot ; his friend , the Virginian will wimble , at free quarter in his mansion ; his negro fat , lazy , and rag : his shrewd little wife rule over they and her husband , who always obey she implicitly when live , and who be pretty speedily console when she die ! I say happy , though his lot would have be intolerable to I : wife , and friend , and plantation , and town life at Richmond   Richmond succeed to the honour of be the capital when our Province become a State . how happy he whose foot fit the shoe which fortune give he ! my income be five time as great , my house in England as large , and build of brick and face with freestone ; my wife would I have change she for any other wife in the world ? my child well , I be content with my Lady Warrington 's opinion about they . but with all these plum and peach and rich fruit out of Plenty 's horn pour into my lap , I fear I have be but an ingrate ; and Hodge , my gatekeeper , who share his bread and scrap of bacon with a family as large as his master 's , seem to I to enjoy his meal as much as I do , though Mrs. Molly prepare her good dish and sweetmeat , and Mr. Gumbo uncork the choice bottle from the cellar . ah I ! sweetmeat have lose their savour for I , however they may rejoice my young one from the nursery , and the perfume of claret pall upon old nose ! our parson have pour out his sermon many and many a time to I , and perhaps I do not care for they much when he first broach they . dost thou remember , honest friend ?   sure he do , for he have repeat the story over the bottle as many time as his sermon almost , and my Lady Warrington pretend as if she have never hear it   I say , Joe Blake , thou rememberest full well , and with advantage , that October evening when we scramble up an embrasure at Fort Clinton and a clubbed musket would have dash these valuable brain out , have not Joe 's sword whip my rebellious countryman through the gizzard . Joe wear a red coat in those day   the uniform of the brave Sixty - third , whose leader , the bold Sill , fall pierce with many wound beside he . he exchange his red for black and my pulpit . his doctrine be sound , and his sermon short . we read the paper together over our wine . not two month ago we read our old friend Howe 's glorious deed of the first of June . we be tell how the noble Rawdon , who fight with we at Fort Clinton , have join the Duke of York : and to - day his Royal Highness be in full retreat before Pichegru : and he and my son Miles have take valencienne for nothing ! ah , parson ! would you not like to put on your old Sixty - third coat ?   though I doubt Mrs. Blake could never make the button and button - hole meet again over your big body . the boy be act a play with my militia sword . oh , that I be young again , Mr. Blake ! that I have not the gout in my toe ; and I would saddle Rosinante and ride back into the world , and feel the pulse beat again , and play a little of life 's glorious game ! 
 the last " hit " which I see play , be gallantly win by our side ; though ' tis true that even in this parti the Americans win the rubber our people gain only the ground they stand on , and the gun , store , and ship which they capture and destroy , whilst our effort at rescue be too late to prevent the catastrophe impend over Burgoyne 's unfortunate army . after one of those delay which always be happen to retard our plan and weaken the blow which our chief intend to deliver , an expedition be get under weigh from New York at the close of the month of September , ' 77 ; that , could it have but advance a fortnight early , might have save the doomed force of Burgoyne . Sed Dis aliter visum . the delay here be not Sir Henry Clinton 's fault , who could not leave his city unprotected ; but the wind and weather which delay the arrival of reinforcement which we have long await from England . the fleet which bring they bring we long and fond letter from home , with the very last news of the child under the care of their good Aunt Hetty and their grandfather . the mother 's heart yearn towards the absent young one . she make I no reproach : but I could read her importunity in her anxious eye , her terror for I , and her longing for her child . " why stay long ? " she seem to say . " you who have no calling to this war , or to draw the sword against your countryman why continue to imperil your life and my happiness ? " I understand her appeal . we be to enter upon no immediate service of danger ; I tell she Sir Henry be only go to accompany the expedition for a part of the way . I would return with he , the reconnaissance over , and Christmas , please Heaven , should see our family once more unite in England . 
 a force of three thousand man , include a couple of slender regiment of American Loyalists and New York Militia   with which latter my distinguished relative , Mr. Will Esmond , go as captain , be embark at New York , and our armament sail up the noble Hudson River , that present fine aspect than the Rhine in Europe to my mind : nor be any fire open upon we from those beetle cliff and precipitous " palisade , " as they be call , by which we sail ; the enemy , strange to say , be for once unaware of the movement we contemplate . our first landing be on the eastern bank , at a place call Verplancks Point , whence the Congress troop withdraw after a slight resistance , their leader , the tough old Putnam   so famous during the war   suppose that our march be to be direct towards the Eastern Highlands , by which we intend to penetrate to Burgoyne . Putnam fall back to occupy these pass , a small detachment of ours be send forward as if in pursuit , which he imagine be to be follow by the rest of our force . meanwhile , before daylight , two thousand man without artillery , be carry over to Stoney Point on the western shore , opposite Verplancks , and under a great hill call the Dunderberg by the old dutch lord of the stream , and which hang precipitously over it . a little stream at the northern base of this mountain intersect it from the opposite height on which Fort Clinton stand , name not after our general , but after one of the two gentleman of the same name , who be amongst the old and most respected of the provincial gentry of New York , and who be at this moment actually in command against Sir Henry . on the next height to Clinton be Fort Montgomery ; and behind they rise a hill call Bear Hill ; whilst at the opposite side of the magnificent stream stand " Saint Antony 's Nose , " a prodigious peak indeed , which the Dutch have quaintly christen . 
 the attack on the two fort be almost simultaneous . half our man be detach for the assault on Fort Montgomery , under the brave Campbell , who fall before the rampart . Sir Henry , who would never be out of danger where he could find it , personally lead the remainder , and hop , he say , that we should have well luck than before the Sullivan Island . a path lead up to the Dunderberg , so narrow as scarcely to admit three man abreast , and in utter silence our whole force scale it , wonder at every rugged step to meet with no opposition . the enemy have not even keep a watch on it ; nor be we descry until we be descend the height , at the base of which we easily disperse a small force send hurriedly to oppose we . the firing which here take place render all idea of a surprise impossible . the fort be before we . with such arm as the troop have in their hand , they have to assault ; and silently and swiftly , in the face of the artillery playing upon they , the troop ascend the hill . the man have order on no account to fire . take the colour of the Sixty - third , and bear they aloft , Sir Henry mount with the stormer . the place be so steep that the man push each other over the wall and through the embrasure ; and it be there that Lieutenant Joseph Blake , the father of a certain Joseph Clinton Blake , who look with the eye of affection on a certain young lady , present himself to the living of Warrington by save the life of the unworthy patron thereof . 
 about a fourth part of the garrison , as we be tell , escape out of the fort , the rest be kill or wound , or remain our prisoner within the work . Fort Montgomery be , in like manner , storm and take by our people ; and , at night , as we look down from the height where the king 's standard have be just plant , we be treat to a splendid illumination in the river below . under Fort Montgomery , and stretch over to that lofty prominence , call Saint Antony 's Nose , a boom and chain have be lay with a vast cost and labour , behind which several american frigate and galley be anchor . the fort be take , these ship attempt to get up the river in the darkness , out of the reach of gun which they know must destroy they in the morning . but the wind be unfavourable , and escape be find to be impossible . the crew therefore take to the boat , and so land , have previously set the ship on fire with all their sail set ; and we behold these magnificent pyramid of flame burn up to the heaven and reflect in the water below , until , in the midst of prodigious explosion , they sink and disappear . 
 on the next day a parlementaire come in from the enemy , to inquire as to the state of his troop leave wound or prisoner in our hand , and the continental officer bring I a note , which give I a strange shock , for it show that in the struggle of the previous evening my brother have be engage . it be date October 7 , from Major - General George Clinton 's divisional headquarter , and it state briefly that " Colonel H. Warrington , of the Virginia line , hop that Sir George Warrington escape unhurt in the assault of last evening , from which the Colonel himself be so fortunate as to retire without the least injury . " never do I say my prayer more heartily and gratefully than on that night , devoutly thank Heaven that my dear brother be spar , and make a vow at the same time to withdraw out of the fratricidal contest , into which I only have enter because Honour and Duty seem imperatively to call I . 
 I own I feel an inexpressible relief when I have come to the resolution to retire and betake myself to the peaceful shade of my own vine and fig - tree at home . I long , however , to see my brother ere I return , and ask , and easily obtain an errand to the camp of the American General Clinton from our own chief . the headquarters of his division be now some mile up the river , and a boat and a flag of truce quickly bring I to the point where his out - picket receive I on the shore . my brother be very soon with I . he have only lately join General Clinton 's division with letter from headquarters at Philadelphia , and he chance to hear , after the attack on Fort Clinton , that I have be present during the affair . we pass a brief delightful night together : Mr. Sady , who always follow Hal to the war , cook a feast in honour of both his master . there be but one bed of straw in the hut where we have quarter , and Hal and I sleep on it , side by side , as we have do when we be boy . we have a hundred thing to say regard past time and present . his kind heart gladden when I tell he of my resolve to retire to my acre and to take off the red coat which I wear : he fling his arm round it . " praise be God ! " say he . " oh , heaven , George ! think what might have happen have we meet in the affair two night ago ! " and he turn quite pale at the thought . he ease my mind with respect to our mother . she be a bitter tory , to be sure , but the Chief have give special injunction regard her safety . " and Fanny "   Hal 's wife   " watch over she , and she be as good as a company ! " cry the enthusiastic husband . " be not   she clever ? be not   she handsome ? be not   she good ? " cry Hal , never , fortunately , wait for a reply to these ardent query . " and to think that I be nearly marry Maria once ! oh , mercy , what an escape I have ! " he add . " hagan pray for the king , every morning and night , at Castlewood , but they bolt the door , and nobody hear . gracious power ! his wife be sixty if she be a day ; and oh , George ! the quantity she drink be ... " but why tell the failing of our good cousin ? I be pleased to think she live to drink the health of King George long after his Old Dominion have pass for ever from his sceptre . 
 the morning come when my brief mission to the camp be end , and the truest of friend and fond of brother accompany I to my boat , which lie wait at the riverside . we exchange an embrace at parting , and his hand hold mine yet for a moment ere I step into the barge which bear I rapidly down the stream . " shall I see thee once more , dear and good companion of my youth ? " I think . " amongst our cold englishman , can I ever hope to meet with a friend like thee ? when hadst thou ever a thought that be not kindly and generous ? when a wish , or a possession , but for I you would sacrifice it ? how brave be you , and how modest ; how gentle , and how strong ; how simple , unselfish , and humble ; how eager to see other ' merit ; how diffident of your own ! " he stand on the shore till his figure grow dim before , I . there be that in my eye which prevent I from see he long . 
 Brilliant as Sir Henry 's success have be , it be achieve , as usual , too late : and serve but as a small set - off against the disaster of Burgoyne which ensue immediately , and which our advance be utterly inadequate to relieve . More than one secret messenger be despatch to he who never reach he , and of whom we never learn the fate . of one wretch who offer to carry intelligence to he , and whom Sir Henry despatch with a letter of his own , we hear the miserable doom . fall in with some of the troop of General George Clinton , who happen to be in red uniform   part of the prize of a british ship 's cargo , doubtless , which have be take by american privateer , the spy think he be in the english army , and advance towards the sentry . he find his mistake too late . his letter be discover upon he , and he have to die for bear it . in ten day after the success at the Forts occur the great disaster at Saratoga , of which we carry the dismal particular in the fleet which bear we home . I be afraid my wife be unable to mourn for it . she have her child , her father , her sister to revisit , and daily and nightly thank to pay to Heaven that have bring her husband safe out of danger . 




 CHAPTER XCII . under Vine and Fig - Tree 

 Need I describe , young folk , the delight of the meeting at home , and the mother 's happiness with all her brood once more under her fond wing ? it be write in her face , and acknowledge on her knee . our house be large enough for all , but Aunt Hetty would not stay in it . she say , fairly , that to resign her motherhood over the eld child , who have be her for nearly three year , cost she too great a pang ; and she could not bear for yet a while to be with they , and to submit to take only the second place . so she and her father go away to a house at Bury St. Edmunds , not far from we , where they live , and where she spoil her eld nephew and niece in private . it be the year after we come home that Mr. B , the Jamaica planter , die , who leave she the half of his fortune ; and then I hear , for the first time , how the worthy gentleman have be greatly enamoured of she in Jamaica , and , though she have refuse he , have thus show his constancy to she . Heaven know how much property of Aunt Hetty 's Monsieur Miles hath already devour ! the price of his commission and outfit ; his gorgeous uniform ; his play - debt and little transaction in the Minories ; do you think , sirrah , I do not know what human nature be ; what be the cost of Pall Mall tavern , petit souper , play even in moderation at the Cocoa - Tree ; and that a gentleman can not purchase all these enjoyment with the five hundred a year which I allow he ? Aunt Hetty declare she have make up her mind to be an old maid . " I make a vow never to marry until I could find a man as good as my dear father , " she say ; " and I never do , Sir George . no , my dear Theo , not half as good ; and Sir George may put that in his pipe and smoke it . " 
 and yet when the good General die   calm , and full of year , and glad to depart , I think it be my wife who shed the most tear . " I weep because I think I do not love he enough , " say the tender creature : whereas Hetty scarce depart from her calm , at least outwardly and before any of we ; talk of he constantly still , as though he be alive ; recall his merry saying , his gentle , kind way with his child   when she brighten up and look herself quite a girl again , and sit cheerfully look up to the slab in church which record his name and some of his virtue , and for once tell no lie . 
 I have fancy , sometimes , that my brother Hal , for whom Hetty have a juvenile passion , always retain a hold of her heart ; and when he come to see we , ten year ago , I tell he of this childish romance of Het 's , with the hope , I own , that he would ask she to replace Mrs. Fanny , who have be gather to her father , and regard whom my wife   with her usual propensity to consider herself a miserable sinner   always reproach herself , because , forsooth , she do not regret Fanny enough . Hal , when he come to we , be plunge in grief about her loss ; and vow that the world do not contain such another woman . our dear old General , who be still in life then , take he in and house he , as he have do in the happy early day . the woman play he the very same tune which he have hear when a boy at Oakhurst . everybody 's heart be very soft with old recollection , and Harry never tired of pour out his grief and his recital of his wife 's virtue to Het , and anon of talk fondly about his dear Aunt Lambert , whom he love with all his heart , and whose praise , you may be sure , be welcome to the faithful old husband , out of whose thought his wife 's memory be never , I believe , absent for any three wake minute of the day . 
 General Hal go to Paris as an American General Officer in his blue and yellow   which Mr. Fox and other gentleman have bring into fashion here likewise , and be make much of at Versailles , although he be present by Monsieur le Marquis de Lafayette to the most christian King and Queen , who do not love Monsieur le Marquis . and I believe a Marquise take a fancy to the Virginian General , and would have marry he out of hand , have he not resist , and flee back to England and Warrington and Bury again , especially to the latter place , where the folk would listen to he as he talk about his late wife , with an endless patience and sympathy . as for we , who have know the poor paragon , we be civil , but not quite so enthusiastic regard she , and rather puzzle sometimes to answer our child 's question about Uncle Hal 's angel wife . 
 the two Generals and myself , and Captain Miles , and Parson Blake   who be knock over at Monmouth , the year after I leave America , and come home to change his coat , and take my living , use to fight the battle of the Revolution over our bottle ; and the parson use to cry , " By Jupiter , General "   he compound for Jupiter , when he lay down his military habit , " you be the Tory , and Sir George be the Whig ! he be always find fault with our leader , and you be for ever stand up for they ; and when I pray for the king last Sunday , I hear you follow I quite loud . " 
 " and so I do , Blake , with all my heart ; I can not   forget I wear his coat , " say Hal . 
 " ah , if Wolfe have be alive for twenty year more ! " say Lambert . 
 " ah , sir , " cry Hal , " you should hear the General talk about he ! " 
 " what general ? " say I   to vex he . 
 " my general , " say Hal , stand up , and fill a bumper . " his Excellency General George Washington ! " 
 " with all my heart , " cry I , but the parson look as if he do not like the toast or the claret . 
 Hal never tired in speak of his General ; and it be on some such evening of friendly converse , that he tell we how he have actually be in disgrace with this General whom he love so fondly . their difference seem to have be about Monsieur le Marquis de Lafayette before mention , who play such a fine part in history of late , and who hath so suddenly disappear out of it . his previous rank in our own service , and his acknowledge gallantry during the war , ought to have secure Colonel Warrington 's promotion in the Continental army , where a whipper - snapper like M. de Lafayette have but to arrive and straightway to be compliment by Congress with the rank of Major - General . Hal , with the freedom of an old soldier , have express himself somewhat contemptuously regard some of the appointment make by Congress , with whom all sort of miserable intrigue and cabal be set to work by unscrupulous officer who be greedy of promotion . Mr. Warrington , imitate perhaps in this the example of his now illustrious friend of Mount Vernon , affect to make the war en gentilhomme take his pay , to be sure , but spend it upon comfort and clothing for his man , and as for rank , declare it be a matter of no earthly concern to he , and that he would as soon serve as colonel as in any high grade . no doubt he add contemptuous remark regard certain General Officers of Congress army , their origin , and the cause of their advancement : notably he be very angry about the sudden promotion of the young french lad just name the Marquis , as they love to call he in the republican army , and who , by the way , be a prodigious favourite of the Chief himself . there be not three officer in the whole Continental force   after poor madcap Lee be take prisoner and disgrace   who could speak the Marquis 's language , so that Hal could judge the young Major - General more closely and familiarly than other gentleman , include the Commander - in - chief himself . Mr. Washington good - naturedly rat friend Hal for be jealous of the beardless commander of Auvergne ; be himself not a little pleased by the filial regard and profound veneration which the enthusiastic young nobleman always show for he ; and have , moreover , the very good politic reason for treat the Marquis with friendship and favour . 
 meanwhile , as it afterwards turn out , the Commander - in - Chief be most urgently press Colonel Warrington 's promotion upon Congress ; and , as if his difficulty before the enemy be not enough , he be at this hard time of winter entrench at Valley Forge , command five or six thousand man at the most , almost without fire , blanket , food , or ammunition , in the face of Sir William Howe 's army , which be perfectly appoint , and three time as numerous as his own ; as if , I say , this difficulty be not enough to try he , he have far to encounter the cowardly distrust of Congress , and insubordination and conspiracy amongst the officer in his own camp . during the awful winter of ' 77 , when one blow strike by the sluggard at the head of the british force might have end the war , and all be doubt , confusion , despair in the opposite camp   save in one indomitable breast alone , my brother have an interview with the Chief , which he have subsequently describe to I , and of which Hal could never speak without give way to the deep emotion . Mr. Washington have win no such triumph as that which the dare - devil courage of Arnold and the elegant imbecility of Burgoyne have procure for Gates and the northern army . save in one or two minor encounter , which prove how dare his bravery be , and how uncease his watchfulness , General Washington have meet with defeat after defeat from an enemy in all point his superior . the Congress mistrust he . many an officer in his own camp hat he . those who have be disappoint in ambition , those who have be detect in peculation , those whose selfishness or incapacity his honest eye have spy out , be all more , or less in league against he . Gates be the chief towards whom the malcontent turn . Mr. Gates be the only genius fit to conduct the war ; and with a vaingloriousness , which he afterwards generously own , he do not refuse the homage which be pay he . 
 to show how dreadful be the trouble and anxiety with which General Washington have to contend , I may mention what at this time be call the " Conway Cabal . " a certain Irishman a Chevalier of St. Louis , and an officer in the french service arrive in America early in the year ' 77 in quest of military employment . he be speedily appoint to the rank of brigadier , and could not be content , forsooth , without an immediate promotion to be major - general . 
 Mr. C. have friend at Congress , who , as the General - in - Chief be inform , have promise he his speedy promotion . General Washington remonstrate , represent the injustice of promote to the high rank the young brigadier in the service ; and whilst the matter be pende , be put in possession of a letter from Conway to General Gates , whom he compliment , say , that " Heaven have be determine to save America , or a weak general and bad councillor would have ruin it . " the General enclose the note to Mr. Conway , without a word of comment ; and Conway offer his resignation , which be refuse by Congress , who appoint he Inspector - General of the army , with the rank of Major - General . 
 " and it be at this time , " say Harry   with many passionate exclamation indicate his rage with himself and his admiration of his leader , " when , by heaven , the glorious Chief be oppress by trouble enough to drive ten thousand man mad that I must interfere with my jealousy about the Frenchman ! I have not say much , only some nonsense to Greene and Cadwalader about get some frog against the Frenchman come to dine with we , and have a bagful of Marquises over from Paris , as we be not able to command ourselves ; but I should have know the Chief 's trouble , and that he have a well head than mine , and might have have the grace to hold my tongue . 
 " for a while the General say nothing , but I could remark , by the coldness of his demeanour , that something have occur to create a schism between he and I . Mrs. Washington , who have come to camp , also see that something be wrong . woman have artful way of soothe man and find their secret out . I be not sure that I should have ever try to learn the cause of the General 's displeasure , for I be as proud as he be , and besides "   say Hal , " when the Chief be angry , it be not pleasant come near he , I can promise you . " my brother be indeed subjugate by his old friend , and obey he and bow before he as a boy before a schoolmaster . 
 " at last , " Hal resume , " Mrs. Washington find out the mystery . ' speak to I after dinner , Colonel Hal , ' say she . ' come out to the parade - ground , before the dining - house , and I will tell you all . ' I leave a half - score of general officer and brigadier drink round the General 's table , and find Mrs. Washington wait for I . she then tell I it be the speech I have make about the box of Marquises , with which the General be offend . ' I should not have heed it in another , ' he have say , ' but I never think Harry Warrington would have join against I . ' 
 " I have to wait on he for the word that night , and find he alone at his table . ' can your Excellency give I five minute ' time ? ' I say , with my heart in my mouth . ' yes , surely , sir , ' say he , point to the other chair . ' will you please to be seat ? ' 
 " ' it use not always to be Sir and Colonel Warrington , between I and your excellency , ' I say . 
 " he say , calmly , ' the time be alter . ' 
 " ' et nos mutamur in illis , ' say I. ' Times and people be both change . ' 
 " ' you have some business with I ? ' he ask . 
 " ' be I speak to the Commander - in - chief or to my old friend ? ' I ask . 
 " he look at I gravely . ' well , to both , sir , ' he say . ' pray sit , Harry . ' 
 " ' if to General Washington , I tell his Excellency that I , and many officer of this army , be not well pleased to see a boy of twenty make a major - general over we , because he be a Marquis , and because he can not   speak the english language . if I speak to my old friend , I have to say that he have show I very little of trust or friendship for the last few week ; and that I have no desire to sit at your table , and have impertinent remark make by other there , of the way in which his Excellency turn his back on I . ' 
 " ' which charge shall I take first , Harry ? ' he ask , turn his chair away from the table , and cross his leg as if ready for a talk . ' you be jealous , as I gather , about the Marquis ? ' 
 " ' jealous , sir ! ' say I. ' an aide - de - camp of Mr. Wolfe be not jealous of a Jack - a - dandy who , five year ago , be be whip at school ! ' 
 " ' you yourself decline high rank than that which you hold , ' say the Chief , turn a little red . 
 " ' but I never bargain to have a macaroni Marquis to command I ! ' I cry . ' I will not , for one , carry the young gentleman 's order ; and since Congress and your Excellency choose to take your general out of the nursery , I shall humbly ask leave to resign , and retire to my plantation . ' 
 " ' do , Harry ; that be true friendship ! ' say the Chief , with a gentleness that surprise I . ' now that your old friend be in a difficulty , ' tis surely the good time to leave he . ' 
 " ' Sir ! ' say I. 
 " ' do as so many of the rest be do , Mr. Warrington . Et tu , Brute , as the play say . well , well , Harry ! I do not think it of you ; but , at least , you be in the fashion . ' 
 " ' you ask which charge you should take first ? ' I say . 
 " ' Ch , the promotion of the Marquis ? I recommend the appointment to Congress , no doubt ; and you and other gentleman disapprove it . ' 
 " ' I have speak for myself , sir , ' say I. 
 " ' if you take I in that tone , Colonel Warrington , I have nothing to answer ! ' say the Chief , rise up very fiercely ; ' and presume that I can recommend officer for promotion without ask your previous sanction . ' 
 " ' be on that tone , sir , ' say I , ' let I respectfully offer my resignation to your Excellency , found my desire to resign upon the fact , that Congress , at your Excellency 's recommendation , offer its high command to boy of twenty , who be scarcely even acquaint with our language . ' and I rise up and make his Excellency a bow . 
 " ' great heaven , Harry ! ' he cry   about this Marquis 's appointment he be beat , that be the fact , and he could not reply to I , ' can not   you believe that in this critical time of our affair , there be reason why special favour should be show to the first Frenchman of distinction who come amongst we ? ' 
 " ' no doubt , sir . if your Excellency acknowledge that Monsieur de Lafayette 's merit have nothing to do with the question . ' 
 " ' I acknowledge or deny nothing , sir ! ' say the General , with a stamp of his foot , and look as though he could be terribly angry if he would . ' be I here to be catechise by you ? stay . Hark , Harry ! I speak to you as a man of the world nay , as an old friend . this appointment humiliate you and other , you say ? be it so ! must we not bear humiliation , along with the other burthen and grief , for the sake of our country ? it be no more just perhaps that the Marquis should be set over you gentleman , than that your Prince Ferdinand or your Prince of Wales at home should have a command over veteran . but if in appoint this young nobleman we please a whole nation , and bring ourselves twenty million of ally , will you and other gentleman sulk because we do he honour ? ' tis easy to sneer at he   though , believe I , the Marquis have many more merit than you allow he ; to my mind it be more generous , as well as more polite , of Harry Warrington to welcome this stranger for the sake of the prodigious benefit our country may draw from he not to laugh at his peculiarity , but to aid he and help his ignorance by your experience as an old soldier : that be what I would do that be the part I expect of thee for it be the generous and manly one , Harry : but you choose to join my enemy , and when I be in trouble you say you will leave I . that be why I have be hurt : that be why I have be cold . I think I might count on your friendship and and you can tell whether I be right or no . I rely on you as on a brother , and you come and tell I you will resign . be it so ! be embark in this contest , by God 's will I will see it to an end . you be not the first , Mr. Warrington , have leave I on the way . ' 
 " he speak with so much tenderness , and as he speak his face wear such a look of unhappiness , that an extreme remorse and pity seize I , and I call out I know not what incoherent expression regard old time , and vow that if he would say the word , I never would leave he . you never love he , George , " say my brother , turn to I , " but I do beyond all mortal man ; and , though I be not clever like you , I think my instinct be in the right . he have a greatness not approach by other man . " 
 " I do not   say no , brother , " say I , " now . " 
 " Greatness , pooh ! " say the parson , growl over his wine . 
 " we walk into Mrs. Washington 's tea - room arm - in - arm , " Hal resume ; ' she look up quite kind , and see we be friend . ' be it all over , Colonel Harry ? ' she whisper . ' I know he have apply ever so often about your promotion ' 
 " ' I never will take it , ' say I. and that be how I come to do penance , " say Harry , tell I the story , " with Lafayette the next winter . "   Hal could imitate the Frenchman very well .   " ' I will go weez heem , ' say I. ' I know the way to Quebec , and when we be not in action with Sir Guy , I can hear his Excellency the Major - General say his lesson . ' there be no fight , you know we could get no army to act in Canada , and return to headquarters ; and what do you think disturb the Frenchman most ? the idea that people would laugh at he , because his command have come to nothing . and so they do laugh at he , and almost to his face too , and who could help it ? if our Chief have any weak point it be this Marquis . 
 " after our little difference we become as great friend as before if a man may be say to be friend with a Sovereign Prince , for as such I somehow could not help regard the General : and one night , when we have sate the company out , we talk of old time , and the jolly day of sport we have together both before and after Braddock 's ; and that pretty duel you be near have when we be boy . he laugh about it , and say he never see a man look more wicked and more bent on kill than you do : ' and to do Sir George justice , I think he have hat I ever since , ' say the Chief . ' ah ! ' he add , ' an open enemy I can face readily enough . ' tis the secret foe who cause the doubt and anguish ! we have sit with more than one at my table to - day , to whom I be oblige to show a face of civility , whose hand I must take when they be offer , though I know they be stab my reputation , and be eager to pull I down from my place . you speak but lately of be humiliate because a junior be set over you in command . what humiliation be yours compare to mine , who have to play the farce of welcome to these traitor ; who have to bear the neglect of Congress , and see man who have insult I promote in my own army ? if I consult my own feeling as a man , would I continue in this command ? you know whether my temper be naturally warm or not , and whether as a private gentleman I should be likely to suffer such slight and outrage as be put upon I daily ; but in the advancement of the sacred cause in which we be engage , we have to endure not only hardship and danger , but calumny and wrong , and may God give we strength to do our duty ! ' and then the General show I the paper regard the affair of that fellow Conway , whom Congress promote in spite of the intrigue , and down whose black throat John Cadwalader send the good ball he ever fire in his life . 
 " and it be here , " say Hal , conclude his story , " as I look at the Chief talk at night in the silence of the camp , and remember how lonely he be , what an awful responsibility he carry , how spy and traitor be eat out of his dish , and an enemy lie in front of he who might at any time overpower he , that I think , ' sure , this be the great man now in the world ; and what a wretch I be to think of my jealousy and annoyance , whilst he be walk serenely under his immense care ! ' " 
 " we talk but now of Wolfe , " say I. " here , indeed , be a great than Wolfe . to endure be great than to dare ; to tire out hostile fortune ; to be daunt by no difficulty ; to keep heart when all have lose it ; to go through intrigue spotless ; and to forgo even ambition when the end be gain who can say this be not greatness , or show the other Englishman who have achieve so much ? " 
 " I wonder , Sir George , you do not take Mr. Washington 's side , and wear the blue and buff yourself , " grumble Parson Blake .
