 " you and I think scarlet most become to our complexion , Joe Blake ! " say Sir George . " and my wife think there would not have be room for two such great man on one side . " 
 " well , at any rate , you be well than that odious , swearing , crazy General Lee , who be second in command ! " cry Lady Warrington . " and I be certain Mr. Washington never could write poetry and tragedy as you can ! what do the General say about George 's tragedy , Harry ? " 
 Harry burst into a roar of laughter   in which , of course , Mr. Miles must join his uncle . 
 " well ! " say he , " it be a fact that Hagan read one at my house to the General and Mrs. Washington and several more , and they all fall sound asleep ! " 
 " he never like my husband , that be the truth ! " say Theo , toss up her head , " and ' tis all the more magnanimous of Sir George to speak so well of he . " 
 and then Hal tell how , his battle over , his country free , his great work of liberation complete , the General lay down his victorious sword , and meet his comrade of the army in a last adieu . the last british soldier have quit the shore of the Republic , and the Commander - in - chief propose to leave New York for Annapolis , where Congress be sit , and there resign his commission . about noon , on the 4th December , a barge be in wait at Whitehall Ferry to convey he across the Hudson . the chief of the army assemble at a tavern near the ferry , and there the General join they . seldom as he show his emotion , outwardly , on this day he could not disguise it . he fill a glass of wine , and say , ' I bid you farewell with a heart full of love and gratitude , and wish your latter day may be as prosperous and happy as those past have be glorious and honourable . ' then he drink to they . ' I can not come to each of you to take my leave , ' he say , ' but shall be oblige if you will each come and shake I by the hand . ' 
 General Knox , who be near , come forward , and the Chief , with tear in his eye , embrace he . the other come , one by one , to he , and take their leave without a word . a line of infantry be form from the tavern to the ferry , and the General , with his officer follow he , walk silently to the water . he stand up in the barge , take off his hat , and wave a farewell . and his comrade remain bareheade on the shore till their leader 's boat be out of view . 
 as Harry speak very low , in the grey of evening , with sometimes a break in his voice , we all sit touch and silent . Hetty go up and kiss her father . 
 " you tell we of other , General Harry , " she say , pass a handkerchief across her eye , " of Marion and Sumpter , of Greene and Wayne , and Rawdon and Cornwallis , too , but you never mention Colonel Warrington ! " 
 " my dear , he will tell you his story in private ! " whisper my wife , cling to her sister , " and you can write it for he . " 
 but it be not to be . my Lady Theo , and her husband too , I own , catch the infection from she , never would let Harry rest , until we have coax , wheedle , and order he to ask Hetty in marriage . he obey , and it be she who now decline . " she have always , " she say , " the true regard for he from the dear old time when they have meet as almost child together . but she would never leave her father . when it please God to take he , she hop she would be too old to think of bear any other name but her own . Harry should have her love always as the good of brother ; and as George and Theo have such a nurseryful of child , " add Hester , " we must show our love to they , by save for the young one . " she send he her answer in writing , leave home on a visit to friend at a distance , as though she would have he to understand that her decision be final . as such Hal receive it . he do not break his heart . Cupid 's arrow , lady , do not   bite very deep into the tough skin of gentleman of our age ; though , to be sure , at the time of which I write , my brother be still a young man , be little more than fifty . Aunt Het be now a staid little lady with a voice of which year have touch the sweet chord , and a head which Time have powder over with silver . there be day when she look surprisingly young and blooming . ah I , my dear , it seem but a little while since the hair be golden brown , and the cheek as fresh as rose ! and then come the bitter blast of love unrequite which wither they ; and that long loneliness of heart which , they say , follow . why should Theo and I have be so happy , and thou so lonely ? why should my meal be garnish with love , and spread with plenty , while yon solitary outcast shiver at my gate ? I bow my head humbly before the Dispenser of pain and poverty , wealth and health ; I feel sometimes as if , for the prize which have fall to the lot of I unworthy , I do not dare to be grateful . but I hear the voice of my child in their garden , or look up at their mother from my book , or perhaps my sick - bed , and my heart fill with instinctive gratitude towards the bountiful Heaven that have so bl I . 
 since my accession to my uncle 's title and estate my intercourse with my good cousin Lord Castlewood have be very rare . I have always suppose he to be a follower of the win side in politic , and be not a little astonished to hear of his sudden appearance in opposition . a disappointment in respect to a place at court , of which he pretend to have have some promise , be partly the occasion of his rupture with the Ministry . it be say that the most August Person in the realm have flatly refuse to receive into the R - y - l household a nobleman whose character be so notoriously bad , and whose example   so the August Objector be pleased to say   would ruin and corrupt any respectable family . I hear of the Castlewoods during our travel in Europe , and that the mania for play have again seize upon his lordship . his impair fortune have be retrieve by the prudence of his wife and father - in - law , he have again begin to dissipate his income at hombre and lansquenet . there be tale of malpractice in which he have be discover , and even of chastisement inflict upon he by the victim of his unscrupulous art . his wife 's beauty and freshness fade early ; we meet but once at Aix - la - Chapelle , where Lady Castlewood beseech my wife to go and see she , and afflict Lady Warrington 's kind heart by story of the neglect and outrage of which her unfortunate husband be guilty . we be willing to receive these as some excuse and palliation for the unhappy lady 's own conduct . a notorious adventurer , gambler , and spadassin , call himself the Chevalier de Barry , and say to be a relative of the mistress of the french king , but afterwards turn out to be an Irishman of low extraction , be in constant attendance upon the Earl and Countess at this time , and conspicuous for the audacity of his lie , the extravagance of his play , and somewhat mercenary gallantry towards the other sex , and a ferocious bravo courage , which , however , fail he on one or two awkward occasion , if common report say true . he subsequently marry , and render miserable a lady of title and fortune in England . the poor little american lady 's interested union with Lord Castlewood be scarcely more happy . 
 I remember our little Miles 's infantile envy be excite by learn that Lord Castlewood 's second son , a child a few month young than himself , be already an ensign on the irish establishment , whose pay the fond parent regularly draw . this piece of preferment my lord must have get for his cadet whilst he be on good term with the Minister , during which period of favour Will Esmond be also shift off to New York . whilst I be in America myself , we read in an english journal that Captain Charles Esmond have resign his commission in his Majesty 's service , as not wish to take up arm against the countryman of his mother , the Countess of Castlewood . " it be the doing of the old fox , Van den Bosch , " Madam Esmond say ; " he wish to keep his virginian property safe , whatever side should win ! " I may mention , with respect to this old worthy , that he continue to reside in England for a while after the Declaration of Independence , not at all deny his sympathy with the american cause , but keep a pretty quiet tongue , and allege that such a very old man as himself be past the age of action or mischief , in which opinion the Government concur , no doubt , as he be leave quite unmolested . but of a sudden a warrant be out after he , when it be surprising with what agility he stir himself , and skip off to France , whence he presently embark upon his return to Virginia . 
 the old man bear the bad reputation amongst the Loyalists of our colony ; and be nickname " Jack the Painter " amongst they , much to his indignation , after a certain miscreant who be hang in England for burn naval store in our port there . he profess to have lose prodigious sum at home by the persecution of the government , distinguish himself by the loud patriotism and the most violent religious outcry in Virginia ; where , nevertheless , he be not much more like by the Whigs than by the party who still remain faithful to the Crown . he wonder that such an old Tory as Madam Esmond of Castlewood be suffer to go at large , and be for ever cry out against she amongst the gentleman of the new Assembly , the Governor , and officer of the State . he and Fanny have high word in Richmond one day , when she tell he he be an old swindler and traitor , and that the mother of Colonel Henry Warrington , the bosom friend of his Excellency the Commander - in - Chief , be not to be insult by such a little smuggling slave - driver as he ! I think it be in the year 1780 an accident happen , when the old Register Office at Williamsburg be burn down , in which there be a copy of the formal assignment of the Virginia property from Francis Lord Castlewood to my grandfather Henry Esmond , Esquire . " oh , " say Fanny , " of course this be the work of Jack the Painter ! " and Mr. Van den Bosch be for prosecute she for libel , but that Fanny take to her bed at this juncture , and die . 
 Van den Bosch make contract with the new Government , and sell they bargain , as the phrase be . he supply horse , meat , forage , all of bad quality ; but when Arnold come into Virginia   in the King 's service   and burn right and left , Van den Bosch 's store and tobacco - house somehow be spar . some secret whig now take their revenge on the old rascal . a couple of his ship in James River , his store , and a quantity of his cattle in their stall be roast amidst a hideous bellowing ; and he get a note , as he be in Arnold 's company , say that friend have serve he as he serve other ; and contain " Tom the Glazier 's compliment to brother Jack the Painter . " nobody pity the old man , though he go well - nigh mad at his loss . in Arnold 's suite come the Honourable Captain William Esmond , of the New York Loyalists , as aide - de - camp to the General . when Howe occupy Philadelphia , Will be say to have make some money keep a gambling - house with an officer of the dragoon of Anspach . I know not how he lose it . he could not have have much when he consent to become an aide - de - camp of Arnold . 
 now , the King 's officer have reappear in the province , Madam Esmond think fit to open her house at Castlewood and invite they thither and actually receive Mr. Arnold and his suite . " it be not for I , " she say , " to refuse my welcome to a man whom my Sovereign have admit to grace . " and she throw her house open to he , and treat he with great though frigid respect whilst he remain in the district . the General go , and , his precious aide - de - camp with he , some of the rascal who follow in their suite remain behind in the house where they have receive so much hospitality , insult the old lady in her hall , insult her people , and finally set fire to the old mansion in a frolic of drunken fury . our house at Richmond be not burn , luckily , though Mr. Arnold have fire the town ; and thither the undaunted old lady proceed , surround by her people , and never swerve in her loyalty , in spite of her ill - usage . " the Esmonds , " she say , " be accustom to royal ingratitude . " 
 and now Mr. Van den Bosch , in the name of his grandson and my Lord Castlewood , in England , set up a claim to our property in Virginia . he say it be not my lord 's intention to disturb Madam Esmond in her enjoyment of the estate during her life , but that his father , it have always be understand , have give his kinsman a life - interest in the place , and only continue it to his daughter out of generosity . now my lord propose that his second son should inhabit Virginia , for which the young gentleman have always show the warm sympathy . the outcry against Van den Bosch be so great that he would have be tar and feather , have he remain in Virginia . he betake himself to Congress , represent himself as a martyr ruin in the cause of liberty , and pray for compensation for himself and justice for his grandson . 
 my mother live long in dreadful apprehension , have in truth a secret , which she do not like to disclose to any one . her title be burn ! the deed of assignment in her own house , the copy in the Registry at Richmond , have alike be destroy by chance ? by villainy ? who could say ? she do not like to confide this trouble in write to I . she open herself to Hal , after the surrender of York Town , and he acquaint I with the fact in a letter by a british officer return home on his parole . then I remember the unlucky word I have let slip before Will Esmond at the coffee - house at New York ; and a part of this iniquitous scheme break upon I . 
 as for Mr. Will : there be a tablet in Castlewood Church , in Hampshire , inscribe , Dulce et decorum est pro patria mori , and announce that " this marble be place by a mourning brother , to the memory of the Honourable William Esmond , Esquire , who die in North America , in the service of his king . " but how ? when , towards the end of 1781 , a revolt take place in the Philadelphia Line of the Congress Army , and Sir Henry Clinton send out agent to the mutineer , what become of they ? the man take the spy prisoner , and proceed to judge they , and my brother   whom they know and love , and have often follow under fire , who have be send from camp to make term with the troop , recognise one of the spy , just as execution be about to be do upon he and the wretch , with horrid outcry , grovel and kneeling at Colonel Warrington 's foot , besought he for mercy , and promise to confess all to he . to confess what ? Harry turn away sick at heart . will 's mother and sister never know the truth . they always fancy it be in action he be kill . 
 as for my lord earl , whose noble son have be the intendant of an illustrious Prince , and who have enrich himself at play with his r -l master : I go to see his lordship when I hear of this astounding design against our property , and remonstrate with he on the matter . for myself , as I show he , I be not concern , as I have determine to cede my right to my brother . he receive I with perfect courtesy ; smile when I speak of my disinterestedness ; say he be sure of my affectionate feeling towards my brother , but what must be his towards his son ? he have always hear from his father : he would take his Bible oath of that : that , at my mother 's death , the property would return to the head of the family . at the story of the title which Colonel Esmond have cede , he shrug his shoulder , and treat it as a fable . " on ne fait pas de ce folie la ! " say he , offer I snuff , " and your grandfather be a man of esprit ! my little grandmother be eprise of he : and my father , the most good - natured soul alive , lend they the virginian property to get they out of the way ! C'etoit un scandale , mon cher , un joli petit scandale ! " oh , if my mother have but hear he ! I might have be dispose to take a high tone : but he say , with the utmost good - nature , " my dear Knight , be you go to fight about the character of our grandmother ? allon donc ! come , I will be fair with you ! we will compromise , if you like , about this virginian property ! " and his lordship name a sum great than the actual value of the estate . 
 Amazed at the coolness of this worthy , I walk away to my coffee - house , where , as it happen , an old friend be to dine with I , for whom I have a sincere regard . I have feel a pang at not be able to give this gentleman my living of Warrington on - Waveney , but I could not , as he himself confess honestly . his life have be too loose , and his example in my village could never have be edify : besides , he would have die of ennui there , after be accustom to a town life ; and he have a prospect finally , he tell I , of settle himself most comfortably in London and the church .   he be the second Incumbent of Lady Whittlesea 's Chapel , Mayfair , and marry Elizabeth , relict of Hermann Voelcker , Esq . , the eminent brewer .   my guest , I need not say , be my old friend Sampson , who never fail to dine with I when I come to town , and I tell he of my interview with his old patron . 
 I could not have light upon a well confidant . " gracious power ! " say Sampson , " the man 's roguery beat all belief ! when I be secretary and factotum at Castlewood , I can take my oath I see more than once a copy of the deed of assignment by the late lord to your grandfather : ' in consideration of the love I bear to my kinsman Henry Esmond , Esq . , husband of my dear mother Rachel , Lady Viscountess Dowager of Castlewood , I , etc . ' so it run . I know the place where ' tis keep let we go thither as fast as horse will carry we to - morrow . there be somebody there never mind whom , Sir George who have an old regard for I . the paper may be there to this very day , and o Lord , o Lord , but I shall be thankful if I can in any way show my gratitude to you and your glorious brother ! " his eye fill with tear . he be an altered man . at a certain period of the port wine Sampson always allude with compunction to his past life , and the change which have take place in his conduct since the awful death of his friend Doctor Dodd . 
 Quick as we be , we do not arrive at Castlewood too soon . I be look at the fountain in the court , and listen to that sweet sad music of its plashing , which my grandfather tell of in his memoire , and people the place with bygone figure , with beatrix in her beauty ; with my Lord Francis in scarlet , call to his dog and mount his grey horse ; with the young page of old who win the castle and the heiress when Sampson come run down to I with an old volume in rough calf - bind in his hand , contain draft of letter , copy of agreement , and various writing , some by a secretary of my Lord Francis , some in the slim handwriting of his wife my grandmother , some bear the signature of the last lord ; and here be a copy of the assignment sure enough , as it have be send to my grandfather in Virginia . " Victoria , Victoria ! " cry Sampson , shake my hand , embrace everybody . " here be a guinea for thee , Betty . we will have a bowl of punch at the Three Castles to - night ! " as we be talk , the wheel of postchaise be hear , and a couple of carriage drive into the court contain my lord and a friend , and their servant in the next vehicle . his lordship look only a little pale than usual at see I . 
 " what procure I the honour of Sir George Warrington 's visit , and pray , Mr. Sampson , what do you do here ? " say my lord . I think he have forget the existence of this book , or have never see it ; and when he offer to take his Bible oath of what he have hear from his father , have simply volunteer a perjury . 
 I be shake hand with his companion , a nobleman with whom I have have the honour to serve in America . " I come , " I say , " to convince myself of a fact , about which you be mistaken yesterday ; and I find the proof in your lordship 's own house . your lordship be pleased to take your lordship 's Bible oath , that there be no agreement between your father and his mother , relative to some property which I hold . when Mr. Sampson be your lordship 's secretary , he perfectly remember have see a copy of such an assignment , and here it be . " 
 " and do you mean , Sir George Warrington , that unknown to I you have be visit my paper ? " cry my lord . 
 " I doubt the correctness of your statement , though back by your lordship 's Bible oath , " I say with a bow . 
 " this , sir , be robbery ! give the paper back ! " bawl my lord . 
 " robbery be a rough word , my lord . shall I tell the whole story to Lord Rawdon ? " 
 " what , be it about the Marquisate ? Connu , connu , my dear Sir George ! we always call you the Marquis in New York . I do not   know who bring the story from Virginia . " 
 I never have hear this absurd nickname before , and do not care to notice it . " my Lord Castlewood , " I say , " not only doubt , but yesterday lay a claim to my property , take his Bible oath that " 
 Castlewood give a kind of gasp , and then say , " great heaven ! do you mean , Sir George , that there actually be an agreement extant ? yes . here it be my father 's handwriting , sure enough ! then the question be clear . upon my o well , upon my honour as a gentleman ! I never know of such an agreement , and must have be mistake in what my father say . this paper clearly show the property be your : and not be mine why , I wish you joy of it ! " and he hold out his hand with the blandest smile . 
 " and how thankful you will be to I , my lord , for have enable he to establish the right , " say Sampson , with a leer on his face . 
 " thankful ? no , confound you . not in the least ! " say my lord . " I be a plain man ; I do not   disguise from my cousin that I would rather have have the property than he . Sir George , you will stay and dine with we . a large party be come down here shoot ; we ought to have you one of we ! " 
 " my lord , " say I , button the book under my coat , " I will go and get this document copy , and then return it to your lordship . as my mother in Virginia have have her paper burn , she will be put out of much anxiety by have this assignment safely lodge . " 
 " what , have Madam Esmond 's paper be burn ? when the deuce be that ? " ask my lord . 
 " my lord , I wish you a very good afternoon . Come , Sampson , you and I will go and dine at the Three Castles . " and I turn on my heel , make a bow to Lord R , and from that day to this I have never set my foot within the hall of my ancestor . 
 shall I ever see the old mother again , I wonder ? she live in Richmond , never have rebuild her house in the country . when Hal be in England , we send her picture of both her son , paint by the admirable Sir Joshua Reynolds . we sate to he , the last year Mr. Johnson be alive , I remember . and the Doctor , peer about the studio , and see the image of Hal in his uniform   the appearance of it cause no little excitement in those day , ask who be this ? and be inform that it be the famous American General General Warrington , Sir George 's brother . " General who ? " cry the Doctor , " General where ? Pooh ! I do not   know such a service ! " and he turn his back and walk out of the premise . my worship be paint in scarlet , and we have replica of both performance at home . but the picture which Captain Miles and the girl declare to be most like be a family sketch by my ingenious neighbour , Mr. Bunbury , who have draw I and my lady with Monsieur Gumbo follow we , and write under the piece , " SIR GEORGE , MY LADY , and their master . " 
 here my master come ; he have poke out all the house - fire , have look to all the bolt , have order the whole male and female crew to their chamber ; and begin to blow my candle out , and say , " Time , Sir George , to go to bed ! twelve o'clock ! " 
 " bless I ! so indeed it be . " and I close my book , and go to my rest , with a blessing on those now around I asleep .
